Fact	CASE NO.
Fact	: Writ Petition (civil) 135 of 1970 PETITIONER: <NAME> and <NAME> RESPONDENT: State of Kerala and Anr DATE OF JUDGMENT: 24/04/1973 BENCH: <NAME> & <NAME> & <NAME> & <NAME> & <NAME> & <NAME> & <NAME> & <NAME> & <NAME> & <NAME> & <NAME> & Y.V.Chandrachud JUDGMENT:  JUDGMENT W.P.
Fact	(C) 135 OF 1970 Appellants: His Holiness <NAME> and <NAME>.
Fact	Vs.  Respondent: State of Kerala and Anr.
Fact	Decided On: 24.04.1973 Hon'ble Judges:  <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME>, JJ.
Fact	JUDGMENT <NAME>, <NAME>  1.
Fact	I propose to divide my judgment into eight parts.
Fact	Part I will deal with Introduction; Part II with interpretation of Golakhnath case; Part III with the interpretation of the original Article 368 , as it existed prior to its amendment; Part IV with the validity of the Constitution (T wenty-fourth Amendment) Act; Part V with the validity of Section 2 of the Constitution (Twenty-fifth Amendment) Act; Part VI with the validity of Section 3 of the Constitution (Twenty-fifth Amendment) Act; Part VII with Constitution (Twenty- ninth Amendment) Act ; and Part VIII with conclusions.
Fact	PART I-Introduction  2.
Fact	All the six writ petitions involve common questions as to the validity of the Twenty- fourth, Twenty-fifth and Twenty-ninth Amendments of the Constitution.
Fact	I may give a few facts in Writ petition No.
Fact	135 of 1970 to show how the question arises in this petition.
Fact	Writ Petition No.
Fact	135 of 1970 was filed by the petitioner on March 21, 1970 under Article 32 of the Constitution for enforcement of his fundamental rights under Articles 25, 26, 14, 19(1)(f) and 31 of the Constitution.
Fact	He prayed that the provisions of the Kerala Land Reforms Act, 1963 (Act 1 of 1964) as amended by the Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act 1969 (Act 35 of 1969) be declared unConstitutional, ultra vires and void.
Fact	He further  prayed for an appropriate writ or order to issue during the pendency of the petition.
Fact	This Court issued rule nisi on March 25, 1970.
Fact	3.
Fact	During the pendency of the writ petition, the Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act 1971 (Kerala Act No.
Fact	25 of 1971) was passed which received the assent of the President on August 7, 1971.
Fact	The petitioner filed an application for permission to urge additional grounds and to impugn the Constitutional validity of the Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act 1971 (Kerala Act No.
Fact	25 of 1971).
Fact	4.
Fact	In the meantime, <ORG> by its judgment dated April 26, 1971 in <CITATION_1778199>  S.C.C.
Fact	364 (Civil Appeals Nos.
Fact	143, 203-242, 274 & 309 of 1971).
Fact	Judgment dated April 26, 1971 upheld the majority judgment of <ORG> in <CITATION_1655569> A.I.R.
Fact	1971 Kerala 98 whereby certain, sections of the Act were struck down.
Fact	5.
Fact	The Constitution (Twenty-fifth Amendment) Act came into force on November 5, 1971, the Constitution (Twenty-fifth Amendment) Act came into force on Ap ril 20, 1972 and the Constitution (Twenty-ninth Amendment) Act came into force on June 9, 1972.
Fact	The effect of the Twenty-ninth Amendment of the Constitution was that it inserted the following Acts in the Ninth Schedule to the Constitution:  65.
Fact	The Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act, 1969 (Kerala Act 35 of 1969).
Fact	66.
Fact	The Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act, 1971 (Kerala Act 25 of 1971).
Fact	6.
Fact	The petitioner then moved an application for urging additional grounds and for amendment of the writ petition in order to challenge the above Constitutional amendments.
Fact	7.
Fact	The <ORG> allowed the application for urging additional grounds and for amendment of the writ petition on August 10, 1972 and issued notices to the Advocates-General to appear before this <ORG> and take such part in the proceedings as they may be advised.
Fact	8.
Fact	When the case was placed before the Constitutional bench, it referred this case to a larger bench to determine the validity of the impugned Constitutional amendments.
Fact	9.
Fact	Similar orders were pass ed in the other writ petitions.
RulingByLowerCourt	10.
RulingByLowerCourt	The larger bench was accordingly constituted.
Fact	It was then felt that it would be necessary to decide whether <CITATION_21266288>  2 S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	762 was rightly decided or not.
RatioOfTheDecision	However, as I see it, the question whether <NAME>  2 S.C.R.
Fact	762 case was rightly decided or not does not matter because the real issue is different and of much greater importance, the issue being : what is the extent of the amending power conferred by Article 368 of the Constitution, apart from Article 13(2 ), on <ORG> ?
Argument	11.
Argument	The respondents claim that <ORG> can abrogate fundamental rights such as freedom of speech and expression, freedom to form associations or unions, and freedom of religion.
Argument	They claim that democracy can even be replaced and one-party rule established.
Argument	Indeed, short of repeal of the Constitution, any form of Government with no freedom to the citizens can be set up by <ORG> by exercising its powers under Article  368.
Argument	12.
Argument	On the side of the petitioners it is urged t hat the power of <ORG> is much more limited.
Argument	The petitioners say that the Constitution gave the Indian citizen freedoms which were to subsist for ever and the Constitution was drafted to free the nation from any future tyranny of the representatives of the people.
Argument	It is this freedom from tyranny which, according to the petitioners, has been taken away by the impugned Article 31C which has been inserted by the Twenty-fifth Amendment.
Fact	If Article 31C is valid, they say, hereafter <ORG> and State Legislatures and not the Constitution, will determine how much freedom is good for the citizens.
Precedent	13.
Precedent	These cases raise grave issues.
Precedent	But however grave the issues may be, the answer must depend on the interpretation of the words in Article 368 , read in accordance with the principles of interpretation which are applied to the interpretation of a Constitution given by the people to themselves.
Precedent	14.
RatioOfTheDecision	I must interpret Article 368 in the setting of our Constitution, in the background of our history and in the light  of our aspirations and hopes, and other relevant circumstances.
RatioOfTheDecision	No other Constitution in the world is like ours.
RatioOfTheDecision	No other Constitution combines under its wings such diverse peoples, numbering now more than 550 millions, with different languages and religions and in different stages of economic development, into one nation, and no other nation is faced with such vast socio-economic problems.
RatioOfTheDecision	15.
RatioOfTheDecision	I need hardly observe that I am not interpreting an ordinary statute, but a Constitution which apart from setting up a machinery for government, has a noble and grand vision.
RatioOfTheDecision	The vision was put in words in the Preamble and carried out in part by conferring fundamental rights on the people.
Precedent	The vision was directed to be further carried out by the application of directive principles.
Precedent	PART II-Interpretation of <NAME> Case.
Precedent	16.
Fact	Before proceeding with the main task, it is necessary to ask : what was decided in <CITATION_21266288>  2 S.C.R.
Fact	762 ?
RatioOfTheDecision	In order to properly appreciate that case, it is necessary first to ha ve a look at <CITATION_1706770>  S.C.R.
Precedent	89 and <CITATION_1308308>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	933.
Precedent	17.
Precedent	The Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951, which inserted inter alia Articles 31A and 31B in the Constitution was the subject matter of decision in <NAME> [1952] S.C.R.
Precedent	89 case.
RulingByLowerCourt	The main arguments relevant to the present case which were advanced in support of the petition before this Court were summarised by <NAME> as he then was, as follows:  First, the power of amending the Constitution provided for under Article 368 was conferred not on <ORG> but on the two <ORG>s of <ORG> as designated body and, therefore, the provisional <ORG> was not competent to exercise that power under Article 379.
RulingByLowerCourt	Fourthly, in any case Article 368 is a complete code in itself and does not provide for any amendment being made in the bill after it has been introduced in the <ORG>.
RulingByLowerCourt	The bill in the present case having been admittedly amended in several particulars during its passage through the <ORG>, the Ame ndment Act cannot be said to have been passed in conformity with the procedure prescribed in Article 368.
RulingByLowerCourt	Fifthly, the Amendment Act , in so far as it purports to take away or abridge the rights conferred by Part III of the Constitution, falls within the prohibition of Article 13(2).
RulingByLowerCourt	X X X  18.
RulingByLowerCourt	As stated in the head note, this Court held:  The provisional <ORG> is competent to exercise the power of amending the Constitution under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fact that the said article refers to the two <ORG> of the <ORG> and the President separately and not to the <ORG>, does not lead to the inference that the body which is invested with the power to amend is not the <ORG> but a different body consisting of the two <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	The words "all the powers conferred by the provisions of this Constitution on <ORG>" in Article 379 are not confined to such powers as could be exercised by the provisional <ORG> consisting of a single chamber, but are wide enough to include the power to amend the Co nstitution conferred by Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	19.
RatioOfTheDecision	I may mention that Mr. <NAME> contends that the conclusion just mentioned was wrong and that the body that amends the Constitution under Article 368 is not <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	20.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Court further held:  The view that Article 368 is a complete code in itself in respect of the procedure provided by it and does not contemplate any amendment of a Bill for amendment of the Constitution after it has been introduced, and that if the Bill is amended during its passage through the <ORG>, the Amendment Act cannot be said to have been passed in conformity with the procedure prescribed by Article 368 and would be invalid, is erroneous.
RatioOfTheDecision	Although "law" must ordinarily include Constitutional law there is a clear demarcation between ordinary law which is made in the exercise of legislative power and Constitutional law, which is made in the exercise of constituent power.
Precedent	In the context of Article 13 , "law" must be taken to mean rules or regulations made in exercise of ordinary legislat ive power and not amendments to the Constitution made in the exercise of constituent power with the result that Article 13(2) does not affect amendments made under Article 368.
Precedent	21.
Precedent	Although the decision in <NAME> [1952] S.C.R.
Precedent	89 case was not challenged in <NAME> [1965] 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	933 case, <NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	thought it fit to give reasons for expressing full concurrence with that decision.
Precedent	22.
Argument	The only contention before the <ORG> was that "since it appears that the powers prescribed by Article 226 are likely to be affected by the intended amendment of the provisions contained in Part III, the bill introduced for the purpose of making such an amendment, must attract the proviso, and as the impugned Act has admittedly not gone through the procedure prescribed by the proviso, it is invalid".
Argument	According to <NAME>, C.J.
Argument	"that raised the question about the construction of the provisions contained in Article 368 and the relation between the substantive part of Article 368 with its provis o.
Argument	23.
Argument	The Chief Justice came to the conclusion that "as a matter of construction, there is no escape from the conclusion that Article 368 provides for the amendment of the provisions contained in Part III without imposing on <ORG> an obligation to adopt the procedure prescribed by the proviso.
Argument	24.
Argument	The learned Chief Justice thought that the power to amend in the context was a very wide power and it could not be controlled' by the literal dictionary meaning of the word "amend".
Argument	He expressed his agreement with the reasoning of <NAME> regarding the applicability of Article 13(2) to Constitution Amendment Act s passed under Article  368.
Argument	He further held that when Article 368 confers on <ORG> the right to amend the Constitution, it can be exercised over all the provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	He thought that "if the Constitution-makers had intended that any future amendment of the provisions in regard to fundamental rights should be subject to Article 13(2 ), they would have taken the pre caution of making a clear provision in that behalf.
Precedent	25.
Precedent	He seemed to be in agreement with the following observations of <NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	in <CITATION_1857950>  S.C.R.
Precedent	88 at p. 100:  the inclusion of Article 13(1) and (2) in the Constitution appears to be a matter of abundant caution.
Precedent	Even in their absence if any of the fundamental rights was infringed by any legislative enactment, the <ORG> has always the power to declare the enactment, to the extent it transgresses the limits, invalid.
Precedent	26.
Precedent	He was of the view that even though the relevant provisions of Part III can be justly described as the very foundation and the cornerstone of the democratic way of life ushered in this country by the Constitution, it cannot be said that the fundamental rights guaranteed to the citizens are eternal and inviolate in the sense that they can never be abridged or amended.
Precedent	27.
Precedent	According to him, it was legitimate to assume that the Constitution-makers visualised that <ORG> would be competent to make amendments in these rights  so as to meet the challenge of the problems which may arise in the course of socio-economic progress and development of the country.
Precedent	28.
Argument	<NAME>, J., as he then was, agreed with the Chief Justice that the 17th Amendment was valid even though the procedure laid down in the proviso to Article 368 had not been followed.
Argument	But he expressed his difficulty in accepting the part of the reasoning in <NAME>  S.C.R.
Precedent	89 case.
Precedent	29.
Precedent	He observed as follows:  It is true that there is no complete definition of the word "law"' in the article but it is significant that the definition does not seek to exclude Constitutional amendments which it would have been easy to indicate in the definition by adding "but shall not include an amendment of the Constitution".
Precedent	30.
Precedent	He further observed:  The meaning of Article 13 thus depends on the sense in which the word "law" in Article 13(2) is to be understood.
Precedent	If an amendment can be said to fall within the term "law", the Fundamental Rights become "eternal and inviolate" to b orrow the language of the Japanese Constitution.
Precedent	Article 13 is then on par with Article 5 of the American Federal Constitution in its immutable prohibition as long as it stands.
Precedent	31.
Precedent	According to him "Our Preamble is more akin in nature to the American Declaration of Independence (July 4, 1776) then to the preamble to the Constittuion of the United States.
Precedent	It does not make any grant of power but it gives a direction and purpose to the Constitution which is reflected in Parts III and IV.
Precedent	Is it to be imagined that a two-thirds majority of the two <ORG> at any time is all that is necessary to alter it without even consulting the States ?
Precedent	It is not even included in the proviso to Article 368 and it is difficult to think that as it has not the protection of the proviso it must be within the main part of Article 368.
Precedent	32.
Precedent	He further observed:  I would require stronger reason than those given in <NAME> case to make me accept the view that Fundamental Rights were not really fundamental but were intended t o be within the powers of amendment in common with the other parts of the Constitution and without the concurrence of the States.
Precedent	33.
Precedent	He held:  What Article 368 does is to lay down the manner of amendment and the necessary conditions for the effectiveness of the amendment....
Precedent	The Constitution gives so many assurances in Part III that it would be difficult to think that they were the play-things of a special majority.
Precedent	To hold this would mean prima facie that the most solemn parts of our Constitution stand on the same footing as any other provision and even on a less firm ground than one on which the articles mentioned in the proviso stand.
Precedent	34.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. although agreeing that the writ petition should be dismissed, raised various doubts and he said that he was reserving his opinion on the question whether <NAME> case was rightly decided.
Precedent	He thought:  The language of Article 368 is plain enough to show that the action of <ORG> in amending the Constitution is a legislative act like one in exe rcise of its normal legislative power.
Precedent	The only difference in respect of an amendment of the Constitution is that the Bill amending the Constitution has to be passed by a special majority (here I have in mind only those amedments which do not attract the proviso to Article 368 ).
Precedent	The result of a legislative action of a legislature cannot be other than 'law' and, therefore, it seems to me that the fact that the legislation deals with the amendment of a provision of the Constitution would not make its result any the less a 'law'.
Precedent	35.
Precedent	He observed:  It is true that the Constitution does not directly prohibit the amendment of Part III.
Precedent	But it would indeed be strange that rights which are considered to be fundamental and which include one which is guaranteed by the Constitution (vide Article 32 ) should be more easily capable of being abridged or restricted than any of the matters referred to in the proviso to Article 368 some of which are perhaps less vital than fundamental rights.
Precedent	It is possible, as suggested by  my learned brother, that Article 368 merely lays down the procedure to be followed for amending the Constitution and does not confer a power to amend the Constitution which, I think, has to be ascertained from the provision sought to be amended or other relevant provisions or the preamble.
Precedent	36.
Precedent	Later, he observed:  Above all, it formulated a solemn and dignified preamble which appears to be an epitome of the basic features of the Constitution.
Precedent	Can it not be said that these are indica of the intention of <ORG> to give a permanency to the basic features of the Constitution ?
Precedent	37.
Precedent	He posed a further question by observing:  It is also a matter for consideration whether making a change in a basic feature of the Constitution can be regarded merely as an amendment or would it be, in effect, rewriting a part of the Constitution; and if the latter, would it be within the purview of Article 368 ?
Precedent	38.
Argument	He then stressed the prime importance of the preamble:  The Constitution indicates three modes of  amendments and assuming that the provisions of Article 368 confer power on <ORG> to amend the Constitution, it will still have to be considered whether as long as the preamble stands unamended, that power can be exercised with respect to any of the basic features of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	To illustrate my point, as long as the words 'sovereign democratic republic' are there, could the Constitution be amended so as to depart from the democratic form of Government or its republic character?
RatioOfTheDecision	If that cannot be done, then, as long as the words "Justice, social, economic and political etc.," are there could any of the rights enumerated in Articles 14 to 19, 21, 25, 31 and 32 be taken away ?
RatioOfTheDecision	If they cannot, it will be for consideration whether they can be modified.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has been said, no doubt, that the preamble is not a part of our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	But, I think, that if upon a comparison of the preamble with the broad features of the Constitution it would appear that the preamble is an epitome of those features or, to  put it differently if these features are an amplification or concretisation of the concepts set out in the preamble it may have to be considered whether the preamble is not a part of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	While considering this question it would be of relevance to bear in mind that the preamble is not of the common run such as is to be found in an Act of a legislature.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has the stamp of deep deliberation and is marked by precision.
Precedent	Would this not suggest that the framers of the Constitution attached special significance to it?
Precedent	39.
Precedent	Coming now to <NAME> case, the petitioner had challenged the validity of the Constitution (Seventeenth Amendment) Act, 1964 which included in the Ninth Schedule, among other acts, the Punjab Security of Land Tenures Act, 1953 (Act 10 of 1953), and the Mysore Land Reforms Act (Act 10 of 1962) as amended by Act 14 of 1965.
Precedent	40.
Precedent	It was urged before the Court that <NAME> [1952] S.C.R.
Precedent	89 case in which the validity of the Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951 and Sajja n <NAME>'s [1965] 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	933 case in which the validity of the Constitution (Seventeenth Amendment) Act was in question had been wrongly decided by this <ORG>.
Precedent	41.
Precedent	<NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	speaking for himself and 4 other judges summarised the conclusions at page 815 as follows:  The aforesaid discussion leads to the following results:  (1) The power of the <ORG> to amend the Constitution is derived from Articles 245, 246 and 248 of the Constitution and not from Article 368 thereof which only deals with procedure.
Precedent	Amendment is a legislative process.
Precedent	(2) Amendment is 'law' within the meaning of Article 13 of the Constitution and, therefore, if it takes away or abridges the rights conferred by Part III thereof, it is void.
Precedent	(3) The Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951, Constitution (Fourth Amendment) Act, 1955, and the Constitution (Seventeenth Amendment) Act, 1964, abridge the scope of the fundamental rights.
Precedent	But, on the basis of earlier decisions of this <ORG>, they were valid.
RulingByPresentCourt	(4) On the application of t he doctrine of 'prospective over-  ruling', as explained by us earlier, our decision will have only prospective operation and, therefore, the said amendments will continue to be valid.
RulingByPresentCourt	(5) We declare that the <ORG> will have no power from the date of this decision to amend any of the provisions of Part III of the Constitution so as to take away or abridge the fundamental rights enshrined therein.
Precedent	(6) As the Constitution (Seventeenth Amendment) Act holds the field, the validity of the two impugned Acts, namely, the Punjab Security of Land Tenures Act X of 1953, and the Mysore Land Reforms Act X of 1962, as amended by Act XIV of 1965, cannot be questioned on the ground that they offend Articles 13, 14 or 31 of the Constitution.
Precedent	42.
Precedent	It must be borne in mind that these conclusions were given in the light of the Constitution as it stood then i.e.
Precedent	while Article 13(2) subsisted in the Constitution.
Precedent	It was then not necessary to decide the ambit of Article 368 with respect to the powers of <ORG> to amend  Article 13(2) or to amend Article 368 itself.
Precedent	It is these points that have now to be decided.
Precedent	43.
Precedent	It may further be observed that the Chief Justice refused to express an opinion on the contention that, in exercise of the power of amendment, <ORG> cannot destroy the fundamental structure of the Constitution but can only modify the provision thereof within the framework of the original instrument for its better effectuation.
Precedent	44.
Precedent	As will be seen later, the first conclusion above, does not survive for discussion any longer because it is rightly admitted on behalf of the petitioners that the Constitution (Twenty Fourth Amendment) Act, 1971, in so far as it transfers power to amend the Constitution from the residuary entry (Entry 97 List 1) or Article 248 of the Constitution to Article 368 , is valid; in other words Article 368 of the Constitution as now amended by the Twenty Fourth Amendment deals not only with the procedure for amendment but also confers express power on <ORG> to amend the Constitutio n.  45.
Precedent	I will also not discuss the merits of the second conclusion as the same result follows in this case even if it be assumed in favour of the respondents that an amendment of the Constitution is not law within Article 13(2) of the Constitution.
Precedent	46.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. as he then was, came to the following conclusions at page 902:  (i) that the Fundamental Rights are outside the amendatory process if the amendment seeks to abridge or take away any of the rights;  (ii) that <NAME> case (and <NAME> case which followed it) conceded the power of amendment over Part III of the Constitution on an erroneous view of Articles 13(2) and 368.
Precedent	(iii) that the First, Fourth and Seventh Amendments being part of the Constitution by acquiescence for a long time, cannot now be challenged and they contain authority for the seventeenth Amendment;  (iv) that this <ORG> having now laid down that Fundamental Rights cannot be abridged or taken away by the exercise of amendatory process in Article 368 , any furth er inroad into these rights as they exist today will be illegal and unConstitutional unless it complies with Part III in general and Article 13(2) in particular;  (v) that for abridging or taking away Fundamental Rights, a Constituent body will have to be convoked; and  (vi) that the two impugned Acts, namely, the Punjab Security of Land Tenures Act, 1,953 (X of 1953) and the Mysore Land Reforms Act, 1961 (X of 1962) as amended by Act XIV of 1965 are valid under the Constitution not because they are included in Schedule 9 of the Constitution but because they are protected by Article 31-A , and the President's assent.
Precedent	47.
Precedent	I am not giving his reasons for these conclusions here because they will be examined when dealing with the arguments addressed to us on various points.
Precedent	48.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. as he then was, also speaking on behalf of 2 other Judges held that <NAME> [1952] S.C.R.
Precedent	89 case was correctly decided and the majority in <NAME> [1965] 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	933 case was correct in following that decisi on.
Precedent	49.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. held:  (1) Article 368 not only prescribes the procedure but also gives the power of amendment;  (2) Article 368 gives the power of amending each and every provision of the Constitution and as Article 13(2) is a part of the Constitution it is within the reach of the amending power;  (3) Article 368 is not controlled by Article 13(2) and the prohibitory injunction in Article 13(2) is not attracted against the amending power; (4) Constitutional amendment under Article 368 is not a law within the meaning of Article 13(2);   (5) The scale of value embodied in Parts III and IV is not immortal.
Precedent	Parts III and IV being parts of the Constitution are not immune from amendment under Article 368.
Precedent	Constition-makers could not have intended that the rights conferred by Part III could not be altered by giving effect to the policies of Part IV.
Precedent	(6) The Preamble cannot control the unambiguous language of the articles of the Constitution.
Precedent	50.
Precedent	Regarding the amendment of the basic features of the Constitut ion, he observed: Counsel said that they could not give an exhaustive catalogue of the basic features, but sovereignty, the republican form of government, the federal structure and the fundamental rights were some of the features.
Precedent	The Seventeenth Amendment has not derogated from the sovereignty, the republican form of government and the federal structure, and the question whether they can be touched by amendment does not arise for decision.
Precedent	For the purposes of these cases, it is sufficient to say that the fundamental rights are within the reach of the amending power.
Precedent	51.
Precedent	<NAME>, J., held:  (1) The amending power under Article 368 is sui generis;  (2) "Law" in Article 13(2) cannot be construed so as to include "Law" made by <ORG> under Articles 4, 169, 392, 5th Schedule Part D and 6th Schedule Para 21.
Precedent	(3) The expression "fundamental rights" does not lift the fundamental rights above the Constitution itself;  (4) Both the power to amend and the procedure to amend are enacted in Article 368.
Precedent	(5) The re were no implied limitations on the amending power and all articles of the Constitution were amendable either under the proviso of Article 368 or under the main part of the article.
Precedent	(6) The Federal structure is not an essential part of our Constitution.
Precedent	(7) The power of amendment is in point of quality an adjunct of sovereignty.
Precedent	If so, it does not admit of any limitations.
Precedent	52.
Precedent	In brief 6 Judges held that in view of Article 13(2) Fundamental Rights could not be abridged or taken away.
Precedent	Five Judges held that Article 13(2) was inapplicable to Acts amending the Constitution.
Precedent	PART III-Interpretation of Article 368   53.
Precedent	Let me now proceed to interpret Article 368.
Precedent	Article 368 , as originally enacted, read as follows:  An amendment of this Constitution may be initiated only by the introduction of a Bill for the purpose in either <ORG> of Parliament, and when the Bill is passed in each <ORG> by a majority of the total membership of that <ORG> and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members of that  <ORG> present and voting, it shall be presented to the President for his assent and upon such assent being given to the Bill, the Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill: Provided that if such amendment seeks to make any change in-  (a) Article 54 , Article 55 , Article 73 , Article 162 or Article 241 , or  (b) Chapter IV of Part V, Chapter V of Part VI, or Chapter I of Part XI, or  (c) any of the Lists in the Seventh Schedule, or  (d) the representation of States in <ORG>, or  (e) the provisions of this article, the amendment shall also require to be ratified by the <ORG> of not less than one-half of the States specified in Parts A and B of the First Schedule by resolutions to that effect passed by those <ORG> before the Bill making provision for such amendment is presented to the President for assent.
Precedent	54.
Precedent	It will be noticed that Article 368 is contained in a separate part and the heading is "Amendment of the Constitution", but the marginal note reads "Pr ocedure for amendment of the Constitution".
Precedent	55.
RatioOfTheDecision	The expression "amendment of the Constitution" is not defined or expanded in any manner, although in other parts of the Constitution, the word "Amend" or "Amendment" has, as will be pointed out later, been expanded.
RatioOfTheDecision	In some parts they have clearly a narrow meaning..
RatioOfTheDecision	The proviso throws some light on the problem.
RatioOfTheDecision	First, it uses the expression "if such amendment seeks to make any change in"; it does not add the words "change of ", or omit "in", and say "seeks to change" instead of the expression "seeks to make any change in".
RatioOfTheDecision	56.
RatioOfTheDecision	The articles which are included in the proviso may be now considered.
RatioOfTheDecision	Part V, Chapter I, deals with "the Executive".
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 52 , provides that there shall be a President of India, and Article 53 vests the executive power of the <ORG> in the President and provides how it shall be exercised.
RatioOfTheDecision	These two articles are not mentioned in the proviso to Article 368 but Articles 54 and 55 are mentioned.
RatioOfTheDecision	57.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 54 provides:  54.
RatioOfTheDecision	The President  shall be elected by the members of an electoral college consisting of-  (a) the elected members of both Houses of Parliament; and  (b) the elected members of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	58.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 55 prescribes the manner of election of the President.
RatioOfTheDecision	59.
RatioOfTheDecision	Why were Articles 52 and 53 not mentioned in the proviso to Article 368 if the intention was that the States would have a say as to the federal structure of the country?
RatioOfTheDecision	One of the inferences that can be drawn is that the Constitution-makers never contemplated, or imagined that Article 52 will be altered and there shall not be a President of India.
RatioOfTheDecision	In other words they did not contemplate a monarchy being set up in India or there being no President.
RatioOfTheDecision	60.
RatioOfTheDecision	Another article which has been included in the proviso to Article 368 is Article 73 which deals with the extent of executive powers of the Union.
RatioOfTheDecision	As far as the Vice- President is concerned, the States have been given no say whether there shall be a Vice- President or not; about the method of  his election, etc.
RatioOfTheDecision	But what is remarkable is that when we come to Part VI of the Constitution, which deals with the "States", the only provision which is mentioned in the proviso to Article 368 is Article 162 which deals with the extent of executive power of States.
RatioOfTheDecision	The appointment of a Governor, conditions of service of a Governor, and the Constitution and functions of <ORG>, and other provisions regarding the Ministers and the conduct of government business are not mentioned at all in the proviso to Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	Another article which is mentioned in Clause (a) of the proviso to Article 368 is Article 241 which originally dealt with High Courts for States in Part C of the First Schedule.
RatioOfTheDecision	61.
RatioOfTheDecision	Chapter IV of Part V of the Constitution which deals with <ORG>, and Chapter V of Part VI which deals with the High Courts in the State are included in the proviso to Article 368 but it is extra-ordinary that Chapter VI of Part VI which deals with subordinate Judiciary is not mentioned  in Clause (b).
RatioOfTheDecision	Chapter I of Part XI is included and this deals with the Legislative Relations between the Union and the States, but Chapter II of Part XI which deals with Administrative Relations between the Union and the States, and various other matters in which the States would be interested are not included.
RatioOfTheDecision	Provisions relating to services under the State and Trade and Commerce are also not included in the proviso.
RatioOfTheDecision	62.
RatioOfTheDecision	This analysis of the provisions contained in Clauses (a) and (b) of the proviso to Article 368 shows that the reason for including certain articles and excluding certain other from the proviso was not that all articles dealing with the federal structure or the status of the States had been selected for inclusion in the proviso.
RatioOfTheDecision	63.
RatioOfTheDecision	Clause (c) of the proviso mentions the Lists in the Seventh Schedule, Clause (d) mentions the representation of States in <ORG>, and Clause (e) the provisions of Article 368 itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	The provisions of Sub-clauses (c), (d) and (e) can rightly be said to invo lve the federal structure and the rights of the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	64.
RatioOfTheDecision	What again is remarkable is that the fundamental rights are not included in the proviso at all.
RatioOfTheDecision	Were not the States interested in the fundamental rights of their people ?
RatioOfTheDecision	The omission may perhaps be understandable because of the express provision of Article 13(2) which provided that States shall not make any law which takes away or abridges the rights conferred by Part III and any law made in contravention of this clause shall to the extent of the contravention be void, assuming for the present that Article 13(2) operates on Constitutional amendments.
RatioOfTheDecision	65.
RatioOfTheDecision	In construing the expression "amendment of this Constitution I must look at the whole scheme of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is not right to construe words in vacuum and then insert the meaning into an article.
Precedent	Lord <NAME> observed in <NAME> v. <ORG> [1948] 2 All E.R.
Precedent	995, 998: The first thing one has to do, I venture to think, in construing words in a sectio n of an Act of Parliament is not to take those words in vacuo, so to speak, and attribute to them what is sometimes called their natural or ordinary meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	Few words in the English language have a natural or ordinary meaning in the sense that they must be so read that their meaning is entirely independent of their context.
RatioOfTheDecision	The method of construing statutes that I prefer is not to take particular words and attribute to them a sort of prima facie meaning which you may have to displace or modify.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is to read the statute as a whole and ask oneself the question: "In this state, in this context, relating to this subject-matter, what is the true meaning of that word ?
RatioOfTheDecision	66.
RatioOfTheDecision	I respectfully adopt the reasoning of Lord <NAME> in construing the expression "the amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	67.
RatioOfTheDecision	Lord <NAME> is not alone in this approach.
RatioOfTheDecision	In <NAME> v. <ORG> [1967] 2 All E.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	576, 578 it is observed:  English words derive colour from those which surround them.
RatioOfTheDecision	Sentences are not mere collections of words t o be taken out of the sentence defined separately by reference to the "dictionary or decided cases, and then put back again into the sentence with the meaning which you have assigned to them as separate words, so as to give the sentence or phrase a meaning which as a sentence or phrase it cannot bear without distortion of the English language.
Precedent	68.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. in <NAME> v. <NAME> 245 U.S. 418; 425 : 62 L. ed.
Precedent	372; 376 had the same thought.
Precedent	He observed:  A word is not crystal, transparent and unchanged; it is the skin of living thought and may vary greatly in colour and content according to the circumstances and the time in which it is used.
Precedent	69.
Precedent	What <NAME> J. said is particularly true of the word "Amendment" or "Amend".
Precedent	70.
Precedent	I may also refer to the observation of <NAME> and Lord <NAME>: A grant of the power in general terms, standing by itself, would no doubt be construed in the wider sense; but it may be qualified by other express provisions in the same enactment, by the implications of the context, and e ven by the considerations arising out of what appears to be the general scheme of the Act".
Precedent	(Per Gwyer C.J.-The Central Provinces and Berar Act , 1939 F.C.R.
Precedent	18 at 42.)
RatioOfTheDecision	The question, then, is one of construction and in the ultimate resort must be determined upon tht actual words used, read not in vacuo but as occurring in a single complex instrument, in which one part may throw light on another.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution has been described as the federal compact, and the construction must hold a balance between all its parts".
Precedent	(Per Lord <NAME>-<NAME> v. Commonwealth of Australia 1936 A.C. 578 at  613).
Precedent	71.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the Constitution the word "amendment" or "amend" has been used in various places to mean different things.
RatioOfTheDecision	In some articles, the word "amendment" in the context has a wide meaning and in another context it has a narrow meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	In Article 107 , which deals with legislative procedure, Clause (2) provides that "subject to the provisions of Articles 108 and 109, a Bill shall not be deemed to have been passed by the  <ORG> unless it has been agreed to by both <ORG>, either without amendment or with such amendments only as are agreed to by both <ORG>."
RatioOfTheDecision	It is quite clear that the word "amendment" in this article has a narrow meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	Similarly, in Article 111 of the Constitution, whereby the President is enabled to send a message requesting the <ORG> to consider the desirability of introducing amendments, the "amendments" has a narrow meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	72.
Statute	The opening of Article 4(1) reads:  4(1) Any law referred to in Article 2 or Article 3 shall contain such provisions for the amendment of the First Schedule and the Fourth Schedule as may be necessary to give effect to the provisions of the law....
RatioOfTheDecision	Here the word "amendment" has a narrower meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	"Law" under Articles 3 and 4 must "conform to the democratic pattern envisaged by the Constitution; and the power which the <ORG> may exercise...is not the power to over-ride the Constitutional scheme.
RatioOfTheDecision	No state can, therefore, be formed, admitted or set up by law u nder Article 4 by the <ORG> which has no effective legislative, executive and judicial organs".
Precedent	(Per Shah J.-Mangal Singh v. <ORG>  2 S.C.R.
Precedent	109 at 112.
RatioOfTheDecision	(Emphasis supplied)  73.
Statute	Article 169(2) reads:  Any law referred to in Clause (1) shall contain such provisions for the amendment of this Constitution as may be necessary to give effect to the provisions of the law and may also contain such supplemental, incidental and consequential provisions as <ORG> may deem necessary.
Statute	Here also the word "amendment" has a narrow meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	74.
Statute	Para 7 of Part D, Fifth Schedule, which deals with amendment of the schedule, reads:  7.
Statute	Amendment of the Schedule.-(1) <ORG> may from time to time by law amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any of the provisions of this Schedule and, when the Schedule is so amended, any reference to this Schedule in this Constitution shall be construed as a reference to such schedule as so amended.
RatioOfTheDecision	Here the word "amend" has been expanded by using the expression "by  way of addition, variation or repeal", but even here, it seems to me, the amendments will have to be in line with the whole Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Similarly, under para 21 of the Sixth Schedule,.
RatioOfTheDecision	which repeats the phraseology of para 7 of the Fifth Schedule, it seems to me, the amendments will have to be in line with the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	75.
RatioOfTheDecision	I may mention that in the case of the amendments which may be made in exercise of the powers under Article 4 , Article 169 , para 7 of the Fifth Schedule, and para 21 of the Sixth Schedule, it has been expressly stated in these provisions that they shall not be deemed to be amendments of the Constitution for the purposes of Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	76.
Statute	It is also important to note that <ORG> which adopted Article 368 on September 17, 1949, had earlier on August 18, 1949, substituted the following section in place of the old Section 291 in the Government of India Act, 1935:  291.
Statute	Power of the Governor-General to amend certain provisions of the Act and orders made thereunder -  (1) The Governor-General may at any time by order make such amendments as he considers necessary whether by way of addition, modification or repeal, in the provisions of this Act or of any order made thereunder in relation to <ORG> with respect to any of the following matters, that is to say-  (a) the composition of the <ORG> or <ORG>s of the <ORG>;  (b) the delimitation of territorial constituencies for the purpose of elections under this Act.
Statute	* * * * * Here, the word "amendment" has been expanded.
Statute	It may be that there really is no expansion because every amendment may involve addition, variation or repeal of part of a provision.
Precedent	77.
Precedent	According to Mr. <NAME>, the power of amendment given by Article 4 , read with Articles 2 and 3, Article 169 , Fifth Schedule and Sixth Schedule, is a limited power limited to certain provisions of the Constitution, while the power under Article 368 is not limited.
Precedent	It is true every provision is prima facie amendable under Article 368 but this  does not solve the problem before us.
Precedent	78.
Precedent	I may mention that an attempt was made to expand the word "amend" in Article 368 by proposing an amendment that "by way of variation, addition, or repeal" be added but the amendment was rejected.
Precedent	79.
Statute	Again, in Article 196(2 ), the word "amendment" has been used in a limited sense.
Statute	Article 196(2) reads:  196(2).
Statute	Subject to the provisions of Articles 197 and 198, a Bill shall not be deemed to have been passed by the <ORG> of the Legislature of a State having <ORG> unless it has been agreed to by both <ORG>, either without amendment or with such amendments only as are agreed to by both <ORG>.
Statute	80.
Statute	Similar meaning may be given to the word "amendment" in Article 197(2 ), which reads:  197(2).
Statute	If after a Bill has been so passed for the second time by <ORG> and transmitted to <ORG>  (a) the Bill is rejected by the <ORG>; or  (b) more than one month elapses from the date on which the Bill is laid before the <ORG>  without the Bill being passed by it; or  (c) the Bill is passed by the <ORG> with amendments to which <ORG> does not agree,  (c) the Bill is passed by <ORG> does not agree, the Bill shall be deemed to have been passed by <ORG> in the form in which it was passed by <ORG> for the second time with such amendments, if any, as have been made or suggested by the Legislative <ORG> and agreed to by <ORG>.
Statute	81.
Statute	Under Article 200 the Governor is enabled to suggest the desirability of introducing any such amendments as he may recommend in his message.
RatioOfTheDecision	Here again "amendment" has clearly a limited meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	82.
Statute	In Article 35(b) the words used are:  Any law in force immediately before the commencement of this Constitution...subject to the terms thereof and to any adaptations and modifications that may be made therein under Article 372 , continue in force until altered or repealed or amended by <ORG>.
Statute	83.
Statute	Here, all the three words are used giving a comprehensive meaning.
Statute	Reliance is not placed by the draftsman only on the word "amend".
Statute	84.
Statute	Similar language is used in Article 372 whereby existing laws continue to be in force until "altered or repealed or amended' by a competent <ORG> or other competent authority.
Statute	85.
Statute	In the original Article 243(2 ), in conferring power on the President to make regulations for the peace and good government of the territories in part D of the First Schedule, it is stated that "any regulation so made may repeal or amend any law made by <ORG>."
Statute	Here, the two words together give the widest power to make regulations inconsistent with any law made by <ORG>  86.
Statute	In Article 252 again, the two words are joined together to give a wider power.
Statute	Clause (2) of Article 252 reads:  252(2).
Statute	Any Act so passed by <ORG> may be amended or repealed by an Act of <ORG> passed or adopted in like manner but shall not, as respects any State to which it applies, be amended o r repealed by an Act of the Legislature of that State.
Statute	87.
Statute	In the proviso to Article 254 , which deals with the inconsistency between laws made by <ORG> and laws made by the Legislatures of States, it is stated: Provided that nothing in this clause shall prevent <ORG> from enacting at any time any law with respect to the same matter including a law adding to, amending, varying or repealing the law so made by <ORG> State;  88.
Statute	In Article 320(5 ), "all regulations made under the proviso to Clause (3)" can be modified "whether by way of repeal or amendment" as both <ORG>s of <ORG> or the <ORG> or both <ORG>s of <ORG> States may make during the session in which they are so laid.
Precedent	89.
Precedent	I have referred to the variation in the language of the various articles dealing with the' question of amendment or repeal in detail because our Constitution was drafted very carefully and I must presume that every word was chosen carefully and should have its proper meaning.
Precedent	I may re ly for this principle on the following observations of <ORG> in <NAME> v. <NAME> (10) L. ed.
Precedent	579; 594 and quoted with approval in <NAME> v. United States (77) L. ed.
RatioOfTheDecision	1372; 1380:  In expounding the Constitution of the United States, every word must have its due force, and appropriate meaning: for it is evident from the whole instrument, that no word was unnecessarily used, or needlessly added....  90.
RatioOfTheDecision	Reference was made to Section 6(2) of the Indian Independence Act, 1947, in which the last three lines read:  ...and the powers of the <ORG> of each Dominion include the power to repeal or amend any such Act, order, rule or regulation in so far as it is part of the law of the Dominion.
RatioOfTheDecision	Here, the comprehensive expression "repeal or amend" gives power to have a completely new Act different from an existing act of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	91.
RatioOfTheDecision	So, there is no doubt from a perusal of these provisions that different words have been used to meet different demands.
RatioOfTheDecision	In view of the great variation o f the phrases used all through the Constitution it follows that the word "amendment" must derive its colour from Article 368 and the rest of the provisions of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no doubt that it is not intended that the whole Constitution could be repealed.
RatioOfTheDecision	This much is conceded by the learned Counsel for the respondents.
RatioOfTheDecision	92.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, in order to appreciate the real content of the expression "amendment of this Constitution", in Article 368 I must look at the whole structure of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution opens with a preamble which reads:  WE THE PEOPLE OF INDIA, having solemnly resolved to constitute India into a SOVEREIGN DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC and to secure to all its citizens:  JUSTICE, social, economic and political;  LIBERTY of thought, expression, belief, faith and worship;  EQUALITY of status and of opportunity; and to promote among them all; FRATERNITY assuring the dignity of the individual and the unity of the Nation;  IN OUR CONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY this Twenty-sixth day of November, 194 9, do HEREBY ADOPT, ENACT AND GIVE TO OURSELVES THIS CONSTITUTION.
RatioOfTheDecision	93.
RatioOfTheDecision	This Preamble, and indeed the Constitution, was drafted in the light and direction of the Objective Resolutions adopted on January 22, 1947, which runs as follows: (1) THIS CONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY declares its firm and solemn resolve to proclaim India as an Independent Sovereign Republic and to draw up for her future governance a Consti-  (2) wherein the territories that now comprise British India, the territories that now form the Indian States, and such other parts of India as are outside British India and the States, as well as such other territories as are willing to be constituted into the Independent Sovereign India, shall be a Union of them all; and (3) wherein the said territories, whether with their present boundaries or with such others as may be determined by <ORG> and thereafter according to the law of the Constitution, shall possess and retain the status of autonomous units, together with residuary powers, and  exercise all powers and functions of government and administration, save and except such powers and functions as are vested in of assigned to the <ORG>, or as are inherent or implied in the <ORG> or resulting therefrom; and (4) wherein all power and authority of <ORG>, its constituent parts and organs of government, are derived from the people; and (5) wherein shall be guaranteed and secured to all people of India justice, social, economic and political; equality of status, of opportunity, and before the law; freedom of thought, expression, belief, faith, worship, vocation, association and action, subject to law and public morality; and (6) wherein adequate safeguards shall be provided for minorities backward and tribal areas, and depressed and other backward classes; and (7) whereby shall be maintained the integrity of the territory of the Republic and its sovereign rights on land, sea, and air according to justice and the law of civilized nations, and (8) this ancient land attains  its rightful and honoured place in the world and makes its full and willing contribution to the promotion of world peace and the welfare of mankind.
RatioOfTheDecision	94.
RatioOfTheDecision	While moving the resolution for acceptance of the Objectives Resolution, Pandit <NAME> said:  It seeks very feebly to tell the world of what we have thought or dreamt for so long, and what we now hope to achieve in the near future.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is in that spirit that I venture to place this Resolution before the <ORG> and it is in that spirit that I trust the <ORG> will receive it and ultimately pass it.
RatioOfTheDecision	And may I, Sir, also with all respect, suggest to you and to the <ORG> that, when the time comes for the passing of this Resolution let it be not done in the formal way by the raising of hands, but much more solemnly, by all of us standing up and thus taking this pledge anew.
RatioOfTheDecision	95.
RatioOfTheDecision	I may here trace the history of the shaping of the Preamble because this would show that the Preamble was in conformity with the Constitution as it was finally accepted.
RatioOfTheDecision	Not only  was the Constitution framed in the light of the Preamble but the Preamble was ultimately settled in the light of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	This appears from the following brief survey of the history of the framing of the Preamble extracted from the Framing of India's Constitution (A study) by <NAME>.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the earliest draft the Preamble was something formal and read : "We, the people of India, seeking to promote the common good, do hereby, through our chosen representatives, enact, adopt and give to ourselves this Constitution, (<NAME> Framing of India's Constitution-A study-p.
RatioOfTheDecision	127.).
RatioOfTheDecision	96.
RatioOfTheDecision	After the plan of June 3, 1947, which led to the decision to partition the country and to set up two independent Dominions of India and Pakistan, on June 8, 1947, a joint sub- committee of the <ORG> and <ORG>, took note that the objective resolution would require amendment in view of the latest announcement of <ORG> The' announcement of June 3 had made it clear tha t full independence, in the form of Dominion Status, would be conferred on India as from August 15, 1947.
RatioOfTheDecision	After examining the implications of partition the sub-committee thought that the question of making changes in the Objectives Resolution could appropriately be considered only when effect had actually been given to the June 3 Plan.
RatioOfTheDecision	(Special Sub-<ORG> minutes June 9, 1947.
RatioOfTheDecision	Later on July 12, 1947, the special sub-committee again postponed consideration of the matter.
RatioOfTheDecision	Select Documents II, 20(ii), p. 617.
RatioOfTheDecision	(<NAME>Framing of India's Constitution-A study-(p. 127 footnote).
RatioOfTheDecision	The Union Constitution <ORG> provisionally accepted the Preamble as drafted by <NAME> and reproduced it in its report of July 4, 1947 without any change, with the tacit recognition at that stage that the Preamble would be finally based on the Objectives Resolution.
Argument	In a statement circulated to members of the <ORG> on July 18, 1947 Pandit <NAME> inter alia, observed that the Preamble was covered more or less by the O bjectives Resolution which it was intended to incorporate in the final Constitution subject to some modification on account of the political changes resulting from partition.
Argument	Three days later, moving the report of <ORG> for the consideration of the <ORG>, he suggested that it was not necessary at that stage to consider the draft of the Preamble since the <ORG> stood by the basic principles laid down in the Objectives Resolution and these could be incorporated in the Preamble in the light of the changed situation(Shiva Rao's-Framing of India's Constitution-A study- pp.
Argument	127-128 (also see footnote 1 p. 128).
Argument	The suggestion was accepted by the <ORG> and further consideration of the Preamble was held over.
Argument	97.
Argument	We need not consider the intermediate drafts, but in the meantime the declaration (See <ORG>t <ORG> Debates, Vol.
Precedent	8, page 2) was adopted at the end of April, 1949 by the Government of the various <ORG> countries and the resolution was ratified by <ORG> t <ORG> on May 17; 1949 after two days' debate.
Precedent	98.
Precedent	In the meantime the process of merger and integration of Indian States had been completed and <NAME> was able to tell the Constituent <ORG> on October 12, 1949, that the new Constitution was "not an alliance between democracies and dynasties, but a real union of the Indian people, built on the basic concept of the sovereignty of the people(<NAME>'s-Framing of India's Constitution-A study-pp.
Precedent	130-  132).
Precedent	99.
Precedent	The draft Preamble was considered by the <ORG> on October 17, 1949.
Precedent	<NAME> observes that "the object of putting the Preamble last, the President of the <ORG> explained, was to see that it was in conformity with the Constitution as accepted.
Precedent	"Once the transfer of power had taken place the question of <ORG> subsequent approval which was visualised in <ORG> original plan of May 1946 could no longer arise.
Precedent	The sovereign character of the Constituent <ORG> thus became automatic w ith the rapid march of events without any controversy, and the words in the Preamble "give to ourselves this Constitution" became appropriate.
Precedent	The Preamble was adopted by the <ORG> without any alteration.
Precedent	Subsequently the words and figure "this twenty-sixth day of November 1949" were introduced in the last paragraph to indicate the date on which the Constitution was finally adopted by the Constituent <ORG>.
Precedent	100.
Precedent	Regarding the use which can be made of the preamble in interpreting an ordinary statute, there is no doubt that it cannot be used to modify the language if the language of the enactment is plain and clear.
Precedent	If the language is not plain and clear, then the preamble may have effect either to extend or restrict the language used in the body of an enactment.
Precedent	"If the language of the enactment is capable of more than one meaning then that one is to be preferred which comes nearest to the purpose and scope of the preamble."
Precedent	(see <CITATION_1677367> )  2 S.C.R.
Precedent	732-  737.
Precedent	101.
Precedent	We are, however, not conce rned with the interpretation of an ordinary statute.
Precedent	As Sir <NAME>, a most eminent lawyer said, "so far as the Preamble is concerned, though in an ordinary statute we do not attach any importance to the Preamble, all importance has' to be attached to the Preamble in a Constitutional statute".
Precedent	(Constituent Assembly Debates Vol.
Precedent	10, p. 417).
Precedent	Our Preamble outlines the objectives of the whole Constitution.
Precedent	It expresses "what we had thought or dreamt for so long.
Precedent	102.
Precedent	In re.
Precedent	Berubari Union and Exchange of Enclaves [1960] 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	250, 281-82 this was said about the Preamble:  There is no doubt that the declaration made by the people of India in exercise of their sovereign will in the preamble to the Constitution is, in the words of <NAME>, "a key to open the mind of the makers" which may show the general purposes for which they made the several provisions in the Constitution; but nevertheless the preamble is not a part of the Constitution, and, as <NAME> has observed about the" preamble to the Ame rican Constitution, "it has never been regarded as the source of any substantive power conferred on <ORG> or any of its departments.
Precedent	Such powers embrace only those expressly granted in the body of the Constitution and such as may be implied from those so granted".
Precedent	What is true about the power is equally true about the prohibitions and limitations.
Precedent	103.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. in <CITATION_120358>  2 S.C.R.
Precedent	762; 838 and 914 relied on <NAME>'s case and said:  on a parity of reasoning we are of opinion that the preamble cannot prohibit or control in any way or impose any implied prohibitions or limitations on the power to amend the Constitution contained in Article  368.
Precedent	104.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. in this case observed:  Moreover the preamble cannot control the unambiguous language of the articles of the Constitution, see <NAME>, Legislative Executive and Judicial powers in Australia, third edition pp.
Precedent	694-5; in Re.
Precedent	<NAME> Union & Exchange of Enclaves.
Precedent	[1960] 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	250, 281-82.
Precedent	105.
Precedent	With respe ct, the <ORG> was wrong in holding, as has been shown above, that the Preamble is not a part of the Constitution unless the court was thinking of the distinction between the Constitution Statute and the Constitution, mentioned by Mr. <NAME>.
Precedent	It was expressly voted to be a part of the Constitution.
Precedent	Further, with respect, no authority has been referred before us to establish the proposition that "what is true about the powers is equally true about the prohibitions and limitations."
Precedent	As I will show later, even from the preamble limitations have been derived in some cases.
Precedent	106.
Precedent	It is urged in the written submission of Mr. <NAME> that there is a distinction between the Indian Constitution Statute and the Constitution of India.
Precedent	He urges as follows:  This Constitution is the Constitution which follows the Preamble.
Precedent	It starts with Article 1 and ended originally with the Eighth Schedule and now ends with the Ninth Schedule after the First Amendment Act, 1951.
Precedent	The way the Preamble is drafted leaves no doubt tha t what follows, or is annexed to, the Preamble, is the Constitution of India.
Precedent	107.
Argument	He has also urged that the Preamble came into force on November 26, 1949 alongwith Articles 5, 6, 7 etc.
Argument	as provided in Article 394 because Articles 5, 6, 7 and the other Articles mentioned therein could hardly come into force without the enacting clause mentioned in the Preamble having come into force.
Argument	He says that the Preamble is a part of the Constitution statute and not a part of the Constitution but precedes it.
Precedent	There is something to be said for his contention but, in my view, it is not necessary to base my decision on this distinction as it is not necessary to decide in the present case whether Article 368 enables <ORG> to amend the Preamble.
Precedent	<ORG> has not as yet chosen to amend the Preamble.
Precedent	108.
Precedent	The Preamble was used by this <ORG> as an aid to construction in <CITATION_1683423>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	613 at p. 653.
Precedent	After referring to Part III, <NAME>, C.J., observed:  We think that the rights described as fundamental ri ghts are a necessary consequence of the declaration in the preamble that the people of India have solemnly resolved to constitute India into a sovereign democratic republic and to secure to all its citizens justice, social, economic and political; liberty of thought, expression, belief, faith and worship; equality of status and of opportunity.
Precedent	These fundamental rights have not been put in the Constitution merely for individual benefits, though ultimately they come into operation in considering individual rights.
Precedent	They have been put there as a matter of public policy and the doctrine of waiver can have no application to provisions of law which have been enacted as a matter of Constitutional policy.
Precedent	109.
Precedent	Similarly in In re.
Precedent	The Kerala Education Bill [1959] S.C.R.
Precedent	995, 1018-1019 1957, <NAME> while considering the validity of the Kerala Education Bill 1957 observed: In order to appreciate the true meaning, import and implications of the provisions of the Bill which are said to have given rise to doubts, it will  be necessary to refer first to certain provisions of the Constitution which may have a bearing upon the questions under consideration and then to the actual provision of the Bill.
RatioOfTheDecision	The inspiring and nobly expressed preamble to our Constitution records the solemn resolve of the people of India to constitute.... (He then sets out the Preamble).
RatioOfTheDecision	Nothing provokes and stimulates thought and expression in people more than education.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is education that clarifies our belief and faith and helps to strengthen our spirit of worship.
RatioOfTheDecision	To implement and fortify these supreme purposes set forth in the preamble, Part III of our Constitution has provided for us certain fundamental rights.
Precedent	110.
Precedent	<CITATION_1308308>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	933; 968 <NAME>, J. after assuming that the Preamble is not a part of the Constitution, observed: While considering this question it would be of relevance to bear in mind that the preamble is not of the common run such as is to be found in an Act of a legislature.
Precedent	It has the stamp of deep deliberation a nd is marked by precision.
Precedent	Would this not suggest that the framers of the Constitution attached special significance to it?
Precedent	111.
Precedent	<NAME> and <NAME> in their "Annotated Constitution of the Australian Commonwealth (1901 p. 283) "adopted the following sentence from Lord <NAME>'s "Practical Legislation, p. 36":  A preamble may be used for other reasons to limit the scope of certain expressions or to explain facts or introduce definitions.
Precedent	112.
Precedent	<NAME> on "Legislative Drafting"-p. 137-opines that "construction of the preamble may have effect either to extend or to restrict general language used in the body of an enactment.
Precedent	113.
Precedent	In Attorney-General v. Prince <NAME> of Hanover [1957] A.C. 436, 460 <ORG> considered the effect of the preamble on the interpretation of Princes Sophia Naturalization Act; 1705.
Precedent	It was held that "as a matter of construction of the Act, there was nothing in the Act or its preamble, interpreted in the light of the earlier relevant statutes...capable of controlling and  limiting the plain and ordinary meaning of the material words of the enacting provisions and that the class of lineal descendants "born or hereafter to be born" meant the class of such descendants in all degrees without any limit as to time."
Precedent	<ORG> further held that "looking at the Act from the point of view of 1705 there was no such manifest absurdity in this construction as would entitle the court to reject it.
Precedent	114.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> referred to the passage from the speech of Lord <NAME>, at p. 467.
RatioOfTheDecision	The passage is lengthy but I may quote these sentences:  It is only when it conveys a clear and definite meaning in comparison with relatively obscure or indefinite enacting words that the preamble may legitimately prevail.
RatioOfTheDecision	If they admit of only one construction, that construction will receive effect even if it is inconsistent with the preamble, but if the enacting words are capable of either of the constructions offered by the parties, the construction which fits the preamble may be preferred.
RatioOfTheDecision	115.
RatioOfTheDecision	V iscount <NAME> put the matter at page 463, thus:  On the one hand, the proposition can be accepted that "it is a settled rule that the preamble cannot be made use of to control the enactments themselves where they are expressed in clear and unambiguous terms".
RatioOfTheDecision	I quote the words of <NAME>, which were cordially approved by Lord <NAME> in <NAME> v. <ORG> (1889) A.C. 143,  185.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the other hand it must often be difficult to say that any terms are clear and unambiguous until they have been studied in their context  116.
RatioOfTheDecision	This case shows that if on reading Article 368 in the context of the Constitution I find the word "Amendment" ambiguous I can refer to the Preamble to find which construction would fit in with the Preamble.
Precedent	117.
Precedent	In <ORG> v. The <ORG> 45 A.L.J.
Precedent	251 which is discussed in detail later, a number of Judges refer to the federal structure of the Constitution.
Precedent	It is in the preamble of the <ORG> of Australia Constitution Act, 1902 that 'one indisso luble Federal Commonwealth' is mentioned.
Precedent	118.
Precedent	There is a sharp conflict of opinion in Australia respecting the question whether an amendment can be made which would be inconsistent with the Preamble of the Constitution Act referring to the "indissoluble" character and the sections which refer to the "Federal" nature of the Constitution.
Precedent	After referring to this conflict, Wynes(Wynes Legislative, Executive and Judicial Powers in Australia, Fourth Edn.
Precedent	p. 506).
Precedent	observes: Apart from the rule which excludes the preamble generally from consideration in statutory interpretation, it is clear that, when all is said and done, the preamble at the most is, only a recital of the intention which the Act' seeks to effect; and it is a recital of a present (i.e., as in 1900) intention.
Precedent	But in any event the insertion of an express reference to amendment in the Constitution itself must surely operate as a qualification upon the mere recital of the reasons for its creation.
Precedent	119.
Precedent	I am not called upon to say which view is corre ct but it does show that in Australia, there is a sharp conflict of opinion as to whether the Preamble can control the amending power.
Precedent	120.
Precedent	Story in his Commentaries on the Constitution of the United States states : [(1883) Vol.
RatioOfTheDecision	1] It (Preamble) is properly resorted to, where doubts or ambiguities arise upon the words of the enacting part; for if they are dear and unambiguous, there seems little room for interpretation, except in cases leading to an obvious absurdity, or to a direct overthrow of the intention express in the preamble.
RatioOfTheDecision	There does not seem any reason why, in a fundamental law or Constitution of government, an equal attention should not be given to the intention of the framers, as stated in the preamble.
RatioOfTheDecision	And accordingly we find, that it has been constantly referred to by statesmen and jurists to aid them in the exposition of its provisions.
Precedent	121.
Precedent	Story further states at page 447-448:  And the uniform doctrine of the highest judicial authority has accordingly been, that it was the act of the peo ple, and not of the states; and that it bound the latter, as subordinate to the people.
Precedent	"Let us turn," said Mr. Chief Justice <NAME>, "to the Constitution.
Precedent	The people therein declare, that their design in establishing it comprehended six objects: (1) To form a more perfect union; (2) to establish justice; (3) to insure domestic tranquillity; (4) to provide for the common defence; (5) to promote the general welfare; (6) to secure the blessings of liberty to themselves and their posterity.
Precedent	It would," he added, "be pleasing and useful to consider and trace the relations, which each of these objects bears to the others; and to show, that, collectively, they comprise every thing requisite, with the blessing of Divine Providence, to render a people prosperous and happy."
Precedent	In <NAME> v. <NAME> (1 Wheat.
Precedent	R. 305, 324), <ORG> say, (as we have seen) "the Constitution of the United States was ordained and established, not by the states in their sovereign capacities, but emphatically, as the preamble of the Constit ution declares, by the people of the "United States;" and language still more expressive will be found used on other solemn occasions.
Precedent	122.
Precedent	"<ORG> (borrowing some of the language of the Preamble to the Federal Constitution) has appropriately stated that the people of the United States erected their Constitutions or forms of government to establish justice, to promote the general welfare, to secure the blessings of liberty, and to protect their persons and property from violence".
Precedent	(American Jurisprudence, 2d.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	16 p. 184).
Precedent	123.
Precedent	In the United States the Declaration of Independence is sometimes referred to in determining Constitutional questions.
Precedent	It is stated in American Jurisprudence (2d.
Precedent	16. p.  189):  While statements of principles contained in the Declaration of Independence do not have the force of organic law and therefore cannot be made the basis of judicial decision as to the limits of rights and duties, yet: it has been said that it is always safe to read the letter of  the Constitution in the spirit of the Declaration of Independence, and the courts sometimes refer to the Declaration in determining Constitutional questions.
Precedent	124.
Precedent	It seems to me that the Preamble of our Constitution is of extreme importance and the Constitution should be read and interpreted in the light of the grand and noble vision expressed in the Preamble.
Precedent	125.
Precedent	Now I may briefly describe the scheme of the Constitution.
Precedent	Part I of the Constitution deals with "the Union and its Territory".
Precedent	As originally enacted, Article 1 read as follows:  1.
Precedent	India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of <ORG>.
Precedent	2.
Precedent	The <ORG> and the territories thereof shall be the <ORG> and their territories specified in Parts A, B and C of the First Schedule.
Precedent	3.
Precedent	The territory of India shall comprise-  (a) the territories of the <ORG>;  (b) the territories specified in Part D of the First Schedule;  and  (c) such other territories as may be acquired.
Precedent	126.
Precedent	Article 2 enabled <ORG> to admit into the Union, or establish, new <ORG> on  such terras and conditions as it thinks fit.
Precedent	Article 3 and 4 dealt with the formation of new States and alteration of areas, boundaries or names of existing States.
Precedent	127.
Precedent	Part II dealt with "Citizenship".
Precedent	The heading of Part III is "Fundamental Rights".
Precedent	It first describes the expression "the State" to include "the <ORG> and <ORG> of India and the <ORG> and the <ORG> of each of the States and all local or other authorities within the territory of India or under the control of the <ORG> of India."
Precedent	( Article 12 ), Article 13 provides that laws inconsistent with or in derogation of the fundamental rights shall be void.
Precedent	This applies to existing laws as well as laws made after the coming into force of the Constitution.
Precedent	For the time being I assume that in Article 13(2) the word "law" includes Constitutional amendment.
Precedent	128.
Precedent	The fundamental rights conferred by the Constitution include right to equality before the law, ( Article 14 ), prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, rac e, caste, sex or place of birth, ( Article 15 ), equality of opportunity in matters of public employment, ( Article 16 ), right to freedom of speech and expression, to assemble peaceably and without arms, to form association or unions, to move freely throughout the territory of India, to reside and settle in any part of the territory of India, to acquire, hold and dispose of property; and to practice any profession or to carry on any occupation, trade or business.
Precedent	( Article 19 ).
Precedent	Reasonable restrictions can be imposed on the rights under Article 19 in respect of various matters.
Precedent	129.
Precedent	Article 20 protects a person from being convicted of any offence except for violation of a law in force at the time of the commission of the act charged as an offence or to be subjected to a penalty greater than that which might have been inflicted under the law in force at the time of the commission of the offence.
Precedent	It further provides that no person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once, and no pe <NAME> accused of any offence shall be compelled to be a witness against himself.
Precedent	130.
Precedent	Article 21 provides that no pe<NAME> shall be deprived of his life or pe<NAME>al liberty except according to procedure established by law.
Precedent	131.
Precedent	Article 22 gives further protection against arrest and detention in certain cases.
Precedent	Article 22(1) provides that "no pe<NAME> who is arrested shall be detained in custody without being informed, as soon as may be, of the grounds for such arrest nor shall he be denied the right to consult, and to be defended by, a legal practitioner of his choice."
Precedent	Article 22(2) provides that "every pe<NAME> who is arrested and detained in custody shall be produced before the nearest magistrate within a period of twenty-four hours of such arrest excluding the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the court of the magistrate and no such pe<NAME> shall be detained in custody beyond the said period without the authority of a magistrate".
Precedent	132.
Precedent	Article 22(4) deals with Preventive Detention.
Precedent	Arti cle 23 prohibits traffic in human beings and other similar forms of forced labour.
Precedent	Article 24 provides that "no child below the age of fourteen years shall be employed to work in any factory or mine or engaged in any other hazardous employment.
Precedent	133.
Precedent	Articles 25, 26, 27 and 28 deal with the freedom of religion.
Precedent	Article 25(1) provides that "subject to public order, morality and health and to the other provisions of this Part, all persons are equally entitled to freedom of conscience and the right freely to profess, practise and propagate religion."
Precedent	Article 26 enables every religious denomination or section thereof, subject to public order, morality and health, to establish and manage institutions for religious and, charitable purposes; to manage their own affairs in matters of religion, to own and acquire movable and immovable property, and to administer such property in accordance with law.
Precedent	Article 27 enables presons to resist payment of any taxes the proceeds of which are specifically appropriated in payment  of expenses for the promotion or maintenance of any particular religion or religious denomination.
Precedent	Article 28 deals with freedom as to attendance at religious instruction or religious worship in certain educational institutions.
Precedent	134.
