Precedent	Article 29(1) gives protection to minorities and provides that "any section of the citizens residing in the territory of India or any part thereof having a distinct language, script or culture of its own shall have the right to conserve the same."
Precedent	Article 29(2) provides that "no person shall be denied admission into any educational institution maintained by the <ORG> or receiving aid out of <ORG> funds on grounds only of religion, race, caste, language or any of them.
Precedent	135.
Precedent	Article 30 gives further rights to minorities whether based on religion or language to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice.
Precedent	Article 30(2) prohibits the <ORG> from discriminating against any educational institution, in granting aid to educational institutions, on the ground that  it is under the management of a minority, whether based on religion or language.
Precedent	136.
Precedent	As will be shown later the inclusion of special rights for minorities has great significance.
Precedent	They were clearly intended to be inalienable.
Precedent	137.
Precedent	The right to property comes last and is dealt with the Article 31.
Precedent	As originally enacted, it dealt with the right to property and prevented deprivation of property save by authority of law, and then provided for compulsory acquisition for public purposes on payment of compensation.
Precedent	It had three significant provisions, which show the intention of the Constitution-makers regarding property rights.
Precedent	The first is Article 31(4).
Precedent	This provision was intended to protect legislation dealing with agrarian reforms.
Precedent	The second provision, Article 31(5)(a ), was designed to protect existing legislation dealing with compulsory acquisition.
Precedent	Some acts, saved by this provision did not provide for payment of full compensation e.g.
Precedent	U.P.
Precedent	Town Improvement Act, 1919.
Precedent	The third provision Article 31(6) pro vided a protective umbrella to similar laws enacted not more than eighteen months before the commencement of the Constitution.
Precedent	138.
Precedent	The fundamental rights were considered of such importance that right was given to an aggrieved person to move the highest court of the land, i.e., <ORG>, by appropriate proceedings for the enforcement of the rights conferred by this part, and this right was guaranteed.
Precedent	Article 32(2) confers very wide powers on <ORG>, to issue directions or orders or writs including writs in the nature of habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto and certiorari, whichever may be appropriate, for the enforcement of any of the rights conferred by this Part.
Precedent	Article 32(4) further provides that "the right guaranteed by this article shall not be suspended except as otherwise provided for by this Constitution.
Precedent	139.
Precedent	Article 33 enables <ORG> by law to "determine to what extent any of the rights conferred by this Part shall, in their application to the members of the  Armed <ORG> or the <ORG> charged with the maintenance of public order, be restricted or abrogated so as to ensure the proper discharge of their duties and the maintenance of discipline among them.
Precedent	140.
Precedent	This articles shows the care with which, the circumstances in which, fundamental rights can be restricted or abrogated were contemplated and precisely described.
Precedent	141.
Precedent	Article 34 enables <ORG>, by law, to indemnify any person in the service of the <ORG>, or of a <ORG> or any other person in connection with acts done while martial law was in force in a particular area.
Precedent	142.
Precedent	Part IV of the Constitution contains directive principles of <ORG> policy.
Precedent	Article 37 specifically provides that "the provisions contained in this Part shall not be enforceable by any court, but the principles therein laid down are nevertheless fundamental in the governance of the country and it shall be the duty of the <ORG> to apply these principles in making laws."
Precedent	This clearly shows, and it has also been laid down by this <ORG>,  that these provisions are not justiciable and cannot be enforced by any <ORG>.
Precedent	The <ORG>s could not, for instance, issue a mandamus directing the <ORG> to provide adequate means of livelihood to every citizen, or that the ownership and control of the material resources of the community be so distributed as best to subserve the common good, or that there should be equal pay for equal work for both men and women.
Precedent	143.
Precedent	Some of the directive principles are of great fundamental importance in the governance of the country.
Precedent	But the question is not whether they are important; the question is whether they override the fundamental rights.
Precedent	In other words, ran <ORG> abrogate the fundamental rights in order to give effect to some of the directive principles ?
Precedent	144.
Precedent	I may now briefly notice the directive principles mentioned in Part IV.
Precedent	Article 38 provides that "the <ORG> shall strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting as effectively as it may a social order in which justice, social, eco nomic and political, shall inform all the institutions of the national life."
Precedent	Now, this directive is compatible with the fundamental rights because surely the object of many of the fundamental rights is to ensure that there shall be justice, social, economic and political, in the country.
Precedent	Article 39 , which gives particular directions to the <ORG>, reads thus:  39.
Precedent	The <ORG> shall, in particular, direct its policy towards securing-  (a) that the citizens, men and women equally, have the right to an adequate means of livelihood;  (b) that the ownership and control of the material resources of the community are so distributed as best to subserve the common good;  (c) that the operation of the economic system does not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to the common detriment;  (d) that there is equal pay for equal work for both men and women;  (e) that the health and strength of workers, men and women, and the tender age of children are not abused and that citizens are not forced by e conomic necessity to enter avocations unsuited to their age or strength;  (f) that childhood and youth are protected against exploitation and against moral and material abandonment.
Precedent	145.
Precedent	Article 40 deals with the organisation of village panchayats.
Precedent	Articles 41 deals with the right to work, to education and to public assistance in certain cases.
Precedent	Article 42 directs that the <ORG> shall make provisions for securing just and humane conditions of work and for maternity relief.
Precedent	Article 43 direct that "the <ORG> shall endeavour to secure, by suitable legislation or economic organisation or in any other way, to all workers, agricultural, industrial or otherwise, work, a living wage, conditions of work ensuring a decent standard of life and full enjoyment of leisure and social and cultural opportunities and, in particular, the <ORG> shall endeavour to promote cottage industries on an individual or cooperative basis in rural areas.
Precedent	146.
Precedent	Article 44 enjoins that the "<ORG> shall endeavour to secure for the citizens a u niform civil code throughout the territory of India."
Precedent	Desirable as it is, the Government has not been able to take any effective steps towards the realisation of this goal.
Precedent	Obviously no Court can compel the Government to lay down a uniform civil code even though it is essentially desirable in the interest of the integrity, and unity of the country.
Precedent	147.
Precedent	Article 45 directs that "the <ORG> shall endeavour to provide, within a period of ten years from the commencement of this Constitution, for free compulsory education for all children until they complete the age of fourteen years."
Precedent	This again is a very desirable directive.
Precedent	Although the Government has not been able to fulfil it completely, it cannot be compelled by any court of law to provide such education.
Precedent	148.
Precedent	Article 46 supplements the directive given above and enjoins the <ORG> to promote with special care the educational and economic interests of the weaker sections of the people, and in particular, of the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes, and t o protect them from social injustice and all forms of exploitation.
Precedent	149.
Precedent	Article 47 lays down as one of the duties of the <ORG> to raise the standard of living and to improve public health, and to bring about prohibition.
Precedent	Article 48 directs the <ORG> to endeavour to organise agriculture and animal husbandry on modern and scientific lines, and in particular, to take steps for preserving and improving the breeds, and prohibiting the slaughter of cows and calves and other milch and draught cattle.
Precedent	150.
Precedent	Article 49 deals with protection of monuments and places and objects of national importance.
Precedent	Article 50 directs that the <ORG> shall take steps to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the <ORG>.
Precedent	This objective has been, to a large extent, carried out without infringing the fundamental rights.
Precedent	151.
Precedent	In his preliminary note on the fundamental Rights, Sir <NAME>, dealing with the directive principles, observed:  The principles set forth in this Part are intended for the general guid ance of the appropriate <ORG> and Government in India (hereinafter referred to collectively as 'the <ORG>').
Precedent	The application of these principles in legislation and administration shall be the care of the <ORG> and shall not be cognizable by any <ORG>.
Precedent	152.
Precedent	After setting out certain directive principles, he observed: It is obvious that none of the above provisions is suitable for enforcement by the courts.
Precedent	They are really in the nature of moral precepts for the authorities of the <ORG>.
Precedent	Although it may be contended that the Constitution is not the proper place for moral precepts, nevertheless Constitutional declaration of policy of this kind are now becoming increasingly frequent.
Precedent	(See the Introduction to the I.L.O.
Precedent	publication Constitutional Provisions concerning Social and Economic Policy, Montreal, 1944).
Precedent	They have at least an educative value.
Precedent	(pages 33-34- <NAME> : Framing of Indian Constitution : Doc.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	II).
Precedent	Then he referred to the genesis of the various articles mentioned in the preliminar y note.
Precedent	153.
Precedent	One must pause and ask the question as to why did the <ORG> <ORG> resist the persistent efforts of <NAME> to make fundamental rights subject to the directive principles.
Precedent	The answer seems plain enough : The Constituent <ORG> deliberately decided not to do so.
Precedent	154.
Precedent	Sir <NAME>, in his note dated March 14, 1947, observed: A distinction has necessarily to be drawn between rights which are justiciable and rights which are merely intended as a guide and directive objectives to state policy.
Precedent	155.
Precedent	It is impossible to equate the directive principles with fundamental rights though it cannot be denied that they are very important.
Precedent	But to say that the directive principles give a directive to take away fundamental rights in order to achieve what is directed by the directive principles seems to me a contradiction in terms.
Precedent	156.
Precedent	I may here mention that while our fundamental rights and directive principles were being fashioned and approved of by the Constituent <ORG>,  on December 10, 1948 <ORG> adopted a Universal Declaration of Human Rights.
Precedent	The Declaration may not be a legally binding instrument but it shows how India understood the nature of Human Rights.
Precedent	I may here quote only the Preamble: Whereas recognition of the inherent dignity of the equal and inalienable rights of all members of the human family is the foundation of freedom, justice and peace in the world.
Precedent	(emphasis supplied)  157.
Precedent	Whereas disregard and contempt for human rights have resulted in barbarous acts which have outraged the conscience of mankind, and the advent of a world in which human beings shall enjoy freedom of speech and belief and freedom from fear and want has been proclaimed as the highest aspiration of the common people.
Precedent	158.
Precedent	Whereas it is essential, if man is not to be compelled to have recourse, as a last resort, to rebellion against tyranny and oppression, that human rights should be protected by the rule of law.
Precedent	159.
Precedent	Whereas it is essential to prom ote the development of friendly relations between nations.
Precedent	160.
Precedent	Whereas the peoples of <ORG> have in the Charter reaffirmed their faith in fundamental human rights, in the dignity and worth of the human person and in the equal rights of men and women and have determined to promote social progress and better standards of life in larger freedom.
Precedent	161.
Precedent	Whereas Member States have pledged themselves to achieve, in cooperation with <ORG>, the promotion of universal respect for and observance of human rights and fundamental freedoms.
Precedent	162.
Precedent	Whereas a common understanding of these rights and freedoms is of the greatest importance for the full realization of this pledge  163.
Precedent	In the Preamble to the International Covenant on Economic and Social and Cultural Rights 1966, inalienability of rights is indicated in the first Para as follows: Considering that, in accordance with the principles proclaimed in the Charter of <ORG>, recognition of the inherent dignity and of the equal and i nalienable rights of all members of the human family is the foundation of freedom, justice and peace in the world.
Precedent	164.
Precedent	Do rights remain inalienable if they can be amended out of existence ?
Precedent	The Preamble Articles 1, 55, 56, 62, 68 and 76 of the United Nations Charter had provided the basis for the elaboration in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.
Precedent	Although there is a sharp conflict of opinion whether respect for human dignity and fundamental human rights is obligatory under the Charter (see Oppenheim's International Law; 8th ed.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	1, pp.
Precedent	740-41; footnote 3), it seems to me that, in view of Article 51 of the directive principles, this <ORG> must interpret language of the Constitution, if not intractable, which is after all a municipal law, in the light of the United Nations Charter and the solemn declaration subscribed to by India.
Precedent	Article 51 reads:  51.
Precedent	The <ORG> shall endeavour to-  (a) promote international peace and security;  (b) maintain just and honourable relations between nations;  (c) fost er respect for international law and treaty obligations in the dealings of organised peoples with one another; and  (d) encourage settlement of international disputes by arbitration.
Precedent	165.
Precedent	As observed by Lord <NAME> in <NAME> v. <ORG> (1969) 1 All E.R.
Precedent	82; 87 "it is the duty of these courts to construe our Legislation so as to be in conformity with international law and not in conflict with it."
Precedent	(See also <NAME> supra, pp.
Precedent	45-46; American Jurisprudence 2nd, Vol.
Precedent	45, p. 351).
Precedent	166.
Precedent	Part V Chapter I, deals with the <ORG>; Chapter II with <ORG>-conduct or its business, qualification of its members, legislation procedure etc.
Precedent	Article 83 provides that:  83.
Statute	(1) <ORG> shall not be subject to dissolution, but as nearly as possible one-third of the members thereof shall retire as soon as may be on the expiration of every second year in accordance with the provisions made in that behalf by <ORG> by law.
Statute	(2) <ORG> unless sooner dissolved, shall cont inue for five years from the date appointed for its first meeting and no longer and the expiration of the said period of five years shall operate as a dissolution of the <ORG>: ...
Statute	Under the proviso this period can be extended while a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation for a period not exceeding in any case beyond a period of six months after the Proclamation has ceased to operate.
Statute	It was provided in Article 85(1) before its amendment by the Constitution (First Amendment) Act 1951 that the <ORG> of <ORG> shall be summoned to meet twice at least in every year, and six months shall not intervene between their last sittings in one session and the date appointed for their first sitting in the next session.
Statute	167.
Statute	Article 123 gives power to the President to promulgate ordinances during recess of <ORG> Chapter IV deals with <ORG>.
Statute	168.
Statute	Part VI, as originally enacted dealt with the States in Part A of the First Schedule-the Executive, the State Legislatures and <ORG>.
Statute	Article 17 4 deals with the summoning of <ORG> and its provisions are similar to that of Article  85.
Precedent	Article 213 confers legislative powers on the Governor during the recess of <ORG> by promulgating ordinances.
Precedent	169.
Precedent	Part XI deals with the relation between the Union and the States; Chapter I regulating legislative relations and Chapter II administrative relations.
Precedent	170.
Statute	Part XII deals with Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits.
Statute	We need only notice Article 265 which provides that "no tax shall be levied or collected except by authority of law".
Statute	171.
Statute	Part XIII deals with Trade, Commerce and Intercourse within the Territory of India.
Statute	Subject to the provisions of this Chapter, trade, commerce and intercourse throughout the territory of India shall be free ( Article 301 ).
Statute	172.
Statute	Part XIV deals with Services under the Union and the States.
Statute	Part XVI contains special provisions relating to certain classes-the Scheduled Castes, the Scheduled Tribes etc.
Statute	It reserved seats in <ORG> le for these classes.
Statute	Article 331 enables the President to nominate not more than two members of the Anglo-Indian community if it is not adequately represented in <ORG>.
Statute	Article 332 deals with the reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in <ORG>.
Statute	In Article 334 it is provided that the above mentioned reservation of seats and special representation to certain classes shall cease on the expiry of a period of ten years from the commencement of this Constitution.
Statute	Article 335 deals with claims of scheduled castles and scheduled tribes to services and posts.
Statute	Article 336 makes special provisions for Anglo-Indian community in certain services, and Article 337 makes special provisions in respect of educational grants for the benefit of Anglo- Indian community.
Statute	Article 338 provides for the creation of a Special Officer for Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, etc.
Statute	to be appointed by the President, and prescribes his duties.
Statute	Article 340 enables t he President to appoint a Commission to investigate the conditions of socially and educationally backward classes within the territory of India which shall present a report and make recommendations on steps that should be taken to remove difficulties and improve their condition.
Statute	Article 341 enables the President to specify the castes, races or tribes or parts of or groups within castes, races or tribes which shall for the purposes of this Constitution be deemed to be Scheduled Castes in relation to that State.
Statute	Similarly, Article 342 provides that the President may specify the tribes or tribal communities or parts of or groups within tribes or tribal communities which shall be deemed to be Scheduled Tribes in relation to that State.
Statute	173.
Statute	Part XVII deals with Official Language, and Part XVIII with Emergency Provisions.
Statute	Article 352 is important.
Statute	It reads:  352.
Statute	(1) If the President is satisfied that a grave emergency exists whereby the security of India or of any part of the territory thereof is threatened, whet her by war or external aggression or internal disturbance, he may, by Proclamation, make a declaration to that effect.
Statute	174.
Statute	Article 353 describes the effect of the Proclamation of Emergency.
Statute	The effect is that the executive power of the <ORG> shall be extended to the giving of directions to any <ORG> as to the manner in which the executive power thereof is to be exercised, and the <ORG> gets the power to make laws with respect to any matter including the power to make laws conferring powers and imposing duties, etc., notwithstanding that it is one which is not enumerated in the <ORG> List.
Statute	Article 354 enables the President by order to make exceptions and modifications in the provisions of Article 268 to 279.
Statute	Under Article 355 it is the duty of the <ORG> to protect every <ORG> against external aggression and internal disturbance and to ensure that the government of every <ORG> is carried on in accordance with the provisions of the Constitution.
Statute	Article 356 contains provisions in case of failure of Consti tutional machinery in a <ORG>.
Statute	175.
Statute	Article 358 provides for suspension of the provisions of Article 19 during Emergency.
Statute	It reads:  358.
Statute	While a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation, nothing in Article 19 shall restrict the power of the <ORG> as defined in Part III to make any law or to take any executive action which the <ORG> would but for the provisions contained in that Part be competent to make or to take, but any law so made shall, to the extent of the incompetency, cease to have effect as soon as the Proclamation ceases to operate, except as respects things done or omitted to be done before the law so ceases to have effect.
Statute	176.
Statute	Article 359 is most important for our purpose.
Statute	It provides that:  359.
Statute	(1) Where a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation the President may by order declare that the right to move any court for the enforcement of such of the rights conferred by Part III as may be mentioned in the order and all proceedings pending in any court for the enforcement of the rights so ment ioned shall remain suspended for the period during which the Proclamation is in force or for such shorter period as may be specified in the order.
Statute	(2) An order made as aforesaid may extend to the whole or any part of the territory of India.
Statute	(3) Every order made under Clause (1) shall, as soon as maybe after it is made be laid before each <ORG> of Parliament.
RatioOfTheDecision	177.
RatioOfTheDecision	These two articles, namely Article 358 and Article 359 show that the Constitution makers contemplated that fundamental rights might impede the <ORG> in meeting an emergency, and it was accordingly provided that Article 19 shall not operate for a limited time, and so also Article 32 and Article 226 if the President so declares by order.
RatioOfTheDecision	If it was the design that fundamental rights might be abrogated surely they would have expressly provided it somewhere.
RatioOfTheDecision	178.
RatioOfTheDecision	I may here notice an argument that the enactment of Articles 358 and 359 showed that the fundamental rights were not treated as inalienable rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	I am unable to infer this deduction from th ese articles.
RatioOfTheDecision	In an emergency every citizen is liable to be subjected to extraordinary restrictions.
RatioOfTheDecision	179.
RatioOfTheDecision	I may here notice some relevant facts which constitute the background of the process of drafting the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> knowing the complexities of the structure of the Indian people expressly provided in Section 6(6) of the Indian Independence Act, 1947, that "the powers referred to in Sub-section (1) of this section extends to the making of laws limiting for the future the powers of the legislature of the Dominion."
Statute	Sub-section (1) of Section 6 reads:  The legislature of each of the new Dominions shall have full power to make laws for that Dominion, including laws having extraterritorial operation.
Statute	That Section 6(1) included making provision as to the Constitution of the Dominion is made clear by Section 8(1) which provided : "In the case of each of the new Dominions, the powers of legislature of the Dominion shall for the purpose of making provision as to the Constitution of the Dom inion be exercisable in the first instance by <ORG> of that Dominion, and references in this Act to the legislature of the Dominion shall be construed accordingly.
RatioOfTheDecision	(Emphasis supplied).
RatioOfTheDecision	180.
RatioOfTheDecision	These provisions of the Indian Independence Act amply demonstrate that when <ORG> started functioning, it knew, if it acted under the Indian Independence Act, that it could limit the powers of the future <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	181.
RatioOfTheDecision	No similar provisions exists in any of the Independence Acts in respect of other countries, enacted by the British <ORG>, e.g., Ceylon Independence Act, 1947, Ghana Independence Act, 1957, Federation of Malaya Independence Act, 1957, Nigeria Independence Act, 1960, Sierra Leone Independence Act, 1961, Tanganyika Independence Act, 1961, Southern Rhodesia Act, 1965, Jamaica Independence Act, 1962.
RatioOfTheDecision	182.
RatioOfTheDecision	I may mention that the aforesaid provisions in the Indian Independence Act were enacted in line with the Cabinet Statement dated May 16, 1947 and the po sition of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Para 20(See : <NAME>-The Framing of India's <ORG>tution, Vol.
RatioOfTheDecision	I, p.  216) of the Statement by <ORG> provided:  <ORG> citizens, minorities, and tribal and excluded areas should contain full representation of the interests affected, and their function will be to report to <ORG> upon the list of Fundamental Rights, the clauses for the protection of minorities, and a scheme for the administration of the tribal and excluded areas, and to advise whether these rights should be incorporated in the Provincial, Group, or Union <ORG>tution.

RatioOfTheDecision	The only limitation, we recognise is that in regard to certain major communal issues the decision should be by a majority of each of the two major communitie s. (P. 251, Supra).

RatioOfTheDecision	(Emphasis supplied)  187.
Fact	In pursuance of the above, a resolution for the setting up of <ORG> on fundamental rights was moved by <NAME> in <ORG> on January 24, 1947.
Fact	He laid special importance on the issue of minorities.
Fact	<ORG> met on February 27, 1947 to constitute various sub-committees including the Minorities Sub-Committee.
Fact	<ORG> met later the same day.
RatioOfTheDecision	A questionnaire was drafted to enquire about political, economic, religious, educational and cultural safeguards.
RatioOfTheDecision	In other words all these safeguards were considered.
RatioOfTheDecision	188.
RatioOfTheDecision	Divergent views were expressed, and the Minorities Sub-Committee met on April 17, 18 and 19, 1947 to consider this important matter.
RatioOfTheDecision	At these meetings the sub- committee considered the int erim proposals of the fundamental rights <ORG>-Committee in so far as these had a bearing on minority rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	These discussions covered such important matters as the prohibition of discrimination on grounds of race, religion, caste, etc.
RatioOfTheDecision	; the abolition of untouchability and the mandatory requirements that the enforcement of any disability arising out of untouchability should be made an offence punishable according to law; freedom to profess, practise and propagate one's religion; the right to establish and maintain institutions for religious and charitable purposes; the right to be governed by one's personal, law; the right to use one's mother-tongue and establish denominational communal or language schools etc.
RatioOfTheDecision	189.
RatioOfTheDecision	Having dealt with the question of fundamental rights for minorities, the Minorities <ORG>-Committee met again on July 21, 1947, to consider the political safeguards for minorities and their presentation in the public services.
RatioOfTheDecision	190.
RatioOfTheDecision	In forwarding the report of <ORG> on the subject o f Minority Rights, <NAME>, in his report dated August 8, 1947, said: ...It should be treated as supplementary to the one forwarded to you with my letter No.
RatioOfTheDecision	CA/24/Com./47, dated the 23rd April 1947 and dealt with by the <ORG> during the April session.
RatioOfTheDecision	That report dealt with justiciable fundamental rights; these rights, whether applicable to all citizens generally or to members of minority communities in particular offer a most valuable safeguard for minorities over a comprehensive field of social life.
RatioOfTheDecision	The present report deals with what may broadly be described as political safeguards of minorities and covers the following points:  (i) Representation in <ORG>; joint versus separate electorates; and weightage.
RatioOfTheDecision	(ii) Reservation of seats for minorities in <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	(iii) Reservation for minorities in the public services.

Fact	They cover a very wide range of the rights of minorities which give them ample protection; and yet there are certain political safeguards which have got to be specifically considered.

RatioOfTheDecision	(Emphasis supplied)  192.
RatioOfTheDecision	The above proceedings show that the minorities were particularly concerned with the fundamental rights which were the subject-matter of discussion by <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	193.
RatioOfTheDecision	The above brief summary of the work of <ORG> and <ORG>-Committee shows that no one ever contemplated that fundamental rights appertaining to the minorities would be liable to be abrogated by an amendment of th e Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The same is true about the proceedings in <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no hint anywhere that abrogation of minorities rights was ever in the contemplation of the important members of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It seems to me that in the context of the British Plan, the setting up of Minorities Sub-Committee, <ORG> and the proceedings of these <ORG>, as well as the proceedings in <ORG> mentioned above, it is impossible to read the expression "Amendment of the Constitution" as empowering <ORG> to abrogate the rights of minorities.
RatioOfTheDecision	194.
RatioOfTheDecision	Both sides relied on the speeches made in <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is, however, a sound rule of construction that speeches made by members of a legislature in the course of debates relating to the enactment of a statute cannot be used as aids for interpreting any of provisions of the statute.
Precedent	The same rule has been applied to the provisions of this Constitution by this <ORG> in <CITATION_1334665> .
Precedent	S.C.R.
Precedent	11 12, 1121 <NAME>, <NAME>, speaking for the <ORG> observed: 'It remains only to point out that the use made by the learned Judges below of the speeches made by the Members of <ORG> in the course of the debates on the draft Constitution is unwarranted.
Precedent	That this form of extrinsic aid to the interpretation of statutes is not admissible has been generally accepted in England, and the same rule has been observed in the construction of Indian statutes-see Administrator-General of Bengal v. <NAME>  22 I.A.
Precedent	107-118.
Precedent	The reason behind the rule was explained by one of us in <NAME>'s  S.C.R.
Precedent	88 case thus: A speech made in the course of the debate on a bill could at best be indicative of the subjective intent of the speaker, but it could not reflect the inarticulate mental process lying behind the majority vote which carried the bill.
RatioOfTheDecision	Nor is it reasonable to assume that the minds of all those legislators were in accord, or, as it is more tersely put in an American case-  Those who did not spe <NAME> may not have agreed with those who did and those who spoke might differ from each other-  United States v. <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	This rule of exclusion has not always been adhered to in America, and sometimes distinction is made between using such material to ascertain the purpose of a statute and using it for ascertaining its meaning.
Precedent	It would seem that the rule is adopted in Canada and Australia-see Craies on Statute Law, 5th Ed.
Precedent	p. 122.
Precedent	195.
Precedent	In Gol<NAME> Nath's [1967] 2 S.C.R.
Precedent	762; 792; 922 case, <NAME>, C.J., referred to certain portions of the speeches made by Pandit <NAME> and Dr. <NAME> but he made it clear at p. 792 that he referred to these speeches "not with a view to interpret the provisions of Article 368 , which we propose to do on its own terms, but only to notice the transcendental character given to the fundamental rights by two of the important architects of the Constitution."
Precedent	<NAME>, J., at p. 922 observed: Before concluding this judgment I must refer to some of the spee ches made by the members of <ORG> in the course of debates on the draft Constitution.
Precedent	These speeches cannot be used as aids for interpreting the <CITATION_1334665> .
Precedent	S.C.R.
Precedent	1112.
Precedent	Accordingly I do not rely on them as aids to construction.
Precedent	But I propose to refer to them, as <NAME> relied heavily on the speeches of Dr. <NAME>.
Precedent	According to him, the speeches of Dr. <NAME> show that he did not regard the fundamental rights as amendable.
Precedent	This contention is not supported by the speeches....  196.

Precedent	He referred to this portion of the speech for the purpose of showing the historical background and the circumstances which necessitated giving certain guarantees to the former rulers.
Precedent	197.

Precedent	198.
Precedent	<CITATION_1235907> ,  2 S.C.R.
Precedent	33 I, on behalf of the majority, before referring to the speeches observed at p. 58 that "we are, however, glad to find from the following extracts from the debates that our interpretation accords with what was intended."
Precedent	There is no harm in finding confirmation of one's interpretation in debates but it is quite a different thing to interpret the provisions of the Constitution in the light of the debates.
Precedent	199.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is an additional reason for not referring to debates for the purpose of interpretation.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution, as far as most of the Indian States were concerned, came into operation only because of the acceptance by the Ruler or <NAME>.
Precedent	This is borne out by the following extract from the statement of Sardar <NAME> in <ORG> on October 12, 1949 (C.A.D.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	X, pp.
Precedent	161-3): Unfortunately we have no properly cons tituted Legislatures in the rest of the States (apart from Mysore, Saurashtra and Travancore and Cochin Union) nor will it be possible to have Legislatures constituted in them before the Constitution of India emerges in its final form.
Precedent	We have, therefore, no option but to make the Constitution operative in these States on the basis of its acceptance by the Ruler of the <NAME>, as the case may be, who will no doubt consult his <ORG>.
Precedent	200.
Precedent	In accordance with this statement, declarations were issued by the Rulers or <NAME>s accepting the Constitution.
Precedent	201.
Precedent	It seems to me that when a Ruler or <NAME> or the people of the State accepted the Constitution of India in its final form, he did not accept it subject to the speeches made during the <ORG> debates.
RatioOfTheDecision	The speeches can, in my view, be relied on only in order to see if the course of the progress of a particular provision or provisions throws any light on the historical background or shows that a common understanding or  agreement was arrived at between certain sections of the people.
Precedent	(See In re.
Precedent	The Regulation and Control of Aeronautics in Canada) [1932] A.C. 54 at p. 70.
Precedent	202.
Precedent	In this connection reference was made to Article 305 of the draft Constitution which provided that notwithstanding anything contained in Article 304 of the Constitution, the provisions of the Constitution relating to the reservation of seats for the Muslims etc., shall not be amended during the period of ten years from the commencement of the Constitution.
Precedent	Although this draft Article 305 has no counterpart in our Constitution, it was sought to be urged that this showed that every provision of the Constitution was liable to be amended.
Precedent	I have come to the conclusion that every provision is liable to be amended subject to certain limitations and this argument does not affect my conclusion as to implied limitations.
Precedent	203.
Precedent	A very important decision of <ORG> in The Bribery Commissioner v. Pedrick Ranasinghe [1965] A.C.  172 throws considerable light on the topic under discussion.
Precedent	The import of this decision was not realised by this <ORG> in <NAME> [1967] 2 S.C.R.
Precedent	762 case.
Precedent	Indeed, it is not referred to by the minority in its judgments, and <NAME>, C.J., makes only a passing reference to it.
Precedent	In order to fully appreciate the decision of <ORG> it is necessary to set out the relevant provisions of the Ceylon Independence Order in Council, 1947, hereinafter referred to as the Ceylon Constitution.
Precedent	204.
Precedent	Part III of the Ceylon Constitution deals with "Legislature".
Precedent	Section 7 provides that "there shall be <ORG> which shall consist of His Majesty, and two <ORG>s to be known respectively as the <ORG> and <ORG>.
Precedent	205.
Precedent	Section 18 deals with voting.
Precedent	It reads:  18.
Precedent	Save as otherwise provided in Sub-section (4) of Section 29 , any question proposed for decision by either <ORG> shall be determined by a majority of votes of the Senators or Members, as the case may be, pres ent and voting.
Precedent	The President or Speaker or other person presiding shall not vote in the first instance but shall have and exercise a casting vote in the event of an equality of votes.
Precedent	206.
Statute	Section 29 deals with the power of <ORG> to make laws.
Statute	It reads: 29(1) Subject to the provisions of this Order, <ORG> shall have power to make laws for the peace, order and good government of the Island.
Statute	(2) No such law shall-  (a) prohibit or restrict the free exercise of any religion, or  (b) make persons of any community or religion liable to disabilities or restrictions to which persons or other communities or religions are not made liable; or  (c) confer on persons of any community or religion any privilege or advantage which is not conferred on persons of other communities or religions; or  (d) alter the Constitution of any religious body except with the consent of the governing authority of that body.
Statute	So, however, that in any case where a religious body is incorporated by law, no such alteration shall be  made except at the request of the governing authority of that body.
RulingByPresentCourt	Provided, however, that the preceding provisions of this subsection shall not apply to any law making provision for, relating to, or connected with the, election of Members of <ORG>, to represent persons registered as citizens of Ceylon under the Indian & Pakistani Residents (Citizenship Act).
RulingByPresentCourt	This proviso shall cease to have effect on a date to be fixed by the Governor-General by Proclamation published in the <ORG>.
RulingByPresentCourt	(3) Any law made in contravention of Sub-section (2) of this section shall, to the extent of such contravention, be void.
RulingByPresentCourt	(4) In the exercise of its powers under this section, <ORG> may amend or repeal any of the provisions of this Order, or of any other Order of Her Majesty in Council in its application to the Island:  Provided that no Bill for the amendment or repeal of any of the Provisions of this Order shall be presented for the Royal Assent unless it has endorsed on it a certificate under h and of the Speaker that the number of votes cast in favour thereof in <ORG> amounted to not less than twothirds of the whole number of members of the <ORG> (including those not present).
Precedent	Every certificate of the Speaker under this sub-section shall be conclusive for all purposes and shall not be questioned in any court of law.
Precedent	207.
Precedent	According to Mr. <NAME>, Section 29(1) corresponds to Articles 245 and 246, and Section 29(4) corresponds to Article 368 of our Constitution, and Sections 29(2) and 29(3) correspond to Article 13(2) of our Constitution, read with fundamental rights.
Precedent	208.
Precedent	The question which arose before the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council was whether Section 41 of the Bribery Amendment Act, 1958 contravened Section 29(4) of the Ceylon Constitution, and was consequently invalid.
Precedent	The question arose out of the following facts.
Precedent	The respondent, <NAME>, was prosecuted for a bribery offence before <ORG> created by the Bribery Amendment Act, 1958.
Precedent	The  <ORG> sentenced him to a term of imprisonment and fine.
Precedent	<ORG> on appeal declared the conviction and orders made against him null and inoperative on the ground that the persons composing the <ORG> were not validly appointed to the <ORG>.
Precedent	209.
Precedent	Section 52 of the Ceylon Constitution provided for the appointment of the Chief Justice and Puisne Judges of <ORG>.
Precedent	Section 53 dealt with the setting up of <ORG>, consisting of the Chief Justice, a Judge of <ORG>, and one other person who shall be, or shall have been, a Judge of <ORG>.
Precedent	It further provided that no person shall be appointed as, or shall remain, a member of <ORG>, if he is Senator or a Member of <ORG>.
Precedent	Section 55 provided for the appointment of other Judicial Officers.
Precedent	Section 55(1) reads:  55.
Precedent	(1) The appointment, transfer, dismissal and disciplinary control of judicial officers is hereby vested in <ORG>.
Precedent	210.
Precedent	The Ju dicial Committee deduced from these provisions thus: Thus there is secured a freedom from political control, and it is a punishable offence to attempt directly or indirectly to influence any decision of the <ORG> ( Section 56 ).
Precedent	211.
Precedent	<ORG> then described the position of <ORG> as follows:  A bribery tribunal, of which there may be any number, is composed of three members selected from a panel ( Section 42 ).
Precedent	The panel is composed of not more than 15 persons who are appointed by the Governor-General on the advice of the Minister of Justice ( Section 41 ).
Precedent	The members of the panel are paid remuneration ( Section 45 ).
Precedent	212.
Precedent	<ORG> held that the members of the <ORG> held judicial office and were judicial officers within Section 55 of the Ceylon Constitution.
Precedent	They found that there was a plain conflict between Section 55 of the Constitution and Section 41 of the Bribery Amendment Act under which the panel was appointed.
Precedent	213.
Precedent	Then <ORG> exami ned the effect of this conflict.
Precedent	After setting out Section 18 , Section 29(1) and Section 29(2)(a) , <ORG> observed: There follow (b), (c) and (d), which set out further entrenched religious and racial matters, which shall not be the subject of legislation.
Precedent	They represent the solemn balance of rights, between the citizens of Ceylon, the fundamental conditions on which inter se they accepted the Constitution; and these are, therefore unalterable under the Constitution.
Precedent	(Emphasis supplied)  214.
Precedent	After making these observations, <ORG> set out Sub-sections (3) and (4) of Section 29 of the Ceylon Constitution.
Precedent	The observations, which I have set out above, are strongly relied on by Mr. <NAME> in support of his argument that Part III similarly entrenched various religious and racial and other matters and these represented solemn balance of rights between the citizens of India, the fundamental conditions on which inter se they accepted the Constitution of India and these are, the refore, unalterable under the Constitution of India.
Precedent	215.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME>, in reply, submitted that the word "entrenched" meant nothing else that than these provisions were subject to be amended only by the procedure prescribed in Section 29(4) of the Ceylon Constitution.
Precedent	But I am unable to accept this interpretation because in that sense other provisions of the Constitution were equally entrenched because no provision of the Ceylon Constitution could be amended without following the procedure laid down in Section 29(4) .
Precedent	216.
Precedent	The interpretation urged by Mr. <NAME> dervies support in the manner <ORG> distinguished <NAME>'s [1920] A.C. 691 case (<NAME> v. <NAME>).
Precedent	I may set out here the observations of <ORG> regarding <NAME>'s case.
Precedent	They observed:  It is possible now to state summarily what is the essential difference between the <NAME> case and this case.
Precedent	There the legislature, having full power to make laws by a majority, except upon one subject that was not in quest ion, passed a law which conflicted with one of the existing terms of its Constitution Act .
Precedent	It was held that this was valid legislation, since it must be treated as pro tanto an alteration of the Constitution, which was neither fundamental in the sense of being beyond change nor so constructed as to require any special legislative process to pass upon the topic dealt with.
Precedent	(Emphasis supplied)  217.
Precedent	It is rightly urged that the expression "which was neither fundamental in the sense of being beyond change" has reference to Section 29(2) of the Ceylon Constitution.
Precedent	I have no doubt that <ORG> held that the provisions of Section 29(2) in the Ceylon Constitution were unamendable.
Precedent	I may mention that Prof. <NAME> in reviewing the book "Reflections on the Constitution and the Constituent Assembly.
Precedent	(Ceylon's Constitution) "by <NAME>, reads the obiter dicta in Bribery Commissioner v. Ranasinghe  A.C. 172, 193-194 indicating that certain provisions of the Constitution were unalterable by th e prescribed amending procedure.
Precedent	218.
Precedent	It may be that these observations are obiter but these deserve our careful consideration, coming as they do from <ORG>.
Precedent	219.
Precedent	Why did <ORG> say that the provisions of Section 29(2) were "unalterable under the Constitution" or "fundamental in the sense of being beyond change" ?
Precedent	There is nothing in the language of Section 29(4) to indicate any limitations on the power of <ORG>.
Precedent	It could "amend or repeal" any provision of the Constitution, which included Section 29(2) and Section 29(4) itself.
Precedent	The reason could only be an implied limitation on the power to amend under Section 29(4) deducible from "the solemn balance of rights between the citizens of Ceylon, the fundamental conditions on which inter se they accepted the Constitution".
Precedent	Unless there was implied a limitation on the exercise of the amending power under Section 29(4) , Section 29(4) could itself be amended to make it clear that Section 29(2) is amendable.
Precedent	220.
Precedent	Thi s case furnishes an exact example where implied limitations on the power to amend the Constitution have been inferred by no less a body than <ORG>.
Precedent	221.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> relied on the portion within brackets of the following passage at pp.
Precedent	197-198:  These passages show clearly that the <ORG> in <NAME>'s case took the view which commends itself to the <ORG> in the present case, that (a legislature has no power to ignore the conditions of law-making that are imposed by the instrument which itself regulates its powers to make law.
Precedent	This restriction exists independently of the question whether the legislature is sovereign, as is the legislature of Ceylon, or whether the Constitution is "uncontrolled," as the <ORG> held the Constitution of Queensland to be.
Precedent	Such a Constitution can, indeed, be altered or amended by the legislature, if the regulating instrument so provides that if the terms of those provisions are compiled with and the alteration or amendment may include the change  or abolition of those very provisions.)
Precedent	But the proposition which is not acceptable is that a legislature, once established, has some inherent power derived from the mere fact of its establishment to make a valid law by the resolution of a bare majority which its own constituent instrument has said shall not be a valid law unless made by a different type of majority or by a different legislative process.
Precedent	And this is the proposition which is in reality involved in the argument.
Precedent	222.
Precedent	The portion, not within brackets, which has been omitted in Mr. <NAME>'s written submissions, clearly shows that <ORG> in this passage was not dealing with the amendment of Section 29(2) of the Ceylon Constitution and had understood <NAME>'s [1920] A.C. 691 case as not being concerned with the question of the amendment of a provision like Section 29(2) of the Ceylon Constitution.
Precedent	This passage only means that a legislature cannot disregard the procedural conditions imposed on it by the constituent instrument pr escribing a particular majority but may amend them if the constituent instrument gives that power.
Precedent	223.
Precedent	The next passage, a part of which I have already extracted, which deals with the difference between <NAME>'s case and <NAME>'s [1965] A.C. 172, 193-194 case shows that <ORG> in the passage relied on was dealing with the procedural part of Section 29(4) of Ceylon Constitution.
Precedent	It reads: It is possible now to state summarily what is the essential difference between the <NAME> case and this case.
Precedent	There the legislature having full power to make laws by a majority, except upon one subject that was not in question, passed a law which conflicted with one of the existing terms of the Constitution Act .
Precedent	It was held that this was valid legislation, since it must be treated as pro tanto an alteration of the Constitution, which was neither fundamental in the sense of being beyond change nor so constructed as to require any special legislative process to pass upon the topic dealt with.
Precedent	In the p resent case, on the other hand, the legislature has purported to pass a law which being in conflict with Section 55 of the Order in Council, must be treated, if it is to be valid, as an implied alteration of the Constitutional provisions about the appointment of judicial officers.
Precedent	Since such alterations, even if express, can only be made by laws which comply with the special legislative procedure laid down in Section 29(4) , the Ceylon legislature has not got the general power to legislate so as to amend its Constitution by ordinary majority resolutions, such as the Queensland legislature was found to have under Section 2 of its Constitution Act , but is rather in the position, for effecting such amendments, that that legislature was held to be in by virtue of its Section 9 , namely, compelled to operate a special procedure in order to achieve the desired result.
Precedent	224.
Precedent	I may mention that <ORG> while interpreting the British North America Act, 1867 had also kept in mind the preservation of the  rights of minorities for they say In re The Regulation and Control of Aeronautics in Canada: [1933] A.C. 54 at p. 70 "inasmuch as the Act (British North America Act) embodies a compromise under which the original Provinces agreed to federate, it is important to keep in mind that the preservation of the rights of minorities was a conditioin on which such minorities entered into the federation, and the foundation upon which the whole structure was subsequently erected.
Precedent	The process of interpretation as the years go on ought not to be allowed to dim or to whittle down the provisions of the original contract upon which the federation was founded, nor is it legitimate that any judicial construction of the Provisions of Sections 91 and 92 should impose a new and different contract upon the federating bodies.
Precedent	225.
Precedent	The words of <ORG> in <NAME>'s case, are apposite and pregnant.
Precedent	"They represent the solemn balance of rights between the citizens of Ceylon, the fundamental conditions on which inter  se they accepted the Constitution and these are, therefore unalterable under the Constitution."
Precedent	It is true that <ORG> in the context of minorities and religious rights in Ceylon used the word "unalterable".
Precedent	But the India context is slightly different.
Precedent	The guarantee of fundamental rights extends to numerous rights and it could not have been intended that all of them would remain completely unalterable even if Article 1.3(2) of the Constitution be taken to include Constitutional amendments.
Precedent	A more reasonable inference to be drawn from the whole scheme of the Constitution is that some other meaning of "Amendment" is most appropriate.
Precedent	This conclusion is also reinforced by the concession of the Attorney-General and Mr. <NAME> that the whole Constitution cannot be abrogated or repealed and a new one substituted.
Precedent	In other words, the expression "Amendment of this Constitution" does not include a revision of the whole Constitution.
Precedent	If this is true-I say that the concession was rightly made-then which  is that meaning of the word "Amendment" that is most appropriate and fits in with the while scheme of the Constitution.
Precedent	In my view that meaning would be appropriate which would enable the country to achieve a social and economic revolution without destroying the democratic structure of the Constitution and the basic inalienable rights guaranteed in Part III and without going outside the contours delineated in the Preamble.
Precedent	226.
Precedent	I come to the same conclusion by another line of reasoning.
Precedent	In a written Constitution it is rarely that everything is said expressly.
Precedent	Powers and limitations are implied from necessity or the scheme of the Constitution.
Precedent	I will mention a few instances approved by <ORG> and this <ORG> and other <ORG>s.
Precedent	I may first consider the doctrine that enables <ORG> to have power to deal with ancillary and subsidiary matters, which strictly do not fall within the legislative entry with respect to which legislation is being undertaken.
Precedent	227.
Precedent	<NAME> in "A short Treatise on Canad ian Constitutional Law" (page 94), puts the matter thus:  But when it is (<ORG>) is legislating upon the enumerated Dominion subject-matters of Section 91 of the Federation Act, it is held that <ORG>, by necessary implication, intended to confer on it legislative power to interfere with, deal with, and encroach upon, matters otherwise assigned to the provincial legislatures under Section 92 , so far as a general law relating to those subjects may affect them, as it may also do to the extent of such ancillary provisions as may be required to prevent the scheme of such a law from being defeated.
Precedent	<ORG> has established and illustrated this in many decisions.
Precedent	228.

RatioOfTheDecision	For instance, in the Constitution of the United States, Clause 18 of Section 8 expressly grants incidental powers: The <ORG> shall have power...to make all laws which shall be necessary and proper for carrying into execution the foregoing powers and all other powers vested by this Constitution in the government of the United States, or in any department or officer thereof.

Precedent	129 (the <ORG>' case) Lord <NAME>'s language in <ORG> (1878) 3 App.

Precedent	245.
Precedent	According to the Chief Justice, the <ORG> in <NAME>' case unequivocally rejected the doctrine that there was an "implied prohibition" in the Constitution against the exercise in relation to a State of a legislative power of the Commonwealth once ascertained in accordance with the ordinary rules of construction, a doctrine which had theretofore been entertained and sought to be founded upon some supposed necessity of "protection", as it were, "against the aggression of some outside and possibly hostile body".
Precedent	The <ORG> emphasized that if protection against an abuse of power were needed, it must be provided by the electorate and not by the judiciary.
Precedent	'The one clear line of judici al inquiry as to the meaning of the Constitution must be to read it naturally in the light of the circumstances in which it was made, with knowledge of the combined fabric of the common law, and the statute law which preceded it and then lucet ipsa per see.
Precedent	246.
Precedent	Now this is the judgment which is relied on by Mr. <NAME> and the learned Attorney General.
Precedent	On the other hand, reliance is placed by Mr. <NAME> on <ORG> judgment:  Does the fact that the Constitution is "federal" carry with it implications limiting the law-making powers of <ORG> with regard to the States ?
Precedent	To this question I have no doubt, both on principle and on authority, that an affirmative answer must be given.
Precedent	A Constitution providing for an indissoluble federal Commonwealth must protect both Commonwealth and States.
Precedent	The States are not outside the Constitution.
Precedent	They are States of the Commonwealth; Section 106 .
Precedent	Accordingly, although the Constitution does, clearly enough, subject the States to laws made b y the <ORG>, it does so with some limitation.
Precedent	247.
Precedent	After making these observations, the learned Judge examined authorities and he found support in <ORG> v. The <ORG> [1947] 74 C.L.R.
Precedent	31, He then examined various other cases in support of the above principles.
Precedent	248.
Precedent	The other passages relied on by the petitioners from the judgments of the other learned Judges on the Bench in that case are as follows:  <NAME>, <NAME>In each case an implication means that something not expressed is to be understood.
Precedent	But in the one case, this involves an addition to what is expressed : in the other it explains, perhaps limits, the effect of what is expressed.
Precedent	It is in the latter sense that in my view of the matter, implications have a place in the interpretation of the Constitution : and I consider it is the sense that <NAME> intended when in <ORG> v. The <ORG> (1945) 71 C.L.R.
Precedent	29, he said (at p. 85) : "We should avoid pedantic and narrow constructions in deali ng with an instrument of government and I do not see why we should be fearful about making implications".
Precedent	His Honour, when Chief Justice, repeated this observation in <NAME> v. <NAME> (1958) 99 C.L.R.
Precedent	132 at p.  144.
Precedent	I said in <NAME> v. <NAME> (1965) 114 C.L.R.
Precedent	226, at p. 272, that it is well to remember it.
Precedent	I still think so.
Precedent	The only emendation that I would venture is that I would prefer not to say "making implications", because our avowed task is simply the revealing or uncovering of implications that are already there.
Precedent	In <ORG> v. The <ORG> (1947) 74 C.L.R.
Precedent	31, <NAME> said (at p. 70) : "The federal character of the Australian Constitution carries implications of its own....  * * * * *  249.
Precedent	"The position that I take is this : The several subject matters with respect to which the <ORG> is empowered by the Constitution to make laws for the peace, order and good government of the Conmmon-wealth are not to be narrowed or limited by implications.
Precedent	Their scope and amplitude depend  simply on the words by which they are expressed.
Precedent	But implications arising from the existence of the <ORG> as parts of the <ORG> and as constituents of the federation may restrict the manner in which the <ORG> can lawfully exercise its power to make laws with respect to a particular subject-matter.
Precedent	These implications, or perhaps it were better to say underlying assumptions of the Constitution, relate to the use of a power not to the inherent nature of the subject matter of the law.
Precedent	Of course whether or not a law promotes peace, order and good government is for the <ORG>, not for a court, to decide.
Precedent	But a law although it be with respect to a designated subject matter, cannot be for the peace, order and good government of the <ORG> if it be directed to the <ORG> to prevent their carrying out their functions as parts of the <ORG>....  * * * * * <NAME>, <NAME>The ordinary principles of statutory construction do not preclude the making of implications when these are necessary to give  effect to the intention of the legislature as revealed in the statute as a whole.
Precedent	The intention of the Imperial legislature in enacting the Constitution Act was to give effect to the wish of the Australian people to join in a federal union and the purpose of the Constitution was to establish a federal, and not a unitary, system for the government of Australia and accordingly to provide for the distribution of the powers of government between the <ORG> and the States who were to be the constituent members of the federation.
Precedent	In some respects the <ORG> was placed in a position of supremacy, as the national interest required, but it would be inconsistent with the very basis of the federation that the <ORG>'s powers should extend to reduce the States to such a position of subordination that their very existence, or at least their capacity to function effectually as independent units, would be dependent upon the manner in which the <ORG> exercised its powers, rather than on the legal lim its of the powers themselves.
Precedent	Thus, the purpose of the Constitution, and the scheme by which it is intended to be given effect, necessarily give rise to implications as to the manner in which the Commonwealth and the <ORG> respectively may exercise their powers, vis-a-vis each other....  250.
Precedent	Wynes(Wynes Legislative, Executive and Judicial Power in Australia, Fourth Edn.

Precedent	(3) The "Constitution" of a <ORG> means its composition, form or nature of the <ORG> or <ORG>s, and excludes any reference to the Crown.
Precedent	* * * * * (6) No Colonial <ORG> can forever abrogate its power of amendment and thereby render its Constit ution absolutely immutable.
Precedent	A law purporting to effect this object would be void under Section 2 of the Act as being repugnant to Section 5 thereof.

Precedent	Quoting the words of Lord <NAME> in <NAME> v. Commonwealth of Australia, (1936) A.C. 578 at p. 627 freedom of discussion means "'freedom governed by law'" he says at p. 107 D.L.R., p. 133 S.C.R.

Precedent	He deduces authority to protect it from the principle that the "powers requisite for the preservation of the Constitution arise by a necessary implication of the Confederation Act as a whole."
Precedent	(Per <NAME> City of Quebec (1953) 4 D.L.R.
Precedent	641 at 671).
RatioOfTheDecision	(Emphasis supplied)  267.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is, however, noteworthy that the Solicitor-General appearing on behalf of the  <ORG> conceded that implications can arise from a Constitution, but said that no implication necessarily arises out of the provisions of Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	268.
Precedent	I may now refer to another decision of <ORG> in <NAME>'s case, [1967] 1 A.C. 259 which was relied on by Mr. <NAME> to show that an amendment of the Constitution cannot be held to be void on the ground of repugnancy to some vague ground of inconsistency with the preamble.
Precedent	269.
Precedent	<ORG> effected various modifications of the Criminal Procedure Code by the Criminal Law (Special Provisions) Act, 1962.
Precedent	The appellants were convicted by <ORG> for various offences like conspiring to wage war against the Queen, etc.
Precedent	270.
Precedent	The two relevant arguments were:  The first is that <ORG> is limited by an inability to pass legislation which is contrary to fundamental principles of justice.
Precedent	The 1962 Acts, it is said, are contrary to such principles in that they not only are directed against ind ividuals but also ex post facto create crimes and punishment, and destroy fair safeguards by which those individuals would otherwise be protected.
Precedent	The appellants' second contention is that the 1962 Acts offended against the Constitution in that they amounted to a direction to convict the appellants or to a legislative plan to secure the conviction and severe punishment of the appellants and thus constituted an unjustifiable assumption of judicial power by the legislature, or an interference with judicial power, which is outside the legislature's competence and is inconsistent with the severance of power between legislature, executive, and judiciary which the Constitution ordains.
Precedent	271.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> relies on the answer to the first contention.
Precedent	According to Mr. <NAME>, the answer shows that constituent power is different from legislative power and when constituent power is given, it is exhaustive leaving nothing uncovered.
Precedent	272.
Precedent	<ORG> after referring to passages from "The Sovereignty of the B ritish Dominions" by Prof. <NAME>, and "The Statutes of Westminster and Dominion Status" by <NAME>, observed at page 284:  Their Lordships cannot accept the view that the legislature while removing the fetter of repugnance to English law, left in existence a fetter of repugnance to some vague unspecified law of natural justice.
RatioOfTheDecision	The terms of the Colonial Laws Validity Act and especially the words "but not otherwise" in Section 2 make it clear that <ORG> was intending to deal with the whole question of repugnancy....  273.

The Queen (1964) A.C.  900)  274.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> sought to argue from this that similarly the amending power of <ORG> under Article 368 has no limitations and cannot be limited by some vague doctrine of repugnancy to natural and inalienable rights and the Preamble.
Precedent	We are unable to appreciate that any analogy exists between Mr. <NAME>'s argument and the argument of Mr. <NAME>.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> relies on the Preamble and the scheme of the Constitution to interpret Article 368 and limit its operation within the contours of the Preamble.
Precedent	The Preamble of the Constitution of India does not seem to prescribe any vague doctrines like the law of natural justice even if the latter, contrary to many decisions of our <ORG>, be considered vague.
Precedent	275.
Precedent	The case, however, furnishes another instance where implied limitations were inferred.
Precedent	After referring to the provisions dealing with "judicature" and the Judges, the <ORG> observed:  These provisions manifest an intention to secure in the judiciary a freedom from political, legislative and  executive control.
Precedent	They are wholly appropriate in a Constitution which intends that judicial power shall be vested only in the judicature.
Precedent	They would be inappropriate in a Constitution by which it was intended that judicial power should be shared by the executive or the legislature.
Precedent	The Constitution's silence as to the vesting of judicial power is consistent with its remaining, where it had lain for more than a century, in the hands of the judicature.
Precedent	It is not consistent with any intention that hence-forth it should pass to or be shared by, the executive or the legislature.
Precedent	276.
Precedent	<ORG> was of the view that there "exists a separate power in the judicature which under the Constitution as it stands cannot be usurped or infringed by the executive or the legislature."
Precedent	<ORG> cut down the plain words of Section 29(1) thus:  277. "
Precedent	Section 29(1) of the Constitution says:  Subject to the provisions of this Order <ORG> shall have power to make laws for the peace order and good go vernment of the Island".
Precedent	These words have habitually been construed in their fullest scope.
Precedent	Section 29(4) provides that <ORG> may amend the Constitution on a two-thirds majority with a certificate of the Speaker.
Precedent	Their Lordships however cannot read the words of Section 29(1) as entitling <ORG> to pass legislation which usurps the judicial power of the judicature-e.g., by passing an Act of attainder against some person or instructing a judge to bring in a verdict of guilty against someone who is being tried-if in law such usurpation would otherwise be contrary to the Constitution.
Precedent	278.
Precedent	In conclusion <ORG> held that there was interference with the functions of the judiciary and it was not only the likely but the intended effect of the impugned enactments, and that was fatal to their validity.
Precedent	279.
Precedent	Their Lordships uttered a warning which must always be borne in dealing with Constitutional cases : "what is done once, if it be allowed, may be done again and in a lesser crisis and less  serious circumstances.
Precedent	And thus judicial power may be eroded.
Precedent	Such an erosion is contrary to the clear intention of the Constitution."
Precedent	This was in reply to the argument that the <ORG> had no such general intention to absorb judicial powers and it had pased the legislation because it was beset by a grave situation and it took grave measures to deal with it, thinking, one must presume, that it had power to do so and was acting rightly.
Precedent	According to their Lordships that consideration was irrelevant and gave no validity to acts which infringed the Constitution.
Precedent	280.
Precedent	<NAME> v. The King [1920] A.C. 691 was strongly relied on by Mr. <NAME>.
Precedent	The case was on appeal from the decision of <ORG>, reported in 26 C.L.R.
Precedent	9.
Precedent	Apart from the questions of interpretation of Sub-section (6), Section 6 , of the Industrial Arbitration Act , 1916 and the construction of the <ORG> which was issued, the main question that was debated before <ORG> and the <ORG> was whether the <ORG> of  Queensland could amend a provision of the Constitution of Queensland without enacting a legislative enactment directly amending the Constitution.
Precedent	The respondents before the Board had contended as follows:  But an alteration to be valid must be made by direct legislative enactment.
Precedent	The Constitution can be altered but cannot be disregarded.
Precedent	So long as it subsists it is the test of the validity of legislation.
Precedent	<ORG> so decided in <NAME>'s case [1907] 4 C.L.R.
Precedent	1304.
Precedent	281.
Precedent	The appellants, on the other hand, had contended that "<ORG> has power, by ordinary enactment passed by both houses and assented to by the Governor in the name of the <ORG>, to alter the Constitution of Queensland, including the judicial institutions of the <ORG>, and the tenure of the judges.... All the laws applying to Queensland which it is competent to <ORG> to alter can be altered in the same manner by ordinary enactment.
Precedent	282.
Precedent	There was difference of opinion in <ORG> <NAME>, <NAME>, was of the opinion that <ORG> could not merely by enacting a law inconsistent with the Constitution Act of 1867 overrule its provisions, although it might be proper formality pass an Act which expressly altered or repealed it.
Precedent	<NAME> and <NAME>, with whom the <ORG> found themselves in almost complete agreement, held to the contrary.
Precedent	The <ORG>, in dealing with the question, first referred to the "distinction between Constitutions the terms of which may be modified or repealled with no other formality than is necessary in the case of other legislation, and Constitutions which can only be altered with some special formality, and in some cases by a specially convened assembly.
Precedent	283.
Precedent	Then Lord Birkenhead, L.C., observed at page 704:  Many different terms have been employed in the text-books to distinguish these two constrasted forms of Constitution.
Precedent	Their special qualities may perhaps be exhibited as clearly by calling the one a controlled and the other an uncontrolled  Constitution as by any other nomenclature.
Precedent	Nor is a Constitution debarred from being reckoned as an uncontrolled Constitution because it is not, like the British Constitution, constituted by historic development but finds its genesis in an originating document which may contain some conditions which cannot be altered except by the power which gave it birth.
Precedent	It is of the greatest importance to notice that where the Constitution is uncontrolled the consequences of its freedom admit of no qualification whatever.
Precedent	The doctrine is carried to every proper consequence with logical and inexorable precision, Thus when one of the learned Judges in the Court below said that, according to the appellant, the Constitution could be ignored as if it were a Dog Act, he was in effect merely expressing his opinion that the Constitution was, in fact, controlled.
Precedent	If it were uncontrolled, it would be an elementary commonplace that in the eye of the law the legislative document or documents which defined it occupied precisely the s ame position as a Dog Act or any other Act, however humble its subject-matter.
Precedent	284.
Precedent	Then, <ORG> proceeded to deal with the Constitution of Queensland and held that it was an uncontrolled Constitution.
Precedent	Later, their Lordships observed: It was not the policy of <ORG>, at any relevant period, to shackle or control in the manner suggested the legislative powers of the nascent Australian Legislatures.
Precedent	Consistently with the genius of the British people what was given was given completely, and unequivocally, in the belief fully justified by the event, that these young communities would successfully work out their own Constitutional salvation.
Precedent	285.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> sought to deduce the following propositions from this case: Firstly-(1) Unless there is a special procedure prescribed for amending any part of the Constitution, the Constitution was uncontrolled and could be amended by an Act in the manner prescribed for enacting ordinary laws, and therefore, a subsequent law inconsistent w ith the Constitution would pro tanto repeal the Constitution;  Secondly-(2) A Constitution largely or generally uncontrolled may contain one or more provisions which prescribe a different procedure for amending them than is prescribed for amending an ordinary law, in which case an ordinary law cannot amend them and the procedure must be strictly followed if the amendment is to be effected;  Thirdly-(3) Implications of limitation of power ought not be imported from general concepts but only from express or necessarily implied limitations (i.e.
Precedent	implied limitation without which a Constitution cannot be worked); and Fourthly-(4) The British Parliament in granting the colonial legislatures power of legislation as far back as 1865- Section 2-refused to put limitations of vague character, like general principles of law, but limited those limitations to objective standards like statutes and provisions of any Act of Parliament or order or regulation made under the Acts of Parliament.
Precedent	286.
Precedent	I agree that the first and t he second propositions are deducible from <NAME>'s case but I am unable_to agree with the learned Counsel that the third proposition enunciated by him emerges from the case.
Precedent	The only implied limitation which was urged by the learned Counsel for the respondents was that the Queensland legislature should first directly amend the Constitution and then pass an act which would otherwise have been inconsistent if the Constitution had not been amended.
Precedent	It appears from the judgment of <NAME>, J., and the <ORG> that two South Australia Judges had earlier held that the legislation must be "with the object of altering the Constitution of the legislature".
Precedent	Lord <NAME>, when Sir <NAME>, and Sir <NAME> expressed dissent from their view and recommended the enactment of a statute like the Colonial Laws Validity Act, 1865.
Precedent	287.
Precedent	The fourth proposition states a fact.
Precedent	The fact that British <ORG> in 1865 refused to put so called vague limitations does not assist us in deciding whether there cannot be imp lied limitations on the amending power under Article 368.
Precedent	288.
Precedent	I shall examine a little later more cases in which limitations on lawmaking power have been implied both in Australia, U.S.A., and in Canada.
Precedent	<NAME>'s case is authority only for the proposition that if the Constitution is uncontrolled then it is not necessary for the legislature to pass an act labelling it as an amendment of the Constitution; it can amend the Constitution like any other act.
Precedent	289.
Precedent	Attorney-General for New South Wales v. <NAME> [1932] A.C. 526 was concerned really with the interpretation of Section 5 of the Colonial Laws Validity Act, 1865, and its impact on the powers of the legislature of the New South Wales.
Statute	The Constitution Act , 1902, as amended in 1929, had inserted Section 7A , the relevant part of which reads as follows:  7A.-(1) <ORG> shall not be abolished nor, subject to the provisions of Sub- section 6 of this section, shall its Constitution or powers be altered except in the manner provided in  this section.
Statute	(2) A Bill for any purpose within Sub- section 1 of this section shall not be presented to the Governor for His Majesty's assent until the Bill has been approved by the electors in accordance with this section.
Statute	(5) If a majority of the electors voting approve the Bill, it shall be presented to the Governor for His Majesty's assent.
Statute	(6) The provisions of this section shall extend to any Bill for the repeal or amendment of this section, but shall not apply to any Bill for the repeal or amendment of any of the following sections of this Act, namely, Sections 13 , 14 , 15 , 18 , 19 , 20 , 21 and 22 .
Statute	290.
Statute	Towards the end of 1930 two bills were passed by both Houses of the New South Wales legislature.
Statute	The first Bill enacted that Section 7A above referred to was repealed, and the second Bill enacted by Clause 2, Sub- section 1 .
Statute	"<ORG> is abolished.
Precedent	291.
Precedent	The contentions advanced before <ORG> were: The appellants urge : (1) That the King, with the  advice and consent of <ORG> and <ORG>, had full power to enact a Bill repealing Section 7A .
Precedent	(2) That Sub-section 6 of Section 7A of the Constitution Act is void, because : (a) <ORG> has no power to shackle or control its successors, the New South Wales Constitution being in substance an uncontrolled "Constitution"; (b) It is repugnant to Section 4 of the Constitution Statute of 1855; (c) It is repugnant to Section 5 of the Colonial Laws Validity Act, 1865.
Precedent	For the respondents it was contended : (1) That Section 7A was a valid amendment of the Constitution of New South Wales, validly enacted in the manner prescribed, and was legally binding in New South Wales.
Precedent	(2) That the legislature of New South Wales was given by Imperial statutes plenary power to alter the Constitution, powers and procedure of such legislature.
