Precedent	(3) That when once the legislature had altered either the Constitution or powers and procedure, then the Constitution and powers a nd procedure as they previously existed ceased to exist, and were replaced by the new Constitution and powers.
Precedent	(4) That the only possible limitations of this plenary power were : (a) it must be exercised according to the manner and form prescribed by any Imperial or colonial law, and (b) the legislature must continue a representative legislature according to the definition of the Colonial Laws Validity Act, 1865.
Precedent	(5) That the addition of Section 7A to the Constitution had the effect of :  (a) making the legislative body consist thereafter of the King, <ORG>, the <ORG> and the people for the purpose of the Constitutional enactments therein described, or (b) imposing a manner and form of legislation in reference to these Constitutional enactments which thereafter became binding ton the legislature by virtue of the colonial Laws Validity Act, 1865, until repealed in the manner and mode prescribed.
Precedent	(6) That the power of altering the Constitution conferred by Section 4 of the Constitution S tatute, 1855, must be read subject to the Colonial Laws Validity Act, 1865, and that in particular the limitation as to manner and form prescribed by the 1865 Act must be governed by subsequent amendments to the Constitution, whether purporting to be made in the earlier Act or not.
Precedent	292.
Precedent	<ORG> considered the meaning and effect of Section 5 of the Act of 1865, read in conjunction with Section 4 of the Constitution Statute.
Statute	It is necessary to bear in mind the relevant part of Section 5 which reads as follows: Section 5 .
Statute	Every colonial legislature...and every representative legislature shall, in respect to the colony under its jurisdiction, have, and be deemed at all times to have had, full power to make laws respecting the Constitution, powers, and procedure of such legislature; provided that such laws shall have been passed in such manner and form as may from time to time be required by any Act of Parliament, letters patent, Order in Council, or colonial law, for the time being in force in th e said colony.
Precedent	293.
Precedent	<ORG> interpreted Section 5 as follows: Reading the section as a whole, it gives to the legislatures of New South Wales certain powers, subject to this, that in respect of certain laws they can only become effectual provided they have been passed in such manner and form as may from time to time be required by any Act still on the statute book.
Precedent	Beyond that, the words "manner and form" are amply wide enough to cover an enactment providing that a Bill is to be submitted to the electors and that unless and until a majority of the electors voting approve the Bill it shall not be presented to the Governor for His Majesty's assent.
Precedent	294.
Precedent	<ORG> first raised the question : "could that Bill, a repealing Bill, after its passage through both chambers, be lawfully presented for the Royal assent without having first received the approval of the electors in the prescribed manner ?
Precedent	", and answered it thus:  In their Lordships' opinion, the Bill could not lawfully be so pr esented.
Precedent	The proviso in the second sentence of Section 5 of the Act of 1865 states a condition which must be fulfilled before the legislature can validly exercise its power to make the kind of laws which are referred to in that sentence.
Precedent	In order that Section 7A may be repealed (in other words, in order that that particular law "respecting the Constitution, powers and procedure" of the legislature may be validly made) the law for that purpose must have been passed in the manner required by Section 7A , a colonial law for the time being in force in New South Wales.
Precedent	295.
RatioOfTheDecision	This case has no direct relevance to any of the points raised before us.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no doubt that in the case before us, the impugned Constitutional amendments have been passed according to the form and manner prescribed by Article 368 of our Constitution.
Precedent	It is, however, noteworthy that in contention No.
Precedent	(4), mentioned above, it was urged that notwithstanding the plenary powers conferred on the <ORG> a possible limitation was that the leg islature must continue a representative legislature according to the definition of the Colonial Laws Validity Act 1865.
Precedent	This is another illustration of a limitation implied on amending power.
Precedent	296.
Precedent	I may also refer to some of the instances of implied limitations which have been judicially accepted in the United States.
Precedent	It would suffice if I refer to <NAME> on Constitutional Limitations and Constitution of the United States of America edited by <NAME> (1952).
Precedent	297.
Precedent	After mentioning express limitations, imposed by the Constitution upon the Federal power to tax, <NAME> on 'Constitutional Limitations' (page 989) states: ...but there are some others which are implied, and which under the complex system of American government have the effect to exempt some subjects otherwise taxable from the scope and reach, according to circumstances, of either the Federal power to tax or the power of the several States.
Precedent	One of the implied limitations is that which precludes the States from taxing the agencies whereby the general  government performs its functions.
Precedent	The reason is that, if they possessed this authority, it would be within their power to impose taxation to an extent that might cripple, if not wholly defeat, the operations of the national authority within its proper and Constitutional sphere of action.
Precedent	298.
Precedent	Then he cites the passage from the Chief Justice <NAME> in <NAME> v. Maryland.
Precedent	4 L. ed.
Precedent	579; 607.
Precedent	299.
Precedent	In "Constitution by the United States of America" by <NAME> (1952)-page 728-729 it is stated:  Five years after the decision in <NAME> v. Maryland that a State may not tax an instrumentality of <ORG>, the Court was asked to and did re-examine the entire question in <NAME> v. <ORG>.
Precedent	In that case counsel for the State of Ohio, whose attempt to tax the <ORG> was challenged, put forward the arguments of great importance.
Precedent	In the first place it was "contended, that, admitting <ORG> to possess the power, this exemption ought to have been expressly assented in the act of incorpo ration; and not being expressed, ought not to be implied by the <ORG>."
Precedent	To which <NAME> replied that : "It is no unusual thing for an act of <ORG> to imply, without expressing, this very exemption from state control, which is said to be so objectionable in this instance.
Precedent	Secondly the appellants relied greatly on the distinction between the bank and the public institutions, such as the mint or the post-office.
Precedent	The agents in those offices are, it is said, officers of <ORG>, * * * Not so the directors of the bank.
Precedent	The connection of the government with the bank, is likened to that with contractOrs."
Precedent	<NAME> accepted this analogy, but not to the advantage of the appellants.
Precedent	He simply indicated that all contractors who dealt with the <ORG> were entitled to immunity from taxation upon such transactions.
Precedent	Thus not only was the decision of <NAME> v. Maryland reaffirmed but the foundation was laid for the vast expansion of the principle of immunity that was to follow in the succeeding decades.
Precedent	300.
Precedent	W e need not examine the exact extent of the doctrine at the present day in the United <ORG> because the only purpose in citing these instances is to refute the argument of the respondents that there cannot be anything like implied limitations.
Precedent	301.
RatioOfTheDecision	The position is given at p. 731, as it existed in 1952, when the book was written.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> sums up the position broadly at p. 736:  Broadly speaking, the immunity which remains is limited to activities of the Government itself, and to that which is explicitly created by statute, e.g.
RatioOfTheDecision	that granted to federal securities and to fiscal institutions chartered by <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	But the term, activities, will be broadly construed.
RatioOfTheDecision	302.
RatioOfTheDecision	Regarding the taxation of <ORG>, <NAME> says at pp.
RatioOfTheDecision	995-997: If the <ORG> cannot tax the means by which the national government performs its functions, neither, on the other hand and for the same reasons, can the latter tax the agencies of the State governments.
RatioOfTheDecision	"The same supreme power which established the departments of the general governme nt determined that the local governments should also exist for their own purposes, and made it impossible to protect the people in their common interest without them.
RatioOfTheDecision	Each of these several agencies is confined to its own sphere, and all are strictly subordinate to the Constitution which limits them, and independent of other agencies, except as thereby made dependent There is nothing in the Constitution of the United <ORG>s which can be made to admit of any interference by <ORG> with the secure existence of any <ORG> authority within its lawful bounds.
RatioOfTheDecision	And any such interference by the indirect means of taxation is quite as much beyond the power of the national legislature as if the interference were direct and extreme.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has, therefore, been held that the law of <ORG> requiring judicial process to be stamped could not Constitutionally be applied to the process of the <ORG> courts; since otherwise <ORG> might impose such restrictions upon the <ORG> courts as would put and end to their effective action , and be equivalent practically to abolishing them altogether.
RatioOfTheDecision	And a similar ruling has been made in other analogous cases.
Precedent	But "the exemption of <ORG> agencies and instrumentalities from national taxation is limited to those which are of a strictly governmental character, and does not extend to those which are used by the <ORG> in the carrying on of an ordinary private business.
Precedent	303.
Precedent	I may mention that what has been implied in the United <ORG>s is the subject-matter of express provisions under our Constitution (see Articles 285, 287, 288 and 289).
Precedent	304.
Precedent	It was urged before us that none of these cases dealt with implied limitations on the amending power.
Precedent	It seems to me that four cases are directly in point.
Precedent	I have referred already to:  1.
Precedent	The Bribery Commissioner v. <NAME> [1965] A.C. 172.
Precedent	2.
Precedent	<NAME> v. <ORG> [1967] 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	109-112.
Precedent	3.
Precedent	<NAME> v. The Attorney-General of Queensland 23 C.L.R.
Precedent	457 and I will be discussing shortly In re The Initiative and Referendum Act [1919] A.C.  9 35.
Precedent	305.
Precedent	What is the necessary implication from all the provisions of the Constitution ?
Precedent	306.
Precedent	It seems to me that reading the Preamble, the fundamental importance of the freedom of the individual, indeed its inalienability, and the importance of the economic, social and political justice mentioned in the Preamble, the importance of directive principles, the non-inclusion in Article 368 of provisions like Articles 52, 53 and various other provisions to which reference has already been made an irresistible conclusion emerges that it was not the intention to use the word "amendment" in the widest sense.
Precedent	307.
Precedent	It was the common understanding that fundamental rights would remain in substance as they are and they would not be amended out of existence.
Precedent	It seems also to have been a common understanding that the fundamental features of the Constitution, namely, secularism, democracy and the freedom of the individual would always subsist in the welfare state.
Precedent	308.
Precedent	In view of the above reasons, a necessary implicatio n arises that there are implied limitations on the power of <ORG> that the expression "amendment of this Constitution" has consequently a limited meaning in our Constitution and not the meaning suggested by the respondents.
Precedent	309.
Precedent	This conclusion is reinforced if I consider the consequences of the contentions of both sides.
Precedent	The respondents, who appeal fervently to democratic principles, urge that there is ho limit to the powers of <ORG> to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	Article 368 can itself be amended to make the Constitution completely flexible or extremely rigid and unamendable.
Precedent	If this is so, a political party with a two-third majority in <ORG> for a few years could so amend the Constitution as to debar any other party from functioning, establish totalitarianism, enslave the people, and after having effected these purposes make the Constitution unamcndable or extremely rigid.
Precedent	This would no doubt invite extra- Constitutional revolution.
Precedent	Therefore, the appeal by the respondents to democratic princip les and the necessity of having absolute amending power to prevent a revolution to buttress their contention is rather fruitless, because if their contention is accepted the very democratic principles, which they appeal to, would disappear and a revolution would also become a possibility.
Precedent	310.
Precedent	However, if the meaning I have suggested is accepted a social and economic revolution can gradually take place while preserving the freedom and dignity of every citizen.
Precedent	311.
Precedent	For the aforesaid reasons, I am driven to the conclusion that the expression "amendment of this Constitution" in Article 368 means any addition or change in any of the provisions of the Constitution within the broad contours of the Preamble and the Constitution to carry out the objectives in the Preamble and the Directive Principles.
Precedent	Applied to fundamental rights, it would mean that, while fundamental rights cannot be abrogated reasonable abridgements of fundamental rights can be effected in the public interest.
Precedent	312.
Fact	It is of course for Parliame nt to decide whether an amendment is necessary.
Fact	The <ORG> will not be concerned with wisdom of the amendment.
Precedent	313.
Precedent	If this meaning is given it would enable <ORG> to adjust fundamental rights in order to secure what the Directive Principles direct to be accomplished, while maintaining the freedom and dignity of every citizen.
Precedent	314.
Precedent	It is urged by Mr. <NAME> that we would be laying down a very unsatisfactory test which it would be difficult for the <ORG> to comprehend and follow.
Precedent	He said that the Constitution-makers had discarded the concept of "due process" in order to have something certain, and they substituted the words "by authority of law" in Article 21.
Precedent	I am unable to see what bearing the dropping of the words "due process" has on this question.
Precedent	The Constitution itself has used words like "reasonable restrictions" in Article 19 which do not bear an exact meaning, and which cannot be defined with precision to fit in all cases that may come before the courts; it would depend upon the facts of  each case whether the restrictions imposed by the <ORG> are reasonable or not.
Precedent	Further, as Lord <NAME> observed in Ridge v. Baldwin  A.C. 40; 64-65:  In modern times opinions have sometimes been expressed to the effect that natural justice is so vague as to be practically meaningless.
Precedent	But I would regard these as tainted by the perennial fallacy that because something cannot be cut and dried or nicely weighed or measured therefore it does not exist.
Precedent	The idea of negligence is equally insusceptible of exact definition, but what a reasonable man would regard as fair procedure in particular circumstances and what he would regard as negligence in particular circumstances are equally capable of serving as tests in law, and natural justice as it has been interpreted in the courts is much more definite than that.
Precedent	(emphasis supplied)  315.
Precedent	It seems to me that the concept of amendment within the contours of the Preamble and the Constitution cannot be said to be a vague and unsatisfactory idea which Parliamentarians  and the public would not be able to understand.
Precedent	316.
Precedent	The learned Attorney-General said that every provision of the Constitution is essential; otherwise it would not have been put in the Constitution.
Precedent	This is true.
Precedent	But this does not place every provision of the Constitution in the same position.
Precedent	The true position is that every provision of the Constitution can be amended provided in the result the basic foundation and structure of the Constitution remains the same.
Precedent	The basic structure may be said to consist of the following features:  (1) Supremacy of the Constitution;  (2) Republican and Democratic form of Government.
Precedent	(3) Secular character of the Constitution;  (4) Separation of powers between the <ORG>, the executive and the judiciary;  (5) Federal character of the Constitution.
Precedent	317.
Precedent	The above structure is built on the basic foundation, i.e., the dignity and freedom of the individual.
Precedent	This is of supreme importance.
Precedent	This cannot by any form of amendment be destroyed.
Precedent	318.
Precedent	The above foundation and th e above basic features are easily discernible not only from the preamble but the whole scheme of the Constitution, which I have already discussed.
Precedent	319.
Precedent	In connection with the question of abrogation of fundamental rights, Mr. <NAME> boldly asserted that there was no such thing as natural or inalienable rights because the scheme of Part III itself shows that non-citizens have not been given all the fundamental freedoms; for example, Article 19 speaks of only citizens.
Precedent	He says that if there were natural rights, why is it that they were not conferred on non-citizens.
Precedent	The answer seems to be that they are natural rights but our country does not think it expedient to confer these fundamental rights, mentioned in Article 19 , on non-citizens.
Precedent	Other rights have been conferred on non-citizens because the Constitution-makers thought that it would not be detrimental to the interests of the country to do so.
Precedent	320.
Precedent	He then said that even as far as citizens are concerned, there is power to modify those rights under Articl e 33 of the Constitution, which enables <ORG> to modify rights in their application to <ORG>.
Precedent	This power has been reserved in order to maintain discipline among the armed forces, which is essential for the security of the country.
Precedent	But it does not mean that the rights cease to be natural or human rights.
Precedent	He then said that similarly Article 34 restricts fundamental rights while martial law is in force in any area.
Precedent	This again is a case where the security of the country is the main consideration.
Precedent	Citizens have to undergo many restrictions in the interest of the country.
Precedent	321.
Precedent	He then pointed out Articles 358 and 359 where certain rights are suspended during Emergency.
Precedent	These provisions are again based on the security of the country.
Precedent	322.
Precedent	He also relied on the words "rights conferred" in Article 13(2) and "enforcement of any rights conferred by this Part" to show that they were not natural or inalienable and could not have been claimed by them.
Precedent	There is no question of the sovereign people claiming  them from an outside agency.
Precedent	The people acting through <ORG> desired that the rights mentioned in Part III shall be guaranteed and, therefore, Part III was enacted.
Precedent	In the context 'conferred' does not mean that some superior power had granted these rights.
Precedent	It is very much like a King bestowing the title of 'His Imperial Majesty on himself.
Precedent	323.
Precedent	I am unable to hold that these provisions show that some rights are not natural or inalienable rights.
Precedent	As a matter of fact, India was a party to the Universal Declaration of Rights which I have already referred to and that Declaration describes some fundamenal rights as inalienable.
Precedent	324.
Precedent	Various decisions of this <ORG> describe fundamental rights as 'natural rights' or 'human rights'.
Precedent	Some of these decisions are extracted bellow.
Precedent	There can be no doubt that the people of India have in exercise of their sovereign will as expressed in the Preamble, adopted the democratic ideal, which assures to the citizen the dignity of the individual and other cheri shed human values as a means to the full evolution and expression of his personality, and in delegating to the legislature, the executive and the judiciary their respective powers in the Constitution, reserved to themselves certain fundamental rights so-called, I apprehend, because they have been retained by the people and made paramount to the delegated powers, as in the American Model."
Precedent	(Per <NAME>, J., in <CITATION_1857950>  S.C.R.
Precedent	88; 198 199).
Precedent	(emphasis supplied)  (ii) "That article ( Article 19 ) enumerates certain freedoms under the caption "right to freedom" and deals with those great and basic rights which are recognised and guaranteed as the natural rights inherent in the status of a citizen of a free country."
Precedent	(Per <NAME>, C.J., in <CITATION_973363>  S.C.R.
Precedent	587; 596).
Precedent	(emphasis supplied) I have no doubt that the framers of our Constitution drew the same distinction and classed the natural rights or capacity of a citizen 'to acquire, hold and dispose of property' with other nat ural rights and freedoms inherent in the status of a free citizen and embodied them in Article 19(1)....   (emphasis supplied) For all these reasons, I am of opinion that under the scheme of the Constitution, all those broad and basic freedoms inherent in the status of a citizen as a free man are embodied and protected from invasion by the <ORG> under Clause (1) of Article 19....   (emphasis supplied)  (iii) "The people, however, regard certain rights as paramount, because they embrace liberty of action to the individual in matters of private life, social intercourse and share in the Government of the country and other spheres.
Precedent	The people who vested the three limbs of Government with their power and authority, at the same time kept back these rights of citizens and also sometimes of noncitizens, and made them inviolable except under certain conditions.
Precedent	The rights thus kept back are placed in Part III of the Constitution, which is headed 'Fundamental Rights', and the conditions under which these rights can be  abridged are also indicated in that Part."
Precedent	(Per <NAME>, in <NAME> v. <ORG> of U.P.
Precedent	[1963] 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	778; 926-7)).
Precedent	(emphasis supplied) <ORG> has described them as follows:  (iv) "...man has certain natural or inalienable rights and that it is the function of the <ORG>, in order that human liberty might be preserved and human personality developed, to give recognition and free play to those rights...  Suffice it to say that they represent a trend in the democratic thought of our age."
Precedent	(<NAME> v. <ORG> of U.P.
Precedent	I.L.R.
Precedent	[1951] 1 All.
Precedent	269; 387-8.).
Precedent	(emphasis supplied)  325.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> relied on the observations of <NAME>, in <CITATION_761967> .
Precedent	Supp.
Precedent	(1) S.C.R.
Precedent	528; 605:  I am of the view that the doctrine of 'natural rights' affords nothing but a foundation of shifting sand for building up a thesis that the doctrine of waiver does not apply to the rights guaranteed in Part III of our Constitution.
Precedent	326.
Precedent	I must point out that the learned Judge was expressing the minority opi nion that there could be a waiver of fundamental rights in certain circumstances.
Precedent	<NAME>, <NAME>, and <NAME>, J., held that there could be no waiver of fundamental rights founded on Article 14 of the Constitution, while <NAME> and <NAME>, JJ.
Precedent	held that there could be no waiver not only of fundamental rights enshrined in Article 14 but also of any other fundamental rights guaranteed by Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	327.
Precedent	Article 14 has been described variously as follows:  (1) "as the basic principle of republicanism" (per Patanjali Sastri <NAME> in <CITATION_184660633>  S.C.R.
Precedent	284, 293.)
Precedent	(2) "as a principle of republicanism" (per <NAME>, J., Ibid.
Precedent	p. 313) (3) "as founded on a sound public policy recognised and valued in all civilized States" (per <NAME> <NAME>, : <CITATION_761967> .
Precedent	Supp.
Precedent	(1) S.C.R.
Precedent	528, 551.)
Precedent	(4) "as a necessary corollary to the high concept of the rule of law" (per <NAME>, <NAME>, in <CITATION_1747577>  3 S.C.R.
Precedent	525; 542.)
Precedent	(5) "as a vital principle of republican institutions" (American Jurisprud ence, Vol.
Precedent	16, 2d.
Precedent	p. 731, Article 391 )  328.
Precedent	How would this test be operative vis-a-vis the Constitutional amendments made hitherto ?
Precedent	It seems to me that the amendments made by the Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951, in Articles 15 and 19, and insertion of Article 31A (apart from the question whether there was delegation of the power to amend the Constitution , and apart from the question as to abrogation ), and the amendment made by the Constitution (Fourth Amendment) Act in Article 31(2 ), would be within the amending power of <ORG> under Article 368.
Precedent	329.
Precedent	Reference may be made to <CITATION_713534>  1 S.C.C.
Precedent	536; 546 where this Court repelled the argument of the learned Counsel that the amendments made to Sections 26 and 27 of the Constitution of Jammu and Kashmir were bad because they destroyed the structure of the Constitution.
Argument	The arguments of the learned Counsel was that fundamentals of the Jammu and Kashmir State Constitution had been destroyed.
Argument	This argument was refuted in the following  words: But the passage cited by him can hardly be availed of by him for the reason that the amendment impugned by him, in the light of what we have already stated about the nature of the explanation to Article 370 of our Constitution, does not bring about any alteration either in the framework or the fundamentals of the Jammu and Kashmir Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The State Governor still continues to be the head of the Government aided by a council of ministers and the only change affected is in his designation and the mode of his appointment.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is not as if <ORG>, by such a change, is made irresponsible to <ORG>, or its fundamental character as a responsible Government is altered.
RatioOfTheDecision	Just as a change in the designation of the head of that Government was earlier brought about by the introduction of the office of <NAME>, so too a change had been brought about in his designation from that of <NAME> to the Governor.
Precedent	That was necessitated by reason of the Governor having been s ubstituted in place of <NAME>.
Precedent	There is no question of such a change being one in the character of that Government from a democratic to a non-democratic system.
Precedent	330.
Precedent	Before parting with this topic I may deal with some other arguments addressed to us.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> devoted a considerable time in expounding principles of construction of statutes, including the Constitution.
Precedent	I do not think it is necessary to review the decisions relating to the principles of interpretation of legislative entries in Article 245 and Article 246 of the Constitution.
Precedent	<ORG> and this <ORG> in this connection have followed the principles enunciated by <ORG> in interpreting Sections 91 and 92 of the Canadian Constitution.
Precedent	I have no quarrel with these propositions but I am unable to see that these propositions have any bearing on the interpretation of Article  368.
Precedent	The fact that legislative entries are given wide interpretation has no relevance to the interpretation of Article 368.
Precedent	The second set of c ases referred to deal with the question whether it is legitimate to consider consequences of a particular construction.
Precedent	331.
Precedent	He referred to <ORG> v. <ORG> [1913] A.C. 107; 117-118.
Precedent	This decision does not support him in the proposition that consequences of a particular construction cannot be considered, for Lord <NAME> observed at p. 117: Now it is "the universal rule," as Lord <NAME> observed in <NAME> v. <NAME> [1857] 6 H.L.C.
Precedent	61; 106 that in construing statutes, as in construing all other written instruments "the grammatical and ordinary" sense of the words is to be adhered to, unless that would lead to some absurdity, or some repugnance or inconsistency with the rest of the instrument, in which case the grammatical and ordinary sense of the words may be modified, so as to avoid that absurdity and inconsistency, but no further.
Precedent	332.
Precedent	Then he observed at p. 118:  In the absence of a preamble there can, I think, be only two cases in which it is permissible to depart fr om the ordinary and natural sense of the words of an enactment.
Precedent	It must be shown either that the words taken in their natural sense lead to some absurdity or that there is some other clause in the body of the Act inconsistent with, or repugnant to, the enactment in question construed in the ordinary sense of the language in which it is expressed.
Precedent	333.
Precedent	Lord <NAME> observed at pp.
Precedent	121-122:  It is no doubt well established that, in construing the words of a statute susceptible of more than one meaning, it is legitimate to consider the consequences which would result from any particular construction for, as there are many things which the <ORG> is presumed not to have intended to bring about, a construction which would not lead to any one of these things should be preferred to one which would lead to one or more of them.
Precedent	But, as Lord <NAME> laid down in <NAME> v. <ORG> [1901] A.C. 102 at p. 107, <ORG> has nothing to do with the reasonableness or unreasonableness of a provision  of a statute, except so far as it may help it in interpreting what the <ORG> has said.
Precedent	If the language of a statute be plain, admitting of only one meaning, the <ORG> must be taken to have meant and intended what it has plainly expressed, and whatever it has in clear terms enacted must be enforced though it should lead to absurd or mischievous results.
Precedent	If the language of this; sub-section be not controlled by some of the other provisions of the statute, it must, since its language is plain and unambiguous, be enforced, and your Lordship's <ORG> sitting judicially is not concerned with the question whether the policy it embodies is wise or unwise, or whether it leads to consequences just or unjust, beneficial or mischievous.
Precedent	334.
Precedent	The next case referred to is <ORG> v. <NAME> [1887] 12 A.C. 575; 586., but this case, is explained in Attorney-General for Alberta v. Attorney-General for Canada [1939] A.C. 117; 132; 133.
Precedent	<ORG> first observed: It was rightly contended on behalf  of the appellant that <ORG> and the <ORG> have no concern with the wisdom of the <ORG> whose Bill is attacked; and it was urged that it would be a dangerous precedent to allow the views of members of the Court as to the serious consequences of excessive taxation on banks to lead to a conclusion that the Bill is ultra vires.
Precedent	Their Lordships do not agree that this argument should prevail in a case where the taxation in a practical business sense is prohibitive.
Precedent	335.
Precedent	Then their Lordships made the following observations on the decision of <ORG> in <ORG> v. <NAME> [1887] 12 A.C. 575; 586:  That case seems to have occasioned a difficulty in the minds of some of the learned Judges in <ORG>.
Precedent	It must, however, be borne in mind that the Quebec Act in that case was attacked on two specific grounds, first, that the tax was not "taxation with the Province," and secondly, that the tax was not a "direct tax."
Precedent	It was never suggested, and there seems to have been no gro und for suggesting, that the Act was by its effect calculated to encroach upon the classes of matters exclusively within the <ORG> powers.
Precedent	Nor, on the other hand, was there any contention, however faint or tentative, that the purpose of the Act was anything other than the legitimate one of raising a revenue for Provincial needs....
Precedent	It was never laid down by the <ORG> that if such a use was attempted to be made of the Provincial power as materially to interfere with the <ORG> power, the action of the province would be infra vires.
Precedent	336.
Precedent	This case further shows that serious consequences can be taken into consideration.
Precedent	337.
Precedent	I agree with the observations of Lord <NAME> in <NAME> v. Judge of <ORG>, [1892] 1 Q.B.
Precedent	273-290 cited by him.
Precedent	These observations are: If the words of an Act are clear, you must follow them, even though they lead to a manifest absurdity.
Precedent	The Court has nothing to do with the question whether the legislature has committed an absurdity.
Precedent	In my opinion the rule has always been  this-if the words or an Act admit of two interpretations, then they are not clear; and if one interpretation leads to an absurdity, and the other does not, the <ORG> will conclude that the legislature did not intend to lead to an absurdity, and will adopt the other interpretation.
Precedent	338.
Precedent	He then relied on the observations of Lord <NAME>, M.R., in <NAME> v. <ORG> [1948] 1 Ch.
Precedent	145; 159:  There is one rule, I think, which is very clear-and this brings me back to where I started, the doctrine of absurdity-that although the absurdity or the non-absurdity of one conclusion as compared with another may be of assistance, and very often is of assistance, to the court in choosing between two possible meanings of ambiguous words, it is a doctrine which has to be applied with great care, remembering that judges may be fallible in this question of an absurdity, and in any event must not be applied so as to result in twisting language into a meaning which it cannot bear; it is a doctrine which  must not be relied upon and must not be used to re-write the language in a way different from that in which it was originally framed.
Precedent	Earlier, he had said at p. 158:  "Absurdity" I cannot help thinking, like public policy, is a very unruly horse...  339.
Precedent	As I read Lord <NAME>, what he meant to say was that "absurdity" was an unruly horse, but it can be of assistance, and very often is of assistance, in choosing between two possible meanings of ambiguous words, and this is exactly the use which this Court is entitled to make of the consequences which I have already mentioned.
Precedent	340.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> referred to <CITATION_1970738>  S.C.R.
Precedent	254, 264.
Precedent	<NAME>, J., observed:  We are in agreement with learned Counsel to this extent only that if the language of the article is plain and unambiguous and admits of only one meaning then the duty of the court is to adopt that meaning irrespective of the inconvenience that such a construction may produce.
Precedent	If however two constructions are possible, then the court must adopt that wh ich will ensure smooth and harmonious working of the Constitution and eschew the other which will lead to absurdity or give rise to practical inconvenience or make well established provisions of existing law nugatory.
Precedent	341.
Precedent	He also referred to the following passage in <CITATION_1560414> .
Precedent	1 S.C.R.
Precedent	896-899: It is one of the well established rules of construction that "if the words of a statute are in themselves precise and unambiguous no more is necessary than to expound those words in their natural and ordinary sense, the words themselves in such case best declaring the intention of the legislature."
Precedent	It is equally well settled principle of construction that "Where alternative constructions are equally open that alternative is to be chosen which will be consistent with the smooth working of the system which the statute purports to be regulating; and that alternative is to be rejected which will introduce uncertainty.
Precedent	342.
Precedent	What he urged before us, relying on the last two cases just referred to, was that if we  construed the word "amendment" in its narrow sense, then there would be uncertainty, friction and confusion in the working of the system, and we should therefore avoid the narrow sense.
Precedent	343.
Precedent	If <ORG> has power to pass the impugned amendment acts, there is no doubt that I have no right to question the wisdom of the policy of <ORG>.
Precedent	But if the net result of my interpretation is to prevent <ORG> from abrogating the fundamental rights, and the basic features outlined above, I am unable to appreciate that any uncertainty, friction or confusion will nesessarily result.
Precedent	344.
Precedent	He also drew our attention to the following observations of <NAME>, J. in <CITATION_1385217>  2 S.C.R.
Precedent	10; 15-16:  Before considering the meaning of the word "held", it is necessary to mention that it is proper to assume that the law-makers who are the representatives of the people enact laws which the society considers as honest, fair and equitable.
Precedent	The object of every legislation is to advance public welfare.
Precedent	In other words, a s observed by <NAME> in his book on Statutory Construction the entire legislative process is influenced by considerations of justice and reason.
Precedent	Justice and reason constitute the great general legislative intent in every piece of legislation.
Precedent	Consequently where the suggested construction operates harshly, ridiculously or in any other manner contrary to prevailing conceptions of justice and reason, in most instances, it would seem that the apparent or suggested meaning of the statute, was not the one intended by the law-makers.
Precedent	In the absence of some other indication that the harsh or ridiculous effect was actually intended by the legislature, there is little reason to believe that it represents the legislative intent.
Precedent	345.
Precedent	I am unable to appreciate how these observations assist the respondents.
Precedent	If anything, these observations are against them for when I come to the question of interpretation of the 25th amendment I may well approach the interpretation keeping those observations in mind.
Precedent	346.
Precedent	Both Mr. <NAME> <NAME> and the learned Attorney General have strongly relied on the decisions of <ORG>, <ORG> and <ORG> on the interpretation of Article V of the Constitution of the United <ORG>s and some <ORG> Constitution.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME>, on the other hand, relied on some <ORG> decisions in support of his submissions.
Precedent	347.
Precedent	Article V of the Constitution of the United <ORG>s differs greatly from Article 368 of our Constitution.
Precedent	For facility of reference Article V is reproduced below: The <ORG>, whenever two thirds of both <ORG> shall deem it necessary, shall propose amendments to this Constitution, or, on the application of the Legislatures of two thirds of several <ORG>s, shall call a convention for proposing amendments, which, in either case, shall be valid to all intents and purposes, as part of this Constitution, when ratified by the Legislatures of three fourths of the several <ORG>s, or by conventions in three fourths thereof, as the one or the other mode of ratification  may be proposed by the <ORG>; Provided that no amendment which may be made prior to the year one thousand eight hundred and eight shall in any manner affect the first and fourth clauses in the ninth section of the first article; and that no <ORG>, without its consent, shall be deprived of its equal suffrage in the <ORG>.
Precedent	348.
RatioOfTheDecision	It will be noticed that Article V provides for two steps to be taken for amending the Constitution.
Fact	The first step is proposal of an amendment and the second step is ratification of the proposal.
Precedent	The proposal can be made either by two thirds of both Houses of <ORG> or by a convention called by the <ORG> on the application of the legislatures of two thirds of several <ORG>s.
Precedent	349.
Precedent	<ORG> determines which body shall ratify the proposal.
Precedent	It can either be the legislatures of three fourths of the <ORG>s or by conventions in three fourth of the <ORG>s.
Precedent	350.
Precedent	If a proposal is made by a <ORG> and ratified by three fourth of the <ORG>s in conventions it can hardly be doubted tha t it is amendment made by the people.
Precedent	Similarly if a proposal is made by the <ORG> and ratified by conventions there cannot be any doubt that it is the people who have amended the Constitution.
Precedent	Proposal by <ORG> and ratification by three fourth legislatures of the States can in this context be equated with action of the people.
Precedent	But what is important to bear in mind is that the <ORG>, a federal legislature, does not itself amend the Constitution.
Precedent	351.
Precedent	In India, the position is different.
Precedent	It is <ORG>, a federal legislature, which is given the power to amend the Constitution except in matters which are mentioned in the proviso.
Precedent	I may repeat that many important provisions including fundamental rights are not mentioned in the proviso.
Precedent	Can we say that an amendment made by <ORG> is an amendment made by the people ?
Precedent	This is one of the matters that has to be borne in mind while considering the proper meaning to be given to the expression "amendment of this Constitution" in Article 368 as it stood b efore its amendment by the 24th Amendment.
Precedent	352.
Precedent	Article V of the U.S. Constitution differs in one other respect from Article 368.
Precedent	There are express limitations on amending power.
Precedent	The first, which has spent its force, was regarding the first and fourth clauses in the ninth section of the first article and the second relates to deprivation of a State's suffrage in the <ORG> without its consent.
Precedent	Apart from the above broad differences in Article V as compared to Article 368 , the Constitution of India is different in many respects which has a bearing on the extent of the power of <ORG> to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	In brief they are : the background of the struggle for freedom, various national aspirations outlined during this struggle, the national objectives as recited in the Objectives Resolution dated January 22, 1947 and the Preamble, the complex structure of the Indian nation consisting as it does of various peoples with different religions and languages and in different stages of economic development.
Precedent	Further the U.S. Constitution has no Directive Principles as has the Indian Constitution.
Precedent	The States in U.S. have their own Constitutions with the right to modify them consistently with the Federal Constitution.
Precedent	In India the States have no power to amend that part of the Indian Constitution which lays down their Constitution.
Precedent	They have legislative powers on certain specified subjects, the residuary power being with <ORG>.
Precedent	353.
Precedent	I may before referring to the decisions of <ORG> say that that court has hitherto not been confronted with the question posed before us : Can <ORG> in exercise of its powers under Article 368 abrogate essential basic features and one fundamental right after another including freedom of speech, freedom of religion, freedom of life ?
Precedent	The American decisions would have been of assistance if this fundamental question had arisen there and if the power to amend the Federal Constitution had been with two third majority of the <ORG>.
Precedent	354.
Precedent	The quest ion before the <ORG> in <NAME> v. <NAME> 64 L. Ed.
Precedent	871 was whether the States while ratifying proposals under Article V of the Constitution were restricted to adopt the modes of ratification mentioned in Article V, i.e.
Precedent	by the legislatures or by conventions therein, as decided by <ORG>, or could they ratify a proposed amendment in accordance with the referendum provisions contained in State Constitutions or statutes.
Precedent	355.
Precedent	The <ORG> held that "the determination of the method of ratification is the exercise of a national power specifically granted by the Constitution" and "the language of the article is plain, and admits of no doubt in its interpretation."
Precedent	The <ORG> also held that the power was conferred on the <ORG> and was limited to two methods : by action of the legislatures of three fourths of the states, or conventions in a like number of states.
Precedent	356.
Precedent	The <ORG> further held that the power to ratify a proposed amendment to the Federal Constitution had its source in the Federal Constitution and the a ct of ratification by the state derived its authority from the Federal Constitution to which the state and its people had alike assented.
Precedent	357.
Precedent	This case is of no assistance to us in interpreting Article 368 of the Constitution.
Precedent	358.
Precedent	I may now refer to decision of <ORG> Rhode Island v. <NAME> 64 L. Ed.
Precedent	946.
Fact	This case was concerned with the validity of the 18th Amendment and of certain general features of the National Prohibition Law known as Volstead Act.
Fact	No reasons were given by the <ORG> for the conclusions arrived at.
Fact	The conclusions which may have some relevance for us are conclusion 4 and 5.
Fact	The learned Counsel sought to deduce the reasons for these conclusions from the arguments addressed and reported in 64 L. Ed.
Fact	and for the reasons given by the learned Judge in 264 Fed.
Fact	Rep. 186 but impliedly rejected by <ORG> by reversing the decision.
Precedent	359.
Precedent	Counsel sought to buttress this argument by citing views of learned American authors that the arguments against the validity of the 18t h Amendment were brushed aside although no reasons are given.
Precedent	I have great respect for the judges of <ORG>, but unless the reasons are given for a judgment it is difficult to be confident about the ratio of the decision.
Precedent	Apart from the decision, I would be willing to hold the 18th Amendment valid if it had been enacted by our <ORG> and added to our Constitution, for I would discern no such taking away of Fundamental rights or altering the basic structure of the Constitution as would place it outside the contours of the Preamble and the basic features of the Constitution.
Precedent	360.
Precedent	United States of America v. <NAME> (75) L. Ed.
Fact	640 was concerned with the validity of the 18th Amendment.
Fact	<ORG> had held 44 F. (2d) 967 that the 18th Amendment had not been properly ratified so as to become part of the Constitution.
Fact	It was the contention of the respondents before <ORG> that notwithstanding the plain language of Article V, conferring upon the <ORG>  the choice of method of ratification, as between action by legislatures and by conventions, this Amendment could only be ratified by the latter.
Fact	The respondents urged that there was a difference in the kind of amendments, as, e.g.
Fact	"mere changes in the character of federal means or machinery, on the one hand, and matters affecting the liberty of the citizen on the other."
RatioOfTheDecision	There was no question as to ambit of the power of amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	In other words, there was no question that the subject-matter of amendment, namely, prohibition, fell within Article V of the Constitution.
Precedent	361.
Precedent	The <ORG> held that the choice of the mode rested solely in the discretion of the <ORG>.
Precedent	They observed:  It was submitted as part of the original draft of the Constitution to the people in conventions assembled.
Precedent	They deliberately made the grant of power to <ORG> in respect to the choice of the mode of ratification of amendments.
Precedent	Unless and until that Article be changed by amendment, <ORG> must function as the delegated agent of t he people in the choice of the method of ratification.
Precedent	362.
Precedent	The <ORG> further held that the 10th Amendment had no limited and special operation upon the people's delegation by Article V of certain functions to the <ORG>.
Precedent	363.
Precedent	I am unable to see how this case helps the respondents in any manner.
Precedent	On the plain language of the article the <ORG> came to the conclusion that the choice of the method of ratification had been entrusted to the <ORG>.
Precedent	We are not concerned with any such question here.
Precedent	364.
Argument	Mr. <NAME> urged that the judgment of the District <ORG> showed that the invalidity of the 18th Amendment to the Constitution could be rested on two groups of grounds; group A consisted of grounds relating to the meaning of the word "amendment" and the impact of the 10th Amendment or the nature of the federal system on Article V of the Constitution, and that Article V by providing the two alternative methods of ratification by convention and legislature showed that the convention method was essential for <NAME> d ratification when the amendment affected the rights of the people.
Statute	Group B consisted of the grounds on which <ORG> declared the 18th amendment to be invalid and those were that "the substance of an amendment, and therefore of course, of an entirely new Constitution, might have to conform to the particular theories of political science, sociology, economics, etc.
RatioOfTheDecision	held by the current judicial branch of the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	365.
RatioOfTheDecision	He then pointed out that grounds mentioned in Group B, which were very much like Mr. <NAME> arguments, were not even urged by counsel in <ORG>, and, therefore we must regard these grounds as extremely unsound.
RatioOfTheDecision	I, however, do not find Mr. <NAME>'s arguments similar to those referred to in Group B.
Precedent	It is true articles like <NAME>'s "The Limitations upon the Amending Power,-33 Harvard Law Rev.
Precedent	232", and <NAME> "Is the Eighteenth Amendment void because of its content ?"
Precedent	(20 Col. Law Rev.
Precedent	499), were brought to our notice but for a different purpose.
Precedent	<ORG> d the District Judge criticised these writers for becoming enmeshed "in a consideration of the Constitutionality of the substance of the amendment"-the point before us.
Precedent	As the District Judge pointed out, he was concerned with the subject-matter of the 18th Amendment because of the relation between that substance or subject-matter and the manner of its adoption.
Precedent	366.
Precedent	I do not propose to decide the validity of the amendment on the touchstone of any particular theory of political science, sociology, economics.
Precedent	Our Constitution is capable of being worked by any party having faith in democratic institutions.
Precedent	The touchstone will be the intention of the Constitution makers, which we can discern from the Constitution and the circumstances in which it was drafted and enacted.
Precedent	367.
Precedent	A number of decisions of <ORG> were referred to by both the petitioners and the respondents.
Precedent	But the State Constitutions are drafted in such different terms and conditions that it is difficult to derive any assistance in the task be fore us.
Precedent	Amendments of the Constitution are in effect invariably made by the people.
Precedent	368.
Precedent	These decisions on the power to amend a Constitution are nor very helpful because "almost without exception, amendment of a state Constitution is effected, ultimately, by the vote of the people.
Precedent	Proposed amendments ordinarily reach the people for approval or disapproval in one of two ways; by submission from a convention of delegates chosen by the people for the express purpose of revising the entire instrument, or by submission from the legislature of propositions which the legislature has approved, for amendment of the Constitution in specific respects.
Precedent	However, in some states Constitutional amendments may be proposed by proceedings under initiative and referendum, and the requirements governing the passage of statutes by initiative and referendum are followed in making changes in the state Constitutions."
Precedent	(American Jurisprudence, Vol.
Precedent	16, 2d., p. 201).
Precedent	In footnote 9 it is stated:  Ratification or non-ratification of  a Constitutional amendment is a vital element in the procedure to amend the Constitution."
Precedent	(<NAME> v. <NAME> 20% Ga 838, 69 SE 2d 742).
Precedent	The question whether the people may, by the terms of the Constitution, delegate their power to amend to others-for example, to a Constitutional convention-is one on which there is a notable lack of authority.
Precedent	An interesting question arises whether this power could be delegated to the legislature, and if so, whether the instrument which the legislature would then be empowered to amend would still be a Constitution in the proper sense of the term.
Precedent	369.
Precedent	This footnote brings out the futility of referring to decisions to interpret a Constitution, wherein power to amend has been delegated to <ORG>.
Precedent	370.
Precedent	That there is a distinction between the power of the people to amend a Constitution and the power of the legislature to amend the same was noticed by <ORG> in Ex Parte Mrs. D.C. Kerby 36, A.L.R.
Precedent	1451; 1455, one of the cases cited before us by the responden t. <NAME>, J. speaking for the <ORG> distinguished the case of <NAME> v. <NAME> in these words:  Petitioner cites only one authority that has any tendency to support the contention that a provision in the bill of rights of a Constitution cannot be amended-the case of <NAME> v. <NAME>, supra.
Precedent	Upon examination that case discloses that the Arkansas Constitution provided that the legislature might, by the observation of a prescribed procedure, amend the Constitution without submitting the proposed amendment to a vote of the people of the state, and the Bill of Rights in that Constitution contained a provision not found in the Oregon Constitution, as follows : "Everything in this article is excepted out of the general powers of government.
Precedent	The court held that the clause quoted exempted the provisions in the Bill of Rights from the authority delegated to the legislature to amend the Constitution, and reserved the right to make any such amendment to the people themselves, so that the case is in fact an authority in sup port of the right of the people to adopt such an amendment.
Precedent	The case is readily distinguished from the instant case, for every proposed amendment to the Oregon Constitution, in order to become effective, must be approved by a majority vote of the people, recorded at a state election, and consequently, when approved and adopted, such an amendment constitutes a direct expression of the will of the people in respect to the subject embraced by the particular measure, whether the same be proposed by initiative petition or by legislative resolution.
Precedent	371.
Precedent	No report of the decision in <NAME> v. <ORG> is available to me but it appears from the annotation at page 1457 that it was conceded that a Constitutional provision might be repealed if done in the proper manner viz.
Precedent	by the people, who have the unqualified right to act in the matter.
Precedent	The <ORG> is reported to have said:  And this unqualified right they can Constitutionally exercise by means of the legislative action of <ORG> in providing by law for t he call of a convention of the whole people to reconstruct or reform the government, either partially or entirely.
Precedent	And such convention, when assembled and invested with the entire sovereign power of the whole people (with the exception of such of these powers as have been delegated to the Federal government), may rightfully strike out or modify any principle declared in the Bill of Rights,' if not forbidden to do so by the Federal Constitution.
Precedent	372.
Precedent	Both sides referred to a number of distinguished and well-known authOrs.
Precedent	I do not find it advantageous to refer to them because the Indian Constitution must be interpreted according to its own terms and in the background of our history and conditions.
Precedent	Citations of comments on the Indian Constitution would make this judgment cumbersome.
Precedent	I have had the advantage of very elaborate and able arguments on both sides and I must apply my own mind to the interpretation.
Precedent	373.
Precedent	The learned Attorney-General brought to our notice extracts from 71 Constitutions.
Precedent	I admire the r esearch undertaken but I find it of no use to me in interpreting Article 368.
Precedent	First the language and the setting of each Constitution is different.
Precedent	Apart from the decisions of the Courts in United <ORG>s there are no judicial decisions to guide us as to the meaning of the amending clauses in these Constitutions.
Precedent	Further, if it is not helpful to argue from one Act of Parliament to another (see Commissioner of Stamps, Straits Settlements v. <ORG>  A.C. 378; 389).
Precedent	much less would it be helpful to argue from one Constitution to another different Constitution (see <ORG> v. <NAME>  12 A.C. 575-787).
Precedent	374.
Precedent	During the course of the arguments I had drawn the attention of the Counsel to the decision of <ORG> in The <ORG> (at the prosecution of <NAME>) v. Captain <NAME> and <NAME>.
Precedent	[1935] Irish Reports 170, and the respondents place great reliance on it.
Precedent	I may mention that this case was not cited before the Bench hearing <NAME> case.
Precedent	On careful consideration of  this case, however.
Precedent	I find that this case is distinguishable and does not afford guidance to me in interpreting Article 368 of the Constitution.
Precedent	375.
Precedent	In order to appreciate the difference between the structure of Article 50 of the Irish Constitution of 1922 and Article 368 of the Indian Constitution, it is necessary to set out Article 50 before its amendment.
Precedent	It reads:  50.
Precedent	Amendments of this Constitution within the terms of the Scheduled Treaty may be made by the <ORG>, but no such amendment, passed by both Houses of the <ORG>, after the expiration of a period of eight years from the date of the coming into operation of this Constitution, shall become law unless the same shall, after it has been passed or deemed to have been passed by the said two Houses of the <ORG>, have been submitted to a Referendum of the people, and unless a majority of the voters on the register shall have recorded their votes on such Referendum, and either the votes of a majority of the voters on the register, or two-  thirds of the votes recorded, shall have been cast in favour of such amendment.
Precedent	Any such amendment may be made within the said period of eight years by way of ordinary legislation, and as such shall be subject to the provisions of Article 47 hereof.
Precedent	376.
Precedent	It will be noticed that after the expiry of the period of eight years mentioned in the article, the amending power was not with the <ORG> as every amendment had to be first passed by the two Houses of the <ORG> and then submitted to a referendum of the people, and the condition of the referendum was that a majority of the votes on the register shall have recorded their votes on such referendum, and either the votes of a majority of the votes on the register, or two-thirds of the votes recorded shall have been cast in favour of such amendment.
Precedent	So, in fact, after the expiry of the first eight years, the amendments had to be made by the people themselves.
Precedent	In our Article 368 people as such are not associated at all in the amending process.
Precedent	377.
Precedent	<NAME> er, the Irish Constitution differed from the Indian Constitution in other respects.
Precedent	It did not have a Chapter with the heading of fundamental rights, or a provision like our Article 32 which is guaranteed.
Precedent	The words "fundamental rights" were deliberately omitted from the Irish Constitution (see foot note 9 page 67, The Irish Constitution by Barra O' Briain, 1929).
Precedent	At the same time, there was no question of any guarantee to any religious or other minorities in Ireland.
Precedent	378.
Precedent	It will be further noticed that for the first eight years an amendment could be made by way of ordinary legislation, i.e., by ordinary legislative procedure.
Precedent	The sixth amendment had deleted from the end of this article the words "and as such shall be subject to the provisions of Article 47 which provided for a referendum hereof.
Precedent	In other words, for the first eight years it was purely a flexible Constitution, a Constitutional amendment requiring no special procedure.
Precedent	379.
Precedent	With these differences in mind, I may now approach the actual decisi on of <ORG>.
Precedent	380.
Precedent	<ORG> and <ORG> were concerned with the validity of the Constitution (Amendment No.
Precedent	17) Act 1931 (No.
Precedent	37 of 1931) having regard to the provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	The validity of that Act depended on the validity of the Constitution (Amendment No.
Precedent	10) Act, 1928, No.
Precedent	8 of 1928, and of the Constitution (Amendment No.
Precedent	16) Act, 1929, No.
Precedent	10 of 1929.
Precedent	381.
Precedent	The Constitution (Amendment No.
Fact	17) Act 1931 was passed as an Act of the Oireachtas on October 17, 1931 i.e.
Fact	some 11 months after the expiry of the period of 8 years mentioned in Article 50 of the Constitution, as originally enacted.
Fact	It was not submitted to a referendum of the people.
Precedent	It was described in its long title as an "Act to amend the Constitution by inserting therein an Article making better provision for safeguarding the rights of the people and containing provisions for meeting a prevalence of disorder."
Precedent	But there is no doubt that it affected various human rights which were granted in the Irish C onstitution.
Precedent	382.
Precedent	The Constitution (Amendment No.
Statute	10) Act No.
Statute	8 of 1928 removed Articles 47 and 48 of the Constitution and also the words "and as such shall be subject to the provisions of Article 47 thereof" from the end of Article 50 as originally enacted.
Statute	Constitution (Amendment No.
Statute	16) Act No.
Statute	10 of 1929 purported to amend Article 50 of the Constitution by deleting the words "eight years" and inserting in place thereof the words "sixteen years" in that Article.
Statute	383.
Fact	The impugned amendment was held valid by <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> the word "amendment" in Article 50 widely relying on <NAME> v. Attorney General of Canada  A.C. 124.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> relied on the fact that the width of the power of amendment for the period during the first eight years was co-extensive with the period after eight years and he could find no distinction between Articles of primary importance or secondary importance.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> could not see any distinction between the word "amendment" and the words "amend or re peal.
RatioOfTheDecision	384.
Fact	In <ORG>, the Chief Justice first noticed "that the Constitution was enacted by the Third Dail sitting as a Constituent Assembly, and not by the <ORG> which, in fact, it created."
Fact	He read three limitations in the Constitution.
Argument	The first, he described as the over-all limitatioin.
Argument	Thus:  <ORG> declared in the forefront of the Constitution Act (an Act which it is not within the power of the <ORG> to alter, or amend, or repeal), that all lawful authority comes from God to the people, and it is declared by Article 2 of the Constitution that "all powers of government and all authority, legislative, executive and judicial, in Ireland are derived from the people of Ireland....  385.
Precedent	The limitation was deduced thus : "It follows that every act, whether legislative, executive or judicial, in order to be lawful under the Constitution, must be capable of being justified under the authority thereby declared to be derived from God.
Precedent	386.
Precedent	Now this limitation in so far as  it proceeds from or is derived from the belief in the Irish State that all lawful authority comes from God to the people, can have no application to our Constitution.
Precedent	387.
Precedent	The second limitation he deduced from Section 2 of the Irish Fret State Act and Article 50 of the Irish Constitution.
Precedent	It Was that any amendment repugnant to the Scheduled Treaty shall be void and inoperative.
Precedent	388.
Precedent	The third limitation was put in these words:  <ORG> has, therefore, as <ORG>, of its own supreme authority, proclaimed its acceptance of and declared, in relation to the Constitution which it enacted, certain principles, and in language which shows beyond doubt that they are stated as governing principles which are fundamental and absolute (except as expressly qualified), and, so, necessarily, immutable.
Precedent	Can the power of amendment given to the <ORG> be lawfully exercised in such a manner as to violate these principles which, as principles, the <ORG> has no power to change ?.
Precedent	In my opini on there can be only one answer to that question, namely, that <ORG> cannot be supposed to have in the same breath declared certain principles to be fundamental and immutable, or conveyed that sense in other words, as by a declaration of inviolability, and at the same time to have conferred upon the <ORG> power to violate them or to alter them.
Precedent	In my opinion, any amendment of the Constitution, purporting to be made under the power given by <ORG>, which would be a violation of, or be inconsistent with, any fundamental principle so declared, is necessarily outside the scope of the power and invalid and void.
Precedent	389.
Precedent	He further said that these limitations would apply even after the expiry of eight years.
Precedent	He said:  I have been dealing with limitations of the power of amendment in relation to the kinds of amendment which do not fall within the scope of the power and which are excluded from it always, irrespective of the time when, i.e.
Precedent	within the preliminary period of eight  years or after, or the process by which, the amendment is attempted.
Precedent	390.
Precedent	He then approached the validity of the 16th Amendment in these words: Was, then, the Amendment No.
Precedent	16 lawfully enacted by Act No.
Precedent	10 of 1929 ?
Precedent	There are two principal grounds for impeaching its validity; the first, the taking away whether validly or not, in any case the effective removal from use, of the Referendum and the right to demand a Referendum; the second, that the Amendment No.
Precedent	16 is not within the scope of the power of amendment, and therefore the <ORG> was incompetent to enact it.
Precedent	391.
Precedent	He thought:  The <ORG>, therefore, which owes its existence to the Constitution, had upon its coming into being such, and only such, power of amendment (if any) as had been given it by <ORG> in the Constitution, that is to say, the express power set out in Article 50 , and amendments of the Constitution could only be validly made within the limits of that power and in the manner prescribed by that power.
Precedent	392.
Precedent	He  then observed:  Now, the power of amendment is wholly contained in a single Article, but the donee of the power and the mode of its exercise are so varied with regard to a point of time as to make it practically two separate powers, the one limited to be exercised only during the preliminary period of eight years, the other, a wholly different and permanent power, to come into existence after the expiry of that preliminary period and so continue thereafter.
Precedent	393.
Precedent	After referring to the condition (it shall be subject to the provisions of Article 47 ) he thought:  <ORG>, even during the preliminary period, would not relax the ultimate authority of the people, and expressly reserved to the people the right to intervene when they considered it necessary to restrain the action of the <ORG> affecting the Constitution.
Precedent	The frame of this provision makes it clear to my mind that, even if, by amendment of the Constitution under the power, Article 47 might cease to apply to ordinary legislation  of the <ORG>, the provisions of that clause were declared, deliberately, expressly and in a mandatory way, to be kept in force and operative for the purpose of amendments of the Constitution during the preliminary period of eight years.
Precedent	394.
Precedent	According to him "the permanent power of amendment, to arise at the expiry of the period of eight years, is a wholly different thing both as to the donee of the power and the manner of its exercise.
Precedent	395.
Precedent	He held that it was apt competent for the <ORG> to remove from the power granted to it by <ORG> the requisites for its exercise attached to it in the very terms of donation of the power.
Precedent	He observed:  That provision of the Statute, No.
Precedent	8 of 1928, was bad, in my opinion as being what is called in the general law of powers 'an excessive execution.'
RatioOfTheDecision	It was outside the scope of the power.
RatioOfTheDecision	We have not been referred to, nor have I found, any precedent for such a use of a power.
RatioOfTheDecision	I do not believe that there can be a precedent because it defies logic  and reason.
RatioOfTheDecision	It was, therefore, invalid in my opinion.
Precedent	396.
Precedent	Regarding the substitution of "sixteen years" for the words "eight years" he said: If this amendment is good there is no reason why the <ORG> should not have inserted or should not even yet insert, a very much larger term of years or, indeed, delete the whole of Article 50 from the words "by the <ORG>" in the second line to the end of the Article.
Precedent	397.
Precedent	Later he observed:  The attempt to take from the people this right, this exclusive power and authority and to confer on the <ORG> a full and uncontrolled power to amend the Constitution without reference to the people (even though for a period of years, whether it be until 1938 or Tibb's Eve, a matter of indifference in the circumstances) was described by counsel in, I think, accurate language, as a usurpation, for it was done in my opinion without legal authority.
Precedent	398.
Precedent	He then repelled the argument that Section 50 conferred the power to amend the Article itself.
Precedent	His reasons for this  conclusion are summarised thus at page 219: In my opinion, on the true interpretation of the power before us, upon a consideration of express prohibition, limitations and requirements of the clause containing it, the absence of any express authority, the donation of the effective act in the exercise of the power to the people as a whole, the relevant surrounding circumstances to which I have already referred and the documents and their tenor in their entirety, there is not here, either expressly or by necessary implication, any power to amend the power of amendment itself.
Precedent	399.
Precedent	I cannot agree with the learned Attorney-General that the sole basis of Kennedy C.J.
Precedent	's decision was that Article 50 did not contain an express power of amending the provisions of Article 50 itself.
Precedent	He gave various reasons which I have referred to above.
Precedent	400.
Precedent	<NAME> held that the word "amendment" was wide enough to include a power to amend or alter or repeal and there is no express prohibition in Article 50 itself that any arti cle of the Constitution including Article 50 could not be amended.
Precedent	The only limitation that he could find was that the provisions of the Scheduled Treaty could not be amended.
Precedent	He observed:  I see no ground for holding that either of these Articles could not have been amended by the <ORG> subject to a Referendum of the people after the period of eight years, and, if so, it follows that the same amendment, e.g., the deletion of the word "no" in Article 43 could be made "by way of ordinary legislation" within that period, or within sixteen, years, after eight had been altered to sixteen.
Precedent	401.
Argument	In other words, according to him, if the <ORG> subject to a referendum of the people mentioned in Article 50 could amend any Article, so could <ORG> during the period of eight years.
Precedent	But he noticed that in other Constitutions, there are articles, laws or provisions which are specifically described as "Fundamental" e.g., Sweden, or "Constitutional" e.g., Austria, Czechoslovakia and France, in respect of which  the Constitution expressly restricts the power of amendment, but in Constitution of the Saorstat there is no such segregation, and the power of amendment which applies to any Article appears to me to be equally applicable to all others, subject, of course, to the restriction in respect of the Scheduled Treaty.
Precedent	He, later observed: Unless, therefore, these rights appear plainly from the express provisions of our Constitution to be inalienable, and incapable of being modified or taken away by any legislative act, I cannot accede to the argument that the <ORG> cannot alter, modify, or repeal them.
Precedent	The framer of our Constitution may have intended "to bind man down from mischief by the chains of the Constitution," but if they did, they defeated their object by banding him the key of the padlock in Article 50.
Precedent	(P. 234)  402.
Precedent	<NAME> stressed the point that "this direct consultation of the people's will does indicate that all matters, however fundamental, might be the subject of amendment.
Precedent	On the other han d the view contended for by the appellants must go to this extreme point, viz., that certain Articles or doctrines of the Constitution are utterly incapable of alteration at any time even if demanded by an absolute majority of the voters.
Precedent	403.
Precedent	This observation really highlights the distinction between Article 50 of the Irish Constitution and Article 368 of the Indian Constitution.
Precedent	As I have already observed, there is no direct consultation of the people's will in Article 368 of our Constitution.
Precedent	404.
Precedent	The only limitation he could find in Article 50 was that the amendment to the Constitution must be within the terms of the Scheduled Treaty.
Precedent	405.
Precedent	As I have observed earlier, I find Article 50 of the Irish Constitution quite different in structure from Article 368 of the Indian Constitution and I do not think it is permissible to argue from Article 50 of the Irish Constitution to Article 368 of the Indian Constitution.
Precedent	Be that as it may, if I had to express my concurrence, I would express concurrence with the v iew of the learned Chief Justice in so far as he said that the <ORG> could not increase its power of amendment by substituting sixteen years for the words "eight years".
Precedent	406.
Precedent	I had also invited attention of Counsel to <NAME> and <NAME>.
Precedent	v. Attorney-General for the Irish Free State and <NAME>.
Fact	[1935] A.C. 484 and the respondents rely heavily on it.
Fact	In this case the validity of the Constitution (Amendment No.
Fact	22) Act, 1933 (Act 6 of 1933) was involved.
RatioOfTheDecision	It was alleged that this amendment was no bar to the maintenance by the petitioners, who were the appellants, of their appeal before <ORG>, as it was Void.
RatioOfTheDecision	407.
RatioOfTheDecision	On May 3, 1933, the <ORG> passed an Act, No.
RatioOfTheDecision	6 of 1933, entitled the Constitution (Removal of Oath) Act, 1933.
RatioOfTheDecision	That Act , by Section 2 , provided that Section 2 of the Constitution of the Irish Free State (Saorstat Eireann) Act, 1922, should be repealed, and, by Section 3 , that Article 50 of the Constitution should be amended by deleting the words "within the terms of the Scheduled  Treaty.
RatioOfTheDecision	408.
RatioOfTheDecision	Finally, on November 15, 1933, the <ORG>, enacted the Constitution (Amendment No.
Precedent	22) Act, 1933, amending Article 66 of the Constitution so as to terminate the right of appeal to His Majesty in Council.
Precedent	409.
Precedent	The Validity of the last amending Act depended on whether the earlier Act, No.
Precedent	6 of 1933, was valid, namely, that which is directed to removing from Article 50 the condition that there can be no amendment of the Constitution unless it is within the terms of the Scheduled Treaty.
Precedent	410.
Precedent	It appears that Mr. <NAME>, arguing for the petitioners, conceded that the Constitution (Amendment No.
Precedent	16) Act, 1929 was regular and that the validity of the subsequent amendments could not be attacked on the ground that they had not been submitted to the people by referendum.
Precedent	411.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is true that <ORG> said that Mr. <NAME> rightly conceded this point but we do not know the reasons which impelled <ORG> to say that the concession was rightly made.
RatioOfTheDecision	In view of the d ifferences between Article 50 of the Irish Constitution and Article 368 of our Constitution, this concession cannot have any importance in the present case.
RatioOfTheDecision	The actual decision in the case is of no assistance to us because that proceeds on the basis that the Statute of Westminster had removed the restriction, contained in the Constitution of the Irish Free State Act, 1922.
RatioOfTheDecision	412.
Argument	Mr. <NAME> challenged the validity of Act No.
Argument	6 of 1933 by urging: The Constitution derived its existence not from any legislature of <ORG> but solely from the operations of an Irish body, <ORG>, which is called in Ireland <ORG>.
Argument	This body, it is said, though mentioned in the Irish Free State (Agreement) Act, 1922, was in fact elected pursuant to a resolution passed on May 20, 1922, by <ORG>, <ORG>.
Argument	<ORG> was thus, it was alleged, set up in Ireland by election of the people of Ireland of their own authority as a Constituent  <ORG> to create a Constitution, and having accomplished its work went out of existence, leaving no successor and no body in authority capable of amending the Constituent Act.
Argument	The result of that argument is that a Constitution was established which Mr. <NAME> has described as a semi- rigid Constitution-that is, "one capable of being amended in detail in the different articles according to their terms, but not susceptible of any alteration so far as concerns the Constituent Act, unless perhaps by the calling together of a new Constituent <ORG> by the people of Ireland.
Precedent	Thus the articles of the Constitution may only be amended in accordance with Article 50 , which limits amendments to such as are within the terms of the Scheduled Treaty.
Precedent	On that view Mr. <NAME> argues that the law No.
