Precedent	6 of 1933 is ultra vires and hence that the amendment No.
Precedent	22 of 1933 falls with it.
Precedent	413.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> referred their Lordships to <ORG> (<NAME> and <NAME>.)
Precedent	v. <NAME> and <NAME>.
Precedent	[1935] <ORG> 170.
Precedent	In that case Chief Justice <NAME> <NAME> is reported to have expressed a view which corresponds in substance to that contended for by Mr. <NAME>.
Precedent	414.
Precedent	Now it is these contentions which I have just set out and which their Lordships could not accept.
Precedent	They observed:  In their opinion the Constituent Act and the Constitution of the Irish Free State derived their validity from the Act of the Imperial Parliament, the Irish Free State Constitution Act, 1922.
Precedent	This Act established that the Constitution, subject to the provisions of the Constituent Act, should be the Constitution of the Irish Free State and should come into operation on being proclaimed by His Majesty, as was done on December 6, 1922.
Precedent	The action of <ORG> was thereby ratified.
Precedent	415.
Precedent	The position was summed up as follows:  (1) The Treaty and the Constituent Act respectively form parts of the Statute Law of the United Kingdom, each of them being parts of an Imperial Act.
Precedent	(2) Before the passing of the Statute of Westminster it was not competent for the Irish Free State P arliament to pass an Act abrogating the Treaty because the Colonial Laws Validity Act forbade a dominion legislature to pass a law repugnant to an Imperial Act.
Precedent	(3) The affect of the Statute of Westminster was to remove the fetter which lay upon <ORG> by reason of the Colonial Laws Validity Act.
Precedent	That Legislature can now pass Acts repugnant to an Imperial Act In this case they have done so.
Precedent	416.
RatioOfTheDecision	I think that summary makes it quite clear that it wag because of the Statute of Westminster that <ORG> was enabled to amend the Constitution Act .
RatioOfTheDecision	PART IV Validity of 24th Amendment  417.
RatioOfTheDecision	Now I may deal with the question whether the Constitution (Twenty-Fourth Amendment) Act, 1971 is valid.
Statute	It reads thus:  ...  (2) In Article 13 of the Constitution, after Clause (3), the following clause shall be inserted, namely:  (4) Nothing in this article shall apply to any amendment of this Constitution made under Article 368.
Statute	(3) Article 368 of the Constitution shall be re- numbered as Clause (2) thereof, and-  (a) for the marginal heading to that article, the following marginal heading shall be substituted, namely:  Power of <ORG> to amend the Constitution and procedure therefor.
Statute	;  (b) before Clause (2) as so re-numbered, the following clause shall be inserted, namely:  (1) Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution, <ORG> may in exercise of its constituent power amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any provision of this Constitution in accordance with toe procedure laid down in this article;  (c) in Clause (2) as so re-numbered, for the words "it shall be presented to the President for his ascent and upon such assent being given to the Bill", the words "it shall be presented to the President who shall give his attest to the Bill and thereupon" shall be substituted;  (d) after Clause (2) as so re-numbered, the following shall be inserted, namely:  (3) Nothing in Article 13 shall apply to any amendment made under this article.
Statute	418.
Statute	According to the petitio ner, the 24th Amendment has sought to achieve five results:  (i) It has inserted an express provision in Article 368 to indicate that the source of the amending power will be found in that Article itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	(ii) It has made it obligatory on the President to give his assent to any Bill duly passed under that Article.
RatioOfTheDecision	(iii) It has substituted the words "amend by way of addition, variation or repeal..." in place of the bare concept of "amendment" in the Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	(iv) It makes explicit that when <ORG> makes a Constitutional amendment under Article 368 it acts "in exercise of its constituent power.
RatioOfTheDecision	(v) It has expressly provided, by amendments in Article 13 and 368 , that the bar in Article 13 against abridging or taking away any of the fundamental rights should not apply to any amendment made under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	419.
RatioOfTheDecision	Mr. <NAME> did not dispute that the amendments covered by (i) and (ii) above were within the amending power of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	I do not find it necessary to go into the question whether S <NAME>, C.J., rightly decided that the amending power was in List I entry 97, or Article 248 , because nothing turns on it now.
RatioOfTheDecision	420.
RatioOfTheDecision	Mr. <NAME> rightly conceded that <ORG> could validly amend Article 368 to transfer the source of amending power from List I entry 97 to Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	421.
RatioOfTheDecision	Mr. <NAME> however contended that "if the amendments covered by (iii) and (iv) above are construed as empowering <ORG> to exercise the full constituent power of, the people themselves, and as vesting in <ORG> the ultimate legal sovereignty of the people, and as authorising <ORG> to alter or destroy all or any of the essential features, basic elements and fundamental principles of the Constitution (hereinafter referred to "essential features"), the amendments must be held, to be illegal and void."
RatioOfTheDecision	He further urges that "if the amendment covered by (v) is construed as authorising <ORG> to damage or destroy the essence of all or any of the fundamental rights, the amendment must be held to be i llegal and void."
RatioOfTheDecision	He says that the 24th Amendment is void and illegal for the following reasons : A creature of the Constitution, as the <ORG> is, can have only such amending power as is conferred by the Constitution which is given by the people unto themselves.
RatioOfTheDecision	While purporting to exercise that amending power, <ORG> cannot increase that very power.
RatioOfTheDecision	No doubt, <ORG> had the power to amend Article 368 itself, but that does not mean that <ORG> could so amend Article 368 as to change its own amending power beyond recognition.
RatioOfTheDecision	A creature of the Constitution cannot enlarge its own power over the Constitution, while purporting to act under it, any more than the creature of an ordinary law can enlarge its own power while purporting to act under that law.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power of amendment cannot possibly embrace the power to enlarge that very power of amendment, or to abrogate the limitations, inherent or implied, in the terms on which the power was conferred.
RatioOfTheDecision	The contrary view would reduce the whole principl e of inherent and implied limitations to an absurdity.
RatioOfTheDecision	422.
Precedent	It is contended on behalf of the respondents that the 24th Amendment does enlarge the power of <ORG> to amend the Constitution, if <NAME> case limited it, and as Article 368 clearly contemplates amendment of Article 368 itself, <ORG> can confer additional powers of amendment on it.
Precedent	423.
Precedent	Reliance was placed on <NAME>'s [1935] Irish Reports 170 case and <NAME>'s [1935] A.C. 484 case.
Precedent	I have already dealt with these cases.
Precedent	424.
RatioOfTheDecision	It seems to me that it is not legitimate to interpret Article 368 in this manner.
RatioOfTheDecision	Clause  (e) of the proviso does not give any different power than what is contained in the main article.
RatioOfTheDecision	The meaning of the expression "Amendment of the Constitution" does not change when one reads the proviso.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the meaning is the same, Article 368 can only be amended so as not to change its identity completely.
Precedent	<ORG>, for instance, could not make the Constitution uncontrolled by changing the prescribed two third majority to s imple majority.
Precedent	Similarly it cannot get rid of the true meaning of the expression "Amendment of the Constitution" so as to derive power to abrogate fundamental rights.
Precedent	425.
Precedent	If the words "notwithstanding anything in the Constitution" are designed to widen the meaning of the word "Amendment of the Constitution" it would have to be held void as beyond the amending power.
Precedent	But I do not read these to mean this.
Precedent	They have effect to get rid of the argument that Article 248 and Entry 97 List I contains the power of amendment.
Precedent	Similarly, the insertion of the words "in exercise of its constituent power" only serves to exclude Article 248 and Entry 97 List I and emphasize that it is not ordinary legislative power that <ORG> is exercising under Article 368 but legislative power of amending the Constitution.
Precedent	426.
Precedent	It was said that if <ORG> cannot increase its power of amendment Clause (d) of Section 3 of the 24th Amendment which makes Article 13 inapplicable to an amendment of the Constitution would be bad.
Precedent	I se e no force in this contention.
Precedent	Article 13(2) as existing previous to the 24th Amendment as interpreted by the majority in <NAME> case prevented legislatures from taking away or abridging the rights conferred by Article  13.
Precedent	In other words, any law which abridged a fundamental right even to a small extent was liable to be struck down under Article 368 <ORG> can amend every article of the Constitution as long as the result is within the limits already laid down by me.
Precedent	The amendment of Article 13(2) does not go beyond the limits laid down because <ORG> cannot even after the amendment abrogate or authorise abrogation or the taking away of fundamental rights.
Precedent	After the amendment now a law which has the effect of merely abridging a right while remaining within the limits laid down would not be liable to be struck down.
Precedent	427.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the result, in my opinion, the 24th Amendment as interpreted by me is valid.
Statute	PART V.-Validity of Section 2 of the Constitution (Twenty-fifth Amendment) Act, 1971.
Statute	428.
Statute	Secti on 2 of the Constitution (Twenty-fifth Amendment) Act, 1971 enacted as follows:  (a) for Clause (2), the following clause shall be substituted, namely: (2) No property shall be compulsorily acquired or requisitioned save for a public purpose and save by authority of a law which provides for acquisition or requisitioning of the property for an amount which may be fixed by such law or which may be determined in accordance with such principles and given in such manner as may be specified in such law; and no such law shall be called in question in any court on the ground that the amount so fixed or determined is not adequate or that the whole or any part of such amount is to be given otherwise than in cash:  <BLOCKQUOTE Provided that in making any law providing for the compulsory acquisition of any property of an educational institution established and administered by a minority, referred to in Clause (1) of Article 30 , the <ORG> shall ensure that the amount fixed by or determined under such law for the acquisit ion of such property is such as would not restrict or abrogate the right guaranteed under that clause.
Statute	(b) after Clause (2A), the following clause shall be inserted, namely: (2B) Nothing in Sub-clause (f) of Clause (1) of Article 19 shall affect any such law as is referred to in Clause (2).
Precedent	429.
Precedent	There cannot be any doubt that the object of the amendment is to modify the decision given by this Court in <CITATION_513801>  3 S.C.R.
Precedent	530 where it was held by ten Judges that the Banking Companies (Acquisition and Transfer of Undertakings) Act violated the guarantee of compensation under Article 31(2) in that it provided for giving certain amounts determined according to principles which were not relevant in the determination of compensation of the undertaking of the named Banks and by the method prescribed the amounts so declared could not be regarded as compensation.
Precedent	430.
Precedent	If we compare Article 31(2) as it stood before and after the 25th Amendment, the following changes seem to have been effected.
Precedent	Whereas before  the amendment, Article 31(2) required the law providing for acquisition to make provision for compensation by either fixing the amount of compensation or specifying the principles on which and the manner in which the compensation should be determined after the amendment Article 31(2) requires such a law to provide for an "amount" which may be fixed by the law providing for acquisition or requisitioning or which may be determined in accordance with such principles and given in such manner as may be specified in such law.
Precedent	In other words, for the idea that compensation should be given, now the idea is that an "amount" should be given.
Precedent	This amount can be fixed directly by law or may be determined in accordance with such principles as may be specified.
Precedent	431.
Precedent	It is very difficult to comprehend the exact meaning which can be ascribed to the word "amount".
Precedent	In this context, it is true that it is being used in lieu of compensation, but the word "amount" is not a legal concept as "compensation" is.
Precedent	432.
Precedent	According to S horter Oxford English Dictionary, Third Edn.
Precedent	p. 57, the word "amount" has the following meaning:  Amount (amount sb.
Precedent	1710, (f. the vb.)
Precedent	1).
Precedent	The sum total to which anything amounts up; spec.
Precedent	the sum of the principal and interest 1796.
Precedent	2. fig.
Precedent	The full value, effect, or significance 1732.
Precedent	3.
Precedent	A quantity or sum viewed as a total 1833.
Precedent	433.
Precedent	According to Webster's Third New International Dictionary, p. 72, "amount" means: amount 1a : the total number of quantity; AGGREGATE (the amount of the fine is doubled); SUM, NUMBER (add the same amount to each column) (the amount of the policy is 10,000 dollars) b : the sum of individuals (the unique amount of worthless IOU's collected during each day's business - <NAME>) c : the quantity at hand or under consideration (only a small amount of trouble involved) (a surprising amount of patience) 2 : the whole or final effect, significance, or import (the amount of bis remarks is that we are hopelessly beaten) 3 : accounting : a principal sum and the interest on it syn see  SUM.
Precedent	434.
Precedent	I have also seen the meaning of the word "amount" in the Oxford English Dictionary, Volume 1 p. 289, but it does not give me much guidance as to the meaning to be put in Article 31(2 ), as amended.
Precedent	The figurative meaning, i.e., the full value, I cannot give because of the deliberate omission of the word "compensation" and substitution of the word "amount" in lieu thereof.
Precedent	435.
Precedent	Let us then see if the other part of the article throws any light on the word "amount".
Precedent	The article postulates that in some cases principles may be laid down for determining the amount and these principles may lead to an adequate amount or an inadequate amount.
Precedent	So this show that the word "amount" here means something to be given in lieu of the property to be acquired but this amount has to and can be worked out by laying down certain principles.
Precedent	These principles must then have a reasonable relationship to the property which is sought to be acquired, if this is so, the amount ultimately arrived at by applying the principles  must have some reasonable relationship with the property to be acquired; otherwise the principles of the Act could hardly be principles within the meaning of Article 31(2).
Precedent	436.
Precedent	If this meaning is given to the word "amount" namely, that the amount given in cash or otherwise is of such a nature that it has been worked out in accordance with the principles which have relationship to the property to be acquired, the question arises : what meaning is to be given, to the expression "the amount so fixed".
Precedent	The amount has to be fixed by law but the amount so fixed by law must also be fixed in accordance with some principles because it could not have been intended that if the amount is fixed by law, the legislature would fix the amount arbitrarily.
Precedent	It could not, for example, fix the amount by a lottery.
Precedent	437.
RatioOfTheDecision	Law is enacted by passing a bill which is introduced.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution and legislative procedure contemplate that there would be discussion, and in debate, the Government spokesman in the legislature would be a ble to justify the amount which has been fixed.
RatioOfTheDecision	Suppose an amendment is moved to the amount fixed.
RatioOfTheDecision	How would the debate proceed ?
RatioOfTheDecision	Can the Minister say-"This amount is fixed as it is the government's wish."
RatioOfTheDecision	Obviously not.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, it follows that the amount, if fixed by the legislature, has also to be fixed according to some principles.
Precedent	These principles cannot be different from the principles which the legislature would lay down.
Precedent	438.
RatioOfTheDecision	In this connection it must be borne in mind that Article 31(2) is still a fundamental right.
RatioOfTheDecision	Then, what is the change that has been brought about by the amendment ?
RatioOfTheDecision	It is no doubt that a change was intended, it seems to me that the change effected is that a person whose property is aquired can no longer claim full compensation or just compensation but he can still claim that the law should lay down principles to determine the amount which he is to get and these principles must have a rational relation to the property sought to be acquired.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the law were to lay down a princip le that the amount to be paid in lieu of a brick of gold acquired shall be the same as the market value of an ordinary brick or a brick of silver it could not be held to be a principle at all.
RatioOfTheDecision	Similarly if it is demonstrated that the amount that has been fixed for the brick of gold is the current value of an ordinary brick or a brick of silver the amount fixed would be illegal.
RatioOfTheDecision	If I were to interpret Article 31(2) as meaning that even an arbitrary or illusory or a grossly low amount could be given which would shock not only the judicial conscience but the conscience of every reasonable human being, a serious question would arise whether <ORG> has not exceded its amending power under Article 368 of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The substance of the fundamental right to property, under Article 31 , consists of three things : one, the property shall be acquired by or under a valid law; secondly, it shall be acquired only for a public purpose; and, thirdly, the person whose property has been acquired shall be given an a mount in lieu thereof, which, as I have already said, is not arbitrary, illusory or shocking to the judicial conscience or the conscience of mankind.
RatioOfTheDecision	I have already held that <ORG> has no power under Article 368 to abrogate the fundamental rights but can amend or regulate or adjust them in its exercise of amending powers without destroying them.
RatioOfTheDecision	Applying this to the fundamental right of property, <ORG> cannot empower legislatures to fix an arbitrary amount or illusory amount or an amount that virtually amounts to confiscation, taking all the relevant circumstances of the acquisition into consideration.
RatioOfTheDecision	Same considerations apply to the manner of payment.
RatioOfTheDecision	I cannot interpret this to mean that an arbitrary manner of payment is contemplated.
RatioOfTheDecision	To give an extreme example, if an amount is determined or fixed at Rs.
Precedent	10,000 a legislature cannot lay down that payment will be made at the rate of Rs.
Precedent	10 per year or Rs.
Precedent	10 per month.
Precedent	439.
Precedent	Reference may be made to two cases that show that if discretion is conferre d it must be exercised reasonably.
Precedent	440.
Precedent	In <NAME> v. <NAME> [1925] A.C. 578; 590 it was held that the discretion conferred upon the <ORG> by Section 62 of the Metropolis Management Act, 1855, must be exercised reasonably.
Precedent	The following observations of Lord <NAME> are pertinent:  It appears to me, for the reasons I have given, that they cannot have brought into account the consideration which they say influenced them, and that they did not base their decision upon the ground that the reward for work is the value of the work reasonably and even generously measured, but that they took an arbitrary principle and fixed an arbitrary sum, which was not a real exercise of the discretion imposed upon them by the statute.
Precedent	441.
Precedent	I may also refer to Lord <NAME>'s observation at p. 613:  I rest my opinion upon higher grounds.
Precedent	A person in whom is vested a discretion must exercise his discretion upon reasonable grounds.
Precedent	A discretion does not empower a man to do what he likes merely because he is minded to do so -  he must in the exercise of his discretion do not what he likes but what he ought.
Precedent	In other words, he must, by use of his reason, ascertain and follow the course which reason directs.
Precedent	He must act reasonably.
Precedent	442.
Precedent	In <NAME> v. <ORG> [1911] A.C. 381 the facts are given in the headnote as follows:  Under Section 4 of the New South Wales Public Service Superannuation Act, 1903, the plaintiff was awarded by <ORG> a gratuity of 23 # 10 $.
Precedent	1 d. per mensem, calculated for each year of service from December 9, 1875, the date of his permanent employment, upto December 23, 1895; and upon his claiming to have his service reckoned up to August 16, 1902, was awarded a further gratuity of one penny in respect of each year subsequent to December 23, 1895, up to August 16, 1902, the date of the commencement of the public Service Act of that year.
Precedent	443.
Precedent	<ORG> held the award to be illusory.
Precedent	<ORG> observed:  ...it seems to their Lordships to be quite pl ain that an illusory award such as this - an award intended to be unreal and unsubstantial - though made under guise of exercising discretion, is at best a colourable performance, and tantamount to a refusal by the <ORG> to exercise the discretion entrusted to them by <ORG>.
Precedent	444.
RatioOfTheDecision	Although I am unable to appreciate the wisdom of inserting Clause (2B) in Article 31 , the effect of which is to make Article 19(1)(f) inapplicable, I cannot say that it is an unreasonable abridgement of rights under Article 19(1)(f).
RatioOfTheDecision	While passing a law fixing principles, the legislatures are bound to provide a procedure for the determination of the amount, and if the procedure is arbitrary that provision may well be struck down under Article 14.
RatioOfTheDecision	445.
RatioOfTheDecision	In view of the interpretation which I have placed on the new Article 31(2 ), as amended, it cannot be said that <ORG> has exceeded its amending power under Article 368 in enacting the new Article 31(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	446.
RatioOfTheDecision	For the reasons aforesaid I hold that Section 2 of the Constitu tion (Twenty-fifth Amendment) Act, 1971, as interpreted by me, valid.
Precedent	447.
Precedent	Part VI-Validity of Section 3 of the Constitution (Twenty-Fifth Amendment) Act, 1971.
Precedent	448.
RatioOfTheDecision	Section 3 of the twenty-fifth amendment, reads thus:  3.
Statute	After Article 31B of the Constitution, the following article shall be inserted, namely:  31.
Precedent	C. Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 , no law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with, or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Article 14 , Article 19 or Article 31; and no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy:  Provided that where such law is made by the legislature of a <ORG>, the provisions of this article shall not apply thereto unless such law, having been reserved for the con sideration of the President, has received his assent.
Precedent	449.
RatioOfTheDecision	It will be noted that Article 31C opens with the expression "notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13".
RatioOfTheDecision	This however cannot mean that not only fundamental rights like Artice 19(1)(f) or Article 31 are excluded but all fundamental rights belonging to the minorities and religious groups are also excluded.
RatioOfTheDecision	The article purports to save laws which a <ORG> may make towards securing the principles specified in Clauses (b) or (c) of Article 39 from being challenged on the ground that it is inconsistent with, or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Articles 14, 19 or 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	This is the only ground on which they cannot be challenged.
RatioOfTheDecision	It will be noticed that the article provides that if the law contains a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy, it shall not be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	In other words, once a declaration is given, no court can question the law  on the ground that it has nothing to do with giving effect to the policy; whether it gives effect to some other policy is irrelevant.
Precedent	Further, a law may contain some provisions dealing with the principles specified in Clauses (b) or (c) of Article 39 while other sections may have nothing to do with it, yet on the language it denies any court power or jurisdiction to go into this question.
Precedent	450.
Precedent	In the face of the declaration, this <ORG> would be unable to test the validity of incidental provisions which do not constitute an essential and integral part of the policy directed to give effect to Article 39(b) and Article 39(c).
Precedent	451.
Precedent	<CITATION_1258563>  Supp.
Precedent	2 S.C.R.
Precedent	691-707 <NAME>, C.J., speaking for the <ORG>, observed:  "A law relating to" a <ORG> monopoly cannot, in the context, include all the provisions contained in the said law whether they have direct relation with the creation of the monopoly or not.
Precedent	In our opinion, the said expression should be construed to mean the law relating to the monopo ly in its absolutely essential features.
Precedent	If a law is passed creating a <ORG> monopoly, the <ORG> should enquire what are the provisions of the said law which are basically and essentially necessary for creating the <ORG> monopoly.
Precedent	It is only those essential and basic provisions which are protected by the latter part of Article 19(6).
Precedent	If there are other provisions made by the Act which are subsidiary, incidental or helpful to the operation of the monopoly, they do not fall under the said part and their validity must be judged under the first part of Article 19(6).
Precedent	452.
Precedent	These observations were quoted with approval by <NAME>, J., speaking on behalf of a larger <ORG> in <ORG> v. <ORG> [1970] 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	530-582.
Precedent	After quoting the observations, <NAME>, J., observed:  This was reiterated in <CITATION_312295>  3 S.C.R.
Precedent	374.
Precedent	<CITATION_1470332> .
Precedent	1 S.C.R.
Precedent	400 and <CITATION_944301>  3 S.C.R.
Precedent	447.
Precedent	453.
Precedent	While dealing with the validity of the Bombay Prohibition Act (XXV of 1949), this <ORG> in <CITATION_334293 >  S.C.R.
Precedent	682 struck down two provisions on the ground that they conflicted with the fundamental rights of freedom of speech and expression guaranteed by Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution.
Precedent	These provisions were Sections 23(a) and 24(1)(a) , which read:  23.
Precedent	No person shall-  (a) commend, solicit the use of, offer any intoxicant or hemp, or....  24(1).
Precedent	No person shall print or publish in any newspaper news-sheet, book, leaflet, booklet or any other single or periodical publication or otherwise display or distribute any advertisement or other matter-  (a) which commends, solicits the use of, or offers any intoxicant or hemp....  454.
Precedent	Section 23(b) was also held to be void.
Precedent	It was held that "the words "incite" and "encourage" are wide enough to include incitement and encouragement by words and speeches and also by acts and the words used in the section are so wide and vague that the clause must be held to be void in its entirety.
Precedent	455.
Precedent	Section 23(b) reads as follows:  23.
Precedent	No person shall-  (a) ...  (b) incite  or encourage any member of the public or any class of individuals of the public generally to commit any act, which frustrates or defeats the provisions of this Act, or any rule, regulation or order made thereunder, or....  456.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> contends, and I think rightly, that this Court would not be able to strike these provisions down if a similar declaration were inserted now in the Bombay Prohibition Act that this law is for giving-effect to Article 47 , which prescribes the duty of the <ORG> to bring about prohibition of the consumption of intoxicating drinks.
Precedent	If a similar provision were inserted in the impugned Kerala Acts making it a criminal offence to criticise, frustrate or defeat the policy of the Acts, the provisions would be protected under Article 31(C).
Precedent	457.
Precedent	The only so-called protection which is given is that if the legislature of a <ORG> passes such a law it must receive the President's assent.
Precedent	It is urged before us that it is no protection at all because the President would give his asse nt on the advice of <ORG>.
Precedent	458.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31C in its nature differs from Article 31A , which was inserted by the Fourth Amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	31A.
RatioOfTheDecision	(1) Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 , no law providing for-  (a) the acquisition by the <ORG> of any estate or of any rights therein or the extinguishment or modification of any such rights, or  (b) the taking over of the management of any property by the <ORG> for a limited period either in the public interest or in order to secure the proper management of the property, or  (c) the amalgamation of two or more corporations either in the public interest or in order to secure the proper management of any of the corporations, or  (d) the extinguishment or modification of any rights of managing agents, secretaries and treasurers, managing directors, directors or managers of corporations, or of any voting rights of shareholders thereof, or  (e) the extinguishment or modification of any rights accruing by virtue of any agreement, lease or licence for th e purpose of searching for, or winning, any mineral or mineral oil, or the premature termination or cancellation of any such agreement, lease or license, shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with, or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Article 14 , Article 19 or Article 31 :   Provided that....  459.
RatioOfTheDecision	In Article 31A the subject-matter of the legislation is clearly provided, namely, the acquisition by the <ORG> of any estate or any rights therein, ( Article 31A(a)).
RatioOfTheDecision	Similarly, the subject-matter of legislation is specifically provided in Clauses (b), (c) and (d) of Article 31A.
RatioOfTheDecision	But in Article 31C the sky is the limit because it leaves to each <ORG> to adopt measures towards securing the principles specified in Clauses (b) and (c) of Article 39.
RatioOfTheDecision	The wording of Articles 39(b) and 39(c) is very wide.
RatioOfTheDecision	The expression "economic system" in Article 39(c) may well include professional and other services.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to <ORG> (1970 Ed.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	9p.
Precedent	p. 600) "economic  systems are forms of social organization for producing goods and services and determining how they will be distributed.
Precedent	It would be difficult to resist the contention of the <ORG> that each provision in the law has been taken for the purpose of giving effect to the policy of the <ORG>.
Precedent	460.
Precedent	It was suggested that if the latter part of Article 31C , dealing with declaration, is regarded as unConstitutional, the Court will be entitled to go into the question whether there is any nexus between the impugned law and Article 39(b) and Article 39(c).
Precedent	I find it difficult to appreciate this submission.
Precedent	There may be no statement of <ORG> policy in a law.
Precedent	Even if there is a statement of policy in the Preamble, it would not control the substantive provisions, if unambiguous.
Precedent	But assuming that there is a clear statement it would be for the <ORG> legislature to decide whether a provision would help to secure the objects.
Precedent	461.
Precedent	The <ORG> will be unable to separate necessarily incidental provisions and merely incidental.
Precedent	F urther, as I have pointed out above, this question is not justiciable if the law contains a declaration that it is for giving effect to such a policy.
Precedent	According to Mr. <NAME>, Article 31C has four features of totalitarianism : (1) There is no equality.
Precedent	The ruling party could favour its own party members, (2) There need not be any freedom of speech, (3) There need be no personal liberty which is covered by Article 19(1)(b ), and (4) The property will be at the mercy of the <ORG>.
Precedent	In other words, confiscation of property of an individual would be permissible.
Precedent	462.
Precedent	It seems to me that in effect, Article 31C enables <ORG>s to adopt any policy they like and abrogate Articles 14, 19 and 31 of the Constitution at will.
Precedent	In other words, it enables the <ORG> to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	Article 14 , for instance, would be limited by the <ORG> according to its policy and not the policy of the amending body, i.e., the <ORG>, and so would be Articles 19 and 31, while these fundamental rights remain in the Constitu tion.
Precedent	It was urged that when an Act of Parliament or a <ORG> <ORG> delegates a legislative power within permissible limits the delegated legislation derives its authority from the Act of Parliament.
Precedent	It was suggested that similarly the <ORG> law would derive authority from Article 31C.
Precedent	It is true that the <ORG> law would derive authority from Article 31C but the difference between delegated legislation and the <ORG> law made under Article 31C is this : It is permissible, within limits, for a legislature to delegate its functions, and for the delegate to make law.
Precedent	Further the delegated legislation would be liable to be challenged on the ground of violation of fundamental rights regardless of the validity of the <ORG> Act .
Precedent	But a <ORG> legislature cannot be authorised to amend the Constitution and the <ORG> law deriving authority from Article 31C cannot be challenged on the ground that it infringes Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	463.
Precedent	It will be recalled that Article 19 deals not only With the right to property b ut it guarantees various rights : freedom of speech and expression; right to assemble peaceably and without arms; right to form associations or unions; right to move freely throughout the territory of India; right to practice any profession or to carry on any occupation, trade or business.
Precedent	I am unable to appreciate the reason for giving such powers to the <ORG> legislature to abrogate the above freedoms.
Precedent	In effect, <ORG> is enabling <ORG> legislatures to declare that "a citizen shall not be free; he will have no freedom of speech to criticise the policy of the <ORG>; he shall not assemble to protest against the policy; he shall be confined to a town or a district and shall not move outside his <ORG>; a resident of another state shall not enter the <ORG> which is legislating; he shall not, if a lawyer, defend people who have violated the law.
Precedent	It could indeed enable legislatures to apply one law to political opponents of the ruling party and leave members of the party outside the purview of the law.
Precedent	In sho rt, it enables <ORG> to set up complete totalitarianism in the <ORG>.
Precedent	It seems that its implications were not realised by <ORG> though Mr. <NAME> submits that every implication was deliberately intended.
Precedent	464.
Precedent	I have no doubt that the <ORG> legislatures and <ORG> in its ordinary legislative capacity will not exercise this new power conferred on them fully but I am concerned with the amplitude of the power conferred by Article 31C and not with what the legislatures may or may not do under the powers so conferred.
Precedent	465.
Precedent	I have already held that <ORG> cannot under Article 368 abrogate fundamental rights.
Precedent	<ORG> equally cannot enable the legislatures to abrogate them.
Precedent	This provision thus enables legislatures to abrogate fundamental rights and therefore must be declared unConstitutional.
Precedent	466.
Precedent	It has been urged before us that Section 3 of the 25th amendment Act is void as it in effect delegates the constituent amending power to <ORG> legislatures.
Precedent	The question arises whether Ar ticle 368 enables <ORG> to delegate its function of amending the Constitution to another body.
Precedent	It seems to me clear that it does not.
Precedent	It would be noted that Article 368 of this Constitution itself provides that amendment may be initiated only by the introduction of a bill for the purpose in either <ORG> of <ORG>.
Precedent	In other words, Article 368 does not contemplate any other mode of amendment by <ORG> and it does not equally contemplate that <ORG> could set up another body to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	467.
Precedent	It is well-settled in India that <ORG> cannot delegate its essential legislative functions.
Precedent	See: (1) Per <NAME> in re The Delhi Laws Act , 1912.
Precedent	(1951) SCR 747 at 984-5.
Precedent	(2) <NAME> v. <ORG> 1955 (1) SCR 290.
Precedent	(3) <NAME> v. State of Madhya Pradesh 1955 (1) SCR 380.
Precedent	(4) <CITATION_1340429> 1961 (1) SCR 341.
Precedent	(5) <CITATION_1417510> 1968 (3) SCR 251.
Precedent	(6) <CITATION_615469> 1959 Supp.
Precedent	(1) SCR 792.
Precedent	468.
Argument	It is also well-settled in countries, where th e courts have taken a position different than in Indian courts, that a legislature cannot create another legislative body.
Precedent	Reference may be made here to In re Initiative and Referendum Act (1919) A.C. 935 and Attorney- General of Nova Scoitia v. Attorney-General of Canada (1951) S.C.R.
Precedent	Canada 31.
Precedent	I have discussed the latter case while dealing with the question of implied limitation.
Precedent	Initiative and Referendum case is strongly relied on by Mr. <NAME> to establish that an amending power cannot be delegated.
Precedent	In this case <ORG> was concerned with the interpretation of Section 92 , head 1 of the British North America Act, 1867, which empowers a Provincial Legislature to amend the Constitution of the Province, "excepting as regards the office of the Lieutenant-Governor".
Precedent	<ORG> enacted the Initiative and Referendum Act, which in effect would compel the Lieutenant Governor to submit a proposed law to a body of voters totally distinct from the leg islature of which he is the Constitutional head, and would render him powerless to prevent it from becoming an actual law if approved by these voters.
Precedent	469.
Precedent	The judgment of <ORG> is reported in 27 Man.
Precedent	L.R.
Precedent	1, which report is not available to me, but the summary of the reasons of the learned Judges of <ORG> are given at page 936 of (1919) A.C. as follows:  The British North America Act, 1867, declared that for each Province there should be a <ORG>, in which Section 92 vested the power of law-making; the legislature could not confer that power upon a body other than itself.
Argument	The procedure proposed by the Act in question would not be an Act of a <ORG> within Section 92 , would be wholly opposed to the spirit and principles of the Canadian Constitution, and would override the <ORG> thereby provided.
Argument	Further, the power to amend the Constitution given by Section 92 , head 1, expressly expected "the office of the Lieutenant-Governor".
Argument	Section 7 of the proposed Act, while  preserving the power of veto and disallowance by the Governor-General provided for by Sections 55 and 90 of the Act of 1867, dispensed with the assent of the Lieutenant-Governor provided for by Sections 56 and 90 of that Act; even if Section 7 was not intended to dispense with that assent, Section 11 clearly did so.
Argument	The proposed Act also violated the provisions of Section 54 (inconjunction with Section 90 ) as to money bills.
Argument	470.
Argument	Their Lordships of <ORG> held at page 944: Their Lordships are of opinion that the language of the Act cannot be construed otherwise than as intended seriously to affect the position of the Lieutenant-Governor as an integral part of the <ORG>, and to detract from rights which are important in the legal theory of that position.
Argument	For if the Act is valid it compels him to submit a proposed law to a body of voters totally distinct from the <ORG> of which he is the Constitutional head, and renders him powerless to prevent it from becoming an actual law if ap proved by a majority of these voters.
Argument	It was argued that the words already referred to, which appear in Section 7 , preserve his powers of veto and disallowance.
RatioOfTheDecision	Their Lordships are unable to assent to this contention.
RatioOfTheDecision	The only powers preserved are those which relate to Acts of the Legislative Assembly, as distinguished from Bills, and the powers of veto and disallowance referred to can only be those of the Governor-General under Section 90 of the Act of 1867, and not the powers of the Lieutenant-Governor, which are at an end when a Bill has become an Act.
RatioOfTheDecision	Section 11 of the Initiative and Referendum Act is not less difficult to reconcile with the rights of the Lieutenant-Governor.
Precedent	It provides that when a proposal for repeal of some law has been approved by the majority of the electors voting, that law is automatically to be deemed repealed at the end of thirty days after the clerk of <ORG> shall have published in <ORG> a statement of the result of the vote.
Precedent	Thus the Lieutenant-G overnor appears to be wholly excluded from the new legislative authority.
Precedent	471.
RatioOfTheDecision	I have set out this passage in extenso because this deals with one part of the reasoning given by <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Regarding the Other part i.e.
RatioOfTheDecision	whether the <ORG> could confer that power on a body other than itself, <ORG> observed at page 945:  Having said so much, their Lordships, following their usual practice of not deciding more than is strictly necessary, will not deal finally with another difficulty which those who contend for the validity of this Act have to meet.
RatioOfTheDecision	But they think it right, as the point has been raised in the Court below, to advert to it.
RatioOfTheDecision	Section 92 of the Act of 1867 entrusts the legislative power in a Province to its legislature, and to that <ORG> only.
RatioOfTheDecision	No doubt a body, with power of legislation on the subjects entrusted to it so ample as that enjoyed by a Provincial <ORG> in Canada, could, while preserving its own capacity intact, seek the assistance of subordinate ag encies, as had been done when in <NAME> v. The <NAME> 9 A.C. 117 <ORG> was held entitled to entrust to <ORG> authority to enact regulations relating to taverns; but it does not follow that it can create and endow with its own capacity a new legislative power not created by the Act to which it owes its own existence.
RatioOfTheDecision	Their Lordships do no more than draw attention to the gravity of the Constitutional questions which thus arise.
RatioOfTheDecision	(Emphasis supplied)  472.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is interesting to note that this position was indicated by Sir <NAME>, a member of <ORG>, while <NAME> v. The <NAME> 9 A.C. 117 was being argued.
RatioOfTheDecision	This appears from <NAME> on <ORG> at p. 387: Upon the argument before <ORG> in <NAME> v. The <NAME>, Mr. <NAME> contended that under this sub-section, (Section 92(1) of Canadian Constitution) provincial legislatures "could do what Lord <NAME>, no doubt correctly, said in The <NAME> v. <NAME> [1878] 3 A.C. 905 the Indian legi slature could not do,-abdicate their whole legislative functions in favour of another body."
RatioOfTheDecision	But, as Sir <NAME> remarked, this they cannot do.
RatioOfTheDecision	"They remain invested with a responsibility.
Precedent	Everything is done by them, and such officers as they create and give discretion to.
Precedent	473.
Precedent	The learned Attorney-General submitted that this case decided only that in the absence of clear and unmistakable language in Section 92 , head 1, the power which the <ORG> possesses through a person directly representing the <ORG> cannot be abrogated.
Precedent	It is true that this was the actual decision but the subsequent observations, which I have set out above, clearly show that <ORG> was prepared to imply limitations as <ORG> had done on the amending power conferred on <ORG> by Section 92 , head 1.
Precedent	474.
Precedent	The Attorney General said that the scope of this decision was referred to in <NAME> v. The King (1926) A.C. 482 where at page 495 reference is made to this case in the following words:   In the case of In re Initiative and Referendum Act Lord <NAME>, in declaring the judgment of the <ORG> referred to "the impropriety in the absence of clear and unmistakable language of construing Section 92 as permitting the abrogation of any power which the <ORG> possesses through a person directly representing it"; an observation which applies with equal force to Section 91 of the Act of 1867 and to the abrogation of a power which remains vested in the <ORG> itself.
Precedent	475.
Precedent	But this passage again dealt with the actual point decided and not the obiter dicta.
Precedent	476.
Precedent	The first para of the head note in <NAME>'s (1926) A.C. 482 case gives in brief the actual decision of <ORG> as follows:  Section 1025 of the Criminal Code of Canada, if and so far as it is intended to prevent the King in Council from giving effective leave to appeal against an order of <ORG> in a criminal case, is invalid.
Precedent	The legislative authority of <ORG> as to criminal law and procedure, under Section 9 1 of the British North America Act, 1867, is confined to action to be taken in Canada.
Precedent	Further, an enactment annulling the royal prerogative to grant special leave to appeal would be inconsistent with the Judicial Committee Acts 1833 and 1844, and therefore would be invalid under Section 2 of the Colonial Laws Validity Act, 1865.
Precedent	The royal assent to the Criminal Code could not give validity to an enactment which was void by imperial statute; exclusion of the prerogative could be accomplished only by an Imperial statute.
Precedent	477.
RatioOfTheDecision	For the aforesaid reasons I am unable to agree with the Attorney General and I hold that the Initiative and Referendum Act case shows that limitations can be implied in an amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	Mr. <NAME> seeks to distinguish this case on another ground.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to him, these observations were obiter dicta, but even if they are treated as considered obiter dicta, they add nothing to the principles governing delegated legislation, for this passage merely repeats what had been laid down as fa r back as 1878 in The Queen v. Burah 5 I.A.
RatioOfTheDecision	178 : (1878) 3 A.C. 889; 904 : 905, where <ORG> in a classical passage, observed:  But their Lordships are of opinion that the doctrine of the majority of the <ORG> is erroneous, and that it rests upon a mistaken view of the powers of <ORG>, and indeed of the nature and principles of legislation.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> has powers expressly limited by the Act of <ORG> which created it, and it can, of course, do nothing beyond the limits which circumscribe these powers.
RatioOfTheDecision	But when acting within those limits, it is not in any sense an agent or delegate of <ORG>, but has, and was intended to have, plenary powers of legislation, as large, and of the same nature, as those of <ORG> itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	The established <ORG>s of Justice, when a question arises whether the prescribed limits have been exceeded must of necessity determine that question; and the only way in which they can properly do so, is by looking to th e terms of the instrument by which, affirmatively, the legislative powers were created, and by which, negatively, they are restricted.
RatioOfTheDecision	If what has been done is legislation, within the general scope of the affirmative words which give the power, and if it violates no express condition or restriction by which that power is limited (in which category would of course be included any Act of the Imperial Parliament at variance with it), it is not for <ORG> to inquire further or to enlarge constructively those conditions and restrictions.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> further says that having laid down the law as set out above, <ORG> added:  Their Lordships agree that the Governor-General in <ORG> could not, by any form of enactment; create in India, and arm with general legislative authority, a new legislative power, not created or authorised by the <ORG>'s Act.
Precedent	478.
Precedent	We are unable to agree with him that the obiter dicta of the Judicial Committee deals with the same subject as <NAME>'s 5 I.A.
Precedent	178 case.
Precedent	<NAME>'s' case was not concerned with the power to amend the Constitution but was concerned only with legislation enacted by the Indian <ORG>.
Precedent	This clearly appears from the passage just cited from <NAME>.
Precedent	The Governor-General in Council had no power to amend the Government of India Act, under which it functioned.
Precedent	479.
Precedent	Reference was also made to the observations of one of us in Delhi Municipality v. <ORG> (1968) S.C. 1232 at p. 1266 where I had observed as follows: Apart from authority, in my view <ORG> has full power to delegate legislative authority to subordinate bodies.
Precedent	This power flows, in my judgment, from Article 246 of the Constitution.
Precedent	The word "exclusive" means exclusive of any other legislation and not exclusive of any subordinate body.
Precedent	There is, however, one restriction in this respect and that is also contained in Article 246.
Precedent	<ORG> must pass a law in respect of an item or items of the relevant list.
Precedent	Negatively this means that <ORG> cannot abdicate its functi ons.
Precedent	480.
Precedent	Reference was also invited to another passage where I had observed: The case of 1919 AC 935 provides an instance of abdication of functions by a legislature.
Precedent	No inference can be drawn from this case that delegations of the type with which we are concerned amount to abdication of functions.
Precedent	481.
Precedent	It is clear these observations are contrary to many decisions of this <ORG> and, as I said, I made these observations apart from authority.
Precedent	482.
Precedent	But neither this <ORG> nor <ORG> in <NAME> v. <NAME> 5 I.A.
Precedent	178 : (1878) 3 A.C. 889 were concerned with an amending power, and the importance of the obiter observations of <ORG> lies in the fact that even in exercise of its amending power the legislature could not "create and endow with its own capacity a new legislative power not created by the Act to which it owes its own existence," and the fact that in Canada the doctrine of limited delegated legislation does not prevail as it does in India.
Precedent	483.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has been urged before us that in  fact there has been no delegation of the amending powers to the <ORG> legislatures by Article 31C and what has been done is that Article 31C lifts the ban imposed by Part III from certain laws.
RatioOfTheDecision	I am unable to appreciate this idea of the lifting of the ban.
RatioOfTheDecision	Fundamental rights remain as part of the Constitution and on the face of them they guarantee to every citizen these fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	But as soon as the <ORG> legislates under Article 31C and the law abrogates or takes away these Constitutional rights, these fundamental rights cease to have any effect.
RatioOfTheDecision	The amendment is then made not by <ORG> as the extent of the amendment is not known till the <ORG> legislates.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is when the <ORG> legislates that the extent of the abrogation or abridgement of the fundamental rights becomes clear.
RatioOfTheDecision	To all intents and purposes it seems to me that it is <ORG> legislation that effects an amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	If it be assumed that Article 31C does not enable the <ORG>s to amend the Constitution then Article 31C  would be ineffective because the law which in effect abridges or takes away the fundamental rights would have been passed not in the form required by Article 368 , i.e.
RatioOfTheDecision	by 2/3rd of the majority of <ORG> but by another body which is not recognised in Article 368 and would be void on that ground.
Precedent	484.
Precedent	The learned Solicitor General, relying on <NAME> v. <NAME> (1968) A.C. 717; 743 urged that there can be implied amendment of the Constitution and Article 31C may be read as an implied amendment of Article 368.
Precedent	What <ORG> decided in this case was that a bill having received a certificate in the hands of the Speaker that the number of votes cast in favour thereof in <ORG> amounted to no lass than two-thirds of the whole number of Members of the <ORG> in effect amounted to a bill for the amendment or repeal of any of the provisions of the order, and the words "amendment or repeal" included implied amendment.
Precedent	485.
Precedent	<NAME>, J., speaking for the  <ORG>, observed: Apart from the proviso to Sub-section (4) therefore the board has found no reason for not construing the words "amend or repeal" in the earlier part of Section 29(4) as extending to amendment or repeal by inconsistent law.... A bill which, if it becomes an Act, does amend or repeal some provision of the order is a bill "for the amendment or repeal of a provision of the order.
Precedent	Later, he observed:  The bill which became the Act was a bill for an amendment of Section 24 of the Constitution simply because its terms were inconsistent with that section.
Precedent	It is the operation that the bill will have upon becoming law which gives it its Constitutional character, not any particular label which may be given to it.
Precedent	A bill described as one for the amendment of the Constitution, which contained no operative provision to amend the Constitution would not require the prescribed formalities to become a valid law whereas a bill which upon its passing into law would, if valid, alter the Constitutio n would not be valid without compliance with those formalities.
Precedent	486.
Precedent	We are not here concerned with the question which was raised before <ORG> because no one has denied that Article 31C is an amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	The only question we are concerned with is whether Article 31C can be read to be an implied amendment of Article 368 , and if so read, is it valid, i.e., within the powers of <ORG> to amend Article 368 itself.
Precedent	487.
Precedent	It seems to me that Article 31C cannot be read to be an implied amendment of Article 368 because it opens with the words "notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13" and Article 31C does not say that "notwithstanding anything contained in Article 368."
Precedent	What Article 31C does is that it empowers legislatures, subject to the condition laid down in Article 31C itself, to take away or abridge rights conferred by Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	At any rate, if it is deemed to be an amendment of Article 368 , it is beyond the powers conferred by Article 368 itself.
Precedent	Article 368 does not enable <ORG> to constitute another legislature to amend the Constitution, in its exercise of the power to amend Article 368 itself.
Precedent	488.
Precedent	For the aforesaid reasons I hold that Section 3 of the Constitution (Twenty-fifth Amendment) Act 1971 is void as it delegates power to legislatures to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	PART-VII.-Twenty-Ninth Amendment The Constitution (Twenty-Ninth Amendment) reads:  2.
Precedent	Amendment of Ninth Schedule In the Ninth Schedule to the Constitution after entry 64 and before the Explanation, the following entries shall be inserted, namely:  65.
Precedent	The Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act, 1969 (Kerala Act 35 of 1969).
Precedent	66.
Precedent	The Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act, 1971 (Kerala Act 25 of 1971).
Precedent	489.
Fact	The effect of the insertion of the two Kerala Acts in the Ninth Schedule is that the provisions of Article 31-B get attracted.
Fact	Article 31-B which was inserted by Section 5 of the Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951, reads:  Insertion of new Article 31B.
Fact	490.
Fact	After Artic le 31A of the Constitution as inserted by Section 4 , the following article shall be inserted, namaly:  31B.
Fact	Validation of certain Acts and Regulations Without prejudice to the generality of the provisions contained in Article 31A , none of the Acts and Regulations specified in the Ninth Schedule nor any of the provisions thereof shall be deemed to be void, or ever to have become void, on the ground that such Act, Regulation or provision is inconsistent with, or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by, any provisions of this Part, and notwithstanding any judgment, decree or order of any court or tribunal to the contrary, each of the said Acts and Regulations shall, subject to the power of any competent <ORG> to repeal or amend it continue in force.
Precedent	491.
Precedent	The First Amendment had also inserted Article 3-A and the Niath Schedule including 13 State enactments dealing with agrarian, reforms.
Precedent	492.
Fact	Before dealing with the points debated before us, it is necessary to mention that a new Article 31 -A was substituted by the Constitution (Fourth Amendment) Act, 1955, for the original article with retrospective effect.
Fact	The new article contained original Article 31A(1) as Clause (a) and added Clauses (b) to (e) and also changed the nature of the protective umbrella.
Fact	The relevant part of Article 31A(1) as substituted has already been set out.
Precedent	493.
Fact	Under Article 31-A as inserted by the First Amendment a law was protected even if it was inconsistent with or took away or abridged any rights conferred by any provisions of Part III.
Fact	Under the Fourth Amendment the protective umbrella extended to only Article 14 , Article 19 or Article 31.
Fact	The Seventeenth Amendment further amended the definition of the word "estate" in Article 31A.
Fact	It also added seven Acts to the Ninth Schedule.
Precedent	494.
Fact	The argument of Mr. <NAME>, on this part of the case, was two fold.
RulingByLowerCourt	First, he contended, that Article 31B , as originally inserted, had intimate relations with agrarian reforms, because at that stage Article 31-A dealt only with  agrarian reforms.
RulingByLowerCourt	The words "without prejudice to the generality of the provisions contained in Article 31A ", according to him, pointed to this connection.
RulingByLowerCourt	He, in effect, said that Article 31-B having this original meaning did not change the meaning or its scope when a new Article 31-A containing Clauses (b) to (e) were included.
RulingByLowerCourt	495.
Argument	I am unable to accede to these contentions.
Argument	The ambit of Article 31-B has been determined by this Court in three decisions.
Argument	<CITATION_49043> (1952) S.C.R.
Argument	889; 914-15, <NAME>, C.J., rejected the limited meaning suggested above by <NAME>, and observed:  "There is nothing in Article 31-B to indicate that the specific mention of certain statutes was only intended to illustrate the application of the general words of Article 31-A.
Precedent	The opening words of Article 31-B are only intended to make clear that Article 31-A should not be restricted in its application by reason of anything contained in Article 31-B and are in no way calculated to restrict the application of the lat ter article or of the enactments referred to therein to acquisition of "estates."
Precedent	496.
Precedent	He held that the decision in <NAME> (1945) F.C.R.
Precedent	195 case afforded no useful analogy.
Precedent	497.
Precedent	<CITATION_45759> (1952) S.C.R.
Precedent	1020-1037.
Precedent	<NAME>, J., repelled the argument in these words:  In my opinion the observations in <NAME> case far from supporting the contention raised negatives it.
Precedent	Article 31-B specifically validates certain acts mentioned in the Schedule despite the provisions of Article 31-A and is not illustrative of Article 31-A.
Precedent	but stands independent of it.
Precedent	498.
Precedent	<CITATION_1681028> (1965) 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	636-648, to which decision I was a party, <NAME>, C.J., observed that " Article 31-B is not governed by Article 31-A and that Article 31-B is a Constitutional device to place the specified statutes beyond any attack on the ground that they infringe Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	499.
Precedent	I may mention that the validity of the device was not questioned before the Court then.
Precedent	500.
Precedent	But even though I  do not accept the contention that Article 31-B can be limited by what is contained in Article 31-A , the question arises whether the Twenty-Ninth Amendment is valid.
Precedent	501.
RatioOfTheDecision	I have held that Article 368 does not enable <ORG> to abrogate or take away fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	If this is so, it does not enable <ORG> to do this by any means, including the device of Article 31-B and the Ninth Schedule.
RatioOfTheDecision	This device of Article 31-B and the Ninth Schedule is bad insofar as it protects statutes even if they take away fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, it is necessary to declare that the Twenty-Ninth Amendment is ineffective to protect the impugned Acts if they take away fundamental rights.
Precedent	502.
Precedent	In this connection I may deal with the argument that the device of Article 31B and the Ninth Schedule has uptill now been upheld by this <ORG> and it is now too late to impeach it.
Precedent	But the point now raised before us has never been raised and debated before.
Precedent	As Lord <NAME> observed in <ORG> v. <NAME> torney-General for Canada (1931) A.C. 310; 317.
Precedent	Their Lordships entertain no doubt that time alone will not validate an Act which when challenged is found to be ultra vires; nor will a history of a gradual series of advances till this boundary is finally crossed avail to protect the ultimate encroachment.
Precedent	503.
Precedent	If any further authority is needed, I may refer to Attorney-General for Australia v. The Queen and <ORG> (1957) A.C. 288; 323.
Precedent	<ORG>, while considering the questionwhether certain sections of the Conciliation and Arbitration Act , 1904 1952 were ultra vires inasmuch as <ORG> had been invested with the executive powers alongwith the judicial powers, referred to the point why for a quarter of century no litigant had attacked the validity of this obviously illegitimate union, and observed: Whatever the reason may be, just as there was a patent invalidity in the original Act which for a number of years went  unchallenged, so far a greater number of years an invalidity which to their Lordships as to the majority of <ORG> has been convincingly demonstrated, has been disregarded.
Precedent	Such clear conviction must find expression in the appropriate judgment.
Precedent	504.
Precedent	We had decided not to deal with the merits of individual cases and accordingly Counsel had not addressed any arguments on the impugned Acts passed by <ORG>.
Precedent	It would be for <ORG> to decide whether the impugned Acts take away fundamental rights.
Precedent	If they do, they will have to be struck down.
Precedent	If they only abridge fundamental rights, it would be for <ORG> to determine whether they are reasonable abridgements essential in the public interest.
Precedent	505.
Precedent	Broadly speaking, Constitutional amendments hitherto made in, Article 19 and Article 15 and, the agrarian laws enacted by various States furnish illustrations of reasonable abridgement of fundamental rights in the public interest.
Precedent	506.
Fact	It was said during the  arguments that one object of Article 31-B was to prevent time- consuming litigation, which held up implementation of urgent reforms.
Fact	If a petition is filed in <ORG> or a suit is filed in a subordinate court or a point raised before a magistrate, challenging the validity of an enactment it takes years before the validity of an enactment is finally determined.
RatioOfTheDecision	Surely, this is not a good reason to deprive persons of their fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	There are other ways available to the <ORG> to expedite the decision.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may for example propose ordinary legislation to enable parties to approach <ORG> for transfer of such cases to <ORG> for determination of substantial questions of interpretation of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	PART VIII : Conclusions To summarise, I hold that:  (a) <NAME> (1967) 2 S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	762 case declared that a Constitutional amendment would be bad if it infringed Article 13(2 ), as this applied not only to ordinary legislation but also to an amendment of the Constituti on.
RatioOfTheDecision	(b) <NAME> (1967) 2 S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	762 case did not decide whether Article 13(2) can be amended under Article 368 or determine the exact meaning of the expression "amendment of this Constitution" in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	(c) The expression "amendment of this Constitution" does not enable <ORG> to abrogate or take away, fundamental rights or to completely change the fundamental features of the Constitution so as to destroy its identity.
RatioOfTheDecision	Within these limits <ORG> can amend every article.
Fact	(d) The Constitution (Twenty-fourth Amendment) Act, 1971, as interpreted by me, has been validly enacted.
Fact	(e) Article 368 does not enable <ORG> in its constituent capacity to delegate its function of amending the Constitution to another legislature or to itself in its ordinary legislative capacity.
Fact	(f) Section 2 of the Constitution (Twenty-fifth Amendment) Act, 1971, as interpreted by me, is valid.
Fact	(g) Section 3 of the Constitution (Twenty-fifth Amendment) Act, 1971 is void as it delegates power to legislatures t o amend the Constitution.
Fact	(h) The Constitution (Twenty-Ninth Amendment) Act, 1971 is ineffective to protect the impugned Acts if they abrogate or take away fundamental rights.
Fact	<ORG> will decide whether the impugned Acts take away fundamental rights or only abridge them, and in the latter case whether they effect reasonable abridgements in the public interest.
Precedent	507.
Precedent	<ORG> will determine the validity of the Constitution (Twenty- sixth Amendment) Act , 1971 in accordance with this judgment, and the law.
Precedent	508.
Precedent	The cases are remitted to <ORG> to be decided in accordance with this judgment, and the law.
Precedent	The parties will bear their own costs.
Precedent	<NAME> and <NAME>, JJ.
Precedent	509.
Fact	All the six writ petitions involve common questions as to the validity of the 24th, 25th and 29th amendments to the Constitution.
Precedent	It is not necessary to set out the facts which have already been succinctly stated in the judgment of the learned Chief Justice.
Precedent	510.
Precedent	It was considered, when the larger  bench was constituted, that the decision of the questions before us would hinge largely on the correctness or otherwise of the decision of this court in <CITATION_21266288> .
Precedent	2 S.C.R.
Precedent	762, according to which it was held, by majority, that Article 13(2) of the Constitution was applicable to Constitutional amendments made under Article 368 and that for that reason the fundamental rights in Part III could not be abriged in any manner or taken away.
Precedent	The decision in <NAME> has become academic, for even on the assumption that the majority decision in that case was not correct, the result on the questions now raised before us, in our opinion, would just be the same.
Precedent	The issues that have been raised travel far beyond that decision and the main question to be determined now is the scope, ambit and extent of the amending power conferred by Article 368.
Precedent	On that will depend largely the decision of the other matters arising out of the 25th and the 29th amendments.
Precedent	511.
RatioOfTheDecision	The respective positions adopted by <ORG> unsel for the parties diverge widely and are irreconcilable.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the side of the petitioners, it is maintained inter alia that the power of the amending body (<ORG>) under Article 368 is of a limited nature.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution gave the Indian citizens the basic freedoms and a polity or a form of government which were meant to be lasting and permanent.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, the amending power does not extend to alteration or destruction of all or any of the essential features, basic elements and fundamental principles of the Constitution which power, it is said, vests in the Indian people alone who gave the Constitution to themselves, as is stated in its Preamble.
RatioOfTheDecision	512.
RatioOfTheDecision	The respondents, on the other hand, claim an unlimited power for the amending body.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is claimed that it has the full constituent power which a legal sovereign can exercise provided the conditions laid down in Article 368 are satisfied.
RatioOfTheDecision	The content and amplitude of the power is so wide that, if it is so desired, all rights contained in Part III (Fundam ental Rights) such as freedom of speech and expression; the freedom to form associations or unions and the various other freedoms guaranteed by Article 19(1) as also the right to freedom of religion as contained in Articles 25 to 28 together with the protection of interests of minorities (to mention the most prominent ones) can be abrogated and taken away.
RatioOfTheDecision	Similarly, Article 32 which confers the right to move this Court, if any fundamental right is breached, can be repealed or abrogated.
RatioOfTheDecision	The directive principles in Part IV can be altered drastically or even abrogated.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is claimed that democracy can be replaced by any other form of government which may be wholly undemocratic, the federal structure can be replaced by a unitary system by abolishing all the States and the right of judicial review can be completely taken away.
RatioOfTheDecision	Even the Preamble which declares that the People of India gave to themselves the Constitution, to constitute India into a Sovereign Democratic Republic for securing the great objectives me ntioned therein can be amended; indeed it can be completely repealed.
Precedent	Thus, according to the respondents, short of total abrogation or repeal of the Constitution, the amending body is omnipotent under Article 368 and the Constitution can, at any point of time, be amended by way of variation, addition or repeal so long as no vacuum is left in the governance of the country.
Precedent	513.
Precedent	These petitions which have been argued for a very long time raise momentus issues of great Constitutional importance.
Precedent	Our Constitution is unique, apart from being the longest in the world.
Precedent	It is meant for the second largest population with diverse people speaking different languages and professing varying religions.
Precedent	It was chiselled and shaped by great political leaders and legal luminaries, most of whom, had taken an active part in the struggle for freedom from the British yoke and who knew what domination of a foreign rule meant in the way of deprivation of basic freedoms and from the point of view of exploitation of the millions of  Indians.
Precedent	The Constitution is an organic document which must grow and it must take stock of the vast socioeconomic problems, particularly, of improving the lot of the common man consistent with his dignity and the unity of the nation.
Precedent	514.
Precedent	We may observe at the threshold that we do not propose to examine the matters raised before us on the assumption that <ORG> will exercise the power in the way claimed on behalf of the respondents nor did the latter contend that it will be so done.
Precedent	But while interpreting Constitutional provisions it is necessary to determine their width or reach in fact the area of operation of the power, its minimum and maximum dimensions cannot be demarcated or determined without fully examining the rival claims.
Precedent	Unless that is done, the ambit, content, scope and extent of the amending power cannot be properly and correctly decided.
Precedent	515.
Precedent	For our purposes it is not necessary to go prior to the year 1934.
Precedent	It was in that year that <ORG> made the demand for a Const ituent <ORG> as part of its policy.
Precedent	This demand was repeated in the Central Legislative <ORG> in 1937 by the represetatives of the <ORG>.
Precedent	By what is known as the Simla Conference 1945 the <ORG> repeated its stand that India could only accept the Constitution drawn by the people.
Precedent	After the end of World War II the demand was put forward very strongly by the Indian leaders including <NAME>.
Precedent	Sir <NAME> representing Britain had also accepted the idea that an elected body of Indians should frame the Indian Constitution.
Precedent	(The facts have been taken mainly from the Indian Constitution, Cornerstone of a Nation, by <NAME>).
Precedent	In September 1945 the newly elected <ORG> announced that it favoured the creation of a constituent body in India.
Precedent	Elections were to be held so that the newly elected provincial legislatures could act as electoral bodies for the Constituent <ORG>.
Precedent	A parliamentary delegation was sent to India in January 1946 and this was followed by what is  known as <ORG>.
Precedent	There were a great deal of difficulties owing to the differences between the approach of <ORG> and <ORG> led by Mr. <NAME>.
Fact	<ORG> devised a plan which was announced on May 16, 1946.
Fact	By the end of June, both <ORG> and the <ORG> had accepted it with reservations.
Fact	<ORG> was elected between July-August 1946 as a result of the suggestion contained in the statement of <ORG>.
Fact	<ORG> efforts to effect an agreement between the <ORG> and <ORG> having failed, the partition of the country came as a consequence of the declaration of the British Government on June 3, 1947.
Fact	As a mult of that declaration certain changes took place in <ORG>.
Fact	There was also readjustment of representation of Indian States from time to time between December 1946 and November 1949.
Fact	Many Smaller States merged into the provinces, many united to from union of States and  some came to be administered as commissioner's provinces.
Fact	There was thus a gradual process by which <ORG> became fully representative of the various communities and interests, political, intellectual, social and cultural.
Fact	It was by virtue of Section 8 of the Indian Independence Act 1947 that <ORG> was vested with the legal authority to frame a Constitution for India.
Precedent	516.
Fact	The first meeting of <ORG> took place on December 9, 1946 when the swearing in of members and election of a temporary president to conduct the business until the installation of a permanent head, took place.
Fact	On December 13, 1946 <NAME> moved the famous "Objectives Resolution" giving an outline, aims and objects of the Constitution.
Fact	This resolution was actually passed on January 22, 1947 by all members of <ORG> (standing) and it declared among other matters that all power and authority of the sovereign Independent India, its constituent parts and o rgans of Government are derived from the people.
Fact	By November 26, 1949 the deliberations of <ORG> had concluded and the Constitution had been framed.
Fact	As recited in the Preamble it was on that date that the people of India in <ORG> adopted, enacted and gave to themselves "this Constitution" which according to Article 393 was to be called "The Constitution of India".
Fact	In accordance with Article 394 that Article and the other Articles mentioned therein were to come into force at once but the remaining provisions of the Constitution were to come into force on the 26th day of January 1950.
Fact	517.
Fact	Before the scheme of the Constitution is examined in some detail it is necessary to give the pattern which was followed in framing it.
Fact	<ORG> was unfettered by any previous commitment in evolving a Constitutional pattern "suitable to the genius and requirements of the Indian people as a whole".
Fact	The <ORG> had before it the experience of the working of the Government o f India Act 1935, several features of which could be accepted for the new Constitution.
Fact	Our Constitution borrowed a great deal from the Constitutions of other countries, e.g.
Fact	United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, Ireland, United States of America and Switzerland.
Precedent	The Constitution being supreme all the organs and bodies owe their existence to it.
Precedent	None can claim superiority over the other and each of them has to function within the four-corners of the Constitutional provisions.
Precedent	The Preamble embodies the great purposes, objectives and the policy underlying its provisions apart from the basic character of the State which was to come into existence i.e.
Precedent	a Sovereign Democratic Republic.
Precedent	Parts III and IV which embody the fundamental rights and directive principles of state policy have been described as the conscience of the Constitution(The Indian Constitution by <NAME> p. 50) The legislative power distributed between <ORG> and <ORG> cannot be so exercised as take away or abrid ge the fundamental rights contained in Part III.
Precedent	Powers of the <ORG> and the <ORG> are further curtailed by conferring the right to enforce fundamental rights contained in Part III by moving <ORG> for a suitable relief See generally, <NAME> in <CITATION_1857950>  S.C.R.
Precedent	88 at pp.
Precedent	96-97, Article 32 itself has been constituted a fundamental right.
Precedent	Part IV containing the directive principles of State policy was inspired largely by similar provisions in the Constitution of the Eire Republic (1937).
