RatioOfTheDecision	The observations of <NAME> in <CITATION_554839>  S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	597 which have become locus classicus need alone be repeated in this connection.
RatioOfTheDecision	Judicial review is undertaken by the courts "not out of any desire to tilt at legislative authority in a crusador's spirit, but in discharge of a duty plainly laid upon them by the Constitution."
RatioOfTheDecision	The respondents have also contended that to let the court have judicial review over Constitutional amendments would mean involving the court in political questions.
RatioOfTheDecision	To this the answer may be given in the words of Lord <NAME> in <ORG> v. <ORG>A.C.
RatioOfTheDecision	235 at 310: The problem to be solved will often be not so much legal as political, social or economic, yet i t must be solved by a court of law.
RatioOfTheDecision	For where the dispute is, as here, not only between <ORG> and citizen but between <ORG> and intervening States on the one hand and citizens and States on the other, it is only the <ORG> that can decide the issue, it is vain to invoke the voice of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is ample evidence in the Constitution itself to indicate that it creates a system of checks and balances by reason of which powers are so distributed that none of the three organs it sets up can become so pre-dominant as to disable the others from exercising and discharging powers and functions entrusted to them.
RatioOfTheDecision	Though the Constitution does not lay down the principle of separation of powers in all its rigidity as is the case in the United Constitution but it envisages such a separation to a degree as was found in <NAME>'s case.
RatioOfTheDecision	The judicial review provided expressly in our Constitution by means of Article 226 and 32 is one of the features upon which hinges the system of checks and balances.
Precedent	Apa rt from that, as already stated, the necessity for judicial decision on the competence or otherwise of an Act arises from the very federal nature of a Constitution (per <ORG> in Att.
Precedent	Gen. for the Commonwealth of Australia v. <ORG>  A.C. 237 and Ex parte <ORG>, In re <NAME>.
Precedent	(1925) 37 C.L.R.
Precedent	36 at p. 58.
RatioOfTheDecision	The function of interpretation of a Constitution being thus assigned to the judicial power of the <ORG>, the question whether the subject of a law is within the ambit of one or more powers of the legislature conferred by the Constitution would always be a question of interpretation of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may be added that at no stage the respondents have contested the proposition that the validity of a Constitutional amendment can be the subject of review by this Court.
Precedent	The Advocate General of Maharasthra has characterised judicial review as undemocratic.
Precedent	That cannot, however, be so in our Constitution because of the provisions relating to the appointment of judges, th e specific restriction to which the fundamental rights are made subject, the deliberate exclusion of the due process clause in Article 21 and the affirmation in Article 141 that judges declare but not make law.
Precedent	To this may be added the none two rigid amendatory process which authorises amendment by means oft 2/3 majority and the additional requirement of ratification.
Precedent	616.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to the learned Attorney General the entire argument on the basis of implied limitations is fundamentally wrong.
RatioOfTheDecision	He has also relied greatly on the decision in <NAME>'s case and other similar decisions.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is pointed out that there can be no inherent limitation on the power of amendment having regard to the purpose for which the power is needed.
RatioOfTheDecision	The argument about the non-amendability of the essential framework of the Constitution is illusive because every part of a Constitutional document admits of the possibility of imperfect drafting or ambiguity.
RatioOfTheDecision	Even basic concepts or ideals undergo progressive changes.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has been strenuously  urged that the Constitution read as a whole did not contemplate the perpetuation of the existing social and economic inequalities and a duty has been cast on the <ORG> to organise a new social order.
Precedent	The Attorney General quoted the opinion of several writers and authors in support of his contention that there must be express words of limitation in a provision which provides for amendment of the Constitution from which it follows that no implied limitations can be read therein.
Precedent	617.
Precedent	The correct approach to the question of limitations which may be implied in any legislative provisions including a Constitutional document has to be made from the point of view of interpretation.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is not a novel theory or a doctrine which has to be treated as an innovation of those who evolve heterodox methods to substantiate their own thesis.
RatioOfTheDecision	The argument that there are no implied limitations because there are no express limitations is a contradiction in terms.
RatioOfTheDecision	Implied limitations can only arise where there are no express limit ations.
Precedent	The contention of the learned Attorney General that no implications can be read in an amending power in a Constitution must be repelled in the words of <NAME> in <NAME> v. Commissioner of Taxation (N.S.W.)
Precedent	[1936-37] 56 C.L.R.
Precedent	657:  Since the <ORG>' case a notion seems to have gained currency that in interpreting the Constitution no implications can be made.
Precedent	Such a method of construction would defeat the intention of any instrument, but of all instruments a written Constitution seems the last to which it could be applied  618.
Precedent	We are equally unable to hold that in the light of the Preamble, the entire scheme of the Constitution the relevant provisions thereof and the context in which the material expressions are used in Article 368 no implied limitations arise to the exercise of the power of amendment.
Precedent	The respondents do not dispute, that, certain limitations arise by necessary implication e.g., the Constitution cannot be abrogated or repealed in its entirety and that the India's polity has to be a  Sovereign Democratic Republic, apart from several other implications arising from Article 368 which have been noticed.
Precedent	619.
RatioOfTheDecision	The argument that the <ORG> cannot grow and that the objectives set out in the Preamble cannot be achieved unless the amending power has the ambit and the width of the power of <ORG> itself or the People themselves appears to be based on grounds which do not have a solid bask The Constitution makers provided for development of the country in all the fields social, economic and political.
RatioOfTheDecision	The structure of the Constitution has been erected on the concept of an egalitarian society.
RatioOfTheDecision	But the Constitution makers did not desire that it should be a society where the citizen will not enjoy the various freedoms and such rights as are the basic elements of those freedoms, e.g., the right to equality, freedom of religion etc., so that his dignity as an individual may be maintained.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has been strongly urged on behalf of the respondents that a citizen cannot have any dignity if  he is economically or socially backward.
RatioOfTheDecision	No one can dispute such a statement but the whole scheme underlying the Constitution is to bring about economic and social changes without taking away the dignity of the individual.
RatioOfTheDecision	Indeed, the same has been placed on such a high pedestal that to ensure the freedoms etc.
RatioOfTheDecision	their infringement has been made justiciable by the highest court in the land.
Precedent	The dictum of <NAME> in Kerala Education Bill case paints the true picture in which there must be harmony between Parts III and IV; indeed the picture will get distorted and blurred if any vital provision out of them is cut out or denuded of its identity.
Precedent	620.
Precedent	The basic structure of the Constitution is not a vague concept and the apprehensions expressed on behalf of the respondents that neither the citizen nor the <ORG> would be able to understand it are unfounded.
Precedent	If the historical background, the Preamble, the entire scheme of the Constitution, the relevant provisions thereof including Article 368 are kept in mind  there can be no difficulty in discerning that the following can be regarded as the basic elements of the Constitutional structure.
Precedent	(These cannot be catalogued but can only be illustrated).
Precedent	1.
Precedent	The supremacy of the Constitution.
Precedent	2.
Precedent	Republican and Democratic form of Government and sovereignty of the country.
Precedent	3.
Precedent	Secular and federal character of the Constitution.
Precedent	4.
Precedent	Demarcation of power between the legislature, the executive and the judiciary.
Precedent	5.
Precedent	The dignity of the individual (secured by the various freedoms and basic rights in Part III and the mandate to build a welfare State contained in Part IV.
Precedent	6.
Precedent	The unity and the integrity of the nation.
Precedent	621.
RatioOfTheDecision	The entire discussion from the point of view of the meaning of the expression "amendment" as employed in Article 368 and the limitations which arise by implications leads to the result that the amending power under Article 368 is neither narrow nor unlimited.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the footing on which we have proceeded the validity of the 25th amendment can be sustained if Article  368 , as it originally stood and after the amendment, is read in the way we have read it.
RatioOfTheDecision	The insertion of Articles 13(4) and 368(3) and the other amendments made will not affect the result, namely, that the power in Article 368 is wide enough to permit amendment of each and every Article of the Constitution by way of addition, variation or repeal so long as its basic elements are not abrogated or denuded of their identity.
RatioOfTheDecision	622.
RatioOfTheDecision	We may next deal with the validity of the Constitution (25th Amendment) Act .
Statute	Section 2 of the Amending Act provides:  2.
Statute	In Article 31 of the Constitution,-  (a) for Clause (2), the following clause shall be substituted, namely:  (2) No property shall be compulsorily acquired or requisitioned save for a public purpose and save by authority of a law which provides for acquisition or requisitioning of the property for a amount which may be fixed by such law or which may be determined in accordance with such principles and given in such manner as may be specified in such law; and no su ch law shall be called in question in any court on the ground that the amount so fixed or determined is not adequate or that the whole or any part of such amount is to be given otherwise than in cash:  Provided...  (b) after Clause (2A), the following clause shall be inserted, namely:  (2B) Nothing in Sub-clause (f) of Clause (1) of Article 19 shall affect any such law as is referred to in Clause (2).
Statute	As stated in the Statement of Objects and Reasons to the Bill (No.
Argument	106 of 1971) the word "compensation" was sought to be omitted from Article 31(2) and replaced by the word "amount".
Argument	It was being clarified that the said "amount" may be given otherwise than in cash.
Precedent	It was also provided that Article 19(1)(f) shall not apply to any law relating to acquisition or requisitioning of property for a public purpose.
Precedent	The position of the respondents is that "compensation" had been given the meaning of market value or the just equivalent of what the owner had been deprived of according to the decisions of this Court.
Precedent	<CIT ATION_1890860> .
Precedent	(1954) S.C.R.
Precedent	558.
Precedent	That had led to the 4th Amendment Act 1955.
Precedent	The later decisions (2) <CITATION_1634289> (1965) S.C.R.
Precedent	614 and <CITATION_602096> .
Precedent	1 S.C.R.
Precedent	255 had continued to uphold the concept of "compensation" i.e.
Precedent	just equivalent of the value of the property acquired in spite of the amendments made in 1955.
Precedent	<CITATION_673450> .
Precedent	3 S.C.R.
Precedent	341 the decision in <ORG>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	255 was overruled which itself was virtually overruled by <ORG> v. <ORG>.
Precedent	3 S.C.R.
Precedent	530.
Precedent	According to the Advocate General of Maharashtra, if <NAME> etc.
Precedent	had not been overruled by <ORG> v. <ORG> there would have been no necessity of amending Article 31(2).
Precedent	623.
Precedent	The first question that has to be determined is the meaning of the word "amount".
Precedent	Unlike the word "compensation" it has no legal connotation.
Precedent	It is a neutral, colourless word.
Precedent	The dictionary meanings do not help in arriving at its true import as used in a Constitutional provision.
Precedent	It can be anyt hing from one paisa to an astronomical figure in rupees.
Precedent	Its meaning has, therefore, to be ascertained by turning to the context in which it is used and the words preceding it as well as following it.
Precedent	624.
Precedent	The scheme of Article 31(2) now is:  (1) The property has to be compulsorily acquired or requisitioned.
Precedent	(2) It has to be for a public purpose.
Precedent	(3) It has to be by a law.
Precedent	(4) The law must provide for an amount which may be-  (i) fixed by such law or  (ii) which may be determined in accordance with such principles as may be specified in such law.
Precedent	(5) The law shall not be questioned in a Court on the ground:  (i) The amount so fixed or determined is not adequate or  (ii) the whole or any part of such amount is to be given otherwise than in cash.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is significant that the amount can be determined in accordance with specified principles, if it is not fixed by the law itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	Moreover, its adequacy cannot be questioned in a court.
RatioOfTheDecision	The use of the word "principles" and the question of inadequacy can only aris e if the amount has some norm.
RatioOfTheDecision	If it has no norm no question of specifying any principles arises nor can there be any occasion for the determination of its adequacy.
RatioOfTheDecision	The very fact that the court is debarred from going into the question of adequacy shows that the "amount" can be adequate or inadequate.
RatioOfTheDecision	Even if it is inadequate, the fixation or determination of that amount is immune from any challenge.
RatioOfTheDecision	It postulates the existence of some standard or norm without which any enquiry into adequacy becomes wholly unnecessary and irrelevant Moreover, either method of giving an amount must bring about the same result In other words, if Rs.
RatioOfTheDecision	1000 is the amount to be given for acquisition of a property, it must be either fixed or must be determinable by the principles specified in the event of its not being fixed.
RatioOfTheDecision	It could not be intended that the two alternative modes should lead to varying results, i.e., it could be fixed at Rs.
RatioOfTheDecision	1000 but if the principles are specified they do not yield that figure.
RatioOfTheDecision	625.
Precedent	The Advocate  General of Maharashtra says that the right of the owner is just what the government determines it to be.
Precedent	It can give what it pleases and when it choses to do so.
Precedent	Such an argument is untenable and introduces an element of arbitrariness which cannot be attributed to the <ORG>.
Precedent	626.
Precedent	In <ORG>, which, on the submission of the Advocate General, enunciated the correct principles relating to Article 31(2) as it then stood, it was laid down that something fixed or determined by the application of specified principles which was illusory or could in no sense be regarded as compensation was not bound to be upheld by the <ORG>, "for to do so would be to grant a charter of arbitrariness and permit a device to defeat the Constitutional guarantees".
RatioOfTheDecision	It was added that the principles could be challenged on the ground that they were irrelevant to the determination of compensation but not on the plea that what was awarded was not just or fair compensation.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thus it was open to the courts to go into the quest ion of arbitrariness of the amount fixed or its being illusory even under the law laid down in <NAME> (supra).
RatioOfTheDecision	The relevance of the principles had also been held to be justiciable.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> case did not lay down different principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	But the observations made therein were understood to mean that the concept of just equivalent not accepted in <NAME>'s case was restored.
RatioOfTheDecision	The amendment now made is apparently aimed at removing that concept and for that reason the word "amount" has been substituted in place of "compensation".
RatioOfTheDecision	This is particularly so as we find no reason for departing from the well-settled rule that in such circumstances the <ORG> made the amendment knowing full well the ratio of the earlier decisions.
Precedent	627.
Precedent	The Advocate General of Maharashtra has submitted that the fixing of the amount or alternatively specifying the principles for determining that amount is entirely within the judgment of the legislature and the whole object of the amendment is to exclude judicial review  which had been introduced by the courts on the basis of the concept of compensation.
Precedent	But even then the members of the legislature must have some basis or principles before them to fix the amount as the same cannot be done in an arbitrary way.
Precedent	He, however, gave an unusual explanation that in the <ORG> system of government it is for the government to determine the amount or specify such principles as it choses to do.
Precedent	The legislators belonging to the ruling party are bound to support the measure whether the basis on which the amount has been determined is disclosed to them or not.
Precedent	It is wholly incomprehensible how there can be any legislative judgment or decision unless there is room for debate and discussion both by members of the ruling party and the opposition.
Precedent	For any discussion on the "amount" fixed or the principles specified the entire basis has to be disclosed.
Precedent	There can be no basis if there is no standard or norm.
Precedent	628.
RatioOfTheDecision	The learned Solicitor General agrees that Article 31(2) after amendment still bin ds the legislature to provide for the giving to the owner a sum of money either in cash or otherwise.
RatioOfTheDecision	In fixing the "amount", the legislature has to act on some principle.
RatioOfTheDecision	This is not because of any particular obligation arising out of Article 31(2 ), but from the general nature of legislative power itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	Whatever, the subject or the nature of legislation it always proceeds on a principle it is based on legislative policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	The principle may include considerations of social justice: Judicial review on the ground of inadequacy of the "amount" and the manner of payment is excluded by express language.
RatioOfTheDecision	No other question is excluded.
RatioOfTheDecision	The expropriated owner still continues to have a fundamental right.
RatioOfTheDecision	This argument is not quite the same as that of the learned Solicitor General.
RatioOfTheDecision	629.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is true that the "amount" to be paid to an owner may not be the market value.
RatioOfTheDecision	The price of the property might have increased owing to various factors to which no contribution has been made by the owner.
RatioOfTheDecision	The element of social justi ce may have to be taken into consideration.
RatioOfTheDecision	But still on the learned Solicitor General's argument, the right to receive the "amount" continues to be a fundamental right That cannot be denuded of its identity.
RatioOfTheDecision	The obligation to act on some principle while fixing the amount arises both from Article 31(2) and from the nature of the legislative power.
RatioOfTheDecision	For, there can be no power which permits in a democratic system an arbitrary use of power.
RatioOfTheDecision	If an aggrieved owner approaches the court alleging that he is being deprived of that right on the grounds now open to him, the <ORG> cannot decline to look into the matter.
RatioOfTheDecision	The <ORG> will certainly give due weight to legislative judgment.
RatioOfTheDecision	But the norm or the principles of fixing or determining the "amount" will have to be disclosed to the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It will have to be satisfied that the "amount" has reasonable relationship with the value of the property acquired or requisitioned and one or more of the relevant principles have been applied and further that the "amount" is neither  illusory nor it has been fixed arbitrarily, nor at such a figure that it means virtual deprivation of the right under Article 31(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	The question of adequacy or inadequacy, however, cannot be gone into.
RatioOfTheDecision	630.
RatioOfTheDecision	As to the mode of payment, there is nothing to indicate in the amended Article that any arbitrary manner of payment is contemplated.
Precedent	It is well known that a discretion has to be exercised reasonably.
Precedent	631.
Precedent	As regards Clause (2B) inserted in Article 31 which makes Article 19(1)(f) inapplicable, there is no reason for assuming that a procedure will be provided which will not be reasonable or will be opposed to the rules of natural justice.
Precedent	Section 2 of the 25th amendment can be sustained on the construction given to it above.
Precedent	632.
Precedent	We now come to the most controversial provision of 25th Amendment, namely, Section 3 which inserted the following Article:  31C Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 , no law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause  (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with, or takes way or abridges any of the rights conferred by Article 14 , Article 19 or Article 31; and no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy:  Provided that where such law is made by the Legislature of a State, the provisions of this Article shall not apply thereto unless such law, having been reserved for the consideration of the President, has received his assent.
Precedent	According to the Statement of Objects and Reasons contained in Bill No.
Precedent	106 of 1971, the new Article has been introduced to provide that if any law is passed to give effect to the Directive Principles contained in Clauses (b) and (c) of Article 39 and contains a declaration to that effect, such law shall not be deemed to be void on the ground that it takes away or abridges any of the rights contained in Articles 14 , 19 or 31 and shall not be questioned on the ground that it does not give effect to these principles.
Precedent	For this provision to apply in case of laws made by <ORG> legislatures, it is necessary that the relevant Bill should be reserved for the consideration of the President and receive his assent.
Precedent	633.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 39 contains certain principles of policy to be followed by the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It enjoins the <ORG> inter alia to direct its policy towards securing:  39 (b) that the ownership and control of the material resources of the community are so distributed as best to subserve the common good;  (c) that the operation of the economic system does not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to the common detriment;  634.
RatioOfTheDecision	These provisions together with the other provisions of the Constitution contain one of the main objectives, namely, the building of A welfare <ORG> and an egalitarian social order in our country.
RatioOfTheDecision	As stated before, the fundamental rights and the directive principles have been described  as the "conscience of our Constitution".
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution makers had, among others, one dominant objective in view and that was to ameliorate and improve the lot of the common man and to bring about a socio-economic transformation based on principles of social justice.
RatioOfTheDecision	While the Constitution makers envisaged development in the social, economic and political fields, they did not desire that it should be a society where a citizen will not have the dignity of the individual.
RatioOfTheDecision	Part III of the Constitution shows that the founding fathers were equally anxious that it should be a society where the citizen will enjoy the various freedoms and such rights as are the basic elements of those freedoms without which there can be no dignity of individual.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution makers did not contemplate any disharmony between the fundamental rights and the directive principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	They were meant to supplement one another.
RatioOfTheDecision	It can well be said that the directive principles prescribed the goal to be attained and the fundamental rights  laid down the means by which that goal was to be achieved.
RatioOfTheDecision	While on behalf of the petitioners greater emphasis has been laid on the fundametal rights, counsel for the respondents say that the fundamental rights should be subordinate to the directive principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> did not accept such a proposal made by <NAME>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has been suggested that a stage has been reached where it has become necessary to abrogate some of the basic freedoms and rights provided the end justifies the means.
Precedent	At an earlier stage in the development of our Constitutional law a view was taken that the Directive Principles of State Policy had to conform and run subsidiary to the Chapter on Fundamental Rights, but Das C.J.
Precedent	in Kerala Education Bill, 1957, laid down the rule of harmonious construction and observed that an attempt should be made to give effect to both the fundamental rights and the directive principles.
Precedent	635.
Argument	According to Mr. <NAME>, Article 31C destroys several essential features of the Constitution.
Argument	He  says that there is a vital distinction between two cases (a) where fundamental rights are amended to permit laws to be validly passed which would have been void before the amendment and (b) the fundamental rights remain unamended, but the laws which are void as offending those rights are validated by a legal fiction that they shall not be deemed to be void.
Argument	He further points out that on the analogy of Article 31(C) it would be permissible to have an omnibus Article that notwithstanding anything contained in the Constitution no law passed by <ORG> or any State legislature shall be deemed to be void on any ground whatsoever.
Argument	Article 31(C) according to him, gives a blank charter not only to <ORG> but all the State Legislatures to amend the Constitution.
Argument	On the other hand, the argument on behalf of the respondents is that Article 31(C) is similar to Articles 31(A) and 31(B) and that the object of inserting the Article is to free certain kinds of laws from the limitation on legislative power imposed by  conferment of fundamental rights by Part III of the Constitution.
Argument	As those rights were justiciable under Article 32 , says the Advocate General of Maharashtra, the only way of doing so was to exclude judicial review of legislation in respect of those laws.
Precedent	If Article 31(A) is valid, there is no reason or justification for saying that Article 31(C) suffers from all the vices pointed out by Mr. <NAME>.
Precedent	636.
Precedent	According to the Solicitor General, Article 31(C) protects only law and not mere executive action.
Precedent	Law can be made by either <ORG> or the State <ORG>.
Precedent	Article 31(C) has been enacted for the purpose of achieving the objectives set out in Clauses (b) and (c) of Article 39.
Precedent	The law enacted under it will operate on "material resources", concentration of wealth and "means of production".
Precedent	The legislative effort would generally involve (i) nationalisation of material resources of the community and (ii) imposition of control on the production, supply and distribution of the products of key industrie s and essential commodities.
Precedent	It, therefore, impinges on a particular kind of economic system only.
Precedent	637.
Precedent	The question of the validity of Article 31(C) to our mind has to be examined mainly from two points of view; the first is its impact on the various freedoms guaranteed by Article 19 , the abrogation of the right of equality guaranteed by Article 14 and the right to property contained in Article 31.
Precedent	The second is whether the amending body under Article 368 could delegate its amending power to the legislatures of the Union and the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	Alternatively, whether the <ORG> and the State <ORG> can, under Article 31(C ), amend the Constitution without complying with the form and manner laid down in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	Now it is quite obvious that under Article 31(C) a law passed by the <ORG> or the State <ORG> shall not be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Articles 14, 19 and 31 so long as the law is declared to  be one for giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) and Clause (c) of Article 39.
RatioOfTheDecision	If Article 31(C) is aimed at the removal of a particular economic system, as suggested by the Solicitor General, it is difficult to understand why the freedoms contained in Clauses  (a) to (d) of Article 19 as also the right of equality under Article 14 had to be taken away.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power of enacting Constitution breaking laws has been entrusted even to a small majority in a <ORG> Legislature.
RatioOfTheDecision	Mr. <NAME> points out that the freedom of the Press, for instance, can be destroyed under Article 31(C) as the respondents claim the right to nationalise any industrial or economic activity.
RatioOfTheDecision	Moreover, a person can be put in prison for commending a policy contrary to the government's policy.
Precedent	Such legislation cannot be challenged as Article 19(1)(a) will not apply and Article 21 permits deprivation of personal liberty according to procedure established by law.
Precedent	The case in the <CITATION_ 334293>  S.C.R.
Precedent	682 is in point.
Precedent	Commending the use of an intoxicant had been made an offence.
Precedent	It was struck down by this <ORG> as violative of Article 19(1)(a).
Precedent	If Article 31(C) is Constitutional, such a provision made in a law enacted under it relating to matters falling within Article 39(a) and (b) would be valid.
Precedent	As a matter of fact no cogent or convincing explanation has been given as to why it was necessary to take away all the freedoms guaranteed by Article 19 and for the abrogation of the prized right of equality under Article 14 of which has been described as the basic principle of republicanism.
Precedent	<CITATION_184660633> (per Patanjali Sastri C.J.)
Precedent	S.C.R.
Precedent	284 at pp.
Precedent	293, (Ibid p. 313 <NAME>).
Precedent	This Article combines the English doctrine of the rule of law and the equal protection clause of the 14th Amendment to the American Constitution.
Precedent	<CITATION_761967> (per <NAME>)  Supp.
Precedent	1 S.C.R.
Precedent	528 at 551.
Precedent	It follows, therefore, that Article 31(C) impinges with full force or several fundamental rights which ar e enabled to be abrogated by the <ORG> and <ORG>.
Precedent	638.
Precedent	As regards the question of delegation of amending power, it is noteworthy that no amendment has been made in Article 368 itself to enable delegation of constituent power.
Precedent	The delegation of such power to <ORG>, in particular, involves serious consequences.
Precedent	It is well settled that one legislature cannot create another legislative body.
Precedent	This has been laid down very clearly in two decisions of <ORG>.
Precedent	In the Initiative and Referendum Act  A.C. 935 which has already been discussed See page 88 by us no doubt was entertained that a body that had the power of legislation on the subjects entrusted to it, even though, the power was so ample as that enjoyed by a provincial legislature in Canada, could not create and endow with its own capacity a new legislative power not created by the Act to which it owed its own existence.
Precedent	Attorney General of Nova Scotia v. The Attorney General of Canada  Can.
Precedent	L.R.
Precedent	31 is another d irect authority for the view that the <ORG> of Canada or any of the legislatures could not abdicate their powers and invest for the purpose of legislation bodies, which by the very terms of the British North American Act were not empowered to accept such delegation and to legislate on such matters.
Precedent	The distinction made by counsel on behalf of the respondents and the cases relied on by them have been fully discussed in the judgment of the learned Chief Justice and we need not go over the same ground.
Precedent	639.
Precedent	The only way in which the Constitution can be amended, apart from Articles 4, 169 and the relevant paras in Schedules V and VI of the Constitution, is by the procedure laid down by Article 368.
Precedent	If that is the only procedure prescribed, it is not possible to understand how by ordinary laws the <ORG> or the State <ORG> can amend the Constitution, particularly, when Article 368 does not contemplate any other mode of amendment or the setting up of another body to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	The othe r difficulty which immediately presents itself while examining Article 31(C) is the effect of the declaration provided for in the Article.
Precedent	It is possible to fit in the scheme of Article 31(C) any kind of social or economic legislation.
Precedent	If the courts are debarred from going into the question whether the laws enacted are meant to give effect to the policy set out in Article 39(b) and (c ), the <ORG> will be precluded from enquiring even into the incidental encroachment on rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	This is not possible with regard to laws enacted under Article 31(A).
Precedent	Those laws can be sustained if they infringe the aforesaid Articles only to the extent necessary for giving effect to them.
Precedent	Although on behalf of the respondents it is said that the <ORG> can examine whether there is any nexus between the laws made under Article 31(C) and Article 39(b) and (c ), there would hardly be any law which can be held to have no nexus with Article 39(b) and (c ), the ambit of which is so wide.
Precedent	640.
Precedent	The  essential distinction between Article 31(A) and 31(C) is that the former is limited to specified topics; whereas the latter does not give the particular subjects but leaves it to the legislatures to select any topic that may purport to have some nexus with the objectives in Article 39(b) and (c).
Precedent	In other words, Article 31(C) deals with objects with unlimited scope.
Precedent	641.
Precedent	The argument that Article 31(C) lifts the ban placed on <ORG> and <ORG> under Articles 14, 19 and 31 and further that it may be considered as an amendment of Article 368 , has been discussed by the learned Chief Justice in his judgment delivered today and we adopt, with respect, his reasoning for repelling them.
Precedent	642.
Precedent	In our judgment Article 31(C) suffers from two kinds of vice which seriously affect its validity.
Precedent	The first is that it enables total abrogation of fundamental rights contained in Articles 14, 19 and 31 and, secondly, the power of amendment contained in Article 368 is of special nature which has been exclusivel y conferred on the <ORG> and can be exercised only in the manner laid down in that Article.
Precedent	It was never intended that the same could be delegated to any other legislature including the State Legislatures.
Precedent	643.
Precedent	The purpose sought to be achieved by Article 31(C) may be highly laudable as pointed out by the learned Solicitor General, but the same must be achieved by appropriate laws which can be Constitutionally upheld.
Precedent	We have no option, in view of what has been said except to hold that the validity of Article 31(C) cannot be sustained.
Precedent	644.
Precedent	The last matter for determination is the validity of the 29th Amendment Act , 1972.
Precedent	The challenge is only against the inclusion of two Acts, namely, the Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act 1969 and a similar <ORG> of 1971 in the Ninth Schedule to the Constitution.
Precedent	645.
Precedent	The main argument on behalf of the petitioners haw been confined to the relationship between Article 31(A) and Article 31(B).
Precedent	It has been contended that Article 31(B) is intimately linked with A rticle 31(A) and, therefore, only those legislative enactments which fall under Article 31(A) can be included in the 9th Schedule under Article 31(B).
Precedent	This matter is no longer open to argument as the same stands settled by a series of decisions of this Court <CITATION_41868> .
Precedent	;  S.C.R.
Precedent	889 <CITATION_45759>  S.C.R.
Precedent	1020 and <CITATION_1681028> .
Precedent	1 S.C.R.
Precedent	636.
Precedent	In all these cases it was held that Article 31 (B) was independent of Article 31(A).
Precedent	A matter which has been settled for all these years cannot be re-opened now.
Precedent	It will still be open, however, to the Court to decide whether the Acts which were included in the Ninth Schedule by 29th Amendment Act or any provision thereof abrogates any of the bask elements of the Constitutional structure or denudes them of their identity.
Precedent	646.
Precedent	Our conclusions may be summarised as follows:  1.
Precedent	The decision in Golak Nath has become academic, for even if it be assumed that the majority judgment that the word 'law' in Article 13(2 ), covered Constitutional amendments was not  correct, the result on the questions, wider than those raised in <NAME>, now raised before us would be just the same.
Precedent	2.
Precedent	The discussion on the 24th Amendment leads to the result that-  (a) the said amendment does no more than to clarify in express language that which was implicit in the unamended Article 368 and that it does not or cannot add to the power originally conferred thereunder;  (b) though the power to amend cannot be narrowly construed and extends to all the Articles it is not unlimited so as to include the power to abrogate or change the identity of the Constitution or its basic features;  (c) even if the amending power includes the power to amend Article 13(2 ), a question not decided in <NAME>, the power is not so wide so as to include the power to abrogate or take away the fundamental freedoms; and  (d) the 24th Amendment Act , read as aforesaid, is valid.
Precedent	3.
Precedent	Clause (2) of Article 31 , as substituted by Section 2 of the 25th Amendment, does not abrogate any basic element of the Consti tution nor does it denude it of its identity because-  (a) the fixation or determination of "amount" under that Article has to be based on some norm or principle which must be relevant for the purpose of arriving at the amount payable in respect of the property acquired or requisitioned;  (b) the amount need not be the market value but it should have a reasonable relationship with the value of such property;  (c) the amount should neither be illusory nor fixed arbitrarily; and  (d) though the courts are debarred from going into the question of adequacy of the amount and would give due weight to legislative judgment, the examination of all the matters in (a), (b) and (c) above is open to judicial review.
Precedent	4.
Precedent	As regards Clause (2B) inserted in Article 31 which makes Article 19(1)(f) inapplicable, there is no reason to suppose that for determination of the amount on the principles laid down in the law any such procedure will be provided which will be unreasonable or opposed to the rules of natural justice.
Precedent	5.
Precedent	O n the above view Section 2 of the 25th Amendment is valid.
Precedent	6.
Precedent	The validity of Section 3 of the 25th Amendment which introduced Article 31C in the Constitution cannot be sustained because the said Article suffers from two vices.
Precedent	The first is that it enables abrogation of the basic elements of the Constitution inasmuch as the fundamental rights contained in Articles 14, 19 and 31 can be completely taken away and, secondly, the power of amendment contained in Article 368 is of a special nature which has been exclusively conferred on <ORG> and can be exercised only in the manner laid down in that Article.
Precedent	The same could not be delegated to any other legislature in the country.
Precedent	Section 3 , therefore, must be declared to be unConstitutional and invalid.
Precedent	7.
Precedent	The 29th Amendment is valid.
Precedent	However, the question whether the Acts included in the Ninth Schedule by that amendment or any provision of those Acts abrogates any of the basic elements of the Constitutional structure or denudes them of their identity will h ave to be examined when the validity of those Acts comes up for consideration.
Precedent	647.
Precedent	The petitions are remitted to <ORG> to be decided in accordance with this judgment and the law.
Precedent	<ORG> will also decide the validity of the 26th Amendment in the light of our judgment.
Precedent	<NAME> and <NAME>, JJ.
Precedent	648.
Precedent	In these writ petitions questions of great Constitutional importance have arisen for consideration.
Precedent	Herein we are called upon to decide the Constitutional validity of the 24th, 25th, 26th and 29th Amendments to the Constitution.
Precedent	We have had the advantage of hearing long and illuminating arguments covering over 65 working days.
Precedent	We have been referred to numerous decisions of this <ORG> and of the courts in England, United States, Canada, Australia, Germany, Ireland and Ceylon.
Precedent	Our attention has also been invited to various writings of jurists, present and past, of several countries.
Precedent	For paucity of time, we have not taken up the question of the validity of the 26th Amendmen t. That question can be conveniently considered later after this bench decides certain fundamental questions of law arising for decision.
Precedent	For the same reason we have also refrained from going into the merits of various writ petitions at this stage.
Precedent	At present we are merely deciding the scope and validity of the 24th, 25th and 29th Amendments to the Constitution.
Precedent	649.
Precedent	In order to decide the validity of the Amendments referred to earlier, it is necessary to go into the scope of the power conferred on <ORG> under Article 368 of the Constitution as it stood prior to its amendment by the 24th Amendment Act which came into force on November 5, 1971.
Precedent	Article 368 is the only article found in Part XX of the Constitution.
Precedent	The title of that part is "Amendment of the Constitution."
Precedent	Its marginal note as it originally stood read "Procedure for amendment of the Constitution".
Precedent	The Article read thus:  An amendment of this Constitution may be initiated only by the introduction of a Bill for the purpose in either <ORG> of  <ORG>, and when the Bill is passed in each <ORG> by a majority of the total membership of that <ORG> and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members of that <ORG> present and voting, it shall be presented to the President for his assent and upon such assent being given to the Bill, the Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill: Provided that if such amendment seeks to make any change in-  (a) Article 54 , Article 55 , Article 73 , Article 162 or Article 241 , or  (b) Chapter IV of Part V, Chapter V of Part VI, or Chapter I of Part XI, or  (c) any of the Lists in the Seventh Schedule, or  (d) the representation of States in <ORG>, or  (e) the provisions of this article, the amendment shall also require to be ratified by the <ORG> of not less than one half of the States by resolutions to that effect passed by those <ORG> before the Bill making provision for such amendment is presented to the President for assent.
Precedent	650.
Precedent	The petitioners' learne d Counsel, Mr. <NAME>, advanced twofold arguments as to the scope of that Article.
Precedent	His first contention was that in the exercise of its powers under.
Precedent	Article 368 as it stood before its amendment, it was impermissible for <ORG> to take away or abridge any of the rights conferred by Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	His second and more comprehensive argument was that the power conferred on the <ORG> under Article 368 did not permit it to damage or destroy any of the basic or fundamental features or essential elements of the Constitution.
Precedent	The arguments on these two aspects naturally ran into each other.
Precedent	But for a proper legal approach, it is necessary to keep them apart as far as possible.
Precedent	Hence while considering the correctness of the first contention, we shall not take into consideration the importance of the Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	On this aspect, our approach to Article 368 will be purely based on the language of Article 368 and Article 13.
Precedent	The importance or transcendental character of the Fundamental  Rights as well as the implied or inherent limitations on the amending power, if any, will be considered While dealing with the second of the two alternative contentions advanced by Mr. <NAME>.
Precedent	651.
Precedent	We shall first take up the question whether by the exercise of the power of amendment conferred by Article 368 , as it originally stood, <ORG> could have taken away any of the Fundamental Rights conferred by Part III.
Precedent	According to Mr. <NAME>, Article 368 as it stood before its amendment merely laid down the procedure for amendment; the power to amend the Constitution must be found somewhere else in the Constitution; the power to be exercised by <ORG> under Article 368 in legislative in character and the resulting product is 'law', hence such a law, in view of Article 13(2) which says "The <ORG> shall not make any law which takes away or abridges the rights conferred by this Part and any law made in contravention of this clause shall, to the extent of the contravention, be void", cannot validly ta ke away or abridge any of the Fundamental Rights.
Argument	He further contended that the word 'law' in Article 13(1) means and includes not merely legislative enactments but also Constitutional measures.
Argument	The Counsel urged, there is no reason why a different meaning should be given to the word 'law' in Article 13(2).
Argument	A more important argument of his was that the power to amend the Constitution, even if, it is assumed to be contained in Article 368 , is by no means an exclusive power because in certain respects and subject to certain conditions, the Constitution can also be amended by <ORG> by a simple majority by enacting a law in the same manner as other legislative measures are enacted.
Precedent	In this connection he drew our attention to Articles 4, 169, Paragraph 7 of the Vth Schedule and Paragraph 21 of the VIth Schedule.
Precedent	Counsel urged that if the amendment of the provisions of the Constitution referred to therein is considered as the exercise of constituent power and consequently such an amendment is not a "law" with in the meaning of that expression in Article 13 , then <ORG> by a simple majority of the members present and voting if the rule regarding the quorum is satisfied, can take away or abridge any of the Fundamental Rights of certain sections of the public in this country.
Precedent	652.
Precedent	On the other hand, the learned Attorney General, the learned Advocate General for the State of Maharashtra, appearing for the State of Kerala and the other Counsel appearing for the various States contended that a plain reading of Article 368 shows that the power to amend the Constitution as well as the procedure of amendment are both contained in that Article; once the form and the manner laid down in that Article have been complied with, the result is the amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	According to them, the expression "an amendment of this Constitution" in Article 368 means an amendment of each and every provision or part of the Constitution; once the form and manner provided in Article 368 have been complied with, the amended Arti cle is as effective as the original Article itself; and, therefore, as in the case of the original Article, the validity of the amended Article also cannot be challenged.
Precedent	They further contended that 'law' in Article 13 means only legislative enactments or ordinances, or orders or bye-laws or rules or regulations or notifications or customs or usages having the force of law in the territory of India and that expression does not include a Constitutional law, though in a comprehensive sense, a Constitutional law is also a law.
Precedent	They further contended that the word 'law' in Article 13 must be harmoniously construed with Article 368 and, if 'it is so construed, there is no room for doubt that the expression 'law' in Article 13 does not include a Constitutional law.
Precedent	They repudiated the contention of Mr. <NAME> that there was any Constitutional law as such in force when the Constitution came into force.
Precedent	Hence according to them the expression 'law' in Article 13(2) does not take in the amendment of the Constitutio n. According to them, laws enacted under Article 4 , Article 169 , Paragraph 7 of Schedule V and Paragraph 21 of Schedule VI are not to be deemed as amendments to the Constitution as is laid down in those provisions, though in fact they do amend the Constitution in certain respects and they are no different from the other legislative measures enacted by <ORG>; hence the laws enacted under those provisions cannot take away or abridge any of the Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	We have now to see which one of those lines of reasoning is acceptable.
Precedent	653.
Precedent	The question whether Fundamental Rights can be abridged by <ORG> by the exercise of its power under Article 368 in accordance with the procedure laid down therein came up for consideration before this <ORG> very soon after the Constitution came into force.
Precedent	The validity of the Constitution (1st Amendment) Act 1951 came up for the consideration of this <ORG> in <CITATION_1706770>  S.C.R.
Precedent	89.
Precedent	In that case the scope of Article 368 vis-a-vis Article 13(2) was debated.
Precedent	This <ORG> rejecting the contention of the petitioners therein that it was impermissible for <ORG> to abridge any of the Fundamental Rights under Article 368 , held that "although 'law' must ordinarily include Constitutional law, there is a clear demarcation between ordinary Jaw which is made in exercise of legislative power, and Constitutional law, which is made in exercise of constituent power".
Precedent	This <ORG> held that "in the context of Article 13 , 'law' must be taken to mean rules or regulations made in exercise of ordinary legislative power and not amendments to the Constitution made in exercise of constituent power, with the result that Article 13(2) does not affect the amendments made under Article 368".
Precedent	In the case this <ORG> also opined that the power to amend the Constitution was explicitly conferred on <ORG> by Article 368 and the requirement of a different majority was merely procedural.
Precedent	It rejected the contention that Article 368 is a complete code by itself and upheld the contention of t he Government that while acting under Article 368 , <ORG> can adopt the procedures to be adopted, except to the extent provided in Article 368 , in enacting other legislative measures.
Precedent	654.
Precedent	The power of <ORG> to abridge Fundamental Rights under Article 368 was again considered by this <ORG> in <CITATION_1308308>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	933.
Precedent	In that case two questions were considered viz.
Precedent	(1) Whether the amendment of the Constitution in so far as it purported to take away or abridge the rights conferred by Part III of the Constitution was within the prohibition of Article 13(2) and (2) Whether Articles 31-A and 31-B (as amended by the 17th Amendment Act ) sought to make changes in Article 132 , Article 136 and Article 226 or any of the Lists in the VIIth Schedule and therefore the conditions prescribed in the proviso to Article 368 had to be satisfied.
Precedent	It is clear from the judgment of the <ORG> that the first question was not debated before the <ORG> though the majority judges as well as the minority judges did c onsider that question evidently without any assittance from the bar.
Precedent	On both those questions Chief Justice <NAME> speaking for himself and <NAME> and <NAME> concurred with the view taken by this <ORG> in <NAME> case.
Precedent	But <NAME> (as he then was) and <NAME> the correctness of that decision on the first question but concurred with the view taken by the majority of judges on the second question.
Precedent	<NAME> and <NAME> agreed in dismissing the writ petitions as the petitioners had not challenged the correctness of the decision of this <ORG> in <NAME> case on the first question.
Precedent	655.
Precedent	The question whether any of the Fundamental Rights can be abridged or taken away by <ORG> in exercise of its power under Article 368 again came up for consideration before this <ORG> in <CITATION_120358> (1957) 2 S.C.R.
Precedent	762.
Precedent	This case was heard by a full court of eleven judges.
Precedent	In that case by a majority of six to five this <ORG> came to the conclusion that S <NAME> case as well as <NAME> case were not correctly decided.
Precedent	The majority held that the expression 'law' in Article 13(2) includes Constitutional amendments as well.
Precedent	The minority agreeing with the earlier decisions held that the expression 'law' in Article 13(2) does not include Constitutional amendments.
Precedent	Five of the majority judges namely <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME> held that Article 368 in terms only prescribes the various steps in the matter of amendment and that the Article assumes the existence of the power to amend somewhere else in the Constitution.
Precedent	According to them the mere completion of the procedural steps mentioned in Article 368 cannot bring about a valid amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	In their opinion, the power to amend cannot be implied from Article 368.
Precedent	They declined to infer such a power by implication in Article 368 as they thought it was not necessary since <ORG> has under Article 248 read with Item 97 of List I of the VIIth Schedule ple nary power to make any law including the law to amend the Constitution subject to the limitations contained therein.
Precedent	They observed that the power of <ORG> to amend the Constitution may be derived from Article 245 , Article 246 and Article 248 read with Item 97 of List I.
Precedent	The remaining six judges held that the power of amendment is not derived from Article 248 read with Entry 97 of List I of the VIIth Schedule.
Precedent	<NAME> (as he then was) and <NAME>, Mitter and Bachawat JJ.
Precedent	held that the power to amend is to be found in Article 368 and <NAME> held that Article 368 confers on <ORG> the right (power) to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	<NAME> (as he then was) held that Article 368 outlines a process, which, if followed strictly, results in the amendment of the Constitution; that article gives the power to no particular person or persons, and that the power of amendment, if it can be called a power at all, is a legislative power but it is sui generis and exists outside the three Lists in Schedul e VII of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	This reasoning of <NAME> may be reasonably read to suggest that the power of amendment] is necessarily implied in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The majority of the judges who held that it was impermissible for <ORG> to take away or abridge any of the Fundamental Rights by an amendment of the Constitution did not proceed to strike down the the 1st, 4th and 17th Amendments.
RatioOfTheDecision	Five of them relied on the doctrine of "Prospective Overruling" (<NAME> <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME>) and <NAME> relied on the doctrine of acquiescence to save those amendments.
RatioOfTheDecision	Evidently in an attempt to get over the effect of the decision in <NAME> case, <ORG> has enacted the 24th Amendment Act , 1971, and the same has been ratified by more than one half of the Legislatures of the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	656.
RatioOfTheDecision	Now, turning back to the contentions advanced on behalf of the parties, we shall first deal with the contention of the Union and some of the States that once the "form and manner" pres cribed in Article 368 are complied with, the Constitution stands amended and thereafter the validity of the amendment is not open to challenge.
RatioOfTheDecision	This contention does not appear to be a tenable one.
RatioOfTheDecision	Before a Constitution can be validly amended, two requirements must be satisfied.
RatioOfTheDecision	Firstly, there must be the power to amend the provision sought to be amended; and secondly, the "form and the manner" prescribed in Article 368 must be satisfied.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the power to amend the Article is wanting, the fact that <ORG> has adhered to the form and manner prescribed in Article 368 becomes immaterial.
Precedent	Hence the primary question is whether <ORG> has power to abridge or take away any of the Fundamental Rights prescribed in Part III of the Constitution ?
Precedent	657.
Precedent	In order to find out whether <ORG> has the power to take away or abridge any of the Fundamental Rights in exercise of its power under Article 368 , we must first ascertain the true scope of that Article.
Precedent	As seen earlier in <NAME> case, this <ORG> rule d that the power to amend the Constitution is to be found in Article 368.
Precedent	The same view was taken by the majority of judges in <NAME> case as well as in <NAME> case.
Precedent	We respectively hold that view to be the correct view.
Precedent	As mentioned earlier, Part XX of the Constitution which purports to deal with amendment of the Constitution contains only one Article, i.e.
Precedent	Article 368.
Precedent	The title of that Part is "Amendment of the Constitution."
Precedent	The fact that a separate part of the Constitution is reserved for the amendment of the Constitution is a circumstance of great significance-see <NAME> and Ors.
Precedent	v. The Queen  1 A.C. 259 at 287 and <CITATION_138715291>  S.C.R.
Precedent	533 at 544.
Precedent	The provisions relating to the amendment of the Constitution are some of the most important features of any modern Constitution.
Precedent	All modern Constitutions assign an important place to the amending provisions.
Precedent	It is difficult to accept the view expressed by <NAME> and the learned judges who agreed with him  that the power to amend the Constitution is not to be found even by necessary implication in Article 368 but must be found elsewhere.
Precedent	In their undoubtedly difficult task of finding out that power elsewhere they had to fall back on Entry 97 of List I.
Precedent	Lists I to III of the VIIth Schedule of the Constitution merely divide the topics of legislation among the Union and the States.
Precedent	It is obvious that these lists have been very carefully prepared.
Precedent	They are by and large exhaustive.
Precedent	Entry 97 in List I was included to meet some unexpected and unforeseen contingencies.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is difficult to believe that our Constitution-makers who were keenly conscious of the importance of the provision relating to the amendment of the Constitution and debated that question for several days, would have left this important power hidden in Entry 97 of List I leaving it to the off chance of the courts locating that power in that Entry.
RatioOfTheDecision	We are unable to agree with those learned judges when they sought to place reliance on Article 245 , Artic le 246 and Article 248 and Entry 97 of List I for the purpose of locating the power of amendment in the residuary power conferred on the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Their reasoning in that regard fails to give due weight to the fact that the exercise of the power under those articles is "subject to the provisions of this Constitution".
RatioOfTheDecision	Hardly few amendments to the Constitution can be made subject to the existing provisions of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Most amendments of the Constitution must necessarily impinge on one or the other of the existing provisions of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	We have no doubt in our minds that Article 245 to Article 248 as well as the Lists in the VIIth Schedule merely deal with the legislative power and not with the amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	658.
RatioOfTheDecision	Now corning back to Article 368 , it may be noted that it has three components; firstly, it deals with the amendment of the Constitution; secondly, it designates the body or bodies which can amend the Constitution, and lastly, it prescibes the form and the manner in which the amendmen t of the Constitution can be effected.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Article does not expressly confer power to amend; the power is necessarily implied in the Article.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Article makes it clear that the amendment of the Constitution can only be made by <ORG> but in cases falling under the proviso, ratification by legislatures of not less than one-half of the States is also necessary.
RatioOfTheDecision	That Article stipulates various things.
RatioOfTheDecision	To start with, the amendment to the Constitution must be initiated only by the introduction of a Bill for that purpose in either <ORG> of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It must then be passed in each <ORG> by a majority of the total membership of that <ORG> and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members of that <ORG> present and voting and if the amendment seeks to make any change in the provisions mentioned in the proviso, it must be ratified by not less than one-half of the State Legislatures.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thereafter, it should be presented to the President for his assent It further says that upon such assent being given to t he Bill "the Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill".
RatioOfTheDecision	To restate the position, Article 368 deals with the amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Article contains both the power and the procedure for amending the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	No undue importance should be attached to the marginal note which says "Procedure for amendment of the Constitution".
RatioOfTheDecision	Marginal note plays a very little part in the construction of a stautory provision.
RatioOfTheDecision	It should have much less importance in construing a Constitutional provision.
RatioOfTheDecision	The language of Article 368 to our mind is plain and unambiguous.
RatioOfTheDecision	Hence we need not call into aid any of the rules of construction about which there was great deal of debate at the hearing.
RatioOfTheDecision	As the power to amend under the Article as it originally stood was only implied, the marginal note rightly referred to the procedure of amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	The reference to the procedure in the marginal note does not negative the existence of the power implied in the Article.
RatioOfTheDecision	659.
RatioOfTheDecision	The next question is wh ether the power conferred under Article 368 is available for amending each and every provision of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Article opens by saying "An amendment of this Constitution" which means an amendment of each and every provision and part of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	We find nothing in that Article to restrict its scope.
RatioOfTheDecision	If we read Article 368 by itself, there can be no doubt that the power of amendment implied in that Article can reach each and every Article as well as every part of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	660.
RatioOfTheDecision	Having ascertained the true scope of Article 368 , let us now turn to Article 13.
RatioOfTheDecision	A great deal of reliance was placed by the learned Counsel for the petitioners on the expression 'law' found in Article 13(1) and (2).
RatioOfTheDecision	As seen earlier, the two judges in <NAME> case as well as the majority of judges in <NAME> case opined that 'law' in Article 13(2) also includes Constitutional law i.e.
RatioOfTheDecision	law which amends the Constitution and we see no substance in the contention that the amendment of a Constitution is  not 'law'.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution is amended by enacting Amendment Act s. The Constitution is not only a law but the paramount law of the country.
RatioOfTheDecision	An amendment of that law must necessarily be a law.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fact that the word 'law' is not used in Article 368 is of little significance.
RatioOfTheDecision	For that matter Article 110 also does not provide that a Bill when assented to by the President becomes law.
RatioOfTheDecision	The amendment of a Constitution is initiated by a Bill and it goes through the procedure laid down in Article 368 , supplemented wherever necessary by the procedure prescribed in Article 107; see <NAME> case.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Bill when passed by both the <ORG> and, in matters coming under the proviso to Article 368 , after securing the necessary ratification by the State Legislatures, is presented to the President for his assent The procedure adopted is the same as that adopted in enacting an ordinary statute except to the extent provided in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	Even if it had been different, there can be hardly any doubt that  the amendment of a Constitution is 'law'.
RatioOfTheDecision	In <NAME> case, <NAME> (as he then was) speaking for the <ORG> had no doubt in ruling that the expression 'law' must ordinarily include 'Constitutional law'.
Precedent	The same view was taken by all the judges in <NAME> case and also by most of the judges in <NAME> case.
Precedent	661.
Precedent	But the question still remains whether our Constitution makers by using the expression 'law' in Article 13(2) intended that that expression should also include the exercise of <ORG>'s amending power under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	We have earlier explained the scope and extent of Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	In understanding the meaning of the word 'law's in Article 13(2) we should bear in mind the scope of Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The two Articles will have to be construed harmoniously.
RatioOfTheDecision	The expression 'law' may mean one of two things, namely, either those measures which are enumerated in Article 13(3) as well as statutes passed by legislatures or in addition thereto Constitutional laws (amendments) as w ell.
Precedent	In this connection reference may be made to a passage in Corpus Juris Secundum (Vol.
RatioOfTheDecision	XVI- Title Constitutional Law Article 1 , p. 20), which says:  The term 'Constitution' is ordinarily employed to designate the organic law in contradistinction to the terms 'law' which is generally used to designate statutes or legislative enactments.
RatioOfTheDecision	Accordingly, the term 'law' under this distinction does not include a Constitutional amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	However, the term 'law' may, in accordance with the context in which it is used, comprehend or include the Constitution or a Constitutional provision or amendment  662.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is true that Article 13(3) contains an inclusive definition of the term 'law' and, therefore, the question whether it includes Constitutional amendment also cannot be answered with reference to that clause.
RatioOfTheDecision	All the same, since the expression 'law' can have two meanings, as mentioned earlier, we must take that meaning which harmonises with Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	As mentioned earlier, Article 368 is unambiguous, whereas Ar ticle 13 is ambiguous because of the fact that the word 'law' may or may not include Constitutional amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	Further, when we speak of 'law' we ordinarily refer to the exercise of legislative power.
Precedent	Hence, law' in Article 13(2) must be construed as referring to the exercise of an ordinary legislative power.
Precedent	663.
Precedent	An examination of the various provisions of our Constitution shows that it has made a distinction between "the Constitution" and "the laws".
Precedent	The two are invariably treated separately-see Article 60 , 61 , proviso to Article 73(1 ), Article 75(4) read with the Third Schedule, Article 76(2);  Article 124(6) read with the Third Schedule, Article 148(5 ), Article 159 and Article 219 read with the Third Schedule.
Precedent	These provisions clearly establish that the Constitution-makers have not used the expression 'law' in the Constitution as including Constitutional law.
Precedent	664.
Argument	Mr. <NAME> contended that the term 'law' in Article 13(1) includes Constitutional law also.
Argument	<NAME> speaking for himself and on be half of two other judges in <NAME>'s case held that on the day the Constitution came into force, no Constitutional law was in force.
Argument	Therefore in his view, the term 'law' in Article 13(1) can only refer to legislative measures or ordinances or bye-laws, rules, regulations, notifications, customs and usages.
Argument	Mr. <NAME> contended that the said finding is not correct.
Argument	In that connection he referred to the treaties and agreements entered into between the former Rulers of the Indian States and <ORG> as well as to certain other measures which were in force when the Constitution came into force which, according to him, are 'Constitutional law' and, on that basis, he contended that certain Constitutional laws were in force on the day when the Constitution came into force.
RatioOfTheDecision	We are not satisfied that this contention is correct.
Precedent	Under Article 395 , the Indian Independence Act, 1947 as well as the Government of India Act, 1935, were repealed.
Precedent	The laws which were continued under Article 372 after th e Constitution came into force did not operate on their own strength.
Precedent	For their validity they had to depend on Article 372 and that Article made it clear that those laws will continue to be in force "subject to the other provisions of the Constitution".
Precedent	Anyway it is not necessary to decide the question whether those laws are Constitutional laws.
Precedent	Article 13(1) does not refer to 'laws' as such.
Precedent	It refers to "laws in force in the territory of India immediately before the commencement of this Constitution".
Precedent	It identifies certain laws and determines the extent of their validity.
Precedent	The scope of Article 13(1) does not bear on the interpretation of the expression 'law' in Article 13(2).
Precedent	665.
Precedent	We shall now examine the contention of Mr. <NAME> based on Articles 4, 169, Paragraph 7 of Schedule V and Paragraph 21 of Schedule VI.
Precedent	He contended and we have no doubt that he did so rightly,-that the Constitution can be amended not only under Article 368 but also under Article 4 , Article 169 , Paragraph 7 of Schedule V and  Paragraph 21 of Schedule VI.
Precedent	Amendments under these provisions can be effected by <ORG> by a simple majority vote of the members present in the <ORG> and voting, if the prescribed quorum is there.
Precedent	If the two <ORG>s do not agree on any amendment under those provisions, the same has to be decided by a Joint sitting of the two <ORG>s as provided in Article 108.
Precedent	That is because of the express exclusion of the application of Article 368 to the amendments made under those provisions.
Precedent	According to Mr. <NAME>, by the exercise of its power under the aforementioned provisions, <ORG> can in certain respects take away or abridge the Fundamental Rights of a section of the people of this country.
Precedent	He painted a gloomy picture as to what can happen by the exercise of power by <ORG> under those provisions.
Precedent	It is true that the power conferred under the aforementioned provisions is amending power but those provisions make it clear that the exercise of the power under those provisions shall not be "deemed to b e the amendment of the Constitution for the purpose of Article 368".
Precedent	666.
Precedent	This brings us to a consideration, what exactly is the intent of the expression "No such law as aforesaid shall be deemed to be an amendment of this Constitution for the purpose of Article 368".
Precedent	There can be little doubt that these words merely mean that the form and manner prescribed in Article 368 need not be compiled with.
Precedent	Once this position is accepted any law made under those provisions takes the character of an ordinary law and that law becomes subject to the other provisions of the Constitution including Article 13(2).
Precedent	667.
Precedent	Counsel either side took us through the debates of <ORG> relating to Article 368.
Precedent	Naturally each one of them relied on those passages from the speeches of the various members who took part in the debate and, in particular, on the speeches of late Prime Minister <NAME> and the then Law Minister Dr. <NAME>, which supported their contention.
Precedent	Having gone through those speeches, we feel conv inced that no conclusive inference can be drawn from those speeches as to the intention of those speakers.
Precedent	Hence, we need not go into the question at this stage whether it is permissible for us to place reliance on those speeches for finding out the true scope of Article 368.
Precedent	668.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> placed a great deal of reliance on the stages through which the present Article 13 passed.
Precedent	It is seen from the <ORG> records that when the <ORG> was considering the provision which resulted in Article 13(2 ), Mr. <NAME> one of the members of the <ORG> moved an amendment to make it clear that the expression 'law' in Article 13(2) does not include an amendment of the Constitution under draft Article 304 (present Article 368 ) and that the amendment was accepted by <NAME>, Chairman of <ORG>.
Precedent	On the basis of that decision, Sir <NAME>, the Constitutional Adviser redrafted the concerned provision by specifically excluding from its operation amendment s of the Constitution.
Precedent	When this matter went before <ORG> consisting of emient lawyers, they redrafted the clause thus:  The <ORG> shall not make any law which takes away or abridges the rights conferred by this part and any law made in contravention of this clause shall to the extent of contravention be void.
Precedent	669.
Precedent	In other words, the drafting committee deleted from Sir <NAME>'s draft those words which specifically excluded from the operation of the clause amendments of the Constitution.
Precedent	From these circumstances, Mr. <NAME> seeks to draw the inference that <ORG> finally decided to bring within the scope of Article 13(2) Constitutional amendments also.
Precedent	We are unable to accept this contention.
Precedent	It is not clear why the drafting committee deleted the reference to the amendment of the Constitution in Article 13(2).
Precedent	It is possible that they were of the opinion that in view of the plain language of the provision relating to the amendment of the Constitution i.e.
Precedent	draft Articl e 304 , it was unnecessary to provide in Article 13(2) that the amendment of the Constitution does not come within its scope.
Precedent	670.
Precedent	It is true that this <ORG> has characterised the Fundamental rights as "paramount" in <CITATION_1857950>  S.C.R.
Precedent	88 at 198, as "sacrosanct" in <CITATION_149321> ,  S.C.R.
Precedent	525, as "rights served by the people" in <CITATION_944601> ,  Supp.
Precedent	1 S.C.R.
Precedent	806 as "inalienable and inviolable" in <CITATION_673012> .
Precedent	1 S.C.R.
Precedent	778 and as "transcendental" in several other cases.
RatioOfTheDecision	In so describing the Fundamental Rights in those cases, this <ORG> could not have intended to say that the Fundamental Rights alone are the basic elements or fundamental features of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Mr. <NAME> conceded that the basic elements and fundamental features of the Constitution are found not merely in Part III of the Constitution but they are spread out in various other parts of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	They are also found in some of the Directive Principles set out in Part IV of the Constitution and in the pro visions relating to the sovereignty of the country, the Republic and the Democratic character of the Constitution.
Precedent	According to the Counsel, even the provisions relating to the unity of the country are basic elements of the Constitution.
Precedent	671.
RatioOfTheDecision	It was urged that since even amendment of several provisions of minor significance requires the concurrence of the legislatures of the majority of the States it is not likely that the Constitution makers would have made the amendment of the provisions relating to Fundamental Rights a plaything of the <ORG> This argument, however, does not lead to any definite conclusion.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is not unlikely that the Constitution-makers thought that the states are specially interested in the provisions mentioned in the proviso to Article 368 , so that the amendment of those provisions should require ratification by the legislatures of the majority of the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	When the language of Article 368 is plain, as we think it is, no question of construction of that Article arises.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is  no need to delve into the intention of the Constitution-makers.
RatioOfTheDecision	672.
RatioOfTheDecision	Every Constitution is expected to endure for a long time.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, it must necessarily be elastic.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is not possible to place the society in a straight jacket.
RatioOfTheDecision	The society grows, its requirements change.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution and the laws may have to be changed to suit those needs.
RatioOfTheDecision	No single generation can bind the course of the generation to come.
RatioOfTheDecision	Hence every Constitution wisely drawn up provides for its own amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	We shall separately consider the contention of Mr. <NAME> that our Constitution embodies certain features which are so basic that no free and civilised society can afford to discard them and in no foreseeable future can those features become irrelevant in this country.
RatioOfTheDecision	For the present we shall keep apart, for later consideration.
