RatioOfTheDecision	Mr. <NAME>'s contention that the <ORG> which is only a constituted body cannot damage or destroy the essential features of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Up till now we have merely confined our attent ion to the question as to the scope and reach of Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	This <ORG> has always attached great importance to the Fundamental Rights guaranteed under our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has given no less imporotance to some of the Directive Principles set out in Part IV.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Directive Principles embodied in Part IV of the Constitution or at any rate most of them are as important as the rights of individuals.
RatioOfTheDecision	To quote the words of <NAME> (The Indian Constitution-Corner Stone of a Nation, page 50):  The Indian Constitution is first and foremost a social document.
RatioOfTheDecision	The majority of its provisions are either directly aimed at furthering the goals of social revolution by establishing the conditions necessary for its achievement yet despite the permeation of the entire Constitution by the aim of national renaissance, the core of the commitment to the social revolution lies in Parts III and IV, in the Fundamental Rights and the Directive Principles of State Policy.
Precedent	These are the conscience of the Constitution.
Precedent	673.
Precedent	Ther efore to implement the duties imposed on the States under Part IV, it may be necessary to abridge in certain respects the rights conferred on the citizens or individuals under Part III, as in the case of incorporation of Clause 4 in Article 15 to benefit the backward classes and Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes and the amendment of Article 19(2) with a view to maintain effectively public order and friendly relations with foreign States.
Precedent	Hence we are unable to construe the amending power in a narrow or pedantic manner.
Precedent	That power, under any circumstance, must receive a broad and liberal interpretation.
Precedent	How large it should be is a question that requires closer examination.
Precedent	Both on principle as well as on the language of Article 368 , we are unable to accede to the contention that no right guaranteed by Part III can be abridged.
Precedent	674.
Precedent	This <ORG> is always reluctant to overrule its earlier decisions.
Precedent	There must be compelling reasons for overruling an earlier decision of this <ORG>.
Precedent	As seen earlier, there are  already conflicting decisions as to the scope of Article 368.
Precedent	As far back as 1951, in <NAME> case, this <ORG> took the view that the power of amendment conferred under Article 368 included within itself the power to abridge and take away the Fundamental Rights incorporated in Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	The correctness of that view was not challenged in several other decisions.
Precedent	The same view was taken in <NAME> case.
Precedent	That view was negatived in <NAME>'s case by a very narrow majority.
Precedent	Bearing in mind the disastrous effect that decision would have had on many important laws that had been enacted by the Union and the States between the years 1951 to 1967, this <ORG> by relying on the doctrines of prospective overruling and the doctrine of acquiescence did not invalidate those laws.
Precedent	675.
Precedent	One other circumstance of great significance is that the 1st Amendment to the Constitution was carried out by the provisional <ORG> which consisted of the very members who were the members of the Co <ORG>.
Precedent	It should be remembered that members of the Co<ORG> continued as the members of the provisional <ORG> till the General Election in 1952.
Precedent	They must have been aware of the intention with which Article 368 was enacted.
Precedent	These are important circumstances.
Precedent	The interpretation we place on a Constitutional provision, particularly on a provision of such great importance as Article 368 must subserve national interest.
Precedent	It must be such as to further the objectives intended to be achieved by the Constitution and to effectuate the philosophy underlying it.
Precedent	To quote the memorable words of Chief Justice <NAME> we must not forget that we are expounding a Constitution.
Precedent	676.
RatioOfTheDecision	We now come to the second contention of Mr. <NAME> that the word 'amendment' has a limited meaning and Article 368 does not permit any damage to or destruction of the basic or fundamental features or essential elements of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Mr. <NAME> urged that the word "amendment" or "amend" ordinarily means  'to make certain changes or effect some improvements in a text'.
RatioOfTheDecision	Those words do not, according to him, except under special circumstances mean the widest power to make any and every change in a document, including a power to abrogate or repeal the basic features of that document.
RatioOfTheDecision	The same, he contended, is true of a power to amend a statute or a Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	In support of his contention, he invited our attention to the various meanings given to the word "amendment" or "amend" in several dictionaries.
RatioOfTheDecision	He further urged that in construing the meaning of the word "amendment" in Article 368 , we must take into consideration the donee to whom the power to amend the Constitution is granted, the atmosphere in which the Constitution came to be enacted, the consequences of holding that power is unlimited in scope as well as the scheme of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	He urged that in the final analysis, the duty of the <ORG> is to find out the true intention of the founding fathers and therefore the question before us is whether  the founding fathers intended to confer on <ORG>, a body constituted under the Constitution, power to damage or destroy the very basis on which our Constitution was erected.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the other hand it was contended on behalf of the Union of India, <ORG> as well as the other States that the power of amendment conferred under Article 368 is of the widest amplitude.
RatioOfTheDecision	It brooks no limitation.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is a power which can be used to preserve the Constitution, to destroy the Constitution and to re-create a new Consitution.
Argument	It was contended that the society can never be static, social ideals and political and economic theories go on changing and every Constitution in order to preserve itself needs to be changed now and then to keep in line with the growth of the society.
Argument	It was further contended that no generation can impose its Will permanently on the future generations.
RatioOfTheDecision	Wise as our founding fathers were, wisdom was not their sole monopoly.
RatioOfTheDecision	They themselves realised it.
RatioOfTheDecision	They knew that in a changing world, there c an be nothing permanent and, therefore, in order to attune the Constitution to the changing concepts of politics, economics and social ideas, they provided in Article 368 a machinery which is neither too flexible nor too rigid and makes it possible to so reshape the Constitution as to meet the requirements of the time.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to them by following the form and manner prescribed in Article 368 , <ORG> can exercise the same power which <ORG> could have exercised.
RatioOfTheDecision	We have now to consider which one of the two contentions is acceptable.
Precedent	677.
Precedent	While interpretating a provision in a statute or, Constitution the primary duty of the court is to find out the legislative intent.
Precedent	In the present case our duty is to find out the intention of the founding fathers in enacting Article 368.
Precedent	Oridnarily the legislative intent is gathered from the language used.
Precedent	If the language employed is plain and unambiguous, the same must be given effect to irrespective of the consequences that may arise.
Precedent	But if the l anguage employed is reasonably capable of more meanings than one, then the <ORG> will have to call into aid various well settled rules of construction and in particular, the history of the legislation-to find out the evil that was sought to be remedied and also in some cases the underlying purpose of the legislation-the legislative scheme and the consequences that may possibly flow from accepting one or the other of the interpretations because no legislative body is presumed to confer a power which is capable of misuse.
Precedent	678.
Precedent	It was conceded at the bar that generally speaking, the word "amendment" like most words in English or for that matter in any language, has no precise meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	Unlike "sale" or "exercise", it is not a term of law.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is capable of receiving a wide meaning as well as a narrow meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power to amend a Constitution in certain context may include even a power to abrogate or repeal that Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may under certain circumstances mean a power to effect changes within narrow limits.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may sometime mean a power that is quite large but yet subject to certain limitations.
RatioOfTheDecision	To put it shortly, the word "amendment" without more, is a colourless word.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has no precise meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	It takes its colour from the context in which it is used.
RatioOfTheDecision	It cannot be interpreted in vacuo.
RatioOfTheDecision	Few words in English language have a natural, or ordinary meaning in the sense that they must be so read that their meaning is entirely independent of the context.
Precedent	As observed by <NAME> in <NAME> v. <NAME>.
Precedent	215 U.S. 418 at 425 "A word is not a crystal, transparent and unchanged; it is the skin of a living thought and may vary greatly in colour and content according to circumstances and the time in which it is used".
Precedent	We must read the word "amendment" in Article 368 not in isolaion but as occurring in a single complex instrument, Article 368 is a part of the Constitution.
Precedent	The Constitution confers various powers on legislatures as well as on other authorities.
Precedent	It also imposes duties on those authorities.
Precedent	The power conferred under A rticle 368 is only one such power.
Precedent	Unless it is plain from the Constitutional scheme that the power conferred under Article 368 is a super power and is capable of destroying all other powers, as contended on behalf of the <ORG> and the <ORG>, the various parts of the Constitution must be construed harmoniously for ascertaining the true purpose of Article 368.
Precedent	679.
RatioOfTheDecision	In our Constitution unlike in the Constitution of the United <ORG> of America the words "amendment" and "amend" have been used to convey different meanings in different places.
RatioOfTheDecision	In some Articles they are used to confer a narrow power, a power merely to effect changes within prescribed limits-see Articles 4, 107(2), 111, 169(2), 196(2), 197(2) and 200.
RatioOfTheDecision	Under Paragraph 7 of the Fifth Schedule as well as Paragraph 21 of the Sixth Schedule to the Constitution, a much larger power to amend those Schedules has been conferred on <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	That power includes power to amend "by way of addition, variation or repeal".
Precedent	Similar is the position under the re pealed Article 243(2 ), Article 252(2) and 350(5).
Precedent	It is true that the power to amend conferred under the Fifth and Sixth Schedules is merely a power to amend those Schedules but if the Constitution-makers were of the opinion that the word "amendment" or "amend" included within its scope, unless limited otherwise, a power to add, vary, or repeal, there was no purpose in mentioning in those Articles or parts "amend by way of addition, variation or repeal".
Precedent	In this connection it may also be remembered that <ORG> amended Section 291 of the Government of India Act, 1935 on August 21, 1949 just a few days before it approved Article 368 i.e.
Precedent	on September 17, 1949.
Precedent	The amended Section 291 empowered the Governor-General to amend certain provisions of the 1935 Act "by way of addition, modification or repeal".
Precedent	From these circumstances, there is prima facie reason to believe that our Constitution makers made a distinction between a mere power to amend and a power to amend by way of "addition, modifica tion or repeal".
Precedent	It is one of the accepted rules of construction that the courts should presume that ordinarily the legislature uses the same words in a statute to convey the same meaning.
Precedent	If different words are used in the same statute, it is reasonable to assume that, unless the context otherwise indicates, the legislature intended to convey different meanings by those words.
Precedent	This rule of interpretation is applicable in construing a Constitution as well.
Precedent	680.
Precedent	Now that we have come to the conclusion that the word "amendment" in Article 368 is not a word of precise import and has not been used in the various Articles and parts of the Constitution to convey always the same precise meaning, it is necessary to take the aid of the other relevant rules of construction to find out the intention of the Constitution makers.
Precedent	681.
Precedent	The question whether there is any implied limitation on the amending power under Article 368 has not been decided by this <ORG> till now.
Precedent	That question did not come up for consideration in  <NAME> case.
Precedent	In <NAME> case neither the majority speaking through <NAME> nor <NAME> (as he then was) went into that question.
Precedent	But <NAME> did foresee the importance of that aspect.
Precedent	He observed in the course of his judgment:  We may also have to bear in mind the fact that ours is a written Constitution.
Precedent	<ORG> which was the repository of sovereignty could well have created a sovereign <ORG> on the British model.
Precedent	But instead it enacted a written Constitution, created three organs of <ORG>, made the <ORG> executive responsible to <ORG> and the <ORG> executive to the <ORG> legislatures, erected a federal structure and distributed legislative power between <ORG> and the <ORG> Legislatures; recognised certain rights as fundamental and provided for their enforcement, prescribed forms of oaths of office or affirmations which require those who subscribe to them to owe true allegiance to the Constitution and further require the members of the  <ORG> and of the Higher judiciary in the States, to uphold the Constitution.
Precedent	Above all, it formulated a solemn and dignified preamble which appears to be an epitome of the basic features of the Constitution.
Precedent	Can it not be said that these are indicia of the intention of <ORG> to give a premanency to the basic features of the Constitution ?
Precedent	It is also a matter for consideration whether making a change in a basic feature of the Constitution can be regarded merely as an amendment or would it be, in effect, rewriting a part of the Constitution; and if the latter, would it be within the purview of Article 368 ?
Precedent	682.
Precedent	For the first time in <NAME> case, the contention that the power of amendment under Article 368 is subject to certain inherent and implied limitations was urged.
Precedent	<NAME> speaking for himself and four of his colleagues, while recognising the force of that contention refrained from pronouncing on the same.
Argument	<NAME> (as he then was) speaking for himself and t wo other judges opined that the power under Article 368 is a very wide power but it may not include a power to abrogate the Constitution.
Argument	He did explain what he meant by "abrogate the Constitution".
Argument	<NAME> (as he then was) did not address himself to that question.
Precedent	<NAME> side-stepped that question by saying that the impugned amendments did not destroy any, basic feature of the Constitution, The only judge who rejected the contention that there are inherent or implied limitations on the amending power was <NAME> >From the above discussion it is seen that in cases that came up for consideration before this <ORG> in the past several judges did consider the possibility of having some limitation on the amending power under Article 368 though they did not definitely pronounce on that question.
Precedent	683.
Precedent	One of the well-recognised rules of construction is the rule laid down in <NAME>'s case.
Precedent	What was the mischief that the Constitution-makers intended to remedy?
Precedent	What was the purpose intended to be ach ieved by the Constitution?
Precedent	To answer this question it is necessary to make a brief survey of our Nationalist movement ever since 1885 and the objectives sought to be achieved by that movement.
Precedent	684.
Precedent	The objectives underlying our Constitution began to take their shape as a result of the forces that operated in the national struggle during the British rule when the British resorted to arbitrary acts of oppression such as brutal assaults on unarmed satyagrahis, internments, deportations, detention without trial and muzzling of the press.
Precedent	The harshness with which the executive operated its repressive measures strengthened the demand for Constitutional guarantees of Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	As far back as 1895, the Constitution of India Bill, prepared by some eminent Indians, envisaged for India a Constitution guaranteeing to everyone of our citizens freedom of expression, inviolability of one's house, right to property, equality before the law, equal opportunity of admission to public offices, right to present claims,  petitions and complaints and right to personal liberty.
Precedent	After the publication of the Montague-Chelmsford Report, <ORG> at its special session held in Bombay in August 1918 demanded that the new Government of India Act should contain "Declaration of Rights of the people of India as British citizens".
Precedent	The proposed declaration was to embody among other things, guarantees in regard to equality before the law, protection in respect of life and liberty, freedom of speech and press and right of association.
Precedent	In its <ORG> in December of the same year, the <ORG> passed another resolution demanding the immediate repeal of all laws, regulations and ordinances restricting the free discussion of political questions and conferring on the executive the power to arrest, detain, intern, extern or imprison any British subject in India outside the process of ordinary Civil or Criminal law and the assimilation of the law of sedition to that of England.
Precedent	The Commonwealth of India Bill, finalised b y the National Convention in 1926 embodied a specific declaration of rights visualising for every person certain rights in terms practically identical with the relevant provisions of the Irish Constitution.
Precedent	The problems of minorities in India further strengthened the general argument in favour of inclusion of Fundamental Rights in the Indian Constitution.
Precedent	In its Madras Session in 1927, <ORG> firmly laid down that the basis of the future Constitution must be a declaration of Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	In 1928, <ORG> in its report incorporated a provision for enumeration of such rights, recommending their adoption as a part of the future Constitution of India.
Precedent	<ORG>, rejected the demand on the plea that an abstract declaration of such rights was useless unless there existed "the will and the means to make them effective".
Precedent	In 1932, in its Karachi Session, <ORG> reiterated its resolve to regard a written guarantee of Fundamental Rights as essenti al in any future Constitutional set up in India.
Precedent	The demand for the incorporation of the Fundamental Rights in the Constitutional document was reiterated by the Indian leaders at the Round Table Conferences.
Precedent	<ORG> of <ORG> rejected those demands.
Precedent	<ORG> (1944-45) was of the opinion that in the peculiar circumstances of India, the Fundamental Rights were necessary not only as assurance and guarantees to the minorities but also prescribing a standard of conduct for the legislatures, governments and the courts.
Precedent	The <ORG> felt that it was for the Constitution-making body to enumerate first the list of Fundamental Rights and then to undertake their further division into justiciable and non-justiciable rights and provide a suitable machinery for their enforcement.
Precedent	685.
Precedent	The atrocities committed during the Second World War and the world wide agitation for human rights, the liberties guaranteed in the Atlantic Charter, the U.N. Charter and the Declaration of Huma n Rights by <ORG> strengthened the demand for the incorporation of Fundamental Rights in our Constitution.
Precedent	<ORG> in 1946 recognised the need for a written guarantee of Fundamental Rights in the Constitution of India.
Precedent	It accordingly recommended the setting up of an advisory committee for reporting, inter alia, on Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	By the Objectives Resolution adopted on January 22, 1947, <ORG> solemnly pledged itself to draw up for India's future governance a Constitution wherein "shall be guaranteed and secured to all the people of India justice, social, economic and political, equality of status, of opportunity and before the law; freedom of thought, expression, belief, faith, worship, vocation, association and action subject to law and public morality and wherein adequate safeguard would be provided for minorities, backward and tribal areas and depressed and other backward classes".
RatioOfTheDecision	The close association between political freedom and social j ustice has become a common concept since the French Revolution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Since the end of the first World War, it was increasingly recognised that peace in the world can be established only if it is based on social justice.
RatioOfTheDecision	The most modern Constitutions contain declaration of social and economic principles, which emphasise, among other things, the duty of the <ORG> to strive for social security and to provide work, education and proper condition of employment for its citizens.
RatioOfTheDecision	In evolving the Fundamental Rights and the Directive Principles, our founding fathers, in addition to the experience gathered by them from the events that took place in other parts of the world, also drew largely on their experience in the past.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Directive Principles and the Fundamental Rights mainly proceed on the basis of Human Rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	Representative democracies will have no meaning without economic and social justice to the common man.
RatioOfTheDecision	This is a universal experience.
RatioOfTheDecision	Freedom from foreign rule can be looked upon only as an opportunity to br ing about economic and social advancement.
RatioOfTheDecision	After all freedom is nothing else but a chance to be better.
Precedent	It is this liberty to do better that is the theme of the Directive Principles of State Policy in Part IV of the Constitution.
Precedent	686.
Precedent	The Objectives Resolution passed by <ORG> in January 1947, is a definite landmark.
Precedent	It is a precursor to the preamble to our Constitution.
Precedent	It sets out in detail the objectives that were before our Constitution-makers.
Precedent	Those objectives have now been incorporated in the preamble to our Constitution which reads: WE, THE PEOPLE OF INDIA, having solemnly resolved to constitute India into a SOVEREIGN DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC and to secure to all its citizens:  JUSTICE, social, economic and political;  LIBERTY of thought, expression, belief, faith and worship;  EQUALITY of status and of opportunity;  and to promote among them all FRATERNITY assuring the dignity of the individual and the unity of the Nation;  IN OUR CONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY this twenty-sixth day of November, 1 949 do HEREBY ADOPT, ENACT AND GIVE TO OURSELVES THIS CONSTITUTION.
Precedent	687.
RatioOfTheDecision	From the preamble it is quite clear that the two primary objectives that were before <ORG> were (1) to constitute India into a Sovereign Democratic Republic and (2) to secure to its citizens the rights mentioned therein.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our founding fathers, at any rate, most of them had made immense sacrifices for the sake of securing those objectives.
RatioOfTheDecision	For them freedom from British rule was an essential step to render social justice to the teeming millions in this country and to secure to one and all in this country the essential human rights.
Precedent	Their Constitutional plan was to build a welfare state and an egalitarian society.
Precedent	688.
Precedent	Now that we have set out the objectives intended to be achieved by our founding fathers, the question arises whether those very persons could have intended to empower the <ORG>, a body constituted under the Constitution to destroy the ideals that they dearly cherished and for which they fought and sac rificed.
Precedent	689.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the nature of the power granted is clear and beyond doubt the fact that it may be misused is wholly irrelevant.
RatioOfTheDecision	But, if there is reasonable doubt as to the nature of the power granted then the <ORG> has to take into consideration the consequences that might ensue by interpreting the same as an unlimited power.
RatioOfTheDecision	We have earlier come to the conclusion that the word "amendment" is not an expression having a precise cannotation.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has more than one meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	Hence it is necessary to examine the consequence of accepting the contention of the <ORG> and the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore let us understand the consequences of conceding the power claimed.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to the <ORG> and the <ORG> that power inter alia, includes the power to (1) destroy the sovereignty of this country and make this country a satellite of any other country; (2) substitute the democratic form of government by monarchical or authoritarian form of government; (3) break up the unity of this country and form various independent <ORG>; (4) d estroy the secular character of this country and substitute the same by a theocratic form of government; (5) abrogate completely the various rights conferred on the citizens as well as on the minorities; (6) revoke the mandate given to the States to build a Welfare State; (7) extend the life of the two <ORG> indefinitely; and (8) amend the amending power in such a way as to make the Constitution legally or at any rate practically unamendable.
RatioOfTheDecision	In fact, their contention was that the legal sovereignty, in the ultimate analysis rests only in the amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	At one stage, <ORG> and the States had grudgingly conceded that the power conferred under Article 368 cannot be used to abrogate the Constitution but later under pressure of questioning by some of us they changed their position and said that by 'abrogation' they meant repeal of the Constitution as a whole.
RatioOfTheDecision	When they were asked as to what they meant by saying that the power conferred under Article 368 cannot be used to repeal  the Constitution, all that they said was that while amending the Constitution, at least one clause in the Constitution must be retained though every other clause or part of the Constitution including the preamble can be deleted and some other provisions substituted.
RatioOfTheDecision	Their submission in short was this that so long as the expression the "Constitution of India" is retained, every other article or part of it can be replaced.
RatioOfTheDecision	They tried to tone down the effect of their claim by saying that, though legally, there is no limitation on the amending power, there are bound to be political compulsions which make it impermissible for <ORG> to exercise its amending power in a manner unacceptable to the people at large.
RatioOfTheDecision	The strength of political reaction is uncertain.
RatioOfTheDecision	It depends upon various factors such as the political consciousness of the people, their level of education, strength of the various political organizations in the country, the manner in which the mass media is used and finally the capacity of the governm ent to suppress agitations.
RatioOfTheDecision	Hence the peoples' will to resist an unwanted amendment cannot be taken into consideration in interpreting the ambit of the amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	Extra legal forces work in a different plane altogether.
RatioOfTheDecision	690.
RatioOfTheDecision	We find it difficult to accept the contention that our Constitution-makers after making immense sacrifices for achieving certain ideals made provision in the Constitution itself for the destruction of those ideals.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no doubt as men of experience and sound political knowledge, they must have known that social, economic and political changes are bound to come with the passage of time and the Constitution must be capable of being so adjusted as to be able to respond to those new demands.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution is not a mere political document.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is essentially a social document.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is based on a social philosophy and every social philosophy like every religion has two main features, namely, basic and circumstantial.
RatioOfTheDecision	The former remains constant but the latter is subject to change.
RatioOfTheDecision	Th e core of a religion always remains constant but the practices associated with it may change.
RatioOfTheDecision	Likewise, a Constitution like ours contains certain features which are so essential that they cannot be changed or destroyed.
RatioOfTheDecision	In any event it cannot be destroyed from within.
RatioOfTheDecision	In other words, one cannot legally use the Constitution to destroy itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	Under Article 368 the amended Constitution must remain 'the Constitution' which means the original Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	When we speak of the 'abrogation' or 'repeal' of the Constitution, we do not refer to any form but to substance.
RatioOfTheDecision	If one or more of the basic features of the Constitution are taken away to that extent the Constitution is abrogated or repealed.
RatioOfTheDecision	If all the basic features of the Constitution are repealed and some other provisions inconsistent with those features are incorporated, it cannot still remain the Constitution referred to in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The personality of the Constitution must remain unchanged.
Precedent	691.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is also necessary to bear in mind that the power to  amend the Constitution is conferred on <ORG>, a body constituted under the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The people as such are not associated with the amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	From the preamble we get that it is the people of this country who conferred this Constitution on themselves.
RatioOfTheDecision	The statement in the preamble that the people of this country conferred the Constitution on themselves is not open to challenge before this <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Its factual correctness cannot be gone into by this <ORG> which again is a creature of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The facts set out in the preamble have to be accepted by this <ORG> as correct.
RatioOfTheDecision	Anyone who knows the composition of <ORG> can hardly dispute the claim of the members of that <ORG> that their voice was the voice of the people.
RatioOfTheDecision	They were truly the representatives of the people, even though they had been elected under a narrow franchise.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution framed by them has been accepted and worked by the people for the last 23 years and it is too late in the day now  to question, as was sought to be done an one stage by the Advocate-General of Maharashtra, the fact, that the people of this country gave the Constitution to themselves.
Precedent	692.
RatioOfTheDecision	When a power to amend the Constitution is given to the people, its contents can be construed to be larger than when that power is given to a body constituted under that Consitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Two-thirds of the members of the two <ORG> need not necessarily represent even the majority of the people of this country.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our electoral system is such that even a minority of voters can elect more than two-thirds of the members of the either <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	That is seen from our experience in the past.
RatioOfTheDecision	That apart, our Constitution was framed on the basis of consensus and not on the basis of majority votes.
RatioOfTheDecision	It provides for the protection of the minorities.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the majority opinion is taken as the guiding factor then the guarantees given to the minorities may become valueless.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is well known that the representatives of the minorities in t he <ORG> gave up their claim for special protection which they were demanding in the past because of the guarantee of Fundamental Rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore the contention on behalf of the Union and the States that the two-thirds of the members in the two <ORG> are always authorised to speak on behalf of the entire people of this country is unacceptable.
Precedent	693.
Precedent	The President of India under Article 60 of the Constitution is required to take an oath before he assumes his office to the effect that he will "to the best of his ability preserve, protect and defend the Constitution".
Precedent	Somewhat similar oaths have to be taken by the Governors of States, Ministers at the Centre and in the States, Judges of the superior courts and other important functionaries.
Precedent	When the President of India is compelled to give assent to a Constitutional amendment which might destroy the basic features of the Constitution, can it be said that he is true to his oath to "preserve, protect and defend the Constitution" or d oes his oath merely mean that he is to defend the amending power of <ORG> ?
Precedent	Can the amending power of <ORG> be considered as the Constitution?
Precedent	The whole scheme and the structure of our Constitution proceeds on the basis that there are certain basic features which are expected to be permanent.
Precedent	694.
Precedent	Implied limitations on the powers conferred under a statute constitute a general feature of all statutes.
Precedent	The position cannot be different in the case of powers conferred under a Constitution.
Precedent	A grant of power in general terms of even in absolute terms may be qualified by other express provisions in the same enactment or may be qualified by the implications of the context or even by considerations arising out of what appears to be the general scheme of the statute.
Precedent	In Re The Central Provinces and Berar (Central Provinces and Berar Act No.
Precedent	XIV of 1938  F.C.R.
Precedent	p. 18, Sir <NAME> observed at p. 42:  A grant of the power in general terms, standing by itself, would no doubt be construed in the wider  sense; but it may be qualified by other express provisions in the same enactment, by the implications of the context, and even by considerations arising out of what appears to be the general scheme of the Act.
Precedent	695.
Precedent	Lord <NAME> in <NAME> v. Commonwealth of Australia [1936] A.C. 578 at 613 stated the law thus:  The question, then, is one of construction, and in the ultimate resort must be determined upon the actual words used, read not in vacuo but as occurring in a single complex instrument, in which one part may throw light on another.
Precedent	The Constitution has been described as the federal compact, and the construction must hold a balance between all its parts.
Precedent	696.
RatioOfTheDecision	Several of the powers conferred under our Constitution have been held to be subject to implied limitations though those powers are expressed in general terms or even in absolute terms.
RatioOfTheDecision	The executive power of the <ORG> is vested in the President and he is authorised to exercise the same either directly or through officers subordinate to him in accord ance with the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Under Article 75 , it is the President who can appoint the Prime Minister and the Ministers are to hold office during his pleasure.
RatioOfTheDecision	Despite this conferment of power in general and absolute terms, because of the scheme of the Constitution, its underlying principles and the implications arising from the other provisions in the Constitution, this <ORG> has held in several cases that the President is a Constitutional head and the real executive power vests in the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Similarly though plenary powers of legislation have been conferred on the <ORG> and the State legislatures in respect of the legislative topics allotted to them, yet this <ORG> has opined that by the exercise of that power neither <ORG> nor the State legislatures can delegate to other authorities their essential legislative functions nor could they invade on the judicial power.
RatioOfTheDecision	These limitations were spelled out from the nature of the power conferred and from the scheme of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	But, it was urged  on behalf of the Union and the States that, though there might be implied limitations on other powers conferred under the Constitution, there cannot bo any implied limitations on the amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	We see no basis for this distinction.
RatioOfTheDecision	The amending power is one of the powers conferred under the Constitution whatever the nature of that power might be.
RatioOfTheDecision	That apart, during the course of hearing the learned Solicitor-General had to concede that there are certain implied limitations on the amending power itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	The amending power of <ORG> in certain respects is subject to the express limitations placed on it by the proviso to Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 prescribes that if <ORG> wants to amend Article 54 , the Article dealing with the election of the President, the amendment in question must be ratified by the legislatures of not less than one half of the States.
Statute	No such express limitation is placed on the amending power of <ORG> in respect of Article 52 which provides that there shall be a President  of India.
Argument	If it be held that Article 52 can be amended without complying with the requirements of the proviso to Article 368 , the limitation placed on <ORG> in respect of the amendment of Article 54 becomes meaningless.
Argument	When this incongruity was pointed out to the learned Solicitor-General, he conceded that in view of the fact that before Article 54 can be amended, the form and the manner laid down in proviso to Article 368 has to be followed, it follows as a matter of implication that the same would be the position for the amendment of Article 52.
Argument	The only other alternative inference is that Article 52 can never be amended at all.
Precedent	It is not necessary to go into the other implications that may arise from the language of Article  368.
Precedent	697.
Precedent	From what has been said above, it is clear that the amending power under Article 368 is also subject to implied limitations.
Precedent	The contention that a power to amend a Constitution cannot be subject to any implied limitation is negatived by the observations of the Judici <ORG> in <CITATION_153174>  A.C. 172.
Precedent	The decision of the Judici<ORG> in <NAME>'s case (supra) held that <ORG> was incompetent to encroach upon the judicial power also lends support to our conclusion that there can be implied limitations on the amending power.
Precedent	698.
Precedent	In support of the contention that there can be no implied limitations on the amending power, our attention was invited to writings of various jurists of eminence.
Precedent	Most of the writings relate to the amending power under Article 5 of the United States Constitution.
Precedent	It is true that in the United States most of the writers are of opinion that there is no implied limitation on the amending power under the United States Constitution.
Precedent	<ORG> of the United States has not specifically pronounced on this question.
Precedent	The only case in which the question of implied limitation on the amending power under the United States Constitution came up for consideration was Rhode Island v. <NAME> 64 L. Edn.
Precedent	946.
Precedent	In that case the Supre me Court of United States rejecting the contention that the 18th Amendment-National Prohibition Amendment-was outside the amending power under Article 5 because of implied limitations on that power, held that the Amendment was valid.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG>, however, did not discuss the question of implied limitations on the amending power as such.
RatioOfTheDecision	In fact the judgment that was rendered in that case gave no reasons.
RatioOfTheDecision	Only certain questions were formulated and answered.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is not clear from the judgment whether the particular limitation pleaded was rejected' or whether the plea of implied limitation on the amending power was rejected though writers of most text books have taken the view that the court rejected the plea of implied limitations on the amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may be noted that in the United States not a single human right has been taken away or even its scope narrowed.
RatioOfTheDecision	There the controversy centred round two questions viz.
RatioOfTheDecision	(1) abolition of slavery and (2) prohibition of sale and consumption of liquor.
RatioOfTheDecision	We will  not be justified in expounding our Constitution on the basis of the controversies relating to those issues.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 5 of the U.S. Constitution is not similar to Article 368 of our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the former Article, there is an express limitation on the amending power i.e.
RatioOfTheDecision	regarding the representation of the States in the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Further the amendment under Article 5 of the United States Constitution can be proposed either by the <ORG> or by State Conventions.
RatioOfTheDecision	They may be ratified either by a minimum of 3/4th of the State Legislatures or by Conventions held in at least 3/4th of the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	Whether a particular amendment should be ratified by the State Legislatures or by the State Conventions is entirely left to the discretion of the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	As held by <ORG>, the decision of the <ORG> on that question is final.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution makers must have proceeded on the basis that the <ORG> is likely to require the amendment of basic elements or fundamental features of the Constit ution to be ratified by State Conventions.
RatioOfTheDecision	The scheme of no two Constitutions is similar.
Precedent	Their provisions are not similar.
Precedent	The language employed in the amending clauses differ from Constitution to Constitution.
Precedent	The objectives lying behind them also are bound to differ.
Precedent	Each country has its own needs, its own philosophy, its own way of life and above all its own problems.
Precedent	Hence in our opinion, we will be clouding the issues, if we allow ourselves to be burdened either by the writings of the various writers on other Constitutions or by the decisions rendered on the basis of the provisions of the other Constittuions, though Counsel on either side spared no efforts to place before us various opinions expressed by various writers as well as the decisions rendered by several courts including <ORG> in United States of America.
Precedent	699.
Precedent	The rule laid down by <ORG> in R. v. Burah (1878) I.A.
Precedent	178 that "if what has been done is legislation, within the general scope of the affirmative words whic h give the power, and if it violates no express condition or restriction by which that power is limited it is not for any court of Justice to inquire further, or to enlarge constructively those conditions and restrictions" was heavily relied on by Mr. <NAME>.
Precedent	That decision, however, has been confined to the interpretation of conditional legislations and the rule that it laid down has not been applied while considering the question whether there are any implied limitations on any of the powers conferred under a statute or Constitution.
Precedent	700.
Precedent	It was strenuously urged on behalf of the Union and the States that if we come to the conclusion that there are implied or inherent limitations on the amending power of <ORG> under Article 368 , it would be well nigh impossible for <ORG> to decide before hand as to what amendments it could make and what amendments it is forbidden to make.
Precedent	According to the Counsel for the Union and the States, the conceptions of basic elements and fundamental features are illusiv e conceptions and their determination may differ from judge to judge and therefore we would be making the task of <ORG> impossible if we uphold the contention that there are implied or inherent limitations on the amending power under Article 368.
Precedent	We are unable to accept this contention.
Precedent	The broad contours of the basic elements or fundamental features of our Constitution are clearly delineated in the preamble.
Precedent	Unlike in most of the other Constitutions, it is comparatively easy in the case of our Constitution to discern and determine the basic elements or the fundamental features of our Constitution.
Precedent	For doing so, one has only to look to the preamble.
Precedent	It is true that there are bound to be border line cases where there can be difference of opinion.
Precedent	That is so in all important legal questions.
Precedent	But the courts generally proceed on the presumption of Constitutionality of all legislations.
Precedent	The presumption of the Constitutional validity of a statute will also apply to Constitutional amendments.
Precedent	It is not correct  to say that what is difficult to decide does not exist at all.
Precedent	For that matter, there are no clear guidelines before the <ORG> to determine what are essential legislative functions which cannot be delegated, what legislations do invade on the judicial power or what restrictions are reasonable restrictions in public interest under Article 19(2) to 19(6) and yet by and large the legislations made by <ORG> or the State legislatures in those respects have been upheld by courts.
Precedent	No doubt, there were occasions when courts were constrained to strike down some legislations as ultra vires the Constitution.
Precedent	The position as regard the ascertainment of the basic elements or fundamental features of the Constitution can by no means be more difficult than the difficulty of the legislatures to determine before hand the Constitutionality of legislations made under various other heads.
Precedent	Arguments based on the difficulties likely to be faced by the legislatures are of very little importance and they are essentially arg uments against judicial review.
Precedent	701.
Precedent	Large number of decisions rendered by courts in U.S.A., Canada, Australia, United Kingdom, Ceylon and Ireland, dealing with the question of implied limitations on the amending power and also as regards the meaning of the word "amendment" were read to us at the hearing.
Precedent	Such of those that are relevant have been considered by the learned Chief Justice in the judgment just now delivered.
Precedent	We entirely agree with the views expressed by him and we cannot usefully add to the same.
Precedent	702.
Precedent	It was contended on behalf of the Union and the States that, the Constitution should not be treated as something sacred.
Precedent	It should be regarded just in the same way as we regard other human institutions.
Precedent	It should be possible to alter every part of it from time to time so as to bring it in harmony with the new and changed conditions.
Precedent	In support of this contention we were invited to the writings of the various writers such as <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> <NAME> etc.
Precedent	It was further urg ed that <ORG> knowing that, it will disperse, had arranged for the recreation of <ORG>, under Article 368 in order to so shape the Constitution as to meet the demands of the time.
Precedent	However, attractive these theories may sound in the abstract, on a closer examination, it will be seen that they are fallacious, more particularly in a <ORG> set up like ours.
Precedent	We have earlier noticed chat under our electoral system, it is possible for a party to get a 2/3rd majority in the two <ORG> even if that party does not get an absolute majority of votes cast at the election.
Precedent	That apart, when a party goes to election, it presents to the electorate diverse programmes and holds out various promises.
Precedent	The programmes presented or the promises held out need not necessarily include proposals for amending the Constitution.
Precedent	During the General Elections to Parliament in 1952, 1957, 1962 and 1967, no proposal to amend the Constitution appears to have been placed before the elec torate.
Precedent	Even when proposals for amendment of the Constitution are placed before the electorate as was done by <ORG> in 1971, the proposed amendments are not usually placed before the electorate.
Precedent	Under these circumstances, the claim that the electorate had given a mandate to the party to amend the Constitution in any particular manner is unjustified.
Precedent	Further a Parliamentary Democracy like ours functions on the basis of the party system.
Precedent	The mechanics of operation of the party system as well as the system of <ORG> government are such that the people as a whole can have little control in the matter of detailed law-making.
Precedent	"...on practically every issue in the modern <ORG>, the serried millions of voters cannot do more than accept or reject the solutions; offered.
Precedent	The stage is too vast to permit of the nice shades of quantitative distinction impressing themselves upon the public mind.
Precedent	It has rarely the leisure, and seldom the information, to do more than indicate the general tendency of its will.
Precedent	I t is in the process of law-making that the subtler adjustments must be effected."
Precedent	(Laski : A Grammar of Politics; Fifth Edn.
Precedent	pp.
Precedent	313-314).
Precedent	703.
Precedent	The assertion that either the majority of members of <ORG> or even 2/3rd members of <ORG> speak on behalf of the nation has no basis in fact.
Precedent	Indeed it may be possible for the ruling party to carry through important Constitutional amendments even after it has lost the confidence of the electorate.
Precedent	The members of <ORG> are elected for a term of five years.
Precedent	The ruling party or its members may or may not enjoy the confidence of the electorate throughout their terms of office.
Precedent	Therefore it will not be correct to say that whenever <ORG> amends the Constitution, it must be held to have done it as desired by the people.
Precedent	704.
Precedent	There is a further fallacy in the contention that whenever Constitution is amended, we should presume that the amendment in question was made in order to adapt the Constitution to respond to the growing needs of the people.
Precedent	We have e arlier seen that by using the amending power, it is theoretically possible for <ORG> to extend its own life indefinitely and also, to amend the Constitution in such a manner as to make it either legally or practically unamendable ever afterwards.
Precedent	A power which is capable of being used against the people themselves cannot be considered as a power exercised on behalf of the people or in their interest.
Precedent	705.
RatioOfTheDecision	On a careful consideration of the various aspects of the case, we are convinced that the <ORG> has no power to abrogate or emasculate the basic elements or fundamental' features of the Constitution such as the sovereignty of India, the democratic character of our polity, the unity of the country, the essential features of the individual freedoms secured to the citizens.
RatioOfTheDecision	Nor has the <ORG> the power to revoke the mandate to build a Welfare State and egalitarian society.
RatioOfTheDecision	These limitations are only illustrative and not exhaustive.
RatioOfTheDecision	Despite these limitations, however, there can be no question that  the amending power is a wide power and it reaches every Article and every part of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	That power can be used to reshape the Constitution to fulfil the obligations imposed on the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It can also be used to reshape the Constitution within the limits mentioned earlier, to make it an effective instrument for social good.
RatioOfTheDecision	We are unable to agree with the contention that in order to build a Welfare <ORG>, it is necessary to destroy some of the human freedoms.
RatioOfTheDecision	That, at any rate is not the perspective of our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution envisages that the <ORG>s should without delay make available to all the citizens of this country the real benefits of those freedoms in a democratic way.
RatioOfTheDecision	Human freedoms are lost gradually and imperceptibly and their destruction 'is generally followed by authoritarian rule.
RatioOfTheDecision	That is what history has taught us.
RatioOfTheDecision	Struggle between liberty and power is eternal.
RatioOfTheDecision	Vigilance is the price that we like every other democratic society have to pay to safeguard the democratic values  enshrined in our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Even the best of governments are not averse to have more and more power to carry out their plans and programmes which they may sincerely believe to be in public interest.
RatioOfTheDecision	But a freedom once lost is hardly ever regained except by revolution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Every encroachment on freedoms sets a pattern for further encroachments.
Precedent	Our Constitutional plan is to eradicate poverty without destruction of individual freedoms.
Precedent	706.
Precedent	In the result we uphold the contention of Mr. <NAME> that the word "amendment" in Article 368 carries with it certain limitation and, further, that the power conferred under Article 368 is subject Co certain implied limitations though that power is quite large.
Precedent	707.
Precedent	Next, we shall take up for consideration the contentions of Mr. <NAME> regarding the validity of the 24th, 25th and 29th Amendments.
Precedent	708.
Precedent	It was contended on behalf of the petitioners that in enacting the 24th Amendment Act , the <ORG> has exceeded its powers.
Precedent	It has purported to enlarge its limit ed power of amendment into an unlimited power, by the exercise of which it can damage or destroy the basic elements or fundamental features of the Constitution.
Precedent	It was said that such an exercise is an unlawful usurpation of power.
RatioOfTheDecision	Consequently, the 24th Amendment Act is liable to be struck down.
RatioOfTheDecision	To pronounce on that contention, it is necessary to examine at the very outset whether the 24th Amendment Act has really enlarged the powers of the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	If we come to the conclusion that it has not enlarged the power of the <ORG>, as we think it has not, the various contentions of Mr. <NAME> do not arise for consideration.
RatioOfTheDecision	709.
RatioOfTheDecision	Now let us see what is the true effect of the Constitution 24th Amendment Act , 1971.
RatioOfTheDecision	That Act amended Article 13 and Article 368.
Fact	By that Act one more sub-article has been added to Article 13 viz.
Statute	Sub- article (4) which reads thus: Nothing in this article shall apply to any amendment of this Constitution made under Article 368.
Statute	710.
Statute	Section 3 of that Act which amends Article  368 reads.
Statute	Article 368 of the Constitution shall be renumbered as Clause (2) thereof, and-  (a) for the marginal heading to that article the following marginal heading shall be substituted, namely:  Power of <ORG> to amend the Constitution and procedure therefor".
Statute	(b) before Clause (2) as so-renumbered, the following clause shall be inserted, namely:  Notwithstanding anything in the Constitution, <ORG> may in exercise of its constituent power amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any provision of this Constitution in accordance with the procedure laid down in this article.
Statute	(c) in Clause (2) as so re-numbered, for the words "it shall be presented to the President for his assent and upon such assent being given to the Bill", the words "It shall be presented to the President who shall give his assent to the Bill and thereupon" shall be substituted;  (d) after Clause (2) as so re-numbered, the following clause shall be inserted, namely-  (3) Nothing in Article 13 shall apply to any amendment  made under this Article.
Statute	711.
Statute	The material changes effected under this Act are:  1.
Statute	Addition of Clause (4) to Article 13 and Clause (3) to Article 368;   2.
Precedent	Change in the marginal heading;  3.
Precedent	Specific mention of the fact that the power is conferred on the <ORG> to amend the Constitution;  4.
Precedent	The power conferred on the <ORG> is claimed to be a constituent power;  5.
Precedent	That power is described as a power to "amend by way of addition, variation or repeal of any provision of this Constitution" and  6.
Precedent	Making it obligatory for the President to give assent to the Bill amending the Constitution.
Precedent	712.
RatioOfTheDecision	In our opinion the 24th Amendment has not made any material change in Article 368 as it stood originally.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is true the original Article did not say specifically that the power to amend rested with <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the other hand, while setting out the procedure of amendment, it referred to the functions of the two Houses of <ORG> and the President.
RatioOfTheDecision	Because of the fact that <ORG> was not specifically  referred to in Article 368 , as it originally stood, the learned Advocate General of Maharashtra wanted us to spell out that the power conferred under Article 368 , as it originally stood was not conferred on <ORG> as such but on the two Houses of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	We have earlier rejected that contention.
RatioOfTheDecision	We agree with the learned Attorney General that the power in question had been conferred on <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 79 says that "There shall be a <ORG> for the Union, which shall consist of the President and two Houses to be known respectively as <ORG> and <ORG>".
RatioOfTheDecision	Whether an enactment refers to the three components of <ORG> separately or whether all the three of them are compendiously referred to as <ORG>, in law it makes no difference.
RatioOfTheDecision	In <NAME> case, in <NAME> case as well as in <NAME>'s case, each one of the Judges who delivered judgments specifically mentioned that the power to amend the Constitution was vested in <ORG> though there  was difference of opinion on the question whether that power could be traced to Article 368 or Article 248 read with Entry 97 of List I.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no ground for taking a different view.
Precedent	713.
RatioOfTheDecision	We have already come to the conclusion that Article 368 as it originally stood comprehended both power as well as procedure to amend the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Hence the change effected in the marginal note has no significance whatsoever.
RatioOfTheDecision	The marginal note as it stood earlier was in a sense incomplete.
RatioOfTheDecision	The expression 'constituent power' is used to describe only the nature of the power of amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	Every amending power, however large or however small it might be, is a fact of a constituent power.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power, though described to be 'constituent power', still continues to be an 'amending power'.
RatioOfTheDecision	The scope and ambit of the power is essentially contained in the word 'amendment'.
RatioOfTheDecision	Hence, from the fact that the new article specifically refers to that power as a constituent power, it cannot be understood that the contents of the power hav e undergone any change.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power conferred under the original Article being a limited power to amend the Constitution, the constituent power to amend the Constitution referred to in the amended Article must also be held to carry with it the limitation to which that power was subject earlier.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is also no significance in the substitution of the expression "amend by way of addition, variation or repeal of any provision of this Constitution" found in the amended Article in the place of the expression "amendment of the Constitution" found in the original Article.
RatioOfTheDecision	Every power to amend a statute must necessarily include within itself some power to make addition, variation or repeal of any provision of the statute.
RatioOfTheDecision	Here again, the power conferred under the original Article being a limited one, that limitation will continue to operate notwithstanding the change in the phraseology.
RatioOfTheDecision	The words 'addition, variation or repeal' only prescribe the modes or manner by which an 'amendment' may be made, but they do not det ermine the scope of the power of 'amendment'.
RatioOfTheDecision	The original Article 368 mentioned that after the bill for amendment of the Constitution is passed by the two <ORG> in the manner prescribed in Article 368 " it shall be presented to the President for his assent and upon such assent being given to the Bill, the Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the bill".
RatioOfTheDecision	The amended Article makes a change.
RatioOfTheDecision	It prescribes that when the Bill is presented to the President, he "shall give his assent to the Bill".
RatioOfTheDecision	Some comment was made at the bar about the inappropriateness of commanding the President to give his assent to the Bill.
RatioOfTheDecision	That is a question of propriety.
RatioOfTheDecision	The substance of the matter is that when the Bill is presented to the President, he shall not withhold his assent.
RatioOfTheDecision	This change cannot be said to have damaged or destroyed any basic element of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	In fact Article 111 which deals with the assent to the Bills specifically prescribes that when a money Bill, after having  been passed by <ORG> is presented to the President he "shall not withhold assent therefrom".
RatioOfTheDecision	Hence it cannot be said that the change made in Article 368 relating to the assent of the President has any great importance in the scheme of our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	In fact under our Constitution the President is only a Constitutional head.
RatioOfTheDecision	Ordinarily he has to act on the advice of the cabinet.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no possibility of the Constitution being amended in opposition to the wishes of the cabinet.
RatioOfTheDecision	714.
RatioOfTheDecision	The only change that remains to be considered is as to the exclusion of the application of Article 13 to an amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	We have earlier come to the conclusion that Article 13 as it stood earlier did not bar the amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 13(4) and 368(3) make explicit what was implicit.
Precedent	715.
Precedent	It was contended that by means of the 24th Amendment <ORG> intended to and in fact purported to enlarge its amending power.
Precedent	In this connection reliance was placed on the statement of  objects and reasons attached to the Bill which resulted in the 24th Amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power of <ORG> does not rest upon its professed intention.
RatioOfTheDecision	It cannot acquire a power which it otherwise did not possess.
RatioOfTheDecision	We are unable to accept the contention that Clause (e) to the proviso to Article 368 confers power on <ORG> to enlarge its own power.
RatioOfTheDecision	In our judgment the power to amend the Constitution as well as the ordinary procedure to amend any part of the Constitution was and is contained in the main part of the Article.
RulingByPresentCourt	The proviso merely places further restrictions on the procedure to amend the articles mentioned therein.
RulingByPresentCourt	Clause (e) to the proviso stipulates that Article 368 cannot be amended except in the manner provided in the proviso.
RulingByPresentCourt	In the absence of that clause, Article 368 could have been amended by following the procedure laid down in the main part.
RatioOfTheDecision	At best Clause (e) of the proviso merely indicates that Article 368 itself comes within its own purview.
RatioOfTheDecision	As we have already seen, the main part of Artic le 368 as it stood earlier, expressly lays down only the procedure to be followed in amending the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power to amend is only implied therein.
RatioOfTheDecision	716.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is difficult to accept the contention that an implied power was impliedly permitted to be enlarged.
RatioOfTheDecision	If that was so, there was nomeaning in limiting that power originally.
RatioOfTheDecision	Limitation on the power to amend the Constitution would operate even when Article 368 is amended.
RatioOfTheDecision	A limited power cannot be used to enlarge the same power into an absolute power.
RatioOfTheDecision	We respectfully agree with the observation of <NAME> (as he then was) in <NAME>'s case that what <ORG> cannot do directly, it also cannot do indirectly.
RatioOfTheDecision	We have earlier held that the "amendment of this Constitution" means the amendment of every part of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It cannot be denied that Article 368 is but a part of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Hence, the mere fact that the mover of the 24th Amendment Act , in the Statement of Objects and Reasons laid claim to certain power does not go to sh ow that <ORG> either endorsed that claim or could have conferred on itself such a power.
RatioOfTheDecision	It must be deemed to have exercised only such power as it possessed.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is a well-accepted rule of construction that if a provision is reasonably capable of two interpretations the <ORG> must accept that interpretation which makes the provsion valid.
Precedent	If the power conferred on <ORG> to amend the Constitution under Article 368 as it stood originally is a limited power, as we think it is, <ORG> cannot enlarge the scope of that power-see Attorney General for the State of New South Wales v. <ORG>; 6, C.L.R.
Precedent	469 Ex Parte Walsh and Johnson; In Re <NAME>; 37, C.L.R.
Precedent	36 at p. 67 and <ORG> v. The <ORG> 83, C.L.R.
Precedent	p 1.
Precedent	717.
Precedent	For the reasons mentioned heretofore, the scope of <ORG>'s power to amend the Constitution or any part thereof must be held to have remained as it was before the 24th Amendment notwithstanding the alterations made in the phra seology of Article 368.
Precedent	The 24th Amendment made explicit, what was implicit in the unamended Article 368.
Precedent	In this view of the matter the 24th Amendment must be held to be valid.
Precedent	718.
Precedent	This takes us to the validity of the Constitution 25th Amendment Act .
Precedent	It is necessary to examine the scope and effect of that Act for deciding the question whether that Act or any one of its provisions can be held to be outside the amending power of the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	That Act has three sections.
RatioOfTheDecision	We are not concerned with the first section which sets out the short title.
Statute	Clause (a) of the second section amends Article 31(2).
Statute	Clause (b) of that section incorporates into the Constitution Article 31(2B).
Statute	Section 3 introduces into the Constitution a new Article viz.
Statute	Article 31C.
Statute	719.
Statute	Let us first take up the newly substituted Article 31(2) in the place of the old Article 31(2) and examine its scope.
RatioOfTheDecision	To do so, it is necessary to examine the history of that Article.
RatioOfTheDecision	720.
Statute	Article 31(2) has undergone several changes.
Statute	As originally ena cted it read thus: No property, movable or immovable, including any interest in, or in any company owning, any commercial or industrial undertaking, shall be taken possession of or acquired for public purposes under any law authorising the taking of such possession or such acquisition, unless the law provides for compensation for the property taken possession of or acquired and either fixes the amount of the compensation, or specifies the principles on which, and the manner in which, the compensation is to be determined and given."
Statute	721.
Statute	That Article was amended first by the Fourth Amendment Act 1955 and, thereafter by the Twenty-fifth Amendment Act, 1971.
Statute	At a later stage, it will be necessary for us to compare Article 31(2) as it stood after the Fourth Amendment Act and as it stands after the Twenty-fifth Amendment Act.
RatioOfTheDecision	Hence we shall quote them side by side.
Statute	Article 31(2) as substituted by Article 3(2) as susbtituted by the 4th Amendment Act 1955 the 25th Amendment Act 1971 No property shall be compulsoril y No property shall be compulsorily acquired or requisitioned save for acquired or requisitioned save for a public purpose and save by a public purpose and save by authority of a law which provides for authority of a law which provides compensation for the property so for acquisition or requisitioning acquired or requisitioned and either of the property for an amount fixes the amount of the compensation which may be fixed by such law or specifies the principles on or which may be determined in which and the manner in which, accordance with such principles the compensation is to be determined and given in such manner as may mined and given; and no such law be specified in such law; and shall be called in question in any no such law shall be called court on the ground that the in question in any court on the compensation provided by that law is ground that the amount so fixed not adequate.
Precedent	or determined is not adequate or that the whole or any part of such amount is to be given otherwise than in cash: Provided  that in making any law providing for the compulsory acquisition of any property of an educatioanl institution established and administered by a minority, referred to Clause (1) of Article 30 , the <ORG> shall eusure that the amount fixed by or determined under such law for the acquisition of such property is Such as would not restrict or abrogate the right guaranteed uder that clause.
Precedent	722.
RatioOfTheDecision	For finding out the true scope of Article 31(2 ), as it stands now, the learned Advocate General of Maharashtra as well as the Solicitor General has taken us through the history of this Article.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to them the Article as it stands now truly represents the intention of the Constitution makers.
RatioOfTheDecision	In support of that contention, we were asked to go through the <ORG> debates relating to that article.
RatioOfTheDecision	In particular, we were invited to go through the speeches made by Pandit <NAME>, Sir <NAME>, Dr. <NAME> and Dr. <NAME>.
RatioOfTheDecision	In our opinion, it is impermissible for us to do so.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is a well settl ed rule of construction that speeches made by members of a legislature in the course of debates relating to the enactment of a statute cannot be used as aids for interpreting any of the provisions of the statute.
RatioOfTheDecision	The same rule is applicable when we are called upon to interpret the provisions of a Constitution.
Precedent	This Court ruled in <CITATION_1334665> .
Precedent	S.C.R.
Precedent	113 that speeches made by the members of <ORG> in the course of the debates on the draft Constitution cannot be used as aid for interpreting the Constitution.
Precedent	In the course of his judgment <NAME> speaking for <ORG> observed at p. 1121 of the Report:  It remains only to point out that the use made by the learned Judges below of the speeches made by the members of <ORG> in the course of the debates on the draft Constitution is unwarranted.
Precedent	That this form of extrinsic aid to the interpretation of statutes is not admissible has been generally accepted in England, and the same rule has been  observed in the construction of Indian Statutes-see Administrator-General of Bengal v. <NAME> [(1895 22 I.A.
Precedent	107, 118].
Precedent	The reason behind the rule was explained by one of us in <NAME>'s case [1950] S.C.R.
Precedent	88, at 144 thus:  A speech made in the course of the debate on a bill could at best be indicative of the subjective intent of the speaker, but it could not reflect the inarticulate mental process lying behind the majority vote which carried the Bill.
Precedent	Nor is it reasonable to assume that the minds of all those legislators were in accord", or as it is more tersely put in a American case-  Those who did not speak may not have agreed with those who did; and those who spoke might differ from each other-United States v. <ORG> 169 U.S. 290, 318.
Precedent	723.
RatioOfTheDecision	No decision of this <ORG> dissenting from the view taken in the above case was brought to our notice.
RatioOfTheDecision	But it was urged that this <ORG> had ignored the rule laid down in <ORG> case (supra) in <NAME>'s case as well as in  what is popularly known as the Privy Purse (1971) 3, S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	9 case.
RatioOfTheDecision	We do not think that this statement is accurate.
RatioOfTheDecision	In <NAME>'s case, <NAME> referred to certain portions of speeches made by Pandit <NAME> and Dr. <NAME>.
RatioOfTheDecision	But he made it clear at p. 792 of the Report, the specific purpose for which he was referring to those speeches.
Precedent	This is what he stated: We have referred to the speeches of Pandit Jawaharlal <NAME> and Dr. <NAME> not with a view to interpret the provisions of Article 368 which we propose to do on its own terms, but only to notice the transcendental character given to the fundamental rights by two of the important architects of the Constitution.
Precedent	724.
Precedent	<NAME> in the course of his judgment also referred to some of the speeches made during the debates on Article 368.
Precedent	But before doing so this is what he observed at p. 922 of the report:  Before concluding this judgment I must refer to some of the speeches made by the members of <ORG> in the course of debates o n the draft Constitution.
Precedent	These speeches cannot be used as aids for interpreting the Constitution-see State of Travancore Cochin and <NAME>.
Precedent	v. <ORG> Accordingly I do not rely on them as aids to construction.
Precedent	But I propose to refer to them, as <NAME> relied heavily on the speeches of Dr. <NAME>.
Precedent	According to him, the speeches of Dr. <NAME> show that he did not regard the fundamental rights as amendable.
Precedent	This contention is not supported by the speeches.
Precedent	725.
Precedent	From these observations, it is clear that the learned judges were not referring to the speeches as aids for interpreting any of the provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	726.
Precedent	Now, let us turn to this Court's Judgment in the Privy Purse case.
Precedent	<NAME> (as he then was) in the course of his judgment (at p. 83 of the report) quoted a portion of the speech of the <ORG> Minister <NAME> not for the purpose of interpreting any provision of the Constitution but for showing the circumstances which necessitated the giving of certain guarant ees to the former ruler.
RatioOfTheDecision	That speech succinctly sets out why certain guarantees had to be given to the rulers.
Precedent	Hence it is not correct to say that <NAME> speaking for himself and six other Judges had used the speech of <NAME> in aid of the construction of any of the articles of the Constitution It is true <NAME> in his dissenting judgment (at p. 121 of the report) used the speech of <NAME> in aid of the construction of Article 363 but the learned judge no where in his judgment discussed the question whether the speeches made by the members of <ORG> were admisible in aid of interpreting any provision of the Constitution.
Precedent	727.
Precedent	Before concluding the discussion on this topic, it is necessary to refer to one more decision of this <ORG> i.e.
Precedent	<CITATION_1235907> .
Precedent	2 S.C.R.
Precedent	33 In that case this <ORG> was called upon to decide whether the provision in the Wealth Tax Act , 1957 providing for the levy of tax on the capital value of agricultural property were Constitutionall y sustainable.
Precedent	By a majority of four against three, this <ORG> upheld the levy.
Precedent	<NAME> who spoke for himself and two other judges after sustaining the validity of the provision on an examination of the relevant provisions of the Constitution as well as the decided cases referred to some of the speeches made during the debates in <ORG> in support of the conclusion already reached by him.
Precedent	Before referring to those speeches this is what the learned judge observed at p. 58: We are, however, glad to find from the following extracts from the debates that our interpretation accords with what was intended.
Precedent	728.
RatioOfTheDecision	From this it is clear that the learned Judge did not seek any aid from the speeches for the purpose of interpreting the relevant provision.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is necessary to note that the learned judge did not dissent from the view earlier taken by the <ORG> in <ORG> case (supra).
Precedent	Hence the law as laid down in <ORG> case is binding on us and its correctness was not challenged before  us.
Precedent	729.
Precedent	The learned Advocate General of Maharashtra is right in his contention that for finding out the true scope of Article 31(2 ), as it stands at present, it is necessary for us to find out the mischief that was intended to be remedied by the present amendment.
Precedent	In other words, we must find out what was the objective intended to be achieved by that amendment.
Precedent	The original Article 31(2) first came up for consideration by this <ORG> in <CITATION_1890860> .,  S.C.R.
Precedent	558 wherein Patanjali <NAME> speaking for the <ORG> observed:  While it is true that the legislature is given the discretionary power of laying down the principle which should govern the determination of the amount to be given to the owner for the property appropriated, such principles must ensure that what is determined as payable Must be compensation, that is, a just equivalent of what the owner has been deprived of.
Precedent	Within the limits of this basic requirement of full indemnification of the expropriated owner, the Constitution allows fre e play to the legislative judgment as to what principles should guide the determination of the amount payable.
Precedent	Whether such principles take into account all the elements which make up the true value of the property appropriated and exclude matters which are to be neglected is a justiciable issue to be adjudicated by the <ORG>.
