RatioOfTheDecision	This, indeed, was not disputed.
RatioOfTheDecision	730.
RatioOfTheDecision	We are told that Article 31(2) came to be amended by means of the 4th Amendment Act in view of the decision of this <ORG> in Mrs. <NAME> case.
RatioOfTheDecision	The scope of the article as amended by the 4th Amendment Act was considered by this <ORG> in <CITATION_1634289> .
Precedent	1 S.C.R.
Precedent	614.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> (as he then was) speaking for a bench consisting of himself, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME> observed (at p. 626): The fact that <ORG> used the same expressions namely "compensation" and "Principles" as were found in Article 31 before the Amendment is a clear indication that it accepted the meaning given by this <ORG> to those e xpressions in Mrs. <NAME>'s case.
RatioOfTheDecision	It follows that a <ORG> in making a law of acquisition or requisition shall provide for a just equivalent of what the owner has been deprived of or specify the principles for the purpose of ascertaining the "just equivalent" of what the owner has been deprived of.
RatioOfTheDecision	If <ORG> intended to enable a <ORG> to make such a law without providing for compensation so defined, it would have used other expressions like "price", "consideration" etc.
Precedent	731.
Precedent	Proceeding further the learned judge observed:  The real difficulty is, what is the effect of ouster of jurisdiction of the court to question the law on the ground that the "compensation" provided by the law is not adequate ?
RatioOfTheDecision	It will be noticed that the law of acquisition or requisition is not wholly immune from scrutiny by the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	But what is excluded from the court's jurisdiction is that the said law cannot be questioned on the ground that the compensation provided by that law is not adequate.
RatioOfTheDecision	It will further b e noticed that the clause excluding the jurisdiction of the <ORG> also used the word "compensation" indicating thereby that what is excluded from the court's jurisdiction is the adequacy of the compensation fixed by the legislature.
RatioOfTheDecision	The argument that the word "compensation" means a just equivalent for the property acquired and, therefore, the court can ascertain whether it is a "just equivalent" or not makes the amendment of the Constitution nugatory.
RatioOfTheDecision	It will be arguing in a circle.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, a more reasonable interpretation is that neither the principles prescribing the "just equivalent" nor the "just equivalent" can be questioned by the court on the ground of the inadequacy of the compensation fixed or arrived at by the working of the principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	To illustrate; a law is made to acquire a house, its value at the time of acquisition has to be fixed; there are many modes of valuation namely estimate by the engineer, value reflected by comparable sales, capitalisation of rent and similar others.
RatioOfTheDecision	The applicatio n of different principles may lead to different results.
RatioOfTheDecision	The adoption of one principle may give a higher value and the adoption of another principle may give a lesser value.
RatioOfTheDecision	But nonetheless they are principles on which and the manner in which compensation is determined.
RatioOfTheDecision	The court cannot obviously say that the law should have adopted one principle and not the other, for it relates only to the question of adequacy.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the other hand, if a law lays down principles which are not relevant to the property acquired or to the value of the property at or about the time it is acquired, it may be said that they are not principles contemplated by Article 31(2) of the Constitution....
RatioOfTheDecision	In such cases the validity of the principles can be scrutinized.
RatioOfTheDecision	The law may also prescribe a compensation which is illusory it may provide for the acquisition of a property worth lakhs of rupees for a paltry sum of Rs.
RatioOfTheDecision	100.
RatioOfTheDecision	The question in that context does not relate to the adequacy of the compensation for it is no compensation at all.
RatioOfTheDecision	The  illustrations given by us are not exhaustive.
RatioOfTheDecision	There may be many others falling on either side of the line.
RatioOfTheDecision	But this much is clear.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the compensation is illusory or if the principles prescribed are irrelevant to the value of the property at or about the time of its acquisition, it can be said that the legislature committed a fraud on power, and therefore, the law is bad.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is a use of the protection of Article 31 in a manner which the Article hardly intended.
Precedent	(emphasis supplied)  733.
Precedent	The principles that emerge from the decision in <NAME>'s case are: (1) compensation means just equivalent of the value of the property acquired; (2) principles prescribed must be principles which provide for compensation; (3) adequacy of compensation fixed or to be determined on the basis of the principles set out cannot be gone into by the court; (4) the principles fixed must be relevant to the property acquired or to the value of the property at about the time it is acquired; (5) the compensation fixed should not be illu sory and (6) courts have power to strike down a law on the ground of fraud on power if the principles fixed are irrelevant or if the compensation granted is illusory.
Precedent	734.
Precedent	The next decision cited to us is the decision of this <ORG> in <CITATION_602096> .
Precedent	(1967) 1, S.C.R.
Precedent	p. 255.
Precedent	It is a decision of <ORG> consisting of <NAME> and <NAME> As that decision was overruled by this <ORG> in <CITATION_673450> .
Precedent	(1969) 3, S.C.R.
Precedent	341 it is not necessary to refer to its ratio.
Precedent	735.
Precedent	This takes us to the decision of this <ORG> in Shantilal's case.
Precedent	This case related to the acquisition of some landed property on behalf of the Borough Municipality of Ahmedabad for making town planning scheme under the Bombay Town Planning Act, 1955.
RatioOfTheDecision	Sections 53 and 57 of that Act fixed certain principles for the determination of compensation for the land acquired.
RatioOfTheDecision	The High <ORG> of Gujarat declared that those provisions were ultra vires in so far as they authorised the local authority to acquire land under a Town Plann ing Scheme and as a corollary to that view declared invalid the City Wall Improvement Town Planning Scheme No.
Precedent	5 framed in exercise of the powers conferred under the Act.
Precedent	In doing so they purported to follow the decision of this <ORG> in <NAME> case.
Precedent	<ORG> of this <ORG> reversed the decision of <ORG>.
Precedent	In that case <NAME> speaking for the <ORG> elaborately reviewed the earlier decisions of this <ORG> bearing on Article 31(2).
Precedent	After doing so, he observed at p. 365 of the report:  Reverting to the amendment made in Clause (2) of Article 31 by the Constitution (Fourth Amendment) Act, 1955, it is clear that adequacy of compensation fixed by the <ORG> or awarded according to the principles specified by the <ORG> for determination is not justiciable.
RatioOfTheDecision	It clearly follows from the terms of Article 31(2) as amended that the amount of compensation payable if fixed by the <ORG>, is not justiciable, because the challenge in such a case, apart from a plea of a buse of legislative power, would be only a challenge to the adequacy of compensation.
RatioOfTheDecision	If compensation fixed by the <ORG>-and by the use of the expression "compensation" we mean what the legislature justly regards as proper and fair recompense for compulsory expropriation of property and not something which by abuse of legislative power though called compensation is not a recompense at all or is something illusory-is not justiciable, on the plea that it is not a just equivalent of the property compulsorily acquired is it open to the courts to enter upon an enquiry whether the principles which are specified by the <ORG> for determining compensation do not award to the expropriated owner a just equivalent ?
RatioOfTheDecision	In our view, such an enquiry is not open to the <ORG> under the statutes enacted after the amendments made in the Constitution by the Constitution (Fourth Amendment) Act.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the quantum of compensation fixed by the <ORG> is not liable to be canvassed before the <ORG> on the ground that it is  not a just equivalent, the principles specified for determination of compensation will also not be open to challenge on the plea that the compensation determined by the application of those principles is not a just equivalent.
RatioOfTheDecision	The right declared by the Constitution guarantees that compensation shall be given before a person is compulsorily expropriated of his property for a public purpose.
RatioOfTheDecision	What is fixed as compensation by statute, or by the application of principles specified for determination of compensation is guaranteed; it does not mean however that something fixed or determined by the application of specified principles which is illusory or can in no sense be regarded as compensation must be upheld by the <ORG>, for, to do so, would be to grant a charter of arbitrariness and permit a device to defeat the Constitutional guarantee.
RatioOfTheDecision	But compensation fixed or determined on principles specified by the <ORG> cannot be permitted to be challenged on the somewhat indefinite plea that it is not a just or f air equivalent.
RatioOfTheDecision	Principles may be challenged on the ground that they are irrelevant to the determination of compensation, but not on the plea that what is awarded as a result of the application of those principles is not just or fair compensation.
RatioOfTheDecision	A challenge to a statute that the principles specified by it do not award a just equivalent will be in clear violation of the Constitutional declaration that inadequacy of compensation provided is not justicable.
RatioOfTheDecision	(emphasis supplied)  736.
Precedent	The Advocate General of Maharashtra contended that if only this decision had not been indirectly overruled by the Bank Nationalisation case R.C.
Precedent	Cooper v. Union of India [1970] 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	530 there would have been no occasion to further amend Article 31(2).
Precedent	That being so, it is necessary to find out clearly as to what are the principles enunciated in this decision.
Precedent	This decision firmly laid down that any arbitrary fixation of recompense is liable to be struck down by the court as an abuse of legislative power.
Precedent	It further laid down t hat the principles laid down may be challenged on the ground that they are not relevant for the purpose of determining the recompense payable to the owner of the property acquired.
Precedent	If the recompense fixed or determined is either not arbitary or illusory or if the principles fixed are relevant to the purpose of acquisition or requisition of the property in question, the courts cannot go into the question of adequacy of the payment.
Precedent	737.
Precedent	Then came the Bank Nationalisation case.
Precedent	The majority judgment in that case was delivered by <NAME> (as he then was).
Precedent	In that judgment he referred somewhat extensively to the decision in <NAME> case and other cases rendered by this <ORG>.
Precedent	He did not purport to deviate from the rule laid down in <NAME>'s case.
Precedent	The ratio of that decision relating to Article 31(2) is found at p. 598 of the report.
Precedent	The learned judge observed:  Both the lines of thought (in <NAME>'s case and <NAME>'s case) which converge in the ultimate result, support the view that the pri nciple specified by the law for determination of compensation is beyond the pale of challenge, if it is relevant to the determination of compensation and is a recognised principle applicable in the determination of compensation for property compulsorily acquired and the principle is appropriate in determining the value of the class of property sought to be acquired.
Precedent	On the application of the view expressed in <NAME> case or in <NAME> case, the Act in our judgment is liable to be struck down as it fails to provide <ORG> the expropriated banks compensation determined according to relevant principles.
Precedent	738.
Precedent	Proceeding further the learned judge observed at p. 599:  We are unable to hold that a principle specified by the <ORG> for determining compensation of the property to be acquired is conclusive.
Precedent	If that view be expressed, the <ORG> will be invested with a charter of arbitrariness and by abuse of legislative process, the <ORG>nstitutional guarantee of the right to compensati on may be severely impaired.
Precedent	The principle specified must be appropriate to the determination of compensation for the particular class of property sought to be acquired.
Precedent	If several principles are appropriate and one is selected for determination of the value of the property to be acquired, selection of that principle to the exclusion of other principles is not open to the challenge for the selection must be left to the wisdom of the <ORG>.
Precedent	739.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is clear from the passages we have quoted above that this case also emphasised that the power of the <ORG> to fix the compensation for the property acquired is not an arbitrary power.
RatioOfTheDecision	Further, the principles prescribed for determining the compensation must be relevant to the subject matter of acquisition or requisition.
RatioOfTheDecision	That decision also laid down that both the questions whether the compensation has been fixed arbitrarily or whether the principles laid down are irrelevant are open to judicial review.
RatioOfTheDecision	740.
RatioOfTheDecision	Let us now examine Article 31(2) as it stands no w in the light of the decisions already referred to.
RatioOfTheDecision	The only material changes made in that Article under the 25th Amendment Act are:  (1) in place of the word 'compensation', the word 'amount' has been used and (2) an additional clause viz.
RatioOfTheDecision	"or that the whole or any part of such amount is to be given otherwise than in cash" has been added.
Precedent	741.
RatioOfTheDecision	We are not concerned in this case as to the effect of the additional clause.
RatioOfTheDecision	No arguments were advanced on that aspect.
RatioOfTheDecision	All that we are concerned with is as to what is the effect of the substitution of the word "amount" in place of the word "compensation".
RatioOfTheDecision	As seen earlier, the word "compensation" has been interpreted in the various decisions referred to earlier as "just equivalent" of the value of the property taken.
RatioOfTheDecision	That concept has now been removed.
RatioOfTheDecision	In other respects, the Article has not been altered.
RatioOfTheDecision	It remains what it was.
RatioOfTheDecision	We have earlier noticed that the decisions of this <ORG> have firmly laid down that while examining the validity of law made under Article 31( 2) as it stood after it was amended under the 4th Amendment Act , it was open to the <ORG> to go into the questions whether the compensation had been fixed arbitrarily and whether the same was illusory.
RatioOfTheDecision	Those decisions further ruled that the <ORG> can go into the relevant of the principles fixed.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> would have undoubtedly known the ratio of those decisions.
RatioOfTheDecision	That is also the legal presumption.
RatioOfTheDecision	Hence if the <ORG> intended to take away the judicial review in any respect other than relating to the adequacy of the amount fixed, it would have expressed its intention by appropriate words.
RatioOfTheDecision	We find no such words in the Article as it stands.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that it has accepted the interpretation placed by this <ORG> in all respects except as regards the concept of compensation.
RatioOfTheDecision	That this is the mischief which the 25th Amendment seeks to remedy by amending Article 31(2) is also clear from the language of the amended Article itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	It says that the law shall not be called in question  on the ground that the amount fixed or determined is not adequate.
RatioOfTheDecision	What is an adequate amount ?
RatioOfTheDecision	An amount can be said to be adequate only when the owner of the property is fully compensated, that is when he is paid an amount which is equivalent in value to the property acquired or requisitioned.
RatioOfTheDecision	And that is also what is connoted by the concept of 'compensation' as interpreted by this <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, stated briefly, what the 25th Amendment makes non-justiciable is an enquiry into the question whether the amount fixed or determined is an equivalent value of or 'compensation' for the property acquired or requisitioned.
RatioOfTheDecision	742.
RatioOfTheDecision	The word "amount" is a neutral word.
RatioOfTheDecision	Standing by itself, it has no norm and is completely colourless.
RatioOfTheDecision	The dictionary meaning of the word appropriate to the present context is "sum total or a figure".
RatioOfTheDecision	We have to find out its connotation from the context.
RatioOfTheDecision	In so doing, we have to bear in mind the fact that Article 31(2) still continues to be a fundamental right.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is not possible to accept t he contention of the learned Advocate General of Maharashtra and the learned Solicitor General that the right of the owner at present is just to get whatever the Government pleases to give, whenever it pleases to give and however it pleases to give.
Precedent	A position so nebulous as that cannot be considered as a right much less a fundamental right, which Article 31(2) still claims to be.
Precedent	743.
Precedent	It is difficult to believe that <ORG> intended to make a mockery of the fundamental right conferred under Article 31(2).
Precedent	It cannot be that the Constitution while purporting to preserve the fundamental right of the citizens to get an "amount" in lieu of the property taken for public purpose has in fact robbed him of all his right.
Precedent	744.
RatioOfTheDecision	Undoubtedly Article 31 empowers the legislature to acquire or requisition the property of a citizen for an "amount".
RatioOfTheDecision	What does the word "amount" mean in that Article ?
RatioOfTheDecision	As we have already said, that word by itself does not disclose any norm.
Precedent	But then the word "amount" is followed by the word s "which may be fixed by such law or which may be determined in accordance with such principles and given in such manner as may be specified in such law and no such law shall be called in question in any court on the ground that the amount so fixed or determined is not adequate.
Precedent	745.
Precedent	If the expression "amount" has no norm and is just what the <ORG> stipulates, there can be no question of prescribing principles for determining that "amount"; nor is there any scope for finding out its adequacy.
Precedent	The legislatures are permitted under the amended Article 31(2) either to fix the "amount" to be paid in lieu of the property acquired or to lay down the principles for determining that "amount".
Precedent	These two alternative methods must bring about nearly the same result.
Precedent	If the relevancy of the principles fixed can be judicially reviewed-as indeed they must be-in view of the dicisions referred to earlier, we fail to see how the fixation of the "amount" which is the alternative method of determining the recompense to be p aid in lieu of the property taken is excluded from judicial review.
Precedent	746.
Precedent	The word "fixed" in Article 31(2) connotes or postulates that there must be some standard or principle by the application of which the legislature calculates or ascertains definitely the amount.
Precedent	In <ORG> (1946) at p. 421, the word 'fix' is defined thus: "To determine; to settle.
Precedent	A Constitutional provision to the effect that <ORG> shall fix the compensation of officers means that it shall prescribe or 'fix' the rule by which such compensation is to be determined".
Precedent	(See also <ORG> v. <NAME> (1945) 70, C.L.R.
Precedent	100 at 128 cited in Saunders, Words and Phrases: Legally Defined Vol.
Precedent	2, p. 258 (1969).
RatioOfTheDecision	This being the meaning of the word 'fix' it would be necessary for the legislature to lay down in the law itself or otherwise indicate the principles on the basis of which it fixes the amount for the acquisition or requisitioning of the property.
RatioOfTheDecision	If this construction is placed on the first mode of  determining the amount, then there would be no difference between this method, and the other method whereby the legislature lays down the principles and leaves it for any other authority to determine the amount in accordance with such principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	Whether the legislature adopts one or the other method, the requirement of Article 31(2) would be the same, namely, there must be principles on the basis of which the amount is determined.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such an amount may be determined either by the legislature or by some other authority authorised by the legislature.
RatioOfTheDecision	The content of the right in Article 31(2) is not dependent upon whether the legislature chooses one or the other method of determining the amount.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no contradiction between these two methods.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is true that in both cases, the judicial review is necessarily limited because it cannot extend to the examination of the adequacy of the amount fixed or to be determined.
Precedent	It was conceded on behalf of the contesting respondents that the court can go into the question  whether the "amount" fixed is illusory.
Precedent	This very concession shows the untenability of the contention advanced on behalf of the <ORG>.
Precedent	For determining whether the "amount" fixed is illusory or not, one has first to determine the value of the property because without knowing the true value of the property, no court can say that the "amount" fixed is illusory.
Precedent	Further, when Article 31(2) says that it is not open to the court to examine whether the "amount" fixed or determined is adequate or not, it necessarily means that the "amount" payable has to be determined on the basis or principles relevant for determining the value of the property acquired or requisitioned.
Precedent	There can be no question of adequacy unless the "amount" payable has been determined on the basis of certain norms and not arbitrarily, without having regard to the value of the property.
Precedent	748.
Precedent	Further, Article 31(2) provides for fixing or determining the amount for the acquisition or requisitioning of the property.
Precedent	The <ORG> action is still describe d as 'acquisition or requisition' and not 'confiscation'.
Precedent	Therefore, the principles for fixing or determining the amount must be relevant to the 'acquisition or requisition', and not to 'confiscation'.
Precedent	The amount fixed or determined should not make it appear that the measure is one of confiscation.
Precedent	The principles for fixing or determining the amount may be said to be relevant to the acquisition or requisition when they bear reasonable relationship to the value of the property acquired or requisitioned.
Precedent	749.
RatioOfTheDecision	Further there is practical difficulty in accepting the contention that the word "amount" in the context in which it is used, has no norm.
RatioOfTheDecision	The amount has to be fixed by the legislatures which means by the members of the legislatures.
RatioOfTheDecision	When a law for acquisition of certain types of property is enacted, it is not as if the members of the legislature-each and every one of them who participates in the making of the law would first go and inspect the property to be acquired and then assess the value of that prop erty.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the very nature of things, the "amount" payable has to be determined on the basis of certain principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	If that be so, as it appears to us to be obvious, then the legislators must have some principles before them to determine the amount.
RatioOfTheDecision	In this connection the Advocate-General of Maharashtra tried to give an explanation, which appears to us to be unsatisfactory and unacceptable.
RatioOfTheDecision	His contention was that our democracy is worked on the basis of party system.
RatioOfTheDecision	The ruling party has the majority of the members of the legislature behind it.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, the members of the opposition party need not know the basis of fixation of the value of the property acquired.
RatioOfTheDecision	Even the members of the ruling party need not be told about the basis on which the value is fixed.
RatioOfTheDecision	The option before them is either to accept the amount fixed by the cabinet or by the Minister concerned or to reject the proposal and face the consequences.
RatioOfTheDecision	If this is the true position, it is, in our opinion, a negation of parliamentary democracy.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our d emocracy like all true parliamentary democracies is based on the principles of debate and discussion.
RatioOfTheDecision	As far as possible, decisions in the legislatures are arrived at on the basis of consensus.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution does not provide for one party rule where there is no room for opposition.
RatioOfTheDecision	Opposition parties have an important role to play under our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Members belonging to the opposition parties have as much right to participate in making laws as the members belonging to the ruling party.
RatioOfTheDecision	Further the learned Advocate General is not correct in his assumption that the function of the members belonging to the ruling party is to blindly support a measure sponsored by the executive.
RatioOfTheDecision	They also have a right, nay, a duty to mould every measure by debate and discussion.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the question of fixation of "amount" under Article 31(2) is considered as the exclusive function of the executive, then, not only the judicial review will be taken away, even the legislature will not have the opportunity of examining the correc tness or appropriateness of the "amount" fixed.
RatioOfTheDecision	A power so arbitrary as that can speedily degenerate into an instrument of oppression and is likely to be used for collateral purposes.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution has created checks and balances to minimise the possibility of power being misused.
RatioOfTheDecision	We have no doubt that the theory propounded by the Advocate General of Maharashtra will be repudiated by our legislatures and the cabinets as something wholly foreign to our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	750.
RatioOfTheDecision	If we bear in mind the fact that the "amount" in question is to be paid in lieu of the property taken, then, it follows that it must have a reasonable relationship with the value of the property taken.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may not be the market value of the property taken.
RatioOfTheDecision	The market value of a property is the result of an inter-action of various forces.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may not have any reasonable relationship with the investment made by its successive owners.
RatioOfTheDecision	The price of the property acquired might have shot up because of various contributions made by the society such  as improvements effected by the <ORG> in the locality in question or the conversion of a rural area into an urban area.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is undoubtedly open to the <ORG> to appropriate to itself that part of the market value of a property which is not the result of any contribution made by its owners.
RatioOfTheDecision	There may be several other relevant grounds for fixing a particular "amount" in a given case or for adopting one or more of the relevant principles for the determination of the price to be paid.
RatioOfTheDecision	In all these matters the legislative judgment is entitled to great weight.
RatioOfTheDecision	It will be for the aggrieved party to clearly satisfy the <ORG> that the basis adopted by the legislature has no reasonable relationship to the value of the property acquired or that the "amount" to be paid has been arbitrarily fixed or that the same is an illusory return for the property taken.
RatioOfTheDecision	So long as the basis adopted for computing the value of the property is relevant to the acquisition in question or the amount fixed can be justified on any such basis,  it is no more open to the court to consider whether the amount fixed or to be determined is adequate.
RatioOfTheDecision	But it is still open to the court to consider whether "amount" in question has been aribtrarily determined or whether the same is an illusory return for the property taken.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is also open to the court to consider whether the principles laid down for the determination of the amount are irrelevant for the acquisition or requisition in question.
RatioOfTheDecision	To put it differently, the judicial review under the amended Article 31(2) lies within narrow limits.
RatioOfTheDecision	The court cannot go into the question whether what is paid or is payable is compensation.
Precedent	It can only go into the question whether the "amount" in question was aribtrarily fixed as illusory or whether the principles laid down for the purpose of determining the "amount" payable have reasonable relationship with the value of the property acquired or requisitioned.
Precedent	751.
Precedent	If the amended Article 31(2) is understood in the manner as laid down above, the right to property can not be said to have been damaged or destroyed.
Precedent	The amended Article 31(2) according to us fully protects the interests of the individual as well as that of the society.
Precedent	Hence its validity is not open to challenge.
Precedent	752.
Precedent	Now, let us rurn to Article 31(2B).
Precedent	It says that "Nothing in Sub-clause (f) of Clause (1) of Article 19 shall affect any such law as is referred to in Clause (2)".
Precedent	This provision has no real impact on the right conferred under Article 31(2).
Precedent	Article 31(2) empowers the <ORG> to compulsorily acquire or requisition property for public purpose.
Precedent	When property is acquired or requisitioned for public purpose, the right of the owner of that property to hold or dispose of that property is necessarily lost.
Precedent	Hence there is no anti-thesis between Article 19(1)(f) and Article 31(2).
Precedent	That being so, the only assistance that the owner of the property acquired or requisitioned would have obtained from Article 19(1)(f) read with Sub- article (5) of that Article would be the right to insist that the law made und er Article 31(2) as it stood before its recent amendment, should have to conform to some reasonable procedure both in the matter of dispossessing him as well as in the matter of determining the "amount" payable to him.
Precedent	In a way, those rights are protected by the principles of natural justice.
Precedent	753.
Precedent	For the reasons mentioned above, we are unable to accept the contention urged on behalf of the petitioners that Section 2 of the 25th Amendment Act , 1971 is invalid.
Precedent	754.
RatioOfTheDecision	This takes us to Section 3 of the 25th Amendment Act which now stands as Article 31C of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	This Article empowers the <ORG> as well as the Local <ORG> to enact laws giving effect to, the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 , completely ignoring in the process, Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	Further it lays down that if the law in question contains a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy, that Jaw shall not be called in question in any court o n the ground that it does not give effect to such policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	The proviso to that Article prescribes that where such law is made by the legislature of a <ORG>, the provisions of Article 31C shall not apply thereto unless such law, having been reserved for the consideration of the President has received his assent.
RatioOfTheDecision	This Article has two parts.
RatioOfTheDecision	The first part says that laws enacted by <ORG> as well as by the Local <ORG> for giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 shall not be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Articles 14, 19 and 31 notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 and the second part provides that no law containing a declaration that is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	Clauses (b) and (c) of Article 39 do not prescribe any s ubject matter of legislation.
RatioOfTheDecision	They contain certain objectives to be achieved.
RatioOfTheDecision	The methods to be adopted to achieve those objectives may be numerous.
RatioOfTheDecision	Those clauses cover a very large field of social and economic activities of the <ORG> and the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Clause (b) of Article 39 says that the <ORG> shall direct its policy towards securing that the ownership and control of the material resources of the community are so distributed as best to subserve the common good and Clause (c) of that Article says that the <ORG> shall direct its policy towards securing that the operation of the economic system does not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to the common detriment.
RatioOfTheDecision	These two provisions lay down a particular political philosophy.
Precedent	They in conjunction with some other provisions of the Constitution direct the <ORG> to build a Welfare <ORG>.
Precedent	755.
RatioOfTheDecision	No one can deny the importance of the Directive Principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Fundamental Rights and the Directive Principles constitute the 'conscience' of our  Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The purpose of the Fundamental Rights is to create an egalitarian society, to free all citizens from coercion or restriction by society and to make liberty available for all.
RatioOfTheDecision	The purpose of the Directive Principles is to fix certain social and economic goals for immediate attainment by bringing about a non-violent social revolution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Through such a social revolution the Constitution seeks to fulfil the basic needs of the common man and to change the structure of bur society.
RatioOfTheDecision	It aims at making the Indian masses free in the positive sense.
Precedent	756.
RatioOfTheDecision	Part IV of the Constitution is designed to bring about the social and economic revolution that remained to be fulfilled after independence.
RatioOfTheDecision	The aim of the Constitution is not to guarantee certain liberties to only a few of the citizens but for all.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution visualizes our society as a whole and contemplates that every member of the society should participate in the freedoms guaranteed.
RatioOfTheDecision	To ignore Part IV is to ignore the substance provided for in the  Constitution, the hopes held out to the <ORG> and the very ideals on which our Constitution is built Without faithfully implementing the Directive Principles, it is not possible to achieve the Welfare <ORG> contemplated by the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	A society like ours steeped in poverty and ignorance satisfying the minimum economic needs of every citizen of this country.
RatioOfTheDecision	Any Government which fails to fulfil the pledge taken under the Constitution cannot be said to have been faithful to the Constitution and to its commitments.
Precedent	757.
Precedent	Equally, the danger to democracy by an over emphasis on duty cannot be minimised.
Precedent	<NAME>, a German Scholar, from his experience of the tragedy of the Nazi Germany warned:  If...these duties of man should be duties towards the "public welfare" of the "society" and the <ORG>, and rights are made conditional on the fulfilment of these duties, the duties will uproot the rights.
Precedent	The rights will wither away...(the) <ORG> can use the allegedly unfulfilled duties to shove aside rights.-Any  Bill of Rights that makes the rights conditional on duties towards society or the <ORG>, however strong its emphasis on human dignity, freedom, God or whatever else, can be accepted by any totalitarian leader.
Precedent	He will enforce the duties while disregarding the right.
Precedent	758.
Precedent	Indeed the balancing process between the individual rights and the social needs is a delicate one.
Precedent	This is primarily the responsibility of the "<ORG>" and in the ultimate analysis of the courts as interpreters of the Constitution and the laws.
Precedent	759.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our founding fathers were satisfied that there is no anti-thesis between the Fundamental Rights and the Directive Principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	One supplements the other.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Directives lay down the end to be achieved and Part III prescribes the means through which the goal is to be reached.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution does not subscribe to the theory that end justifies the means adopted.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Counsel for the petitioners urged that the Fundamental Rights are not the cause of our failure to implement the Directive Principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to him, it is not the Constituion that has failed as; but we have failed to rise up to its expectations.
RatioOfTheDecision	He urged that the attack against Fundamental Rights is merely an alibi and an attempt to find a scape-goat on the part of those who were unable or willing to implement the Directives.
RatioOfTheDecision	These allegations are 'denied on behalf of the Union and the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	It was urged on their behalf that interpretations placed by the courts on some of the Articles in Part III of the Constitution have placed impediments in the way of States, in implementing the Directives.
RatioOfTheDecision	These controversies are not capable of being decided by courts.
RatioOfTheDecision	760.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no doubt that the power conferred under Article 31C , if interpreted in the manner contended on behalf of the Union and the States would result in denuding substantially the contents of the right to equality, the right to the seven freedoms guaranteed under Article 19 and the right to get some reasonable return by the person whose property is taken for public purpose.
RatioOfTheDecision	Unlike Article 31A , Article 31C is not confined to some particular subjects.
RatioOfTheDecision	It can take in a very wide area of human activities.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power conferred under it, is an arbitrary power.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is capable of being used for collateral purposes.
RatioOfTheDecision	It can be used to stifle the freedom of speech, freedom to assemble peaceably, freedom to move freely throughout India, freedom to reside and settle in any part of India, freedom to acquire, hold and dispose of property and freedom to practise any profession or carry on any occupation, trade or business.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power conferred under that provision is a blanket power.
RatioOfTheDecision	Even a small majority in a legislature can use that power to truncate or even destroy democracy.
RatioOfTheDecision	That power can be used to weaken the unity and integrity of this country.
RatioOfTheDecision	That Article is wholly out of tune with our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Its implications are manifold.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is force in the contention of the petitioners that this Article has the potentiality of shaking the very foundation of our Constitution.
Precedent	761.
Fact	What is the  nature of the power conferred under Article 31C ?
RatioOfTheDecision	It is claimed to have empowered <ORG> and the State <ORG> to enact laws pro tanto abrogating Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	A power to take away directly or indirectly a right guaranteed or a duty imposed under a Constitution, by an ordinary law, is a power to pro tanto abrogate the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the legislature is empowered to amend the Constitution by ordinary legislative procedure, any law enacted by it, even if it does not purport to amend the Constitution, but all the same, is inconsistent with one or more of the provisions of the Constitution has the effect of abrogating the Constitution to the extent of inconsistency.
RatioOfTheDecision	That position is clear from the judgment of <ORG> in <NAME> v. The King  A.C. 691.
RatioOfTheDecision	In other words, the power conferred under the Article is a power to amend the Constitution in certain essential respects while enacting legislations coming within the purview of that Article.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is a power not merely to abridge bu t even to take away the rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 19 and 31 by ordinary law.
Precedent	Further that power is conferred not only on the <ORG> but also on the State <ORG>.
Precedent	762.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 specifically provides that amendment of the Constitution can be done only in the manner provided therein.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is true that there are provisions in the Constitution under which the <ORG> can amend some parts of the Constitution by ordinary law- see Article 2 to 4, Article 169 , Paragraph 7 of Schedule V and Paragraph 21 of Schedule VI.
RatioOfTheDecision	But these provisions clearly provide that the laws enacted under those provisions "are not to be deemed as amendments to the Constitution for the purpose of Article 368".
RatioOfTheDecision	There are also some transitional provisions in the Constitution which can be changed by the <ORG> by law.
RatioOfTheDecision	Leaving aside for separate consideration Article 31-A , which was first introduced by the 1st Amendment Act , 1951, there is no provision in the Constitution apart from Article 31(4) which permitted t he State Legislatures to enact laws contravening one or more of the provisions in Part III.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31(4) relates to legislations pending before the State Legislatures at the time the Constitution came into force.
RatioOfTheDecision	Their scope was known to the Constitution-makers.
RatioOfTheDecision	That provision was enacted to protect certain Zamindari Abolition laws which were on the anvil.
RatioOfTheDecision	But it must be remembered that the original provisions in the Constitution were not controlled by Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	That Article is as much a creature of the Constitution as the other Articles are.
RatioOfTheDecision	The form and manner prescribed in Article 368 did not govern the procedure of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	The mandates contained in Article 368 are applicable only to the amendments made to the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power to amend the Constitution was exclusively given to the <ORG> and to no other body.
RatioOfTheDecision	The manner of exercising that power is clearly prescribed.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31C gives a very large power to the State Legislatures as well as to <ORG> to pro tanto amend t he Constitution by enacting laws coming within its ambit.
Precedent	To put it differently, Article 31C permits <ORG> and the <ORG> to enact Constitution-breaking laws by a simple majority vote of the members present and voting, if the rule regarding quorum is satisfied.
Precedent	763.
Precedent	It cannot be said that Article 31C is similar to Articles 4, 169, Paragraph 7 of Schedule V and Paragraph 21 of Schedule VI.
Precedent	Each one of those Articles makes it clear that the laws passed under those Articles are not to be deemed to be an amendment of the Constitution for the purpose of Article 368.
Precedent	Those laws cannot affect the basic features of the Constitution.
Precedent	They operate within narrow fields.
Precedent	764.
Argument	The learned Advocate-General of Maharashtra contended that Article 31C lifts the ban placed on <ORG> and <ORG> under Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is true that there are several provisions in the Constitution which lift the ban placed by one or the other Article of the Constitution on the legislative power o f the State Legislatures and <ORG> e.g.
RatioOfTheDecision	Articles 15(4), 16(3), 16(4), 16(5), 19(2) to 19(6), 22(3), 22(6), 23(2), 28(2), 31(4), 31(6) etc.
RatioOfTheDecision	Each one of these Articles lifts the limitations placed on the legislative power of the legislatures by one or more of the provisions of the Constitution particularly those contained in Part III.
RatioOfTheDecision	But when the limitation is so lifted, there will be no conflict between the law enacted and Article 13.
RatioOfTheDecision	In such a situation, there is no occasion for providing that the law enacted will not be deemed to be void notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13.
RatioOfTheDecision	The laws made under the provisions set out earlier cannot in their very nature take away any of the fundamental features of the Constitution.
RulingByPresentCourt	They can merely modify one or other of those features.
RulingByPresentCourt	Article 31C proceeds on the basis that the laws enacted under that Article are in conflict with Article 13 and are prima facie void.
Precedent	Otherwise there was no purpose in providing in that Article "Notwithstanding anything contain ed in Article 13 , no law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any rights conferred by Article 14 , Article 19 or Article 31...." Hence the contention that limitations imposed by Articles 14, 19 and 31 on the legislative power of the <ORG> and the <ORG>s are lifted to the extent provided in Article 31C cannot be accepted.
Precedent	765.
Precedent	It is true that there is some similarity between the laws made under Article 31A and those made under Article 31C.
Precedent	The scope of the latter article is much wider than that of the former.
Precedent	The character of the laws made under both those Articles is somewhat similar.
Precedent	It was urged that if laws made under Article 31-A , without more, are valid even if they take away or abridge the rights conferred under Articles 14, 19 and 31, for the same reason, laws made under Article 31C must also be held valid.
Precedent	It was cont ended, now that this Court has upheld the validity of Article 31-A , we should also uphold the validity of Article 31C.
Precedent	In that connection, reliance was placed on the following observations of <ORG> <ORG> in <NAME> v. <NAME> : 66 L. Ed.
Precedent	p. 595(511)=258 U.S.13.
RatioOfTheDecision	This Amendment (19th Amendment) is in character and phraseology precisely similar to the 15th.
RatioOfTheDecision	For each the same method of adoption was pursued.
RatioOfTheDecision	One cannot be valid and the other invalid.
RatioOfTheDecision	That the 15th is valid...has been recognised and acted upon for half a century....
Precedent	The suggestion that the 15th was incorporated in the Constitution not in accordance with law, but practically as a war measure which has been validated by acquiescence cannot be entertained.
Precedent	766.
Precedent	These observations do not lay down any principle of law.
Precedent	The validity of the 19th Amendment was upheld on various grounds and not merely because the 15th amendment was upheld.
Precedent	767.
Precedent	The laws enacted under Article 31A by their very nature can hardly abrogate  the rights embodied in Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	Those laws can encroach upon the rights guaranteed under Articles 14, 19 and 31 only to the extent necessary for giving effect to them.
Precedent	The laws made must be those made under the topics of legislation mentioned in Article 31A.
Precedent	Hence the encroachment of the rights guaranteed under Article 14 , 19 and 31 must necessarily be incidental.
Precedent	If the encroachment is found to be excessive, the same can be struck down.
Precedent	In this connection reference may be usefully made to the decision of this <ORG> in <CITATION_1258563>  Supp.
Precedent	2 S.C.R.
Precedent	691.
Precedent	Therein the validity of a provision of a statute enacted under Article 19(6)(ii) i.e.
Precedent	law providing for <ORG> monopoly in Kendu Leaves, came up for consideration.
Fact	The question for decision before the <ORG> was whether that law can unreasonably encroach upon the right guaranteed under Article 19(1)(g).
Fact	That question was answered by <NAME> (as he then was) speaking for the <ORG>, thus:  "A law relating to" a <ORG> monopoly cannot,  in the context include all the provisions contained in the said law whether they have direct relation with the creation of the monopoly or not.
RatioOfTheDecision	In our opinion, the said expression should be construed to mean the law relating to the monopoly in its absolutely essential feature.
RatioOfTheDecision	If a law is passed creating a <ORG> monopoly, the <ORG> should enquire what are the provisions of the said law which are basically and essentially necessary for creating the <ORG> monopoly.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is only those essential and basic provisions which are protected by the latter part of Article 19(6).
RatioOfTheDecision	If there are other provisions made by the Act which are subsidiary, incidental or helpful to the operation of the monopoly, they do not fall under the said part and their validity must be judged under the first part of Article 19(6).
Precedent	In other words, the effect of the amendment made in Article 19(6) is to protect the law relating to the creation of monopoly and that means that it is only the provisions of the law which are integrally and essential ly connected with the creation of the monopoly that are protected.
Precedent	The rest of the provisions which may be incidental do not fall under the latter part of Article 19(6) and would inevitably have to satisfy the test of the first part of Article 19(6).
Precedent	768.
Precedent	The same principle was reiterated by the full <ORG> in the Bank Nationalisation case.
Precedent	769.
Precedent	As far back as in 1951 this <ORG> ruled in <CITATION_334293>  S.C.R.
Precedent	682 that merely because law was enacted to implement one of the Directive Principles, the same cannot with impunity encroach upon the Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	The ratio of <NAME> case would be equally applicable in respect of the laws made under Article 31A which speaks of the "law providing for the" topics mentioned therein.
RatioOfTheDecision	But that ratio cannot be effectively applied when we come to laws made under Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	The reach of Article 31C is very wide.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is possible to fit into the scheme of that Article almost any economic and social legislation.
RatioOfTheDecision	Further, the <ORG> cannot go into the questio n whether the laws encted do give effect to the policy set out in Article 39(b) and (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	We were told on behalf of the Union and the States that it is open to the courts to examine whether there is a nexus between the laws made under Article 31C and Article 39(b) and (c) and all that the courts are precluded from examining is the effectiveness of the law in achieving the intended purpose.
RatioOfTheDecision	But, such a power in its very nature is tenuous.
RatioOfTheDecision	There can be few laws which can be held to have no nexus with Article 39(b) and (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	At any rate, most laws may be given the appearance of aiming to achieve the objectives mentioned in Article 39(b) and (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	Once that facade is projected, the laws made can proceed to destroy the very foundation of our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Encroachment of valuable Constitutional guarantees generally begins imperceptibly and is made with the best of intentions but, once that attempt is successful further encroachments follow as a matter of course, not perhaps with any evil motives, and may be, out of s trong convictions regarding the righteousness of the course adopted and the objectives intended to be achieved but they may all the same be wholly unConstitutional.
Precedent	Lord <NAME> observed in <ORG> and Ors.
Precedent	v. Attorney General for Canada and Ors.
Precedent	A.C. 311 at 317.
Precedent	Both the Act and the sections have a legislative history which is relevant to the discussion.
Precedent	Their Lordships entertain no doubt that time alone will not validate an Act which when challenged is found to be ultea vires; nor will a history of a gradial series of advances till this boundary is finally crossed avail to protect the ultimate encroachment.
Precedent	770.
Precedent	The observation of Lord <NAME> "nor will a history of a gradual series of advances till this boundary is finally crossed avail to protect the ultimate encroachment" is extremely apposite for our present purpose.
Precedent	The First Amendment Act permitted enactment of Constitution breaking laws in respect of one subject; the Fourth Amendment Act enlarged that field and permitt ed the <ORG> to make laws ignoring Articles 14, 19 and 31 in respect of five subjects.
Precedent	Now the Twenty-Fifth Amendment has finally crossed the boundary.
Precedent	771.
RatioOfTheDecision	It cannot be said that under Article 31C <ORG> merely delegated its own amending power to State <ORG> and such a delegation is valid.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power conferred on <ORG> under Article 368 in its very nature is one that cannot be delegated.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is a special power to be exclusively exercised by <ORG> and that in the manner prescribed in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The State <ORG> are not institutions subordinate to <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> as well as State <ORG> in their respective allocated fields are supreme.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> cannot delegate its legislative powers-much less the amending power-to the State <ORG>.
Precedent	The question whether the legislatures can confer power on some other independent legislative body to exercise its legislative power came up for consideration before <ORG> in re The Initiative and Referendum Act   A.C. p. 935 at 945 P.C.
Precedent	<NAME> speaking for the <ORG> observed:  Section 92 of the Act of 1867 (British North American Act) entrusts the legislative power in a Province to its legislature and to that legislature only.
Precedent	No toubt a body with a power of legislation on the subjects entrusted to it so ample as that enjoyed by a Provincial <ORG> in Canada, could, while preserving its own capacity intact, seek the assistance of subordinate agencies, as had been done in <NAME> v. The <NAME> 19 App.
Precedent	Cas.
Precedent	117 the <ORG> of Ontario was held entitled to entrust to a <ORG> of Commissioners authority to enact regulations relating to <ORG>; but it does not follow that it can create and endow with its own capacity a new legislative power not created by the Act to which it owes its own existence.
Precedent	Their Lordships do no more than draw attention to the gravity of the Constitutional questions which thus arise.
Precedent	772.
Precedent	In <NAME> v. <NAME>, (1878) 5 I.A.
Precedent	178 at 194 <ORG> observed: Their L ordships agree that the Governor General in Council could not, by any form of enactment, create in India, and arm with general legislative authority, a new legislative power, not created or authorised by the Councils' Act.
Precedent	773.
RatioOfTheDecision	We respectfully agree with these observations.
RatioOfTheDecision	From these observations it follows that <ORG> was incompetent to create a new power-a power to ignore some of the provisions of the Constitution-and endow the same on the State Legislatures.
RatioOfTheDecision	That power was exclusively conferred on <ORG> so that the unity and integrity of this country may not be jeopardised by parochial considerations.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution makers were evidently of the opinion that the sovereignty of the country, the democratic character of the polity, and the individual liberties etc.
RatioOfTheDecision	would be better safeguarded if the amending power is exclusively left in the hands of the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	This exclusive conferment of amending power on the <ORG> is one of the basic features of the Constitution and the same cannot be  violated directly or indirectly.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31A made a small dent on this feature and that went unnoticed.
RatioOfTheDecision	That provision is now protected by the principle of stare decisis.
RatioOfTheDecision	Public interest will suffer if we go back on these decisions and take away the protection given to many statutes.
RatioOfTheDecision	Now, to use the words of Lord <NAME> in <ORG> case, the 'boundary line has been crossed' and a challenge to the very basic conceptions of the Constitution is posed.
RatioOfTheDecision	Hence the neglect or avoidance of the question in previous cases cannot be accepted as a sound argument.
Precedent	774.
Precedent	In <NAME> v. <NAME> and Ors.
Precedent	(1956) 94, C.L.R.
Precedent	295 Dixon C.J.
RatioOfTheDecision	observed: These cases, and perhaps other examples exist, do no doubt add to the weight of the general considerations arising from lapse of time, the neglect or avoidance of the question in previous cases and the very evident desirability of leaving undisturbed assumptions that have been accepted as to the validity of the provisions in question.
RatioOfTheDecision	At the same time , the <ORG> is not entitled to place very great reliance upon the fact that, in cases, before it where occasions might have been made to raise the question for argument and decision, this was not done by any member of the <ORG> and that on the contrary all accepted the common assumption of the parties and decided the case accordingly.
RatioOfTheDecision	Undesirable as it is that doubtful questions of validity should' go by default, the fact is that, the court usually acts upon the presumption of validity until the law is specifically challenged.
Precedent	775.
Precedent	Similar was the view expressed by <NAME> speaking for <ORG> in Attorney-General of Commonwealth of Australia v. The Queen and Ors.
Precedent	95, C.L.R.
Precedent	529 at 547 It is therefore asked and no one can doubt that it is a formidable question, why for a quarter of a century no litigant has attacked the validity of this obviously illegitimate unions.
Precedent	Why in <NAME>'s case (1918) 25, C.L.R.
Precedent	434) itself was no challenge made ?
RatioOfTheDecision	How came it that in a series of cases, w hich are enumerated in the majority and the dissentient, judgments it was assumed without question that the provisions now impugned were valid ?
RatioOfTheDecision	It is clear from the majority judgment that the learned Chief Justice and the Judges who shared his opinion were heavily pressed by this consideration.
RatioOfTheDecision	It could not be otherwise.
RatioOfTheDecision	Yet they were impelled to their conclusion by the clear conviction that consistently with the Constitution the validity of the impugned provision could not be sustained.
RatioOfTheDecision	Whether the result would have been different if their validity had previously been judicially determined after full argument directed to the precise question and had not rested on judicial dicta and common assumption it is not for their Lordships to say.
RatioOfTheDecision	Upon a question of the applicability of the doctrine of stare decisis to matters of far reaching Constitutional importance they would imperatively require the assistance of <ORG> itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	But here no such question arises.
RatioOfTheDecision	Whatever the reason may be, just as there was  a patent invalidity in the original Act which for a number of years went unchallenged, so far a greater number of years an invalidity which to their Lordships as to the majority of <ORG> has been convincingly demonstrated, has been disregarded.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such clear conviction must find expression, in the appropriate judgment.
Precedent	776.
Precedent	The contention that Article 31C may be considered as an amendment of Article 368 is not tenable.
Precedent	It does not purport to be so.
Precedent	That Article does not find a place in Part XX of the Constitution.
Precedent	It is not shown as a proviso to Article 368 , the only Article which deals with the amendment of the Constitution as such.
Precedent	Article 31C does not say that the powers conferred under that Article are available "notwithstanding anything contained in Article 368" or "notwithstanding anything in this Constituion".
Precedent	There is no basis for holding that the <ORG> intended that Article 31C should operate as an amendment of Article 368.
Precedent	We have earlier come to the conclusion that <ORG> res cannot be invested with the power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	777.
Precedent	If the purpose of Article 31C is to secure for the <ORG>, the control of means of production in certain economic spheres exclusively or otherwise, the same can be achieved by the exercise of legislative power under Article 31(2) or under Article 31(2) read with Article 19(6)(ii).
Precedent	If on the other hand, the object is to reduce the existing economic disparity in the country, that object can be achieved by exercising the various powers conferred on the legislatures under the Constitution, in particular by the exercise of the power to tax, a power of the largest amplitude.
Precedent	That power can be exercised without discriminating against any section of the people.
Precedent	One of the basic underlying principles of our Constitution is that every governmental power, which includes both the power of the executives as well as of the legislatures, must be so exercised as to give no room for legitimate complaint, that it was exercised with an evil eye or an une ven hand.
Precedent	778.
Precedent	For the reasons mentioned above, we hold that Article 31C permits the destruction of some of the basic features of our Constitution and consequently, it is void.
Precedent	779.
Precedent	Lastly, we come to the validity of the 29th Amendment Act , 1972.
Precedent	Contentions relating to the 29th Amendment Act of the Constitution lie within narrower limits.
Precedent	The only plea taken was that if any of the provisions in the two Acts included in the IXth Schedule to the Constitution by means of the 29th Amendment Act does not satisfy the requirements of Article 31A(1)(a ), the said provision does not get the protection of Article 31-B.
Precedent	780.
Precedent	As a result of the 29th Amendment Act , the Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act, 1969, (Kerala Act 33 of 1969) and Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act, 1971 (Kerala Act 25 of 1971) were added as items 65 and 66 in the IXth Schedule of the Constitution.
Precedent	The IXth Schedule is an appendage to Article 31-B , which says: Without prejudice to the generality of the provisions contained in Article 31A  none of the Acts and Regulations specified in the Ninth Schedule nor any of the provisions thereof shall be deemed to be void, or ever to have become void, on the ground that such Act, Regulation or provision is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by, any provisions of this Part and notwithstanding any judgment, decree or order of any court or tribunal to the contrary, each-of the said Acts and Regulations shall, subject to the power of any competent <ORG> to repeal or amend it, continue in force.
Precedent	781.
RatioOfTheDecision	The learned Counsel for the petitioners did not challenge the validity of Article 31B.
RatioOfTheDecision	Its validity has been accepted in a number of cases decided by this Court.
Argument	His only contention was that before any Act or any provision in an Act, included in the IXth Schedule can get the protection of Article 31B , the Act or the provision in question must satisfy the requirements of one or the other of the provisions in Article 31A.
Argument	For this contention of his, he relied on the ope ning words of Article 31B namely "without prejudice to the generality of the provisions contained in Article 31A".
Argument	He urged that, if Article 31B had been an independent provision having no connection whatsoever with Article 31A as contended on behalf of the contesting respondents, there was no occasion for using the words referred to earlier in Article 31B.
Argument	He also attempted to trace the history of Articles 31A and 31B and establish that there is link between those two Articles.
RatioOfTheDecision	Though there is some force in those contentions, the question of law raised is no more res integra.
Precedent	It Is concluded by a scries of decisions of this <ORG> and we see no justification to reopen that question.
Precedent	782.
Precedent	<CITATION_41868> .
Precedent	S.C.R.
Precedent	889 a contention similar to that advanced by Mr. <NAME> was advanced by Mr. <NAME>.
Precedent	That contention was rejected by <NAME> speaking for the <ORG> with these observations:  Mr. <NAME>, however, submitted that the opening words of Article 31-B , namely "Without prejudice to the g enerality of the provisions contained in Article 31A" showed that the mention of particular statutes in Article 31-B read with the Ninth Schedule was only illustrative, and that, accordingly, Article 31-B could not be wider in scope.. Reliance was placed in support of this argument upon the decision of <ORG> in <NAME> case.
Precedent	(1945) F.C.R.
Precedent	195).
Precedent	I cannot agree with that view.
Precedent	There is nothing in Article 31-B to indicate that the specific intention of certain statutes was only intended to illustrate the application of the general words of Article 31-A.
Precedent	The opening words of Article 31-B are only intended to make clear that Article 31-A should not be restricted in its application by reason of anything contained in Article 31-B and are in no way calculated to restrict the application of the latter article or of the enactments referred to therein to acquisition of "estates".
Precedent	783.
Precedent	<CITATION_45759> (1952) S.C.R.
Precedent	1020 <NAME> (as he then was) reiterated the same view.
Precedent	He observed:  It was con tended that Article 31-B was merely illustrative of the rule stated in Article 31-A and if Article 31-A had no application, that article also should be left out of consideration....  On the basis of the similarity of the language in the opening part of Article 31-B with that of Sub-section (2) of Section 2 of the Defence of India Act "without prejudice to the generality of the provisions contained in Article 31-A ", it was urged that Article 31-B was merely illustrative of Article 31- A and as the latter was limited in its application to estates as defined therein, Article 31-B was also so limited.
Precedent	In my opinion, the observations in <NAME> case far from supporting the contention raised, negatives it.
Precedent	Article 31-B specifically validates certain Acts mentioned in the Schedule despite the provisions of Article 31-A , but stands independent of it.
Precedent	The impugned Acts in this situation qua the acquisition of the eight malguzari villages cannot be questioned on the ground that it contravenes the provisio ns of Article 31(2) of the Constitution or any of the other provisions of Part III.
Precedent	784.
Precedent	A similar view was expresed by this <ORG> in <CITATION_1681028> .
Precedent	<NAME>, S.C.R.
Precedent	636 <NAME> (as he then was) speaking for the <ORG> observed thus:  The learned Attorney General contended that Article 31-A and Article 31- B should be read together and that if so read Article 31-B would only illustrate cases that would otherwise fall under Article 31-A and, therefore, the same construction as put upon Article 31-B should also apply to Article 31-A of the Constitut on.
Precedent	This construction was sought to be based upon the opening words of Article 31-B , namely "without prejudice to the generality of the provisions contained in Article 31-A".
RatioOfTheDecision	We find it difficult to accept this argument.
RatioOfTheDecision	The words "Without prejudice to the generality of the provisions" indicate that the Acts and regulations specified in the Ninth Schedule would have the immunity even if they did not attract Article 31-A of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	If ev ery Act in the 9th Schedule would be covered by Article 31-A , this article would become redundant.
RatioOfTheDecision	Indeed, some of the <ORG> mentioned therein, namely, items 14 to 20 and many other <ORG> added to the 9th Schedule, do not appear to relate to estates as defined in Article 31-A(2) of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	We, therefore, hold that Article 31-B is not governed by Article 31A and that Article 31B is a Constitutional device to place the specified statutes beyond any attack on the ground that they infringe Part III of the Constitution....  785.
RatioOfTheDecision	Several other decisions of this Court proceed on the basis that Article 31-B is independent of the Article 31A.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is too late in the day to reopen that question.
Precedent	Whether the <ORG> which were brought into the IXth Schedule by the 29th Amendment Act or any provision in any of them abrogate any of the basic elements or essential features of the Constitution can be examined when the validity of those <ORG> is gone into.
Precedent	786.
Precedent	For the foregoing reasons, we reject the contention of the p etitioners that before an Act can be included in the IXth Schedule, it must satisfy the requirements of Article 31- A.
Precedent	787.
Precedent	In the result we hold:  (1) The power to amend the Constitution under Article 368 as it stood before its amendment empowered the <ORG> by following the form and manner laid down in that Article, to amend each and every Article and each and every Part of the Constitution.
Precedent	(2) The expression "law" in Article 13(2) even before Article 13 was amended by the 24th Amendment Act , did not include amendments to the Constitution.
Precedent	(3) Though the power to amend the Constitution under Article 368 is a very wide power, it does not yet include the power to destroy or emasculate the basic elements or the fundamental features of the Constitution.
Precedent	(4) The 24th Amendment Act did not enlarge the amending power of the <ORG> It merely made explicit what was implicit in the original Article.
Precedent	Hence it is valid.
Precedent	(5)(A) The newly substituted Article 31(2) does not destroy the right to property be cause-  (i) the fixation of "amount" under that Article should have reasonable relationship with the value of the property acquired or requisitioned;  (ii) the principles laid down must be relevant for the purpose of arriving at the "amount" payable in respect of the property acquired or requisitioned;  (iii) the "amount" fixed should not be illusory and  (iv) the same should not be fixed arbitrarily.
Precedent	5(B) The question whether the "amount" in question has been fixed arbitrarily or the same is illusory or the principles laid down for the determination of the same are relevant to the subject matter of acquisition or requisition at about the time when the property in question is acquired or requisitioned are open to judicial review.
Precedent	But it is no more open to the court to consider whether the "amount" fixed or to be determined on the basis of the principles laid down is adequate.
Precedent	(6) Clause 2(b) of the 25th Amendment Act which incorporated Article 31 (2B) is also valid as it did not damage or destroy any essent ial features of the Constitution.
Precedent	(7) Clause (3) of the 25th Amendment Act which introduced into the Constitution Article 31C is invalid for two reasons i.e.
Precedent	(1) it was beyond the amending power of the <ORG> in so far as the amendment in question permits destruction of several basic elements or fundamental features of the Constitution and (2) it empowers the <ORG> and <ORG> to pro tanto amend certain human freedoms guaranteed to the citizens by the exercise of their ordinary legislative power.
Precedent	(8) The 29th Amendment Act is valid but whether the Acts which were brought into the IXth Schedule by that Amendment or any provision in any of them abrogate any of the basic elements or essential features of the Constitution will have to be examined when the validity of those Acts is gone into.
Precedent	In the circumstances of the case we direct the parties to bear their own costs in these cases uptill this stage.
Precedent	<NAME>, J.
Precedent	788.
Precedent	The validity of the Constitution 24th, 25th and 29th Amendment Act  s is challenged.
Precedent	The Constitution 24th Amendment Act amended Article 368.
Precedent	Article 368 in the unamended form speaks of "Amendment of this Constitution" and how the Constitution shall stand amended.
Precedent	The Constitution 24th Amendment Act enacts that <ORG> may in exercise of its constituent power amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any provision of this Constitution in accordance with the procedure laid down in that Article.
Precedent	The other part of the amendment is that nothing in Article 13 shall apply to any amendment under Article 368.
Precedent	The Constitution 25th Amendment Act has amended Article 31(2) and also Article 31(2A).
Precedent	The effect of these two amendments with regard to Articles 31(2) and 31 (2A) is two-fold.
Precedent	First, no property shall be compulsorily acquired or requsitioned save for a public purpose and save by authority of law which provides for an amount which may be fixed by law or which may be determined in accordance with such principles.
Precedent	Secondly, nothing in Article 19(1)(f) shall affect any law  as is referred to in Article 31(2).
Precedent	The second part of the Constitution 25th Amendment Act is introduction of Article 31C which enacts that notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 no law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing principles prescribed in Clauses  (b) and (c) of Article 39 shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Articles 14, 19 and 31; and no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy.
Precedent	By the Constitution 29th Amendment Act the Kerala Land Reforms Amendment Act 1969 and the Kerala Land Reforms Amendment Act 1971 have been introduced into the Ninth Schedule of the Constitution.
Precedent	789.
Fact	The principal question which falls for determination is whether the power to amend is under any express limitation of Article 13(2).
Fact	Another question is whether there are implie d and inherent limitation on the power of amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	Can there be any implied or inherent limitation in the face of any express power of amendment without any exception?
RatioOfTheDecision	Question have been raised that essential features of the Constitution cannot be amended.
RatioOfTheDecision	Does the Constitution admit of distinction between essential and non-essential features ?
RatioOfTheDecision	Who is to determine what the essential features are?
RatioOfTheDecision	Who is the authority to pronounce as to what features are essential?
RatioOfTheDecision	The preeminent question is whether the power of amendment is to be curtailed or restricted, though the Constitution does not contain any exception to the power of amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	The people gave the Constitution to the people.
Precedent	The people gave the power of amendment to <ORG>.
Precedent	Democracy proceeds on the faith and capacity of the people to elect their representatives and faith in the representatives to represent the people.
Precedent	Thoroughout the history of man-kind if any motive power has been more potent than another it is that of faith in themselves.
Precedent	The  ideal of faith in ourself is of the greatest help to us.
Precedent	<NAME> the historian of Greece said that the diffusion of Constitutional morality, not merely among the majority of any community but throughout the whole, is the indispensible condition of a government at once free and peaceful.
Precedent	By Constitutional morality <NAME> meant a paramount reverence for the forms of the Constitution, with a perfect confidence in the bosom of every citizen amidst the bitterness of party contest that the forms of the Constitution will not be less sacred in the eyes of opponents than in his own.
Precedent	The question is "He that planted the car, shall he nor hear?
Precedent	or he that made the eye, shall he no see".
Precedent	790.
Precedent	The real question is whether there is any power to amend the Constitution and if so whether there is any limitation on the power.
Precedent	The answer to this question depends on these considerations.
Precedent	First, what is the correct ratio and effect of the decision in <CITATION_21266288> .
Precedent	(1967) 2 S.C.R.
Precedent	762.
Precedent	Second, should that ratio be upheld.
Precedent	Th ird, is there any limitation on the power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	Fourth, was the 24th Amendment validly enacted.
Precedent	If it was, is there any inherent and implied limitation on that power under Article 368 as amended.
Precedent	791.
Precedent	The scope and power under Article 368 as it stood prior to the Constitution (24th) Amendment Act to amend the Constitution falls for consideration.
Precedent	792.
Precedent	Two principal questions arise.
Precedent	First, is the Constitution as well as an amendment to the Constitution law within the meaning of Article 13(2).
Precedent	Second, is there any implied and inherent limitation on the power of amendment apart from Article 13(2).
Precedent	793.
Argument	Mr. <NAME> contends that the unamended Article 368 was subject to Article 13(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	It is said that amendment of the Constitution is law, and, therefore, any law which contravenes fundamental rights is void.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is also said that Article 368 does not prevail over or override Article 13.
RatioOfTheDecision	The four bars under Article 13 are said to be these.
RatioOfTheDecision	The bar is imposed against the <ORG>, that is to sa y the totality of all the forces of the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Second, all categories of law are covered by the bar, whether they are Constitutional amendments or bye-laws or executive Orders and Notifications.
RatioOfTheDecision	Third, all laws in force under Article 372 and all laws to be brought into force at any future date are brought within the scope of this bar.
Precedent	Fourth, the effect of the bar is to render the law void.
Precedent	794.
