Precedent	Mr. <NAME> said that the preamble makes it clear that the object of the Constitution is to secure basic human freedom, and this guarantee will be meaningless if the <ORG> against whom the guarantee is to operate is at liberty to abrogate the guarantees.
Precedent	It is said that law is comprehensive enough to include both ordinary law and Constitutional law.
Precedent	The various forms of oath in the Third Schedule of the Constitution refer to "Constitution as by law established".
Precedent	It is, therefore, submitted by the petitioner that the Constitution itself was originally established by law and every amendment has likewise to be  established by law in order to take effect.
Precedent	It is emphasised that the Constitutional amendment is a law, and, therefore, the word "law" in Article 13(2) includes Constitutional amendments.
Precedent	795.
Precedent	The Attorney General and Mr. <NAME> said that the Constitution is the supreme higher law.
Precedent	An amendment to the Constitution is in exercise of constituent power.
Precedent	The amending power is not a legislative power.
Precedent	Law in Article 13(2) embodies the doctrine of ultra vires to render void any law enacted under the Constitution.
Precedent	796.
Precedent	This Court in <CITATION_1706770> (1952) S.C.R.
Precedent	89 and <CITATION_1308308> (1965) 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	933 examined the power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	797.
Precedent	In Shankari Prasad case the Constitution First Amendment Act was challenged.
Precedent	The principal contention was that the First Amendment in so far as it purported to take away or abridge the rights conferred by Part III of the Constitution fell within the prohibition of Article 13(2) of the Constitution.
Precedent	798.
Precedent	The unanimous view of this Court in Shankari Prasad  case was that although law must ordinarily include Constitutional law there is a clear demarcation between ordinary law which is made in exercise of legislative power and Constitutional law which is made in exercise of constituent power.
Precedent	In the absence of a clear indication to the contrary it is difficult to hold that the framers of the Constitution intended to make the fundamental rights immune of Constitutional amendment The terms of Article 368 are general to empower <ORG> to amend the Constitution without any exception.
Precedent	Article 13(2) construed in the context of Article 13 means that law in Article 13(2) would be relateable to exercise of ordinary legislative power and not amendment to the Constitution.
Precedent	799.
Precedent	The Constitution Fourth Amendment Act came into existence on 5 October, 1963.
Precedent	The Constitution Seventeenth Amendment Act came into force on 20 June, 1964.
Precedent	By the Seventeenth Amendment Act Article 31A Clause (1) was amended by inserting one more proviso.
Precedent	A fresh Sub-clause (a) was substituted for  original Sub-clause (a) of Clause (2) of Article 31 retrospectively.
Precedent	44 Acts were added in the Ninth Schedule.
Precedent	The validity of the Seventeenth Amendment was challenged before this Court in <NAME> case.
Precedent	800.
Precedent	The main contention in <NAME> case was that the power prescribed by Article 226 was likely to be affected by the Seventeenth Amendment, and, therefore, it was necessary that the special procedure laid down in the proviso to Article 368 should have been followed.
Precedent	The Seventeenth Amendment Act was said to be invalid because that procedure was not followed.
Precedent	801.
Precedent	The majority view of this Court in <NAME> case was that Article 368 plainly and unambiguously meant amendment of all the provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	The word "law" in Article 13(2) was held not to take in the Constitution Amendment Act s passed under Article 368.
Precedent	It was also said that fundamental rights in Article 19 could be regulated as specified in Clauses (2) to (6) and, therefore, it could not be said to have been assumed  by the Constitution makers that fundamental rights were static and incapable of expansion.
Precedent	It was said that the concept of public interest and other important considerations which are the basis of Clauses (2) to (6) in Article 19 " may change and may even expand".
Precedent	The majority view said that "The Constitution makers knew that <ORG> could be competent to make amendments in those rights (meaning thereby fundamental rights) so as to meet the challenge of the problem which may arise in the course of socio economic progress and the development of the country".
Precedent	802.
Precedent	The minority view in <NAME> case doubted the correctness of the unanimous view in <NAME> case.
Precedent	The doubt was on a question as to whether fundamental rights could be abridged by exercise of power under Article 368.
Precedent	The minority view in <NAME> case was that the rights of society are made paramount and are placed above those of the individual.
Precedent	But the minority view was also that though fundamental rights could be restricted unde r Clause (2) to (6) of Article 19 there could be no "removal or debilitation" of such rights.
Precedent	803.
Precedent	In <NAME> case the Punjab Security of Land Tenures Act, 1953 was challenged as violative of fundamental rights and as not being protected by the Constitution First Amendment Act, 1951, the Constitution Fourth Amendment Act, 1955 and the Constitution Seventeenth Amendment Act, 1964.
Precedent	The validity of the Mysore Reforms Act, 1962 as amended by Act 14 of 1965 was also challenged on the same grounds.
Precedent	The Punjab Act and the Mysore Act were included in the Ninth Schedule.
Precedent	It was common case that if the Seventeenth Amendment Act adding the Punjab Act and the Mysore Act in the Ninth Schedule was valid the two Acts could not be impugned on any ground.
Precedent	804.
Precedent	The majority decision of this Court in <NAME> case was that an amendment of the Constitution was law within the meaning of Article 13(2).
Precedent	There were two reasonings in the majority view arriving at the same conclusion.
Precedent	The majority view where <NAME>, C.J., sp oke was as follows: The power to amend the Constitution is derived from Articles 245, 246 and 248 of the Constitution and not from Article 368.
Precedent	Article 368 deals only with procedure.
Precedent	Amendment is a legislative process.
Precedent	Amendment is law within the meaning of Article 13.
Precedent	Therefore, if an amendment takes away or abridges rights conferred by Part III of the Constitution it is void.
Precedent	The Constitution First Amendment Act, the Constitution Fourth Amendment Act and the Constitution Seventeenth Amendment Act abridged the scope of fundamental rights.
Precedent	On the basis of earlier decisions of this <ORG> the Constitution Amendment Acts were declared to be valid.
Precedent	On the application of the doctrine of prospective over-ruling the amendments will continue to be valid.
Precedent	<ORG> will have no power from the date of this decision (meaning thereby the decision in <NAME> case) to amend any of the provisions of Part III of the Constitution so as to take away or abridge the fundamental rights.
Precedent	The Constitution Seventeenth Amendment  Act holds the field.
Precedent	Therefore, the Punjab Act and the Mysore Act cannot be questioned.
Precedent	805.
Precedent	The concurring majority view of <NAME>, J. was this.
Precedent	The fundamental rights are outside the amendatory process if the amendment seeks to abridge or take away any of the rights.
Precedent	The First, the Fourth and the Seventh Amendment Acts being Part of the Constitution by acquiescence for a long time cannot be chellenged.
Precedent	These Constitution Amendment Act s contain authority for the Seventeenth Amendment Act.
Precedent	Any further inroad into fundamental lights as they exist on the date of the decision will be illegal and unConstitutional unless it complies with Part III in general and Article 13(2) in particular.
Precedent	The constituent body will have to be Convened for abridging or taking away fundamental rights.
Precedent	The Punjab Act and the Mysore Act are valid not because they are included in the Ninth Schedule of the Constitution but because they are protected by Article 31A and the assent of the President.
Precedent	806.
Precedent	The two views forming the  majority arrived at the same conclusion that an amendment of the Constitution being law within the meaning of Article 13(2) would be unConstitutional if such an amendment abridged any fundamental right.
Precedent	The leading majority view did not express any final opinion as to whether fundamental rights could be abridged by <ORG> exercising its residuary power and calling <ORG> "for making a new Constitution or radically changing it".
Precedent	The concurring majority view held that the fundamental rights could be abridged by suitably amending Article 368 to convoke <ORG>.
Precedent	The concurring majority view was that <ORG> could be called by passing a law under Entry 97 of List I and then that <ORG> would be able to abridge or take away fundamental rights.
Precedent	807.
Precedent	The minority view of five learned Judges expressed in 3 judgments as against the majority view of six learned Judges in <NAME> case was this.
Precedent	808.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. spoke for himself and two concurring learned Judges as  follows.
Precedent	Article 368 contains both the power and the procedure for amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	It is incomprehensible that the residuary power of <ORG> will apply to amendment of the Constitution when the procedure for amendment speaks of amendment by ratification by the States.
Precedent	When an entire part of the Constitution is devoted to amendment it will be more appropriate to read Article 368 as containing the power to amend because there is no specific mention of amendment in Article 248 or in any Entry of List I.
Precedent	The Constitution is the fundamental law and without express power to affect change legislative power cannot effect any change in the Constitution.
Precedent	Legislative Act s are passed under the power conferred by the Constitution.
Precedent	Article 245 which gives power to make law for the whole or any part of India is subject to the provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	If, however, power to amend is in Article 248 read with the residuary Entry in List I that power is to be exercised subject to the Constitution and i t cannot change the Constitution which is the fundamental law.
Precedent	It is because of the difference between the fundamental law and the legislative power under the Constitution that the power to amend cannot be located in the Residuary Entry which is law making power under the Constitution.
Precedent	809.
Precedent	Article 368 confers power on <ORG> subject to the procedure provided therein for amendment of any provision of the Constitution, It is impossible to introduce in the concept of amendment, any idea of improvement.
Precedent	The word "amendment" must be given its full meaning.
Precedent	This means that, by amendment an existing Constitution or law can be changed.
Precedent	This change can take the form either of addition to the existing provisions, or alteration of existing provisions and their substitution by others or deletion of certain provisions altogether.
Precedent	An amendment of the Constitution is not an ordinary law made under the powers conferred under Chapter I of Part XI of the Constitution, and therefore, it cannot be subject to Article 13(2).
Precedent	It is strange that the power conferred by Article 368 will be limited by putting an interpretation on the word "law" in Article 13(2) which will include Constitutional law also.
Precedent	The possibility of the abuse of any power has no relevance in considering the question about the existence of the power itself.
Precedent	The power of amendment is the safety valve which to a large extent provides for stable growth and makes violent revolution more or less unnecessary.
Precedent	810.
Precedent	The two other supporting minority views were these.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. arrived at these conclusions.
Precedent	No limitation on the amending power can be gathered from the language of Article 368.
Precedent	Therefore, each and every part of the Constitution may be amended under Article 368.
Precedent	The distinction between the Constitution and the laws is so fundamental that the Constitution is not regarded as a law or a legislative Act.
Precedent	It is because a Constitution Amendment Act can amend the Constitution that it is not a law and Article 368 avoids all reference to law making by <ORG> .
Precedent	As soon as a Bill is passed in conformity with Article 368 the Constitution stands amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill.
Precedent	Amendment or change in certain Articles does not mean necessarily improvement.
Precedent	811.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. expressed these views.
Precedent	The definition of law in Article 13(3) does include in terms a Constitutional amendment though it includes any ordinance, order, bye-law, rule, regulation, notification, custom or usage.
Precedent	The language of Article 368 is perfectly general and empowers <ORG> to amend the Constitution without any exception whatever.
Precedent	If it had been intended by the Constitution makers that the fundamental righto guaranteed under Part III should be completely outside the scope of Article 368 it is reasonable to assume that they would have made an express provision to that effect.
Precedent	The expression "fundamental" does not lift the fundamental rights above the Constitution itself.
Precedent	In a matter of Constitutional amendment it is not permissible to assume that there will be abuse of pow er and then utilise it as a test for finding out the scope of amending power.
Precedent	812.
Precedent	The majority view in <NAME> case was that an amendment of the Constitution pursuant to Article 368 is law within the meaning of Article 13(2 ), and, therefore, an amendment of the Constitution abridging fundamental rights will be void.
Precedent	The majority view was on the basis that there was conflict between Article 13(2) and Article 368 and this basis was the result of the nature and quality of fundamental rights in the scheme of the Constitution.
Precedent	813.
Precedent	It is, therefore, to be seen at the threshold as to whether there is any conflict between Article 13(2) and Article 368 , namely, whether amendment of Constitution is law within the meaning of law in Article 13(2).
Precedent	Article 368 provides in clear and unambiguous terms that an amendment bill after compliance with the procedure stated therein and upon the President giving assent to such bill the Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the bill.
Precedent	This Constitu tional mandate does not admit or provide any scope for any conflict with any other Article of the Constitution.
Precedent	This is the fundamental law.
Precedent	No other Article of the Constitution has limited its scope.
Precedent	The moment the President gives his assent to an amendment bill the amendment becomes a part of the Constitution.
Precedent	There cannot be a law before the assent of the President.
Precedent	Therefore, the validity of any such supposed law cannot arise.
Precedent	An amendment of the Constitution becomes a part of the fundamental law.
Precedent	The legality of an amendment is no more open to attack than of the Constitution itself.
Precedent	The opening part of amended Article 368 , viz., "An Amendment of this Constitution may be initiated" and its concluding part before the proviso, viz., "The Constitution shall stand amended" show clearly that the whole Constitution can be amended and no part of the Constitution is excluded from the amendment.
Precedent	Herein lies the vital distinction between the Constitution and the ordinary law.
Precedent	814.
Precedent	The distinction lies in the crit erion of validity.
Precedent	The validity of an ordinary law can be questioned.
Precedent	When it is questioned it must be justified by reference to a higher law.
Precedent	In the case of the Constitution the validity is inherent and lies within itself.
Precedent	The validity of Constitutional law cannot be justified by reference to another higher law.
Precedent	Every legal rule or norm owes its validity to some higher legal rule or norm.
Precedent	The Constitution is the basic norm.
Precedent	The Constitution generates its own validity.
Precedent	It is valid because it exists.
Precedent	The Constitution is binding because it is the Constitution.
Precedent	Any other law is binding only if and in so far as it is in conformity with the Constitution.
Precedent	The validity of the Constitution lies in the social fact of its acceptance by the community.
Precedent	The Constitutional rules are themselves the basic rules of the legal system.
Precedent	The Constitution prevails over any other form of law not because of any provision to that effect either in the Constitution or else where but because of the underlying assumption to that effect by  the community.
Precedent	If <ORG> passes a law under any of the items in the Union List abridging a fundamental right and also provides in that law itself that it shall not be invalid notwithstanding anything in Article 13 or Part III of the Constitution, yet the law made by <ORG> will be invalid to the extent of its inconsistency with Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	It will be invalid because Article 13 occurs in the Constitution which is supreme.
Precedent	The impugned Act cannot enact that it will be valid notwithstanding the Constitution.
Precedent	815.
Precedent	The real distinction is that Constitutional law is the source of all legal validity and is itself always valid.
Precedent	Ordinary law on the other hand must derive its validity from a higher legal source, which is ultimately the Constitution.
Precedent	Law in Article 13(2) of the Constitution could only mean that law which needs validity from a higher source and which can and ought to be regarded as invalid when it comes in conflict with higher law.
Precedent	It cannot possibly include a law which is sel f validating and which is never invalid.
Precedent	The definition of law in Article 13 enumerates more or less exhaustively all forms of law which need validation from higher source and which are invalid when they are in conflict with the Constitution.
Precedent	The definition does not mention Constitutional amendment.
Precedent	It is because an amendment being the Constitution itself can never be invalid.
Precedent	An amendment is made if the procedure is complied with.
Precedent	Once the procedure is complied with it is a part of the Constitution,  816.
Precedent	The expression "law" has been used in several Articles in Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	These are Articles 17, 19 Clauses (2) to (6), 21, 22, 25, 26, 31, 33, 34 and  35.
Precedent	To illustrate, Article 17 states that untouchability is abolished and its practice in any form is forbidden.
Precedent	Article 17 also states that the enforcement of any disability arising out of untouchability shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law.
Precedent	The word "law" in Article 17 does not mean the Constitution.
Precedent	The Constitution leaves t he matter of enforcement and punishment to law.
Precedent	817.
Precedent	The foundation of the majority view in <NAME> case that Article 13(2) takes in Constitutional law within its purview is that an amendment is a legislative process and is an exercise of legislative power.
Precedent	The majority relied on the decision in <NAME> v. The King (1920) A.C. 691 and <ORG> v. <NAME> 1965 A.C. 172 in support of the view that there is no distinction between ordinary legislation and Constitutional amendment.
Precedent	The basis of the unanimous decision in <ORG> case was on the distinction between legislative power and the constituent power.
Precedent	Therefore, the majority view in <NAME> case overruled the view in <ORG> case.
Precedent	Article 13(2) expressly declares that law taking away or abridging the rights conferred by Part III shall be void.
Precedent	This principle embodies the doctrine of ultra vires in a written Constitution.
Precedent	The observation of <NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	in <CITATION_1857950> 1950 S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	88 that Article 13(2) w as introduced ex majore cautela because even if Article 13 were not there any law abridging or taking away fundamental rights would be void to the extent of contravention or repugnancy with fundamental rights in Part III refers to the doctrine of ultra vires which is a necessary implication of our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, there is no distinction between Article 13(2) which expressly affirms the doctrine of ultra vires and the necessary implication of the doctrine of ultra vires which has been applied to every part of our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the express doctrine of ultra vires prevented an amendment of Part III of the 'Constitution contrary to its terms, equally an amendment of other parts of the Constitution contrary to their terms would be prevented by the implied doctrine of ultra vires.
RatioOfTheDecision	The result would be that an amendment of the Constitution which contravened the terms of the existing Constitution would be void.
RatioOfTheDecision	This would result in absurdity.
RatioOfTheDecision	That is why Article 368 expressly provides for the amendment of  the Constitution.
Precedent	818.
Argument	Mr. <NAME> on behalf of the petitioner submitted that Constitution amendment was law, within Article 13(2) and was void to the extent to which it contravened the fundamental rights and Article 368 did not prevail over or override Article 13 for these reasons.
Precedent	Reference was made to the form of oath in the Third Schedule which uses the words "Constitution as by law established".
Precedent	This is said to mean that our Constitution was originally established by law and, therefore, every amendment thereto was likewise to be established by law.
Precedent	Article 13(1) is also said to cover Constitutional law because though Article 395 repealed the Indian Independence Act, 1947 and the Government of India Act 1935 the Constitutional laws of the Indian Princely States or some other Constitutional laws of British India were in existence.
Precedent	Therefore, the word "Law" in Article 13(2) will also include Constitutional law.
Precedent	The word "law" in Article 13(2) will in its ordinary sense embrace Constitutional law, and th ere is no reason for reading the word "law", in a restricted sense to confine it to ordinary laws.
Precedent	The real question is not whether there are any words of limitation in Articles 368 but whether there are any words of limitation in Article 13(2).
Precedent	It was amplified to mean if a limitation has to be read in either of the two Articles 368 and 13(2) there is no reason why it should be read in such a way as to enable parliament to take away or abridge fundamental rights.
Precedent	819.
Precedent	In Article 368 the word "law" is not used at all.
Precedent	Consequently the language of Article 368 raises no question about the applicability of Article 13(2).
Precedent	It is inconceivable that Constitutional laws of Indian Princely States or Constitutional laws of British India exist as Constitutional laws after the coming into existence of our Constitution.
Precedent	Our Constitution is the only fundamental law.
Precedent	All other laws which continue under our Constitution are ordinary laws.
Precedent	The fundamental error in including amendment of the Constitution in law under Article  13(2) is by overlooking the vital difference between the constituent and the legislative powers and in wrongly equating these powers.
Precedent	The definition of "<ORG>" in Article 12 includes <ORG>.
Precedent	Part V of the Constitution contains provisions relating to the powers of the three organs of <ORG>.
Precedent	Chapter II of Part V relates to the legislative power of <ORG>.
Precedent	Under Article 79 <ORG> is the Union Legislature provided for by the Constitution.
Precedent	Therefore, law in Article 13(2) must mean a law of <ORG> functioning under Chapter II of Part V. It cannot mean the Constitution itself or an amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	The reason is that the Constitution with its amendment is the supreme authority and the three organs of the <ORG> derive their powers from this supreme authority.
Precedent	820.
Precedent	The word "law" when used in relation to Constitutional law which is fundamental law and ordinary law is not a mere homonym.
Precedent	If the word "law" here is not a mere homonym then it is a mistake to think that all the  instances to which it is applied must possess either a single quality or a single set of qualities in common.
Precedent	There is some general test or criterion whereby the rules of the fundamental law or the rules of the system of ordinary laws are tested and identified.
Precedent	When the word "law" is spoken in connection with Constitutional law it cannot have the same meaning as ordinary law.
Precedent	It is not arbitrary to use the word "law" in relation to Constitutional law in spite of its difference from ordinary law.
Precedent	821.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> contended that Constitutional laws of Princely States and of British India prior to our Constitution survived as laws in force under Article 372.
Precedent	Article 372 became necessary to make a provision similar to Section 292 of the Government of India Act, 1935 following the repeal of the 1935 Act and the Indian Independence Act, 1947.
Precedent	The purpose of Article 372 is to negative the possibility of any existing law in India being held to be no longer in force by reason of the repeal of the law authorising  its enactment.
Precedent	A saving clause of the type of Article 372 is put in to avoid challenge to laws made under the repealed Constitution.
Precedent	The total volume of law in the then British India had the legal authority up to 14 August 1947 by reason of the Government of India Act 1935.
Precedent	The Government of India Act 1935 with adaptations and the Indian Independence Act 1947 preserved the authority of those laws upto 25 January 1950.
Precedent	In so far as it is indisputable that the Government of India Act, 1935 and the Indian Independence Act, 1947 were repealed, the repeal of those Acts was repeal of the Constitutional law represented by those Acts.
Precedent	By our Constitution there was a repeal of all other Constitutional laws operating in our country.
Precedent	There was repeal of "Constitution" in Princely States.
Precedent	822.
Precedent	A distinction arises between the provisions of a Constitution which are described as Constitutional law and provisions of a statute dealing with a statute which is treated to have Constitutional aspects.
Precedent	An example of the latter  type is a statute which provides for the judicature.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> rightly said that the two distinct senses of Constitutional law are mixed up in the contention of Mr. <NAME>.
Precedent	In the first sense, Constitutional law is applicable to a provision of the Constitution, and in the second sense, to a law enacted under the Constitution dealing with certain classes of subject matter.
Precedent	Laws of the second class fluctuate.
Precedent	An amendment of the Constitution becomes a part of the Constitution itself.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME>l rightly contended that in order to show that law in Article 13(2) includes amendment of the Constitution it is also necessary to show that the expression "laws in force" in Article 13(1) includes Constitution amendment or the Constitution itself It is impossible to accept the submission that the word "law" in Article 13(2) includes the Constitution.
Precedent	The Constitution itself cannot include the Constitution.
Precedent	It is the Constitution which continues the laws in force.
Precedent	Therefore, law in Article 13 is law other than the  Constitution and a fortiori it is other than amendment to the Constitution.
Precedent	823.
Precedent	In non-British territory on the Constitution coming into force the Constitution of Princely States lost its character as Constitutional law in the strict sense.
Precedent	It is in that strict sense that <NAME>, J. rightly said in <NAME> case that on our Constitution coming into existence no other Constitutional law survived.
Precedent	Article 393 of our Constitution says that the Constitution may be called the "Constitution of India".
Precedent	The Preamble recites that the People in <ORG> gave this Constitution meaning thereby the Constitution of India.
Precedent	Therefore, the people gave themselves no other Constitution.
Precedent	All other laws whatever their previous status as strict Constitutional law became subordinate laws subject to the provisions of our Constitution and this position is clear from the language of Article 372.
Precedent	824.
Precedent	In a broad sense law may include the Constitution and the law enacted by the legislature.
Precedent	There is however a clea r demarcation between ordinary law in exercise of legislative power and Constitutional law which is made in exercise of constituent power.
Precedent	Therefore, a power to amend the Constitution is different from the power to amend ordinary law.
Precedent	It was said by Mr. <NAME> that legislative power is power to make law and constituent power is the power to make or amend Constitutional law and since law in its ordinary sense, includes Constitutional law the legislative power is the genus of which the constituent power is the species.
Precedent	The difference between legislative and constituent power in a flexible or uncontrolled Constitution is conceptual depending upon the subject matter.
Precedent	A Dog Act in England is prima facie made in exercise of legislative power.
Precedent	The Bill of Rights was made in the exercise of constituent power as modifying the existing Constitutional arrangement But this conceptual difference does not produce different legal consequences, since the provisions of a Dog Act inconsistent with the earlier provisions of  the Bill of Rights would repeal those provisions pro tanto.
Precedent	In a rigid or controlled <ORG> the distinction between legislative power and constituent power is not only conceptual but material and vital in introducing legal consequences.
Precedent	In a controlled <ORG> it is not correct to say that legislative power is the genus of which constituent power is the species.
Precedent	The question immediately arises as to what the differentia is which distinguishes that species from other species of the same genus.
Precedent	It would be correct to say that the law making power is the genus of which legislative power and constituent power are the species.
Precedent	The differentia is found in the different procedure prescribed for the exercise of constituent power as distinguished from that prescribed for making ordinary laws.
Precedent	The distinction between legislative power and constituent power is vital in a rigid or controlled <ORG>, because it is that distinction which brings in the doctrine that a law ultra vires the <ORG> is vo id, since the Constitution is the touchstone of validity and that no provision of the Constitution can be ultra vires.
Precedent	825.
Precedent	The legislatures constituted under our Constitution have the power to enact laws on the topics indicated in Lists I to III in the Seventh Schedule or embodied specifically in certain provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	The power to enact laws carries with it the power to amend or repeal them.
Precedent	But these powers of legislatures do not include any power to amend the Constitution, because it is <ORG> which enacted the Constitution and the status given by Article 368 to <ORG> and the State legislatures, is the status of <ORG>.
Precedent	The distinction between the power to amend the Constitution and the ordinary power to enact laws is fundamental to all federal Constitution.
Precedent	When <ORG> is engaged in the amending process it is not legislating.
Precedent	It is exercising a particular power which is sui generis bestowed upon it by the amending clause in the Constitution.
Precedent	Th us an amendment of the Constitution under Article 368 is constituent law and not law within the meaning of Article 13(2) and law as defined in Article 13(3)(a).
Precedent	826.
Precedent	The procedure that Bill for amendment of the Constitution has to be introduced in either <ORG> of <ORG> and passed by both <ORG>s does not alter the status of <ORG> to amend the Constitution as a Constituent Assembly and does not assimilate it to that of the Union legislature.
Precedent	At this stage it may be stated that in <NAME> case it was said that law in general sense may include the Constitution and the procedure of amendment is assimilated to ordinary legislative procedure.
Precedent	Assimilation of procedure does not make both the procedure same.
Precedent	Nor are the two separate powers to be lost sight of.
Precedent	<ORG> which has summoned on 19 December, 1946 to frame a Constitution was also invested after independence with legislative power.
Precedent	It framed the Constitution as <ORG>.
Precedent	It enacted ordinary laws as legislat ure.
Precedent	Under Article V of the American Constitution the <ORG> functions not as a legislature but as <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	In Australia when a Bill for amendment has to be passed by <ORG> and then has to be submitted to the verdict of the electorate the process is not ordinary legislative process of the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	In our Constitution when the amendment falls within the proviso to Article 368 it requires that the amendment must be ratified by at least one half of the State legislatures and the process is radically different from ordinary legislative procedure.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Union legislature acting under Chapter II of Part V has no connection with the State legislatures.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, when amendment is affected under the proviso to Article 368 <ORG> does not act as a Union legislature.
RatioOfTheDecision	The feature that in the passage of the bill for amendment of the Constitution the House of <ORG> has to adopt the procedure for ordinary legislation has little bearing.
Precedent	If the intention of the fram ers of the Constitution was to leave to the Union legislature the power to effect amendments of the Constitution it would have been sufficient to insert a provision in Chapter II of Part V in that behalf without enacting a separate part and inserting a provision therein for amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	827.
Precedent	Under Clause (e) of Article 368 the Article itself can be amended.
Precedent	Therefore, an amendment of Article 368 providing that provisions in Part III can be amended will be Constitutional.
Precedent	If it was intended by Article 13(2) to exclude Part III altogether from the operation of Article 368 Clause (e) would not have been enacted.
Precedent	<ORG> thus enacted Article 368 so that the power to amend should not be too rigid nor too flexible.
Precedent	Clause (s) of Article 368 requires an amendment to be ratified by not less than half the number of States.
Precedent	The title of Part XX and the opening words of Article 368 show that a provision is being made for "amendment of this Constitution" which in its ordinary sense mea ns every part of the Constitution.
Precedent	This would include Article 368 itself.
Precedent	There is no limitation imposed upon or deception made to the amendments which can be made.
Precedent	It is not permissible to add to Article 368 words of limitation which are not there.
Precedent	828.
Precedent	The initiative for an amendment of the Constitution is with <ORG> and not with the States.
Precedent	A bill for amendment is to be introduced in either <ORG> of <ORG>.
Precedent	Again, a bill must be passed by each <ORG> by not less than two thirds of the members present and voting, the requisite quorum in each <ORG> being a majority of its total membership.
Precedent	In cases coming under the proviso the amendment must be ratified by the legislatures of not less than half the number of States.
Precedent	Ordinary legislative process is very different; A bill initiating a law may be passed by majority of members present and voting at a sitting of each <ORG> and at a joint sitting of <ORG>, the quorum for the meeting of either <ORG> being one tenth of the total members of the <ORG>.
Precedent	829.
Precedent	The legislative procedure is prescribed in Articles 107 to 111 read with Article 100.
Precedent	Article 100 states "save as otherwise provided in the Constitution all questions at any sitting of either <ORG> or joint sitting shall be determined by a majority of votes of the members present and voting".
Precedent	Though Article 368 falls into two parts of the Article is one integral whole as is clear from the words "the amendment shall also require to be ratified".
Precedent	The first part of Article 368 requires that a bill must be passed in each <ORG> (1) by majority of the total membership of that <ORG> and (2) by a majority of not less than two thirds of the members of that <ORG> present and voting.
Precedent	These provisions rule out a joint sitting of either <ORG> under Article 108 to resolve the disagreement between the two <ORG>s.
Precedent	Again the majority required to pass a bill in each <ORG> is not a majority of members of that <ORG> present and voting as in Article 100 but a majority of the total membership of each <ORG> and a majority of not l ess than two thirds of the members of that <ORG> present and voting.
Precedent	These provisions are not only important safeguards when amending the Constitution, but also distinguishing features of Constituent power as opposed to legislative power.
Precedent	Under the first part of unamended Article 368 when a bill is passed by requisite majority of each <ORG> the bill must be presented for the President's assent.
Precedent	830.
Precedent	<ORG>'s power to enact laws is not dependent on <ORG> legislature, nor can it be frustrated by a majority of <ORG> legislatures.
Precedent	The provisions in the proviso to Article 368 for ratification by the legislatures of the <ORG> constitute a radical departure from the ordinary legislative process of <ORG>, <ORG> legislative process of ratification cannot possibly be equated with ordinary legislative process.
Precedent	If the bill is not ratified the bill fails.
Precedent	If it is ratified it is to be presented to the President for his assent.
Precedent	If the President assents the procedure prescribed by Article 368 gomes to an end and t he consequence prescribed comes into operation that the Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the bill.
Precedent	But the result is not law, but a part of the Constitution and no court can pronounce any part of the Constitution to be invalid.
Precedent	831.
Precedent	The exercise of the power of ratification by the <ORG> legislatures is constituent power and not ordinary law making power.
Precedent	It cannot be said that Article 368 confers constituent power under its proviso but not under the main part.
Precedent	If the procedure has been followed the invalidity of an amendment cannot arise.
Precedent	832.
Precedent	The provisions in Articles 4, 169, paragraph 7(2) of the Fifth Schedule and paragraph 21(2) of the Sixth Schedule were referred to for the purpose of showing that the word "law" is used in those provisions relating to amendments to the Constitution.
Precedent	It is, therefore, said that similar result will follow in the case of all amendments.
Precedent	These four provisions confer on <ORG> limited power of amendment.
Precedent	There are two features common to all these pro visions.
Precedent	First, they confer on <ORG> a power to make a law which inter alia provides for the specific class of amendments.
Precedent	Second, each of these provisions states that "no such law as aforesaid shall be deemed to be an amendment of the Constitution for the purpose of Article 368".
Precedent	The power to amend under any of these four provisions is a specific power for specific amendments and not a legislative power contained in the Legislative List or Residuary Legislative List.
Precedent	833.
Precedent	The amendment under Article 4 follows a law providing for the formation of new States and alteration of areas, boundaries and names of existing States.
Precedent	It is obligatory on <ORG> to make amendment of Schedules 1 and 4 and it is necessary to make amendments which are supplemental, incidental and consequential.
Precedent	In making such a law in so far as it affects the State but not Union territory a special procedure has to be followed.
Precedent	834.
Precedent	Under Article 169 which provides for the abolition or creation of a State legislative <ORG> Parlia ment has power to make a necessary law on a resolution being passed by <ORG> for such abolition or creation by a majority of the membership of the <ORG> and by majority of not less than two thirds of the members present and voting.
Precedent	It <ORG> makes such a law that law must make the necessary amendments to the Constitution.
Precedent	835.
Precedent	Schedules 5 and 6 provide for the administration of the Scheduled and Tribal areas which are governed by Part X and not by Part XI by which the <ORG> and States are governed.
Precedent	The Scheduleds provide a mode of governance of those areas which is radically different from <ORG> and the <ORG>.
Precedent	Part X of the Constitution unlike Part XI is not "subject to the provisions of this Constitution".
Precedent	Paragraph 7 of Schedule 5 and paragraph 21 of Schedule 6 confer on <ORG> a power to amend the schedules by law but no special procedure is prescribed for making such a law.
Precedent	836.
Precedent	No question relating to those four provisions, however arises in th e present case.
Precedent	In Article 368 the word "law" is not based at all.
Precedent	These four provisions for amendment deal with matters in respect of which it was considered desirable not to impose requirements of Article 368 , and, therefore, it became necessary expressly to provide that such amendments shall not be deemed to be amendments of the Constitution for the purpose of Article 368.
Precedent	These four provisions indicate the distinction between the constituent power and the legislative power.
Precedent	If the power of amendment was located in the residuary Entry No.
Precedent	97 in the Union List it would not have been necessary to grant that power of amendment again in these four provisions.
Precedent	These four provisions indicate that the Constitution makers intended to confer on <ORG> power to make amendments in the provisions of the Constitution and having provided for a particular procedure to be followed in respect of matters covered by those four provisions it conferred a general power on <ORG> to make an amendment to the other Articl es after complying with the requirements of Article 368.
Precedent	837.
Precedent	The majority view in <NAME> case said that <ORG> could call <ORG> either directly under the residuary power or pass a law under the Residuary Entry to call <ORG> for amendment of fundamental rights.
Precedent	Of the two views forming the majority one view did not express any opinion as to whether such <ORG> could take away or abridge fundamental rights but the other view expressed the opinion that such <ORG> could abridge fundamental rights.
Precedent	The majority view in <NAME> case was that <ORG> is a constituted body and not a constituent body and a constituted body cannot abridge or take away fundamental rights.
Precedent	The majority view indicates that a constituent power was required to amend the fundamental rights.
Precedent	838.
Precedent	The majority view has totally ignored the aspect that constituent power is located in Article 368 , and, therefore, amendment under the Article is not a law within t he meaning of Article 13(2).
Precedent	If <ORG> is a constituted body as was said by the majority view in <NAME> case it would be difficult to hold that such a body could bring about <ORG>.
Precedent	The well-known principle that what cannot be done directly cannot be achieved indirectly will establish the basic infirmity in that majority view.
Precedent	If fundamental rights can be abridged by <ORG> calling <ORG> under the Residuary Entry such <ORG> will be a body different from <ORG> and will frame its own rules of business and Article 368 cannot have any application.
Precedent	That will have a strange and startling result.
Precedent	839.
Precedent	In the scheme of the Constitution containing Article 368 <ORG> will be called extra Constitutional means and not one under the Constitution.
Precedent	A Constitution can be amended only in accordance with the process laid down in the Constitution.
Precedent	No other method is Constitutionally possible than that indicated in the provision for amendment of th e Constitution.
Precedent	Once the Constitution has vested the power to amend in the bodies mentioned therein that is the only body for amending the Constitution.
Precedent	The people who gave the Constitution have expressed how it is to be changed.
Precedent	840.
Precedent	The distinction between constituent and legislative power is brought out by the feature in a rigid Constitution that the amendment is by a different procedure than that by which ordinary laws may be altered.
Precedent	The amending power is, therefore, said to be a re- creation of <ORG> every time <ORG> amends re-creation in accordance with Article 368.
Precedent	841.
Precedent	The two decisions in <NAME> v. The King 1920 A.C. 691 and <ORG> 1965 A.C. 172 on which the majority view in <NAME> case relied to hold that amendment to the Constitution is an ordinary legislative process do not support that conclusion.
RatioOfTheDecision	The difference between flexible or uncontrolled and rigid or controlled Constitutions in regard to amendment is that there may b e special methods of amendment in rigid or controlled Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	In a rigid Constitution amendment is not by exercise of ordinary legislative power.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power to amend is, therefore, described in a rigid Constitution as constituent power because of the nature of the power.
RatioOfTheDecision	In a flexible Constitution the procedure for amendment is the same as that of making ordinary law.
Precedent	A Constitution being uncontrolled the distinction between legislative and constituent powers gets obliterated because any law repugnant to the Constitution pro tanto repeals a Constitution as was held in <NAME> case.
Precedent	<NAME> in his <ORG> (10th Ed.)
Precedent	illustrates the view by his opinion that if the Dentists Act said anything contrary to the Bill of Rights which can be described as Constitutional document the Dentists Act would prevail.
Precedent	In a flexible or unwritten Constitution the word Constitutional law is imprecise as it is used in respect of subject matter of law, e.g.
Precedent	a law dealing with the legislature.
Precedent	In a rigid or writte n Constitution whatever is in the Constitution would be the law of the Constitution.
Precedent	842.
Precedent	In <NAME> case the validity of the appointment of <NAME> as a Judge of <ORG> was challenged as void on the allegation that Section 6 Sub-section (6) of the Industrial Arbitration Act of 1916 was contrary to the provisions of the Constitution of Queensland 1867.
Precedent	The Industrial Arbitration Act of 1916 by Section 6 Sub-section (6) authorised the Governor to appoint any Judge of <ORG> to be a Judge of <ORG> and provided that a Judge so appointed shall have the jurisdiction of both offices and shall hold office as a Judge of <ORG> during good behaviour.
Precedent	The sub-section further provided that Judge of <ORG> shall hold office for seven years.
Precedent	The Governor in Council by commission reciting Section 6 Sub-section (6) appointed <NAME> who was a Judge and the President of <ORG> on to be a Judge of <ORG> during good behaviour.
Precedent	By Sections 15 and 16 of the Constitution of 1867 the period during which Judges of <ORG> were to hold office was during good behaviour.
Precedent	The contention was that the appointment of <NAME> under the Industrial Arbitration Act 1916 for a limited period of seven years was invalid since the Act was inconsistent with the Constitution Act 1867 and further that the Act of 1916 could not repeal or modify the provisions of the Constitution Act .
Precedent	843.
Precedent	<ORG> held that <ORG> had power both under the Colonial Laws Validity Act 1865 Section 5 and apart therefrom under Clauses 2 and 22 of the Order-in-<ORG> of 1859, Section 7 of the Act 18 & 19 Vict.
Precedent	c. 54 and Sections 2 and 9 of the Constitution Act of 1867 to authorise the appointment of a Judge of <ORG> for a limited period.
Precedent	Section 7 of the Act 18 & 19 Vict.
Precedent	c. 54 intended an order in <ORG> to make provision for the government of the Colony and  for the establishment of a legislature.
Precedent	The Order-in-<ORG> 1859 by Clause 2 gave full power to the legislature of the Colony to make further provision in that behalf.
Precedent	The Order-in- <ORG> of 1859 by Clause 22 gave the legislature full power and authority from time to time to make laws altering or repealing all or any of the provisions of this Order in the same manner as any other laws for the good Government of the colony.
Precedent	844.
Precedent	Section 5 of the Colonial Laws Validity Act gave the legislature full power to alter the Constitution.
Precedent	845.
Precedent	Section 2 of the Constitution Act of 1867 gave the legislature power to make laws for the peace, welfare and good government of the Colony.
Precedent	Section 9 of the Constitution required a two thirds majority of the legislative <ORG> and <ORG> as a condition precedent of the validity of legislation altering the Constitution of the Legislative <ORG>.
Precedent	Section 6 Sub-section (6) which authorised an appointment as a Judge of <ORG> only during the period dur ing which the person appointed was a Judge of <ORG> was found to be valid legislation.
Precedent	It was found that the Constitution of Queensland was a flexiable as distinct from rigid Constitution.
Precedent	Power to alter the Constitution by ordinary law was also said to exist both in virtue of the Colonial Laws Validity Act, 1865 Section 5 and independently of that Act in virtue of Clause 22 of the Order in Council 1859 and Sections 2 and 9 of the Constitution Act of 1867.
Precedent	846.
Precedent	The decision in <NAME> case shows that unless there is a special procedure prescribed for amending any part of the Constitution the Constitution is uncontrolled and can be amended by the manner prescribed for enacting an ordinary law and therefore a subsequent law inconsistent with the Constitution would pro tanto repeal the Constitution.
Precedent	The decision also established that a Constitution largely or generally uncontrolled may contain one or more provisions which prescribe a different procedure for amending the provisions  of the Constitution.
Precedent	If this is prescribed the procedure for amendment must be strictly followed.
Precedent	847.
Precedent	The legislature of Queensland was found to be master of its own household except in so far as its powers were restricted in special cases.
Precedent	No such restriction was established in the case before <ORG>.
Precedent	The legislature had plenary power there.
Precedent	The legislature was not required to follow any particular procedure or to comply with any specified conditions before it made any law inconsistent with any of the provisions of Constitutional document.
Precedent	848.
Precedent	The contention of the respondent in <NAME> case was that the Constitution of Queensland was controlled and that it could not be altered merely by enacting legislation inconsistent with its Articles but that it could be altered by an Act which in plain and unmistakable intention of the legislature to alter consequently gave effect to that intention by operative provisions.
Precedent	<ORG> thought this Constitution would amount to a Constitution  which was neither controlled nor uncontrolled.
Precedent	It was not controlled because the future generation could by a merely formal Act correct it at pleasure.
Precedent	It was said to be not uncontrolled because the framers prescribed to their successors a particular mode by which they are allowed to effect Constitutional changes.
Precedent	Section 22 of the Order in Council conferred power and authority in legislature from time to time to make laws altering or repealing all or any of the provisions of the Order in Council in the same manner as any other laws for the good government of the country.
Precedent	The Constitution Act of 1867 was contended to enact certain fundamental organic provisions of such a nature as to render the Constitution controlled.
Precedent	It was found impossible to point to any document or instruction giving or imposing on the Constitution of Queensland such a quality.
Precedent	The decision in <NAME> case related to uncontrolled Constitution which gave the legislature full power to make laws except on one subject and, therefore, a law  made by the legislature under such a Constitution could pro tanto conflict with and repeal the Constitution.
Precedent	That is not our Constitution.
Precedent	849.
Precedent	In Ranasinghe case the validity of the appointment of <ORG> was challenged.
Precedent	<ORG> took the view that the <ORG> was not appointed by <ORG> in accordance with the provisions of Section 55 of the Ceylon Constitution Order in Council.
Precedent	It was, therefore, not lawfully appointed.
Precedent	It was common ground that the appointment of the <ORG> was not in accordance with Section 55 of the Ceylon Constitution Order in Council, 1946.
Precedent	Section 55 vested in <ORG>er the appointment, dismissal and disciplinary control of Judicial Officers, viz., Judges of lesser rank.
Precedent	The removal of Judges of <ORG> could be by the Governor General on an address of the <ORG> and <ORG>.
Precedent	850.
Statute	Section 29 of the Ceylon (Constitution) Order in Council provided in Sub-secti ons (1), (2), (3) and (4) as follows:  29(1) Subject to the provisions of this Order, <ORG> shall have power to make laws for the peace, order and good government of the Island.
Statute	(2) No such law shall-(a) prohibit or restrict the free exercise of any religion;  (3) Any law made in contravention of Sub-section (2) of this section shall, to the extent of such contravention, be void.
Statute	(4) In the exercise of its powers under this section <ORG> may amend or repeal any of the provisions of this Order, or of any other Order of Her Majesty in Council in its application to the Island:  Provided that no Bill for the amendment or repeal of any of the provisions of this Order shall be presented for the Royal Assent unless it has endorsed on it a certificate under the hand of the Speaker that the number of votes cast in favour thereof in <ORG> amounted to not less than two-thirds of the whole number of Members of the <ORG> (including those not present).
Precedent	Every certificate of the Speaker und er this sub-section shall be conclusive for all purposes and shall not be questioned in any court of law.
Precedent	851.
Precedent	<ORG> found that there was a conflict between Section 55 of the Ceylon <ORG>nstitution Order and Section 41 of the Bribery Amendment Act.
Precedent	<ORG> found that Section 29(4) of the order was attracted but the requirements of Section 29(4) had not been complied with and, therefore, the appointment of <ORG> was invalid.
Precedent	The certificate of the Speaker under the proviso to Section 29(4) of the Ceylon <ORG>nstitution Order was an essential part of the legislative process.
Precedent	There was no such certificate in the case of the legislation under which the appointment of the impugned <ORG> was made.
Precedent	<ORG> said that a legislature has no power to ignore the conditions of law making that are imposed by the regulating instrument.
Precedent	This restriction exists independently of the question whether the legislature is sovereign as the legislature of Ceylon or whether the <ORG> nstitution is uncontrolled as happened in <NAME> case with regard to the Constitution of Queensland.
Precedent	852.
Precedent	<ORG> said "A Constitution can, indeed, be altered or amended by the legislature, if the regulating instrument so provides and if the terms of those provisions are complied with; and the alteration or amendment may include the change or abolition of these provisions.
Precedent	But the proposition which is not acceptable is that a legislature, once established, has some inherent power derived from the mere fact of its establishment to make a valid law by the resolution of a bare majority which its own constituent instrument has said shall not be valid law unless made by a different type of majority or by a different legislative process".
Precedent	853.
Precedent	It was contended that just as the legislature of the Colony of Queensland had power by mere majority vote to pass an Act that was inconsistent with the provisions of the existing Constitution of that Colony as to the tenure of <ORG> so the legisla ture of Ceylon had no less a power to depart from the requirements of a section such as Section 55 of the Ceylon Constitution, notwithstanding the wording of Sections 18 and 29(4) .
Precedent	Section 18 in effect says that a legislation can be passed by a majority of votes subject to the provisions in Section 29(4) of the Constitution.
Precedent	<ORG> said that in <NAME> case the legislature had full power to make laws by a majority except upon one subject that was not in question and the legislation was held to be valid because it was treated as pro tanto an alternation of the Constitution which was neither fundamental in the sense of being beyond change nor so constituted as to require any special process to pass a law upon the topic dealt with.
Precedent	The word "fundamental" in the sense of "being beyond change" refers to express limitations as to power or manner and form of change.
Precedent	These words do not mean as Mr. <NAME> contended that there are fundamental features of the Constitution which cannot be amended.
Precedent	854.
Precedent	The legislature purported to pass a law which being in conflict with Section 55 of the Order in Council must be treated if it is to be valid, as an implied alteration of the Constitutional provisions about the appointment of judicial officers.
Precedent	Such alterations could only be made by laws which complied with the special legislative procedure laid down in Section 29(4) .
Precedent	The provisions in Section 29(4) were found not to confer on the Ceylon legislature the general power to legislature so as to amend the Constitution by ordinary majority resolution which the Queensland legislature was found to have under Section 2 of the Queensland Constitution <ORG>  855.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> case shows that <ORG> which by its own <ORG> imposed procedural conditions upon the legislative process is no more limited or non-sovereign than a legislature which has such conditions imposed on it by the Constitutional instrument.
RatioOfTheDecision	A Constitutional instrument which places procedural restraints upon the forms of law making places the legislatur e under a compulsion to obey them.
RatioOfTheDecision	In <NAME> case it was said that <ORG> with plenary powers could treat the Constitutional document which defined its powers as if it were a Dog Act This proposition as a result of <NAME> case is narrowed to the extent that where provisions for procedural special majority are laid down in the Constitutional document they cannot be treated as a provision in the Dog Act might be.
RatioOfTheDecision	856.
RatioOfTheDecision	These decisions indicate the distinction between procedural and substantive limitations on the legislative process.
RatioOfTheDecision	In <NAME> case the issue was one of personal liberty in the sense that the respondent claimed the right not to be imprisoned except by a valid law.
RatioOfTheDecision	No question was raised about the right of religion protected by Sections 29(2) and (3) of the Ceylon Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It was also not the respondent's case there that any provision was unamendable.
RatioOfTheDecision	It would be unusual for <ORG> to say by way of an obiter dictum that a provision was not amendable contr ary to the respondent's submission.
RatioOfTheDecision	Though <ORG> did not use the words "legislative and constituent" in distinguishing ordinary law from law amending the Constitution, <ORG> in referring to the <ORG> instrument showed that the familiar distinction is the basis of the judgment.
RatioOfTheDecision	857.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> is dealing with Section 29 took note of the special heading under which Section 29 appears in the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	That special heading is "legislative power and procedure".
RatioOfTheDecision	The opening words of Section 29 are that subject to the provisions of this order <ORG> shall have powers to make laws.
RatioOfTheDecision	These are similar to the opening words in Article 245 of our Constitution.
Precedent	Section 18 of the <ORG> prescribes the ordinary legislative procedure for making laws by a bare majority unless otherwise provided for by the Constitution, which is to be found in Section 29(4) of the <ORG>.
Precedent	Our Constitution in Article 100 makes an identical provision for ordinary legi slative procedure.
Precedent	Section 29(2) confers rights of freedom of religion and Section 29(3) states that no laws shall be made prohibiting or restricting such freedom.
Precedent	Part III of our Constitution contains among other fundamental rights, rights to freedom of religion.
Precedent	Section 29(3) expressly makes laws in contravention of Section 29(2) void to the extent of contravention.
Precedent	Article 13(2) of our Constitution expressly makes law which takes away or abridges fundamental rights void to the extent of the contravention.
Precedent	Section 29(4) of the Ceylon Constitution dealing with the amendment of the Constitution does not expressly make void a law amending the Constitution.
Precedent	858.
Precedent	It follows from <NAME> case and <NAME> case that a legislature has no power to ignore the conditions of law making imposed upon it which regulate its power to make law.
Precedent	The Ceylon legislature had no general power to legislate so as to amend its general power by ordinary majority resolution such as Queensland legislature was found to have under Se ction 2 of the Queensland Constitution.
Precedent	Peace, order and good government in Section 29(1) of the Ceylon Constitution is not the same as amendment contemplated in Section 29(4) of the Ceylon Constitution.
Precedent	In Ranasinghe case <ORG> referred to the social compact.
Precedent	The compact is this.
Precedent	The inhabitants of Ceylon accepted the Ceylon Constitution on the footing that the various rights conferred, liabilities imposed and duties prescribed under the law cannot be altered in the ordinary course of legislation by a bare majority.
Precedent	But if all these were to be changed then such a change could only be made under the strongest safeguard of the amending process which in the case of Ceylon was not less than two-third of the absolute membership.
Precedent	These rights are the solemn compact.
Precedent	These valuable rights are conferred on the people.
Precedent	Under ordinary law by ordinary majority they cannot be taken away.
Precedent	859.
Precedent	The absence of an express provision in Section 29(4) of the Ceylon Constitution that an amendment of the Consti tution in contravention of the terms of that sub-section shall be void need not support the conclusion that such an amendment was valid.
Precedent	Section 29(1) of the Ceylon Constitution is expressed to be "subject to the provisions of this Order" and any power under Section 29(4) is expressly subject to the proviso there.
Precedent	<ORG> held that the opening words of Section 29 introduced into the Constitution of Ceylon the necessarily implied doctrine of ultra vires.
Precedent	The proposition will apply directly to the same opening words of our Article 245.
Precedent	<ORG> accepted the distinction made in <NAME> case between controlled and uncontrolled Constitutions by emphasising the observation in <NAME> case with reference to Section 9 of the Queensland Constitution.
Precedent	The description of Section 29(2) of the Ceylon Constitution as an entrenched provision means that it can be amended but only by special procedure in Section 29(4) .
RatioOfTheDecision	That is the meaning of the word "entrenched".
RatioOfTheDecision	This meaning alone is consistent with th e clear language of the amending power and also with the decision.
Precedent	Section 29(4) does not limit the sovereignty of the Ceylon legislature because the legislature can always pass the amendment after getting two-thirds majority and the certificate.
Precedent	860.
Precedent	Counsel for the respondent in Ranasinghe case stated that there was no limitation except the procedure and even that limitation could be removed by amendment complying with Sub-section (4).
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> affirmed that position.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is nothing to prevent by appropriate amendment a deletion of Section 29(4) of the Ceylon Constitution which would then empower <ORG> to achieve the power to amend by an ordinary majority.
RatioOfTheDecision	Section 29(1) is not legislative power alone but a composite power when read along with Section 29(4) in the context of the Ceylon Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It includes both legislative and constituent power.
RatioOfTheDecision	Sub-sections (2) and (3) of Section 29 are not the grant of power but limitation on power.
RatioOfTheDecision	Its terms show that limitation is at any rate on the  legislative power of enacting laws contrary to Sub-sections (2) and (3) of Section 29 .
RatioOfTheDecision	If Section 29(1) is a composite legislative and constituent power and Sub-section (2) and (3) are a restraint on legislative power the constituent power under Sub-section (4) remains unaffected.
RatioOfTheDecision	The sequiter is that Section 29(4) is consistent only with the view that so far as amendment of Sub-sections (2) and (3) is concerned amendment is permited and there is no limitation on constituent power under Section 29(4) .
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> took the widest view of the amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	In fact the narrower view was not argued.
Precedent	861.
Precedent	Our Constitution in Article 13(2) by its express declaration with reference to law and the <ORG> widely defined has no higher efficacy in rendering a law in contravention of its terms void than the opening words of Article 245 have in rendering a law void in contravention of term mentioned therein.
Precedent	Therefore, in treating Article 13(2) as having that effect in regard to Constitutional amendment the maj ority judgment in <NAME> case was inept.
Precedent	In rejecting the distinction between legislative and constitutent powers the leading majority view in <NAME> case was induced by the absence of the use of the labels but the same concepts were clearly indicated by <ORG> by wholly describing the characteristic features of legislative and constituent powers.
Precedent	862.
Precedent	If Article 368 had begun with a non-obstante clause it could not have been said that amendment under Article 368 would be law within the meaning of Article 13(2).
Precedent	The Attorney General rightly said that there is no non-obstante clause in Article 368 because of the quality of amending power and because the amending power is a constituent power and not ordinary legislative power.
RatioOfTheDecision	This is the position of the amending clause in a written Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	When the power under Article 368 is exercised <ORG> acts as a recreation of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, such power cannot be restricted by or widened by any other provision.
RatioOfTheDecision	As soon as an  amendment is made it becomes a part of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	An amendment prevails over the Article or Articles amended.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fact that Article 368 confers constitutent powers is apparent from the special conditions prescribed in the Article.
RatioOfTheDecision	Those conditions are different from ordinary law making process.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 puts restraints on the ordinary law making process and thus confers constituent power.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> was fully aware that if any limitation was to be put on the amending power the limitation would have to be expressly provided for.
Precedent	Article 305 of the Draft Constitution provided reservation of seats for certain sections of people in the legislature for 10 years.
Precedent	This reservation was not accepted by <ORG>.
Precedent	This shows that if <ORG> or <ORG> wanted to exclude fundamenal rights from the operation of Article 368 corresponding to Article 304 in the Draft Constitution they could have expressly done so.
Precedent	863.
Precedent	<CITATION_924262> (1967 2 S.C.R.
Precedent	27 1 it was said there was a distinction between deprivation of fundamental rights by force of a Constitutional provision itself and such deprivation by an order made by President in exercise of a power conferred on him under Constitutional provision.
Precedent	The dissenting view in <NAME> case was that an order of the President was not a law within the meaning of Article 13(2).
Precedent	<CITATION_1436792> (1968) 2 S.C.R.
Precedent	227 the majority view of <ORG> was that an order of the President under Article 359 was not law within the meaning of Article 13(2).
Precedent	There is no distinction between Article 358 and Article 359(1).
Precedent	Article 358 by its own force suspends the fundamental rights guaranteed by Article 19.
Precedent	Article 359(1) on the other hand does not suspend any fundamental rights of its own force but it gives force to order by the President declaring suspension of the enforcement of any fundamental right during the period of emergency.
Precedent	In <NAME> case it was said that it could not mean that an order under A rticle 359(1) suspending the enforcement of a particular fundamental right had still to be tested under the vary fundamental right which it suspended.
Precedent	<NAME> case establishes that the expression "law" in Article 13(2) is not all embracing in spite of the exclusive definition of law in Article 13(3)(a).
Precedent	864.
Precedent	The word "law" appears in various Articles of our Constitution but not in Article  368.
Precedent	The reason is that the power under Article 368 is not a power to make ordinary laws under the Constitution but is the constituent power.
Precedent	There could be no law within the meaning of Article 13(2) at any stage before the amendment became a part of the Constitution under Article 368.
Precedent	There is no hiatus between an amendment being a law and thereafter a part of the Constitution.
Precedent	Immediately upon the passage of the Bill for the amendment the Constitution stands amended.
Precedent	865.
Precedent	The historical background of Article 13(2) throws some light on the question as to whether Article 13(2) prevails over Article 368.
Precedent	On 17 March,  1947 the Constitutional Advisor Sir <NAME> had addressed a letter to the members of Central and Provincial legislatures.
Precedent	A questionnaire was annexed to that letter.
Precedent	Question No.
Precedent	27 was "What provisions should be made regarding amendments to the Constitution".
Precedent	A note was appended to that question which will be found in Shiva Rao Framing of India's Constitution referred to as Shiva Rao Vol.
Precedent	II pp.
Precedent	448-451.
Precedent	The methods of amendment of Constitution in the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, United States of America, Switzerland and Ireland were elucidated in that note.
Precedent	The note also drew attention that the fact that in various Constitution express limitations were put on amending certain provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	The portion of the note relating to the Constitution of Australia indicated such limitations.
Precedent	866.
Precedent	The draft report of the sub-Committee on fundamental rights dated 3 April 1947 contained an annexure which dealt with fundamental rights.
Precedent	See Shiva Rao Vol.
Precedent	II p. 137 seq.
Precedent	Clause 2 of the annexure w as as follows:  Any law or usage in force within the territories of the Union immediately before the commencement of this Constitution and any law which may hereafter be made by the <ORG> inconsistent with the provisions of this Chapter/Constitution shall be void to the extent of such inconsistency.
Precedent	867.
Precedent	The Constitutional Adviser suggested that the word "Constitution" was preferable to the word "chapter" because the entire Constitution was to prevail over law.
Precedent	868.
Precedent	On 23 April, 1947 <ORG> presented an interim report addressed to the President of <ORG> containing an annexure providing for justiciable fundamental rights.
Precedent	See Shiva Rao Vol.
Precedent	II pp.
Precedent	294-296 seq.
Precedent	Clause 2 of the Annexure to that report was as follows:  All existing laws, notification, regulations, customs or usages in force within the territories of the Union inconsistent with the rights guaranteed under this part of the Constitution shall stand abrogated to the extent of such inconsisten cy nor shall the <ORG> or any unit may make any law taking away or abridging any such right.
Precedent	869.
Statute	Clause 2 of the annexure to the interim report was discussed in <ORG> on 29 April, 1947.
Statute	<NAME> moved an amendment to Clause 2.
Statute	The amendment was as follows : In Clause 2 for the words "nor shall the <ORG> or any unit make any law taking away or abridging any such right" the following be substituted: "Nor shall any such right be taken away or abridged except by an amendment of the Constitution".
Precedent	The amendment was accepted as will appear in Constituent Assembly Debates Vol.
Precedent	III p. 416.
Precedent	870.
Precedent	In October, 1947 the Draft Constitution was prepared by the Constitutional Advisor.
Precedent	Clause 9(2) of the said Draft Constitution which later on corresponded to Article 13(2) of our Constitution was as follows:  Nothing in this Constitution shall be taken to empower the <ORG> to make any law which curtails or taking away any of the rights conferred by Chapter II of this Part except by way of amendme nt of this Constitution under Section 232 and any law made in contravention of this sub-section shall, to the extent of the Contravention, be void.
Precedent	871.
Precedent	It will be seen that Clause 9(2) in the Draft Constitution included the qualification "except by way of amendment of the Constitution under Section 232 ".
Precedent	Clause 232 in the Draft Constitution prepared by the Constitutional Advisor became Article 304 in the Constitution prepared by <ORG> and eventually became Article 368 of our Constitution.
Precedent	In <NAME>, Vol.
Precedent	III p. 325 it appears that <ORG> on 30 October, 1947 at a meeting gave a note forming the minutes of that meeting that Clause 9(2) should be revised as follows:  The <ORG> shall not make any law which takes away or abridges the rights conferred by this Part and any law made in contravention of this sub- section shall, to the extent of the contravention, be void.
Precedent	872.
RatioOfTheDecision	No reason is recorded in these minutes as to why the resolution adopted by <ORG> by  passing <NAME> amendment was disregarded.
RatioOfTheDecision	No indication was given in the forwarding letter of Dr. <NAME> in the Note appended thereto as to why the amendment of <NAME> which had been accepted by <ORG> was deleted.
RatioOfTheDecision	Nor does the Draft Constitution indicate either by sidelines or in any other manner that the decision of <ORG> had been disregarded.
RatioOfTheDecision	873.
RatioOfTheDecision	This history of the formation and framing of Article 13(2) shows that the intention of <ORG> was that Article 13(2) does not control the Article relating to the amending of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It must be assumed that <ORG> consisting of eminent men considered that an express exclusion of the amending Article from the operation of the clause corresponding to Article 13(2) was unnecessary and the fear that that Article would cover the amending Article was groundless.
RatioOfTheDecision	It also appears that no discussion took place after the Draft Constitution had been presented to the Constit uent Assembly by Dr. <NAME> on the deletion or disregard of <NAME> amendment.
Precedent	The history of Article 13(2) shows that <ORG> clearly found that it did not apply to an amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	874.
Precedent	The distinction between constitutent and legislative power in a written Constitution is of enormous magnitude.
Precedent	No provision of the Constitution can be declared void because the Constitution is the touchstone of validity.
Precedent	There is no touchstone of validity outside the Constitution.
Precedent	Every provision in a controlled Constitution is essential or so thought by the framers because of the protection of being amendable only in accordance with the Constitution.
Precedent	Every Article has that protection.
Precedent	The historical background of Article 13(2) indicates that the Constitution-makers dealt separately with legislative power by providing for the same in Part XI and entrusted the constituent power to authorities mentioned in Article 368 and that authority has the same power as the Constituent Assembl y because it has not put any fetter upon it.
Precedent	The draft Article 305 which provided for a limitation as to time for amendment of certain matters was eventually deleted.
Precedent	If the framers of the Constitution wanted to forbid something they would say so.
Precedent	875.
Precedent	The vitality of the constituent power not only indicates that the Constitution is in the words of <NAME> the suprema potestas but also the fact that the amending power is put in a separate Article and Part of the Constitution establishing that it deals with a topic other than legislative power and the power is meant to be exhaustive leaving nothing uncovered.
Precedent	The vary fact that amending power is not put in any legislative power or is not attached to a subject which is the subject matter of legislative power leaving aside the four sets of provisions, namely, Articles 4, 169, paragraph 7 Schedule 5 and paragraph 21 Schedule 6 containing specific power of amendment shows that that amending power was meant to be exhaustive and plenary.
Precedent	If a power of amendment wi thout any express limitation was given it was because a legal Constitutional way of bringing a change in the Constitution was desirable or necessary.
Precedent	Otherwise there would be no legal way of effecting the change.
