Precedent	It cannot be attributed to the framers of the Constitution that they intended that the Constitution or any part of it could be changed by unConstitutional or illegal methods.
Precedent	876.
Precedent	If an amendment of the Constitution is made subject to Article 13(2) the necessary conclusion then is that no amendment of the Constitution is possible.
Precedent	The opening words of Article 245 which deals with legislative power indicate that any law made under Article 246(1) read with List I of the Seventh Schedule is subject to the limitations on legislative power imposed by all the Articles in the Constitution.
Precedent	These limitations cannot be altered or amended in exercise of legislative power, if the power of amendment is said to be located in the Residuary Entry 97 in List I.
Precedent	The history of residuary power in <ORG> dia Act, 1935 whose scheme was adopted in the Constitution shows that the topic of amendment was not only present to the mind of <ORG> but also that the Constituent power could not reside in the residuary power.
Precedent	877.
Precedent	The conclusions on the question as to whether Article 13(2) overrides Article 368 are these.
Precedent	Article 13(2) relates to laws under the Constitution.
Precedent	Laws under the Constitution are governed by Article 13 (2).
Precedent	Article 368 relates to power and procedure of amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	Upon amendment of the Constitution the Constitution shall stand amended.
Precedent	The Constitution is self validating and self executing.
Precedent	Article 13(2) does not override Article 368.
Precedent	Article 13(2) is not a fundamental right.
Precedent	The Constitution is the touchstone.
Precedent	The constituent power is sui generis.
Precedent	The majority view in <NAME> case that Article 13(2) prevails over Article 368 was on the basis that there was no distinction between constituent and legislative power and an amendment of the Constitution wa s law and that such law attracted the opening words of Article 245 which in its turn attracted the provisions of Article 13(2).
Precedent	<ORG> took notice of the two conflicting views which had been taken of the unamended Article 368 , took notice of the fact that the preponderating judicial opinion, namely, the decisions in Shankari Prasad case <NAME> case and the minority views of five learned Judges in <NAME> case were in favour of the view that Article 368 contained the power of amendment and that power was the constituent power belonging to <ORG> <NAME>, <NAME> rightly said in <NAME> case that the power under Article 368 is a constituent power to change the fundamental law, that is to say, the Constitution and is distinct from ordinary legislative power.
Precedent	So long as this distinction is kept in mind <ORG> will have power under Article 368 to amend the Constitution and what <ORG> does under Article 368 is not ordinary law making which is subject to Article 13(2) or any other Article of  the Constitution.
Precedent	This view of <NAME>, J. was adopted by <ORG> in the Constitution 24th Amendment Act which made explicit that under Article 368 <ORG> has the constituent power to amend this Constitution.
Precedent	878.
Precedent	In order to appreciate and assess Mr. <NAME>'s other contention of implied and inherent limitations on the amending power, it is necessary to find out the necessity and importance of the amending power to arrive at the true meaning of the expression "amendment".
Precedent	879.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> made these submissions.
Precedent	The word "amendment" means on the one hand not the power to alter or destroy the essential features and on the other there are inherent and implied limitations on the power of amendment.
Precedent	It is imperative to consider the consequences of the plea of limited power and also of the plea of limitless power.
Precedent	The test of the true width of a power is not how probable it is that it may be exercised, but what can possibly be done under it.
Precedent	The hope and expectation that it will never be used is n ot relevant.
Precedent	Reliance is placed on the observations in Maxwell on the Interpretation of Statutes, 12th Ed.
Precedent	(1969) pp.
Precedent	103-106 that it is important to consider the effects or consequences which would result from it, for they often point out the real meaning of the words, before adopting any proposed construction of a passage susceptible of more than one meaning.
Precedent	The reasonableness of the consequences which follow from a particular construction on the one hand and the unreasonable result on the other are the two alternatives in the quest for the true intention of <ORG>.
Precedent	Crawford Construction of Statutes (1940 Ed.)
Precedent	pp.
Precedent	286-290 was referred to for the proposition that where the statute is ambiguous or susceptible to more than one meaning, the construction which tends to make the statute unreasonable should be avoided.
Precedent	Uncertainty, friction or confusion on a construction is to be avoided because preference is to be given to the smooth working of the statute.
Precedent	The <ORG> adopts which is just reasonable and sensi ble rather than that which is none of these things.
Precedent	It is not to be presumed that the legislature intended the legislation to produce inequitable results.
Precedent	Usurpation of power contrary to the Constitution is to be avoided.
Precedent	880.
Precedent	Reliance was placed by Mr. <NAME> on American Jurisprudence 2d.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	16 Article 59 at pp.
Precedent	231-232, Article 72 at p. 251, Article 287 at pp.
Precedent	270-71 and Article 88 at pp.
Precedent	273-74 in support of these propositions.
Precedent	First, questions of Constitutional construction are in the main governed by the same general principles which control in ascertaining the meaning of all written instruments particularly statutes.
Precedent	External aids or arbitrary rules applied to the construction of a Constitution are of uncertain value and should be used with hesitation and circumspection.
Precedent	Second, Constitutions are general and many of the essentials with which Constitutions treat are impliedly controlled or dealt with by them and implication plays a very important part in Constitutional construction.
Precedent	What is implie d is as much a part of the instrument as what is expressed.
Precedent	Third, a <ORG> may look to the history of the times and examine the state of things existing when the Constitution was framed and adopted.
Precedent	The <ORG> should look to the nature and object of the particular powers, duties and rights in question with all the light and aids of the contemporary history.
Precedent	Fourth, proceedings of conventions and debates are of limited value as explaining doubtful phrases.
Precedent	Similarly, the opinions of the individual members are seldom considered as of material value.
Precedent	881.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> said that the word "amend" may have three meanings.
Precedent	First, it may mean to improve or better to remove an error, the quality of improvement being considered from the stand point of the basic philosophy underlying the Constitution.
Precedent	Second, it may mean to make changes which may not fall within the first meaning but which do not alter or destroy any of the basic essential or any of the essential features of the Constitution.
Precedent	Third, it may mean to mak e any changes in the Constitution including changes falling outside the second meaning.
Precedent	The first meaning was preferred.
Precedent	The second was said to be a possible construction.
Precedent	The third was ruled out.
Precedent	882.
Precedent	The crux of the matter is the meaning of the word "amendment" The <ORG> meaning of the word is to make professed improvements in a measure before <ORG>; formally, to alter in detail, though practically it may be to alter its principle, so as to thwart it.
Precedent	The <ORG> meanings are also alteration of a bill before <ORG>; a clause, paragraph, or words proposed to be substituted for others, or to be inserted in a bill (the result of the adoption of which may even be to defeat the measure).
Precedent	In Words and Phrases Permanent Edition, Volume 3 the meaning of the word "amend" and "amendment" are change or alteration.
Precedent	Amendment involves an alteration or change, as by addition, taking away or modification.
Precedent	A broad definition of the word "amendment" will include any alteration or change.
Precedent	The  word "amendment" when used in connection with the Constitution may refer to the addition of a provision on a new independent subject, complete in itself and wholly disconnected from other provisions, or to some particular article or clause, and is then used to indicate an addition to, the striking out, or some change in that particular article or clause.
Precedent	883.
Precedent	The contention that the word "amendment" in Article 368 should bear a limited meaning in view of the expression "amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any of the provisions of this Schedule" occurring in paragraphs 7 and 21 in Schedules 5 and 6, is unsound for the following reasons.
Precedent	884.
Precedent	First, the power of amendment conferred by the four provisions, namely, Article 4 read with Articles 2 and 3, Article 169 , paragraphs 7 and 21 in Schedules 5 and 6 is a limited power.
Precedent	It is limited to specific subjects.
Precedent	The exercise of the power of amendment under those four provisions, if treated by Articles themselves, is an uncontrolled power since the powe r can be exercised by an ordinary law.
Precedent	But as a part of the Constitution the power is a subordinate power because these Articles themselves are subject to the amending provisions of Article 368.
Precedent	Article 368 is the only provision of the Constitution which provides for the amendment of this Constitution which means the Constitution of India and every part hereto.
Precedent	It may be mentioned that in construing Article 368 the title of the part "Amendment of the Constitution" is an important aid to construction.
Precedent	The marginal note which speaks of the procedure of amendment is not complete by itself because the procedure when followed results in the product, namely, an amendment of the Constitution which is not only a matter of procedure.
Precedent	885.
Precedent	Second, these four provisions which are in the same terms, namely, "no such law shall be deemed to be an amendment of this Constitution for the purpose of Article 368" show that but for these terms the amendment would have fallen within Article 368 and was being taken out of it Thi s is an important consideration particularly in connection with Schedules 5 and 6 which provide that <ORG> may, from time to time by law, amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any of the provisions of this Schedule.
Precedent	These provisions show that an amendment by way of addition, variation or repeal will also fall within the amendment of the Constitution provided for in Article 368 but is being taken out of Article 368.
Precedent	This express exclusion contains intrinsic evidence that the meaning of the word "amendment" in Article 368 includes amendment by way of addition, alteration or repeal.
Precedent	886.
Precedent	Third, paragraphs 7 and 21 in Schedules 5 and 6 which provide that <ORG> may from time to time by law, amend by way of addition, variation or repeal indicate the necessity of amendments from time to time.
Precedent	The expression "by way of" does not enlarge the meaning of the word "amendment" but clarifies.
Precedent	The expression "by way of" shows that the words addition, variation or repeal are substitutes of the word "amendm ent" and are forms of intention.
Precedent	The whole Schedule cannot be repealed either by paragraph 7 or by paragraph 21, because Article 244 provides for the administration of Scheduled Areas and tribal areas on the application of the two respective Schedules.
Precedent	The words "from time to time" also indicate that because of subject matter amendments may be from time to time.
Precedent	The history behind the two Schedules originates in Section 91 and 92 of the Government of India Act, 1935 dealing with excluded areas and partially excluded areas.
Precedent	887.
Precedent	Fourth, reference was made to Section 9(1)(c) of the India Independence Act 1947 which empowered the Governor General to make omissions from, additions to and adaptations and modification to the Government of India Act, 1935.
Precedent	The Government of India Third Amendment Act 1949 amended Section 291 of the 1935 Act and empowered the Governor General to make such amendments as he considers necessary whether by way of addition, modification or repeal.
Precedent	It was, therefore, said that when our Con stitution did not use the expression "by way of addition, modification or repeal" the word "amendment" in Article 368 will have a narrower meaning.
Precedent	The expression "amendment" has been used in several Articles of the Constitution.
Precedent	These are Articles 4(1) and (2), 108(4), 109(3), and 4, 111, 114(2), 169(2), 196(2), 198(3) and (4), 200, 201, 204(2), 207(1), (2), 240(2), 274(1), 304(b) and 349.
Precedent	In every case amendment is to be by way of variation, addition or repeal.
Precedent	Again, different expression have been used in other Articles.
Precedent	In Article 35(b) the words are alter, repeal.
Precedent	In Article 243(1) the words are repeal or amend.
Precedent	In Article 252(2 ), the expression is amend or repeal.
Precedent	In Article 254(2) proviso the words are add to, amending, variation or repeal.
Precedent	In Article 320(4) the words are such modifications whether by way of repeal or amendment.
Precedent	In Article 372(1) the words are altered or repealed or amended.
Precedent	In Article 372(2) the words are such adaptations and modifications by way of repeal or amendment.
Precedent	In Article 39 2(1) the expression is such adaptations by way of modification, addition or commission.
Precedent	Again, in Article 241(2) the words are modification or exceptions.
Precedent	In Article 364 the words used are exceptions or modifications.
Precedent	In Article 370(1)(d) and (3) the words are modifications and exceptions.
Precedent	Again, in Schedule 5 paragraph 5(1) and Schedule 6 paragraphs 12(a), (b), 19(1)(a) the word used are exceptions or modifications.
Precedent	Modifications in Article 370(1)(d) must be given the widest meaning in the context of a Constitution and in that sense it includes an amendment and it cannot be limited to such modifications as do not make any radical transformation.
Precedent	888.
Precedent	The several Constitution Amendment Act s show that amendments to the Constitution are made by way of addition, substitution, repeal.
Precedent	The Attorney General is right in his submission that the expression "amendment of this Constitution" has a clear substantive meaning in the context of a written Constitution and it means that any part of the Constitution can be am ended by changing the same either by variation, addition of repeal.
Precedent	889.
Precedent	The words "Amendment of this Constitution may be initiated" and the words "Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill" in Article 368 indicate that the word "amendment" is used in an unambiguous and clear manner.
Precedent	The Attorney General said that our Constitution is not the first nor is the last one to use the word "amendment".
Precedent	The American Constitution in 1787 used the word "amend".
Precedent	Several Constitutions of other countries have used the word "amend".
Precedent	The word "amend" is used in a Constitution to mean any kind of change.
Precedent	In some Constitutions the words alteration or revision have been used in place of the word amend or along with the word amendment.
Precedent	Some times alteration and revision of the Constitution are also spoken of as amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	890.
Precedent	Constitutional provisions are presumed to have been carefully and deliberately framed.
Precedent	The words alterations or amendments, the words amendments or re visions, the words revision and alteration are used together to indicate that these words have the same meaning in relation to amendment and change in Constitution.
Precedent	891.
Precedent	The meaning and scope of amending power is in the object and necessity for amendment in a written Constitution.
Precedent	892.
Precedent	The various amendments which have already been carried out to our Constitution indicate that provisions have been added, or varied or substituted.
Precedent	The Attorney General gave two correct reasons for the object and necessity of the power of amendment in a written Constitution.
Precedent	First, the object and necessity of amendment in a written Constitution means that the necessity is for changing the Constitution in an orderly manner, for otherwise the Constitution can be changed only by an extra Constitutional method or by revolution, Second, the very object of amendment is to make changes in the fundamental law or organic law to make fundamental changes in the Constitution, to change the fundamental or the basic principles in the Consti tution.
Precedent	Otherwise there will be no necessity to give that importance to the high amending power to avoid revolution.
Precedent	893.
Precedent	The object of amendment is to see that the Constitution is preserved.
Precedent	Rebellion or revolution is an illegal channel of giving expression to change.
Precedent	The "consent of the governed" is that each generation has a right to establish its own law.
Precedent	Conditions change.
Precedent	Men Change, Opportunities for corresponding change in political institutions and principles of Government therefore arise.
Precedent	An unamendable Constitution was the French Constitution which by an amendment to the Constitution adopted in 1884 declared that <ORG> shall never entertain a proposal for abolition of the republican form of Government.
Precedent	The United <ORG>s Constitution provided that no amendment could be made prior to 1808 affecting the First and Fourth Clauses of Section 9 of Article 1 relative to the prohibition of the importation of slaves, and that no <ORG> without its consent shall be deprived of equal suffrage i n the <ORG>.
Precedent	These are examples of limiting the sovereign power of the people to change the Constitution.
Precedent	894.
Precedent	An unamendable Constitution is said to be the worst tyranny of time.
Precedent	<NAME> said in 1789 that each generation has a right to determine a law under which it lives.
Precedent	The earth belongs in usufruct to the living; the dead have neither powers nor rights over it.
Precedent	The machinery of amendment is like a safety valve.
Precedent	It should not be used with too great facility nor should be too difficult.
Precedent	That will explode and erode the Constitution.
Precedent	895.
Precedent	Most Constitutions are rigid in the sense that they are amendable only by a different process than that by which ordinary laws may be altered.
Precedent	Thus they distinguish clearly between the constituent power and the legislative power, each being exerciseable by different organs according to different processes.
Precedent	Chief Justice <NAME> said that the opponents of change want changes just as much as any one else.
Precedent	They want however to determine what the changes shall be.
Precedent	896.
Precedent	A mendment is a form of growth of the Constitution inasmuch as amendment means fundamental changes.
Precedent	The Constitution devises special organs or special methods to amend or change the fundamental principles that create the Government.
Precedent	The methods of amendment may be by ordinary law making body as in Great Britain or by the ordinary law making body with special procedure or unusual majority or by special organs of government created for the purpose such as Constitutional convention or by the electorate in the form of referendum or of initiating a referendum.
Precedent	In case a written Constitution makes no provision for amendment it is usually held that the national law making body by ordinary procedure may amend the Constitution.
Precedent	If a Constitution provides the method of amendment that method alone is legal.
Precedent	Any other method of amendment would be a revolution.
Precedent	The deliberative and restrictive processes and procedure ensure a change in the Constitution in an orderly fashion in order to give the expression to social necessit y and to give permanence to the Constitution.
Precedent	897.
Precedent	The people expressed in the Preamble to our Constitution gave the Constitution including the power to amend the Constitution to the bodies mentioned in Article 368.
Precedent	These bodies represent the people.
Precedent	The method to amend any part of the Constitution as provided for in Article 368 must be followed.
Precedent	Any other method as for example convening Constituent Assembly or Referendum will be extra Constitutional or revolutionary.
Precedent	In our Constitution Article 368 restricts only the procedure or the manner and form required for amendment but not the kind or the character of the amendment that may be made.
Precedent	There are no implied limitations to the amending power.
Precedent	The Attorney General summed up pithily that the Constitution Act s not only for the people but on the people.
Precedent	898.
Precedent	The Attorney General relied on several American decisions in support of these propositions.
Precedent	First, the word "amendment" does not mean improvement.
Precedent	The view in <NAME> v. <NAME> 102 Cal.
Precedent	118 of a single  learned Judge that amendment means improvement was not accepted in <NAME> v. <ORG> Vol.
Precedent	33, p. 1130.
Precedent	Second, ratification by people of <ORG>s would be void when a federal amendment proposed by <ORG> is required to be ratified by the legislatures of the <ORG>s.
Precedent	Ex-parte <NAME> Federal Reporter No.
Precedent	262 p. 563.
Precedent	The legislature is a mere agency for ratification of a proposed amendment.
Precedent	Ex-parte <NAME> did not accept the view of the learned single Judges in Livermore v. <NAME> that amendment means only improvement.
Precedent	Third, the argument that the word "amendment" carries its own limitations regarding fundamental principles or power of <ORG> or control of the conduct of the individuals by devising a method of referendum by <ORG> legislatures is adding a new method of amendment.
Precedent	This is not permissible.
Precedent	<NAME> v. <NAME> 264 Federal Reporter 186.
Precedent	The only method of amendment is that prescribed by the Constitution.
Precedent	The theory of referendum by <ORG> legislatures is not valid.
Precedent	Fourth, the a ssumption that ratification by State legislatures will voice the will of the people is against the prescribed method of amendment and grant of authority by the people to <ORG> in the manner laid down in Article V of the American Constitution.
Precedent	It is not the function of <ORG> or legislative bodies to alter the method which the Constitution has fixed.
Fact	Ratification is not an act of legislation.
Fact	It derives its authority from the Constitution.
Fact	<NAME> v. <NAME> 253 U.S. 221; <NAME> v. <NAME> 256 U.S. 358, <NAME> v. <NAME> 258 U.S. 130.
Fact	Fifth, the power of amendment extends to every part of the Constitution.
Fact	In amending the Constitution <ORG> acts in the character and capacity of a convention expressing the supreme will or the sovereign people and is unlimited in its power save by the Constitution.
Fact	Ex-parte Mrs. <NAME> American Law Reports Annotated, Vol.
Fact	36, p. 1451.
Fact	Sixth, the argument that amendments which touch rights of the people must be by convention is rejected by <ORG> in American  Article V of the American Constitution is clear in statement and meaning and contains no ambiguity.
Fact	Where the intention is clear there is no room for construction.
Fact	Rhode Island v. <NAME> 253 U.S. 350; U.S. v. <NAME> 282 U.S. 716.
Fact	Seventh, principles of the Constitution can be changed under Article V Schneiderman v. United States of America 320 U.S. 118.
Fact	Eight, the Constitution provides the method of alteration.
Precedent	While the procedure for amending the Constitution is restricted here is no restraint on the kind of amendment that may be made.
Precedent	<NAME> v. <NAME> 389 U.S. 54.
Precedent	899.
Precedent	Except for special methods of amendment in a rigid or controlled Constitution although the methods may vary in different <ORG> and except for express limitations, if any, in rigid or controlled <ORG>, the meaning and scope of the amending power is the same in both the flexible and rigid forms.
Precedent	900.
Precedent	The flexible Constitution is one under which every law of every description can be legally changed with the same case and i n the same manner by one and the same body.
Precedent	Laws in a flexible Constitution are called Constitutional because they refer to subjects supposed to affect the fundamenal institutions of the <ORG>, and not because they are legally more sacred or difficult to change than other laws.
Precedent	901.
Precedent	A rigid Constitution is one under which certain laws generally known as Constitutional or fundamental laws cannot be changed in the same manner as ordinary laws.
Precedent	The rigidity of the Constitution consists in the absence of any right of the legislatures when acting in its ordinary capacity to modify or repeal definite laws termed Constitutional or fundamental.
Precedent	In a rigid Constitution the term "Constitution" means a particular enactment belonging to the Articles of the Constitution which cannot be legally changed with the same ease and in the same manner as ordinary laws.
Precedent	902.
Precedent	The special machinery for Constitutional amendment is the limitation of the power of the legislature by greater law than by the law of the ordinary legislati on.
Argument	<ORG> knowing that it will disperse and leave the actual business of legislation to another body, attempts to bring into the Constitution that it promulgates as many guides to future action as possible.
Argument	It attempts to arrange for the "recreation of a constituent assembly" whenever such matters are in future to be considered, even though that assembly be nothing more than the ordinary legislature acting under certain restrictions.
Precedent	There may be some elements of the Constitution which the constituent assembly wants to remain unalterable.
Precedent	These elements are to be distinguished from the rest.
Precedent	The Fifth Clause in the United States Constitution is that no State without its own consent shall be deprived of its equal suffrage in the <ORG>.
Precedent	The Attorney General rightly sard that just as there are no implied limitation in flexible Constitutions similarly there are no implied limitations in a rigid Constitution.
Precedent	The difference is only in the method of amendment.
Precedent	Amendment can be made by ordinary l egislature under certain restrictions, or by people through referendum or by majority of all the units of a federal <ORG> or by a special convention.
Precedent	903 In a rigid Constitution the legislatures by reason of their well matured long and deliberately formed opinion represent the will of the undoubted majority.
Precedent	But even such will can be thwarted in the amendment of the organic law by the will of the minority.
Precedent	In case where the requisite majority is not obtained by the minority thwarting an amendment, there is just as much danger to the <ORG> from revolution and violence as there is from what is said to be the caprice of the majority.
Precedent	The safeguards against radical changes' thus represent a better way and a natural way of securing deliberation, maturity and clear consciousness of purpose without antagonising the actual source of power in the democratic state.
Precedent	904.
Precedent	The term "amendment" connotes a definite and formal process of Constitutional change.
Precedent	The force of tradition and custom and the judicial interpretati on may all affect the organic structure of the <ORG>.
Precedent	These processes of change are the evolution of Constitution.
Precedent	905.
Precedent	The background in which Article 368 was enacted by <ORG> has an important aspect on the meaning and scope of the power of amendment.
Precedent	906.
Fact	On 12 November, 1946 Sir <NAME> Constitutional Adviser prepared a brochure containing Constitution of the British Commonwealth Countries and the Constitutions of other countries.
Fact	Different countries having different modes of amendments were referred to.
Fact	In the same volume the fundamental rights under 13 heads were extracted from 13 selected countries like U.S.A., Switzerland, Germany, Russia, Ireland, Canada, Australia.
Fact	Two features follow from that list.
RatioOfTheDecision	First, there is no absolute standard as to what constitutes fundamental right.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no such thing as agreed fundamental rights of the world.
RatioOfTheDecision	Second, fundamental rights which are accepted in our Constitution are not superior to fundamental rights in other Constitutions nor can it  be said that the fundamental rights are superior to Directive Principles in our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	907.
Fact	On 17 March, 1947 a questionnaire was circulated under the subject as to what provisions should be made regarding the amendment of the Constitution.
Fact	The draft clause of amendment to the Constitution prepared by the Constitutional Adviser at that time indicates that an amendment may be initiated in either <ORG> Parliament and when the proposed amendment is passed in each <ORG> by a majority of not less than two thirds of the total number of members of that <ORG> and is ratified by the legislatures of not less than two thirds of the units of the <ORG>, excluding the Chief Commissioners' Provinces, it shall be presented to the President for his assent; and upon such assent being given the amendment shall come into operation.
Fact	There were two explanations to that clause.
Fact	908.
Fact	On 29 April, 1947 <NAME> amendment to the draft clause was accepted.
Fact	The amendment was "that this clause also if neces sary may be amended in the same way as any other clause in the Constitution".
Fact	In June, 1947 the drafting of the amending clause started.
Fact	Originally it was Numbered 232.
Fact	Eventually, Articles 304 and 305 came into existence in place of draft Article 232.
Fact	The first draft of the amendment clause was given by Sir <NAME> in March, 1947.
Fact	By June, 1947 and thereafter he recommended the procedure favoured by Sir <NAME> and Sir <NAME>, namely, passage by two thirds majority in <ORG> and ratification by like majority of Provincial legislatures.
Fact	On 21 February, 1948 the draft Constitution was ready.
Precedent	Draft Articles 304 and 305 related to amendment Article 305 provided for reservation of seats for minorities for ten years unless continued in operation by an amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	909.
Fact	The following features emerge.
Fact	First, <ORG> made no distinction between essential and non-essential features.
Fact	Secondly, no one in <ORG> said that fundamenta l rights could not be amended.
Fact	The framers of the Constitution did not have any debate on that.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thirdly, even in the First Constitution Amendment debate no one doubted change or amendment of fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	At no stage it appeared that fundamental rights are absolute.
RatioOfTheDecision	While a Constitution should be made sound and basic it should be flexible and for a period it should be possible to make necessary changes with relative facility.
RatioOfTheDecision	910.
Fact	Certain amendments to Article 304 were proposed.
Fact	One proposed amendment No.
Fact	118 was that amendment was to be passed in two <ORG> by a clear majority of the total membership of each <ORG>.
Fact	Another proposed amendment No.
Fact	210 was that for a period of three years from the commencement of the Constitution, any amendment certified by the President to be not one of substance might be made by a simple majority.
Fact	This also' stated that it would include any formal amendment recommended by a majority of the Judges of <ORG> on the ground of removing difficulties in the admini stration of the Constitution or for the purpose of carrying out the Constitution in public interest.
Fact	The third proposed amendment No.
Fact	212 was that no amendment which is calculated to infringe or restrict or diminish the scope of any individual rights, any rights of a person or persons with respect to property or otherwise, shall be permissible and any amendment which is or is likely to have such an effect shall be void and ultra vires of any legislature.
Fact	It is noteworthy that this amendment was withdrawn.
Precedent	See Constituent Assembly Debates Vol.
Precedent	IX p. 1665.
Precedent	911.
Fact	In the first category the framers devised amendment by <ORG> by a simple majority.
Fact	These are Articles 2 and 4 which deal with States.
Fact	As far as creation or re- Constitution of States is concerned, it is left to <ORG> to achieve that by a simple majority.
Fact	Again, draft Article 148A which eventually became Article 169 dealing with Upper Chambers in the States gave <ORG> power to abolish the Upper Chambers or to create new Second Chambers.
Fact	Sc hedules 5 and 6 were left to be amended by <ORG> by simple majority.
Fact	The second category of amendment requires two thirds majority.
Fact	It is in that connection that the statement of Dr. <NAME> "If the future <ORG> wishes to amend any particular Article which is not mentioned in Part III or Article 304 all that is necessary for them is to have the two thirds majority then they can amend it" was invoked by Mr. <NAME> to support his submission that Part III was unamendable.
Precedent	That is totally misreading the speech.
Precedent	The speech shows that some Articles would be amendable by bare majority, others would require two thirds majority and the third category would require two thirds majority plus ratification by the States.
Precedent	912.
Precedent	Proceedings in <ORG> show that the whole Constitution was taken in broad prospective and the amendments fell under three categories providing for simple majority, or two thirds; majority or two thirds majority and ratification by the States.
Precedent	These different procedure s were laid down to avoid rigidity.
Precedent	913.
Precedent	The Constitution First Amendment Act which added Article 15 (4 ), substituted words in Articles 19(2) and Article 19(6 ), inserted Article 31A indicates interesting features.
Precedent	The two criticisms at that time were as to what was the hurry and secondly that the Government was trying to take more power to itself.
Precedent	The answers are that a Constitution which is responsive to the people's will and their ideas and which can be varied here and there, will command respect and people will not fight against change.
Precedent	Otherwise, if people feel that it is unchangeable and cannot be touched, the only tiling to be done by those who wish to change it is to try to break it.
Precedent	That is a dangerous thing and a bad thing.
Precedent	914.
Precedent	In this background there is no doubt about the meaning and scope of Article 368.
Precedent	The Attorney General rightly said that if there be any doubt contemporaneous practical exposition of the Constitution is too strong and obstinate to be shaken or controlled.
Precedent	In <NAME> v. B lacker 146 U.S. 1 it is said that where plain and clear words occur there is no difficulty but where there is doubt and ambiguity contemporaneous and practical exposition is a great weight.
Precedent	<CITATION_304499> .
Precedent	(1963) 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	491 this <ORG> took notice of the feature that Constitution makers had deep knowledge of Constitutions and Constitutional problems of other countries.
Precedent	915.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> relying on <ORG> v. <NAME> (1935) A.C. 500 submitted that in interpreting a constituent or organic statute that construction most beneficial to the widest possible amplitude of powers must be adopted.
Precedent	A strict construction applicable to penal or taxing statute will be subversive of the real intention of <ORG> if applied to an Act passed to ensure peace, order and good government.
Precedent	Largest meaning is given to the allocated specific power.
Precedent	If there are no limitations on the power it is the whole power.
Precedent	Grant of power of amendment cannot be cut down except by express or implied limitations.
Precedent	The conclusio n is that the meaning of the word amendment is wide and not restricted.
Precedent	916.
Precedent	The contention of Mr. <NAME> on behalf of the petitioner is that under Article 368 as it stood prior to the amendment there were implied and inherent limitations on the power of amendment.
Precedent	It was said that the word "amendment" would preclude the power to alter or destroy the essential features and the basic elements and the fundamental principles of the Constitution.
Precedent	This contention was amplified as follows.
Precedent	The Constitution is given by the people unto themselves.
Precedent	The power to decide upon amendment is given to the 5 year <ORG> which is a creature of the Constitution.
Precedent	Article 368 does not start with the non-obstante clause.
Precedent	Article 368 uses the word "amendment" simpliciter.
Precedent	Less significant amendment powers in others parts of the Constitution use the words "add, alter, repeal or vary" in addition to the word "amendment", as will appear in Articles 31B, 25(b), 252(2), 372, 372A(2), paragraph 7 Schedule 5, paragraph 21 Schedul e 6.
Precedent	Article 368 talks of an amendment of this Constitution and does not extend the amending power to "all or any of the provisions of this Constitution".
Precedent	On a wide construction of the word "amendment" all fundamental rights can be taken away by the requisite majority whereas much less significant matters require the concurrence of at least half the States under the proviso to that Article.
Precedent	917.
Precedent	The basic human freedom are all of the most fundamental importance to all the States and all the citizens.
Precedent	Article 32 is no less important to the citizens of States than Article 226.
Precedent	The Preamble is not a part or provision of the Constitution.
Precedent	Therefore, the Preamble cannot be amended under Article 368.
Precedent	The nature and the contents of the Preamble are such that it is incapable of being amended.
Precedent	If the Preamble is unalterable it necessarily follows that those features of the Constitution which are necessary to give effect to the Preamble are unalterable.
Precedent	Fundamental rights are intended to give effect to the Preamble.
Precedent	T hey cannot, therefore, be abridged or taken away.
Precedent	The provisions of Article 368 themselves can be amended under that very Article.
Precedent	If the word "amendment" is read in the widest sense <ORG> will have the power to get rid of the requisite majority required by Article 368 and make any Constitutional amendments possible by bare majority, <ORG> can provide that hereafter the Constitution shall be unamendable.
Precedent	<ORG> can reduce India to a status which is neither sovereign nor democratic nor republic and where the basic human rights are conspicuous by their absence.
Precedent	918.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> submits that the principle of inherent or implied limitations on power to amend the controlled Constitution stems from three basic features.
Precedent	First, the ultimate legal sovereignty resides in the people.
Precedent	Second, <ORG> is only a creature of the Constitution.
Precedent	Third, power to amend the Constitution or destroy the essential features of the Constitution is an application of ultimate legal sovereignty.
Precedent	919.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME>  enumerated 12 essential features.
Precedent	These were as follows : (1) The supremacy of the Constitution.
Precedent	(2) The sovereignty of India.
Precedent	(3) The integrity of the country.
Precedent	(4) The democratic way of life.
Precedent	(5) The republican form of Government.
Precedent	(6) The guarantee of basic human rights elaborated in Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	(7) A secular State.
Precedent	(8) A free and independent judiciary.
Precedent	(9) The dual structure of the <ORG> and the States.
Precedent	(10) The balance between the legislature, the executive and the judiciary.
Precedent	(11) a <ORG>ary form of Government as distinct from the presidential form of Government.
Precedent	(12) Article 368 can be amended but cannot be amended to empower <ORG> to alter or destroy any of the essential features of the Constitution, make Che Constitution literally or practically unamendable, make it generally amendable by a bare majority in <ORG>, confer the power of amendment either expressly or in effect on the State Legislatures and delete the proviso and deprive the States of the power of ratificati on which is today available to them in certain broad areas.
Precedent	920.
Precedent	The Constitution 24th Amendment Act was impeached by Mr. <NAME> on three grounds.
Precedent	First, by substituting the words "amend by way of addition, variation or repeal" in place of the word "amendment" in Article 368 the power was widened.
Precedent	Second, the 24th Amendment made explicit that when <ORG> makes a Constitutional amendment under Article 368 it acts in exercise of constituent power.
Precedent	Third, it had provided by amendment in Articles 13 and 368 that the power in Article 13(2) against abridging or taking away of the fundamental rights shall not apply to any amendment under Article  368.
Precedent	The Constitution 24th Amendment Act is, therefore, to be construed as empowering <ORG> to exercise full constituent power of the people and to vest in <ORG> the ultimate legal sovereignty of the people as authorising <ORG> to alter or destroy all or any of the essential features, basic elements and fundamental principles of the Constitution.
Precedent	<NAME> ewise, <ORG> is construed by the Constitution 24th Amendment Act to be authorised to damage or destroy the essence of all or any of the fundamental rights.
Precedent	Therefore, the amendment must be illegal and invalid.
Precedent	921.
Precedent	In the alternative it was submitted on behalf of the petitioner that if the Constitution 24th Amendment is valid it can be only on a reading down of the amended provisions of Article 13 and 368 which reading would preserve the original inherent and implied limitations.
Precedent	Even after the Constitution 24th Amendment Act <ORG> will have no power to alter or destroy the essential features of the Constitution and secondly, fundamental rights are among the essential features of the Constitution and, therefore, the essence of any of the fundamental rights cannot be altered or destroyed or damaged even when they are sought to be abridged.
Precedent	922.
Precedent	The Attorney General stressed the background in which Article 368 was enacted by <ORG> to show that any limitation on the amending power  was never in controversy.
Precedent	The only controversy was regarding the degree of flexibility of an amendment of all the provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	Our Constitution has adopted three methods of amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	Certain provisions of the Constitution may be amended by a simple majority in <ORG>.
Precedent	Others may be amended by two-thirds majority.
Precedent	The third category relates to provisions where amendments must be ratified by one half of the States.
Precedent	This scheme strikes a good balance by protecting the rights of the States while leaving the remainder of the Constitution easy to amend.
Precedent	Of the three ways of amending the Constitution two are laid down in Article 368 itself and the third is provided for in about 24 other Articles.
Precedent	923.
Precedent	The Constitutional Adviser incorporated in his draft Constitution prepared by him in October, 1947 a recommendation contained in the supplementary <ORG>.
Precedent	Following the recommendation of <ORG> he included a proviso that th e provisions in the Constitution relating to the reservation of seats for the Muslims, the Scheduled Castes, the Scheduled Tribes, the Indian Christians and the Sikhs, either in <ORG> or in any Provincial <ORG>, should not be amended before the expiry of ten years from the commencement of the Constitution.
Precedent	924.
Fact	<ORG> in February, 1948 considered the provisions for amendment.
Fact	It made three material changes in the provisions made by the Constitution Adviser.
Precedent	First, the <ORG> framed a self contained and independent Article regarding the reservation of seats in the legislatures for minorities.
Precedent	These provisions could not be amended for a period of ten years and would then cease to have effect unless continued in operation by an amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	The second proposed change gave a limited power of initiating Constitutional amendments to the State legislatures.
Precedent	This power related to two matters.
Precedent	These were the methods of choosing Governors and the establishmen t or abolition of <ORG> in the States.
Precedent	The third amendment suggested was that changes in any of the legislative lists (not merely federal List) should receive ratification of at least one half of the Provincial legislatures and one third of the legislatures of Indian States.
Precedent	925.
Precedent	The entire history of the power of amendment of the Constitution shows first that the Draft Constitution eliminates the elaborate and difficult procedures such as a decision by convention or a reterendum.
Precedent	The powers of amendments are left with the legislatures of the Union and the States.
Precedent	Secondly, it is only for amendments of specific matters that the ratification by the State legislatures is required.
Precedent	All other Articles are left to be amended by <ORG> with only limitation of majority of not less than a two-thirds of the members of each <ORG> present and voting and the majority of the total membership of each <ORG>.
Precedent	Thirdly, the provisions for amendment of the Constitution Were made simple and not difficult when  comparison is made with the American and the Australian Constitutions.
Precedent	926.
Precedent	The theory of inherent and implied limitations on the amending power is based on the assumption of a narrow and restricted meaning of the word amendment to suggest that the basic features or the essential features and the democratic republican character of the Constitution cannot be damaged and destroyed.
Precedent	Emphasis is laid on the Preamble of the Constitution to suggest that inherent and implied limitations all spring from the Preamble.
Precedent	The Preamble is said not to be a part of the Constitution.
Precedent	The Preamble is said to be unalterable.
Precedent	Therefore, it is contended that other provisions which gave effect to the Preamble cannot be amended.
Precedent	927.
Precedent	Reliance is placed on the decision of this <ORG> in <NAME> case (1960) 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	250 in support of the proposition that the Preamble is not a part of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The conclusion drawn is that no amendment of the Constitution inconsistent with the Preamble can be made.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Preamble is said to b e an implied limitation on the power of amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	This <ORG> in <NAME> case said that the Preamble has never been regarded as the source of any substantive power, because such powers are expressly granted in the body of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	This <ORG> said "what is true about the powers is equally true about prohibitions and limitations".
RatioOfTheDecision	In <NAME> case it was suggested that the Preamble to the Constitution postulated that like a democratic republican form of the Government the entire territory of India was beyond the reach of <ORG> and could not be affected either by ordinary legislation or even by Constitutional amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Preamble was invoked to cut down the power to cede territory either by ordinary law or by amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	This <ORG> said that the Preamble is, in the words of <NAME> "a key to open the minds of the makers, but nevertheless the Preamble could not be said to postulate a limitation on one of the very important attributes of sovereignty".
RatioOfTheDecision	This <ORG> rejected the theo ry that the Preamble can impose serious limitations on the essential attribute of sovereignty.
RatioOfTheDecision	The suggested limitation that the Preamble affirmed the inviolability of the territory of India so that the power of amendment should be implied limited to exclude the ceding territory, is negatived by this decision.
Precedent	928.
Precedent	The petitioner's contention that the Preamble is not a part of the Constitution is nullified by the petitioner's reference to and reliance on the Preamble as the source of all inherent limitations.
Precedent	The <NAME> case held that Article I could be amended under Article 368 and a part of the territory of India could be ceded by such amendment.
Precedent	The Preamble did not limit the power to cede territory by-amendment of Article I.
Precedent	929.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the <NAME> case there is an observation that the Preamble is not a part of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Preamble was taken up by <ORG> at the end as it had to be in conformity with the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Preamble was debated and voted upon and the motion "The  Preamble stand part of the Constitution" was adopted.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, Mr. <NAME> rightly contended that the Preamble is an integral part of the status.
Precedent	The Preamble can be repealed (See Craies on Statute 6th Ed.
Precedent	page 200 seq.
Precedent	and Halsbury Laws of England, 3rd Ed.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	36 p. 370).
Precedent	930.
Precedent	In <NAME> case (1950) S.C.R.
Precedent	88 an argument was advanced on the Preamble that the people gave themselves guaranteeing to the citizens fundamental rights, and, therefore, the provisions of Part III must be construed as being paramount to the legislative will as otherwise the fundamental rights to life and personal liberty would have no protection against legislative action.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME>, J., said that the high purpose and spirit of the Preamble as well as the Constitutional significance of a declaration of Fundamental Rights should be borne in mind.
RatioOfTheDecision	The language of the provisions, it was said there, could not be stretched in disregard of the cardinal rule of interpretation of any enactment, Constitution or other, that its spiri t no less than its intendment should be collected primarily from the natural meaning of the words used.
RatioOfTheDecision	The words "procedure established by law" in Article 21 must be taken to refer to a procedure which had a statutory origin.
RatioOfTheDecision	The word "law" was said not to mean the immutable and universal principle of natural justice.
RatioOfTheDecision	The reasoning given by <NAME> was "no procedure is known or can be said to have been established by such vague and uncertain concepts as the immputable and universal principles of natural justice".
Precedent	This <ORG> in <NAME> case refused to read due process as an implication of the Constitution.
Precedent	931.
Precedent	In the Kerala Education Bill 1957 case (1959) S.C.R.
Precedent	995 <NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	referred to the Preamble and said "to implement and fortify the supreme purpose set forth in the Preamble, Part III of our Constitution has provided for us certain fundamental rights".
Precedent	In the same case, <NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	said "so long as the Constitution stands as it is and is not altered, it is inconceiveably the duty of this Cou rt to uphold the fundamental rights and thereby honour our sacred obligation to the minority community who are of our own".
Precedent	This observation shows that fundamental rights can be amended and the Preamble does not stand in the way.
Precedent	932.
Precedent	<CITATION_761967> (1955) Supp.
Precedent	1 S.C.R.
Precedent	528 <NAME>, J. referred to the Preamble in discussing the question of waiver of fundamental right and compared our Preamble to the Preamble to the United States Constitution.
Precedent	The Preamble to the American Constitution is without the Bill of Rights and the Bill of Sights which became part of the United States Constitution substantially altered its character and broadly speaking, differed in no way, in principle, from our fundamental rights.
Precedent	933.
Precedent	The Preamble is properly resorted to where doubts or ambiguities arise upon the words of the enacting part.
Precedent	If the enacting words are clear and unambiguous, there is little room for interpretation, except the cases leading to an obvious absurdity, or to a direct overthrow of the intention express ed in the Preamble.
Precedent	This is the view of <NAME>.
Precedent	The Preamble can never be resorted to enlarge the powers confided to the general government The Preamble can expound the nature, extent and application of the powers actually conferred by the Constitution and not substantively create them.
Precedent	934.
Precedent	The decision of this <ORG> in <NAME> case, the Coal Bearing Areas Act case (1962) 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	44, and <CITATION_1669028> (1965) 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	276 are that if the language of the enactment is clear the Preamble cannot nullify or cut down the enactment.
Precedent	<ORG> in The Secretary of State for India in <CITATION_615647> .
Precedent	43 Mad.
Precedent	529 said that the legislature may well intend that the enacting part should extend beyond the apparent ambit of the Preamble or the immediate mischief.
Precedent	See also Attorney General v. <NAME> 1957 A.C.  436.
Precedent	The American decision in <NAME> v. Commonwealth of Massachusetts 197 U.S. 11 indicates that power is not conferred by the Preamble but must be found in the  Constitution.
Precedent	935.
Precedent	The Preamble may be relevant in the case of an ambiguity in an enactment in a statute.
Precedent	A statute does not contain an amending power for the simple reason that the statute can be amended under legislative power.
Precedent	The Attorney General rightly said that the Preamble in a Constitution refers to the frame of the Constitution at the time of the Preamble, and, therefore, it can possibly have no relevance to the constituent power in the future, when that Constitution itself can be changed.
Precedent	The position would be the same so far as the Preamble is concerned whether the constituent power is exercised by the amending body provided for by the people themselves in the Constitution or by referendum if so provided for in the Constitution.
Precedent	The Attorney General supported his submission by relying on the views of <NAME> and <NAME> on the similar interpretation of Section 128 of the Australian Constitution.
Precedent	936.
Precedent	<NAME> in the Failure of Federalism in Australia in discussing Section 128 of the Australian Con stitution under the heading "Alteration of the Constitution" expresses the view that the section must be read as a substantive grant of power to alter the Constitution and that the negative form of the section in no way detracts from the amplitude of that power.
Precedent	<NAME> further says that it is not permissible to refer to the Preamble in connection with the effect of Section 128 and if nevertheless such reference is made there is nothing adverse to the conclusion that there is full power of amendment.
Precedent	The Preamble recites a preliminary agreement to unite in one indissoluble Federal Commonwealth.
Precedent	Section 128 of the Australian Constitution forms an integral part of the Constitution.
Precedent	As from the time of the agreement it must have been contemplated that the Constitution should be alterable to the full extent of power conferred by that section.
Precedent	Therefore, the word "alter" in Section 128 of the Australian Constitution is not restricted by any reference to the Preamble.
Precedent	937.
Precedent	<NAME> in Legislative, Executive and.
Precedent	<NAME> cial Powers in Australia 4th Ed.
Precedent	at pp.
Precedent	505-506 expresses the view that apart from the rule which excludes the Preamble generally from consideration in statutory interpretation it is clear that, when all is said and done, the Preamble at the most is only a recital of a present intention.
Precedent	The insertion of an express reference to an amendment in the Constitution itself is said to operate as a qualification upon the mere recital of the reasons for its creation.
Precedent	938.
Precedent	At the second reading of the Draft Constitution in <ORG> a resolution was adopted that the Preamble do form part of our Constitution.
Precedent	The Preamble is a part of the Constitution.
Precedent	On 26 November, 1949 certain Articles of the Constitution were brought into force.
Precedent	Article 393 did come into force on 26 November, 1949.
Precedent	Therefore, the Preamble did not come into force on 26 November, 1949.
Precedent	As regards general laws the position is that the Preamble has been treated as part of the statute.
Precedent	939.
Precedent	Clear Constitutional provisions are imperative  both on the legislatures and the <ORG>.
Precedent	Where a Constitutional provision is comprehensive in scope and leaves no room for interpretation the <ORG> is without power to amend, add to or detract from a Constitutional provision or to create exceptions thereof by implication (See Corpus Juris Secumdum Vol.
Precedent	16 p. 65).
Precedent	Where the people express themselves in careful and measured terms in framing the Constitution and they leave as little as possible to implications, amendments or changes in the existing order or conditions cannot be left to inserting implications by reference to the Preamble which is an expression of the intention at the time of the framing of the Constitution.
Precedent	Therefore, the power to amend the Constitution is not restricted and controlled by the Preamble.
Precedent	940.
Precedent	The contention that essential features are not amendable under Article 368 as it stood before the Constitution 24th Amendment Act is not only reading negative restrictions on the express power of amendment but is also putting the clock back.
Precedent	One of the salutary principles of construction of a statute is to be found in R.V.
Precedent	Burah 3 A.C.  889.
Precedent	It was a case to determine whether the prescribed limitations of a colonial legislature had been exceeded.
Precedent	<ORG> said that a duty must be performed by looking to the terms of the instrument by which affirmatively legislative powers are created, and by which, negatively, they are restricted.
Precedent	"If what has been done is legislation within the general scope of the affirmative words which give power, and if it violates no express condition or restriction by which that power is limited, it is not for, any court of justice to enquire further or to enlarge constructively those conditions and restrictions".
Precedent	The maxim Expressum facit cessare taciturn was similarly applied in <NAME> v. <NAME> 1907 A.C. 89.
Precedent	The theory of implied and inherent limitations can be best described as a subtle attempt to annihilate the affirmative power of amendment.
Precedent	Lord <NAME> in <NAME> v. <NAME> 1896 A.C. 600 said that if  the legislature had full power to make laws it was difficult to see how the power was taken away.
Precedent	The power is always sufficient for the purpose.
Precedent	Lord <NAME> in <NAME> v. <NAME> 1910 A.C. 514 said "express enactment shuts the door to further implication".
Precedent	941.
Precedent	It was said that the essential features could be amended by way of improvement but could not be damaged or destroyed.
Precedent	It was said India could not be converted into a totalitarian dictatorship.
Precedent	The entire approach of the petitioner to the power of amendment contained in Article 368 ignores the fact that the object of the Constitution is to provide for the organs of <ORG> like the judicature, legislature and the executive for the governance of the country.
Precedent	Apart from the essential functions of defence against external aggression and of maintenance of internal order a modern <ORG> is organised to secure the welfare of the people.
Precedent	India is a sovereign democratic republic which means that <ORG> and <ORG> legislatures are elected on adult universal s uffrage.
Precedent	The country is governed by the <ORG> system of government with ministries responsible to the <ORG> ok the People and to <ORG> respectively.
Precedent	In a democracy the determination of policies to be pursued can only be determined by a majority vote cast at election and then by a majority of the elected representatives in the legislature.
Precedent	<NAME>, J., said "In a democracy the people have the right to embody their opinion in law".
Precedent	942.
Precedent	The argument that if unbridled power were conferred the Constitution could be subverted or destroyed is not supported by actual experience in India.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> emphasised that since 1951 when Shankari Prasad case recognised unlimited power of amendment till Golak Nath case in 1967 the normal democratic process of the departments of the <ORG> functioned as provided by the Constitution.
Precedent	Elections have been held as provided by the Constitution.
Precedent	If any body or organised party were bent upon subverting our free Constitution, then even if there were no power of  amendment, <ORG> has powers which would enable such destruction to be brought about.
Precedent	Great and wide powers are conferred for the governance of great sovereign countries and such powers cannot be withheld on the ground that they may be used externally or oppressively.
Precedent	Well settled principles of construction in interpreting Constitutions preclude limiting the language of the Constitution by political, juristic or social concepts independently of the language of the Constitution to be interpreted.
Precedent	<NAME>s Court in <CITATION_570453> .
Precedent	(1959) Supp.
Precedent	2 S.C.R.
Precedent	8 relied on the test laid down in <NAME> v. <NAME> (1878) 5 I.A.
Precedent	179 that the terms of the instrument by which affirmatively the powers are created, and by which they are negatively restricted are to be looked into.
Precedent	<ORG> v. Attorney General for Canada 1912 A.C. 571 tersely stated the legal principles as follows : "If the text is explicit the text is conclusive, alike in what it directs and what it forbids".
Precedent	<NAME> s is the golden rule of construction of a written Constitution.
Precedent	943.
Precedent	In <NAME> case 1950 S.C.R.
Precedent	88 this <ORG> was invited to read into the Constitution implications derived from the "spirit of the Constitution".
Precedent	Kania, <NAME> said that to strike down the law on an assumed principle of construction would be "to place in the hands of the judiciary powers too great and too indefinite either for its own security or the protection of private rights".
Fact	Kania, <NAME> also said that a large and liberal interpretation should be given to the Constitution.
Fact	That does not mean that a <ORG> is free to stretch or pervert the language of the Constitution in the interest of any legal or Constitutional theory.
Fact	This <ORG> in <CITATION_656658> 1951 S.C.R.
Fact	228 rejected the contention that the spirit of the Constitution should be invoked in interpreting the Constitution.
Fact	In <ORG> case 72 I.A.
Precedent	57, <ORG> reversed the judgment of the Federal <ORG> observing that questions of jurisprudence or policy were not rele vant to the construction of power conferred in an affirmative language and not restricted in any negative terms.
Precedent	944.
Precedent	A Constitution is essentially a frame of government laying down governmental powers exercisable by the legislature, executive and the judiciary.
Precedent	Even so other provisions are included in the Constitution of a country which provisions are considered by the framers of that Constitution to have such special importance that those should be included in the Constitution or organic law.
Precedent	Thus all provisions of the Constitution are essential and no distinction can be made between essential and non-essential features from the point of view of amendment unless the makers of the Constitution make it expressly clear in the Constitution itself.
Precedent	The Attorney General rightly said that if the positive power of "amendment of this Constitution" in Article 368 is restricted by raising the walls of essential features or core of essential features, the clear intention of <ORG> will be nullified a nd that would make a mockery of the Constitution and that would lead to destruction of the Constitution by paving the way for extra Constitutional or revolutionary changes in the Constitution.
Precedent	The theory of implied and inherent limitations cannot be allowed to act as a boa constrictor to the clear and unambiguous power of amendment.
Precedent	945.
Precedent	If there is no express prohibition against amendment in Article 368 the ommission of any such restriction did not intend to impose any restriction.
RatioOfTheDecision	When certain restrictions are imposed it is not intended that other undefined restrictions should be imposed by implication.
RatioOfTheDecision	The general rule is not to import into statutes words which are not found there.
RatioOfTheDecision	Words are not to be added by implication into the language of a statute unless it is necessary to do so to give the paragraph sense and meaning in its context.
RatioOfTheDecision	If a matter is altogether omitted from statute it is not allowable to insert it by implication.
RatioOfTheDecision	Where the language of an Act is clear and explicit, effect is to be given  to it whatever may be the consequences.
RatioOfTheDecision	The words of the statute speak the intention of the legislature.
RatioOfTheDecision	Where the reading of a statute produces an intelligible result there is no ground for reading any words or changing any words according to what may be supposed intention of the legislature.
Precedent	If a statute is passed for the purpose of enabling something to be done but omits to mention in Terms some detail which is of great importance to the proper performance of the work which the statute has in contemplation the courts are at liberty to infer that the statute by implication empowers the details to be carried out.
Precedent	The implication is to empower the authority to do that which is necessary in order to accomplish the ultimate object.
Precedent	946.
RatioOfTheDecision	The implication sought to be raised by Mr. <NAME> is for the purpose of reading negative words into Article 368 to destroy the positive power to amend.
RatioOfTheDecision	The provisions of out Constitution in the light of historical background and special problems of the country will show th at no provision can be considered as non-essential.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution- makers did not think so.
Precedent	The Attorney General rightly contended that no one has the power or authority to say that any single provision is more essential than another or that the amending power under Article 368 does not operate on any provision on the ground of alleged essentiality when Article 368 provides amendment of this Constitution which obviously means the whole Constitution including every provision.
Precedent	In a Constitution different methods of amendment may be laid down depending upon the degree of importance attached to particular parts of the Constitution.
Precedent	Apart from the language of Article 368 the draft Constitution as it emerged through <ORG> shows that no provision of the Constitution was excepted from the amending power.
Precedent	947.
Precedent	The provisions for the purpose of amendment were divided into four categories.
Precedent	The first two categories are to be found in Article 368.
Precedent	Certain provisions require ratification by the req uisite number of <ORG>s as are mentioned in the proviso.
Precedent	Other provisions which do not fall within the proviso are amendable by a double majority provided there.
Precedent	The third category consists of Articles 4, 169, 240(1), paragraph 7 Schedule 5, and paragraph 21 Schedule 6.
Precedent	The fourth category consists of provisions which were said by the Attorney General to confer enabling power on <ORG> to change the provisions by by the expression "unless <ORG> otherwise provides" or similar expression.
Precedent	He gave the examples which are Articles 73(2), 100(3), 105(3), 118(2), 120(2), 125, 133(3), 171(2), 189(3), 194(3), 210(2), 241(2), 283(1) and (2), 285(1) and (2), 343(3), 345, 348(1).
Precedent	948.
Precedent	The character of the provisions which are amendable under the proviso to Article 368 itself shows that petitioner's submission that essential features are unamendable is a baseless vision.
Precedent	Article 54 speaks of the method of election of the President.
Precedent	This may be changed.
Precedent	The manner or scale of representation of the different <ORG> s in regard to the election of the President may also be changed.
Precedent	The executive power of the <ORG> and the States may be changed.
Precedent	Chapter IV of Part V (the <ORG> Judiciary), Chapter V of Part VI (the High Courts in the States) are also mentioned in Article 368 as liable to be changed.
Precedent	Article 141 may also be changed.
Precedent	Chapter I of Part XI and the Seventh Schedule (legislative relations between <ORG> and the States) may be changed.
Precedent	The representation of the States in <ORG> (Articles 80 and 81) may be changed.
Precedent	The number of representation may be increased or reduced.
Precedent	The method of election of such representatives as <ORG> may by law prescribe and the number of the members of <ORG> may be increased or reduced.
Precedent	The method of election to <ORG> may be changed.
Precedent	Finally the provisions of Article 368 itself, which is the most important part of the Constitution may be changed.
Precedent	949.
Precedent	To find out essential or non-essential features is an exercise in imponderables.
Precedent	When the Constit ution does not make any distinction between essential and non-essential features it is incomprehensible as to how such a distinction can be made.
Precedent	Again, the question arises as to who will make such a distinction.
Precedent	Both aspects expose the egregious character of inherent and implied limitations as to essential features or core of essential features of the Constitution being unamendable.
Precedent	Who is to judge what the essential features are ?
Precedent	On what touchstone are the essential features to be measured?
Precedent	Is there any yardstick by which it can be gauged ?
Precedent	How much is essential and how much is not essential?
Precedent	How can the essential features or the core of the essential features be determined?
Precedent	If there are no indications in the Constitution as to what the essential features are the task of amendment of the Constitution becomes an unpredictable and indeterminate task.
Precedent	There must be an objective data and standard by which it can be predicated as to what is essential and what is not essential.
Precedent	If <ORG> cannot judge these f eatures <ORG> cannot, amend the Constitution.
Precedent	If, on the other hand, amendments are carried out by <ORG> the petitioner contends that eventually court will find out as to whether the amendment violates or abridges essential features or the core of essential features.
Precedent	In the ultimate analysis it is the <ORG> which will pronounce on the amendment as to whether it is permissible or not.
Precedent	This construction will have the effect of robbing <ORG> of the power of amendment and reposing the final power of expressing validity of amendment in the courts.
Precedent	950.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> said that though the essential features could be amended the core of essential features could not be amended.
Precedent	He said that there was no esoteric test to find out what is essential and what is not essential and if no precise definition could be given that was no reason to hold that the essential features and the core of essential features could be amended.
Precedent	It was said that the appreciation of the trained judicial mind is the only way to  find out what essential features are.
Precedent	951.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> rightly contended that there is no foundation for the analogy that just as Judges test reasonableness in law, similarly the judicial mind will find out the essential features on the test of, reasonableness.
Precedent	Reasonableness in law is treated as an objective criterion because reason inheres in man as rational being.
Precedent	The citizen whose rights are affected applies reason and when he assails a law he possesses a standard by which he can persuade the <ORG> that the law is unreasonable.
Precedent	The legislature which makes a law has the standard of reasonableness and has the further qualification to apply the standard because of familiarity with the needs, desires and the wants of the people whom the legislature represents.
Precedent	As regards the Judge not only does he share the reasonableness of the reasonable man but his trained mind enables him to see certain aspects clearly.
Precedent	The process of judicial review of legislation as laid down by <ORG> is that the <ORG> will start wit h the presumption that laws enacted are reasonable.
Precedent	The objective standard is reasonableness.
Precedent	That is why in the law of contract reasonable price is to be ascertained by the <ORG>.
Precedent	In the law of torts the <ORG> find out what reasonable care is.
Precedent	In the law of property reasonable conduct is found out by the <ORG> to avoid evil consequences.
Precedent	Reasonableness is to be judged with reference to the right which is restricted when Article 19 is considered.
Precedent	952.
Precedent	The American <ORG> evolved a test of reasonableness by the doctrine of substantive due process which means not that the law is unreasonable but that on political, social and economic grounds the majority of Judges consider that the law ought not be permitted to be made.
Precedent	The crucial point is that in contradistinction to the American Constitution where rights are couched in wide general terms leaving it to the <ORG> to evolve necessary limitations our Constitution limited it by precise words of limitation as for example in Articles 19 and 21.
Precedent	In Article 21 t he Constitution-makers substituted "procedure established by law" for the words "due process of law".
Precedent	The reason for the change was that the procedure established by law was specific.
Precedent	The framers of the Constitution negatived the vague undefinite reasonableness of laws on political, social and economic grounds.
Precedent	In <NAME> case due process was rejected, by clearly limiting the rights acquired and by eliminating the indefinite due process.
Precedent	The Constitution makers freed judicial review of subjective determination.
Precedent	Due process as a test of invalidity of law was deliberately withheld or denied.
Precedent	<ORG> are not concerned with the wisdom or policy of legislation.
Precedent	The <ORG> are equally not concerned with the wisdom and policy of amendments to the Constitution.
Precedent	953.
Precedent	Reliance was placed by Mr. <NAME> on <NAME> v. <NAME> 1964 A.C. 40 where it is said that opinions that natural justice is so vague as to be practically meaningless, are tainted by the perennial fallacy that because something cannot be cut and dried or  nicely weighed or measured therefore it does not exist.
Precedent	In the same case it was said that the idea of negligence is equally insusceptible or exact definition, but what a reasonable man would regard as fair procedure in particular circumstances and what he would regard as negligence in particular circumstances are equally capable of serving as tests in law.
Precedent	Extracting those observations it was said by Mr. <NAME> that though the border-line between essential features and non-essential features could not be stated or it was not possible to specify exhautively the amendment which could be invalid on that principle yet there was no reason why the principle of inherent and implied limitations to amend our Constitution should not be accepted.
Precedent	Inherent and implied limitations cannot originate in an oracle when the Constitution does not contain any express prohibition against amending any provision.
Precedent	When Article 368 speaks of changes in the provisions of the Constitution as are set out in Clauses (a) to (d) of th e proviso it is manifest that the makers of the Constitution expressed their intention with unerring accuracy that features which can broadly be described as federal features, and from that point of view "Essential features" could be amended.
Precedent	In the face of these express provisions it is impossible to hold that the Constitution does not contemplate an amendment of the so called essential features of the Constitution.
Precedent	The proviso confers that power with relation to the judiciary, the executive and the legislature, none of which could be said to be inessential.
Precedent	Indeed it is difficult to imagine that the Constitution contained any provision which was inessential.
Precedent	It need be hardly said that amendment not only means alteration, addition or repeal of provision but also deletion of some part, partial repeal and addition of a new part.
Precedent	954.
Precedent	It was said that if our Parliamentary system was changed to a Presidential system it would be amending the core of our Constitution.
Precedent	But such a change is permissible under Artic le 368.
Precedent	Whether the people would adopt such an amendment is a different matter and does not fall for consideration here.
Precedent	The core of the federal form of Government in our country is greater power in <ORG> than States for preserving the integrity of the country.
Precedent	There can be changes by having a confederation or by conferring greater power on the <ORG>.
Precedent	Those contentions about unamendability of essential features do not take into consideration that the extent and character of any change in the provisions of the Constitution is to be determined by legislatures as amending bodies under Article 368 and as representatives of the people in a democracy and it is not the function of the <ORG> to make any such determination.
Precedent	955.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> contends that the Constitution 24th Amendment Act is unConstitutional because <ORG> cannot exceed the alleged implied and inherent limitations on the amending power as it stood before the 24th Amendment.
Precedent	The 24th Amendment has substituted the marginal note  "Power of <ORG> to amend the Constitution and procedure therefor" for the original note "procedure for amendment of the Constitution".
