Precedent	This change is due to the fact that according to the leading majority judgment in <NAME> case the unamended Article dealt only with the procedure for amendment and that the power of amendment was in the residuary power of legislation.
Precedent	The 24th Amendment has declared that the power to amend the Constitution is in Article  368.
Precedent	That was the view of this <ORG> in earlier decisions.
Precedent	That was the minority view in <NAME> case.
Precedent	By amendment that view has become the Constitutional mandate.
Precedent	956.
Precedent	The other change as a result of the 24th Amendment is that "<ORG> may in the exercise of its constituent power amend" in place of words "amendment of this Constitution may be initiated".
Precedent	The reasons for this change are to give effect to the decisions of this <ORG> in <ORG> case which in considering the validity of the First Amendment recognised and affirmed the vit al distinction between constituent power and legislative power and decided that the word "law" in Article 13(2) applied to the exercise of legislative power and did not apply to an amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	In <NAME> case the same distinction was upheld by the majority of this Court.
Precedent	In <NAME> case the majority and the concurring judgment denied the distinction between legislative and constituent power and held that Article 13(2) applied to an amendment of the Constitution under Article 368 because there was no distinction between legislative and constituent power.
Precedent	As a consequence the leading majority judgment in <NAME> case held that <ORG> could not amend fundamental rights.
Precedent	The dissenting judgments in <NAME> case upheld the vital distinction between legislative and constituent powers and held that the decision in <NAME> case and the majority decision in <NAME> case were correct and that <ORG> had power to amend the fundamental rights since an amendment of the  Constitution was not law within the meaning of Article 13(2).
Precedent	These features give the reason why the expression "<ORG> may in the exercise of constituent power" was introduced by the 24th Amendment.
Precedent	<ORG> took notice of two conflicting views and the unamended Article 368.
Precedent	<ORG> took notice of the preponderating judicial opinion in favour of the view that Article 368 contained the power of amendment and that power was a constituent power.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. held that the power under Article 368 is constituent power to change the fundamental law, that is to say the Constitution.
Precedent	The constituent power under the Constitution belonged to <ORG> because the Constitution gave it.
Precedent	The Amendment made explicit what the judgment in <NAME> case and the majority judgment in <NAME> case and the dissenting judgment in <NAME> case said, namely that <ORG> has the constituent power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	957.
Precedent	The unamended Article used the words "An amendment of this Constitution".
Precedent	The  24th Amendment used the words "<ORG> may...amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any provision of this Constitution".
Precedent	This has been done because the leading majority judgment in <NAME> case expressed the view that there is considerable force in the argument that the expression "amendment" in Article 368 has a positive and negative content in exercise of which <ORG> cannot destroy the structure of the Constitution but it can only modify the provisions thereof within the framework of the original instrument for its better effect.
Precedent	This observation in <NAME> case raised a doubt as to the meaning of the word "amendment".
Precedent	The 24th Amendment has expressly clarified that doubt.
Precedent	958.
Precedent	The leading majority judgment and the concurring judgment in <NAME> case both held that the fundamental rights could not be amended by <ORG>.
Precedent	The leading majority judgment with reference to the meaning of the word "amendment" and without deciding the matter observed that there was great force in the ar gument that certain fundamental features e.g.
Precedent	the concept of federalism, the institutions of the President and the Parliamentary executive could not be abolished by amendment.
Precedent	<ORG> case, <NAME> case and the dissenting minority judgment in <NAME> case took the view that every provision of the Constitution could be amended in exercise of constituent power.
Precedent	As a necessary corollary, the 24th Amendment excludes the operation of Article 13 by amending Article 13 by a new Sub- article (4) that nothing in Article 13 shall apply to any amendment of this Constitution under Article 368.
Precedent	The amendment of Article 13 by an insertion of Sub- article (4) is also reinforced by the opening words introduced in Article 368 by the 24th Amendment, viz., notwithstanding anything contained in this Constitution, which would certainly exclude Article 13.
Precedent	959.
Fact	The Constitution 24th Amendment Act raises three aspects.
Fact	First, does the word "amend" include abrogation or repeal of the whole Constitution?
Fact	Does amendm ent mean that there is some feature of the Constitution which cannot be changed.
Fact	Secondly, what light does the proviso to Article 368 throw on the nature of the amending power and on the doctrine of inherent and implied limitations on the amending power that essential features of the Constitution cannot be damaged or destroyed.
Fact	Thirdly, does Clause (e) of the proviso to Article 368 enable <ORG> and the requisite majority of the States to increase the power of amendment that was conferred by Article 368.
Precedent	960.
Precedent	Article 368 in the unamended form contained power as well as self executing procedure which if followed by the prescribed authorities would result in an amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	Both the Attorney General and Mr. <NAME> rightly said that the words "Constitution shall stand amended" in Article 368 will exclude a simple repeal that is without substituting anything in place of the repealed Constitution.
Precedent	If the Constitution were totally repealed and a vacuum was created it could not be said that  the Constitution stands amended.
Precedent	The Constitution means the mode in which a <ORG> is constituted or organised specially as to the location of sovereign power.
Precedent	The Constitution also means the system or body of fundamental principles according to which the nation, <ORG> and body politic is constituted and governed.
Precedent	In the case of a written Constitution the Constitution is more fundamental than any particular law and contains a principle with which all legislation must be in harmony.
Precedent	Therefore, an amendment of the Constitution is an amendment of something which provides a system according to which a <ORG> or nation is governed.
Precedent	An amendment of the Constitution is to make fundamental changes in the Constitution.
Precedent	Fundamental or basic principles can be changed.
Precedent	There can be radical change in the Constitution like introducing a Presidential system of government for a cabinet system or a unitary system for a federal system.
Precedent	But such amendment would in its wake bring all consequential changes for the smooth working of  the new system.
Precedent	961.
Precedent	However radical the change the amendment must provide for the mode in which the <ORG> is constituted or organised.
Precedent	The question which was often put by Mr. <NAME> drawing a panorama of a totalitarian <ORG> in place of the existing Constitution can be simply answered by saying that the words "The Constitution shall stand amended" indicate that the Constitution of India is being referred to.
Precedent	The power of amendment is unlimited so long as the result is an amended Constitution, that is to say, an organic instrument which provides for the making interpretation and implementation of law.
Precedent	962.
Precedent	The theory of unamendability of so called essential features is unmeritorious in the face of express provisions in Article 368 particularly in Clauses (a) to (d) of the proviso.
Precedent	Clauses (a) to (d) relate to 66 Articles dealing with some of the most important features of the Constitution.
Precedent	Those Articles relate to the judiciary, the legislature and the executive.
Precedent	The legislative relations between the U nion and the States and the distribution of legislative power between them are all within the ambit of amendment.
Precedent	963.
Precedent	The question which was raised by Mr. <NAME> as to whether under proviso (e) to the unamended Article 368 the power of amendment could be increased is answered in the affirmative.
Precedent	The reasons broadly stated are three.
Precedent	964.
Precedent	First, under Article 368 proviso (e) any limitation on the power of amendment alleged to be found in any other Article of the Constitution can be removed.
Precedent	The full magnitude of the power of amendment which would have existed but for the limitation could be restored and the power of amendment increase.
Precedent	In <NAME> case the majority view was that Article 13(2) operated as a limitation on the power of amendment.
Precedent	The 24th Amendment took note of that decision and removed all doubts by amending Article 13(2) and providing a new Sub- article (4) there and also by amending Article 368 to the effect that Article 13(2) shall not apply to any amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	If t he express limitation which had been judicially held to constitute a bar to the amendment of fundamental rights could be removed by amending Article 368 under Clause (e) to the proviso any other alleged implied limitation can be similarly removed.
Precedent	965.
Precedent	Secondly, judicial decisions show that by amending the Article conferring the power of amendment a greater power to amend the Constitution can be obtained than was conferred by the original Article.
Precedent	In <NAME> case 1935 Irish Report 170 all the learned Judges excepting the Chief Justice held that by first amending Section 50 of the Irish Constitution which conferred the power of amendment subject to certain restrictions thereon so as to remove the restrictions contained in that section, the Irish Parliament effectively increased its power in the sense that an amendment could be made which those express restrictions would have prohibited.
Precedent	Again in Ranasinghe case 1965 A.C. 172 it was said that a legislature has no power to ignore the conditions of law making that  are imposed by the instrument which regulates its power.
Precedent	This restriction created by the instrument exists independently of the question whether the legislature is sovereign or whether the Constitution is uncontrolled.
Precedent	<ORG> held that "such a Constitution can indeed be altered or amended by the legislature if the regulating instrument so provides and if the terms of those provisions are complied with and the alteration or amendment may include the change or abolition of those very provision".
Precedent	Thus a controlled Constitution can be converted into an uncontrolled Constitution vastly increasing the power of amendment.
Precedent	966.
Precedent	Thirdly, the power to amend the amending Article must include the power to add, alter or repeal any part of that Article and there is no reason why the addition cannot confer a power of amendment which the authorities named in Article 368 did not possess.
Precedent	By the exercise of the amending power provision can be made which can increase the powers of <ORG> or increase the pow ers of the States.
Precedent	Again, by amendment future amendments can be made more difficult.
Precedent	The picture drawn by Mr. <NAME> that a future amendment would be rendered impossible either by absolutely forbidding amendment or by prescribing an impractically large majority does not present any legal impediment to such an amendment.
Precedent	The safeguard against such action is external.
Precedent	The contingency of any such amendment being proposed and accepted is extremely remote because such an amendment might sow the seeds of revolution which would be the only way to bring about the change in the Constitution.
Precedent	The Solicitor General rightly said that the effect of the amendment is that "it shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill".
Precedent	The product is not required to be "this Constitution".
Precedent	It will not be identically the old Constitution.
Precedent	It will be a changed or amended Constitution and its resemblance will depend on the extent of the change.
Precedent	More rigid process like referendum or initiative or greater majority or ratif ication by a larger number of States might be introduced by amendment.
Precedent	967.
Precedent	It is important to note that proviso (e) to Article 368 , namely, the power to amend Article 368 is unlike perhaps some Constitutions which were before <ORG> when our Constitution was framed.
Precedent	Neither the American nor the Australian Constitution provided for any power to amend the amending provision itself.
Precedent	The Attorney General rightly contended that this forcefully expresses a clear and deliberate intention of <ORG> that apart from providing for a less rigid amending formula <ORG> took care to avoid the controversy in America as to whether express limitation on Article V of the American Constitution itself regarding equal suffrage of the States in the <ORG> could be amended or the controversy in Australia as to whether Section 128 of the Australian Constitution itself could be amended as there was no express limitation on such amendment.
Precedent	<ORG> provided in Cl ause (e) to Article 368 express and specific power of amendment of Article 368 itself.
Precedent	968.
Precedent	The amplitude of the amending power in our Constitution stands in bold relief in comparison with Article V of the American Constitution, Section 128 of the Australian Constitution and Section 50 of the Irish Constitution none of which confers such a power.
Precedent	Dr. <NAME> in his Legislative Powers in Australia 4th Ed.
Precedent	p. 505 expresses the view that though Section 128 is negative in form but the power of amendment extends to alteration "of this Constitution" and this power is implied by its terms.
Precedent	Dr. <NAME> also states that by the consent of the States the last part of Section 128 could be amended.
Precedent	This is only to illustrate as to how other Constitutions are understood by jurists in their countries.
Precedent	Our Article 368 contains no express limitation on the power of amendment.
Precedent	The provision of Clause (e) in the proviso to Article 368 is not limited to federal features.
Precedent	969.
Precedent	The words "amendment of this Constitution" in Section 5 0 of the Irish Constitution which formed the subject of decision in <NAME> case 1935 Irish Report 170 were read by <NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	in his dissenting view to mean that if power to amend Section 50 itself was intended to be given the framers of the Constitution would have said so.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> relied on this dissenting view.
Precedent	Other learned Judges who formed the majority held that the words "amendment of this Constitution" conferred power to amend that Section 50 as well.
Precedent	If no intention to amend that section itself is expressed there is nothing which can be implied was the dissent.
Precedent	Therefore, it would follow even according to the dissent that no implied limitations on the power of amendment can be read in Section 50 if an express power of amendment has been conferred by the Constitution.
Precedent	970.
Argument	Mr. <NAME> contended that the people reserved the power to themselves to amend the essential features of the Constitution and if any such amendment were to be made it should be referred to the people by referendum.
Argument	It wa s said that the Constitution makers did not intend that essential features should be damaged or destroyed even by the people, and therefore, the Constitution did not provide for referendum.
Argument	The other contention on behalf of the petitioner was that referendum was not provided for because it might have been difficult to have the Constitution accepted on those terms.
Argument	The second view would not eliminate the introduction of referendum as a method of amendment.
Precedent	If a referendum were introduced by an amendment people would have complete power to deal with essential features.
Precedent	The other question would be as to whether the Preamble and the fundamental rights would be a limitation on the power of the people.
Precedent	On behalf of the petitioner it was said that it was not necessary to decide the questions.
Precedent	Both the Attorney General and Mr. <NAME> correctly said that the submissions made on behalf of the petitioner indicated that if essential features could be amended by the people the very fact that <ORG> did  not include referendum as one of the methods of amendment and that the Constitution makers excluded no part of the Constitution from amendment established that the amendment of a written Constitution can be legally done only by the method prescribed by the Constitution.
Precedent	If the method of referendum be adopted for purpose of amendment as suggested by Mr. <NAME> that would be extra Constitutional or revolutionary.
Precedent	The amending body to amend the Constitution represents the will of the people.
Precedent	971.
Argument	Therefore, as long as Article 368 may be amended under proviso (e) any amendment of the Constitution by recourse to referendum would be revolutionary.
Argument	Mr. <NAME> on behalf of the petitioner did not rely on the majority decision in <NAME> case that the fundamental rights could be abridged or taken away only by convening <ORG>, but based his argument on a theory of legal sovereignty of the people.
Precedent	The Constitution is binding on all the organs of government as well as on the people.
Precedent	The Attorn ey General rightly submitted that the concept of popular sovereignty is well settled in parliamentary democracy and it means that the people express their will through their representatives elected by them at the general election as the amending body prescribed by the Constitution.
Precedent	972.
Precedent	Are fundamental rights unamendable?
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> contended that apart from Article 13(2) fundamental rights are based on Universal Declaration of Human Rights and are natural rights, and, therefore, they are outside the scope of amendment.
Precedent	In <NAME> case the majority view declined to pronounce any opinion on alleged essential features other than fundamental rights.
Precedent	The concurring view was that fundamental rights were unamendable because they were fundamental.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. for himself and two other learned Judges and <NAME>, J. rightly rejected the theory of implied limitations.
Precedent	The three reasons given by <NAME>, J. are these.
Precedent	First, the doctrine of essential and non-essential features would introduce uncertainty.
Precedent	Sec ondly, constituent power of amendment does not admit of any impediment of implied restrictions.
Precedent	Thirdly, because there is no express limitation there can be no implied limitation.
Precedent	973.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> correctly contended that there is intrinsic evidence in the provisions of Part III itself that our Constitution does not adopt the theory that fundamental rights are natural rights or moral rights which every human being is at all times to have simply because of the fact that as opposed to other things he is rational and moral.
Precedent	The language of Article 13(2) shows that these rights are conferred by the people of India under the Constitution and they are such rights as the people thought fit to be in the organised society or <ORG> which they were creating.
Precedent	These rights did not belong to the people of India before 26 January 1950 and would not have been claimed by them.
Precedent	Article 19 embodies valuable rights.
Precedent	Rights under Article 19 are limited only to citizens.
Precedent	Foreigners are human beings but they are not given fundamen tal rights because these rights are conferred only on citizens as citizens.
Precedent	974.
Precedent	Article 33 enacts that <ORG> may by law modify rights conferred by Part III in their application to Armed Forces.
Precedent	<ORG> may restrict or abrogate any of the rights conferred by Part III so as to ensure the proper discharge of the duties of the Armed Forces and the maintenance of discipline among them.
Precedent	Therefore, Article 33 shows that citizens can be denied some of these rights.
Precedent	If these are natural rights these cannot be abrogated.
Precedent	Article 34 shows that <ORG> may by law indemnify any person in respect of any act done by him in connection with the maintenance or restoration of order in any area where martial law was in force or validate any sentence passed, punishment inflicted, forfeiture ordered or other act done under martial law in such area.
Precedent	Article 34 again shows restriction on rights conferred by Part III while martial law is in force in any area.
Precedent	The dominant concept is social good.
Precedent	Where there is no restrai nt the society fails.
Precedent	975.
Precedent	Articles 352 and 358 also illustrate as to how while the proclamation of emergency is in operation provisions of Article 19 are suspended during emergency.
Precedent	The framers of the Constitution emphasised the social content of those rights.
Precedent	The basic concept of fundamental right is therefore a social one and it has a social function.
Precedent	These rights are conferred by the Constitution.
Precedent	The nature of restriction on fundamental rights shows that there is nothing natural about those rights.
Precedent	The restrictions contemplated under Article 19(2) with regard to freedom of speech are essential partis of a well organised developed society.
Precedent	One must not look at location of power but one should see how it acts.
Precedent	The restrictions contemplated in Article 19 are basically social and political.
Precedent	Friendly relations with foreign states illustrate the political aspect of restrictions.
Precedent	There are similar restrictions on right to move freely.
Precedent	The protection of Scheduled Tribes is also reasonable in the interest of soc iety.
Precedent	This Court in <CITATION_761967> (1959) Supp.
Precedent	1.
Precedent	S.C.R.
Precedent	528 said that there are no natural rights under our Constitution and natural rights played no part in the formulation of the provisions therein.
Precedent	976.
Precedent	Articles 25 and 26 by their opening words show that the right to the freedom of religion is subject to the paramount interest of society and there is no part of the right however important to devotee which cannot and in many cases have not been denied in civilised society.
Precedent	977.
Precedent	<NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	in <NAME> case equated fundamental rights with natural rights or promodial rights.
Precedent	The concurring majority view in <NAME> case, however, said that there is no natural right in property and natural rights embrace the activity outside the status of citizen.
Precedent	Fundamental rights as both the Attorney General and Mr. <NAME> rightly contended are given by the Constitution, and, therefore, they can be abridged or taken away by the people themselves acting as an organised society in a State by the representative s of the people by means of the amending process laid down in the Constitution itself.
Precedent	There are many Articles in Part III of our Constitution which cannot in any event be equated with any fundamental right in the sense of natural right.
Precedent	To illustrate Article 17 deals with abolition of untouchability.
Precedent	Article 18 speaks of abolition of titles.
Precedent	Article 20 deals with protection in respect of conviction for offences.
Precedent	Article 23 refers to prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour.
Precedent	Article 24 deals with prohibition of employment of children in factories, etc.
Precedent	Article 27 speaks of freedom as to liability for taxes levied for promotion of any particular religion.
Precedent	Article 28 contemplates freedom as to attendance at religious instruction or religious worship in certain educational institutions.
Precedent	Article 29 deals with protection of interests of minorities.
Precedent	Article 31(2) prior to the Constitution 25th Amendment Act spoke of payment of just equivalent for acquisition or requisition of property.
Precedent	Article 31(4)  deals with legislation pending at the commencement of the Constitution.
Precedent	Articles 31(5) and (6) save certain types of laws.
Precedent	Article 31A saves laws providing for acquisition of estates etc.
Precedent	Article 32 confers right to move <ORG>.
Precedent	978.
Precedent	The Constitution is the higher law and it attains a form which makes possible the attribution to it of an entirely new set of validity, the validity of a statute emanating from the sovereign people.
Precedent	Invested with statutory form and implemented by judicial review higher law becomes juristically the most fruitful for people.
Precedent	There is no higher law above the Constitution.
Precedent	979.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> relied on an Article by Conrad on Limitation of Amendment Procedure and the Constitutional Power.
Precedent	The writer refers to the West German Provincial Constitution which has expressly excluded basic rights from amendment.
Precedent	If that is so the question of basic rights being unamendable on the basis of higher law or natural law does not arise.
Precedent	The conclusion of the writer is that whereas the  American courts did not consider declaring a Constitutional norm void because of a conflict with higher law the German Jurisprudence broadened the concept of judicial review by recourse to natural law.
Precedent	The post-war Constitution of West Germany distinguished between superior and inferior Constitutional norms in so far as certain norms are not subject to amendment whereas others are.
Precedent	980.
Precedent	The Attorney General relied on <NAME> Legal Theory 5th Ed.
Precedent	on pp.
Precedent	350 seq.
Precedent	to show that there was a revival of natural law theory in contemporary German Legal Philosophy.
Precedent	This theory of natural law springs from the reaction against the excess of the Nazi regime.
Precedent	The view of <NAME> is that natural law may disguise to pose itself the conflict between the values which is a problem of constant and painful adjustment between competing interests, purposes and policies.
Precedent	This conflict is resolved by ethical or political evolution which finds place in legislative policies and also on the impact of changing ideas on the growth o f law.
Precedent	981.
Precedent	Fundamental rights are social rights conferred by the Constitution.
Precedent	There is no law above the Constitution.
Precedent	The Constitution does not recognise any type of law as natural law.
Precedent	Natural rights are summed up under the formula which became common during the Puritan Revolution namely life, liberty and property.
Precedent	982.
Precedent	The theory of evolution of positive norms by supra-positive law as distinguished from superior positive law had important consequences in the post-war revival of natural law in some countries particularly Germany.
Precedent	Most of the German Constitutions from the early 19th Century to the Nazi Regime did not provide for judicial review.
Precedent	Under the Weimar regime, the legislature reigned supreme and legal positivism was brought to an extreme.
Precedent	The re-action after World War II was characterised by decreases of legislative power matched by an increase of judicial power.
Precedent	It is in this context that <NAME>'s writing on which Mr. <NAME> relied is to be understood.
Precedent	The entire suggestion is that norms co uld not only be judged by a superior law namely Constitutional law but by natural law to broaden the scope of judicial review.
Precedent	The acceptance of the doctrine of judicial review has been considered as a progress in Constitutional theory made between Declaration of Independence and the Federal Convention at Philadelphia.
Precedent	983.
Precedent	On the one hand there is a school of extreme natural law philosophers who claim that a natural order establishes that private capitalism is good and socialism is bad.
Precedent	On the other hand, the more extreme versions of totalitarian legal philosophy deny the basic value of the human personality as such.
Precedent	Outside these extremes, there is a far greater degree of common aspirations.
Precedent	The basic autonomy and dignity of human personality is the moral foundation of the teaching of modern natural law philosophers, like <NAME>.
Precedent	It is in this context that our fundamental rights and Directive Principles are to be read as having in the ultimate analysis a common good.
Precedent	The Directive Principles do not const itute a set of subsidiary principles to fundamental rights of individuals.
Precedent	The Directive Principles embody the set of social principles to shape fundamental rights to grant a freer scope to the large scale welfare activities of the <ORG>.
Precedent	Therefore, it will be wrong to equate fundamental rights as natural, inalienable, primodial rights which are beyond the reach of the amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	It is in this context that this <ORG> in <CITATION_761967> (1959) Supp.
Precedent	1 S.C.R.
Precedent	528 said that the doctrine of natural rights is nothing but a foundation of shifting sand.
Precedent	984.
RatioOfTheDecision	Mr. <NAME> rightly said that if the power of amendment of the Constitution is co- extensive with the power of the judiciary to invalidate laws, the democratic process and the co-ordinate nature of the great departments of the <ORG> are maintained.
RatioOfTheDecision	The democratic process is maintained because the will of the people to secure the necessary power to enact laws by amendment of the Constitution is not defeated.
RatioOfTheDecision	The democratic process is also r espected because when the judiciary strikes down a law on the ground of lack of power, or on the ground of violating a limitation on power, it is the duty of the legislature to accept that position, but if it is desired to pass the same law by acquiring the necessary power, an amendment validly enacted enables the legislatures to do so and the democratic will to prevail.
RatioOfTheDecision	This process harmonises with the theory of our Constitution that the three great departments of the <ORG>, the legislature, the judiciary and the executive are co-ordinate and that none is superior to the other.
Precedent	The normal interaction of enactment of law by the legislation, of interpretation by the courts, and of the amendment of the Constitution by the legislature, go on as they were intended to go on.
Precedent	985.
Precedent	If the power of amendment does not contain any limitation and if this power is denied by reading into the Constitution inherent limitations to extinguish the validity of all amendments on the principles of essential features of the Const itution which are undefined and untermed, the courts will have to lay down a new Constitution.
Precedent	986.
Precedent	It is said that the frame of the Government cannot be changed or abrogated by amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	There is before us no aspect of abrogation of the form of Government of the changes apprehended by the petitioners like the abrogation of the judiciary or extending the life of <ORG>.
Precedent	987.
Precedent	The problems of the times and the solutions of those problems are considered at the time of framing the Constitution.
Precedent	But those who frame the Constitution also know that new and unforseen problems may emerge, that problems once considered important may lose their importance, because priorities have changed; that solutions to problems once considered right and inevitable are shown to be wrong or to require considerable modification; that judicial interpretation may rob certain provisions of their intended effect; that public opinion may shift from one philosophy of government to another.
Precedent	Changes in the Constituti on are thus actuated by a sense of duty to the people to help them get what they want out of life.
Precedent	There is no destiny of man in whose service some men can rightfully control others; there are only the desires and performances and ambitions that men actually have.
Precedent	The duty to maximise happiness means that it is easier to give people what they want than to make them want what you can easily give.
Precedent	The framers of the Constitution did not put any limitation on the amending power because the end of a Constitution is the safety, the greatness and well being of the people.
Precedent	Changes in the Constitution serve these great ends and carry out the real purposes of the Constitution.
Precedent	988.
Precedent	The way in which the doctrine of inherent and implied limitations was invoked by Mr. <NAME> in interpreting the Constitution was that the test of power under the Constitution must be to ascertain the worst that can be done in exercise of such power.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> submitted that if unbridled power of amendment were allowed the basic fe atures of our Constitution, namely, the republican and/or democratic form of government and fundamental Tights could be destroyed and India could be converted into a totalitarian dictatorship.
Precedent	The Court was invited to take into account the consequences of the kind described.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> suggested that a wide power of amendment would lead to borrow his words to the liquidation of our Constitution.
Precedent	989.
Precedent	The Attorney General rightly said that the unambiguous meaning of amendment could not be destroyed to nurse the theory of implied limitations.
Precedent	He also said that the live distinction between power and exercise of power is subject to popular will and popular control.
Precedent	The theory of implied and inherent limitation was a repudiation of democratic process.
Precedent	The Attorney General and Mr. <NAME> also rightly said that the approach of the petitioner to the power of amendment contained in Article 368 of the Constitution ignores the fact that the object of the Constitution is to provide for departments of States like th e judiciary, the legislature and the executive for the governance of a country.
Precedent	Apart from the essential functions of defence against external aggression and of maintenance of internal order a modern <ORG> is organised to secure the welfare of the people.
Precedent	<ORG> and <ORG> legislatures are elected on adult universal suffrage.
Precedent	The country is governed by the <ORG> system of Government with ministries responsible to the Houses of <ORG> and to <ORG>.
Precedent	990.
Precedent	In a democracy the determination of the right policies to be pursued can only be determined by a majority vote cast at election and then by a majority of the elected representatives in the legislature.
Precedent	Democracy proceeds on the faith in the capacity to elect their representatives, and faith in the representatives to represent the people.
Precedent	The argument that the Constitution of India could be subverted or destroyed might have hortative appeal but it is not supportable by the actual experience in our country or in any country.
Precedent	The two  basic postulates in democracy are faith in human reason and faith in Human nature.
Precedent	There is no higher faith than faith in democratic process.
Precedent	Democracy on adult suffrage is a great experiment in our country.
Precedent	The roots of our democracy are in the country and faith in the common man.
Precedent	That is how Mr. <NAME> said that between 1951 when this <ORG> recognised in <NAME> case unlimited power of amendment till <NAME> decision in 1967 the normal democratic process in our country functioned as provided by the Constitution.
Precedent	991.
Precedent	The principle underlying the theory of taking consequences into account is best expressed in <ORG> v. <ORG> 1913 A.C. 107, where it was said that if any particular construction in construing the words of a statute was susceptible to more than one meaning, it was legitimate to consider the consequences which would result from any particular construction.
Precedent	The reason is that there are many things which the legislation is presumed not to have intended to  bring about and therefore a construction which would not lead to any of these things should be preferred to one which would lead to one or more of them.
Precedent	992.
Precedent	The doctrine of consequences has no application in construing a grant of power conferred by a Constitution.
Precedent	In considering a grant of power the largest meaning should be given to the words at the power in order to effectuate it fully.
Precedent	The two exceptions to this rule are these.
Precedent	First, in order to reconcile powers exclusively conferred on different legislatures, a narrower meaning can be given to one of the powers in order that both may operate as fully as is possible.
Precedent	(See <ORG> case 1938 F.C.R.
Precedent	18 and <ORG> v. Governor General 72 I.A.
Precedent	93).
Precedent	Second, technical terms must be given their technical meaning even though it is narrower than the ordinary or popular meaning.
Precedent	<CITATION_1425329> .
Precedent	1959 S.C.R.
Precedent	379.
Precedent	In our Constitution powers are divided between federation and the States.
Precedent	An attempt must be made to find the power in some entry or  other because it must be assumed that no power was intended to be left out.
Precedent	993.
Argument	The theory of consequences is misconstrued if it is taken to mean that considerations of policy, wisdom and social or economic policies are included in the theory of consequences.
Argument	In <NAME> case it was said that the judicial tribunal, has nothing to do with the policy of any Act and the only duty of the <ORG> is to expound the language of the Act in accordance with the settled rules of construction.
Precedent	In Attorney General for Ontario v. Attorney General for Dominions 1912 A.C. 571 <ORG> refused to read an implication in the Constitution of Canada that there was no power to refer a matter for the advisory opinion of the highest <ORG> because advisory opinions were prejudicial to the correct administration of justice and were embarrassing to Judges themselves who pronounced them, for humanly speaking it would be difficult for them to hear a case on merits if they have already expressed an opinion.
Precedent	<ORG> reject ed this argument and said that so far as it was a matter of wisdom and policy it was for the determination of <ORG>.
Precedent	In <ORG> (1887) 12 A.C. 575 <ORG> was invited to hold that the legislature of a province could not levy a tax on capital stock of the <ORG>, for that power might be exercised to destroy the <ORG> altogether.
Precedent	<ORG> observed that if on a true construction of Section 92 of the British North America Act the power fell within the section, it would be wrong to deny its existence because by some possibility it might be abused.
Precedent	994.
Precedent	The absurdity of the test of the worst that can be done in exercise of power is demonstrated by the judgment of Chief Justice <NAME> in Gross-man 69 L.Ed.
Precedent	527 where it was said that if those who were in separate control of each of the three branches of <ORG> were bent upon defeating the action of the other, normal operations of <ORG> would come to a halt and could be paralysed.
Precedent	Normal operations of the <ORG> assume  that all three branches must co-operate if Government is to go on.
Precedent	Where the meaning is plain the <ORG> must give effect to it even if it considers that such a meaning would produce unreasonable result.
Precedent	In the Bihar Land Reforms case 1952 S.C.R.
Precedent	889 Mahajan, <NAME> said that agrarian laws enacted by the legislature and protected by Articles 31(3) and (4) provided compensation which might appear to the <ORG> unjust and inequitable.
Precedent	But the <ORG> gave effect to Articles 31(3) and (4) because the results were intended and the remedy for the injustice lay with the legislature and not with the <ORG>.
Precedent	The construction to avoid absurdity must be used with great caution.
Precedent	995.
Precedent	In <NAME> case 1948 Ch.
Precedent	145 it was said in choosing between two possible meanings of ambiguouos words, the absurdity or the nonabsurdity of one conclusion as compared with another might be of assistance and in any event was not to be applied as to result in twisting the language into a meaning which it could not bear.
Precedent	996.
Precedent	The Attorney General ri ghtly submitted that if power is conferred which is in clear and unambiguous language and does not admit of more than one construction there can be no scope for narrowing the clear meaning and width of the power by considering the consequences of the exercise of the power and by so reading down the power.
Precedent	The question is not what may be supposed to be intended but what has been said.
Precedent	See <NAME> v. <NAME> 1930 A.C. 1.
Precedent	<ORG> in <NAME> v. <ORG> 207 U.S. 463 said that you cannot destroy in order to save or save in order to destroy.
Precedent	The real import is that a new law cannot be made by construction.
Precedent	The question is one of intention.
Precedent	A meaning cannot be different which it cannot reasonably bear or will be inconsistent with the intention.
Precedent	The very basis of <ORG> democracy is that the exercise of power is always subject to the popular will and popular control.
Precedent	The petitioner's theory of implied and inherent limitations is a repudiation of this democratic process.
Precedent	The  underlying theory of democratic government is "the right of a majority to embody their opinion in law subject to the limitations imposed by the Constitution", per <NAME>, J. in <NAME> v. New York 198 U.S. 45.
Precedent	In our Constitution Article 368 contains no express limitation on the amendment of any provision of the Constitution.
Precedent	997.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> relied on the amending provisions in the Constitution of America, Canada, Australia, Ireland and Ceylon and also decisions on the power of amendment in those countries in support of his submissions that a restricted meaning should be attributed to the word "amendment" and implied and inherent limitations should be read into the meaning and power of amendment.
Precedent	998.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> also relied on the opinion of <NAME> in a Treatise on the Constitutional Limitations at pages 36-37 that "a written Constitution is in every instance a limitation upon the powers of government in the hands of agents; for there never was a written republican Constitution which delegated to f unctionaries all the latent powers which lie dormant in every nation, and are boundless in extern, and incapable of definition".
Precedent	This view of <NAME> is not relevant to the amending power in Article V of the American Constitution.
Precedent	This view relates to the legislative power that a written Constitution is a limitation upon the powers of the Government, namely, the legislature, the executive and the judiciary.
Precedent	999.
Precedent	The other views of <NAME> in Constitutional Limitations at pages 341-343, 345-348, 351-354 are these.
Precedent	First except where the Constitution has imposed limitations upon the legislative power it must be considered as practically absolute, whether it operates according to natural justice or not in any particular case.
Precedent	Second, in the absence of Constitutional restraint the legislative department of a <ORG> Government has exclusive and ample power and its utterance is the public policy of the <ORG> upon that subject, and the <ORG> are without power to read into the Constitution a restraint of the legislatu re with respect thereto.
Precedent	Third, if the <ORG> are not at liberty to declare statutes void because of their apparent injustice of impolicy, neither can they do so because they appear to the minds of the Judges to violate fundamental principles of republican Government, unless it shall be found that those principles are placed beyond legislative encroachment by the Constitution.
Precedent	The principles of republican government are not a set of inflexible rules, vital and active in the Constitution, though unexpressed, but they are subject to variation and modification from motives of policy and public necessity.
Precedent	Fourth, the <ORG> are not at liberty to declare an act void, because in their opinion it is opposed to a spirit supposed to pervade the Constitution, but not expressed in words.
Precedent	1000.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> relied on the views of <NAME> published in <ORG> (1919-1920) pages 21-225 to build the theory of implied and inherent limitations.
Precedent	The views extracted are these.
Precedent	The power given by the Cons titution cannot be construed to authorise a destruction of other powers in the same instrument.
Precedent	The essential form and character of the <ORG>, being determined by the location and distribution of power, cannot be changed, only the exercise of governmental functions can be regulated.
Precedent	A somewhat different view of <NAME> in the same <ORG> is that it is not likely that <ORG> would put any limitations upon the power of <ORG> to propose amendments and in construing the Fifth Article it would be unwilling to say <ORG> had proposed an amendment which it did not deem necessary.
Precedent	The discretion is left entirely with <ORG>.
Precedent	1001.
Precedent	The other view on which Mr. <NAME> relied is of <NAME> published in 33 Harvard <ORG> (1919-1920) at pp.
Precedent	223-235.
Precedent	The views which Mr. <NAME> extracted are that it may be safely premised that the power to amend the Constitution was not intended to include the power to destroy it.
Precedent	<NAME> relies on <NAME> v. <NAME> 102 Cal.
Precedent	118 where it is  stated that the term "amendment" implies such an addition or change within the lines of the original instrument as will effect an improvement, or better carry out the purpose for which it was framed.
Precedent	1002.
Precedent	There are other views of <NAME> on which the Attorney General relied and which were not extracted by Mr. <NAME>.
Precedent	Those views are that after excluding from the scope of its amending power in Article V of the American Constitution such amendments as take away legislative powers of the <ORG> there is still left a very broad field for its operation.
Precedent	All sorts of amendments might be adopted which would change the framework of the federal Government, the thing which the Constitution was created to establish, which would change the distribution of power among the various departments of the Government, place additional limitations upon them, or abolish old guarantees of civil liberty and establish new ones.
Precedent	1003.
Precedent	The Attorney General also relied on the view of <NAME> published in 33 <ORG> pp.
Precedent	659-666 as a reply to <NAME>.
Precedent	<NAME>'s view is that the security for the <ORG>s was provided for by the provision for the necessity of ratification by three- fourths of the <ORG>s.
Precedent	The Constitution committed to <ORG> and not to the <ORG> the duty of determining what amendments were necessary.
Precedent	The rights of the <ORG>s would certainly be safer in the hands of three-fourths of the <ORG>s themselves.
Precedent	This is considered by the framers of the Constitution to ensure integrity of <ORG>s.
Precedent	1004.
Precedent	The Attorney General also relied on the view of <NAME> published in Vol.
RatioOfTheDecision	20 <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> points out a distinction between a political society or <ORG> on the one hand and governmental organs on the other to appreciate that Constitutional limitations are against governmental organs.
RatioOfTheDecision	The writer's view is that an individual has no legal rights against a sovereign organised political society except what the society gives.
RatioOfTheDecision	The doctrine of national sovereignty means that people who made the existing distrib ution of powers between the federal and the State Governments may alter it.
RatioOfTheDecision	Amendment is left to legislatures because as a matter of convenience the legislatures generally express the will of the people.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the Constitution the people prescribe the manner in which they shall amend the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> states that an amendment of a particular statute means usually it is a change germane to the subject matter of that statute.
RatioOfTheDecision	Any change in <ORG> is germane to the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Any change altering the dispositions of power would therefore be germane to the purposes of the instrument.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME>'s view is that it is clear that no limitation on the amending power can be found in this notion of necessity for germaneness.
Precedent	1005.
Precedent	The Attorney General also relied on an Article "On the views of W.F.
Precedent	<NAME> published in <ORG> p. 321 seq.
Precedent	and of <NAME>, published in <ORG> p. 647 seq.
Precedent	The view of <NAME> is this.
Precedent	There are no implied limitations on the amending power.
Precedent	<ORG> in the National Prohibition cases rejected the arguments presented in favour of implied limitations.
Precedent	To narrow down the meaning of amendment or to adopt implied limitations would not only narrow down the use of the amending power but would also leave the question of amending power in each case to judicial decision without the guidance of any legal principle.
Precedent	<NAME>'s view is that by reason of the Tenth Amendment which provided that the powers not delegated to the United States by the Constitution nor prohibited by it to the States are reserved to the States respectively or to the people, the amending power in Article V of the American Constitution was not limited by the Tenth Amendment 1006.
Precedent	The question which has arisen on the Fifth Article of the American Constitution is whether there are implied limitations upon the power to amend.
Precedent	The two express limitations were these.
Precedent	First, no amendment which may be made prior to 1808 shall in any manner effect the First and the Fourth clauses in the N inth Section of the First Article.
Precedent	That Limitation became exhausted by passage of time.
Precedent	The second express limitation is that no State without its consent shall be deprived of its equal suffrage in the <ORG>.
Precedent	The express limitation is to safeguard the equal representation of the smaller States in the <ORG>.
Precedent	The limitation can only be changed by unanimous consent of the States.
Precedent	1007.
Precedent	The 18th Amendment was vigorously attacked in the National Prohibition Cases on the ground that it overstepped alleged implied limitations on the Constitution amending power.
Precedent	The arguments advanced were these.
Precedent	First, the 18th Amendment which introduced prohibition was not in fact an amendment for an amendment is an alteration or improvement of that which is already contained in the Constitution and the term is not intended to include any addition of entirely new grants of power.
Precedent	Secondly, the amendment was not an amendment within the meaning of the Constitution because it is in its nature legislation and that an amendment of th e Constitution can only affect the powers of government and cannot act directly upon the rights of individuals.
Precedent	Third, that the Constitution in all its parts looks to an indestructible nation composed of indestructible States.
Precedent	The power of amendment was given for the purpose of making alterations and improvements and any attempt to change the fundamental basis of the Union is beyond the power delegated by the Fifth Article.
Precedent	The decision in the National Prohibition Cases is that there is no limit on the power to amend the Constitution except that State may not without its consent be deprived of its equal suffrage in the <ORG>.
Precedent	1008.
Precedent	In Rhode Island v. <NAME> 253 U.S. 350 the 18th Amendment was challenged to be not within the purview of Article V. The judgment in Rhode Island case was that the amendment was valid.
Precedent	In Rhode Island case the grounds of attack were that the amendment was legislative in character and an invasion of natural rights and an encroachment on the fundamental principles of dual sovereignty  but the contentions were overruled.
Precedent	1009.
Precedent	In <NAME> v. <NAME> 253 U.S. 221 a question arose as to whether the action of <ORG> ratifying the 18th Amendment known as National Prohibition could be referred to the electors of the State under the provisions of the State Constitution.
Precedent	It was held that these provisions of he State were inconsistent with the Constitution of the United States.
Precedent	The decision of the <ORG> was unanimous.
Precedent	The two methods of ratification prescribed by Article V of the Constitution are by action of the legislatures of the three-fourths of the States or conventions in the like number of States.
Precedent	The determination of the method of ratification is the exercise of a national power specifically granted by the Constitution.
Precedent	That power is conferred upon <ORG>.
Precedent	Article V was held to be plain and to admit of no doubt in its interpretation.
Precedent	The choice of means of ratification was wisely withheld from conflicting action in the several States.
Precedent	1010.
Precedent	Again, in <NAME> v. <NAME> 2 58 U.S. 130 there was a suit to strike out the names of women from the register of voters on the ground that the State Constitution limited suffrage to men and that the 19th Amendment to the Federal Constitution was not validity adopted.
Precedent	The 19th Amendment stated that right of citizens to vote shall not be denied on account of sex.
Precedent	It was contended that the amending power did not extend to that situation.
Precedent	<ORG> there rejected that contention.
Precedent	<ORG> said that the function of a State legislature in ratifying the proposed amendment to the federal Constitution like the function of <ORG> in proposing the amendment is a federal function derived from the federal Constitution; and it transcends any limitations sought to be imposed by the people of a State.
Precedent	1011.
Precedent	In United States v. <NAME> 282 U.S. 716 a contention was advanced that the 10th Amendment recognised a distinction between powers reserved to the States and powers reserved to the people and that State legislatures were competent  to delegate only the former to <ORG>; delegation of the latter required action of the people through conventions in the several states.
Precedent	The 18th Amendment being of the latter character, the ratification by State legislatures was contended to be invalid.
Precedent	<ORG> rejected the argument.
Precedent	It found the language of Article V too clear to admit of reading any exceptions into it by implication.
Precedent	1012.
Precedent	The decisions in Rhode Island v. <NAME> 253 U.S. 350, <NAME> v. <NAME> 253 U.S. 221, <NAME> v. <NAME> 258 U.S. 130 and United States v. <NAME> 282 U.S. 716 are all authorities for the proposition that there is no implied limitation on the power to amend.
Precedent	The 18th Amendment was challenged on the ground that ordinary legislation could not be embodied in a Constitutional amendment and that <ORG> cannot Constitutionally propose any amendment which involves the exercise or relinquishment of the sovereign powers of a State.
Precedent	The 19th Amendment was attacked on the narrower ground that a State which  had not ratified the amendment would be deprived of its equal suffrage in the <ORG> because its representatives in that body would be persons not of its choosing.
Precedent	<ORG> brushed aside these arguments as wholly unworthy of serious attention and held both the amendments valid.
Precedent	1013.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> contended the word "amendment" in Article 368 would take its colour from the words "change in the provisions" occurring in the proviso.
Precedent	The American decisions illustrate how <ORG> consistently rejected the attempts to limit the meanings of the word "amend" in Article V of their Constitution because of the reference to ratification by legislatures or conventions.
Precedent	Where words are read in their context there is no question of implication for context means parts that precede or follow any particular passage or text and fix its meaning.
Precedent	1014.
Precedent	The rule of nosciitur a sociis means that where two or more words which are susceptible of analogous meaning are coupled together, they are understood to be  used in their cognate sense.
Precedent	They take their colour from each other, the meaning of the more general being restricted to a sense analogous to that of the less general.
Precedent	1015.
Precedent	This rule has been found to have no application to Article V of the American Constitution because conventions and legislatures are both deliberative bodies and if an amendment can be submitted either to the legislatures of States or to conventions at the absolute discretion of the <ORG>, it is difficult to say that the character of the amendment is in any way affected by the machinery by which the amendment is to be ratified.
Precedent	In Rhode Island case the contention that an amendment of the Constitution should be ratified by conventions and not by legislatures was rejected.
Precedent	In Sprague case the contention that matters affecting the liberty of citizens could only be ratified by conventions was not accepted and <ORG> refused to read any implication into Article V of the American Constitution.
Precedent	<ORG> said that in spite of  the clear phraseology of Article V, the <ORG> was asked to insert into it a limitation on the discretion conferred on it by the <ORG>.
Precedent	The Supreme <ORG> did not accept any implied limitation.
Precedent	Where the intention is clear there is no room for construction and no excuse for interpolation or addition.
Precedent	In <NAME> v. <NAME> 264 F. 186 it has been said when the people delegated the power of amendment to their representatives the power of amendment cannot be excluded in any way other than prescribed nor by any instrumentality other than there designated.
Precedent	1016.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> relied on some Canadian decisions the Initiative and Referendum case 1919 A.C. 935, <NAME> v. <NAME> 1957 Canada Law Reports 285, <NAME> v. <NAME> (1949) 4 Dominion Law Report 199; and <NAME> v. City of Quebec and Attorney General of Quebec (1953) 4 D.L.R.
Precedent	641 and <NAME> v. <ORG> and Attorney General for Quebec (1958) 12 D.L.R.
Precedent	796, in support of three propositions.
Precedent	First, unlimited legislative jurisdiction o f <ORG> in Canada is under inherent limitation by reason of the preamble to the British North America Act which states that the Constitution is similar in principle to the United Kingdom.
Precedent	Second, the Dominion legislature cannot detract from the basic rights of freedom of speech and political association which are available in the United Kingdom.
Precedent	Third, rights which find their source in natural law cannot be taken away by positive law.
Precedent	1017.
Precedent	In the Initiative and Referendum case <ORG> said that Section 92 of the British North America Act entrusted legislative power in a province to its legislature and to that legislature only.
Precedent	A power of legislation enjoyed by a provincial legislature in Canada can while preserving its own capacity intact seek the assistance of subordinate agencies as in <NAME> v. <NAME> 9 App.
Precedent	Cas.
Precedent	117 the legislature of Ontario was held to be entitled to entrust to <ORG> authority to enact regulations.
Precedent	It does not follow that such a legis lature can create and endow with its own capacity a legislative power.
Precedent	The Initiative and Referandum case decided that in the absence of clear and unmistakable language the power which the <ORG> possessed through a person directly representing the <ORG> could not be abrogated.
Precedent	The Lieutenant Governor under the British North America Act referred to as the B.N.A.
Precedent	Act was an integral part of the legislature.
Precedent	The Initiative and Referendum Act was found to be one which wholly excluded the Lieutenant Governor from legislative authority.
Precedent	The only powers of veto and disallowance preserved by the Initiative and Referendum Act were related to acts of legislative <ORG> as distinguished from Bills.
Precedent	Therefore the powers of veto and disallowance referred to could only be those of the Governor General under Section 90 of the B.N.A.
Precedent	Act and not the powers of the Lieutenant Governor which are at an end when a Bill has become an Act.
Precedent	Section 11 of the Act provided that when a proposal for repeal of some law has been approve d by majority of the electors voting that law is automatically to be deemed repealed, at the end of 30 days after the publication in the <ORG>.
Precedent	Thus the Lieutenant Governor appears to be wholly excluded from the legislative authority.
Precedent	The Initiative and Referendum decision related to an Act of the legislature and secondly to the Act being ultra vires the provisions of the B.N.A.
Precedent	Act.
Precedent	This is not at all, relevant to the amending power of a Constitution.
Precedent	The Act was found to be invalid because the machinery which it provided for making the Laws was contrary to the machinery set up by the B.N.A.
Precedent	Act.
Precedent	The impugned Act rendered the Lieutenant Governor powerless to prevent a law which had been submitted to voters from becoming an actual law if approved by the voters.
Precedent	The impugned Act set up a legislature different from that constituted by the B.N.A.
Precedent	Act and this the legislature had no power to do.
Precedent	1018.
Precedent	The other Canadian decisions are based on three views.
Precedent	The first view is based on the preamble to the B.N.A.
Precedent	Ac t that the Provinces expressed their desire to be federally united into one Dominion, with a Constitution similar to that of the United Kingdom.
Precedent	The corollary extracted from the preamble is that neither <ORG> nor Provincial legislatures may infringe on the traditional liberties because of the Preamble to the B.N.A.
Precedent	Act and a reference to British Constitutional History.
Precedent	The second view expressed in the decisions is that the basic liberties are guaranteed by implication in certain sections of the B.N.A.
Precedent	Act.
Precedent	Section 17 establishes a <ORG> for Canada.
Precedent	Section 50 provides that <ORG> shall continue longer than five years.
Precedent	These sections are read by the Canadian decisions to mean that freedom of speech and freedom of political association should continue.
Precedent	The third view is that some rights find their source in natural law which cannot be taken away by positive law.
Precedent	1019.
Precedent	The first view found expression in <NAME> case.
Precedent	There was an Act respecting communistic propaganda.
Precedent	The majority Judges  found that the subject matter was not within the powers assigned to the Province by Section 92 of the B.N.A.
Precedent	Act.
Precedent	They further held that the Act constituted unjustifiable interference with freedom of speech and expression essential under the democratic form of government established in Canada.
Precedent	The Canada Elections Act, the B.N.A.
Precedent	Act provided for election of <ORG> every five years, meeting of <ORG> once a year.
Precedent	It was contended that it was implicit in all legislations the right of candidates to criticise, debate and discuss political, economic and social principles.
Precedent	1020.
Precedent	<NAME> case raised a question of jurisdiction of the Court to grant bail.
Precedent	Under Section 1025A of the Criminal Code a person was detained in custody.
Precedent	Section 1025A provided that an accused might be detained in custody without bail pending an appeal to the Attorney General.
Precedent	1021.
Precedent	The <NAME> case related to a municipal bye-law requiring permission for distribution of books and tracts in the city streets.
Precedent	The <NAME> case relied on the  observations of <NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	in Re <NAME> Legislation 1938 S.C.R.
Precedent	100 that the right of free public discussion on public affairs is the breath of life for parliamentaly institutions.
Precedent	1022.
Precedent	In Chabot case public schools in the Province of Quebec were operated by School Commissioners elected by tax payers of whom the religious majority were Catholics.
Precedent	A dissident tax payer raised the question as to whether dissidents might establish their own schools or they might send them to a school of a neighbouring municipality and thereupon become exempt from paying tax.
Precedent	The majority held that certain regulations passed by <ORG> were intra vires because they must be construed as confined to Catholic children.
Precedent	1023.
Argument	The Canadian decision show first that certain Judges relying on the Preamble to the B.N.A.
Argument	Act that the Canadian Constitution is to be similar in principle to that of the United Kingdom raised the vires of some of the legislations affecting freedom of speech.
Argument	Secondly, the Canadian Constitution  was given by <ORG> and if the Judges who used such dicta referred to that part of the Preamble they were emphasising that the rights of the Canadian people were similar to those in England.
Precedent	Thirdly, it has to be remembered that the Canadian Constitution has been developed through usage and conventions.
Precedent	1024.
Fact	None of these decisions relates to amendment of the Constitution.
Fact	None of these decisions indicates that there is any inherent limitation on the amendment of the Constitution.
Fact	The Preamble to the B.N.A.
RatioOfTheDecision	Act shows that the Canadian Constitution enjoined observance of fundamental principles in British Constitutional practice.
RatioOfTheDecision	The growth of the Canadian Constitution was through such usage and convention.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution is of a sovereign independent republican country.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution does not draw sustenance from any other Constitution.
Precedent	Our Constitution does not breathe through conventions and principles of foreign countries.
Precedent	1025.
Precedent	There are no explicit guaranteed liberties in the Brit ish North America Act.
Precedent	In Canada the Constitutional issue in civil liberties legislation is simply whether the particular supersession or enlargement is competent to the Dominion or the Province as the case may be.
Precedent	Apart from the phrase "civil rights in the Province" in Section 92(13) there is no language in Sections 91 and 92 which even remotely expresses civil liberties values.
Precedent	1026.
Precedent	The Canadian Bill of Rights assented to in 1960 in Section 2 states that every law of Canada shall unless it is expressly declared by an Act of Parliament of Canada that it shall operate notwithstanding the Canadian Bill, of Rights be so construed and applied as not to abrogate, or infringe or authorise abrogation abridgement or infringement of any of the rights of freedom recognised and declared.
Precedent	The view of <NAME> in Canadian Constitutional Law (3rd Edition) (1969) is that in terms of legislative power the political liberties represent independent Constitutional values which are exclusively in federal keeping.
Precedent	Since the enac tment of the Canadian Bill of Rights the question has hardly any substantive effect because <ORG> can make a declaration in terms of Section 2 of the Bill of Rights that a law abrogating a freedom in the Bill of Rights is operative.
Precedent	1027.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> relied on the Australian decisions in <NAME> v. Attorney General of Queensland 23 C.L.R.
Precedent	457 and Victoria v. <ORG> <ORG> in support of the proposition that there is inherent and implied limitation on the power of amendment.
Precedent	1028.
Precedent	In <NAME> case the Parliamentary Bills Referendum Act of 1908 was challenged.
Precedent	The Parliament Bills Referendum Act provided that when a Bill passed by <ORG> in two successive sessions has in the same two sessions been rejected by <ORG>, it may be submitted by referendum to the electors, and, if affirmed by them, shall be presented to the Governor for His Majesty's assent, and upon receiving such assent the Bill shall become an Act of Parliament in t he same manner as if passed by both Houses of Parliament, and notwithstanding any law to the contrary.
Precedent	The Australian States Constitution Act, 1907 provided that it shall not be necessary to reserve, for the signification of His Majesty's pleasure thereon, any Bill passed by the legislatures of any of the States if the Governor has previously received instructions from His Majesty to assent and does assent accordingly to the Bill.
Precedent	1029.
Precedent	In 1915 <ORG> passed a Bill to amend the Constitution of Queensland by abolishing <ORG>.
Precedent	The Bill was passed by <ORG>.
Precedent	<ORG> rejected the Bill.
Precedent	<ORG> again passed the Bill <ORG> again rejected the Bill.

Precedent	1030.
Fact	The questions for the opinion of  the Court were : (1) Is the Constitution Act , Amendment Act of 1908 a valid and effective Act of <ORG>?

Precedent	1031.
Precedent	The Colonial Laws Validity Act 1865 in Section 5 conferred full power on every representative legislature to make laws respecting the Constitution, powers and procedures of such legislature; provided that such laws shall have been passed in such manner and form as may from time to time be required by any Act of <ORG>, letters patent, Order in Council, or colonial laws for the time being in force in the said colony.
Precedent	The <ORG>ary Bills Referendum Act was held to be an Act respecting the powers of the legislature.
Precedent	Section 5 of the Colonial Laws Validity Act provided the authority for the legisl ation.
Precedent	1032.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> extracted three propositions from the <NAME> case.
Precedent	First, probably the power to make laws respecting the Constitution, power and procedure of such legislature does not extend to authorise the elimination of the representative character of the legislature within the meaning of the Act p. 468 per <NAME>, J.
Precedent	Second, probably the representative character of the legislature is a basic condition of the power relied on, and is preserved by the word "such" in the collocation of words in the Constitution "of such legislature" p. 474 per <NAME>, J.
Precedent	Third, when power is given to a Colonial legislature to alter the Constitution of the legislature that must be read subject to the fundamental conception that consistently with the very nature of the Constitution as an Empire, the Crown is not included in the ambit of such power p. 474 per <NAME>, J.
Precedent	1033.
Precedent	The decision in <NAME> case was to the effect that the Acts did not alter the representative character of the legislature as defined in Sectio n 1 of the Colonial Laws Validity Act, 1865, nor did they affect the position of the <ORG>.
Precedent	The first two propositions on which Mr. <NAME> relied, namely, the observations of <NAME> and <NAME>, JJ.
Precedent	p. 468 and p. 474 were both prefaced by the word "probably" which amply shows that the observations are obiter.
Precedent	The question whether the representative character of the legislature could be changed or whether the <ORG> could be eliminated did not call for decision.
Precedent	The other learned Judges <NAME> and <NAME>, JJ.
Precedent	said "It may perhaps be that the legislature must always remain a representative legislature as defined by the statute, but it is unnecessary in the present case to determine whether that is so or not".
Precedent	1034.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. held in that case that the word "legislature" did not include the <ORG> because Section 7 of the Colonial Laws Validity Act used the expression "legislature" followed by the words "or by persons or bodies of persons for the time being acting as such legislature" to show that the legis lature was exclusive of the <ORG>.
Precedent	The assent of the Queen or the Governor was thus regarded as an additional factor.
Precedent	Therefore, <NAME>, J. said that when a power is given to the Colonial legislature to alter the Constitution that must be read subject to the fundamental conception, that the <ORG> is not included in the ambit of such power.
Precedent	Those observations are made in the context of the provisions of the Colonial Laws Validity Act where a "colony" as defined to include all of Her Majesty's possessions abroad".
Precedent	The observations therefore mean that when power to alter the Constitution was conferred upon a colony which is a part of Her Majesty's possessions abroad it is reasonable to assume that such power did not include power to eliminate the Queen as a part of a colonial legislature.
Precedent	1035.
Precedent	The representative character of the legislature does not involve any theory of implied limitation on the power of amendment.
Precedent	Such legislature as was emphasised by <NAME>, J. shows that the limitation on the power of amen dment flowed from express language of Section 5 of the Colonial Laws Validity Act and was not dependent upon any implication.
Precedent	1036.
Precedent	In the State of Victoria case the validity of the Pay-Roll Tax Act, 1941 was impugned on the ground that it was beyond the legislative competence of the Commonwealth.
Precedent	The Pay Roll Tax Assessment Act 1941-69 made the <ORG> liable to pay tax on the wages payable to named categories of employees of the State of Victoria.
Precedent	<ORG>, in the exercise of its power under Section 51(ii) of the Constitution to make laws with respect to taxation, but so as not to discriminate between States or parts of State was held competent to include the <ORG> in right of a State in the operation of a law imposing tax or providing for the assessment of a tax.
Precedent	The inclusion of the <ORG> in right of a State in the definition of "employed" in Section 3(1) of the Pay- Roll Tax Assessment Act 1941-1969 thus making the <ORG> in right of a State liable to pay the tax in respect of wages paid  to employees including employees of departments engaged in strictly governmental functions was also held to be a valid exercise of the power of the <ORG> under Section 51 of the Constitution.
Precedent	Section 114 of the Constitution enacts ban on the imposition by the <ORG> of a tax on property of a <ORG>.
Precedent	This ban was not offended.
Precedent	A law which in substances takes a <ORG> or its powers or functions of government as its subject matter is invalid because it cannot be supported upon any grant of legislative power, but there is no implied limitation on <ORG> legislative power under the Constitution arising from the federal nature of the Constitution.
Precedent	There was no necessary implication restraining the <ORG> from making a law according to the view of three learned Judges.
Precedent	Four other learned Judges held that there is an implied limitation as lack of <ORG> legislative power but the Act did not offend such limitation.
Precedent	1037.
Precedent	The limitation which was suggested to be accepted was that a Common wealth law was bad if it discriminated against <ORG> in the sense that it imposed some special burden or disability upon them so that it might be described as a law aimed at their restriction or control.
Precedent	1038.
Precedent	In the Australian case <NAME>, <NAME> stated that the basic principles of construction of the Australian Constitution were definitely enunciated in <ORG> v. <ORG> (1920) 28 C.L.R.
Precedent	129 which unequivocally rejected the doctrine that there was an implied prohibition in the Constitution against the exercise in relation to a State of a legislative power of the Commonwealth in accordance with the ordinary rules of Constitution.
Precedent	1039.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> relied on some Irish cases in support of theory of implied and inherent limitations.
Precedent	1040.
Precedent	In Rayan case 1935 Irish Report 170 the validity of amendment of Article 50 of the Irish Constitution which came into existence in 1922 fell for consideration.
Precedent	Article 50 provided that within 8 years from the commencemen t of the Constitution amendments to the Constitution were to be made by ordinary legislation.
Precedent	After the expiry of 8 years amendments were to be made by referendum.
Precedent	The other provision in Article 50 was that amendment "shall be subject to the provisions of Article 47" of the Constitution.
Precedent	Article 47 made provisions for the suspension in certain events of any Bill for a period of 90 days and for the submission of any bill so suspended to referendum if demand should be made.
Precedent	By an Amendment Act in 1928 reference to the provisions of Article 47 was repealed.
Precedent	In 1929 before the expiry of 8 years there was an amendment of the Constitution whereby the period of 8 years was changed to 16 years.
Precedent	Both the amendments were upheld.
Precedent	Amendment were challenged on two grounds : First, that many Articles of the Constitution are so fundamental as to be-incapable of alteration.
Precedent	Second, Article 50 does not authorise any change in these fundamental Articles.
Precedent	1041.
Precedent	The decision of <ORG> in <NAME> and Ors.
Precedent	v. <NAME> y General for the Irish Free State and Ors.
Precedent	1935 A.C. 484 throws a flood of light on the question of amendment of the amending power in a written Constitution.
Precedent	The Treaty and the Constituent Act scheduled to the Irish Free Constitution Act, 1922 being parts of an Imperial Act formed parts of the statute law of the United Kingdom.
Precedent	The first clause of the Treaty provided that Ireland shall have the same Constitutional status in the community of nations known as the British Empire as the Dominion of Canada, Commonwealth of Australia, the Dominion of New Zealand, and the Union of South Africa with a <ORG> having force to make laws for the peace, order and good government of Ireland and an Executive responsible to that <ORG> and shall be styled and known as the Irish Free State.
Precedent	The second clause of the Treaty provided that the law practice and Constitutional usage governing the relationship of the Crown or the representative of the Crown and of the Imperial <ORG> to the Dominion of Canada shall gov ern their relationship to the Irish Free State.
Precedent	Of the Articles of the Constitution, Article 12 created a legislature known as the <ORG> and the sole and exclusive power of making laws for the peace, order and good government of the Irish Free State was vested in the <ORG>.
Precedent	1042.
