Precedent	Article 50 provided that amendments of the Constitution within the terms of the Scheduled Treaty might be made by the <ORG>.
Precedent	Article 66 provided that <ORG> would have appellate jurisdiction from all decisions of <ORG> and the decision of <ORG> would be final and conclusive.
Precedent	The proviso to that Article stated that nothing in the Constitution shall impair the right of any person to petition His Majesty for special leave to appeal from <ORG> to His Majesty in Council.
Precedent	The proviso to Article 66 was inserted to give effect to Article 2 of the Treaty and hence under Article 50 of the Constitution it was argued that the proviso to Article 66 could not be am ended in the way it was sought to amend it by abolishing the right of appeal.
Precedent	Article 50 contained another limitation that amendments within the terms of the Treaty might be made.
Precedent	Clause 2 of the Treaty provided that relations with <ORG> should be the same as the Canadian.
Precedent	By Amendment Act No.
Precedent	6 of 1933 the words "within the terms of the Treaty" were deleted from Article 50.
Precedent	Thereafter Amendment Act No.
Precedent	22 of 1933 was passed abrogating right of appeal to <ORG>.
Precedent	1043.
Precedent	<ORG> in <NAME> case noticed that "Mr. <NAME> for the petitioners rightly conceded that Amendment Act No.
Precedent	16 of 1929 which substituted for the 8 years specified in Article 50 as the period during which amendment might be made without a referendum a period of 16 years was regular and that the validity of the subsequent amendments could not be attacked on the ground that they had not been submitted to the people by referendum.
Precedent	1044.
Argument	It was argued by Mr. <NAME> in that case that the Constituent  <ORG> having accomplished its work went out of existence leaving no successor and no body in authority capable of amending the Constituent Act.
Argument	The argument was in effect that the Constitution was a semi rigid Constitution that is one capable of being amended in detail in the different Articles according to their terms, but not susceptible of any alteration so far as concerns the Constituent Act, unless perhaps by the calling together of a new Constitution assembly by the people of Ireland.
Argument	The decision of <ORG> in <NAME> case was referred to by <ORG>.
Argument	<ORG> held that the <ORG> had power to repeal or amend the Constitution Act and in repealing or amending of parts of an imperial Statute, namely, the Irish Free State Constitution Act, 1922 what the <ORG> did must be deemed to have been done in the Way in which alone it could legally be done, that is by virtue of the powers given by the statute.
Precedent	The abolition of appeals to <ORG> was a v alid amendment.
Precedent	1045.
Precedent	The decision in <NAME> v. <NAME> (1967) 1 A.C. 259 was also relied on by Mr. <NAME> for the theory of implied and inherent limitations.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Criminal Law Amendment Act passed by <ORG> in 1962 contained substantial modifications of the Criminal Procedure Code .
RatioOfTheDecision	There was ex post facto legislation of detention for 60 days of any person suspected of having committed an offence against the <ORG> by widening the class of offences for which trial without jury by three judges nominated by the Minister of Justice would be ordered.
RatioOfTheDecision	An arrest without warrant for waging war against the <NAME> became permissible and new minimum penalties for that offence were prescribed and for conspiring to wage war against the <NAME> and overawe the government by criminal force, and by widening the scope of that offence.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Act also provided for the admission in evidence of certain confessions and statements to the police inadmissible under the Evidence Code.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Act was expressed to be retro spective to cover an abortive coup d'etat on 27 January, 1962 in which <NAME> and others took part, and was to cease to be operative after the conclusion of all legal proceedings connected with or incidental to any offence against the <ORG> committed on or about the date of the commencement of the Act, whichever was later.
Statute	The second Criminal Law Amendment Act of 1962 (No.
Precedent	31 of 1962) substituted the Chief Justice for the Minister of Justice as the person to nominate the three Judges but left unaffected other provisions for the former Act.
Precedent	1046.
Precedent	<ORG> convicted the appellants and sentenced them to 10 years rigorous imprisonment the minimum prescribed by the Criminal Law Act 1 of 1962.
Precedent	1047.
Precedent	<ORG>, held the legislation to be ultra vires on two grounds.
Precedent	The Act s could not be challenged on the ground that they were contrary to fundamental principles of Justice.
Precedent	The Colonial Laws Validity Act 1865 which provided that colonial laws should be void to the extent of repugnancy t o an Act of the United Kingdom, and should not be void on the ground of repugnancy to the law of England did not leave in existence a fetter or repugnancy to some vague and unspecified law of natural justice.
Precedent	The Ceylon Independence Act 1947 conferred on <ORG> full legislative powers of a sovereign independent State.
Precedent	The Act s were declared to be bad because they involved a usurpation and infringement, by the legislature of judicial powers inconsistent with the written Constitution of Ceylon.
Precedent	The silence of the Constitution as to the vesting of judicial power was inconsistent with any intention that it should pass to or be shared by the executive or the legislature.
Precedent	The ratio of the decision is that the legislature could not usurp judicial power.
Precedent	There is an observation at page 289 of the report that Section 29(1) of the Ceylon Constitution confers power on <ORG> to pass legislation which does not enable a law to usurp the judicial power of the judicature.
Precedent	<ORG> answered  the question which was posed as to what the position would be if <ORG> sought to procure such a result by first amending the Constitution by a two-thirds majority by stating that such a situation did not arise there and if any Act was passed without recourse to Section 29(4) of the Ceylon Constitution it would be ultra vires.
Precedent	<ORG> found that under Section 29(4) of the Ceylon Constitution there could be an amendment only by complying with the proviso, which would be the manner and form and would not be a limitation on the width of the power.
Precedent	The Ceylon case is not an authority for the proposition of implied and inherent limitation on the amending power.
Precedent	1048.
Precedent	In Liyanage case <ORG> rejected the contention that powers of the Ceylon Legislation should be cut down by reference to the vague and uncertain expression "fundamental principles of British Law".
Precedent	In deciding whether the Constitution of Ceylon provided for a separation between the legislature and the judiciary <ORG> ouncil did not refer to consequences at all, but referred to the fact that the provisions relating to the legislature and the judicature were found in two separate parts of the Constitution.
Precedent	The provisions for appointment of the subordinate judiciary by a <ORG> consisting exclusively of Judges with a prohibition against any legislator being a member thereof and the further provision that any attempt to influence the decision was a criminal offence were held by <ORG> to show that the judiciary was intended to be kept separate from the legislature and the executive.
Precedent	This conclusion was based on a pure construction of the provisions of the Act.
Precedent	The reference to consequences was in a different context.
Precedent	<ORG> recognised that the impugned law dealt with a grave exceptional situation and were prepared to assume that the legislature believed that it had power to enact it.
Precedent	1049.
Precedent	Again in <NAME> case 1968 A.C. 717 <ORG> considered a Ceylon Act which was inconsisten t with the Ceylon Constitution.
Precedent	The Act imposed civic disabilities for 7 years on person to whom the Act applied and provided for the vacation of the scat as a Member of <ORG>.
Precedent	The words amend or repeal in Section 29(4) of the Ceylon Constitution were read by <ORG> to cover an amendment or repeal by inconsistent act.
Precedent	The plain words amend or repeal did not admit ambiguity.
Precedent	1050.
Precedent	To introduce into our Constitution the doctrine of implied and inherent limitations on the meaning of the word "amendment" by upholding the power to amend the essential features but not the core on the theory that only people can change by referendum is to rewrite the Constitution.
Precedent	The decisions in <NAME> case 1965 A.C. 172 and <NAME> case 1968 A.C. 717 are authorities for two propositions.
Precedent	First, that in the exercise of the pouter of amendment a controlled Constitution can be converted into an uncontrolled one.
Precedent	Second, the word "amendment" means alteration.
Precedent	In <NAME> case 1964 A.C. 900 the Judicial Com mittee said that if the Ceylon legislature abrogated the appeal to <ORG> it would be an amendment of its judicial structure.
Precedent	1051.
Precedent	The decision in <CITATION_1672729> (1967) 2 S.C.R.
Precedent	109 has been relied on by Mr. <NAME> in support of the proposition that the power of amendment is subject to implied limitation.
Precedent	Article 4 of the Constitution which was interpreted in <NAME> case has to be read with Articles 2 and 3.
Precedent	Article 4 contains a limited power of amendment, limited to amend Schedules 1 and 4 as may be necessary to give effect to a law mentioned in Articles 2 and 3 and of making supplemental, incidental and consequential provisions.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. in <NAME> case said that power with which <ORG> is invested by Articles 2 and 3 is a power to admit, establish or form new <ORG>s or to admit, establish or admit new <ORG>s which conform to the democratic pattern envisaged by the Constitution and is not a power to override the Constitutional scheme.
Precedent	It is manifest that when a new <ORG> i s created in accordance with Articles 2 and 3 the amendment under Article 4 will be followed up as necessary to give effect to the same.
Precedent	Such an amendment does not override the Constitutional scheme.
Precedent	It is an amending power of a limited nature and is supplemental, incidental or consequential to the admission, establishment or formation of a <ORG> as contemplated by the Constitution.
Precedent	This decision does not say that there are implied limitations to the amending power.
Precedent	1052.
Precedent	The petitioner challenges the legality and the validity of the Constitution (25th) Amendment Act .
Precedent	1053.
Precedent	The Constitution (25th) Amendment Act has first amended Article 31(2 ), second added Article 31 (2B) and third introduced Article 31C.
Precedent	Article 31(2) is amended in two respects.
Precedent	First, it substituted the word "amount" for the word "compensation" for property acquired or requisitioned.
Precedent	Second, it is provided that the acquisition or requisition law shall not be called in question on the ground that whole or any part of the amount is to be  given otherwise than in cash.
Precedent	Article 31 (2B) has been inserted to the effect that nothing in Sub-clause (f) of Clause (1) of Article 19 shall effect any such law as is referred to in Clause (2).
Precedent	1054.
Precedent	Article 31C states that notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 no law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause  (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Article 14 or Article 19 or Article 31 and no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy.
Precedent	It is provided that where such law is made by the legislature of a <ORG> the provisions of this Article shall not apply thereto unless such law having been reserved for the consideration of the President has received his assent.
Precedent	1055.
Precedent	The basic controversy is really re garding the right to property and the acquisition of property by the <ORG>.
Precedent	The Constitution of India was intended to achieve political liberty on the one hand and economic and social, liberty on the other for all citizens of India.
Precedent	The Directive Principles in the Constitution are also fundamental in the governance of the country and it shall be the duty of the <ORG> to apply these principles in making laws.
Precedent	That is Article 37.
Precedent	It can be achieved by making changes in the economic and social structure of the society.
Precedent	1056.
Precedent	The resolutions of the <ORG> in 1929, 1931, 1945 and the objective resolution of 22 January, 1947 and the resolution of All-India <ORG> Working Committee in 1947 are not only a remembrance of things past.
Precedent	In 1929 the <ORG> resolution was that it was essential to make revolutionary changes in the economic and social structure of the society and to remove the gross inequalities.
Precedent	It was also resolved that political freedom must include the economic freedom of the starving millions.
Precedent	In  such economic and social programme the <ORG> is to own or control the key industries and services, mineral resources, railways, waterways, shipping and other means of public transport.
Precedent	In 1945 <ORG> said that the concentration of wealth and power in the hands of individuals and groups was to be prevented.
Precedent	Social control of the mineral resources and of the principal methods of production and distribution in land, industry and in other departments of national activity would be necessary to develop the country into cooperative commonwealth.
Precedent	In the case of industries which in their nature must be run on a large scale and on centralised basis, it was felt that they should belong to the community and they should be so organised that the workers become not only co-sharers in the profits but also increasingly associated with the management and administration of the industry.
Precedent	Land and all other means of production as well as distribution and exchange must belong to and be regulated by the community in  its own interest.
Precedent	The framers of the Constitution wanted a social structure which would avoid the acquisitive economy of private capitalism and the regimentation of a totalitarian <ORG>.
Precedent	1057.
Precedent	In this background the Constitution was created with the object of effecting social revolution.
Precedent	The core of the commitment to the social revolution lies in Part III and Part IV of the Constitution.
Precedent	They are described to be "conscience of the Constitution".
Precedent	The object of Part III was to "liberate the power of man equally for distribution to the common good".
Precedent	The <ORG> would have to bear the responsibility for the welfare of citizens.
Precedent	The Directive Principles are a declaration of economic independence so that our country men would have economic as well as political control of the country.
Precedent	1058.
Precedent	The centre of the fundamental rights is said by Mr. <NAME> to be Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is right to property.
RatioOfTheDecision	But the Directive Principles are also fundamental.
RatioOfTheDecision	They can be effective if they are to prevail over fundamental  rights of a few in order to subserve the common good and not to allow economic system to result to the common detriment.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is the duty of the <ORG> to promote common good.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the motives for co-operating with others consist in the mere desire to promote their private good they would be treating their fellowmen as means only and not also an end.
RatioOfTheDecision	The notion of common good was needed to explain away the difference between the principles of reasonable self love and benevolence.
RatioOfTheDecision	The distribution of material resources is to subserve the common good.
RatioOfTheDecision	The ownership and control of the material resources is to subserve common good.
RatioOfTheDecision	The economic system is to work in such a manner that there is no concentration of wealth to the common detriment.
RatioOfTheDecision	Again, the economic system is to work in such a manner that the means of production are not used to the common detriment.
RatioOfTheDecision	1059.
RatioOfTheDecision	The declaration of human rights on which Mr. <NAME> relied for the unamendability of fundamental rights is rightly said by the Attorney General t o be no impediment to the power of amendment nor to support the petitioner's contention regarding the inviolability of the right to property.
RatioOfTheDecision	For the purpose of promoting the general welfare in a democratic <ORG> the Directive Principles were said by the Attorney General to be fundamental in achieving rights of men and economic and social rights for human dignity.
RatioOfTheDecision	Every citizen asserts enjoyment for fundamental rights under the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It becomes the corresponding duty of every citizen to give effect to fundamental rights of all citizens, dignity of all citizens, by allowing the <ORG> to achieve the Directive Principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	The duty of the <ORG> is not limited to the protection of individual interest but extends to acts for the achievement of the general welfare in all cases where it can safely act and the only limitations on the governmental actions are dictated by the experience of the needs of time.
RatioOfTheDecision	A fundamental right may be regarded as fundamental by one generation.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may be considered to be inconven ient limitation upon legislative power by another generation.
RatioOfTheDecision	Popular sovereignty means that the interest which prevails must be the interest of the mass of men.
RatioOfTheDecision	If rights are built upon property those who have no property will have no rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	That is why the <ORG> has to balance interest of the individual with the interest of the society.
RatioOfTheDecision	Industrial democracy is the necessary complement to polticial democracy.
RatioOfTheDecision	The <ORG> has to serve its members by organising an avenue of consumption.
RatioOfTheDecision	This can be done by socialisation of those elements in the common welfare which are integral to the well being of the community.
Precedent	1060.
Precedent	The petitioner's challenge to the amendment on Article 31(2) is as follows.
Precedent	The right to property is one of the essential features of the Constitution.
Precedent	It is the hand maid to various other fundamental rights.
Precedent	The right to freedom of the Press under Article 19(1)(a) is meaningless if the publisher could be deprived of his printing plant and the building in which it is housed without compensation.
Precedent	The fundamental right under Article 19(1)(c) to form trade unions will be denuded of its true content if the property of a trade union could be acquired by the <ORG> without compensation.
Precedent	The right to practise any profession or carry on any occupation, trade or business under Article 19(1)(g) will be the right to do forced labour for the <ORG> if the net savings from the fruits of a citizen's personal exertion are liable to be acquired by the <ORG> without compensation.
Precedent	The freedom of religion in Article 26 will lose a great deal of its efficacy if the institutions maintained by a community for its religious and charitable purposes could be acquired without compensation.
Precedent	The implication of the proviso to Article 31(2) is that the <ORG> may fix such an amount for acquisition of the property as may abridge or abrogate any of the other fundamental rights.
Precedent	Exercise of fundamental rights would be affected by the deprivation of property without compensation in the legal sense and the only exception to this power of  the <ORG> is the case of educational institution dealt with in the proviso.
Precedent	Article 31(2) as a result of the Constitution (25th) Amendment Act will empower the <ORG> to fix an amount on a basis which need not be disclosed even to the members of the legislature and which may have no relation to the property sought to be acquired.
Precedent	The amount is not to satisfy any of the principles of compensation.
Precedent	It need not be paid in cash and it will yet not be considered to be a ground of challenge to the validity of law.
Precedent	Article 31(2) has nothing to do with estate, zamindaries, land reforms or agrarian reforms which are specifically dealt with by Article 31A.
Precedent	1061.
Precedent	The right to acquire, hold and dispose of property under Article 19(1)(f) is subject under Article 19(5) to reasonable restrictions in the interests of the general public If Article 19(5) permits such reasonable restrictions it is said by the petitioner that the only object of making Article 19(1)(f) inapplicable by Article 31(2B) is to enable acquisition and  requisition laws to contain restrictions or provisions which are unreasonable and not in the public interest.
Precedent	Reliance was placed by Mr. <NAME> on the Bank Nationalisation case (1970) 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	530 and the observations at p. 577 that if Article 19(1)(f) applied to acquisition or requisition, law which permitted a property to be taken without the owner being heard where the rules of natural justice would require the owner to be heard, would be void as offending Article 19(1)(f).
Precedent	Extracting that observation it is said that the amount fixed without giving him a hearing or amending the Land Acquisition Act to provide that any man's land or house can be acquired without notice to the owner to show cause or to prove what amount should be fairly paid to him for the property acquired will damage the essence or core of fundamental right to property.
Precedent	1062.
Precedent	After the substitution of the neutral expression "amount" for "compensation" in Article 31(2) by the Constitution (25th) Amendment Act the Article still binds t he legislature to provide for the giving to the owner a sum of money either in cash or otherwise.
Precedent	The legislature may either lay down principles for the determination of the amount or may itself fix the amount.
Precedent	Before the amendment the interpretation of Article 31(2) was that the law was bound to provide for the payment of compensation in the sense of equivalent in value of the property acquired.
Precedent	This was the interpretation given in the Bank Nationalisation case even after the Constitution 24th Amendment Act , which said that the adequacy of compensation could not be challenged.
Precedent	The Constitution 25th Amendment Act states that the law no longer need provide for the giving of equivalent in value of the acquired property.
Precedent	The quantum of the amount if directly fixed by the law and the principles for its quantification are matters for legislative judgment.
Precedent	Specification of principles means laying down general guiding rules applicable to all persons or transactions covered thereby.
Precedent	In fixing the amount the legislat ure will act on the general nature of the legislative power.
Precedent	The principle may be specified.
Precedent	The principle which may be acted upon by the legislature in fixing the amount may include considerations of social justice as against the equivalent in value of the property acquired.
Precedent	Considerations of social justice will include the relevant Directive Principles particularly in Article 39(b) and (c).
Precedent	These principles are to subserve the common good and to prevent common detriment.
Precedent	The question of adequacy has been excluded from Article 31(2) by the Constitution Fourth Amendment Act.
Precedent	It cannot be said that the legislature would be under the necessity of providing a standard to measure an adequacy with reference to fixing the amount.
Precedent	The Constitution does not allow judicial review of a law on the ground of adequacy of the amount and the manner as to how such amount is to be given otherwise than in cash.
Precedent	1063.
Precedent	If the word "compensation" as it stood prior to the amendment of Article 31(2) must mean equivalent value in c ash it is said by the Solicitor General that the concentration of wealth will remain unchanged and justice social, economic, and political amplified in Articles 39, 41, 42, 43, 45, 46 and 47 will be thwarted.
Precedent	The fulfilment of the Directive Principles is in a sense more fundamental than the mere right to property.
Precedent	Re- adjustment in the social order may not be practicable in a smooth manner unless the Directive Principles are effectively implemented.
RatioOfTheDecision	The emergence of a new social order is a challenge to present day civilisation.
RatioOfTheDecision	If nations wanted independence and supremacy in the latter half of the 19th century and the first half of the 20th century individual dignity, individual freedom, individual status in a well organised and well planned society are opening the frontiers since the mid-century.
RatioOfTheDecision	In this background the 25th Amendment protects the law in one respect, namely, that amount payable to the owner is no longer to be measured by the standard of equivalent in value of the acquired property.
RatioOfTheDecision	The quantu m cannot be a matter for judicial review.
RatioOfTheDecision	Ever since the Fourth Amendment the adequacy of compensation is excluded by the Constitution.
Precedent	The reason is that the Constitution declares in clear terms that adequacy is not justiciable and therefore, it cannot be made justiciable in an indirect manner by holding that the same subject matter which is expressly barred is contained implicitly in some other provision and is, therefore, open to examination.
Precedent	1064.
Precedent	Just as principles which were irrelevant to compensation were invalid prior to the Constitution 25th Amendment it was said that if any principles are adopted which are irrelevant to the concept of amount as a legal concept or as having a norm the law would be invalid because the amount would be purely at the will or at the discretion of the <ORG>.
Precedent	Therefore, it was said that when the law fixes the amount it might indicate the principles on which the amount had been arrived at or the <ORG> might enquire into on which the amount had been fixed.
Precedent	Any contrary view  according to the petitioner would mean that under Article 31(2) state would have authority to specify principles which could be arbitrary or specify the amount which could be arbitrary.
Precedent	1065.
Precedent	It was also said that as a result of the proviso to Article 31(2) after the 25th Amendment the law providing for compulsory acquisition of property of an educational institution established by a minority referred to in Article 31(1) the <ORG> was to ensure that the amount fixed or determined was such as would not restrict or abrogate the right guaranteed under that clause.
Precedent	The amount would have to be higher than the amount which would be sufficient not to damage the essence of that right.
Precedent	But under Article 31(2) after the 25th Amendment where the proviso did not apply it was said that the core or essence of the fundamental rights would be damaged or destroyed.
Precedent	1066.
RatioOfTheDecision	The word "amount" in Article 31(2) after the 25th Amendment is to be read in the entire collocation of words.
RatioOfTheDecision	No law shall be called in question in any Cour t on the ground that the amount so fixed or determined is inadequate or the whole or part of it or any part of such amount is given in cash.
Statute	In Article 31(2) the use of the word "amount" in conjunction with payment in cash shows that a sum of money is being spoken of.
Statute	Amount is a sum meaning a quantity or amount of money, or, in other words, amount means a sum of money.
Precedent	1067.
Precedent	Article 31(2) prior to as well as after the 25th Amendment indicates two alternatives to the legislatures either to specify the principles for determination of the amount or to fix the amount or "compensation" prior to the amendment.
Precedent	In fixing the amount or compensation the legislature is not required to set out in the law the principles on which compensation had been fixed in the unamended clause or the amount is fixed in the amended clause.
Precedent	1068.
Precedent	Article 19(1)(f) provides that all citizens shall have the right to hold, acquire or dispose of property whereas Article 31(2) deals with law by which the property is acquired.
Precedent	Such law acqu iring property directly extinguishes the right to hold or dispose of property acquired.
Precedent	Article 19(1)(f) is excluded from Article 31(2) in order to make Article 31(2) self contained.
Precedent	The right to hold property cannot coexist with the right of the <ORG> to acquire property.
Precedent	That is why Article 31(2) is to be read with Article 31A , 31B and 31C , all the Articles being under the heading "Right to Property".
Precedent	1069.
Precedent	It has been held by this <ORG> in F.N.
Precedent	Rana case (1964) 5 S.C.R.
Argument	294 that Land Acquisition Act does not give the right of quasi-judicial procedure or the requirements of natural justice as Section 5A of that Act has been held to be administrative.
Argument	It has also been held by this <ORG> that a Requisition Act which did not give a right of representation before an order for requisition was made did not violate Article 19(1)(f).
Argument	( <CITATION_668022> A.I.R.
Fact	1971 SC 961).
Fact	1070.
Fact	The other part of the 25th Amendment which is challenged by the petitioner is Article 31C.
Fact	Article 31C is said by Mr. <NAME> to d estroy several essential features of the Constitution for these reasons.
Fact	First, there is a distinction between cases where the fundamental rights are amended and laws which would have been void before the 25th Amendment are permitted to be validly passed and cases where the fundamental rights remain unamended but the laws which are void as offending those rights are validated by a legal fiction that they shall not be deemed to be void.
Fact	The law is in the first case Constitutional in reality whereas in the second case the law is unConstitutional in reality but is deemed by a fiction of law not to be void with the result that laws which violate the Constitution are validated and there is a repudiation of the Constitution.
Fact	If Article 31C is valid it would be permissible to <ORG> to amend the Constitution so as to declare all laws to be valid which are passed by <ORG> or State legislatures in excess of legislative competence or which violate basic human rights enshrined in Part III or the freedom of inte r-State Trade in Article 301.
Fact	Article 31C gives a blank charter to <ORG> and the State legislatures to defy the Constitution or damage or destroy the supremacy of the Constitution.
Fact	Secondly, Article 31C subordinates fundamental rights to Directive Principles.
Fact	The right to enforce fundamental rights is guaranteed under Article  32.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Directive Principles are not enforceable by reason of Article 37.
RatioOfTheDecision	Yet it is said that while giving effect to Directive Principles fundamental rights are abrogated.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thirdly, whereas an amendment of a single fundamental right would require a majority of at least two-thirds of the members of <ORG> present and voting, a law within Article 31C which overrides and violates several fundamental rights can be passed by a simple majority.
Fact	Fourthly, every fundamental right is an essential feature of the Constitution and Article 31C purports to take away a large number of those fundamental rights.
Fact	Fifthly, the Court is precluded from considering whether law under Article 31C is s uch that it can possibly secure Directive Principles in question.
Fact	Sixthly, no State legislature can amend the fundamental rights or any other part of the Constitution but Article 31C empowers the State legislaure to pass laws which virtually involve repeal of the fundamental rights.
Fact	Power of amending the Constitution is delegated to State legislatures.
Precedent	1071.
Precedent	Finally, it is said that the fundamental rights under Article 14 , 19 and 31 which are sought to be superseded by Article 31C are necessary to make meaningful specific rights of the minorities which are guaranteed by Articles 25 to 30.
Precedent	The proviso to Article 31(2) shows that in the case of acquisition of property of an educational institution established by a minority an amount fixed should be such as not to restrict or abrogate the right of the minorities under Article 31.
Precedent	It is, therefore, said that the implication is that if property is acquired in cases other than those of minorities an amount can be fixed which restricts or abrogates any of the funda mental rights.
Precedent	Again, it is said that if a law violates the right of the minority under Articles 25 to 30 such a law would be no law.
Precedent	Therefore, deprivation of property under such law would violate Article 31(1).
Precedent	But the 25th Amendment by Article 31C abrogates Article 31(1) and minorities can be deprived of their properties held privately or upon public, charitable or religious trusts by law which violates Articles 25 to 30.
Precedent	1072.
RatioOfTheDecision	The pre-eminent feature of Article 31C is that it protects only law.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, any question of violation of Article 31(1) does not arise.
RatioOfTheDecision	Law referred to in Article 31C must be made either by <ORG> or by the State legislature, according to the legislative procedure for enacting a law.
RatioOfTheDecision	There are several Articles in the Constitution where the expression "law" with reference to the authority to make law has been used.
RatioOfTheDecision	These are Articles 17, 19(2) to (6), 21, 22, 23(1), 26, 31, 33, 34 and 35.
RatioOfTheDecision	These Articles indicate that the expression "law" there means law made by the legislatur e in accordance with its ordinary legislative procedure.
RatioOfTheDecision	The expression "law" does not include within itself ordinance, order, bye-law; rule, regulation, notification, custom or usage having the force of law nor an amendment of the Constitution in accordance with the procedure prescribed in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	In Article 13 the term "law" has been used in a wide sense.
RatioOfTheDecision	For this a definition was given in Article 13(3) to include certain other categories.
RatioOfTheDecision	The definition in Article 13(3) is expressly limited for Article 13.
Precedent	Law in Article 31C must have the same meaning as it has in other Articles generally, namely, a statute passed by the legislature.
Precedent	1073.
Precedent	It is true that such law may need details to be filled up by other agencies but the essential elements of Article 31C must be supplied directly by that enactment.
Precedent	A question arose with reference to Article 254 as to whether a clause of the Sugar Control Order 1955 made under the Essential Commodities Act had the effect of repealing the corresponding Uttar Pradesh St ate Law.
Precedent	This Court held that the power of repeal was vested in <ORG> and <ORG> alone could exercise it by enacting an appropriate provision in that regard.
Precedent	<ORG> could not delegate the power of repeal to any executive authority.
Precedent	(See Ch.
Precedent	Tika Ramji and Ors.
Precedent	Etc.
Precedent	v. The State of Uttar Pradesh and Ors.
Precedent	1956 S.C.R.
Precedent	393).
Precedent	1074.
Precedent	Article 31C is inextricably bound up with Article 39(b) and (c) because the purpose and the phraseology in both the Articles are essentially identifical.
Precedent	The legislative efforts to implement Directive Principles in Article 39 (b) and (c) were set in motion in some States to achieve reforms in land law.
Fact	Articles 31A and 31B were introduced by the Constitution First Amendment Act 1951.
Fact	The main reason for introducing Articles 31A and 31B was to exclude the operation of Part III as a whole from those provisions.
Fact	The true relationship between Directive Principles in Part IV and the fundamental rights in Part III became clear.
Fact	It was realised that though the liberty of individu al was valuable it should not operate as an insurmountable barrier against the achievement of Directive Principles.
Precedent	In <NAME> case (1965) 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	933 it was said that "the rights of society are made paramount and they are placed above those of the individual".
Precedent	In the Bihar Land Reforms case 1952 S.C.R.
Precedent	889 it was said that "a fresh outlook which placed the general `interest of the community above the interest of the individuals, pervades over Constitution".
Precedent	1075.
Precedent	Law contemplated in Article 31C will operate on the ownership and control of the material resources of the community to be distributed as best to subserve the common good.
Precedent	The operation of the economic system should not result in concentration of wealth.
Precedent	Means of production should not be used to the common detriment.
Precedent	The ownership and control of the material resources of the community can be achieved by nationalisation and planned economy.
Precedent	The operation of the economic system will mean imposition of control on the production, supply and distr ibutions of products of key industries and essential commodities.
Precedent	There can be laws within Schedule 7 List III Entries No.
Precedent	42, 43; List I Entry No.
Precedent	52 to 54 and List II Entries No.
Precedent	23, 24, 26 and 27.
Precedent	1076.
Precedent	The provisions in Article 31C that no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy was questioned by the petitioner to exclude judicial review and, therefore, to be illegal.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31C was in the second place said to enable the <ORG> legislatures to make discriminatory laws destructive of the integrity of India.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thirdly, Article 31C was said to delegate the amending power to <ORG> legislatures or <ORG> in its ordinary legislative capacity.
RatioOfTheDecision	1077.
RatioOfTheDecision	The declaration mentioned in Article 31C is for giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles in Article 39 (b) or (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	Such a declaration in a law shall not be called in question on the ground that it does  not give effect to such policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	The laws which receive protection under Article 31C are laws for securing the Directive Principles of Articles 39(b) and (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	The nexus or connection between the law and the objectives set out in Article 39(b) and (c) is a condition precedent for the applicability of Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	On behalf of the <ORG> and the <ORG> it was not contended that whether there was such nexus or not was not justiciable.
RatioOfTheDecision	The real reason for making the declaration free from question in a Court of law on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy is to leave legislative policy and wisdom to the legislature.
RatioOfTheDecision	The legislative measure might not according to some views give effect to Directive Principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, legislatures are left in charge of formulating their policy and giving effect to it through legislation.
Precedent	It is the assessment and judgment of such measures which is sought to be excluded from judicial review by the declaration.
Precedent	1078.
Precedent	In order to decide whether a statute is within Ar ticle 31C the court may examine the nature and the character of legislation and the matter dealt with as to whether there is any nexus or the law to the principles mentioned in Article 39(b) and (c).
Precedent	If it appears that there no nexus between the legislation and the objectives and principles mentioned in Article 39(b) and (c) the legislation will not be within the protective umbrella.
Precedent	The Court can tear the veil to decide the real nature of the statute if the facts and circumstances warrant such a course.
Precedent	1079.
Precedent	The reason for excepting Articles 14, 19 and 31 from Article 31C is the same as in Article 31A.
Precedent	The Solicitor General rightly said that the fear of discrimination is allayed by three safeguards.
RatioOfTheDecision	The first and the foremost safeguard is the good sense of the legislature and the innate good sense of the community.
RatioOfTheDecision	The second safeguard is the President's assent.
RatioOfTheDecision	The third safeguard is that in appropriate cases it can be found as to whether there is any nexus between law and Directive Principles sought to b e achieved.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no better safeguard than the character of the citizen, the character of the legislature, the faith of the people in the representatives and the responsibility of the representatives to the nation.
RatioOfTheDecision	No sense of irresponsibility can be ascribed or attributed to the representatives of the people.
RatioOfTheDecision	The exclusion of Article 14 is to evolve new principles of equality in the light of Directive Principles.
Precedent	The exclusion of Article 19 is on the footing that laws which are to give effect to Directive Principles will constitute reasonable restrictions on the individual's liberty.
Precedent	The exclusion of Article 31(2) is to introduce the consideration of social justice in the matter of acquisition.
Precedent	Directive Principles are not limited to agrarian reforms.
Precedent	Directive Principles are necessary for the uplift and growth of industry in the country.
Precedent	1080.
Precedent	Article 31(4) and 31(6) speak of certain class of laws not being called in question on the ground of contravention of Article 31(2).
Precedent	Article 31A relates to law o f the class mentioned therein not to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the fundamental rights conferred by Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	Article 15(4) states that nothing in Article 15 or in Article 29(2) shall prevent the <ORG> from making any special provision for the advancement of any socially and educationally backward classes of citizens or for the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes.
Precedent	Article 31(5)(b)(ii) states that nothing in Article 31(2) shall affect the provisions of any law which the <ORG> may make for the promotion of public health.
Precedent	Article 33 speaks of law with regard to members of <ORG> charged with the maintenance of public order, so as to ensure the proper discharge of their duties and the maintenance of discipline among them and for that purpose the operation of some fundamental right in Part III is modified.
Precedent	1081.
Precedent	The Solicitor General rightly said that similarly Article 31C creates a legislative field with reference to the objec t of legislation.
Precedent	It is similar to laws contemplated in Article 15(4 ), Article 31(5)(b)(ii) and Article 33.
Precedent	Each of these Articles carves out an exception to some Article or Articles conferring fundamental rights.
Precedent	The field carved out by the various Articles are of different dimensions.
Precedent	The entire process of exception of the legislative field from the operation of some of the Articles relating to fundamental rights is the mandate of the Constitution.
Precedent	It is wrong to say that the Constitution delegates power of amendment to <ORG> or the States.
Precedent	As a result of the 25th Amendment the existing legislative field is freed from the fetters of some provisions of Part III of our Constitution on the legislative power.
Precedent	1082.
Precedent	Article 31C substantially operates in the same manner in the industrial sphere as Article 31A operates in the agrarian sphere.
Fact	The problems are similar in nature though of different magnitude.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitutional method adopted to solve the problem is similar.
Precedent	The Solicitor General is correct i n summing up Article 31C as an application of the principles underlying Articles 31(4) and 31(6) and Article 31A to the sphere of industry.
Precedent	1083.
Precedent	A class of legislation can be identified and the legislative field can be carved out from the operation of fundamental rights or some of those can be excluded by a provision of the Constitution.
Precedent	Articles 31(4) and 31(6) identify the laws with reference to the period during which they were made.
Precedent	Article 31(4) relates to a bill pending at the commencement of the Constitution in the legislature of a State to have been passed by such legislature and to have received the assent of the President to be not called in question on the ground that it contravenes Article 31(2).
Precedent	Article 31(6) relates to law of the State enacted not more than 18 months from the commencement of the Constitution to he submitted to the President for his certification and upon certification by the President not to be called in question on the ground of contravention of Article 31(2).
Precedent	Articles 31(2)  and 31A identify the legislative field with reference to the subject matter of law.
Precedent	Articles 15(4) and 33 and Article 31(5)(b)(ii) identify laws with reference to the objective of the legislature.
Precedent	The exceptions to some part or some Articles of Part III of the Constitution is created by the Constitution and any law which is made pursuant to such power conferred by the Constitution does not amend the operation or application of these Articles in Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	The crux of the matter is that modification or exception regarding the application of some of the Articles in Part III is achieved by the mandate of the Constitution and not by the law which is to be made by <ORG> or <ORG> under Article 31C.
Precedent	Therefore, there is no delegation of amending powers.
Precedent	There is no amendment of any Constitutional provision by such law.
Precedent	1084.
Precedent	The Constitution First Amendment Act 1951 introduced Articles 31A and 31B and Schedule 9 which are to be read together.
Precedent	Article 31A excluded a challenge under the whole o f Part III for the laws of the kind mentioned in that Article.
Precedent	Article 31B restrospectively validated laws mentioned in Schedule 9 from challenge under Part III and also on the ground that they violated Section 299 of the Government of India Act, 1935.
Precedent	It may be stated here that <ORG> which passed the Constitution First Amendment Act 1951 was <ORG> functioning as a legislature, till elections were held and a <ORG> as provided for under the Constitution could be formed.
Precedent	Articles 31A and 31B carried out the intention of the framers of the Constitution as stated in Articles 31(4) and 31(6) that land legislation or agrarian reform was to be enforced and fundamental rights were not to be allowed to stand in the way of implementing the Directive Principles of State Policy contained in Article 39.
Precedent	The fundamental right conferred under Article 31(2) was subordinated to Article 39(b) and (c) in order to protect laws referred to in Article 31(4) and 31(6).
Precedent	When that object failed and the la w was struck down under Article 14 , <ORG> gave effect to the policy underlying Articles 31(4) and 31(6) by excluding a challenge under every Article in Part III.
Precedent	In the Bihar Land Reforms case this <ORG> said that the purpose behind the Bihar Land Reform Act was to bring about a reform of the land distribution system in Bihar for the general benefit of the community and the legislature was the best judge of what was good for the community and it was not possible for this <ORG> to say that there was no public purpose behind the acquisition contemplated in the statute.
Precedent	1085.
Precedent	This <ORG> in <CITATION_1890860> 1954 S.C.R.
Precedent	558 held that the word "compensation" means just equivalent or full indemnity for the property expropriated.
Precedent	<CITATION_1880952> .
Precedent	1954 S.C.R.
Fact	674 this <ORG> struck down the law for taking over the management of <ORG> on the ground that it amounted to acquisition and since no compensation was provided for, the law was held to be void.
Fact	The Constitution Fourth Amendment Act 1955 came  to remedy the implementation of essential welfare legislation.
Fact	One of the measures in the Fourth Amendment Act was the amendment of Article 31 by making adequacy of compensation non-justiciable and the other was to amend Article 31A.
Fact	The formula which had been used in Articles 31(4) and 31(6) to exclude the contravention of Article 31(2) was adopted with regard to adequacy of compensation.
Fact	As a result of the amendment of Article 31A new categories were added to the Article and new Acts were added to the Ninth Schedule.
Fact	The 17th Amendment Act made changes in Article 31A(1) and the proviso and amended Schedule 9 by inserting new Acts therein.
Precedent	1086.
Precedent	The successive amendments of the Constitution merely carried out the principle embodied in Article 31 Clauses (4) and (6) that legislation designed to secure the public good and to implement the Directives under Article 39(b) and (c) should have priority over individual rights and that therefore fundamental rights were to fee subordinate to Directive or State Policy .
Precedent	1087.
Statute	Article 31(2) as it originally stood spoke of compensation for acquisition or requisition of property.
RatioOfTheDecision	The meaning given to compensation by the <ORG> was full market value.
RatioOfTheDecision	There was no scope for giving effect to the word "compensation".
RatioOfTheDecision	There was no flexibility of social interest in Article 31(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	Every concept of social interest became irrelevant by the scope of Article 13(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	It is this mischief which was sought to be remedied by the 25th Amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	If Directive Principles are to inter-play with Part III legislation will have to give expression to such law.
RatioOfTheDecision	Parts III and IV of the Constitution touch each other and modify.
RatioOfTheDecision	They are not parallel to each other.
RatioOfTheDecision	Different legislation will bring in different social principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	These will not be permissible without social content operating in a flexible manner.
Precedent	That is why in the 25th Amendment Article 31(2) is amended to eliminate the concept of market value for property which is acquired or requisitioned.
Precedent	1088.
Precedent	If compensation means an amount determi ned on principles of social justice there will be general harmony between Part III and Part IV.
Precedent	Secondly, if compensation means market price then the concept of property right in Part III is an absolute right to own and possess property or to receive full price, while the concept of property right in Part IV is conditioned by social interest and social justice.
RatioOfTheDecision	There would be an inherent conflict in working out the Directive Principles of Part IV with the guarantee in Part III.
RatioOfTheDecision	That is why Clauses (4) and (6) of Article 31 illustrate the vital principle that to make effective a legislative effort to bring about changes in accordance with Directive Principles particularly those contained in Article 39(b) and (c)  Article 31(2) may have to be abridged.
RatioOfTheDecision	The social interest and justice may vary from time to time and territory to territory and individual rights may have to be limited.
Precedent	1089.
Precedent	Just as the amount can be fixed on principles of social justice the principles for determining the amount can be specified o n the same consideration of social justice.
Precedent	Amount is fixed or the principles are specified by the norm of social justice in accordance with Directive Principles.
Precedent	1090.
Precedent	In amending Article 31(2) under the 25th Amendment by substituting the word "amount" for "compensation" the amount fixed is made non-justiciable and the jurisdiction of the <ORG> is excluded because no reasons for fixing such amount would or need appear in the legislation.
RatioOfTheDecision	If any person aggrieved by the amount fixed challenges the <ORG> can neither go into the question of adequacy nor as to how the amount is fixed.
RatioOfTheDecision	If adequacy cannot be questioned any attempt to find out as to why the particular amount is fixed or how that amount has been fixed by law will be examining the adequacy which is forbidden as the Constitutional mandate.
RatioOfTheDecision	If one alleges that the amount is illusory one will meet the insurmountable Constitutional prohibition that the adequacy or the alleged arbitrariness of the amount fixed is not within the area of challenge in courts .
RatioOfTheDecision	1091.
RatioOfTheDecision	The amount fixed is not justiciable.
RatioOfTheDecision	The adequacy cannot be questioned.
RatioOfTheDecision	The correctness of the amount cannot be challenged.
RatioOfTheDecision	The principles specified are not justiciable.
Precedent	1092.
Precedent	If on the other hand, the legislature does not fix the amount but specifies the principles for determining the amount, the contention that principles for determining the amount must not be irrelevant loses all force because the result determining the amount by applying the specified principles cannot be challenged on the ground of inadequacy.
Precedent	If principles are specified for determining the amount and as a result of the application of the principles the result is less than the market value it will result in the same question of challenging adequacy.
Precedent	1093.
Precedent	The relevancy of the principles cannot be impugned.
Precedent	Nor can the reasonableness of the principles be impeached.
Precedent	1094.
Precedent	Article 14 has the flexibility of classification.
Precedent	Article 19 has the flexibility of reasonable restrictions.
Precedent	Social justice will determine the nature of the in dividual right and also the restriction on such right.
Precedent	Social justice will require modification or restriction of rights under Part III.
Precedent	The scheme of the Constitution generally discloses that the principles of social justice are placed above individual rights and whenever or wherever it is considered necessary individual rights have been subordinated or cat down to give effect to the principles of social justice.
Precedent	Social justice means various concepts which are evolved in the Directive Principles of the <ORG>.
Precedent	1095.
Fact	The 25th Amendment has amended Article 31(2) and also introduced Article 31(2B) in order to achieve two objects.
Fact	The first is to eliminate the concept of market value in the amount fixed for acquisition or requisition of the property.
Fact	The second is to exclude in Clause (2B) of Article 31 the applicability of Article 19(1)(f).
Fact	Articles 31A and 31B applied to acquisition and requisition of property.
Fact	The purpose of Article 31C is to confer by Constitutional mandate power on <ORG> and <ORG> to m ake laws for giving effect to Directive Principles.
Precedent	The significance of the total exclusion of Part III from Articles 31A and 31B is that it brings about in unmistakable manner the true relationship between the provisions of Part IV and Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	1096.
Precedent	With reference to land legislation subordination of fundamental rights of individual to the common good was clear in Clauses (4) and (6) of Article 31.
Precedent	It was made clearer by the Constitution First Amendment Act which introduced Articles 31A, 31B and Schedule 9.
Precedent	Articles 31A, 31B, Schedule 9 and Article 31C merely removed the restrictions which Part III of the Constitution imposes on legislative power.
Precedent	Article 31A after the Fourth Amendment removed the restrictions on legislative power imposed by Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	In enacting Clauses (b), (c) and (d) in Article 31A <ORG> was giving effect to social control which though less urgent than land reform became in course of time no less vital.
Fact	Article 31B by the First Amendment retrospec tively validated the laws specified in Schedule 9 by retrospectively removing all invalidity from the law because of the transgression of rights in Part III.
Fact	Again, the seven new Acts added in the Ninth Schedule by the Fourth Amendment Act had nothing to do with agrarian reform, but dealt with subjects of great national importance.
Precedent	The Constitution Fourth Amendment Act was intended to remove the barriers of Articles 14, 19 and 31(2) in respect of land legislation considered essential for public good.
Precedent	1097.
Precedent	State legislatures cannot remove the fetter.
Precedent	They have no power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	<ORG> cannot remove the fetter by ordinary law.
Precedent	By amendment of the Constitution <ORG> can remove the fetter by either deleting one or more fundamental right or rights or by excluding certain laws or certain kinds of laws from the fetter.
Precedent	1098.
RatioOfTheDecision	The pattern of Articles 31A, 31B, the Ninth Schedule and Article 31C is best understood by the observations of <NAME>, <NAME> in <NAME> case and of W <NAME>, J. in <NAME> case.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME>, <ORG> said in <NAME> case "Articles 31A and 31B really seek to save a certain class of laws and certain specified laws already passed from the combined operation of Article 13 read with other relevant Articles of Part III.
RatioOfTheDecision	The new Articles being thus essentially amendments of the Constitution have the power of enacting them.
RatioOfTheDecision	It was said that <ORG> could not validate the law which it has no power to enact.
RatioOfTheDecision	The proposition holds good whether the validity of the impugned provision turns on whether the subject matter, falls within or without the jurisdiction of the legislature which passed it.
RatioOfTheDecision	But to make law, which contravenes the Constitution, Constitutionally valid is a matter of Constitutional amendment and as such it falls within the exclusive power of <ORG>".
Precedent	W<NAME>, J. said of Article 31B " The laws had already been passed by the State legislature and it was their Constitutional infirmity, if any, which was being cured by the device adopte d in Article 31B read with the Ninth Schedule.... Parliament alone could do it under Article 368 and there was no need for any ratification under the proviso for amendment of Part III is not entrenched in the proviso".
Precedent	1099.
Precedent	The conclusiveness of declaration introduced by the 25th Amendment in a law under Article 31C is to be appreciated in the entire context of Article 31C.
Precedent	In removing restrictions of Part III in respect of a law under Article 31C there is no delegation of power to any legislature.
Precedent	There is only removal of restriction on legislative power imposed by Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	Article 31C does not confer any power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	The exclusion of Article 31 is a necessary corollary to protecting the impugned law from challenge under Articles 14, 19 and 31 because Article 13(2) would but for its exclusion in Article 31C render such laws void.
Precedent	The declaration clause is comparable to Section 6(3) of the Land Acquisition Act "1894 which contains a conclusive evidence clause that declarat ion shall be conclusive evidence that the land is needed for a public purpose and for a company as the case may be.
Precedent	A conclusive declaration would not be permissible so as to defeat a fundamental right.
Precedent	In Article 31(5) it is provided that nothing in Clause (2) shall effect (a) the provisions of any existing law other than a law to which the provisions of Clause (6) apply and since the Land Acquisition Act 1894 is an existing law the conclusive declaration clause prevails and is not justiciable.
Precedent	<CITATION_463201> .
Precedent	(1961) 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	128.
Precedent	The same view was reiterated by this <ORG> in <CITATION_1536600> .
Precedent	(1963) 2 S.C.R.
Precedent	774 that a declaration under the Land Requisition Act was not only conclusive about the need but was also conclusive for the need was for a public purpose.
Precedent	1100.
Precedent	Conclusive proof is defined in the Indian Evidence Act .
RatioOfTheDecision	It is, therefore, seen that the legislative power carries with it the power to provide for conclusive proof so as to oust the jurisdiction of a <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	The declaration is for the p urpose of excluding the process of evaluation of legislation on a consideration of the virtues and defects with a view to seeing if the laws has led to the result intended.
RatioOfTheDecision	If a question arises as to whether a piece of legislation with such declaration has a nexus with the Directive Principles in Article 39(b) and (c) the <ORG> can go into the question for the purpose of process of identification of the legislative measure on a consideration of the scope and object and pith and substance of the legislation.
Fact	Therefore, the 25th Amendment is valid.
Precedent	1101.
Precedent	A contention was advanced on behalf of the petitioner that Article 31B applies to agrarian reforms or in the alternative Article 31B is linked to Article 31A and is to be read as applying to laws in respect of five subject matters mentioned in Article 31A.
Precedent	The 13 Acts mentioned in the Ninth Schedule as enacted by the First Amendment Act, 1951 dealt with estates and agrarian reforms.
Precedent	There is nothing in Article 31B to indicate that it is linked with the same sub ject matter as Article 31A.
Precedent	In the Bihar Land Reforms case <NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	said at pp.
Precedent	914-915 of the report (1952 S.C.R.
Precedent	889) that the opening words of Article 31B are only intended to make clear that Article 31A should not be restricted in 'its application by reason of anything contained in Article 31B and are not in any way calculated to restrict the application of the latter Article or of the enactments referred to therein to acquisition of estates.
Precedent	1102.
Precedent	<CITATION_45759> 1952 S.C.R.
Precedent	1020 it was urged that Article 31B was merely illustrative of Article 31A and as the latter was limited in is application to estates as defined therein Article 31B was also similarly limited.
Precedent	That contention was rejected and it was said that Article 31B specifically validates certain Acts mentioned in the Schedule despite the provisions of Article 31A and is not illustrative of Article 31A but stands independent of it.
Precedent	1103.
Precedent	Again, in <NAME> v. Assistant Collector (1965) 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	616 it was contended that Articles  31A and 31B should be read together and if so read Article 31B would only illustrate the cases that would otherwise fall under.
Precedent	Article 31B , and, therefore, the same construction as put upon Article 31B should apply to Article 31A.
Precedent	This Court did not accept the argument It was said that the words "without prejudice to the generality of the provisions contained in Article 31A" indicate that the Acts and Regulations specified in the Ninth Schedule would have the same immunity even if did not attract Article 31A of the Constitution.
Precedent	If every Act in the Ninth Schedule would be covered by Article 31A , Article 31B would be redundant Some of the Act s mentioned in the Ninth Schedule, namely, items 14 to 20 and many other Acts added to the Ninth Schedule, do not appear to relate to estates as defined in Article 31A(2) of the Constitution.
Precedent	It was, therefore, held in <NAME> case that Article 31B was a Constitutional device to place the specific statute beyond any attack on the ground that they infringe Part III o f the Constitution.
Precedent	1104.
Precedent	The words "without prejudice to the generality of the provisions contained in Article 31A" occurring in Article 31B indicate that Article 31B stands independent of Article 31A.
Precedent	Article 31B and the Schedule are placed beyond any attack on the ground that they infringe Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	Article 31B need not relate to any particular type of legislation.
Precedent	Article 31B gives a mandate and complete protection from the challenge of fundamental rights to the Scheduled Acts and the Regulations.
Precedent	Article 31A protects laws in respect of five subject matters from the challenge of Articles 14, 19 and 31, but not retrospectively.
Precedent	Article 31B protects Scheduled Acts and the Regulations and none of the Scheduled Acts are deemed to be void or even to have become void on the ground of contravention of any fundamental right.
Precedent	1105.
Precedent	The validity of the Constitution 29th Amendment Act lies within a narrow compass.
Precedent	Article 31B has been held by this <ORG> to be a valid amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31B has  also been held by this <ORG> to be an independent provision.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31B has no connection with Article 31A.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Bihar Land Reforms case and <NAME> case are well settled authorities for that proposition.
RatioOfTheDecision	It, therefore, follows that Mr. <NAME>'s contention cannot be accepted that before the Acts can be included in the Ninth Schedule requirements of Article 31A are to be complied with.
Precedent	1106.
Precedent	For the foregoing reasons these are the conclusions.
Precedent	1107.
Precedent	First, the power to amend the Constitution is located in Article 368.
Precedent	Second, neither the Constitution nor an amendment of the Constitution can be or is law within the meaning of Article 13.
Precedent	Law in Article 13 means laws enacted by the legislature subject to the provision of the Constitution.
Precedent	Law in Article 13(2) does not mean the Constitution.
Precedent	The Constitution is the supreme law.
Precedent	Third, an amendment of the Constitution is an exercise of the constituent power.
Precedent	The majority view in <NAME> case is with respect wrong.
Precedent	Fourth, there are no express limitation s to the power of amendment.
Precedent	Fifth, there are no implied and inherent limitations on the power of amendment.
Precedent	Neither the Preamble nor Article 13(2) is at all a limitation on the power of amendment.
Precedent	Sixth, the power to amend is wide and unlimited.
Precedent	The power to amend means the power to add, alter or repeal any provision of the Constitution.
Precedent	There can be or is no distinction between essential and in-essential features of the Constitution to raise any impediment to amendment of alleged essential features.
Precedent	<ORG> in exercise of constituent power can amend any provision of this Constitution.
Precedent	Under Article 368 the power to amend can also be increased.
Precedent	The 24th Amendment is valid.
Precedent	The contention of Mr. <NAME> that unlimited power of amendment would confer power to abrogate the Constitution is rightly answered by the Attorney General and Mr. <NAME> that amendment does not mean mere abrogation or wholesale repeal of the Constitution.
Precedent	The Attorney General and Mr. <NAME> emphasised that an amendment would leave  an organic mechanism providing the Constitution organisation and system for the <ORG>.
Precedent	If the Constitution cannot have a vital growth it needs must wither.
Precedent	That is why it was stressed on behalf of the respondents that orderly and peaceful changes in a Constitutional manner would absorb all amendments to all provisions of the Constitution which in the end would be "an amendment of this Constitution".
Precedent	1108.
Precedent	The 25th Amendment is valid.
Precedent	The adequacy of amount fixed or the principles specified cannot be the subject matter of judicial review.
Precedent	The amendment of Article 31(2B) is valid.
Precedent	Article 31(2) is self contained and Articles 31(2) and 19(1)(f) are mutually exclusive.
Precedent	Amendment of fundamental right prior to the amendment was and is now after the 24th Amendment valid.
Precedent	Article 31C does not delegate or confer any power on the <ORG> legislature to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	Article 31C merely removes the restrictions of Part III from any legislation giving effect to Directive Principles under Article 39(b) and (c).
Precedent	T he power of <ORG> and of State legislatures to legislate on the class of legislation covered by Article 31C is rendered immune from Articles 14, 19 and  31.
Precedent	1109.
Precedent	The inclusion of the Kerala Act 35 of 1969 and the Kerala Act 25 of 1971 by the 29th Amendment in the Ninth Schedule is valid.
Precedent	Article 31B is independent of Article 31A.
Precedent	1110.
Precedent	In the result the contentions of Mr. <NAME> fail.
Precedent	Each party will pay and bear its own costs.
Precedent	The petitions will be placed before the Constitution Bench for disposal in accordance with law.
Precedent	<NAME>, J.
Precedent	1111.
Precedent	The detailed contentions addressed before us for 66 days have been set out in the judgment of My Lord the Chief Justice just pronounced, and I would only refer to such of those as are necessary for dealing with the relevant issues.
Precedent	Though I agree with some of the conclusions arrived at by him, but since the approach in arriving at a conclusion is as important as the conclusion itself, and particularly in matters involving vital Constitutional issue s having a far-reaching impact on fundamental freedoms of the people of this country and on the social objectives which the <ORG> is enjoined to achieve under the Directive Principles of <ORG> Policy, I consider it my duty to express my views in my own way for arriving at those conclusions.
Precedent	1112.
Precedent	In this case the validity of the Constitution (Twenty-fourth) and (Twenty-fifth) Amendment Acts of 1971 and the Constitution (Twenty-ninth) Amendment Act of 1972 has been challenged as being outside the scope of the power of amendment conferred on <ORG> by Article 368 of the Constitution and consequently void.
Precedent	1113.
Precedent	The validity of the Twenty-fourth Amendment would depend upon the interpretation of two crucial articles, Article 13 and Article 368 , and two words, one in each article, namely, `law' in the former, and 'amendment' in the latter.
Precedent	For the purposes of ascertaining the true intent and scope of these articles in <CITATION_120358> , (1967) 2 S.C.R.
Precedent	762 the basic question which the <ORG> first considered  was, where was power to amend the Constitution of India to be found?
Precedent	<NAME>, C.J., with whom <NAME> and <NAME>, JJ., as they then were, and <NAME> and <NAME>, JJ., concurred, (hereinafter referred to as the leading majority judgment), held that the power was contained in Articles 245, 246 and 248 read with Entry 97 of List I of Schedule VII, and not in Article 368 which only provided for the procedure to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	<NAME>, J., as he then was, in his concurring judgment held that the procedure of amendment, if it can be called a power at all, is a legislative power, but it is sui generis and outside the three Lists of the Constitution, and that Article 368 outlines a process which, if followed strictly, results in the amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	He was, therefore, of the view that the Article gives power to no particular person or persons.
Precedent	All the named authorities have to act according to the letter of the Article to achieve the result.
Precedent	1114.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. as he then was, for himse lf and two other Judges, <NAME> and <NAME>, JJ., found the power in Article 368 itself and not in Articles 245, 246 and 248 read with Entry 97 of List I.
Precedent	1115.
Precedent	It is, therefore, contended by the learned Advocate-General of Maharashtra, firstly, that the finding in the leading majority judgment that the fundamental rights cannot be amended is based on the decision that the amending power is to be found in the residuary Article 248 read with Entry 97 of List I of Schedule VII.
Precedent	This finding is deprived of its foundation, since six Judges held that the amending power is not to be found in the residuary Article and Entry 97 of List I. Secondly, the conclusion that the fundamental rights cannot be amended was reached by the leading majority judgment on the basis that Article 13(2) was attracted by the opening words of Article 245 and, therefore, a law amending the Constitution under entry 97 of List I was a law referred to in Article 245 , and as it was in conflict with Article 13(2) the law was void.
Precedent	1116.
Argument	It is again contended that this conclusion loses its validity once its basis is destroyed by five Judges holding that the amending power is not to be found in entry 97 of List I, but in Article 368.
Argument	In view of the conclusion of <NAME>, J., that the power of amendment as well as procedure therefor was contained in Article 368 itself, he submits that there is no ratio binding on this <ORG> unless it be that the power of amendment is not in the residuary article but in Article 368.
Argument	This argument is of little validity, because the ratio of the decision, where a question is directly raised before the <ORG> for decision, is that which it decides, and in that case wherever the power may have been found, whether in Article 368 or in the residuary entry 97 of List I of Schedule VII, the controversy was whether an amendment made under Article 368 is a 'law' within the meaning of Article 13(2 ), and if it is so, a <ORG> cannot make a law taking away or abridging fundamental rights conferred by Part III of the Cons titution.
Precedent	That question being answered in the affirmative by the majority, the ratio of <NAME>'s decision is that an amendment under Article 368 is a 'law' within the meaning of Article 13(2).
Precedent	What the leading majority judgment in that case did not decide, however, is whether Article 368 itself could be amended under the proviso of that article conferring a power to amend the whole Constitution.
Precedent	At p. 805, <NAME>, C.J., observed, "In the view we have taken on the scope of Article 368 vis-a-vis the fundamental rights, it is also unnecessary to express our opinion on the question whether the amendment of the fundamental rights is covered by the proviso to Article 368."
Precedent	While five Judges who were in minority held that each and every article of the Constitution could be amended in exercise of the power under, and by following the procedure in, Article 368 , <NAME> held that by amending.
Precedent	Article 368 , <ORG> could not do indirectly what it could not do directly, namely, amend Article 13(2) or  override the provisions thereunder, because as he said, "The whole Constitution is open to amendment.
Precedent	Only two dozen articles are outside the reach of Article 368.
Precedent	That too because the Constitution has made them fundamental."
Precedent	(See p.  878).
Argument	There is, therefore, warrant for the submission that <NAME>'s case is not determinative of the question now raised before this <ORG> as to whether the power to amend Article 368 could be exercised to amend the fundamental rights in Part III.
Precedent	At any rate, five of the six Judges who expressed an opinion on this aspect support the proposition that this can be done.
Precedent	1117.
Precedent	It was also submitted that no question in fact arose for decision in <NAME>'s case that in future <ORG> could not amend the fundamental rights, because what that case was concerned with was the past exercise of the power to amend the fundamental rights, and, therefore, the observations in the majority judgments of <NAME>, C.J., and <NAME>, J., as he then was, about the future exercise of  that power are clearly obiter.
Precedent	It may be pointed out that the majority judgment as well as the minority judgment concurred in dismissing the petition, the former on the ground that the First, Fourth and Seventeenth Amendments were not affected either on the basis of the doctrine of prospective overruling or on the basis of acquiescence or on the ground that they were made by virtue of a valid exercise of the amending power under Article 368.
Precedent	On this basis it is submitted that no ratio can be found in that case for the majority declaring that <ORG> in future cannot amend fundamental rights which is binding on this <ORG> nor can it amend the amending article to take away or abridge fundamental rights.
Precedent	1118.
Precedent	Whether the First, Fourth and Seventeenth Amendments have been rightly held to be valid or not, the ratio of the decision as was observed earlier is that under Article 368 as it was before its amendment, <ORG> could not amend the Constitution to take away or abridge any of the fundamental rights co nferred by Part III of the Constitution, and that question will only assume importance if this <ORG> comes to the conclusion, following <NAME>, decision, that <ORG> cannot amend Article 368 under proviso (e) thereof to take away or abridge any of the fundamental rights or to amend Article 13(2) making it subject to an amendment under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	If such a power exists, the question whether an amendment in Article 368 is a 'law' 'within the meaning of Article 13(2) may not prima facie be of significance.
RatioOfTheDecision	There are, however, two aspects to this problem, firstly, whether 'law' in Article 13(2) includes an amendment of the Constitution under Article 368: and secondly, if this <ORG> holds that 'law' in Article 13(2) does not include an amendment under Article 368 , then the question would be, has the Constitution (Twenty-fourth) Amendment purported to exercise a power in effecting that amendment which was not granted under that Article ?
RatioOfTheDecision	In other words, are there any limitations to the amending p ower under Article 368 ?
Precedent	If, as was held by <NAME>, J., that the power of amendment conferred on <ORG> under Article 368 is not a constituent power, and any amendment made thereunder is a legislative power, which is 'law' within the meaning of Article 13(2 ), then <ORG> cannot do indirectly what it cannot do directly.
Precedent	1119.
Precedent	The first question which would arise for decision is what does 'law' in Article 13(2) signify, and is there any internal evidence which would indicate that that word has been used to include an amendment under Article 368 , and if it does, whether it is subject to any limitations, and if so, what ?
Precedent	It is contended that the word 'law' in Article 13(2) not only includes ordinary legislative law, but also Constitutional law.
Precedent	1120.
