Precedent	The various <ORG>s had already passed laws or were in the process of passing laws on the subject, and specific provision was made in the two clauses, securing such laws from challenge on the ground that they were not acquired by the <ORG> for a public purpose or that adequate compensation was not paid.
Precedent	The first case under the Bihar Land Reforms Act, 1950, <CITATION_41868>  S.C.R.
Precedent	889 shows that the law was highly unjust (from the prevailing point of view of 'justice') and the compensation payable was in some cases purely illusory.
Precedent	(See : <NAME> .
Precedent	p. 936).
Precedent	And yet by virtue of Article 31(4) there could be no challenge to that Act and other similar laws on those grounds.
Precedent	By oversight, challenge to such laws under Articles 14 and 19 had not been expressly excluded, and so when the case was pending in this Court, the first Amendment Act was passed inserting Articles 31A and 31B by which, to take no chances, a challenge based on all fundamental rights in Part III was wholly excluded.
Precedent	The course taken by the Constitution and its first Amendment leaves no doubt that Zamindaris and Estates were intended to be expropriated from the very beginning and no 'core' with regard to payment of compensation was sought to be safeguarded.
Precedent	By the time the 4th Amendment was made in 1955, it became apparent that the challenge to any scheme of redistribution or breaking up of concentration of property was confined generally to Articles 14, 19 and 31, and hence Article 31A Was amended.
Precedent	By the amendment all intermediaries, including small absentee landlords, were permitted t o be eliminated and challenge to Article 31A was excluded only under Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	In short, rights in landed agricultural property were extinguished without a thought to the necessity of paying fair compensation.
RatioOfTheDecision	In a real sense concentration of wealth in the form of agricultural lands was broken and community resources were distributed.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the other hand, a protectionist economic system, reinforced by controls, followed in the realm of trade and industry with a view to achieve greater production of goods and services led to other forms of concentration of wealth and means of production in the wake of Independence.
RatioOfTheDecision	So comes the 25th Amendment, the object of which is the same viz implementation of Article 39(b)  & (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	It has made clear that owners of property when it is acquired for a public purpose are not entitled to compensation as interpreted by this <ORG>, and any law made with the aforesaid object cannot be challenged on the grounds arising out of Articles 14, 19 and  31.
RatioOfTheDecision	In principle, there  is no difference in Article 31A and the new Article 31C inserted by the 25th Amendment.
Argument	In trying to support his arguments on the core principle of essential features, Mr. <NAME> tried to play down the role of Article 31(4)  & (6) and Article 31A excusing them on the ground that they related to very necessary agrarian reforms to which the majority party in <ORG> was for years before the Constitution, committed.
Precedent	But that is not a legal argument.
Precedent	Articles 31(4)(6) and Article 31A clearly show that community interests were regarded as supreme and those Articles were only a step in the implementation of the Directive Principles in Article 39(b)  & (c).
Precedent	(Compare the observations of <NAME> in 1952 S.C.R.
Precedent	889 at pages 996 to 999.)
Precedent	The Constitution definitely refused to accept the 'core' principle with regard to property rights, if property was required to be expropriated in the common interest in pursuance of the Directive Principles.
Precedent	The mood of the majority party is reflected in the speech o f Pandit <NAME>, the then Chief Minister of Uttar Pradesh.
Precedent	Speaking in <ORG> on Article 31 and after justifying the provision of Article 31(4)  & (6) in relation to laws regarding Zamindaris and agricultural estates (there were 20 lakh Zamindars) according to him, in U.P.
Precedent	alone (he said "I presume that if at any time this legislature chooses to nationalise industry, and take control of it, whether it be all the industries or any particular class of it, such as the textile industry or mines, it will be open to it to pass a law and to frame the Principles for such purpose, and those principles will be invulnerable in any court.
Precedent	They will not be open to question, because the only condition for disputing them, as has been pointed out by <NAME>, (<NAME>) one of the most eminent jurists which our country has ever produced, is this, that it should he a fraud on the Constitution)."
Precedent	(See : Constituent Assembly Debates Vol.
Precedent	IX page 1289).
RatioOfTheDecision	It shows that Article 31(4)(6) we re the first step as applied to land legislation, in the direction of implementing the Directive Principles of Article 39(b) &  (c), and it was only a matter of time when the principles would be applied to other types of concentration of wealth and its distribution.
RatioOfTheDecision	As <NAME>, observed in <CITATION_41868> at pages 929-30, our Constitution raised the obligation to pay compensation for compulsory acquisition of property to the status of a fundamental right.
RatioOfTheDecision	At the same time by specifically inserting Clauses (4) & (6) in Article 31 , it made the issues of public purpose and compensation prescribed in Article 31(2) non-justiciable in some specified laws dealing with concentration and distribution of wealth in the form of landed agricultural property.
RatioOfTheDecision	This clearly negatived the idea of protecting concentration of wealth in a few hands as an essential feature of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME>, J. was saying practically the same thing when he remarked in <NAME> case that it was an error to include property rig hts in Part III and that they were the weakest of fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	1339.
RatioOfTheDecision	I have already discussed the amplitude of power conferred by the amending clause of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	In countries like America and Australia where express limitations have been imposed in the amending clause itself there is substantial authority for the view that even these express limitations can be removed by following the procedure laid down in the amending clause.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to them this could be done in two steps the first being to amend the amending clause itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is not necessary for us to investigate the matter further because Article 368 does not contain any express limitation.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the other hand, the power is wide enough even to amend the provisions of Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	See : proviso (e) of that article.
RatioOfTheDecision	In other words, Article 368 contains unqualified and plenary powers to amend the provisions of the Constitution including the Amending clause.
RatioOfTheDecision	Prima facie, therefore, to introduce implied prohibitions to cut down a clear affir mative grant in a Constitution would be contrary to the settled rules of construction.
Precedent	(See the dissenting judgment of <NAME> and <NAME> in <NAME> v. The King 26 C.L.R.
Precedent	43-68 approved by <ORG> in 1920 A.C. 691).
Precedent	1340.
Precedent	When such an Amending clause is amended without affecting the power the amendment will principally involve the Amending procedure.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may make amendment easier or more difficult.
RatioOfTheDecision	The procedure may also differ substantially.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> may be eliminated from the process leaving the amendment to the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	The proviso might be dropped, enlarging the role of the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the other hand, the <ORG> and <ORG> may be divested of the function by providing for a referendum plebiscite or a special convention.
RatioOfTheDecision	While, thus the power remains the same, the instrumentalities may differ from time to time in accordance with the procedure prescribed.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME>, with respect, was right in pointing out that the power to amend is not entrusted to this or that body.
RatioOfTheDecision	The p ower is generated when the prescribed procedure is followed by the instrumentalities specified in the Article.
RatioOfTheDecision	Since the instrumentalities are liable to be changed by a proper amendment it will be inaccurate to say that <ORG> had entrusted the power to any-body.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the authority which is required to follow the procedure is the <ORG> for the time being, it may be convenient to describe <ORG> as the authority to whom the power is granted or entrusted, but strictly that would be inaccurate, because there is no grant to any body.
RatioOfTheDecision	Whichever may be the instrumentality for the time being, the power remains unqualified.
Precedent	1341.
Precedent	If the theory of implied limitations is sound-the assumption made being that the same have their origin in the rest of the Constitutional provisions including the Preamble and the fundamental rights-then these limitations must clog the power by whatever Agency it is exercised.
Precedent	The rest of the Constitution does not change merely because the procedure prescribed in Art icle 368 is changed.
Precedent	Therefore, the implied limitations should continue to clog the power.
Precedent	Logically, if Article 368 is so amended as to provide for a convention or a referendum, the latter will be bound to respect the implied limitations-a conclusion which Mr. <NAME> is not prepared to accept.
Precedent	He agrees with the jurists who hold that a convention or a referendum will not be bound by any limitations.
Precedent	The reason given is that the people directly take part in a referendum or, through their elected representatives, in a convention.
Precedent	Even in Golak Nath it was accepted that any part of the Constitution including the fundamental rights could be amended out of existence by a Constituent Assembly.
Precedent	1342.
Precedent	The argument seems to be that a distinction must be made between the power exercised by the people and the power exercised by <ORG>.
Precedent	In fact Mr. <NAME>'s whole thesis is that the <ORG> is a creature of the Constitution and the limitation is inherent in its being a constituted authority.
Precedent	We have <NAME> <NAME> examined the question and shown that where the people have withdrawn completely from the process of Amendment, the <ORG> bo<NAME> to whom the power is entrusted can exercise the power to the same extent as a <ORG> Assembly and that the power does not vary according to the <ORG> to whom the power is entrusted.
Precedent	Therefore, this reason also viz.
Precedent	that <ORG> is a constituted bo<NAME> and, therefore, it suffers from inherent limitations does not hold good.
Precedent	1343.
Precedent	From the conclusion that the power of Amendment remains unqualified by whomsoever it is exercised, it follows that there can be no implied or inherent limitations on the Amending power.
Precedent	If a special convention admittedly does not suffer from limitations, any other constituent bo<NAME> cannot be subject to it.
Precedent	1344.
Precedent	The leading majority judgment in <NAME> case had seen some force in this, doctrine of implied limitations (808), but did not find it necessary to decide on the issue.
Precedent	To remove all doubts on that score the 24th Amendment is now sui tably amended.
Precedent	Its first clause says that <ORG> may amend any provision of the Constitution notwithstanding anything in it.
Precedent	Therefore, in the matter of amendment <ORG> may not, now, be inhibited by the other express provisions of the Constitution, which would mean that it may also ignore all implications arising therefrom.
Precedent	1345.
Precedent	Where power is granted to amend the Amending power, as in our Constitution, there is no limit to the extent this may be done.
Precedent	It may be curtailed of 'enlarged'.
Precedent	This is well illustrated in <NAME> v. <NAME> [1935] <ORG>, 170.
Precedent	Under the Irish State Constitution Act of 1922, the <ORG> (<ORG>) had been given power to amend the Constitution under Article 50 of the Act.
Precedent	Under that Article, amendments during the first eight years of the Constitution, could be validly made without having recourse to a referendum unless specially demanded by the persons, and in the manner specified in Article 47 , but amendments made after that period had to be approved in every case  by a referendum and the people.
Fact	By a Constitutional amendment of 1928 (Amendment NO.
Fact	10) the compulsion of Article 47 was got rid of, and by an amendment of 1929 (Amendment No.
Fact	16) made within the eight year period already referred to, the period of 8 years was extended to 16 years.
Fact	The result was that the Constitution now authorized the <ORG> to amend by ordinary legislation its Constitution for the period of 16 years from the commencement of the Constitution without being required to have recourse to a referendum.
Fact	In 1931 by a further Amendment (Amendment No.
Fact	17) extensive alterations were made by which inter alia, personal liberty was curtailed, denying trial by Jury or by the regular courts.
Fact	<NAME> who was one of the victims of the new law applied to <ORG> for a Writ of Habeas Corpus on the ground that the several amendments were invalid, especially No.
Fact	16, by which the period of 8 years had been extended to 16 years.
Fact	If Amendment No.
Fact	16 was invalid, that would have automatically resulted in  Amendment No.
Precedent	17 being invalid, having been made after the first period of 8 years.
Precedent	<ORG> (3 JJ) unanimously held that all the Amendments were valid.
Precedent	In appeal to <ORG> that decision was confirmed by a majority, <NAME>, Chief Justice, dissenting.
Precedent	One of the chief contentions directed against Amendment No.
Precedent	16 was that the <ORG> could not have 'enlarged' its power from 8 to 16 years to change the Constitution without a referendum by ordinary legislation.
Precedent	This conten tion was rejected by the majority.
Precedent	<NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	took a different view of the amendment.
Precedent	He held that Article 50 did not provide for the amending of the Amendatory power, conceding that otherwise the power could have been so 'enlarged'.
Precedent	Since there is no dispute in our case that by reason of Clause (e) of proviso of Article 368 power is given to amend the amendatory power, it was open to <ORG> to 'enlarge' the power by amendment.
Precedent	If it is assumed-and we have shown there is no ground to make such an assumption-that the re was some implied limitation to be derived from other provisions of the Constitution, that limitation, if any, is now removed by the non-obstante clause in Clause 1 of the Amended Article 368.
Precedent	1346.
Precedent	It is of some interest to note here that in a case which later went to <ORG>, <NAME> v. Attorney General for the Irish State  A.C. 484 and in which a Constitutional amendment made by the Irish Parliament in 1933 (Amendment No.
Precedent	22) was challenged, Mr. <NAME> (Later Lord <NAME>) conceded before <ORG> that Amendment No.
Precedent	16 of 1929 was valid and their Lordships observed (494) "Mr. Wilfied <NAME> for the petitioners rightly conceded that Amendment No.
Precedent	16 was regular and that the validity of these subsequent amendments could not be attacked on the ground that they had not been submitted to the people by referendum."
Precedent	The question of validity of Amendment No.
Precedent	16 was so vital to the petitioner's case that it is impossible to believe that a counsel of the standing of Lord <NAME> would not have chall enged the same and, in the opinion of their Lordships, 'rightly'.
Precedent	According to <NAME> the judgment of <NAME>, <NAME> in <NAME>'s case was wrong.
Precedent	See : Letters on Imperial Relations Indian Reform Constitutional and International Law 1916-1935 page 157.
Precedent	1347.
Precedent	The importance of <NAME>'s case lies in the fact that though Article 50 of the Irish Free State Constitution did not expressly say that Article 50 itself is liable to be amended, no less than five judges of <ORG> held it could be amended though the amendment resulted in the 'enlargement' of the power of <ORG> to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	How wide the power was further established in <NAME>'s case which held that Amendment No.
Precedent	22 was valid, though by this Amendment even the Royal Prerogative regarding appeals to <ORG> was held to have been abrogated by the combined operation of the, Statute of Westminster and the Constitutional Amendment, in spite of Article 50 having been originally limited by the terms of the Scheduled Trea ty of 1922.
Precedent	In our case Article 368 authorizes its own amendment and such an amendment can enlarge the powers of the <ORG>, if such was the need.
Precedent	1348.
Precedent	Apart from reasons already given, we will consider, on first principles, whether the constituent body is bound to respect the so-called 'essential feature' of the fundamental rights especially that of right to property.
Precedent	The fact that some people regard them as good and desirable is no adequate reason.
Precedent	The question really is whether the constituent body considers that they require to be amended to meet the challenge of the times.
Precedent	The philosophy of the amending clause is that it is a safety-valve for orderly change and if the good and desirable feature has lost its appeal to the people the constituent body would have undoubtedly the right to change it.
Precedent	1349.
Precedent	Indeed, if there are some parts of the Constitution which are made expressly unamendable the constituent body would be incompetent to change them, or if there is anything in the provisions of the Const itution embodying those essential features which by necessary implication prohibit their amendment those provisions will also become unamendable.
Precedent	The reason is that in law there is no distinction between an express limitation and a limitation which must be necessarily implied.
Precedent	Secondly, it is an accepted rule of construction that though a provision granting the power does not contain any limitation that may not be conclusive.
Precedent	That limitation may be found in other parts of the statute.
Precedent	But we have to remember that Article 368 permits the amendment of all the provisions of the Constitution expressly.
Precedent	And if that power is to be cut down by something that is said in some other provision of the Constitution the latter must be clear and specific.
Precedent	As far back as 1831 <NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	delivering the unanimous opinion of the Judges in <ORG> in <NAME> v. <NAME> (1831) II Dow & <NAME>, 480 observed at page 500 "No rule of construction can require that, when the words of one part of a statute convey a clear  meaning...it shall be necessary to introduce another part of the statute which speaks with less perspicuity, and of which the words may be capable of such construction as by possibility to diminish the efficacy of the other provisions of the Act."
Precedent	To control the true effect of Article 368 " you must have a context even more plain or at least as plain as the words to be controlled".
Precedent	See : <NAME> in <NAME> v. <NAME> (1876-77) 4 Ch.
Precedent	D. 588 (592).
RatioOfTheDecision	Neither the text nor the context of the articles embodying the fundamental rights shows that they are not exposed to Article 368.
Precedent	Moreover, when we are concerned with a power under a statute, it is necessary to remember the following observations of Lord <NAME> in Reg.
Precedent	v. Burah (1878) 3 App.
Precedent	CAS.
Precedent	889 at pp.
Precedent	904 & 905 "The established <ORG> of Justice, when a question arises whether the prescribed limits have been exceeded, must of necessity determine that question; and the only way in which they can properly do so, is by looking to the terms of the instrumen t by which, affirmatively, the legislative powers were created, and by which, negatively, they are restricted.
Precedent	If what has been done is legislation, within the general scope of the affirmative words which give the power, and if it violates no express condition or restriction by which that power is limited it is not for <ORG> to inquire further, or to enlarge constructively those conditions and restrictions."
Precedent	Similarly <NAME> in Attorney-General for the Province of Ontario v. Attorney-General for the Dominion of Canada (1912) App.
Precedent	Cas.
Precedent	571 observed at page 583 "In the interpretation of a completely self-governing Constitution founded upon a written organic instrument such as the British North America Act, if the text is explicit the text is conclusive, alike in what it directs and what it forbids.
Precedent	When the text is ambiguous, as for example, when the words establishing too mutually exclusive jurisdictions are wide enough to bring a particular power within either, recourse must be had to th e context and scheme of the Act."
Precedent	The only course which is open to courts is to determine the extent of power expressly granted after excluding what is expressly or by necessary implication excluded.
Precedent	That is the view of <ORG> in <NAME> v. Outrim  A.C. 81 the effect of which is summarized by <NAME>, J. in <ORG> v. <ORG> and Ors.
Precedent	28 C.L.R.
Precedent	129 at p. 150 as follows: ...we should state explicitly that the doctrine of "implied prohibition" against the exercise of a power once ascertained in accordance with ordinary rules of construction, was definitely rejected by <ORG> in <NAME> v. <NAME>.
Precedent	1350.
Precedent	Having regard to the rules of construction relating to power referred to above, we have to see if either the provisions relating to the fundamental right to property or any related provisions of the Constitution contain words of prohibition or limitation on the amending power.
Precedent	Right to property is sought to be safeguarded under Article 31 , a nd Article 19 deals with freedoms having relation to property, profession, trade and business.
Precedent	We find nothing in these provisions to suggest that rights to property cannot be abridged by an amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	On the other hand, Article 31(1) suggests that one can be deprived of property under the authority of law.
Precedent	The right to receive compensation under Clause (2) of Article 31 , as it stood at the time of the commencement of the Constitution, had been considerably cut down by several provisions contained in the other clauses of that article.
Precedent	Article 31(4)  & (6) not only envisaged breaking up of concentration of landed property in the hands of Zamindars and the like but also expropriation without payment of just compensation.
Precedent	That necessarily called for the exclusion of Articles 14, 19 and 31, because no scheme for expropriation or extinguishment of rights in property would succeed without their exclusion.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thereafter there has been a spate of amendments curtailing property rights and none of the m seems to have been challenged on the ground that there was something in the provisions themselves (apart from the fact that they affect a 'transcedental' fundamental right) suggesting an implied or inherent limitation on the amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	The last sentence from Lord <NAME>'s judgment quoted about embodies a well-known rule of construction which is useful when the text of a statute is ambiguous.
RatioOfTheDecision	Where the text is clear and unambiguous there can be no recourse to the context or the scheme of the Act; nor can the context or the scheme be utilised to make ambiguous what is clear and unambiguous.
RatioOfTheDecision	Moreover the rule does not permit in case of ambiguity recourse to the scheme and context which is unhelpful in resolving the ambiguity.
RatioOfTheDecision	It does not authorize investigating the scheme and context with an effect of delimiting the power referred to in the 'ambiguous' text, if the scheme and the context do not contain words which expressly or by necessary implication have the effect.
RatioOfTheDecision	All this is important in connection  with the construction of the word 'Amendment in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	We have already shown that the word 'Amendment' used in the context of a Constitution is clear and unambiguous.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, the scheme and the context are irrelevant.
RatioOfTheDecision	The scheme and the context on which reliance is placed before us consist principally of the alleged dominating statuts of the Preamble and the alleged transcedental character of the fundamental rights neither of which helps us in the legal interpretation of the word 'Amendment'.
RatioOfTheDecision	They are being pressed into service merely to create an ambiguity where there is none.
Precedent	Actually the context and scheme are here used to cut down the ambit and scope of the expression 'amendment of the Constitution' by investing them with that effect where neither expressly nor by necessary implication do they contain any prohibition or limitation on the Amending power.
Precedent	Therefore, as a matter of construction no implied limitations can be inferred from the Preamble or the fundamental rights, being as much part of  a legal document as any other provision of the Constitution, are subject to equal consideration in the matter of legal construction.
Precedent	To be relevant, the scheme and context must say or reasonably suggest something with regard to Amending power.
Precedent	1351.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> sought to draw support for his doctrine of implied limitations from the preamble.
Precedent	According to him the Preamble sets out the objectives of the Constitution and, therefore, any tampering with these objectives would destroy the identity of the Constitution.
Precedent	And since an amendment of the Constitution, howsoever made, must preserve the identity of the Constitution the objectives of the Preamble should be treated as permanent and unamendable.
Precedent	On that basis he further contended that since the fundamental rights are mostly an elaboration of the objectives of the Preamble, it was implied that the fundamental rights or, at least, the essence of them was not liable to be damaged or destroyed by an amendment.
Precedent	1352.
Precedent	The submission that the fundamental rights  are an elaboration of the preamble is an over-statement and a half truth.
Precedent	According to the Preamble the people of India have given unto themselves the Constitution to secure to all its citizens (a) JUSTICE, social, economic and political; (b) LIBERTY of thought, expression, belief, faith and worship;  (c) EQUALITY of status and of opportunity; and to promote among the citizens (d) FRATERNITY assuring the dignity of the individual and the unity of the Nation.
Precedent	There is no doubt that the Constitution is intended to be a vehicle by which the goals set out in it are hoped to be reached.
Precedent	Indeed, being a part of the Constitution, strictly speaking, it is amendable under Article 368.
Precedent	But we will assume that the people of India will not be rash enough to amend the glorious words of the Preamble; and as long as the Preamble is there the Governments will have to honour the Preamble and the Constitution will have to continue as a vehicle which would lead us to the goals.
Precedent	But to say that the fundamental rights are an ela boration of these goals would be a caricature.
Precedent	Most of the fundamental rights may be traced to the principles of LIBERTY and EQUALITY mentioned in the Preamble.
Precedent	But whereas the concepts of LIBERTY and EQUALITY are mentioned in absolute terms in the Preamble the fundamental rights including the several freedoms are not couched in absolute terms.
Precedent	They reflect the concepts of LIBERTY and EQUALITY in a very attenuated form with several restrictions imposed in the interest of orderly and peaceable Government.
Precedent	1353.
Precedent	The pre-eminent place in the Preamble is given to JUSTICE-social, economic and political, and it is obvious that without JUSTICE the other concepts of LIBERTY, EQUALITY and FRATERNITY would be illusory.
Precedent	In a democratic country whose institutions are informed by JUSTICE-social, economic and political, the other three concepts of LIBERTY, EQUALITY and FRATERNITY will be automatically fostered.
Precedent	Social and political Justice takes care of Liberty; and Justice, social and economic, takes care of Equality of  status and of opportunity.
Precedent	Therefore, even in the Directive Principles the supreme importance of Justice-social, economic and political-is highlighted in Article 38 , in which the <ORG> is given a mandate to strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting a social order in which justice-social, economic and political shall inform all the institutions of the National life.
Precedent	Where genuine and honest efforts are made in the implementation of this mandate the content and ambit of the concepts of <ORG> and Equality are bound to increase and expand.
Precedent	As <NAME> has pointed out in his introduction to <NAME>'s Law of the Constitution at page lxxxii "<ORG> today involves the ordering of social and economic conditions by governmental authority, even in those countries where political, if not economic equality of its citizens, has been attained.
Precedent	Without expansion of that authority, which Federal <ORG>s must find more difficult to achieve than a unitary <ORG> like the United Kingdom, there is inevita bly a risk that the Constitution may break down before a force which is not limited by considerations of Constitutional niceties."
Precedent	Again he points out at pages xxiv and xxv that the modern <ORG> is a forum in which both parties put forward incessant demands for the remedying of some social or economic ill of the body politic...and the changing conditions have all been brought about by the action of <ORG>.
Precedent	In doing that, <NAME> says, it could not be denied that legislation has shifted the emphasis on individual liberty to the provision of services for the public good.
Precedent	In the terms of our Constitution especially the Preamble and Article 38 , the shift of emphasis is from individual liberty to Justice-social, economic and political.
Precedent	1354.
Precedent	The absolute concepts of Liberty and Equality are very difficult to achieve as goals in the present day organised society.
Precedent	The fundamental rights have an apparent resemblance to them but are really no more than rules which a civilized government is expected to fo llow in the governance of the country whether they are described as fundamental rules or not.
Precedent	England developed these rules in its day to day Government under the rule of law and does not make a song and dance about them.
Precedent	British rulers of India tried to introduce these rules in the governance of this country, as proof of which we can point out to the vast mass of statutes enacted during the British period which have been continued, practically without change, under our Constitution.
Precedent	No body can deny that when Imperial interests were in jeopardy, these rules of good government were applied with an unequal hand, and when the agitation for self rule grew in strength these rules were thrown aside by the rulers by resorting to repressive laws.
Precedent	It was then that people in this country clamoured for these elementary human rights.
Precedent	To them their value in our social and political life assumed such importance that when the Constitution was framed we decided that these rules of Civilized government must find a place in t he Constitution, so that even our own Governments at the centre and the <ORG> should not overlook them.
Precedent	That is the genesis of our fundamental rights.
Precedent	The importance of these rights as conferred in the Constitution lies not in their being something extraordinary but in the bar that the Constitution imposed against laws which contravened these rights and the effective remedy supplied under Article 32.
Precedent	Indeed the framers of the Constitution took good care not to confer the fundamental rights in absolute terms because that was impractical.
Precedent	Knowing human capacity for distorting and misusing all liberties and freedoms, the framers of the Constitution put restrictions on them in the interest of the people and the <ORG> thus emphasizing that fundamental rights i.e.
Precedent	rules of civilized government are liable to be altered, if necessary, for the common good and in the public interest.
Precedent	1355.
Precedent	And yet, as we have seen above, even in U.K. individual Liberty as it was understood a generation or two ago is no longer so sacr osanct, especially, in relation to ownership of property Several statutes in the economic and social field have been passed which while undoubtedly impinging upon the individual liberties of a few have expanded social and economic justice for the many; If U.K. had stood staunchly by its Victorian concept of laissez faire and individual liberty, the progress in social and economic justice which it has achieved during the last half a century would have been difficult.
Precedent	Even so, though very much more advanced than our country, U.K. cannot claim that it has fully achieved social and economic justice for all its citizens.
Precedent	But there is no doubt that the parties which form the Governments there have always this goal in view though their methods may be different.
Precedent	In a country like ours where we have, on the one hand, abject poverty on a very large scale and great concentration of wealth on the other, the advance towards social and economic justice is bound to be retarded if the old concept of individual liberty is to  dog our footsteps.
Precedent	In the ultimate analysis, liberty or freedoms which are so much praised by the wealthier sections of the community are the freedom to amass wealth and own property and means of production, which, as we have already seen, our Constitution does not sympathise with.
Precedent	If the normal rule is that all rules of civilized government are subject to public interest and the common weal, those rules will have to undergo new adjustments in the implementation of the Directive Principles.
Precedent	A blind adherence to the concept of freedom to own disproportionate wealth will not take us to the important goals of the Preamble, while a just and sympathetic implementation of the Directive Principles has at least the potentiality to take us to those goals, although, on the way, a few may suffer some dimunition of the unequal freedom they now enjoy.
Precedent	That being the philosophy underlying the Preamble the fundamental rights and the Directive Principles taken together, it will be incorrect to elevate the fundamental rights  as essentially an elaboration of the objectives of the Preamble.
Precedent	As a matter of fact a law made for implementing the Directive Principles of Article 39(b) and (c ), instead of being contrary to the Preamble, would be in conformity with it because while if may cut down individual liberty of a few, it widens its horizon for the many.
Precedent	1356.
Precedent	It follows that if in implementing such a law the rights of an individual under Articles 14, 19 and 31 are infringed in the course of securing the success of the scheme of the law, such an infringement will have to be regarded as a necessary consequence and, therefore, secondary.
Precedent	The Preamble read as a whole, therefore, does not contain the implication that in any genuine implementation of the Directive Principles, a fundamental right will not suffer any dimunition.
Precedent	Concentration and control of community resources, wealth and means of production in the hands of a few individuals are, in the eyes of the Constitution, an evil which must be eradicate from the social organization , and hence, any fundamental right, to the extent that it fosters this evil, is liable to be abridged or taken away in the interest of the social structure envisaged, by the Constitution.
Precedent	The scheme of the fundamental rights in Part III itself shows that restrictions on them have been placed to guard against their exercise in an evil way.
Precedent	1357.
Precedent	Nor is there anything in the Preamble to suggest that the power to amend the fundamental right to property is cut down.
Precedent	Actually there is no reference to the right to property.
Precedent	On the other hand, while declaring the objectives which inspired the framers of the Constitution to give unto themselves the Constitution which, they hoped, would be able to achieve them, they took good care to provide for the amendment of "this Constitution".
Precedent	It was clearly implied that if the operative parts of the Constitution failed to put us on the road to the objectives, the Constitution was liable to be appropriately amended.
Precedent	Even the Preamble, which, as we know, had been adopted by the  constituent assembly as a part of the Constitution.
Precedent	(Constituent Assembly Debates Vol.
Precedent	X p. 456) was liable to be amended.
Precedent	Right to property was, perhaps, deliberately not enthroned in the Preamble because that would have conflicted with the objectives of securing to all its citizens justice, social, economic and political, and equality of opportunity, to achieve which Directive Principles were laid down in Articles 38 to 51.
Precedent	Moreover the Preamble, it is now well settled-can neither increase nor decrease the power granted in plain and clear words in the enacting parts of a statute.
Precedent	See : <ORG> [1960] 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	250 at pp.
Precedent	281 and 282.
Precedent	Further, the legislature may well-intend that the enacting part do extend beyond the apparent ambit of the Preamble.
Precedent	See : <CITATION_1405204> 43 Madras 529 P.C.
Precedent	at 536.
Precedent	As a matter of fact if the enacting part is clear and unambiguous it does not call for construction.
Precedent	In <NAME>'s case <ORG> had been called upon to cons true Article V, the amending clause, so as to cut down the amending power by implications arising out of certain other provisions of the Constitution itself.
Precedent	Replying to the argument the court observed, "the United 'State asserts that Article V is clear in statement and in meaning contains no ambiguity and calls for no resort to rules of construction.
Precedent	A mere reading demonstrates that this is true."
Precedent	These observations apply with greater force to our amending clause namely Article 368 , for in Article V of the American Constitution there was some room for play of argument on the basis of alternative methods permitted for the ratification of the proposed amendments.
Argument	On the basis of the alternative methods provided in Article V-one by the State legislature and the other by the State convention-it was argued that, the State convention was the appropriate method Bo the exclusion of the State legislature, because the prohibition amendment (18th amendment) directly affected personal liberty.
Argument	Where personal liberty wa s involved, it was submitted, the people alone through their convention could ratify an amendment, especially, as under Article X the people had reserved to themselves the powers which were not expressly conferred on the federal Constitution.
Argument	This argument was rejected by <ORG> on the ground that the language of Article V was clear and unambiguous and though alternative methods were provided for, the ultimate authority as to which alternative method should be adopted was the <ORG> and if the <ORG> chose the method of ratification by the State legislature there was an end of the matter.
Precedent	The court observed "In the Constitution words and phrases were used in their normal and ordinary as distinct from technical meaning.
Precedent	When the intention is clear, there is no room for construction and no excuse for interpolation".
Precedent	By interpolation the court specifically meant an addition in the nature of a proviso to Article V limiting the power of the <ORG> as to the choice of the body it would make for the  purposes of ratification.
Precedent	1358.
Precedent	Reference was made to certain cases with a view to show that though there were no words suggesting a limitation on a power, implied limitations or prohibitions are noticed by courts.
Precedent	In a recent Australian case of Victoria v. The <ORG> 45 A.L.I.R.
Precedent	251 the question arose as to the power of the <ORG> Parliament under Section 51(ii) of the Constitution to make laws with respect to taxation under the Pay-roll Tax Assessment Act, 1941-1969.
Precedent	It was unanimously held by the court that the <ORG> Parliament had the power.
Precedent	During the course of arguments, the question arose, which has been troubling the Australian courts for years, whether there were implied limitations on commonwealth Legislative power under the Constitution in view of the fact that the Preamble to the Constitution recited that the people had agreed "to unite in one indissoluble federal commonwealth under the <ORG>."
Precedent	In <ORG> case, already referred to, which had been regarded for a l ong time as the final word on the question, the alleged implied prohibition or limitation had been rejected.
Precedent	The question was held to be a question of construction with regard to the extent of power and if the power was ascertained from the express words, there could be no further limitation thereon by implication.
Precedent	But in the case referred to above, while three Judges accepted that view as still good, the other four were of the contrary opinion.
Precedent	Whichever view is correct that really makes no difference to me question before us.
Precedent	We are concerned with the amending power.
Precedent	In the Australian case the Judges were concerned with legistative power and that had to be ascertained within the four corners of the Constitution by which the power had been created and under which it had to be exercised.
Precedent	There was room for construction on the basis of the words and structure of the Constitution, especially, the Preamble which was not liable to be amended by the Commonwealth.
Precedent	On the other hand, since the power to amend the Con stitution is a superior power it cannot Be bound by any provision of the Constitution itself, the obvious reason being that even such a provision is amendable under the Constitution.
Precedent	In re The Initiative and Referendum Act,  A.C. 935 it was held by <ORG> that the British North America Act, 1867, Section 92 , head 1, which empowers a Provincial legislature to amend the Constitution of the Province, "excepting as regards the office of Lieutenant-Governor," excludes the making of a law which abrogates any power which the <ORG> possesses through the Lieutenant- Governor who directly represents the <ORG>.
Precedent	By the Initiative and Referendum Act the legislative assembly of Manitoba-a Province in Canada-compelled the Lieutenant- Governor to submit a proposed law to a body of voters totally distinct from the legislature of which he is the Constitutional head, and would render him powerless to prevent it from becoming an actual law if approved by those voters.
Precedent	It was held that this directly affected the offic e of the Lieutenant-Governor as part of the legislature and since the amendment to the Constitution had the effect of affecting that office which was expressly excepted from the amending power the law was void.
Precedent	It is thus seen that there was no question of an implied limitation.
Precedent	In the other case cited before us namely <NAME> and <NAME>.
Precedent	v. The Queen  A.C. 259 no question of amending the Constitution arose.
Precedent	There by an ordinary act of the legislature made in 1962 under Section 29(1) of the Ceylon (Constitution and Independence) Orders in Council, 1946-47 an attempt was made to partially vest in the legislature and the executive the judicial powers of the judges which vested in them under a separate Imperial Charter viz.
Precedent	the Charter of Justice, 1833 the effective operation of which was recognized in the Constitution of 1946-47.
Precedent	It was held that the Act was ultra vires the Constitution.
Precedent	Some more cases like <NAME>'s  A.C. 172 case, <NAME> v. Attorney General of Queensland 23 C.L.R.
Precedent	4 57, <CITATION_1672729>  2 S.C.R.
Precedent	109 at 112, were cited to show that Constitutional laws permit implications to be drawn where necessary.
Precedent	No body disputes that proposition.
Precedent	Courts may have to do so where the implication is necessary to be drawn.
Precedent	In <NAME>'s case <ORG> is supposed to have expressed the opinion on a construction of Section 29 of the Ceylon (Constitution) Order in Council, 1946 that Sub- sections 2 and 3 are unamendable under the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the first place, the observation is obiter, and it is doubtful if their Lordships intended to convey that even under Section 29(4) , they were unamendable.
RatioOfTheDecision	A plain reading of the latter provision shows they were amend able by a special majority.
Precedent	Secondly, in an earlier portion of the judgment provisions 29(2) & (3) are described as 'entrenched', the plain dictionary meaning of which is that they are not to be repealed except under more than stringent conditions.
Precedent	See also <NAME>'s Introduction to Dicey pages xxxvi to xxxvii.
Precedent	<NAME> in his  Constitution of Ceylon (1949) points out at page 22 that the limitations of 29(2) & (3) can be altered or abridged by the special procedure under Section 29(4) .
Precedent	Similarly we are in Constitutional Structure of the Commonwealth 1960 reprinted in 1963 pages 83-84.
Precedent	In any event, that was a pure matter of construction on a reading of Sub-sections 1 to 4 of Section 29 together.
Precedent	In <NAME>'s case the question for consideration was as to the interpretation of the expression 'Constitution of such legislature' in Section 5 of the Colonial Laws Validation Act, 1865.
Precedent	At the time in question the legislature consisted of a lower house and an upper house and it was held that the expression 'Constitution of such legislature" 'was wide enough to include the conversion of a bicameral legislature into a unicameral one.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. also held 'legislature' in the particular context meant the houses of legislature and did not include the.
Precedent	<ORG>.
Precedent	In <NAME> case it was merely held that if by law made under Article 4 of our C onstitution a state was formed, that state must have legislative, executive and judicial organs which are merely the accoutrements of a state as understood under the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The connotation of a 'state' included these three organs.
RatioOfTheDecision	That again was a matter of pure construction.
RatioOfTheDecision	None of the cases sheds any light on the question with which we are concerned viz.
RatioOfTheDecision	whether an unambiguous and plenary power to amend the provisions of the Constitution, which included the Preamble and the fundamental rights, must be frightened by the fact that some superior and transcedental character has been ascribed to them.
RatioOfTheDecision	1359.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the other hand, in America where implied limitations were sought to be pressed in cases dealing with Constitutional amendments, the same were rejected.
RatioOfTheDecision	In <NAME>'s case <ORG> rejected the contention of implied limitation supposed to arise from some express provisions in the Constitution itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	Referring to this case <NAME> in Cases in Constitutional Law, 5th edition pages 1375-1387 say s"This case it is hoped puts an end to the efforts to have the court examine into the subject matter of Constitutional amendment" In The National Prohibition 65 Law, edn.
RatioOfTheDecision	994 cases decided earlier, the Prohibition Amendment (18th) was challenged, as the briefs show, on a host of alleged implied limitations based on the Constitution, its scheme and its history.
RatioOfTheDecision	The opinion of the court did not accept any of them, in fact, did not even notice them.
RatioOfTheDecision	American jurists are clearly of the opinion that <ORG> had rejected the argument of implied limitations.
Precedent	See for example Cooley Constitutional Law, 4th edition, 46-47; Burdick Law of American Constitution pp.
Precedent	45 to 48.
Precedent	1360.
Precedent	The argument that essential features (by which Mr. <NAME> means "essential features, basic elements or fundamental principles")' of the Constitution, though capable of amendment to a limited extent are not liable to be damaged or destroyed is only a variation on the argument previously urged before this <ORG> on the basis of the  socalled "spirit of the Constitution" which had been rejected as far back as 1952.
Precedent	See : <CITATION_41868>  S.C.R.
Precedent	889.
Precedent	That case arose out of the Bihar Land Reforms Act, 1950 which was pending in <ORG> at the time of the commencement of the Constitution.
Precedent	After it became law it was reserved for the consideration of the President who gave assent to it.
Precedent	Thus it became one of the laws referred to in Article 31(4) of the Constitution and in virtue of that provision it could not be called in question on the ground that it contravened the provisions of Clause 2 of Article 31.
Precedent	Under that law Zamindari was abolished and the lands vested in the <ORG>.
Precedent	The Zamindars received what was described as illusory compensation.
Precedent	As there was danger of challenge under Articles 14, 19 and 31, the Constitution was amended to incorporate Article 31A and Article 31B to take effect from the date of the commencement of the Constitution and this Act along with similar other Acts were included in the Ninth Schedule.
Precedent	In San <NAME> case Che amendment was held valid and when the case came before this Court the arguments became limited in scope.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> who appeared for the Zamindars tried to skirt the bar under Article 31(4) by relying on Entry 36 List II and Entry 42 in List III arguing that the law in so far as it did not acquire the Zamindaris for a public purpose or make provision for adequate compensation was incompetent under those entries.
Precedent	Dr. <NAME> who appeared for other Zamindars took a different stand.
Precedent	In the words of <NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	"He maintained that a Constitutional prohibition against compulsory acquisition of property without public necessity and payment of compensation was deducible from what he called the "spirit of the Constitution", which, according to him was a valid test for judging the Constitutionality of a statute.
Precedent	The Constitution, being avowedly one for establishing liberty, justice and equality and a government of a free people with only limited powers, must be held to contain an  implied prohibition against taking private property without just compensation and in the absence of a public purpose.
Precedent	(Emphasis is supplied) He relied on certain American decisions and text-books as supporting the view that a Constitutional prohibition can be derived by implication from the spirit of the Constitution where no express prohibition has been enacted in that behalf.
Precedent	Articles 31-A and 31-B barred only objections based on alleged infringements of the fundamental rights conferred by Part III, but if, from the other provisions thereof, it could be inferred that there must be a public purpose and payment of compensation before private property could be compulsorily acquired by the <ORG>, there was nothing in the two articles aforesaid to preclude objection on the ground that the impugned Acts do not satisfy these requirements and are, therefore, unConstitutional."
Precedent	(Emphasis supplied) This argument was rejected in these words "In the face of the limitations on the <ORG>'s power of compulsory acquisition  thus incorporated in the body of the Constitution, from which "estates" alone are excluded, it would, in my opinion, be contrary to elementary canons of statutory construction to read, by implication, those very limitations into entry 36 of List II, alone or in conjunction with entry 42 of List III of the Seventh Schedule, or to deduce them from "the spirit of the Constitution", and that too, in respect of the very properties excluded."
Precedent	The argument was that having regard to the Preamble and the fundamental rights which established liberty, justice and equality and a government of a free people with only limited powers, taking of private property without just compensation and in the absence of a public purpose was unConstitutional, and this conclusion should be drawn by implied prohibition in spite of Article 31(4 ), 31A  & 31B expressly barring challenge on those very grounds.
Precedent	In other words, an express provision of the Constitution validating a state law was sought to be nullified on the basis of 'essentia l features and basic principles' underlying the Preamble and the fundamental rights, but the attempt was negatived.
Precedent	I sec no distinction between Dr. <NAME>'s argument in the above case and the case before us, because the plenary power of amendment under Article 368 is sought to be limited by implications supposed to arise from those same 'essential features and basic principles'.
Precedent	1361.
Precedent	A legislature functioning under a Constitution is entitled to make a law and it is not disputed that such a law can be amended in any way the legislature likes by addition, alteration or even repeal.
Precedent	This power to amend is implicit in the legislative power to make laws.
Precedent	It can never be suggested that when the legislature amends its own statute either directly or indirectly it is inhibited by any important or essential parts of that statute.
Precedent	It can amend the important, desirable, parts as unceremoniously as it can any other unimportant parts of the statute.
Precedent	That being so, one does not see the reasonableness of refusing this l atitude to a body which is specifically granted the unqualified power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	While the legislature's power to amend operates on each and every provision of the statute it is difficult to see why the amending clause in a Constitution specifically authorising the amendment of the Constitution should stand inhibited by any of the Constitution.
Precedent	Essential parts and unessential parts of a Constitution should make no difference to the amending power (Compare passage from <NAME>'s case already quoted at p. 43-4) That a legislature can repeal an act as a whole and the constituent body does not repeal the Constitution as a whole is not a point of distinction.
Precedent	A legislature repeals an Act when it has outlived its utility.
Precedent	But so far as a Constitution is concerned it is an organic instrument continuously growing in utility and the question of its repeal never arises as long as orderely change is possible.
Precedent	A Constitution is intended to last.
Precedent	Legislative acts do not have that ambition.
Precedent	It is the natur e and character of the Constitution as a growing, organic, permanent and sovereign instrument of government which exclude the repeal of the Constitution as a whole and not the nature and character of the Amending power.
Precedent	1362.
Precedent	Since the 'essential features and basic principles' referred to by Mr. <NAME> are those culled from the provisions of the Constitution it is clear that he wants to divide the Constitution into parts-one of provisions containing the essential features and the other containing non-essential features.
Precedent	According to him the latter can be amended in any way the <ORG> likes, but so far as the former provisions are concerned, though they may be amended, they cannot be amended so as to damage or destroy the core of the essential features.
Precedent	Two difficulties arise.
Precedent	Who is to decide what are essential provisions and nonessential provisions ?
Precedent	According to Mr. <NAME> it is the court which should do it If that is correct, what stable standard will guide the court in deciding which provisio n is essential and which is not essential?
Precedent	Every provision, in one sense, is an essential provision, because if a law is made by the <ORG> or the State legislatures contravening even the most insignificant provision of the Constitution, that law will be void.
Precedent	From that point of view the courts acting under the Constitution will have to look upon its provisions with an equal eye.
Precedent	Secondly, if an essential provision is amended and a new provision is inserted which, in the opinion of the constituent body, should be presumed to be more essential than the one repealed, what is the yardstick the court is expected to employ?
Precedent	It will only mean that whatever necessity the constituent body may feel in introducing a change in the Constitution, whatever change of policy that body may like to introduce in the Constitution, the same is liable to be struck down if the court is not satisfied either about the necessity or the policy.
Precedent	Clearly this is not a function of the courts.
Precedent	The difficulty assumes greater proportion  when an amendment is challenged on the ground that the core of an essential feature is either damaged or destroyed.
RatioOfTheDecision	What is the standard?
RatioOfTheDecision	Who will decide where the core lies and when it is reached ?
RatioOfTheDecision	One can understand the argument that particular provisions in the Constitution embodying some essential features are not amendable at all.
RatioOfTheDecision	But the difficulty arises when it is conceded that the provision is liable to be amended, but not so as to touch its 'core'.
RatioOfTheDecision	Apart from the difficulty in determining where the 'core' of an 'essential feature' lies, it does not appear to be sufficiently realized what fantastic results may follow in working the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Suppose an amendment of a provision is made this year.
Precedent	The mere fact that an amendment is made will not give any body the right to come to this Court to have the amendment nullified on the ground that it affects the core of an essential feature.
Precedent	It is only when a law is made under the amended provision and that law affects some individual's right, that he may  come to this Court.
Precedent	At that time he will first show that the amendment is bad because it affects the core of an essential feature and if he succeeds there, he will automatically succeed and the law made by the <ORG> in the confidence that it is protected by the amended Constitution will be rendered void.
Precedent	And such a challenge to the amendment may come several years after the amendment which till then is regarded as a part of the Constitution.
Precedent	In other words, every amendment, however innocuous it may seem when it is made is liable to be struk down several years after the amendment although all the people have arranged their affairs on the strength of the amended Constitution.
Precedent	And in dealing with the challenge to a particular amendment and searching for the core of the essential feature the court will have to do it either with reference to the original Constitution or the Constitution as it stood with all its amendments upto date.
Precedent	The former procedure is clearly absurd because the Constitution has already  undergone vital changes by amendments in the meantime.
Precedent	So the challenged amendment will have to be assessed on the basis of the Constitution with all its amendments made prior to the challenged amendment.
Precedent	All such prior amendments will have to be accepted as good because they are not under challenge, and on that basis Judges will have to deal with the challenged amendment.
Precedent	But the other amendments are also not free from challenge in subsequent proceedings, because we have already seen that every amendment can be challenged several years after it is made, if a law made under it affects a private individual.
Precedent	So there will be a continuous state of flux after an amendment is made and at any given moment when the court wants to determine the core of the essential feature, it will have to discard, in order to be able to say where the core lies, every other amendment because these amendments also being unstable will not help in the determination of the core.
Precedent	In other words, the courts will have to go by the origina l Constitution to decide the core of an essential feature ignoring altogether all the amendments made in the meantime, all the transformations of rights that have taken place after them, all the arrangements people have made on the basis of the validity of the amendments and all the laws made under them without question.
Precedent	An argument which leads to such obnoxious results can hardly be entertained.
Precedent	In this very case if the core argument were to be sustained, several previous amendments will have to be set aside because they have undoubtedly affected the core of one or the other fundamental right.
Precedent	Prospective overruling will be the order of the day.
Precedent	1363.
Precedent	The argument of implied limitations in effect invites us to assess the merits and demerits of the several provisions of the Constitution as a whole in the light of social, political and economic concepts embodied therein and determine on such an assessment what is the irreducible minimum of the several features of the Constitution.
Precedent	Any attempt by amendment, it  is contended, to go beyond such irreducible minimum-also called the 'core' of essential features-should be disallowed as invalid.
Precedent	In other words, we are invited to resort to the substantive due process doctrine of <ORG> in the interpretation of a Constitutional Amendment.
Precedent	That doctrine was rejected long ago by this <ORG> (<NAME>'s case) even in its application to ordinary legislation.
Precedent	See 1950 S.C.R.
Precedent	88 (<NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	110) (<NAME>, J.
Precedent	312).
Precedent	The argument does not have anything to do with the meaning of the expression 'Amendment of the Constitution' because it is conceded for the purpose of this argument that 'amendment of this Constitution' means amendment of all provisions by way of addition, alteration or repeal' What is contended, is that by the very implications of the structure, general principles and concepts embodied in the Constitution, an amendment can go only thus far and no further.
Precedent	In other words, the scope of amendment is circumscribed not by what the constituent body thinks, b ut by what the Judges ultimately think is its proper limits.
Precedent	And these limits, it is obvious, will vary with individual Judges, and as in due process, the limits will be those fixed by a majority of Judges at one time, changed, if necessary, by a bigger majority at another.
Precedent	Every time an amendment is made of some magnitude as by the Twenty-fifth Amendment we will have, without anything to go on, to consider how, in our opinion, the several provisions of the Constitution react on one another, their relative importance from our point of view, the limits on such imponderable concepts as liberty, equality, justice, we think proper to impose, whether we shall give preponderance to directive principles in one case and fundamental rights in another-in short, determine the 'spirit of the Constitution' and decide how far the amendment conforms with that 'spirit'.
Precedent	We are no longer, than construing the words of the Constitution which is our legitimate province but determining the spirit of the Constitution-a course depr ecated by this <ORG> in <NAME>'s case at pages 120-121.
Precedent	When concepts of social or economic justice are offered for our examination in their interaction on provisions relating to right to property-matters traditionally left to legislative policy and wisdom, we are bound to flounder "in labyrinths to the character of which we have no sufficient guides.
Precedent	1364.
Precedent	It is true that Judges do judicially determine whether certain restrictions imposed in a statute are reasonable or not.
Precedent	We also decide questions involving reasonableness of any particular action.
Precedent	But Judges do this because there are objective guides.
Precedent	The Constitution and the <ORG> specifically leave such determination to the higher courts, not because they will be always right, but because the subject matter itself defies definition and the legislatures would sooner abide by what the judges say.
Precedent	The same is true about limits of delegated legislation or limits of legislative power when it encroaches on the judicial or any other field.
Precedent	Since the det erminataon of all these questions is left to the higher judiciary under the Constitution and the law, the judges have to apply themselves to the tasks, however difficult they may be, in order to determine the legality of any particular legislative action.
Precedent	But all this applies to laws made under the Constitution and have no relevance when we have to deal with a Constitutional amendment.
Precedent	The Constitution supplies the guides for the assessment of any statute made under it.
Precedent	It does not supply any guides to its own amendment which is entirely a matter of policy.
Precedent	1365.
Precedent	The 'core' argument and the division into essential and nonessential parts are fraught with the greatest mischief and will lead to such insuperable difficulties that, if permitted, they will open a Pandora's box of endless litigation creating uncertainty about the provisions of the Constitution which was intended to be clear and certain.
Precedent	Every single provision emobies a concept, a standard, norm or rule which the framers of the Constitution thought w as so essential that they included it in the Constitution.
Precedent	Every amendment thereof will be liable to be assailed on the ground that an essential feature or basic principle was seriously affected.
Precedent	Our people have a reputation of being litigious lot.
Precedent	We shall be only adding to this.
Precedent	1366.
Precedent	When an amendment is successfully passed, it becomes part of the Constitution having equal status with the rest of the provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	If such an amendment is liable to be struck down on the ground that it damages or destroys an essential feature, the power so claimed should, a fortiori, operate on the Constitution as it stands.
Precedent	It will be open to the court to weigh every essential feature like a fundamental right and, if that feature is hedged in by limitations, it would be liable to be struck down as damaging an essential feature.
Precedent	Take for example personal liberty, a fundamental right under the Constitution.
Precedent	If the court holds the opinion that the provision with regard to preventive detention in Article 22 d amages the core of personal liberty it will be struck down.
Precedent	The same can be said about the freedom in Article 19.
Precedent	If this Court feels that the provision with regard to, say State monopolies damages the fundamental right of trade of a citizen, it can be struck down.
Precedent	In other words, if an amendment which has become part of the Constitution is liable to be struck down because it damages an essential feature it should follow that every restriction originally placed on that feature in the Constitution would necessarily come under the pruning knife of the courts.
Precedent	1367.
Precedent	In short, if the doctrine of unamendability of the core of essential feature is accepted, it will mean that we add some such proviso below Article 368 : " Nothing in the above Amendment will be deemed to have authorized an Amendment of the Constitution, which has the effect of damaging or destroying the core of the essential features, basic principles and fundamental elements of the Constitution as may be determined by the <ORG>."
Precedent	This is quite imp ermissible.
Precedent	1368.
Precedent	It is not necessary to refer to the numerous authorities cited before us to show that what are described as some of the essential features are not unamendable.
Precedent	It will be sufficient to refer to only a few.
Precedent	<NAME> in his book "The American Commonwealth" New and revised edition, Vol.
Precedent	I says at pages 366-67 with reference to Article V of the American Constitution "But looking at the Constitution simply as a legal document, one finds nothing in it to prevent the adoption of an amendment providing a method for dissolving the existing Federal tie, whereupon such method would be applied so as to form new unions, or permit each State to become an absolutely sovereign and independent commonwealth.
Precedent	The power of the people of the United States appears competent to effect this, should it ever be desired, in a perfectly legal way, just as <ORG> is legally competent to redivide Great Britain into the sixteen or eighteen independent kingdoms which existed within the island in the eighth ce ntury."
Precedent	<NAME> in his revised edition, 1964 The Constitutional Problems under Lincoln, says at page 394 with reference to Article V "Aside from the restriction concerning the "equal suffrage" of the States in the <ORG>, the Constitution, since 1808, has contained no amendable part, and it designates no field of legislation that may not be reached by the amending power.
Precedent	An Amendment properly made becomes "valid, to all intents and purposes, as part of this Constitution", having as much "force as any other article.
Precedent	There is no valid distinction between "the Constitution itself" and the amendments.
Precedent	The Constitution at any given time includes all up to the latest amendments, and excludes portions that have not survived the amending process.
Precedent	We should think not of "the Constitution and its amendments," but of "the Constitution as amended".
Precedent	This is especially true when we reflect that certain of the amendments supplant or construe portions of the original document."
Precedent	<NAME> in his book, The General Principles of C onstitutional Law in the United <ORG>s of America, fourth edition, says at pages 46-47 "Article V of the Constitution prohibits any amendment by which any <ORG> "without its consent shall be deprived of its equal suffrage in the <ORG>".
Precedent	Beyond this there appears to be no limit to the power of amendment.
Precedent	This, at any rate, is the result of the decision in the so-called National Prohibition Cases....
Precedent	The amendment was attacked on the grounds that it was legislative in its character, an invasion of natural rights and an encroachment on the fundamental principles of dual sovereignty, hut the contention was overruled.
Precedent	The decision totally negatived the contention that "An amendment must be confined in its scope to an alteration or improvement of that which is already contained in the Constitution and cannot change its basic structure, include new grants of power to <ORG>, nor relinquish to the <ORG> those which already have been granted to it."
Precedent	<NAME> and <NAME> writing in the "Annotated Constitut ion of the Australian Commonwealth" (1901) observe as follows at p. 989 with regard to the amending clause of the Constitution namely Section 128 .
Precedent	"It may be concluded that there is no limit to the power to amend the Constitution, but that it can only be brought into action according to certain modes prescribed.
Precedent	We will consider the modes and conditions of Constitutional reforms further; meanwhile it is essential to grasp the significance and comprehensiveness of the power itself.
Precedent	For example, the Constitution could be amended either in the direction of strengthening or weakening <ORG>; strengthening it, by conferring on it new and additional powers; weakening it, by taking away powers.
Precedent	The Constitution could be amended by reforming the structure of <ORG> and modifying the relation of the two <ORG>; by increasing or diminishing the power of the <ORG> in reference to Money Bills; by making the <ORG> subject to dissolution at the same time as <ORG>.
Precedent	I t is even contended by some daring interpreters that the Constitution could be amended by abolishing the <ORG>.
Precedent	It could certainly be amended by remodeiling <ORG>, abolishing what is known as Responsible Government, and introducing a new system, such as that which prevails in Switzerland; according to which the administration of the public departments is placed in the hands of officers elected by the Federal legislature.
Precedent	The Constitution could be amended by altering the tenure of the judges, by removing their appointment from the <ORG>, and authorizing the election of judges by the <ORG> or by the people.
Precedent	The Constitution could be amended in its most vital part, the amending power itself, by providing that alterations may be initiated by the people, according to the plan of <ORG>; that proposed alterations may be formulated by the <ORG> and submitted to the people; that proposed alterations may, with certain Constitutional exceptions, become law on being  approved of by a majority of the electors voting, dispensing with the necessity of a majority of the States.
Precedent	1369.
Precedent	On a consideration, therefore, of the nature of the amending power, the unqualified manner in which it is given in Article 368 of the Constitution it is impossible to imply any limitations on the power to amend the fundamental rights.
Precedent	Since there are no limitations express or implied on the amending power, it must be conceded that all the Amendments which are in question here must be deemed to be valid.
Precedent	We cannot question their policy or their wisdom.
Precedent	1370.
Precedent	Coming to the actual amendments made in the Constitution by the twenty-fifth amendment Act, we find in the first place that the original Clause (2) of Article 31 is recast to some extent by deleting any reference to 'compensation' in cases of compulsory acquisition and requisition for a public purpose.
Precedent	The fundamental right now is not to receive 'compensation' which this Court construed to mean 'a just equivalent' but to receive an "amount"  which the legislature itself may fix or which may be determined in accordance with the principles as may be specified by the law.
Argument	Then again the "amount" may be given in cash or in such manner as the law may specify.
Argument	The principal objection to the amendment is that the clause arms the legislature with power to fix any amount which it considers fit and such fixation may be entirely arbitrary having no nexus whatsoever with the property of which a person is actually deprived.
Argument	In similar cases, it is submitted, the amount fixed may be more in one and very much less in another depending entirely on the whim of the legislature.
Argument	Conceivably the amount may be illusory having regard to the value of the property.
Argument	The principles for determining the amount may equally be arbitrary and unrelated to the deprivation.
Argument	Therefore, it is contended, the amendment is bad.
Argument	It is difficult to understand how an amendment to the Constitution becomes invalid because the Constitution authorizes the legislatures to fix an "amount" or  to specify the principles on which the "amount" is to be determined instead of fixing the "compensation" or specifying the principles for determining "compensation".
Argument	Even compensation ultimately is an "amount".
Argument	All that the amendment has done is to negative the interpretation put by this <ORG> on the concept of compensation, Clause (2) recognizes the fundamental right to receive an amount in case of compulsory acquisition or requisition and all that it wants to clarify is that the fundamental right is not to receive compensation as interpreted by this <ORG> but a right to receive an amount in lieu of the deprivation which the legislature thinks fit.
Argument	It is not the case that if a fair amount is fixed for the acquisition or fair principles to determine it are laid down, the amendment would still be invalid.
Argument	The contention is that it becomes invalid because there is a possibility of the abuse of the power to fix the amount.
Precedent	There is no power which cannot be abused.
Precedent	All Constitutions grant power to legislatures to  make laws on a variety of subjects and the mere possibility of the power being used unwisely, injuriously or even abused is not a valid ground to deny legislative power.
Precedent	See : <ORG> v. <NAME> 1887, Vol.
Precedent	XII-Appeal Cases 575 at pages 586-587.
Precedent	If that is the position with regard to legislative power, there does not appear to be any good reason why the possibility of abuse of it by the legislature should inhibit an amendment of the Constitution which gives the power.
Precedent	Whether a particular law fixes an amount which is illusory or is otherwise a fraud on power denying the fundamental right to receive an amount specifically conferred by Clause (2) will depend upon the law when made and is tested on the basis of Clause (2).
Precedent	One cannot anticipate any such matters and strike down an amendment which, in all conscience, does not preclude a fair amount being fixed for payment in the circumstances of a particular acquisition or requisition.
Precedent	The possibility of abuse of a power given by an amendment of the Constitu tion is not determinative of the validity of the amendment.
Precedent	1371.
Precedent	The new Clause 2B inserted in Article 31 having the consequence of excluding the application of Article 19(1)(f) to a law referred to in Clause (2) of Article 31 is merely a re-statement of the law laid down by this <ORG> after the constiution came into force.
Precedent	The mutual exclusiveness of Article 19(1)(f) and Article 31(2) had been recognized by this <ORG> in a scries of cases.
Precedent	See : <CITATION_1332919> .
Precedent	(2) S.C.R.
Precedent	949.
Precedent	That principle is now embodied in the new amendment.
Precedent	1372.
Precedent	The only substantial objection to the twenty-fifth amendment is based on the new Article 31C inserted in the Constitution by Section 3 of the twenty-fifth amendment act.
Precedent	1373.
Precedent	The new article is as follows:  31C.
Precedent	Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 , no law giving effect to the policy of the state towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with, or take s away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Article 14 , Article 19 or Article 31; and no law containing a declaration chat it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy.
