Precedent	Provided that where such law is made by <ORG> State, the provisions of this article shall not apply thereto unless such law, having been reserved for the consideration of the President, has received his assent.
Precedent	1374.
Precedent	Ignoring the proviso for the moment, one finds that the main clause of the article falls into two parts.
Precedent	The first part provides that a law of a particular description shall not be deemed to be void on the ground that it affects injuriously somebody's fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	The second part provides that if such a law contains a particular declaration, courts shall not entertain a particular kind of objection.
Precedent	1375.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the first place, it should be noted that what is saved by Article 31C is  a law i.e.
RatioOfTheDecision	a law made by a competent legislature.
RatioOfTheDecision	Secondly since Article 31C comes under the specific heading 'Right to property' in Part III dealing with fundamental rights it is evident that the law must involve right to property.
RatioOfTheDecision	That it must of necessity do so is apparent from the description of the law given in the article.
RatioOfTheDecision	The description is that the law gives effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clauses (b) & (c) of Article 39.
RatioOfTheDecision	That article is one of the several articles in Part IV of the Constitution dealing with Directive Principles of <ORG> Policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 37 provides that though the Directive Principles are not enforceable by any court, they are nevertheless fundamental in the governance of the country and it shall be the duty of the <ORG> to apply these principles in making laws.
RatioOfTheDecision	It follows from this that the Governments and <ORG> are enjoined to make laws giving effect to the Directive Principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	We are immediately concerned with the Directive Pri nciples contained in Article 39(b) and (c) namely, that the <ORG> shall direct its policy towards securing (b) that the ownership and control of the material resources of the community are so distributed as best to subserve the common good; and (c) that the operation of the economic system does not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to the common detriment.
RatioOfTheDecision	In short Clause (b) contemplates measures to secure what is known as equitable distribution of community resources and Clause (c) contemplates measures for preventing concentration of wealth and means of production in a few private hands.
Precedent	Read along with Article 38 and other principles in this Part, they justify the conclusions of <NAME> in his Indian Constitution : Cornerstone of a Nation-that our Constitution is informed by social democratic principles.
Precedent	See : pages 41-52 of the book.
Precedent	The final conclusion he came to is expressed in this way:  By establishing these positive obligations of the state, the members of the C onstituent <ORG> made it the responsibility of future Indian governments to find a middle way between individual liberty and the public good, between preserving the property and the privilege of the few and bestowing benefits on the many in order to liberate the powers of all men equally for contributions to the common good.
Precedent	p. 52.
Precedent	The philosophy which informs the Constitution looks on concentration of wealth and means of production as a social evil because such concentration, resulting in the concentration of political and economic power in the hands of a few private individuals, not only leads to unequal freedom, on the one hand, but results, on the other, in undermining the same in the case of many.
Precedent	In such conditions it is widely believed that the goals of Equality and Justice, social, economic and political, become unreal, and since the Constitution itself directs that laws may be made to inhibit such conditions it is inevitable that these laws aimed at the reduction of unequal freedoms enjoyed by a  few will impair to some extent their fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	That would be justified even on the 'core' theory of Mr. <NAME> because he admits the possibility of an abridgement of a fundamental right in similar cases.
Precedent	Therefore, Article 31C provides, even as Article 31A provided many years ago, that such laws should not be called in question on the grounds furnished by Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	If a law is made with a view to giving effect to the Directive Principles mentioned in Article 39(b) and 39(c) the law is in conformity with the direct mandate of the Constitution and must be deemed to be Constitutional.
Precedent	The effect of the first part of Article 31C is the I same as if, a proviso had been inserted below Article 13(2) or each of the several Articles 14, 19 and 31 excluding its application to the particular type of law mentioned in Article 31C.
Precedent	If the law does not genuinely purport to give effect to the specified Directive Principles it will not be secure against the challenge un der Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	Indeed since the Directive Principles are couched in general terms they may present some difficulty in judging whether any individual law falls within the ambit of the description given in Article 31C but such a difficulty is no reason for denying, the validity of the amendment.
Precedent	Courts had no difficulty in deciding whether any particular law did fall under Article 31A or not.
Precedent	1376.
Precedent	The real difficulty is raised by the second part of Article 31C which provides "No law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy."
Precedent	The contention is that if any law makes a declaration as stated, that is conclusive of the fact that it is covered by Article 39(b) or (c) and courts will be debarred from entertaining any objection on the ground that it is not so covered.
Precedent	In other words, it is submitted, the declaration when made in a law whether genuinely falling under Article 39(b)  or (c) or not will conclude the issue and the courts will be debarred from questioning the declaration.
Precedent	The result is, according to the submission, that the legislatures may with impunity make a law contravening provisions of the Constitution and by the simple device of a declaration insert the law as an exception to Articles 14, 19 and 31-i.e.
Precedent	in other words amend the Constitution which the legislature cannot do.
Precedent	The Constitution, it is pointed out, may be amended only in the way prescribed in Article 368 and no other and, therefore, Article 31C authorising an amendment in a way other than the one laid down in Article 368 , which still forms part of the Constitution with full force, is invalid.
Precedent	1377.
Argument	On behalf of the <ORG>, however, it is claimed that the new Article 31C does not have the effect, attributed to it on behalf of the petitioners.
Argument	It is, submitted, that Article 31C does not prevent judicial review as to whether the law referred to therein is of the description it maintains it is.
Argument	If on a conside ration of its true nature and character the court considers that the legislation is not one having a nexus with the principles contained in Article 39(b) or (c), it will not be saved under Article 31C.
Precedent	The sole purpose of the declaration', according to the submission is to remove from the scope of judicial review a question of a political nature the reason for it being, as explained in <NAME> v. Illinois 343 U.S. 250.
Precedent	"The legislative remedy in practice might not mitigate the evil or might itself give rise to new problems which would only manifest once again the paradox of reform.
Precedent	It is the price to be paid for the trial and error inherent in legislative efforts to deal with obstinate social issues.
Precedent	1378.
Precedent	It appears to us that the approach suggested on behalf of the <ORG> is the correct approach to the interpretation of Article 31C.
Precedent	1379.
RatioOfTheDecision	The <ORG>'s functional policy is to strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting as effectively as it may a social order in which justice,  social, economic and political shall, inform all the institutions of the national life.
RatioOfTheDecision	( Article 38 ).
RatioOfTheDecision	That is the goal of the <ORG> poticy.
RatioOfTheDecision	As practical steps, the <ORG> is commanded in the next following articles from Articles 39 to 51 to direct its policy towards securing some aims which, being well-known concepts of social democratic theory, are described as 'principles'.
RatioOfTheDecision	See for example the marginal note of Article 39.
RatioOfTheDecision	Compendiously these are described as Directive Principles of <ORG> Policy under the heading of Part IV.
RatioOfTheDecision	1380.
RatioOfTheDecision	We are concerned with Article 39(b) and (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	The <ORG> is commanded, in particular, to direct its policy towards securing two aims, one described in (b) and the other in (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	In directing its policy towards securing the aims, the <ORG> will evidently have to make laws.
RatioOfTheDecision	A description of such a law is given in the first part of Article 31C-as a law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles (aims) specified in Articles 39(b) or (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	If a law truly answe rs that description it will be secure against a challenge under Articles 14, 19 and 31; otherwise not.
RatioOfTheDecision	When such a challenge is made, it will be the obvious duty of the court to ascertain on an objective consideration of the law whether it falls within the description.
RatioOfTheDecision	What the court will have to consider is whether it is a law which can reasonably be described as a law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the aims of Article 39(b) or (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	That is an issue which is distinct from the other issue whether the law doesnot give effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the said aims.
RatioOfTheDecision	A law reasonably calculated to serve a particular aim or purpose may not actually serve that aim or purpose; and it is this latter issue which is excluded from judicial review.
RatioOfTheDecision	In doing so the declaration does no more than what the courts themselves have beenalways saying viz.
RatioOfTheDecision	that they are not concerned with the wisdom or policy of the legislation.
RatioOfTheDecision	Prohibition laws-for example in U.S.A. and elsewhere, th ough made in order to give effect to the policy of the <ORG> to secure the eradication of the evil of drink did not have that effect.
RatioOfTheDecision	That may have been so because the law was inadequate or because the law gave rise to problems which were unforeseen.
RatioOfTheDecision	But that did not impair the genuineness of the law as being reasonably calculated to achieve a certain result.
Precedent	The two questions are different.
Precedent	One involves the process of identification of the type of legislation by considering its scope and object, its pith and substance.
Precedent	The other involves a process of evaluation by considering its merits and defects, the adequacy or otherwise of the steps taken to implement it or their capability of producing the desired result.
Precedent	A law made to give effect to the <ORG>'s policy of securing eradication of the drink evil can be properly identified, as such, if such identification is necessary to bemade by a court in order to see the application of a Constitutional provision.
Precedent	But it is an entirely different proposition to say that  thelaw does not actually give effect to the <ORG>'s policy of securing the eradication of drink.
Precedent	That would require an enquiry which courts cannot venture to undertake owing to lack of adequate means of knowledge and sources of information.
Precedent	An enquiry, like that of a <ORG>, will lead to debatable questions as to the adequacy of the provisions of the law, its deficiencies, the sufficiency and efficiency of the executive side of the Government to implement it effectively, the problems that arise in the course of implementation of the law and the like, ail of which do not legitimately fall within the ambit of an enquiry by a court.
Precedent	The problems are problems of legislative policy.
Precedent	It is for the legislature to decide what should go into the law to give effect to its policy towards securing its purpose.
Precedent	The legislature will have to consider the divergent views in the matter and make its own choice as to how it can effectuate its policy.
Precedent	The courts are not concerned with that aspect of the matter and even if a  Jaw is considered a failure, courts cannot refuse to give effect to the same.
Precedent	The declaration does no more than forbid such an enquiry by the courts which the courts themselves would not have undertaken.
Precedent	The declaration is only by way of abundant caution.
Precedent	1381.
Precedent	No other ground is precluded from judicial review under Article 31C.
Precedent	It was rightly conceded on behalf of the <ORG> that the court in deciding whether the law falls within the general description given of it in Article 31C will be competent to examine the true nature and character of the legislation, its design and the primary matter dealt with, its object and scope.
Precedent	See : e.g.
Precedent	<NAME> v. The Queen  (VII) Appeal Cases 829 (838-840).
Precedent	If the court comes to the conclusion that the above object of the legislation was merely a pretence and the real object was discrimination or something other than the object specified in Article (b) and (c ), Article 31C would not be attracted and the validity of the Statute would have to be tested independently o f Article 31C.
Precedent	Similarly as observed in Attorney-General v. <ORG>  3 Appeal Cases 1090 "if the legislation ostensibly under one of the powers conferred by the Constitution is in truth and fact really to accomplish an unauthorised purpose the court would be entitled to tear the veil and decide according to the real nature of the statute.
Precedent	1382.
Precedent	In that view of the true nature of Article 31C it cannot be said that the amendment is invalid.
Precedent	1383.
Precedent	The twenty-fifth Amendment Act is, therefore, valid.
Precedent	1384.
Precedent	By the twenty-ninth Amendment, the two Kerala <ORG> challenged in this petition were included in the Ninth Schedule.
Precedent	Like other <ORG> included in that Schedule they are immune from challenge by reason of the protection given to the Schedule by Article 31B.
Precedent	It was sought to be argued that unless the <ORG> related to agrarian reform, implicit in the words 'Without prejudice to the generality of the provisions contained in Article 31A' with which Article 31B opens, the protection was not available.
Precedent	That  argument has been rejected previously.
Precedent	See for example <CITATION_1681028>  (1) S.C.R.
Precedent	636.
RatioOfTheDecision	Actually the argument does not amount to a challenge to the validity of the Amendment, hut an attempt to show that in spite of the Amendment, the two laws would not be saved by Article 316.
RatioOfTheDecision	The twenty-ninth Amendment is not different from several similar Amendments made previously by which Statutes were added from time to time to the ninth schedule and whose validity has been upheld by this <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	The twenty-ninth Amendment is, therefore, valid.
Precedent	1385.
Precedent	My conclusions are:  (1) The power and the procedure for the amendment of the Constitution were contained in the unamended Article 368.
Precedent	An Amendment of the Constitution in accordance with the procedure prescribed in that Article is not a 'law' within the meaning of Article 13.
Precedent	An Amendment of the Constitution abridging or taking away a fundamental right conferred by Part III of the Constitution is not void as contravening the provisions of Article 13(2).
Precedent	The majority dec ision in <CITATION_21266288> is with respect, not correct.
Precedent	(2) There were no implied or inherent limitations on the Amending power under the unamended Article 368 in its operation over the fundamental rights.
Precedent	There can be none after its amendment.
Precedent	(3) The twenty fourth, the twenty-fifth and the twenty-ninth Amendment Act s are valid.
Precedent	1386.
Precedent	The case will now be posted before the regular bench for disposal in accordance with law.
Precedent	<NAME>1387.
Precedent	Questions relating to the validity of the Constitution (Twentyfourth Amendment) Act, Constitution (Twentyfifth Amendment) Act and Constitution (Twehtyninth Amendment) Act, as well as the question whether the <ORG> acting under Article 368 of the Constitution can amend the provisions of Part III of the Constitution so as to take away or abridge fundamental rights arise for determination in this petition under Article 32 of the Constitution.
Precedent	A number of other important questions, to which reference would be made hereafter, have also been posed during discus sion, and they would be dealt with at the appropriate stage.
Precedent	Similar questions arise in a number of other petitions, and the counsel of the parties in those cases have been allowed to intervene.
Precedent	1388.
Precedent	The necessary facts may now be set out, while the details which have no material bearing for the purpose of this decision can be omitted.

Fact	The petitioner filed the present writ petition on March 21, 1970 challenging the Constitutional Validity of the Kerala Land Reforms Act,.

Fact	The aforesaid Act was also challenged in a number of petitions before <ORG>.

Precedent	1395.
Precedent	The Constitution (Twentyninth Amendment) Act, as mentioned earlier, inserted the following as entries No.
Precedent	65 and 66 respectively in the Ninth Schedule to the Constitution:  (i) The Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act, 1969 (Kerala Act 35 of 1969); and  (ii) The Kerala Land Reforms (Amerdment) Act, 1971 (Kerala Act 25 of 1971).
Precedent	1396.
Precedent	The question as to whether the fundamental rights contained in Part III of the Constitution could be taken away or abridged by amendment was first considered by this Court in the case of <CITATION_1706770> .
Precedent	S.C.R.
Precedent	89 In that case the appella nt challenged the First Amendment of the Constitution.
Fact	The First Amendment made changes in Articles 15 and 19 of the Constitution.
Fact	In addition, it provided for insertion of two Articles, 31A and 31B, in Part III.
Fact	Article 31A provided that no law providing for acquisition by the <ORG> of any estate or of any such rights therein or the extinguishment or modification of any such right, shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it was inconsistent with or took away or abridged any of the rights conferred by any provision in Part III.
Fact	The word "estate" was also defined for the purpose of Article 31A.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31B provided for validation of certain Acts and Regulations which were specified in the Ninth Schedule to the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The said Schedule was added for the first time in the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Ninth Schedule at that time contained 13 Acts, all relating to estates, passed by various Legislatures off the Provinces or <ORG>s.
RatioOfTheDecision	It was provided that those Acts and Regulations would not be deemed to be void o r ever to have become void on the ground that they were inconsistent with or took away or abridged any of the rights conferred by any provision of Part III.
RatioOfTheDecision	It further provided that notwithstanding any judgment, decree or order of any court or <ORG> to the contrary, all such Acts and Regulations, subject to the power of any competent <ORG> to repeal or amend them, would continue in force.
RatioOfTheDecision	1397.
RatioOfTheDecision	The attack on the validity of the First Amendment was based primarily on three grounds.
RatioOfTheDecision	Firstly, that amendments to the Constitution made under Article 368 were liable to be tested under Article 13(2); secondly, that in any case as Articles 31A and 31B inserted in the Constitution by the First Amendment affected the powers of <ORG> under Article 226 and of this <ORG> under Articles 132 and 136, the Amendment required ratification under the proviso to Article 368; and thirdly, that Articles 31A and 31B were invalid on the ground that they related to matters covered by the State List.
RatioOfTheDecision	This <ORG> reject ed all the three contentions.
RatioOfTheDecision	It held that although "law" would ordinarily include Constitutional law, there was a clear demarcation between ordinary law made in the exercise of legislative power and Constitutional law made in the exercise of constituent power.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the context of Article 13 , "law" must be taken to mean rules or regulations made in exercise of ordinary legislative power and not amendments to Constitution made in the exercise of constituent power.
Statute	Article 13(2 ), as such, was held not to affect amendments made under Article 368.
Fact	This <ORG> further held that Articles 31A and 31B did not curtail the power, of this <ORG> and of the High <ORG> and as such did not require ratification under the proviso contained in Article 368.
Fact	Finally, it was held that Articles 31A and 31B were essentially amendments to the Constitution and the <ORG> had the power to make such amendments.
Fact	In consequence, the First Amendment to the Constitution was held to be valid.
Precedent	1398.
Precedent	The second case in which there arose t he question of the power of the <ORG> to amend fundamental rights was <CITATION_1308308>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	933.
Precedent	In this case the Seventeenth Amendment made on June 29, 1964 was challenged.
Precedent	By the Seventeenth Amendment changes were made in Article 31A of the Constitution and 44 Acts were included in the Ninth Schedule to the Constitution to give them complete protection from attack under any provision of Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	One of the contentions advanced in <NAME> case was that, as Article 226 was likely to be affected by the Seventeenth Amendment, it required ratification under the proviso to Article 368 and that the decision in <NAME> case (supra) which had negatived such a contention required reconsideration.
Precedent	It was also urged that the Seventeenth Amendment was legislation with respect to land and the <ORG> had no right to legislate in that respect.
Precedent	It was further argued that as the Seventeenth Amendment provided that Acts put in the Ninth Schedule would be valid in spite of t he decision of the courts, it was unConstitutional.
Precedent	This <ORG> by a majority of 3 to 2 upheld the correctness of the decision in <NAME> case.
Precedent	This <ORG> further held unanimously that the Seventeenth Amendment did not require ratification under the proviso to Article 368.
Precedent	The <ORG>, it was held, in enacting the amendment was not legislating with respect to land and that it was open to <ORG> to validate legislation which had been declared invalid by courts.
Precedent	By a majority of 3 to 2 the <ORG> held that the power conferred by Article 368 included the power to take away fundamental rights guaranteed by Part III and that the power to amend was a very wide power which could not be controlled by the literal dictionary meaning of the word "amend".
Precedent	The word "law" in Article 13(2 ), it was held, did not include an amendment of the Constitution made in pursuance of Article 368.
Precedent	The minority, however, doubted the correctness of the view taken in <NAME> case to the effect that the word "law" in  Article 13(2) did not include amendment to the Constitution made under Article 368.
Precedent	1399.
Precedent	The correctness of the decision of this <ORG> in <NAME> case and of the majority in <NAME> case was questioned in the case of <CITATION_21266288> .
Precedent	2 S.C.R.
Precedent	762.
Precedent	The case was heard by a special bench consisting of 11 judges.
Precedent	This <ORG> in that case was concerned with the validity of the Punjab Security of Land Tenures Act, 1953 and of the Mysore Land Reforms Act.
Precedent	These two Acts had been included in the Ninth Schedule to the Constitution by the Constitution (Seventeenth Amendment) Act, 1964.
Precedent	It was held by <NAME> <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME>.
Precedent	<NAME> concurring) that fundamental rights cannot be abridged or taken away by the amending procedure in Ariticle 368 of the Constitution.
Precedent	An amendment of the Constitution, it was observed, is "law" within the meaning of Article 13(2) and is, therefore, subject to Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	<NAME> who gave the judgment on  his own behalf as well as on behalf of <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME> <NAME> gave his conclusions as under:  (1) The power of the <ORG> to amend the Constitution is derived from Articles 245, 246 and 248 of the Constitution and not from Article 368 thereof which only deals with procedure.
Precedent	Amendment is a legislative process.
Precedent	(2) Amendment is 'law' within the meaning of Article 13 of the Constitution and, therefore, if it takes away or abridges the rights conferred by Part III thereof, it is void.
Precedent	(3) The Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951 Constitution (Fourth Amendment) Act, 1955, and the Constitution (Seventeenth Amendment) Act, 1964, abridge the scope of the fundamental rights.
Precedent	But, on the basis of earlier decisions of this <ORG>, they were valid.
RulingByPresentCourt	(4) On the application of the doctrine of 'prospective over-ruling', as explained by us earlier, our decision will have only prospective operation and, therefore, the said amendments will continue to be valid.
RulingByPresentCourt	(5) We declare that the <ORG> wi ll have no power from the date of this decision to amend any of the provisions of Part III of the Constitution so as to take away or abridge the fundamental rights enshrined therein.
Precedent	(6) As the Constitution (Seventeenth Amendment) Act holds the field, the validity of the two impugned Acts, namely, the Punjab Security of Land Tenures Act X of 1953, and the Mysore Land-Reforms Act X of 1962, as amended by Act XIV of 1965, cannot be questioned on the ground that they offend Articles 13, 14 or 31 of the Constitution.
Precedent	<NAME> summed up his conclusions as under:  (i) that the Fundamental Rights are outside the amendatoryprocess if the amendment seeks to abridge or take away any of the rights;  (ii) that <NAME> case (and <NAME> which followed it) conceded the power of amendment over Part III of the Constitution on an erroneous view of Articles 13(2) and 368;  (iii) that the Fust, Fourth and Seventh Amendments being part of the Constitution by acquiescence for a long time, cannot now b e challenged and they contain authority for the Seventeenth Amendment  (iv) that this <ORG> having now laid down that Fundamental Rights cannot be abriged or taken away by the exercise of amendtory process in Article 368 , any further inroad into these rights as they exist today will be illegal and unConstitutional unless it complies with Part III in general and Article 13(2) in particular;  (v) that for abridging or taking away Fundamental Rights, a Constituent body will have to be convoked; and  (vi) that, the two impugned Acts, namely, the Punjab Security of Land Tenures Act, 1953 (X of 1953) and the Mysore Land Reforms Act, 1961 (X of 1962) as amended by Act XIV of 1965 are valid under the Constitution not because they are included in Schedule 9 of the Constitution but because they are protected by Article 31-A , and the President's assent.
Precedent	As against the view taken by the majority, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME>, JJ.
Precedent	gave dissenting judgments.
Precedent	According to them, Article 368 carried th e power to amend all parts of the Constitution including the fundamental rights in Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	An amendment, according to the five learned Judges, was not "law" for the purpose of Article 13(2) and could not be tested under that article.
Precedent	The learned Judges accordingly reaffirmed the correctness of the decision in the cases of <NAME> and <NAME>.
Precedent	Some of the conclusions arrived at by <NAME>, who gave the judgment on his own behalf as well as on behalf of <NAME> and Mitter JJ.
Precedent	may be reproduced as under:  (i) The Constitution provides a separate part headed 'Amendment of the Constitution' and Article 368 is the only article in that Part.
Precedent	There can therefore, be no doubt that the power to amend the Constitution must be contained in Article 368.
Precedent	(ii) There is no express limitation on power of amendment in Article 368 and no limitation can or should be implied therein.
Precedent	If the Constitution makers intended certain basic provisions in the Constitution, and Part III in particular , to be not amendable there is no reason why it was not so stated in Article 368.
Precedent	(iii) The power conferred by the words of Article 368 being unfettered, inconsistency between that power and the provision in Article 13(2) must be avoided Therefore in keeping with the unfettered power in Article 368 the word "law" in Article 13(2) must be read as meaning law passed under the ordinary legislative power and not a Constitutional amendment.
Precedent	(iv) Though the period for which <NAME> case has stood unchallenged is not long, the effects which have followed on the passing of <ORG> laws on the faith of that decision, are so overwhelming that the decision should not be disturbed, otherwise chaos will follow.
Precedent	This is the fittest possible case in which the principle of stare decisis should be applied.
Precedent	(v) The doctrine of prospective overruling cannot be accepted in this country.
Precedent	The doctrine accepted here is that courts declare law and that a declaration made by a court is the law of the land and takes effect  from the date the law came into force.
Precedent	It would be undesirable to give up that doctrine and supersede it with the doctrine of prospective overruling.
Precedent	The main conclusions of <NAME> were as under:  (i) Article 368 not only prescribes the procedure but also gives the power of amendment.
Precedent	(ii) The power to amend the Constitution cannot be said to reside in Article 248 and List I, item 97 because if amendment could be made by ordinary lagislative process Article 368 would be meaningless.
Precedent	(iii) The contention that a Constitutional amendment under Article 368 is a law within the meaning of Article 13 must be rejected.
Precedent	(iv) There is no conflict between Articles 13(2) and 368.
Precedent	The two articles operate in different fields, the former in the field of law, the latter in that of Constitutional amendment.
Precedent	(v) If the First, Fourth, Sixteenth & Seventeenth Amendment Acts are void they do not legally exist from their inception.
Precedent	They cannot be valid from 1951 to 1957 and invalid thereafter.
Precedent	To say that they were valid  in the past and will be invalid in the future is to amend the <ORG>.
Precedent	Such a naked power of amendment is not given to the Judges and therefore the doctrine of prospective overruling cannot be adopted.
Precedent	We may now set out some of the conclusions of <NAME> as under:  (i) In a written <ORG> the amendment of the <ORG> is a substantive constituent act which is made in the exercise of the sovereign power through a predesigned procedure unconnected with ordinary legislation.
Precedent	The amending power in Article 368 is hence sui generis and cannot be compared to the lawmaking power of <ORG> pursuant to Article 246 read with Lists I and III.
Precedent	It follows that the expression 'law' in Article 13(2) cannot be construed as including an amendment of the <ORG> which is achieved by <ORG> in exercise of its sovereign constituent power, but must mean law made by <ORG> in its legislative capacity under Article 246 read with List I and List III of the 7th Schedule.
Precedent	(ii) The language of Art icle 368 is perfectly general and empowers <ORG> to amend the Constitution without any exception whatsoever.
Precedent	The use of the word 'fundamental' to describe the rights in Part III and the word 'gurantecd' in Article 32 cannot lift the fundamental lights above the Constitution itself.
Precedent	(iii) There is no room for an implication in the construction of Article 368.
Precedent	If the Constitution makers wanted certain basic features to be unamendable they would have said so.
Precedent	(iv) It cannot be assumed that the Constitution makers intended to forge a political strait-jacket for generations to come.
Precedent	Today at a time when absolutes are discredited, it must not be too readily assumed that there are basic features of the Constitution which shackle the amending power and which take precedence over the general welfare of the nation and the need for agrarian and social reform.
Precedent	(v) If the fundamental rights are unamendable and if Article 368 does not include any such power it follows that the amendment of, say Article 31 by insert ions of Articles 31A and 31B can only be made by a voilent revolution.
Precedent	It is doubtful if the proceedings of a new <ORG> that may be called will have any legal validity for if the Constitution provides its own method of amendment, any other method will be unConstitutional and void.
Precedent	(vi) It was not necessary to express an opinion on the doctrine of prospective overruling of legislation.
Precedent	1400.
Precedent	Before dealing with Article 368 , we may observe that there are twotypes of Constitutions, viz., rigid and flexible.
Precedent	It is a frequently held but erroneous impression that this is the same as saying nondocumentary or documentary.
Precedent	Now, while it is true that a non-documentary Constitution cannot be other than flexible, it is quite possible for a documentary Constitution not to be rigid.
Precedent	What, then, is that makes a Constitution flexible or rigid?
Precedent	The whole ground of difference here is whether the process of Constitutional law-making is or is not identical with the process of ordinary law-making.
Precedent	The Constitut ion which can be altered or amended without any special machinery is a flexible Constitution.
Precedent	The Constitution which requires special procedure for its alteration or amendment is a rigid Constitution (see p. 66-68 of the Modern Political Constitutions by <NAME>).
Precedent	Lord Birkenhead L.C.
Precedent	adopted similar test in the Australian (Queensland) case of <NAME> v. The King  A.C. 763 though he used the nomenclature controlled and uncontrolled Constitutions in respect of rigid and flexible Constitutions.
Precedent	He observed in this connection:  The difference of view, which has been, the subject of careful analysis by writers upon the subject of Constitutional law, may be traced mainly to the spirit and genius of the nation in which a particular Constitution has its birth.
Precedent	Some communities, and notably Great Britain, have not in the framing of Constitutions felt it necessary, or thought it useful, to shackle the complete independence of their successOrs.
Precedent	They have shrunk from the assumption that a degree of wisdom and fores ight has been conceded to their generation which will be, or may be, wanting to their successors, in spite of the fact that those successors will possess more exprience of the circumstances and necessities amid which their lives are lived.
Precedent	Those Constitution framers who have adopted the other view must be supposed to have believed that certainty and stability were in such a matter the supreme desiderata.
Precedent	Giving effect to this belief, they have created obstacles of varying difficulty in the path of those who would lay rash hands upon the ark of the Constitution.
Precedent	1401.
Precedent	Let us now deal with Article 368 of the Constitution.
Precedent	As amendments in Articles 13 and 368 of the Constitution were made in purported exercise of the powers conferred by Article 368 in the form it existed before the amendment made by the Twentyfourth Amendment, we shall deal with the article as it was before that amendment.
Precedent	It may be mentioned in this context that Article 4 , Article 169 , Fifth Schedule Para 7 and Sixth Schedule Para 21 empower  the <ORG> to pass laws amending the provisions of the First, Fourth, Fifth and Sixth Schedules and making amendments of the Constitution consequential on the formation of new States or alteration of areas, boundaries, or names of existing States, as well as on abolition or creation of legislative councils in States.
Precedent	Fifth Schedule contains provisions as to administration of controlled areas and scheduled tribes while Sixth Schedule contains provisions as to the administration of tribal areas.
Precedent	It is further expressly provided that no such law would be deemed to be an amendment of the Constitution for the purpose of Article 368.
Precedent	There are a number of articles which provide that they would continue to apply till such time as a law is made in variance of them.
Precedent	Some of those articles are:  10, 53(3), 65(3), 73(2), 97, 98(3), 106, 120(2), 135, 137, 142(1), 146(2), 148(3), 149, 171(2), 186, 187(3), 189(3), 194(3), 195, 210(2), 221(2), 225, 229, 239(1), 241(3), 283(1) and (2), 285 (2), 287, 300(1), 313, 345 and   373.
Precedent	1402.
Precedent	The other provisions of the Constitution can be amended by recourse to Article 368 only.
Precedent	1403.
Precedent	Article 368 finds its place in Part XX of the Constitution and is the only article in that part.
Precedent	The part is headed "Amendment of the Constitution".
Precedent	It is not disputed that Article 368 provides for the procedure of amending the Constitution.
Precedent	Question, however, arises as to whether Article 368 also contains the power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	It may be stated in this connection that all the five Judges who gave the dissenting judgment in the case of Golaknath, namely, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME> JJ.
Precedent	expressed the view that Article 368 dealt with not only the procedure of amending the Constitution but also contained the power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	The argument that the power to amend the Constitution was contained in the residuary power of <ORG> in Article 248 read with item 97 of List I was rejected.
Precedent	<NAME> agreed with the view that amendment to the Constitutio n is not made under power derived from Article 248 read with entry 97 of List I.
Precedent	According to him, the power of amendment was sat generis.
Precedent	As against that, the view taken by <NAME> <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME> was that Article 368 merely prescribed the various steps in the matter of amendment of the Constitution and that power to amend the Constitution was derived from Articles 245, 246 and 248 read with item 97 of List I.
Precedent	It was said that the residuary power of <ORG> can certainly take in the power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	1404.
Precedent	Amendment of the Constitution, according to the provisions of Article 368 , is initialed by the introduction of a Bill in either <ORG> of <ORG> The Bill has to be passed in each <ORG> by a majority of total membership of that <ORG> and by a majority of not less than two-thirds members of the <ORG> present and voting.
Precedent	After it has been so passed, the Bill is to be presented to the President for his assent.
Precedent	When the President gives his assent to the Bill , the Constitution, according to Article 368 , shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill.
Precedent	There is a proviso added to Article 368 with respect to amendment of certain articles and other provisions of the Constitution including Article 368.
Precedent	Those provisions can be amended only if the Bill passed by the two <ORG> by necessary majority, as mentioned earlier, is ratified by the Legislatures of not less than one-half of the States by iresolutions to that effect.
Precedent	In such a case, the Bill has to be presented to the President for his assent only after the necessary ratification by the State Legislatures.
Precedent	On the assent being given, the Constitution stands amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill.
Precedent	1405.
Precedent	The words in Article 368 " the Constiuttion shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill", in my opinion, clearly indicate that the said article provides not merely the procedure for amending the Constitution but also contains the power to amend Article 368.
Precedent	The  fact that a separate Part was provided with the heading "Amendment of the Constitution" shows that the said part was confined not merely to the procedure for making the amendment but also contained the power to make the amendment.
Precedent	It is no doubt true that Article 248 read with item 97 of List I has a wide scope, but in spite of the width of its scope, it cannot, in my opinion, include the power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	The power to legislate contained in Articles 245, 246 or 248 is subject to the provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	If the argument were to be accepted that the power to amend the Constitution is contained in Article 248 read with item No.
Precedent	97 List I, it would be difficult to make amendment of the Constitution because the amendment would in most of the cases be inconsistent with the article proposed to be amended.
Precedent	The only amendments which would be permissible in such an event would be, ones like those contemplated by Articles 4 and 169 which expressly provide for a law being made for the purpose  in variance of specified provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	Such law has to be passed by ordinary legislative process.
Precedent	Article 368 would thus become more or less a dead letter.
Precedent	1406.
Precedent	Article 248 read with entry 97 List I contemplates legislative process.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the amendment of the Constitution were such a legislative process, the provision regarding ratification by the legislatures of not less than one-half of the States in respect of certain amendments of the Constitution would be meaningless because there is no question of ratification of a legislation made by <ORG> in exercise of the power conferred by Article 248 read with entry 97 List I.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is noteworthy that ratification is by means of resolutions by State <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	The passing of resolution can plainly be not considered to be a legislative process for making a law.
RatioOfTheDecision	The State Governors also do not come into the picture for the purpose of ratification.
RatioOfTheDecision	The State <ORG> in ratifying, it has been said, exercise a constituent function.
RatioOfTheDecision	Ratifying p rocess, according to <NAME>, is equivalent to roll call of the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	Ratification by a State of Constitutional amendment is not an act of legislation within the proper sense of the word.
Precedent	It is but the expression of the assent of the States to the proposed amendment (see The Amending of the Federal Constitution p. 62-63).
Precedent	1407.
Precedent	The fact that the marginal note of Article 368 contained the words "Procedure for Amendment of the Constitution" would not detract from the above conclusion as the marginal note cannot control the scope of the article itself.
Precedent	As mentioned earlier, the words in the article that "the Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill" indicate that the power to amend the Constitution is also contained in Article 368.
Precedent	The existence of such a power which can clearly be discerned in the scheme and language of Article 368 cannot be ruled out or denied by invoking the marginal note of the article.
Precedent	1408.
Precedent	The various subjects contained in entries in List I, List II an d List III of Seventh Schedule to the Constitution were enumerated and specified at great length.
Precedent	Our Constitution in this respect was not written on a tabula rasa.
Precedent	On the contrary, the scheme of distribution of legislative lists in the Government of India Act, 1935 was to a great extent adopted in the Constitution.
Precedent	Referring to the' said distribution of lists and the residuary provisions in the Government of India Act, <NAME> observed in the case In re.
Precedent	The Central Provinces and Berar Sales of Motor Spirit and Lubricants Taxation Act, 1938 [1939] F.C.R.
Precedent	38.
RatioOfTheDecision	The attempt to avoid a final assignment of residuary powers by an exhaustive enumeration of legislative subjects has made the Indian Constitution Act unique among federal Constitutions in the length and detail of its Legislative Lists.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution-makers made list of the legislative entries still more exhaustive and the intention obviously was that the subjects mentioned should be covered by one or other of the specific entries, so that as few  subjects as possible and which did not readily strike to the Constitution-makers should be covered by the residuary entry 97 in List 1.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution-makers, in my opinion, could not have failed to make an entry in the lists in the Seventh Schedule for amendment of the Constitution if they had wanted the amendment of Constitution to be dealt with as an ordinary legislative measure under Articles 245, 246 and 248 of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fact that they provided separate Part in the Constitution for amendment of the Constitution shows that they realised the importance of the subject of amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is difficult to hold that despite their awareness of the importance of Constitutional amendment, they left it to be dealt with under and spelt out of entry 97 List I which merely deals with "any other matter not enumerated in List II or List III including any tax not mentioned in either of those lists.
RatioOfTheDecision	1409.
RatioOfTheDecision	The residuary entry is essential in a federal Constitution and the sole object of the  residuary entry is to confer on the federal legislature or <ORG>, as the case may be, the power to make ordinary laws under and in accordance with the Constitution in respect of any matter, not enumerated in any other list for legislation.
RatioOfTheDecision	By the very nature of things the power to amend the Constitution cannot be in the residuary entry in a federal Constitution because the power to amend the Constitution would also include the power to alter the distribution of subjects mentioned in different entries.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such a power can obviously be not a legislative power.
RatioOfTheDecision	1410.
RatioOfTheDecision	It was originally intended that the residuary power of legislation should be vested in the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	This is clear from the Objective Resolution which was moved by Pt.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> in <ORG> before the partition of the country on December 13, 1946 (see <ORG> debates, Vol.
RatioOfTheDecision	I, p. 59).
RatioOfTheDecision	After the partition, the residuauy power of legislation was vested in the <ORG> and was taken out of the State List.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the inte ntion to vest residuary powers in <ORG> had been eventually carried out, no argument could possibly have been advanced that the power to amend the Constitution was possessed by the <ORG> and not by the Union.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fact that subsequently <ORG> vested the residuary power in <ORG> subject to ratification by State Legislatures in certain cases, would not go to show that the residuary clause included the power to amend the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	1411.
RatioOfTheDecision	I am therefore of the view that Article 368 prescribes not only the procedure for the amendment of the Constitution but also confers power of amending the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	1412.
RatioOfTheDecision	Irrespective of the source of power, the words in Article 368 that "the Constitution shall stand amended" indicate that the process of making amendment prescribed in Article 368 is a self-executing process.
RatioOfTheDecision	The article shows that once the procedure prescribed in that article has been complied with, the end product is the amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	1413.
RatioOfTheDecision	Question  then arises as to whether there is any power under Article 368 of amendment of Part III so as to take away or abridge fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	In this respect we find that Article 368 contains provisions relating to amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	No words are to be found in Article 368 as may indicate that a limitation was intended on the power of making amendment of Part III with a view to take away or abridge fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the contrary, the words used in Article 368 are that if the procedure prescribed by that article is complied with, the Constitution shall stand amended.
RatioOfTheDecision	The words "the Constitution shall stand amended" plainly cover the various articles of the Constitution, and I find it difficult in the face of those clear and unambiguous words to exclude from their operation the articles relating to fundamental rights in Part III of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is an elemental rule of construction that while dealing with a Constitution every word is to be expounded in its plain, obvious and commonsense unle ss the context furnishes some ground to control, qualify or enlarge it and there cannot be imposed upon the words any recondite meaning or any extraordinary gloss (see Story on Constitution of the United States, Vol.
RatioOfTheDecision	I, Para 451).
RatioOfTheDecision	It has not yet been erected into a legal maxim of Constitutional construction that words were meant to conceal thoughts.
RatioOfTheDecision	If framers of the Constitution had intended that provisions relating to fundamental rights in Part III be not amended, it is inconceivable that they would not have inserted a provision to that effect in Article 368 or elsewhere.
RatioOfTheDecision	I cannot persuade myself to believe that the framers of the Constitution deliberately used words which cloaked their real intention when it would have been so simple a matter to make the intention clear beyond any possibility of doubt.
RatioOfTheDecision	1414.
Precedent	In the case of The Queen v. <NAME> [1878] 3 A.C. 889 at p. 904-5 Lord <NAME> observed:  The established courts of justice, when a question arises whether the prescribed limits have been exceeded, mus t of necessity determine that question; and the only way in which they can properly do so, is by looking to the terms of the instrument by which, affirmatively, the legislative powers were created, and by which, negatively, they are restricted.
Precedent	If what has been done is legislation, within the general scope of the affirmative words which give the power, and if it violates no express condition or restriction by which that power is limited,...it is not for any court of justice to inquire further, or to enlarge constructively those conditions or restrictions.
Precedent	Although the above observations were made in the context of the legislative power, they have equal, if not greater, relevance in the context of the power of amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	:  1415.
Precedent	It also cannot be said that even though the framers of the Constitution intended that Part III of the Constitutilon relating to fundamental rights should not be amended, by inadvertent omission they failed to make an express provision for the purpose.
Fact	Reference to  the proceedings dated September 17, 1949 of <ORG> shows that an amendment to that effect was moved by Dr. <NAME>.
Fact	This amendment which related to insertion of Article 304A after Article 304 (which corresponded to present Article 368 ) was in the following words:  Notwithstanding anything contained in this Constitution to the contrary, no amendment which is calculated to infringe or restrict or diminish the scope of any individual rights, any rights of a person or persons with respect to property or otherwise, shall be permissible under this Constitution and any amendment which is or is likely to have such an effect shall be void and ultra vires of any <ORG>.
Fact	The above amendment, which was subsequently withdrawn, must have been incorporated in the Constitution if the framers of the Constitution had intended that no amendment of the Constitution should take away or abridge the fundamental rights in Part III of the Constitution.
Fact	1416.
Fact	Before the Constitution was framed, Mr. <NAME>  <NAME>, Constitutional Adviser, sent a questionnaire along with a covering letter on March 17, 1947 to the members of <ORG>.
Fact	Question 27 was to the effect as to what provision should be made regarding the amendment of the Constitution.
Fact	The attention of the members of <ORG> was invited in this context to the provisions for amendment in the British, Canadian, Australian, South African, US, Swiss and Irish Constitutions.
Fact	Some of those Constitutions placed limitations on the power of amendment and contained express provisions in respect of those limitations.
Fact	For instance, Article 5 of the United <ORG>s contained a proviso "that no amendment which may be made prior to the year one thousand eight hundred and eight shall in any manner affect the first and fourth clauses in the ninth section of the first article and that no <ORG>, without its consent, shall be deprived of its equal suffrage in the <ORG>".
Fact	It is inconceivable that, despite the awarenes s of the fact that in the Constitutions of other countries where restriction was sought to be placed on the power of amendment an express provision to that effect had been inserted, the framers of our Constitution would omit to insert such a provision in Article 368 or in some other article if, in fact, they wanted a limitation to be placed on the power of amendment in respect of articles relating to fundamental right.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the contrary, there is clear indication that <ORG> was conscious of the need of having an express provision regarding limitation on the power of amendment in case such a limitation was desired.
RatioOfTheDecision	This is clear from Article 305 of the Draft Constitution which immediately followed Article 304 corresponding to Article 368 of the Constitution as finally adopted.
Statute	Article 305 of the Draft Constitution, which was subsequently dropped, was in the following terms:  305.
Statute	Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 304 of this Constitution, the provisions of this Constitution relating  to the reservation of seats for the Muslims, the Scheduled Castes, the Scheduled Tribes or the Indian Christians either in <ORG> or in the <ORG> of any State for the time being specified in Part I of the First Schedule shall not be amended during a period of ten years from the commencement of this Constitution and shall cease to have effect on the expiration of that period unless continued in operation by an amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 305 of the Draft Constitution reproduced above makes it manifest that <ORG> made express provision for limitation on the power of, amendment in case such a limitation was desired.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fact that in the Constitution as ultimately adopted, there was no provision either in Article 368 or in any other article containing a limitation on the power of amendment shows that no such limitation was intended.
RatioOfTheDecision	1417.
RatioOfTheDecision	The speech of Dr. <NAME> made on September 17, 1949 while dealing with the provision relating to amendment of the Constitution also makes  it clear that he divided the various articles of the Constitution into three categories.
RatioOfTheDecision	In one category were placed certain articles which would be open to amendment by <ORG> by simple majority.
RatioOfTheDecision	To that category belonged Articles 2 and 3 of the Draft Constitution relating to the creation and reConstitution of the existing States as well as some other articles like those dealing with upper chambers of the State Legislatures.
RatioOfTheDecision	The second category of articles were those which could be amended by two-thirds majority of members present and voting in each House of <ORG>.
Fact	The third category dealt with articles which not only required two- thirds majority of each House of <ORG> but also the ratification of not less than half of the Legislatures of the States.
Fact	There was nothing in the speech of Dr. <NAME> that apart from the three categories of articles, there was a fourth category of articles contained in Part III which was not amendable and as such, could not be the subject of amendment.
Fact	1418.
Fact	It  may be mentioned that according to the report of the <ORG> debates, the speech of Dr. <NAME> delivered on September 17, 1949 contains the following sentence:  If the future <ORG> wishes to amend any particular article which is not mentioned in Part III or Article 304 , all that is necessary for them is to have two-thirds majority.
Fact	(Vol IX.
RatioOfTheDecision	P. 1661) The words "Part III" in the above sentence plainly have reference to the third category of articles mentioned in the proviso to draft Article 304 (present Article 368 ) which required two-thirds majority and ratification by at least half of the State Legislatures.
RatioOfTheDecision	These words do not refer to Part III of the Constitution, for if that were so the sentence reproduced above would appear incongruous in the context of the entire speech and strike a discordant note against the rest of the speech.
RatioOfTheDecision	Indeed, the entire tenor of the above speech, as also of the other speeches delivered by Dr. <NAME> in the <ORG>, was that all the articles  of the Constitution were subject to the amendatory process.
RatioOfTheDecision	1419.
RatioOfTheDecision	Another fact which is worthy of note is that the Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951 was passed by the Provisional <ORG> which had also acted as <ORG> for the drafting of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	By the First Amendment, certain fundamental eights contained in Article 19 were abridged and amended.
Fact	Speeches in support of the First Amendment were made by Pt.
Fact	<NAME> and Dr. <NAME>.
Fact	It was taken for granted that the <ORG> had by adhering to the procedure prescribed in Article 368 the right to amend the Constitution, including Part III relating to fundamental rights.
Fact	Dr. <NAME> who opposed the First Amendment expressly conceded that <ORG> had the power to make the aforesaid amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	If it had ever been the intention of the framers of the Constitution that the provisions relating to fundamental rights contained in Part III of the Constitution could not be amended, it is difficult to believe that Pt .
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> and Dr. <NAME> who played such an important role in the drafting of the Constitution would have supported the amendment of the Constitution or in any case would have failed to take note of the fact in their speeches that Part III was not intended to be amended so as to take away or abridge fundamental rights.
Precedent	Pt.
Precedent	<NAME> in the course of his speech in support of the First Amendment after referring to the need of making the Constitution adaptable to changing social and economic conditions and changing ideas observed:  It is of the utmost importance that people should realise that this great Constitution of ours, over which we laboured for so long, is not a final and rigid thing, which must either be accepted or broken.
Precedent	A Constitution which is responsive to the people's will which is responsive to their ideas, in that it can be varied here and there, they will respect it all the more and they will not fight against, when we want to change it.
Precedent	Otherwise, if you make them feel that it is unchangeable and  cannot be touched, the only thing to be done by those who wish to change it is to try to break it.
Precedent	That is a dangerous thing and a bad thing.
Precedent	Therefore, it is a desirable and a good thing for people to realise that this very fine Constitution that we have fashioned after years of labour is good in so far as it goes but as society changes, as conditions change we amend it in the proper way.
Precedent	It is not like the unalterable law of the Medes and the Persians that it cannot be changed, although the world around may change.
Precedent	1420.
Precedent	The First Amendment is contemporaneous practical exposition of the power of amendment under Article 368.
Precedent	Although as observed elsewhere, the provisions of Article 368 in my view are plain and unambiguous and contain no restrictions so far as amendment of Part III is concerned, even if it may be assumed that the matter is not free from doubt the First Amendment provides clear evidence of how the provisions of Article 368 were construed and what they were intended and assumed to convey by th ose who framed the Constitution and how they acted upon the basis of the said intention and assumption soon after the framing of the Constitution.
Precedent	The contemporaneous practical exposition furnishes considerable aid in resolving the said doubt and construing the provisions of the article.
Precedent	It would be pertinent to reproduce in this context the observations of Chief Justice <NAME> while speaking for <ORG> in the case of <NAME> v. <NAME> : 146 U.S. 1.
RatioOfTheDecision	The framers of the Constitution employed words in their natural sense; and where they are plain and clear, resort to collateral aids to interpretation is unnecessary and cannot be indulged in to narrow of enlarge the text; but where there is ambiguity or doubt, or where two views may well be entertained contemporaneous and subsequent practical construction are entitled to the greatest weight.
RatioOfTheDecision	Certainly, plaintiffs in error cannot reasonably assert that the clause of the Constitution under consideration soplainly sustains their p osition as to entitle them to object that contemporaneous history and practical construction are not to be allowed their legitimate force, and, conceding that their argument inspires a doubt sufficient to justify resort to the aids of interpretation thus afforded, we are of opinion that such doubt is thereby resolved against them, the contemporaneous practical exposition of the Constitution being too strong and obstinate to be shaken or controlled.
Precedent	I may also reproduce in this context the following passage from pages 49- 50 of Willoughby's Constitution of the United States, Vol.
Precedent	I:  In <ORG> v. <NAME> 111 U.S. 53 the court declared : The construction placed upon the Constitution by the first act of 1790 and the act of 1802 by the men who were contemporary with its formation, many of whom were members of the <ORG> who framed it, is of itself enttitled to very great weight, and when it is remembered that the rights thus established have not been disputed during a period of nearly a century, it  is almost conclusive.
Precedent	1421.
Precedent	So far as the question is concerned as to whether the speeches made in <ORG> can be taken into consideration, this <ORG> has in three cases, namely, <CITATION_21266288> .
Precedent	(supra), <CITATION_660275>  3 S.C.R.
Precedent	9 and <CITATION_1235907>  2 S.C.R.
Precedent	33 taken the view that such speeches can be taken into account.
Precedent	In <NAME> case <NAME> who spoke for the majority referred to the speeches of Pt.

Precedent	Reference was also made to the speech of Dr. <NAME> by <NAME> in that case on page 924.
Precedent	In the case of <NAME>, <NAME> who gave the leading majority judgment relied upon the speech of <NAME>, who was Minister for Home Affairs, in <ORG> (see page 83).
Precedent	Reference was also made to the speeches in <ORG> by <NAME> on pages 121 and 122.

Precedent	It can, therefore, be said that this Court has now accepted the view in its decisions since <NAME> case that speeches made in <ORG> can be referred to while dealing with the provision of the Constitution.
Precedent	1422.
RatioOfTheDecision	The speeches in <ORG>, in my opinion, can be referred to for finding the history of the Constitutional provision and the background against which the said provision was drafted.
RatioOfTheDecision	The speeches can also shed light to show as to what was the mischief which was sought to be remedied and what was the object which was sought to be attained in drafting the provision.
RatioOfTheDecision	The speeches cannot, however, form the basis for construing the provisions of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The task of interpreting the provision of the Constitution has to be done independently and the reference to the speeches made in <ORG> does not absolve the court from perfor ming that task.
RatioOfTheDecision	The draftsmen are supposed to have expressed their intentions in the words' used by them in the provisions.
RatioOfTheDecision	Those words are final repositories of the intention and it would be ultimately from the words of the provision that the intention of the draftsmen would have to be gathered.
RatioOfTheDecision	1423.
Fact	The next question which arises for consideration is whether the word "law" in Article 13(2) includes amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to Article 13(2 ), the <ORG> shall not make any law which takes away or abridges the rights conferred by this Part and any law made in contravention of this clause shall, to the extent of the contravention, be void.
RatioOfTheDecision	"<ORG>" has been defined in Article 12 to include, unless the context otherwise requires, the Government and <ORG> of India and the Government and the <ORG> of each of the <ORG>s and all local or other authorities within the territory of India or under the control of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	The stand taken on behalf of the petitioners is that amen dment of the Constitution constitutes "law" for the purpose of Article 13(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	As such, no amendment of the Constitution can take away or abridge the fundamental rights conferred by Part III of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Reference has also been made to Clause (1) of Article 13 , according to which all laws in force in the territory of India immediately before the commencement of this Constitution in so far as they are inconsistent with the provisions of this Part, shall, to the extent of such inconsistency, be void.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is urged that word "law" in Article 13(2) should have the same meaning as that word in Article 13(1) and if law in Article 13(1) includes Constitutional law, the same should be its meaning for the purpose of Article 13(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	Our attention has also been invited to Article 372(1) of the Constitution which provides that notwithstanding the repeal by this Constitution of the enactment referred to in Article 395 but subject to the other provisions of the Constitution, all the law in force in the territory of In dia immediately before the commencement of this Constitution shall continue in force therein until altered or repealed or amended by a competent <ORG> or other competent authority.
Precedent	According to Explanation I to Article 372 , the expression "law in force" shall include a law passed or made by a <ORG> or other competent authority in the territory of India before the commencement of this Constitution and not previously repealed notwithstanding that it or parts of it may not be then in operation either at all or in particular areas.
Precedent	The same is the definition of "law in force" in Article 13(3).
Precedent	1424.
RatioOfTheDecision	I find it difficult to accept the contention that an amendment of Constitution made in accordance with Article 368 constitutes law for the purpose of Article 13(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	The word "law" although referred to in a large number of other articles of the Constitution finds no mention in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to that article, the Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill after it has  been passed in compliance with the provisions of that article.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 thus contains an indication that what follows as a result of the compliance with Article 368 is an amendment of the Constitution and not law in the sense of being ordinary legislation.
RatioOfTheDecision	In a generic sense.
RatioOfTheDecision	"law" would include Constitutional laws, including amendment of the Constitution, but that does not seem to be the connotation of the word "law" as used in Article 13(2) of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is a clear distinction between statutory law made in exercise of the legislative power and Constitutional law which is made in exercise of the constituent power and the distinction should not be lost sight of A Constitution is the fundamental and basic law and provides the authority under which ordinary law is made.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution of West Germany, it may be stated, is called the basic law of the Federal Republic of Germany.
RatioOfTheDecision	A Constitution derives its authority generally from the people acting in their sovereign capacity and speaking throug h their representatives in <ORG> or <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It relates to the structure of the government, the extent and distribution of its powers and the modes and principles of its operation, preceding ordinary laws in the point of time and embracing the settled policy of the nation.
RatioOfTheDecision	A statute on the other hand is law made by the representatives of the people acting in their legislative capacity, subject to the superior authority, which is the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Statutes are enactments or rules for the government of civil conduct or for the administration or for the defence of the government.
RatioOfTheDecision	They relate to law and order, criminal offences, civil disputes, fiscal matters and other subjects on which it may become necessary to have law.
RatioOfTheDecision	Statutes are quite often tentative, occasional, and in the nature of temporary expedients (see Constitutional Law and Its Administration by S.P.
Precedent	Weaver, p. 3), Article 13(2) has reference to ordinary piece of legislation.
Precedent	It would also, in view of the definition given in Cla use (a) of Article 13(3 ), include any ordinance, order, bye-law, rule, regulation, notification, custom or usage having in the territory of India the force of law.
Precedent	The Constitution has thus made it clear in matters in which there could be some doubt as to what would constitute "law".
Precedent	If it had been the intention of the framers of the Constitution that the "law" in Article 13 would also include Constitutional law including laws relating to the amendment of Constitution, it is not explained as to why they did not expressly so state in Clause (a) of Article 13(3).
Precedent	The Constitution itself contains indications of the distinction between the Constitution and the laws framed under the Constitution.
Precedent	Article 60 provides for the oath or affirmation to be made and subscribed by the President before entering upon office.
Precedent	The language in which that oath and affirmation have been couched, though not crucial, has some bearing.
Precedent	The form of the oath or affirmation is as under:  swear in the name of God I, A.B., do __________ _______________ solemnly affirm that I will faithfully execute the office of President (or discharge the functions of the President) of India and will to the best of my ability preserve, protect and defend the Constitution and the law and that I will devote myself to the service and well-being of the people of India.
Precedent	The facts that both the words "the Constitution and the law" have been used in the above form tends to show that for the purpose of the Constitution the law and the Constitution are not the same.
Precedent	1425.
Precedent	It may be mentioned that Articles 56(1)(b) and 61(1) which deal with impeachment of the President refer only to "violation of the Constitution".
Precedent	There is no reference in those articles to violation of law.
Precedent	Article 69 which prescribes the oath for the Vice- President refers to "allegiance to the Constitution as by law established".
Precedent	The words "as by law established" indicate the legal origin of the Constitution.
Precedent	Article 143 , to which our attention has been invited, gives power to the President to r efer to <ORG> a question of law or fact of such importance that it is expedient to obtain the opinion of this Court.
Precedent	It is pointed out that question of law in that article would include a question relating to Constitutional law.
Precedent	This no doubt is so but this is due to the fact that words "questions of law or fact" constitute a well known phrase in legal terminology and have acquired a particular significance.
Precedent	From the use of those words in Article 143 it cannot be inferred that the framers of the Constitution did not make a distinction between the Constitution and the law.
Precedent	1426.
Precedent	Articles 245, 246 and 248 deal with the making of laws.
Precedent	The words "shall not make any law" in Article 13(2) seem to echo the words used in Articles 245, 246 and 248 of the Constitution which deal with the making of laws.
Precedent	The words "make any law" in Article 13 as well as the above three articles should carry, in my opinion, the same meaning, namely, law made in exercise of legislative power.
Precedent	In addition to that, the law in  Article 13 in view of the definition in Article 13(3) shall also include special provisions mentioned in Clause (3).
Precedent	1427.
Precedent	It has already been mentioned above that there is no question in the case of a law made by the <ORG> of its ratification by the resolutions passed by the State <ORG>.
Precedent	The fact that in case of some of the amendments made under Article 368 such ratification is necessary shows that an amendment of the Constitution is not law as contemplated by Article 13(2) or Articles 245, 246 and 248.
Precedent	1428.
Precedent	Article 395 of the Constitution repealed the Indian Independence Act, 1947 and the Government of India, Act, 1935, together with all enactments amending or supplementing the latter Act, but not including the Abolition of Privy Council Jurisdiction Act, 1949.
Precedent	The law in force mentioned in Article 372(1) has reference not to any Constitutional law in the sense of being a law relating to the Constitution of either the territory of erstwhile British India or the territory comprised in the Indi an States.
Precedent	So far as the territory of British India was concerned, the law before January 26, 1950 relating to the Constitution was contained in the Government of India Act, 1935 and the Indian Independence Act, 1947.
Precedent	Both these Acts were repealed by Article 395 when the Constitution of India came into force.
Precedent	As regards the territory comprised in Indian States, the law relating to their Constitutions in so far as it was inconsistent with the provisions of the Constitution of India also came to an end before January 26, 1950 when the said Constitution came into force.
Precedent	The only Constitution which was in force since that date was the Constitution of India and it applied to the whole of India, including the erstwhile Indian States and the British India.
Precedent	The various notifications which were issued before January 26, 1950 mentioned that with effect from that date "the Constitution of India shortly to be adopted by <ORG> shall be the Constitution for the States as for other parts of India  and shall be enforced as such" (see White Paper on Indian States, pages 365 to 371).
Precedent	It would thus appear that hardly any law containing the Constitutions of territory of erstwhile Indian States remained in force after the coming into force of the Constitution of India with all its exhaustive provisions.
Precedent	If the law in force contemplated by Article 372(1) must be such as was continued after January 26, 1950, it would follow that Article 372 does not relate to the Constitutional law in the sense of being law relating to the Constitution of a territory.
Precedent	1429.
Precedent	Although the law in force referred to in Article 372(1) would not include law relating to the Constitutions of the territory of erstwhile British India or the Indian States, it did include law relating to subjects dealt with by the Constitutions in force in those territories.
Precedent	Such a law which partakes of the nature of either a statutory law or an Order made under the organic provisions of those Constitutions, continued in force under Article 372(1).
Precedent	A stat utory law or Order is obviously of an inferior character and cannot have the same status as that of a Constitution.
Precedent	Article 372(1) in the very nature of things deals with laws made under the provisions of Constitutions which were in force either in the erstwhile British India or the territory comprised in Indian States.
Precedent	The opening words of Article 372(1) " notwithstanding the repeal by this Constitution of the enactments referred to in Article 395" indicate that the laws in force contemplated by Article 372 are those laws which were framed under the repealed Indian Independence Act, 1947 and the Government of India Act, 1935 or similar other legislative enactments or orders made under the provisions of Constitutions of erstwhile Indian States.
Precedent	Such legislative enactments or Orders were inferior in status to a Constitution.
Precedent	I am, therefore, of the view that the word "law" in Article 372 has reference to law made under a Constitution and not to the provisions of a Constitution itself.
Precedent	1430.
Precedent	Article 372(1) is  similar to the provisions of Section 292 of the Government of India Act, 1935.
Precedent	As observed by <NAME> in the case of <ORG> and Ors.
Precedent	2 F.C.R.
Precedent	110 such a provision is usually inserted by draftsmen to negative the possibility of any existing law being held to be no longer in force by reason of the repeal of the law which authorized its enactment.
Precedent	The question with which we are concerned is whether law in Article 13 or Article 372 could relate to the provisions of the Constitution or provisions relating to its amendment.
Precedent	So far as that question is concerned, I am of the opinion that the language of Articles 372 and 13 shows that the word "law" used therein did not relate to such provisions.
Precedent	The Constitution of India was plainly not a law in force at the time when the Constitution came into force.
Precedent	An amendment of the Constitution in the very nature of things can be made only after the Constitution comes into force.
