Precedent	As such, a law providing for amendment of the Constitution ca nnot constitute law in force for the purpose of Article 13(1) or Article 372(1).
Precedent	1431.
Precedent	The language of Article 13(2) shows that it was not intended to cover amendments of the Constitution made in accordance with Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is difficult to accede to the contention that even though the framers of the Constitution put no express limitations in Article 368 on the power to make amendment, they curtailed that power by implication under Article 13(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	In order to find the true scope of Article 13(2) in the context of its possible impact on the power of amendment, we should read it not in isolation but along with Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The rule of construction, to use the words of Lord <NAME> in <NAME> v. Commonwealth of Australia  A.C. 578 is to read the actual words used "not in vacuo but as occurring in a single complex instrument in which one part may throw light on another".
RatioOfTheDecision	A combined reading of Article 13(2) and Article 368 , in my view, clearly points to the conclusion that extinguishment or abridgement of f undamental rights contained in Part III of the Constitution is not beyond the amendatory power conferred by Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The alleged conflict between Article 13(2) and Article 368 is apparent and not real because the two provisions operate in different fields and deal with different objects.
RatioOfTheDecision	1432.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution itself treats the subject of ordinary legislation as something distinct and different from that of amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Articles 245 to 248 read with Seventh Schedule deal with ordinary legislation, while amendment of Constitution is the subject matter of Article 368 in a separate Part.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 is independent and self-contained.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 does not contain the words "subject to the provisions of this Constitution" as are to be found at the beginning of Article 245.
RatioOfTheDecision	The absence of those words in Article 368 thus shows that an amendment of the Constitution made under that article has a status higher than that of legislative law and the two are of unequal dignity.
RatioOfTheDecision	If there is any limita tion on power of amendment, it must be found in Article 368 itself which is the sole fountain-head of power to amend, and not in other provisions dealing with ordinary legislation.
RatioOfTheDecision	As stated on pages 24-26 in the Amending of Federal Constitution by <NAME>, 'limitation on the scope of amendment should be found written in the amending clause and the other articles of the Constitution should not be viewed as limitations'.
RatioOfTheDecision	The very fact that the power of amendment is put in a separate Part (Part XX) and has not been put in the Part and Chapter (Part XI Chapter I) dealing with legislative powers shows that the two powers are different in character and operate in separate fields.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is also a vital difference in the procedure for passing ordinary legislation and that for bringing about a Constitutional amendment under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fact that an amendment Bill is passed by each <ORG> of Parliament and those two <ORG>s also pass ordinary legislation does not obliterate the difference between the constituent po wer and the legislative power nor does it warrant the conclusion that constituent power is a species of legislative power.
RatioOfTheDecision	1433.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our attention has been invited on behalf of the petitioners to the proceedings of <ORG> on April 29, 1947.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> on that day made a move in <ORG> that Clause (2) be accepted.
Statute	Clause (2) which provided the basis for Clauses (1) and (2) of Article 13 as finally adopted was in the following words: All existing laws, notifications, regulations, customs or usages in force within the territories of the <ORG> inconsistent with the rights guaranteed under this part of the Constitution shall stand abrogated to the extent of such inconsistency, nor shall the <ORG> or any unit make any law taking away or abridging any such right.
Statute	Mr. <NAME> then moved an amendment for substituting the concluding words of Clause (2) by the following words:  Nor shall any such right be taken away or abridged except by an amendment of the Constitution.
Fact	The abo ve amendment was accepted by <NAME>.
Statute	Motion was thereafter adopted accepting the amended clause which was in the following words: All existing laws, notifications, regulations, customs or usages in force within the territories of the Union inconsistent with the rights guaranteed under that part of the Constitution shall stand abrogated to the extent of such inconsistency, nor shall any such right be taken away or abridged except by an amendment of the Constitution.
Statute	1434.
Statute	In October 1947 the Constitutional Adviser prepared the Draft Constitution, Sub- clause (2) of Clause 9 of which was as under:  (2) Nothing in this Constitution shall be taken to empower the <ORG> to make any law which curtails or takes away any of the rights conferred by Chapter II of this Part except by way of amendment of this Constitution under Section 232 and any law made in contravention of this sub-section shall, to the extent of the contravention, be void.
RatioOfTheDecision	Minutes of <ORG> of October 13, 1947 show that it was  decided to revise Clause 9.
Statute	Revised Clause 9 was put in the appendix as follows:  9.
Statute	(1) All laws in force immediately before the commencement of this Constitution in the territory of India, in so far as they are inconsistent with any of the provisions of this Part, shall, to the extent of such inconsistency, be void.
Statute	(2) The State shall not make law which takes away or abridges the rights conferred by this Part and any law made in contravention of this sub- section shall, to the extent of the contravention be void.
Statute	(3) In this section, the expression 'law' includes any ordinance, order, bye- law, rule, regulation, notification, custom or usage having the force of law in the territory of India or any part thereof.
Statute	On February 21, 1948 Dr. <NAME> forwarded the Draft Constitution of India to the President of <ORG> along with a covering letter.
Statute	Clause 9 in this Draft Constitution was numbered as Clause 8.
Statute	Sub-clause (2) of Clause 9 was retained as Sub- clause (2) of Clause 8.
Precedent	A proviso wa s also added to that sub-clause, but that is not material for the purpose of the present discussion.
Precedent	The Constitution was thereafter finally adopted and it contained Article 13 , the provisions of which have been reproduced earlier.
Precedent	1434.
Precedent	It has been argued on behalf of the petitioners that the members of <ORG> who were eminent lawyers of India, deliberately revised Clause 9 of the Draft Constitution prepared by the Constitutional Adviser with a view to undo the effect of the amendment moved by Mr. <NAME> which had been accepted by <ORG>, because the members of <ORG> wanted that the fundamental rights should not be abridged or taken away by the amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	1435.
RatioOfTheDecision	I find it difficult to accept the above argument.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is inconceivable that the members of <ORG> would reverse the decision which had been taken by <ORG> when it accepted the amendment moved by Mr. <NAME> and adopted the motion for the pas <NAME> of clause containing that amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	It would appear from the speech of Mr. <NAME> that he had moved the amendment in order to remove doubt.
RatioOfTheDecision	Although there is nothing in the minutes to show as to why the members of <ORG> did not specifically incorporate Mr. <NAME>'s amendment in the revised clause, it seems that they did so because they took the view that it was unnecessary.
RatioOfTheDecision	In his letter dated February 21, 1948 Dr. <NAME>, Chairman of <ORG> wrote to the President of <ORG>; In preparing the Draft <ORG> was of course expected to follow the decisions taken by <ORG> or by the various Committees appointed by <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	This <ORG> has endeavoured to do as far as possible.
RatioOfTheDecision	There were however some matters in respect of which <ORG> felt it necessary to suggest certain changes.
RatioOfTheDecision	All such changes have been indicated in the draft by underlining or side-lining the relevant  portions.
RatioOfTheDecision	Care has also been taken by <ORG> to insert a footnote explaining the reason for every such change.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is, therefore, plain that if it had been decided to make a material change in the draft article with a view to depart from the decision of <ORG>, the change would have been indicated by underlining or sidelining the relevant provision and also by inserting a footnote explaining reasons for the change.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the absence of any underlining, sidelining or footnote, it can be presumed that members of <ORG> did not intend to make a change.
RatioOfTheDecision	A very material fact which should not be lost sight of in this context is the note which was put in October 1948 under the draft Article 8.
RatioOfTheDecision	It was stated in the Note:  Clause (2) of Article 8 does not override the provisions of Article 304 of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The expression "law" used in the said clause is intended to mean "ordinary legislation".
RatioOfTheDecision	However, to remove any possible doubt, the following amendment may be m ade in Article 8:   In the proviso to Clause (2) of Article 8 , after the words "nothing in this clause shall" the words "affect the provisions of Article 304 of this Constitution or be inserted."
Precedent	(see page 26 <NAME> "The Framing of India's Constitution" Vol.
Precedent	IV).
Precedent	The above note and other such notes were made by the Constitutional Adviser and reproduced fully the views of <ORG> and/or of <ORG> (see page 4 <NAME> "The Framing of India's Constitution" Vol.
RatioOfTheDecision	I).
RatioOfTheDecision	It would thus appear that there is no indication that the members of <ORG> wanted to deviate from the decision of <ORG> by making the provisions relating to fundamental rights unamendable.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the contrary, the note shows that they accepted the view embodied in the decision of <ORG>.
Precedent	1436.
Precedent	Apart from that I am of the view that if the preservation of the fundamental rights was so vital an important a desideratum, it would seem logical that a proviso would have  been added in Article 368 expressly guaranteeing the continued existence of fundamental rights in an unabridged form.
Precedent	This was, however, not done.
Precedent	1437.
Precedent	The next question which should now engage our attention is about the necessity of amending the Constitution and the reasons which weighed with the framers of the Constitution for making provision for amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	A Constitution provides the broad outlines of the administration of a country and concerns itself with the problems of the Government.
Precedent	This is so whether the Government originates in a forcible seizure of power or comes into being as the result of a legal transfer of power.
Precedent	At the time of the framing of the Constitution many views including those emanating from conflicting extremes are presented.
Precedent	In most cases the Constitution is the result of a compromise between conflicting views.
Precedent	Those who frame a Constitution cannot be oblivious of the fact that in the working of a Constitution many difficulties would have to be encountered an d that it is beyond the wisdom of one generation to hit upon a permanently workable solution for all problems which may be faced by the <ORG> in its onward march towards further progress.
Precedent	Sometimes a judicial interpretation may make a Constitution broad-based and put life into the dry bones of a Constitution so as to make it a vehicle of a nation's progress.
Precedent	Occasions may also arise where judicial interpretation might rob some provision of a Constitution of a part of its efficacy as was contemplated by the framers of the Constitution.
Precedent	If no provision were made for the amendment of the Constitution, the people would be left with no remedy or means for adapting it to the changing need of times and would per force have recourse to extra-Constitutional methods of changing the Constitution.
Precedent	The extra-Constitutional methods may sometimes be bloodless but more often they extract a heavy toll of the lives of the citizen and leave a trail of smouldering bitterness.
Precedent	A <ORG> without the means of some change, as was said  by <NAME> in his Reflections on Revolution, is without the means of its conservation.
Precedent	Without such means it might even risk the loss of that part of the Constitution which it wished the most religiously to preserve.
Precedent	According to <NAME>, twelve unchangeable Constitutions of France have each lasted on an average for less than ten years, and have frequently perished by violence.
Precedent	<NAME> monarchy was destroyed within seven years of the time when <NAME> pointed out that no power existed legally capable of altering the articles of the Charter.
Precedent	On one notorious instance at least-and other examples of the same phenomenon might be produced from the annals of revolutionary France-the immutability of the Constitution was the ground or excuse for its voilent subversion.
Precedent	To quote the words of <NAME>:  Nor ought the perils in which France was involved by the immutability with which the statement of 1848 invested the Constitution to be looked upon as exceptional; they arose from a defect which is inherent in eve ry rigid Constitution.
Precedent	The endeavour to create laws which cannot be changed is an attempt to hamper the exercise of sovereign power; it therefore tends to bring the letter of the law into conflict with the will of the really supreme power in the <ORG>.
Precedent	The majority of the French electors were under the Constitution the true sovereign of France; but the rule which prevented the legal re-election of the President in effect brought the law of the land into conflict with the will of the majority of the electors, and produced, therefore, as a rigid Constitution has a natural tendency to produce, an opposition between the letter of the law and the wishes of the sovereign.
Precedent	If the inflexibility of French Constitutions has provoked revolution, the flexibility of English Constitutions has, once at least, saved them from violent overthrow.
Precedent	The above observations were amplified by <NAME> in the following words: To a student, who at this distance of time calmly studies the history of the first Reform Bill, it is apparent,  that in 1832 the supreme legislative authority of <ORG> enabled the nation to carry through a political revolution under the guise of a legal reform.
Precedent	The rigidity in short, of a Constitution tends to check gradual innovation; but, just because it impedes change, may, under unfavourable circumstances occasion or provoke revolution.
Precedent	According to <NAME>, the amending clause is so fundamental to a Constitution that it may be called the Constitution itself (see The Theory and Practice of Modern Government, p. 156-157).
Precedent	The amending clause, it has been said, is the most important part of a Constitution.
Precedent	Upon its existence and truthfulness, i.e.
Precedent	its correspondence with real and natural conditions, depends the question as to whether the state shall develop with peaceable continuity or shall suffer alterations of stagnation, retrogression, and revolution.
Precedent	A Constitution, which may be imperfect and erroneous in its other parts, can be easily supplemented and corrected, if only the state be truthfully organized i n the Constitution; but if this be not accomplished, error will accumulate until nothing short of revolution can save the life of the state (see Political Science and Comparative Constitutional Law, Vol.
Precedent	I by <NAME>, p. 137).
Precedent	<NAME> further expressed himself in the following words:  It is equally true that development is as much a law of state life as existence.
Precedent	Prohibit the former, and the latter is the existence of the body after the spirit has departed.
Precedent	When, in a democratic political society, the well-matured, long and deliberately formed will of the undoubted majority can be persistently and successfully thwarted, in the amendment of its organic law, by the will of the minority, there is just as much danger to the state from revolution and voilence as there is from the caprice of the majority, where the sovereignty of the bare majority is acknowledged.
Precedent	The safeguards against too radical change must not be exaggerated to the point of dethroning the real sovereign.
Precedent	(ibid p. 152) Justfying the amendment o f the Constitution to meet the present conditions, relations and requirements, <NAME> said we must not, as <NAME> finely expressed it, lose the grande morale in the petite morale.
Precedent	1438.
Precedent	According to <NAME>, no Constitution can expect to be permanent unless it guarantees progress as well as order.
Precedent	Human societies grow and develop with the lapse of time, and unless provision is made for such Constitutional readjustments as their internal development requires, they must stagnate or retrogress (see Political Science and Government by <NAME> p. 536, 537).
Precedent	1439.
Precedent	<NAME> in his book on the Constitutional Law of the United States has dealt with the question of amendment of the Constitution in the following words: Why should change and growth in Constitutional law stop with the present?
Precedent	We have always had change and growth, We have needed change and growth in the past because there have been changes and growth in our economic and social life.
Precedent	There will probably continue to be changes in our economi c and social life and there should be changes in our Constitutional law in the future to meet such changes just as much as there was need of change in the past.
Precedent	The Fathers in <ORG> expected changes in the future : otherwise they would not have provided for amendment.
Precedent	They wanted permanency or our Constitution and there was no other way to obtain it.
Precedent	The people of 1789 had no more sovereign authority than do the people of the present.
Precedent	1440.
Precedent	Pleading for provision for amendment of a Constitution and at the same time uttering a note of caution against a too easy method of amendment, <NAME> wrote: If no provision for amendment were provided, there would be a constant danger of revolution.
Precedent	If the method of amendment were made too easy, there would be the danger of too hasty action all of the time.
Precedent	In either case there would be a danger of the overthrow of our political institutions.
Precedent	Hence the purpose of providing for amendment of the Constitution is to make it possible gradually to change  the Constitution in an orderly fashion as the changes in social conditions make it necessary to change the fundamental law to correspond with such social change.
Precedent	1441.
Precedent	We may also recall in this connection the words of <NAME> in his tribute to Justice <NAME> and the latter's approach to the provision of the US Constitution.
Precedent	Said <NAME>:  The American Constitution was not made to compel the twentieth-century American to move in the swaddling clothes of his ancestors' ideas.
Precedent	The American Constitution must be moulded by reason to fit new needs and new necessities....
Precedent	The law must recognize change and growth even where the lawyer dislikes their implications.
Precedent	He may be skeptical of their implications; he has not the right to substitute his own pattern of Utopia for what they seek to accomplish.
Precedent	1442.
Precedent	According to <NAME>, flexibility is regarded as a merit and rigidity a defect because it is impossible for the framers of a Constitution to foresee the conditions in which it would apply and the problems  which will arise.
Precedent	They have not the gift of prophecy.
Precedent	A Constitution has to work not only in the environments it was drafted, but also centuries later (see Some Characteristics of Indian Constitution, p. 14-15).
Precedent	It has consequently been observed by <NAME>:  The real difficulty is that the problems of life and society are infinitely variable.
Precedent	A draftsman thinks of the problems that he can foresee, but he sees through a glass, darkly.
Precedent	He cannot know what problems will arise in ten, twenty, fifty or a hundred years.
Precedent	Any restriction on legislative power may do harm, because the effect of that restriction in new conditions cannot be foreseen.
Precedent	1443.
Precedent	The machinery of amendment, it has been said, should be like a safety valve, so devised as neither to operate the machine with too great facility nor to require, in order to set it in motion, an accumulation of force sufficient to explode it.
Precedent	In arranging it, due consideration should be given on the one hand to the requisities of growth and on the other hand to thos e of conservatism.
Precedent	The letter of the Constitution must neither be idolized as a sacred instrument with that mistaken conservatism which ding to its own worn out garments until the body is ready to perish from cold, nor yet ought it to be made a plaything of politicians, to be tampered with and degraded to the level of an ordinary statute (see Political Science and Government by J.W.
Precedent	Garner, p. 538).
Precedent	1444.
Precedent	The framers of our Constitution were conscious of the desirability of reconciling the urge for change with the need of continuity.
Precedent	They were not oblivious of the phenomenon writ large in human history that change without continuity can be anarchy; change with continuity can mean progress; and continuity without change can mean no progress.
Precedent	The Constitution-makers have, therefore, kept the balance between the danger of having a non-amendable Constitution and a Constitution which is too easily amendable.
Precedent	It has accordingly been provided that except for some not very vital amendments which can be brought about  by simple majority, other amendments can be secured only if they are passed in <ORG> by a majority of the total membership of that <ORG> and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members of each <ORG> present and voting.
Precedent	Provision is further made that in respect of certain matters which affect the interest of the States the amendment must also be ratified by the legislatures of not less than one half of the States by resolution to that effect.
Precedent	It can, therefore, be said that while a provision has been made for amendment of the Constitution, the procedure for the bringing about of amendment is not so easy as may make it a plaything of politicians to be tampered with and degraded to the level of ordinary statute.
Precedent	The fact that during the first two decades after the coming into force of the Constitution the amending Bills have been passed wihout much difficulty with requisite majority is a sheer accident of history and is due to the fact that one party has happened to be in absolute  majority at the <ORG> and many of the States.
Precedent	This circumstance cannot obliterate the fact that in normal circumstances when there are well balanced parties in power and in opposition the method of amending the Constitution is not so easy.
Precedent	1445.
Precedent	Another circumstance which must not be lost sight of is that no generation has monopoly of wisdom nor has any generation a right to place fetters on future generations to mould the machinery of government and the laws according to their requirements.
Precedent	Although guidelines for the organization and functioning of the future government may be laid down and although norms may also be prescribed for the legislative activity, neither the guidelines should be so rigid nor the norms so inflexible and unalterable as should render them to be incapable of change, alteration and replacement even though the future generations want to change, alter or replace them.
Precedent	The guidelines and norms would in such an event be looked upon as fetters and shackles upon the free exercise of the  sovereign will of the people in times to come and would be done away with by methods other than Constitutional.
Precedent	It would be nothing short of a presumptous and vain act and a myopic obsession with its own wisdom for one generation to distrust the wisdom and good sense of the future generation and to treat them in a way as if the generations to come would not be sui juris.
Precedent	The grant of power of amendment is based upon the assumption that as in other human affairs, so in Constitutions, there are no absolutes and that the human mind can never reconcile itself to fetters in its quest for a better order of things.
Precedent	Any fetter resulting from the concept of absolute and ultimate inevitably gives birth to the urge to revolt.
Precedent	<NAME> once said : "Why is there sometimes a right to revolution?
Precedent	Why is there sometimes a duty to loyalty?
Precedent	Because the whole transcendal philosophy, if made ultimate, is false, and nothing but a selfish perspective hypostasized, because the will is absolute neither in the individual nor in the  humanity..." (see German Philosophy and Politics (1915) 645-649 quoted by <NAME> in "Mr. Justice Holmes" 931 Ed.
Precedent	page 117).
Precedent	What is true of transcendal philosophy is equally true in the mundane sphere of a Constitutional provision.
Precedent	An unamendable Constitution, according to <NAME>, is the worst tyranny of time, or rather the very tyranny of time.
Precedent	It makes an earthly providence of a convention which was adjourned without day.
Precedent	It places the sceptre over a free people in the hands of dead men, and the only office left to the people is to build thrones out of the stones of their sepulchres (see Political Science and Government by J.W.
Precedent	Garner pages 537, 538).
Precedent	1446.
Precedent	According to <NAME>, political liberty is the right of those who are governed to adjust government to their own needs and interest.
Precedent	<NAME> in this context quoted <NAME> who had said that every generation sets before itself some favourite object which it pursues as the very substance of liberty and happiness.
Precedent	The ideals of liberty  cannot be fixed from generation to generation; only its conception can be, the large image of what it is.
Precedent	Liberty fixed in unalterable law would be no liberty at all.
Precedent	Government is a part of life, and, with life, it must change, alike in its objects and in its practices; only this principle must remain unaltered, this principle of liberty, that there must be the freest right and opportunity of adjustment.
Precedent	Political liberty consists in the best practicable adjustment between the power of the government and the privilege of the individual; and the freedom to alter the adjustment is as important as the adjustment itself for the ease and progress of affairs and the contentment of the citizen (see Constitutional Government in the United States by <NAME>, p. 4-6).
Precedent	1447.
Precedent	Each generation, according to <NAME>, should be considered as a distinct nation, with a right by the will of the majority to bind themselves but none to bind the succeeding generations, more than the inhabitant of another country.
Precedent	The e arth belongs in usufruct to the living, the dead have neither the power nor the right over it.
Precedent	<NAME> even pleaded for revision or opportunity for revision of Constitution every nineteen years.
Precedent	Said the great American statesman:  The idea that institutions established for the use of the nation cannot be touched or modified, even to make them answer their and, because of rights gratuitously supposed in those employed to manage them in the trust for the public, may perhaps be a salutary provision against the abuses of a monarch, but is most absurd against the nation itself.
Precedent	Yet our lawyers and priests generally inculcate this doctrine and suppose that preceding generations held the earth more freely than we do, had a right to impose laws on us, unalterable by ourselves, and that we, in the like manner, can make laws and impose burdens on future generations, which they will have no right to alter; in fine that the earth belongs to the dead and not the living.
Precedent	The above words were quoted during the course of  the debate in <ORG> (see Vol.
Precedent	XI <ORG> debates, p. 975) 1448.
Precedent	<NAME> gave expression to the same view in the following words: There never did, there never will, and there never can, exist a parliament, or any description of men, or any generation of men, in any country, possessed of the right or the power of binding and controlling posterity to the 'end of time', or of commanding for ever how the world shall be governed, or who shall govern it; and therefore all such clauses, acts or declarations by which the makers of them attempt to do what they have neither the right not the power to do, nor take power to execute, are in themselves null and void.
Precedent	Every age and generation must be as free to act for itself in all cases as the ages and generations which preceded it The vanity and presumption of governing beyond the grave is the most ridiculous and insolent of all tyrannies.
Precedent	Man has no property in man; neither has any generation a property in the generations which are to  follow.
Precedent	We may also reproduce the words of Pt.
Precedent	<NAME> in His speech to <ORG> on November 11, 1948:  And remember this that while we want this Constitution to be as solid and as permanent a structure as we can make it, nevertheless there is no permanence in Constitutions.
Precedent	Their should be a certain flexibility.
Precedent	If you make anything rigid and permanent you stop a Nation's growth, the growth of living vital organic people.
Precedent	Therefore it has to be flexible.
Precedent	1449.
Precedent	If it is not permissible under Article 368 to so amend the Constitution as to take away or abridge the fundamental rights in Part III, as has been argued on behalf of the petitioners, the conclusion would follow that the only way to take away or abridge fundamental rights, even if the overwhelming majority of people, e.g.
Precedent	90 per cent of them want such an amendment, is by resort to extra-Constitutional methods like revolution.
Precedent	Although, in my opinion, the language of Article 368 is clear and, contains no limitation on the power to make a mendment so as to take away or abridge fundamental rights, even if two interpretations were possible, one according to which the abridgement or extinguishment of fundamental rights is permissible in accordance with the procedure prescribed by Article 368 and the other according to which the only way of bringing about such a result is an extra-Constitutional method like revolution, the court, in my opinion, should lean in favour of the first interpretation.
Precedent	It hardly needs much argument to show that between peaceful amendment through means provided by the Constitution and the extra-Constitutional method with all its dangerous potentialities the former method is to be preferred.
Precedent	The contrast between the two methods is so glaring that there can hardly be any difficulty in making our choice between the two alternatives.
Precedent	1450.
Precedent	The aforesaid discussion would also reveal that the consequences which would follow from the acceptance of the view that there is no power under Article 368 to abridge or take away fundament al rights would be chaotic because of the resort to extra- Constitutional methods.
Precedent	As against that the acceptance of the opposite view would not result in such consequences.
Precedent	Judged even in this light, I find it difficult to accede to the contention advanced on behalf of the petitioner.
Precedent	1451.
Precedent	I may at this stage deal with the question, adverted to by the learned Counsel for the petitioners as to how far the consequences have to be taken into account in construing the provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	In this connection, I may observe that it is one of the well-settled rules of construction that if the words of a statute are in themselves precise and unambiguous, no more is necessary than to expound those words in their natural and ordinary sense, the words themselves in such case best declaring the intention of the legislature.
Precedent	It is equally well-settled that where alternative constructions are equally open that alternative is to be chosen which will be consistent with the smooth working of the system which the  statute purports to be regulating; and that alternative is to be rejected which will introduce uncertainty, friction, or confusion into the working of the system (see <CITATION_1560414> .
Precedent	1 S.C.R.
Precedent	896 (on p. 899)).
Precedent	These principles of construction apply with greater force when we are dealing with the provisions of a Constitution.
Precedent	1452.
Precedent	I have kept the above principles in view and am of the opinion that as the language of Article 368 is plain and unambiguous, it is not possible to read therein a limitation on the power of <ORG> to amend the provisions of Part III of the Constitution so as to abridge or take away fundamental rights; Apart from that, I am of the view that if two constructions were possible, the construction which I have, accepted would, as mentioned above, avoid chaotic consequences and would also prevent the introduction of uncertainty, friction or confusion into the working of our Constitution.
Precedent	1453.
Precedent	It is also, in my opinion, not permissible in the face of the plain language of Article  368 to ascertain by any process akin to speculation the supposed intention of the Constitution-makers.
Precedent	We must act on the principle that if the word's are plain and free from any ambiguity the Constitution-makers should be taken to have incorporated their intention in those words.
Precedent	1454.
Precedent	It seems inconceivable that the framers of the Constitution in spite of the precedents of the earlier French Constitutions which perished in violence because of their non-amendability, inserted in the Constitution a Part dealing with fundamental rights which even by the unanmious vote of the people could not be abridged or taken away and which left with people no choice except extra-Constitutional methods to achieve that object.
Precedent	The mechanics of the amendment of the Constitution, including those relating to extinguishment or abridgement of fundamental rights, in my opinion, are contained in the Constitution itself and it is not necessary to have recourse to a revolution or other extra- Constitutional methods to achieve that  object.
Precedent	1455.
Precedent	Confronted with the situation that if the stand of the petitioners was to be accepted about the inability of the <ORG> to amend Part III of the Constitution except by means of a revolution or other extra-Constitutional methods, the learned Counsel for the petitioners has argued that such an amendment is possible by making law for convening <ORG> or for holding a referendum.
Precedent	It is urged that there would be an element of participation of the people in the conventing of such <ORG> or the holding of a referendum and it is through such means that Part III of the Constitution can be amended so as to take away or abridge fundamental rights.
Precedent	The above argument, in my opinion, is untenable and fallacious.
Precedent	If <ORG> by a two-thirds majority in each <ORG> and by following the procedure laid down in Article 368 cannot amend Part III of the Constitution so as to take away or abridge fundamental rights, it is not understood as to how the same <ORG> can by law c reate a body which can make the requisite amendment.
Precedent	If it is not within the power of <ORG> to take away or abridge fundamental rights even by a vote of two-thirds majority in each <ORG>, would it be permissible for the same <ORG> to enact legislation under entry 97 List I of Seventh Schedule by simple majority for creating <ORG> in order to take away or abridge fundamental rights ?
Precedent	Would not such <ORG> be a creature of statute made by parliament even though such a body has the high-sounding name of <ORG> ?
RatioOfTheDecision	The nomenclature of the said <ORG> cannot conceal its real nature as being one created under a statute made by the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	A body created by the <ORG> cannot have powers greater than those vested in the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is not possible to accept the contention that what the <ORG> itself could not legally do, it could get done through a body created by it.
RatioOfTheDecision	If something is impermissible, it would continue to be so even though two steps  are taken instead of one for bringing about the result which is not permitted.
RatioOfTheDecision	Apart from the above if we were to hold that the <ORG> was entitled under entry 97 List I to make a law for convening <ORG> for taking away or abridging fundamental rights, some startling results are bound to follow.
Precedent	A law made under entry 97 List I would need a simple majority in each <ORG> of the <ORG> for being brought on statute book, while an amendment of the Constitution would require a two-thirds majority of the members of each <ORG> present and voting.
Precedent	It would certainly be anamolous that what <ORG> could not do by two-thirds majority, it can bring about by simple majority.
Precedent	This apart, there are many articles of the Constitution, for the amendment of which ratification by not less than half of the State <ORG> is required.
Precedent	The provision regarding ratification in such an event would be set at naught.
Precedent	There would be also nothing to prevent <ORG> while making a law for convening a Co <ORG> to exclude effective representation or voice of <ORG> <ORG> in the covening of Co<ORG>.
Precedent	1456.
Precedent	The argument that provision should be made for referendum is equally facile.
Precedent	Our Constitution-makers rejected the method of referendum.
Precedent	In a country where there are religious and linguistic minorities, it was not considered a proper method of deciding vital issues.
Precedent	The leaders of the minority communities entertained apprehension regarding this method.
Precedent	It is obvious that when passions are roused, the opinion of the minority in a popular referendum is bound to get submerged and lose effectiveness.
Precedent	1457.
Precedent	It also cannot be said that the method of bringing about amendment through referendum is a more difficult method.
Precedent	It is true that in Australia over 30 amendments were submitted to referendum, out of which only four were adopted and two of them were of trivial nature.
Precedent	As against that we find that the method of referendum for amending the Constitution has hardly provided much diffic ulty in Switzerland.
Precedent	Out of 64 amendments proposed for amending the federal Constitution, 49 were adopted in a popular referendum.
Precedent	So far as the method of amendment of the Constitution by two-third majority in either <ORG> and the ratification by the State Legislatures is concerned, we find that during first 140 years since the adoption of the United States Constitution, 3,113 proposals of amendment were made and out of them, only 24 so appealed to the <ORG> as to secure the approval of the <ORG> and only 19 made sufficient appeal to the State legislatures to secure ratification (see Constitutional Law of United States by Willis, p. 128).
Precedent	It, therefore, cannot be said that the method of referendum provides a more effective check on the power of amendment compared to the method of bringing it about by prescribed majority in each house of the <ORG>.
Precedent	1458.
Precedent	Apart from that I am of the view that it is not permissible to resort to the method of referendum unless there be a Con stitutional provision for such a course in the amendment provision.
Precedent	In the case of <NAME> v. <NAME> as Secretary of <ORG> of Ohio 64 Lawyers Ed.
Precedent	871 <ORG> was referred in the context of ratification by the <ORG>s of the Eighteenth Amendment to the Constitution of the Ohio <ORG> which contained provision for referendum.
Precedent	It was urged that in the case of such a <ORG> ratification should be by the method of referendum.
Precedent	Repelling this contention, the court held:  Referendum provisions of <ORG> Constitutions and statutes cannot be applied in the ratification or objection of amendments to the Federal Constitution without violating the requirement of Article 5 of such Constitution, that such ratification shall be by the legislatures of the several states, or by conventions therein, as <ORG> shall decide.
Precedent	The same view was reiterated by <ORG> in <ORG> v. <NAME> Secretary of <ORG> and other connected cases better known as National Prohibiti on Cases 253 S.C.R.
Precedent	350 64 Lawyers Edition 946.
Precedent	1459.
Precedent	Argument has been advanced on behalf of the petitioner that there is greater width of power for an amendment of the Constitution if the amendment is brought about by a referendum compared to the power of amendment vested in the two <ORG> or <ORG> even though it is required to be passed by a prescribed majority and has to be ratified by the State Legislatures.
Precedent	In this respect we find that different Constitutions have devised different methods of bringing about amendment.
Precedent	The main methods of modern Constitutional amendment are:  (1) by the ordinary legislature, but under certain restrictions; (2) by the people through a referendum;  (3) by a majority of all the units of a federal state;  (4) by a special convention.
Precedent	In some cases the system of amendment is a combination of two or more of these methods.
Precedent	1460.
Precedent	There are three ways in which the legislature may be allowed to amend the Constitution, apart from the case where it may  do so in the ordinary course of legislation.
Precedent	The simplest restriction is that which requires a fixed quorum of members for the consideration of proposed amendments and a special majority for their passage.
Precedent	The latter condition operated in the now defunct Constitution of Rumania.
Precedent	According to Article 146 of the Constitution of USSR the Constitution may be amended only by a decision of <ORG> adopted by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the votes in each of its chambers.
Precedent	A second sort of restriction is that which requires a dissolution and a general election on the particular issue, so that the new legislature, being returned with a mandate for the proposal, is in essence, a constituent assembly so far as that proposal is concerned.
Precedent	This additional check is applied in Belgium, Holland, Denmark and Norway (in all of which, however, also a two-thirds parliamentary majority is required to carry the amendment after the election) and in Sweden.
Precedent	A third method of Constitutional change by the  legislature is that which requires a majority of the two <ORG> in joint session, that is to say, sitting together as one <ORG>, as is the case, for example, in South Africa.
Precedent	1461.
Precedent	The second method is that which demands a popular vote or referedum or plebiscite.
Precedent	This device was employed in France during the Revolution and again by <NAME>, and in Germany by <NAME>.
Precedent	This system prevails in Switzerland, Australia, Eire, May, France (with certain Presidential provisios in <ORG>) and in Denmark.
Precedent	1462.
Precedent	The third method is peculiar to federations.
Precedent	The voting on the proposed measure may be either popular or by the legislatures of the states concerned.
Precedent	In Switzerland and Australia the referendum is in use; in the United States any proposed amendment requires ratification by the legislatures, or special conventions of three fourth of the several states.
Precedent	1463.
Precedent	The last method is one in which a special body is created ad hoc for the purpose of Constitutional revision.
Precedent	In some of the states of the U nited States, for example, this method is in use in connection with the Constitution of the states concerned.
Precedent	Such a method is also allowed if <ORG> proposes this method for amendment of the United States Constitutions.
Precedent	This method is prevalent in some of the states in Latin America also (see Modern Political Constitutions by C.F.
Precedent	Strong, p. 153-154).
Precedent	1464.
Precedent	The decision as to which method of amending the Constitution should be chosen has necessarily to be that of <ORG>.
Precedent	This decision is arrived at after taking into account the national requirements, the historical background, conditions prevailing in the country and other factors or circumstances of special significance for the nation.
Precedent	Once a method of amendment has been adopted in a Constitution, that method has to be adhered to for bringing about the amendment.
Precedent	The selection of the method of amendment having been made by <ORG> it is not for the court to express preference for another method of amendment.
Precedent	Amendment brought about by one method prescribed by the Constitution is as effective as it would have been if the Constitution had prescribed another method of bringing about amendment unless there be something in the Constitution itself which restricts the power of amendment.
Precedent	Article 138 of the Italian Constitution makes provision for referendum to bring about amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	It has however, been expressly provided in the article that referendum does not take place if a law has been approved in its second vote by a majority of two- thirds of the members of each chamber.
Precedent	The Italian Constitution thus makes a vote of majority of two-thirds of the members of each chamber at the second voting as effective as a referendum.
Precedent	Article 89 of the Constitution of the French Fifth Republic like-wise makes provision for referendum for amendment of Constitution.
Precedent	It is, however, provided in that article that the proposed amendment is not submitted to a referendum when the President of the Republic decides to  submit it to <ORG> convened in <ORG>; in that case the proposed amendment is approved only if it is accepted by three-fifth majority of the votes cast.
Precedent	1465.
Precedent	We may at this stage advert to Article 5 of the United <ORG>s Constitution which reads as under:  The <ORG>, whenever two-thirds of both <ORG> shall deem it necessary, shall propose amendments to this Constitution, or, on the application of the legislature of two-thirds of the several <ORG>s, shall call a convention for proposing amendments, which in either case, shall be valid to all intents and purposes, as part of this Constitution when ratified by the legislatures of three fourths of the several <ORG>s, or by conventions in three fourth thereof, as the one or the other mode of ratification may be proposed by the <ORG>; Provided that no amendment which may be made prior to the year one thousand eight hundred and eight shall in any manner affect the first and fourth clauses in the ninth section of the first article; and that no <ORG>, w ithout its consent, shall be deprived of its equal suffrage in the <ORG>.
Precedent	The above article makes it clear that there are two methods of framing and proposing amendments.
Precedent	(A) <ORG> may itself, by a two-thirds vote in each house, prepare and propose amendments.
Precedent	(B) The legislatures of two-thirds of the States may require <ORG> to summon a Constitutional Convention.
Precedent	<ORG> shall thereupon dc so, having no option to refuse; and the Convention when called shall draft and submit amendments.
Precedent	No provision is made as to the election and composition of the Convention, matters which would therefore appear to be left to the discretion of <ORG>.
Precedent	1466.
Precedent	There are the following two methods of enacting amendments framed and proposed in either of the foregoing ways.
Precedent	It is left to <ORG> to prescribe one or other method as <ORG> may think fit.
Precedent	(X) The legislatures of three-fourths of the States may ratify any amendments submitted to them.
Precedent	(Y) Conventions may be called in the several States, and three-fou rths of these conventions may ratify.
Precedent	1467.
Precedent	Except for Twentyfirst Amendment, on all the occasions on which the amending power has been exercised, method A has been employed and method X for ratifying-i.e., no drafting conventions of the whole Union or ratifying conventions in the several States have ever been summoned.
Precedent	The consent of the President is not required to a Constitutional amendment (see American Commonwealth by James Bryce, pp.
Precedent	365-366).
Precedent	1468.
Precedent	There is one provision of the Constitution which cannot be changed by this process.
Precedent	It is that which secures to each and every State equal representation in one branch of the legislature because according to proviso to Article V, no State without its consent shall be deprived of its equal suffrage in the <ORG>.
Precedent	1469.
Precedent	The question as to whether the width of power of amendment is greater in case the amendment is passed by a people's convention compared to the width of the power if it is passed by the prescribed majority in the legislatures arose in the case  of United States v. <NAME> 282 U.S. 716 decided by <ORG> of the United States.
Precedent	In that case the Constitutional validity of the Eighteenth Amendment was assailed on the ground that it should have been ratified by the Conventions because it took away the powers of the States and conferred new direct powers over individuals.
Precedent	The trial court rejected all these views and yet held the Eighteenth Amendment unConstitutional on theories of "political science," the "political thought" of the times, and a "scientific approach to the problem of government."
Precedent	<ORG> on appeal upheld the Eighteenth Amendment.
Precedent	After referring to the provisions of Article 5 Roberts J., who gave the opinion of the court, observed:  The choice, therefore, of the mode of ratification, lies in the sole discretion of <ORG>.
Precedent	Appellees, however, pointed out that amendments may be of different kinds, as e.g., mere changes in the character of federal means or machinery, on the one hand, and matters affecting t he liberty of the citizen on the other.
Precedent	They say that the framers of the Constitution expected the former sort might be ratified by legislatures, since the States as entities would be wholly competent to agree to such alterations, whereas they intended that the latter must be referred to the people because not only of lack of power in the legislatures to ratify, but also because of doubt as to their truly representing the people.
Precedent	Repelling the contention on behalf of the appellees, the court observed: If the framers of the instrument had any thought that amendments differing in purpose should be ratified in different ways, nothing would have been simpler than so to phrase Article 5 as to exclude implication or speculation, The fact that an instrument drawn with such meticulous care and by men who so well understood how to make language fit their thought does not contain any such limiting phrase affecting the exercise of discretion by the <ORG> in choosing one or the other alternative mode of ratification  is persuasive evidence that no qualification was intended.
Precedent	The court referred to the Tenth Amendment which provided that "the powers not delegated to the United States by the Constitution nor prohibited by it to the States, are reserved to the States respectively or to the people."
Precedent	The argument that the language of the Tenth Amendment demonstrates that the people reserved to themselevs powers over their personal liberty, that the legislatures were not competent to enlarge the powers of <ORG> in that behalf and that the people never delegated to the <ORG> the unrestricted power of choosing the mode of ratification of a proposed amendment was described by the <ORG> to be complete non sequitur.
Precedent	The fifth Article, it was observed, does not purport to delegate any governmental power to the United States, nor to withhold any from it.
Precedent	On the contrary, that article is a grant of authority by the people to <ORG>, and not to the United States.
Precedent	The court further observed: They (the people) deliber ately made the grant of power to <ORG> in respect to the choice of the mode of ratification of amendments.
Precedent	Unless and until that Article be changed by amendment, <ORG> must function as the delegated agent of the people in the choice of the method of ratification.
Precedent	1470.
Precedent	I am, therefore, of the view that there is no warrant for the proposition that as the amendments under Article 368 are brought about by the prescribed majority of the two <ORG> and in certain cases are ratified by the State Legislatures and the amendments are not brought about through referendum or passed in a <ORG>, the power of amendment under Article 368 is on that account subject to limitations.
Precedent	1471.
Precedent	Argument has then been advanced that if power be held to be vested in <ORG> under Article 368 to take away or abridge fundamental rights, the power would be, or in any case could be, so used as would result in repeal of all provisions containing fundamental rights.
Precedent	India, it is urged, in such an event would be r educed to a police state wherein all cherished values like freedom and liberty would be non-existent.
Precedent	This argument, in my opinion, is essentially an argument of fear and distrust in the majority of representatives of the people.
Precedent	It is also based upon the belief that the power under Article 368 by two-thirds of the members present and voting in <ORG> would be abused or used extravagently.
Precedent	I find it difficult to deny to the <ORG> the power to amend the Constitution so as to take away or abridge fundamental rights by complying with the procedure of Article 368 because of any such supposed fear or possibility of the abuse of power.
Precedent	I may in this context refer to the observations of Marshall C.J.
Precedent	regarding the possibility of the abuse of power of legislation and of taxation in the case of <ORG> v. <ORG>.
Precedent	29 U.S. 514 This vital power may be abused; but the Constitution of the United States was not intended to furnish the corrective for every abuse of power which may  be committed by the <ORG> governments.
Precedent	The interest, wisdom, and justice of the representative body, and its relations with its constituents furnish the only security where there is no express contract against unjust and excessive taxation, as well as against unwise legislation generally.
Precedent	1472.
Precedent	That power may be abused furnishes no ground for denial of its existence if government is to be maintained at all, is a proposition, now too well established (fee the unanimous opinion of <ORG> in Exparte <NAME> 15 U.S. 93).
Precedent	Same view was expressed by <ORG> in the case of <ORG> and Lambe 12, A.C. 575 while dealing with the provisions of Section 92 of the British North America Act relating to the power of Quebec legislature.
Precedent	1473.
RatioOfTheDecision	Apart from the fact that the possibility of abuse of power is no ground for the denial of power if it is found to have been legally vested, I find that the power of amendment under Article 368 has been vested not in one individual but in the majo rity of the representatives of the people in <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	For this purpose, the majority has to be of not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting in each <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	In addition to that, it is required that the amendment Bill should be passed in each <ORG> by a majority of the total membership of that <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is, therefore, not possible to pass an amendment Bill by a snap vote in a <ORG> wherein a small number of members are present to satisfy the requirement of the rule of quorum.
RatioOfTheDecision	The condition about the passing of the Bill by each <ORG>, including <ORG>, by the prescribed majority ensures that it is not permissible to get the Bill passed in a joint sitting of the two <ORG>s (as in the case of ordinary legislation) wherein the members of <ORG> can be outvoted by the members of <ORG> because of the latter's greater numerical strength.
RatioOfTheDecision	The effective voice of <ORG> in the passing of the amendment Bill further ensures that unless the prescribed majority of the rep resentatives of the states agree the Bill cannot be passed.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> under our Constitution is a perpetual body; its members are elected by the members of <ORG> and one-third of them retire every two years.
Precedent	We have besides that the provision for the ratification of the amendment by not less than one-half of <ORG> in case the amendment relates to certain provisions which impinge upon the rights of the States.
Precedent	The fact that a prescribed majority of the people's representatives is required for bringing about the amendment is normally itself a guarantee that the power would not be abused.
Precedent	The best safeguard against the abuse or extravagant use of power is public opinion and not a letter on the right of people's representatives to change the Constitution by following the procedure laid down in the Constitution itself.
Precedent	It would not be a correct approach to start with a distrust in the people's representatives in the <ORG> and to assume that majority of them would have ah a version for the liberties of the people and would act against the public interest.
Precedent	To quote the words of Justice <NAME> in Missouri Kansas & Texas <ORG> v. May 194 U.S. 267 (on p. 270).
Precedent	Great Constitutional provisions must be administered with caution.
Precedent	Some play must be allowed for the joints of the machine and it must be remembered that legislatures are ultimate guardians of the liberties and welfare of the people in quite as great a degree as the courts.
Precedent	1474.
Precedent	<NAME> has dealt with the question of the abuse of power in his book "The Amending of Federal Constitution", in the following words on page 123: 'Abuse' of the amending power is an anomalous term.
Precedent	The proponents of implied limitations resort to the method of reductio ad absurdum in pointing out the abuses which might occur if there were no limitations on the power to amend....
Precedent	The amending power is a power of an altogether different kind from the ordinary governmental powers.
Precedent	If abuse occurs, it occurs at the hands of a special organization of t he nation and of the states representing an extraordinary majority of the people, so that for all practical purposes it may be said to be the people, or at least the highest agent of the people, and one exercising soverign powers.
Precedent	Thus the people merely take the consequences of their own acts :  It has already been mentioned above that the best safeguard against the abuse of power is public opinion.
Precedent	Assuming that under the sway of some overwhelming impulse, a climate is created wherein cherished values like liberty and freedom lose their significance in the eyes of the people and their representatives and they choose to do away with all fundamental rights by amendment of the Constitution, a restricted interpretation of Article 368 would not be of much avail.
Precedent	The people in such an event would forfeit the claim to have fundamental rights and in any case fundamental rights would not in such an event save the people from political enslavement, social stagnation or mental servitude.
Precedent	I may in this context refer to  the words of <NAME> in his eloquent address on the Spirit of Liberty:  I often wonder whether we do not rest our hopes too much upon Constitutions, upon laws and upon courts.
Precedent	These are false hopes; believe me, these are false hopes.
Precedent	Liberty lies in the hearts of men and women; when it dies there, no Constitution, no law no court can save it; no Constitution, no law, no court can even do much to help it.
Precedent	While it lies there it needs no Constitution, no law, no court to save it.
Precedent	And what is this liberty which must lie in the hearts of men and women?
Precedent	It is not the ruthless, the unbridled will; it is not freedom to do as one likes.
Precedent	That is the denial of liberty, and leads straight to its overthrow.
Precedent	A society in which men recognize no check upon their freedom soon becomes a society where freedom is the possession of only a savage few; as we have learned to our sorrow.
Precedent	(see pages 189-190 Spirit of Liberty edited by <NAME>).
Precedent	Similar idea was expressed in another celebrated passage by <NAME> in t he Contribution of an Independent Judiciary to Civilization:  You may ask what then will become of the fundamental principles of equity and fair play which our Constitutions enshrine; and whether I seriously believe that unsupported they will serve merely as counsels of moderation.
Precedent	I do not think that anyone can say what will be left of those principles; I do not know whether they will serve only as counsels; but this much I think I do know that a society so riven that the spirit of moderation is gone, no court can save; that a society where that spirit flourishes, no court need save; that in a society which evades its responsibility by thrusting upon the courts the nurture of that spirit, that spirit in the end will perish.
Precedent	(see p. 164 supra).
Precedent	1475.
Precedent	It is axiomatic that the involvement of a nation in war by a declaration of war against another country can change the entire course of history of the nation.
Precedent	A wrong decision in this respect can cause untold suffering, result in national humiliation, take toll  of thousands of lives and cripple the economy of the nation for decades to come.
Precedent	If the Government and the <ORG> can be entrusted with power of such far reaching magnitude on the assumption that such a power would not be abused but would be exercised reasonably in the national interest, it would seem rather anomalous to have an approach of distrust in those very organs of the state and to deny to the <ORG> the power of amendment of fundamental rights because of the supposed possibility of the abuse of such power.
Precedent	1476.
Precedent	There is one other aspect of the matter which may be not lost sight of.
Precedent	Part III deals with a number of fundamental rights.
Precedent	Assuming that one relating to property, out-of the many fundamental rights, is found to be an obstacle in pushing forward certain ameliorative measures and it is proposed to abridge that fundamental right and it is also decided not to abridge or take away any other fundamental right, the present position, according to the stand taken on behalf of the petitioners , is that there is no power under Article 368 to abridge the obstructive fundamental right.
Precedent	The result is that even though reference is made on behalf of the petitioners to those fundamental rights as enshrine within themselves the valued concept of liberty of person and freedom of expression, the protection which is, in fact, sought is for the fundamental right to property which causes obstruction to pushing forward ameliorative measures for national weal.
Precedent	It is not, in my opinion, a correct approach to assume that if <ORG> is held entitled to amend Part III of the Constitution so as to take away or abridge fundamental rights, it would automatically or necessarily result in the abrogation of all fundamental rights.
Precedent	I may mention in this context that for seventeen years, from 1950 till 1967 Golak Nath case (supra) was decided, the accepted position was that the <ORG> had the power to amend Part III of the Constitution so as to take away or abridge fundamental rights.
Precedent	Despite the possession of that p ower by the <ORG>, no attempt was made by it to take away or abridge fundamental rights relating to cherished values like liberty of person and freedom of expression.
Precedent	If it was not done in the past, why should we assume that the majority of members of the <ORG> in future would acquire sudden aversion and dislike for these values and show an anxiety to remove them from the Constitution.
Precedent	There is a vital distinction, in my opinion, between the vesting of a power, the exercise of the power and the manner of its exercise.
Precedent	What we are concerned with is as to whether on the true construction of Article 368 , the <ORG> has or has not the power to amend the Constitution so as to take away or abridge fundamental rights.
Precedent	So far as this question is concerned, the answer, in my opinion, should be in the affirmative, as long as the basic structure of the Constitution is retained.
Precedent	1476.
Precedent	In the context of abuse of power of the amendment, reference has been made on behalf of the petitioners to the Constitutio n of <ORG> and it is urged that unless there are restrictions on the power of amendment in so far as fundamental rights are concerned, the danger is that the Indian Constitution may also meet the same fate as did the Weimar Constitution at the hands of <NAME>.
Precedent	This argument, in my opinion, is wholly misconceived and is not based upon correct appreciation of historical facts.
Precedent	Following military reversals when <NAME> fled to Holland in 1918 his mutinous subjects proclaimed a republic in Germany.
Precedent	There was thus a break in the continuity of the authority and the <ORG> had to face staggering political problems.
Precedent	It had to bear the burden of concluding a humilitating peace.
Precedent	It was later falsely blamed for the defeat itself by some of the politicians who were themselves responsible for the collapse and capitulation of 1918.
Precedent	The Republic had to wrestle, within a decade and a half, with two economic crises of catastrophic proportions which ruined and made desperate the ordinarily stable elements of  society.
Precedent	The chaos with political party divisions in the country was reflected in <ORG> where no party obtained a clear majority.
Precedent	There were 21 cabinets in 14 years.
Precedent	It was in those conditions that <NAME> emerged on the scene.
Precedent	He made use of Article 48 of the Weimar Constitution which dealt with emergency powers.
Precedent	Under Article 48 of the Constitution, the President was empowered to issue decrees suspending the rights guaranteed by the basic law and to make direct use of the army and navy should emergency conditions so require.
RatioOfTheDecision	The purpose of the provisions was, of course, to provide the executive with means to act in the event of some grave national emergency where the immediate and concentrated use of the power of the state might become suddenly necessary.
RatioOfTheDecision	But what happened was that almost from its beginning the government found itself in one emergency after another, so that rule by executive decrees issued under the authority provided for by Article 48 supplanted the normal functioning of the legislative  branch of government.
RatioOfTheDecision	The increasing division among the political parties, the staggering economic problem and the apparent failure of the parliamentary government to function, were accompanied by the steady growth in power of <ORG> under <NAME>.
RatioOfTheDecision	In less than two years, the Weimar Republic was transformed into a totalitarian dictatorship.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Enabling Act of March 23, 1933, pushed through the <ORG> by a narrow Nazi majority, provided government by decree without regard to Constitutional guarantees.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Act empowered the Government to enact the statutes without the sanction of the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> made a show of following the Constitution, but the acts of his party in and out of the government in practice violated the basic law.
RatioOfTheDecision	The few limitations imposed upon the government were ignored, and <NAME>'s Third Reich was launched (see Modern Constitutions by R.F.
RatioOfTheDecision	Moore, p. 86-87 and The Constitutions of Europe by <NAME>, p. 99-100).
RatioOfTheDecision	It would thus appear that it was not by use of the  power of amending the Constitution but by acting under the cover of Article 48 of the Constitution dealing with emergency powers that <NAME> brought about the Nazi dictatorship.
Precedent	He thus became what has been described as "...the supreme political leader of the people, supreme tender and highest superior of the administration, supreme judge of the people, supreme commander of the armed forces and the source of all law.
Precedent	1477.
Precedent	Apart from the fact that the best guarantee against the abuse of power of amendment is good sense of the majority of the members of <ORG> and not the unamendability of Part III of the Constitution, there is one other aspect of the matter.
Precedent	Even if Part III may be left intact, a mockery of the entire parliamentary system can be made by amending Articles 85 and 172, which are not in Part III and according to which the life of <ORG> and <NAME> of the States, unless sooner dissolved, would be five years, and by providing that the life of existing <ORG> and <NAME> s hall be fifty years.
Precedent	This would be a flagrant abuse of the power of amendment and I refuse to believe that public opinion in our country would reach such abysmal depths and the standards of political and Constitutional morality would sink so low that such an amendment would ever be passed.
Precedent	I need express no opinion for the purpose of this case as to whether this Court would also not quash such an amendment In any case such an amendment would be an open invitation for and be a precursor of revolution.
Precedent	1478.
Precedent	Even without amending any article, the emergency provisions of the Constitution contained in Article 358 and 359 can theoretically be used in such a manner as may make a farce of the democratic set up by prolonging the rule of the party in power beyond the period of five years since the last general election after the party in power has lost public support.
Precedent	A Proclamation of Emergency under Article 352 can be issued by the President if he is satisfied that a grave emergency exists whereby the security of In dia or of any part of the territory thereof is threatened, whether by war or external aggression or even by internal disturbance.
Precedent	Such a Proclamation has to be laid before <ORG>.
Precedent	Resolution approving the Proclamation has thereafter to be passed by <ORG>.
Precedent	According to Article 83 , <ORG>, unless sooner dissolved, shall continue for five years from the date appointed for its first meeting and no longer and the expiration of the said period of five years shall operate as a dissolution of the <ORG> provided that the said period may, while a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation, be extended by <ORG> by law for a period not exceeding one year at a time and not extending in any case beyond a period of six months after the Proclamation has ceased to operate.
Precedent	As the Government and <ORG> play a vital part in the Proclamation and continuation of emergency, the emergency provisions can theoretically be used for avoiding the election and continuing a pa rty in power even though it has lost popular support by extending the life of <ORG> in accordance with Article 83(2).
Precedent	The effective check against such unabashed abuse of power is the sense of political responsibility, the pressure of public opinion and the fear of popular uprising.
Precedent	We need not go into the question as to whether the court would also intervene in such an event.
Precedent	It is, in my opinion, inconceivable that a party would dare to so abuse the powers granted by the emergency provisions.
Precedent	The grant of the above power under Article 83 (2) is necessarily on the assumption that such a power would not be abused.
Precedent	1479.
Precedent	Argument has then been advanced on behalf of the petitioners that the power of amendment might well be used in such a manner as might result in doing away with the power of amendment under Article 368 or in any case so amending that articles as might make it impossible to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	It is, in my opinion, difficult to think that majority of members of future <ORG>  would attempt at any time to do away with the power of amendment in spite of the knowledge as to what was the fate of unamendable Constitutions in other countries like France.
Precedent	Assuming that at any time such an amendment to abolish all amendments of Constitution is passed and made a part of the Constitution, it would be nothing short of laying the seeds of a future revolution or other extra-Constitutional methods to do away with unamendable Constitution.
Precedent	It is not necessary for the purpose of this case to go into the question of the Constitutional validity of such an amendment.
Precedent	1480.
Precedent	We may now deal with the question as to what is the scope of the power of amendment under Article 368.
Precedent	This would depend upon the connotation of the word "amendment".
Precedent	Question has been posed during arguments as to whether the power to amend under the above article includes the power to completely abrogate the Constitution and replace it by an entirely new Constitution.
Precedent	The answer to the above question, in my opinion, should be i n the negative.
Precedent	I am further of the opinion that amendment of the Constitution necessarily contemplates that the Constitution has not to be abrogated but only changes have to be made in it.
Precedent	The word "amendment" postulates that the old Constitution survives without loss of its identity despite the change and continues even though it has been subjected to alterations.
Precedent	As a result of the amendment, the old Constitution cannot be destroyed and done away with; it is retained though in the amended form.
Precedent	What then is meant by the retention of the old Constitution?
Precedent	It means the retention of the basic structure or framework of the old Constitution.
Precedent	A mere retention of some provisions of the old Constitution even though the basic structure or framework of the Constitution has been destroyed would not amount to the retention of the old Constitution.
Precedent	Although it is permissible under the power of amendment to effect changes, "howsoever important, and to adapt the system to the requirements of changing conditions, it is no t permissible to touch the foundation or to alter the basic institutional pattern.
Precedent	The words "amendment of the Constitution" with all their wide sweep and amplitude cannot have the effect of destroying or abrogating the basic structure or framework of the Constitution.
Precedent	It would not be competent under the garb of amendment, for instance, to change the democratic government into dictatorship or hereditary monarchy nor would it be permissible to abolish <ORG> and <ORG>.
Precedent	The secular character of the state according to which the state shall not discriminate against any citizen on the ground of religion only cannot likewise be done away with.
Precedent	Provision regarding the amendment of the Constitution does not furnish a pretence for subverting the structure of the Constitution nor can Article 368 be so construed as to embody the death wish of the Constitution or provide sanction for what may perhaps be called its lawful harakiri.
Precedent	Such subversion or destruction cannot be described to be amendment of the  Constitution as contemplated by Article 368.
Precedent	1481.
Precedent	The words "amendment of this Constitution" and "the Constitution shall stand amended" in Article 368 show that what is amended is the existing Constitution and what emerges as a result of amendment is not a new and different Constitution but the existing Constitution though in an amended form.
Precedent	The language of Article 368 thus lends support to the conclusion that one cannot, while acting under that article, repeal the existing Constitution and replace it by a new Constitution.
Precedent	1482.
Precedent	The connotation of the amendment of the Constitution was brought out clearly by Pt.
Precedent	<NAME> in the course of his speech in support of the First Amendment wherein he said that "a Constitution which is responsive to the people's will, which is responsive to their ideas, in that it can be varied here and there, they will respect it all the more and they will not fight against, when we want to change it."
Precedent	It is, therefore, plain that what Pt.
Precedent	<NAME> contemplated by amendment was the va rying of the Constitution "here and there" and not the elimination of its basic structure for that would necessarily result in the Constitution losing its identity.
Precedent	1483.
Precedent	Reference to some authorities in the United States so far as the question is concerned as to whether the power to amend under Article 5 of US Constitution would include within itself the power to alter the basic structure of the Constitution are not helpful because there has been no amendment of such a character in the United States.
Precedent	No doubt the Constitution of the United States had in reality, though not in form, changed a good deal since the beginning of last century; but the change had been effected far less by formally enacted Constitutional amendments than by the growth of customs or institutions which have modified the working without altering the articles of the Constitution (see The Law of the Constitution by <NAME> Tenth Ed.
Precedent	p. 129).
Precedent	1484.
Precedent	It has not been disputed during the course of arguments that the power of amendment unde r Article 368 does not carry within itself the power to repeal the entire Constitution and replace it by a new Constitution.
Precedent	If the power of amendment does not comprehend the doing away of the entire Constitution but postulates retention or continuity of the existing Constitution, though in an amended form, question arises as to what is the minimum of the existing Constitution which should be left intact in order to hold that the existing Constitution has been retained in an amended form and not done away with.
Precedent	In my opinion, the minimum required is that which relates to the basic structure or framework of the Constitution.
