RatioOfTheDecision	As those belonging to the lower strata constitute the bulk of the population, the disparities provide a fertile soil for violent upheavals.
RatioOfTheDecision	The prevention of such upheavals is not merely necessary  for the peaceful evolution of society, it is also in the interest of those who belong to the upper strata to ensure that the potential causes for violent upheaval are eliminated.
RatioOfTheDecision	Various remedies have been suggested in this connection and the stress has been laid mainly upon having what is called a welfare state.
RatioOfTheDecision	The modern states have consequently to take steps with a view to ameliorate the conditions of the poor and to narrow the chasm which divides them from the affluent sections of the population.
RatioOfTheDecision	For this purpose the state has to deal with the problems of social security, economic planning and industrial and agrarian welfare.
RatioOfTheDecision	Quite often in the implementation of these policies, the state is faced with the problem of conflict between the individual rights and interests on the one side and rights and welfare of vast sections of the population on the other.
RatioOfTheDecision	The approach which is now generally advocated for the resolving of the above conflict is to look upon the rights of the individuals as conditioned by so cial responsibility.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> while dealing with this matter has observed in <ORG>:  The struggle for freedom is largely transferred from the plane of political to that of economic rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	Men become less interested in the abstract fragment of political power an individual can secure than in the use of massed pressure of the groups to which they belong to secure an increasing share of the social product....
RatioOfTheDecision	So long as there is inequality, it is argued, there cannot be liberty.
RatioOfTheDecision	The historic inevitability of this evolution was seen a century ago by <NAME>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is interesting to compare this insistence that the democratization of political power mean equality and that its absence would be regarded by the masses as oppression with the argument of Lord <NAME> that liberty and equality are antitheses.
RatioOfTheDecision	To the latter liberty was essentially an autocratic ideal; democracy destroyed individuality, which was the very pith of liberty, by seeking identity of conditions.
RatioOfTheDecision	The modern  emphasis is rather toward the principle that material equality is growing inescapable and that the affirmation of personality must be effective upon an immaterial plane.
Precedent	(see Vol.
Precedent	IX, p.  445).
Precedent	1530.
Precedent	I may also refer to another passage on page 99 of Grammar of Politics by <NAME>:  The <ORG>, therefore, which seeks to survive must continually transform itself to the demands of men who have an equal claim upon that common welfare which is its ideal purpose to promote.
Precedent	We are concerned here, not with the defence of anarchy, but with the conditions of its avoidance.
Precedent	Men must learn to subordinate their self- interest to the common welfare.
Precedent	The privileges of some must give way before the rights of all.
Precedent	Indeed, it may be urged that the interest of the few is in fact the attainment of those rights, since in no other environment is stability to be assured.
Precedent	1531.
Precedent	A modern state has to usher in and deal with large schemes having social and economic content.
Precedent	It has to undertake the challenging task of what has b een called social engineering, the essential aim of which is the eradication of the poverty, uplift of the downtrodden, the raising of the standards of the vast mass of people and the narrowing of the gulf between the rich and the poor.
Precedent	As occasions arise quite often when the individual rights clash with the larger interests of the society, the state acquires the power to subordinate the individual rights to the larger interests of society as a step towards social justice.
Precedent	As observed by <NAME> on page 434 of Volume I of Jurisprudence under the heading "Limitations on the Use of Property":  Today the law is imposing social limitations-limitations regarded as involved in social life.
Precedent	It is endeavouring to delimit the individual interest better with respect to social interests and to confine the legal right or liberty or privilege to the bounds of the interest so delimited.
Precedent	To quote the words of <NAME> in Legal Theory:  But modern democracy looks upon the right to property as one conditioned by social  responsibility by the needs of society, by the 'balancing of interests' which looms so large in modern jurisprudence, and not as preordained and untouchable private right.
Precedent	(Fifth Edition, p. 406).
Precedent	1532.
Precedent	With a view to bring about economic regeneration, the state devises various methods and puts into operation certain socio-economic measures.
Precedent	Some of the methods devised and measures put into operation may impinge upon the property rights of individuals.
Precedent	The courts may sometimes be sceptical about the wisdom behind those methods and measures, but that would be an altogether extraneous consideration in determining the validity of those methods and measures.
RatioOfTheDecision	We need not dilate further upon this aspect because we are only concerned with the impact of the Preamble.
RatioOfTheDecision	In this respect I find that although it gives a prominent place to securing the objective of social, economic and political justice to the citizens, there is nothing in it which gives primacy to claims of individual right to property over the claims of s ocial, economic and political justice.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is, as a matter of fact, no clause or indication in the Preamble which stands in the way of abridgement of right to property for securing social, economic and political justice.
RatioOfTheDecision	Indeed, the dignity of the individual upon which also the Preamble has laid stress, can only be assured by securing the objective of social, economic and political justice.
RatioOfTheDecision	1533.
RatioOfTheDecision	Reference has been made on behalf of the petitioners to <ORG> in order to show that in the pre-independence days, it was one of the objectives of nationalist leaders to have some kind of charter of human rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	This circumstance, in my opinion has not much material bearing on the point of controversy before us.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution- makers did incorporate in Part III of the Constitution certain-rights and designated them as fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	In addition to that, the Constitution-makers put in Part IV of the Constitution certain Directive Principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	Although those Directive Principles were not to be enfor ceable by any court, Article 37 declared that those principles were nevertheless fundamental in the governance of the country and it should be the duty of the <ORG> to apply those principles in making laws.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Directive Principles embody a commitment which was imposed by the Constitution-makers on the <ORG> to bring about economic and social regeneration of the teeming millions who are steeped in poverty, ignorance and social backwardness.
RatioOfTheDecision	They incorporate a pledge to the coming generations of what the <ORG> would strive to usher in.
RatioOfTheDecision	No occasion has arisen for the amendment of the Directive Principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	Attempt have, however, been made from time to time to amend the fundamental rights in Part III.
RatioOfTheDecision	The question with which we are concerned is whether there is power of amendment under Article 368 so as to take away or abridge the fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	This question would necessarily have to depend upon the language of Article 368 as well as upon the width and scope of the power of amendment under Article 368 and t he consideration of the <NAME> Report in this context would be not helpful.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the language of Article 368 warrants a wide power of amendment as may include the power to take away or abridge fundamental rights, the said power cannot be held to be non- existent nor can its ambit be restricted by reference to <NAME> Report.
Precedent	The extent to which historical material can be called in aid has been laid down in Maxwell on Interpretation of Statutes on page 47-48 as under:  In the interpretation of statutes, the interpreter may call to his aid all those external or historical facts which are necessary for comprehension of the subject-matter, and may also consider whether a statute was intended to alter the law or to leave it exactly where it stood before.
Precedent	But although we can have in mind the circumstances when the Act was passed and the mischief which then existed so far as these are common knowledge...we can only use these matters as an aid to the construction of the words which <ORG> has used.
Precedent	We cannot encroach  on its legislative function by reading in some limitation which we may think was probably intended but which cannot be inferred from the words of the Act.
Precedent	The above observations hold equally good when we are construing the provisions of a Constitution.
Precedent	Keeping them in view we can get no material assistance in support of the petitioners contention from <ORG>.
Precedent	1534.
Precedent	Apart from what has been stated above, we find that both before the dawn of independence as well as during the course of debates of <ORG> stress was laid by the leaders of the nation upon the necessity of bringing about economic regeneration and thus ensuring social and economic justice.
Precedent	The Congress Resolution of 1929 on social and economic changes stated that "the great poverty and misery of the Indian people are due, not only to foreign exploitation in India but also to the economic structure of society, which the alien rulers support so that their exploitation may continue.
Precedent	In order therefore to remove this povert y and misery and to ameliorate the condition of the Indian masses, it is essential to make revolutionary changes in the present economic and social structure of society and to remove the gross inequalities".
Precedent	The resolution passed by the <ORG> in 1931 recited that in order to end the exploitation of the masses, political freedom must include real economic freedom of the starving millions.
Precedent	The Objectives Resolution which was moved by Pt.
Precedent	<NAME> in <ORG> on December 13, 1946 and was subsequently passed by <ORG> mentioned that there would be guaranteed to all the people of India, "justice, social, economic, and political; equality of status, of opportunity and before the law; freedom of thought, expression, belief, faith, worship, vocation, association and action subject to law and public morality".
Precedent	It would, therefore, appear that even in the Objectives Resolution the first position was given to justice, social, economic and political.
Precedent	Pt.
Precedent	<NAME> in the course of one of his  speeches, said:  The service of India means the service of the millions who suffer.
Precedent	It means the ending of poverty and ignorance and disease and inequality of opportunity.
Precedent	The ambition of the greatest man of our generation has been to wipe every tear from every eye.
RatioOfTheDecision	That may be beyond us, but as long as there are tears and suffering, so long our work will not be over.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> in his book "Extracts from the Indian Constitution : Cornerstone of a Nation" after quoting the above words of Pt.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> has stated:  Two revolutions, the national and the social, had been running parallel in India since the end of the First World War.
RatioOfTheDecision	With independence, the national revolution would be completed, but the social revolution must go on.
RatioOfTheDecision	Freedom was not an end in itself, only 'a means to an end', <NAME> had said, 'that end being the raising of the people...to higher levels and hence the general advancement of humanity'.
RatioOfTheDecision	The first task of this <ORG> (<NAME> told the members) is to free India through a new Constit ution, to feed the starving people, and to clothe the naked masses, and to give every Indian the fullest opportunity to develop himself according to his capacity.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME>, a prominent southern member of the <ORG> and editor of a major newspaper, described the situation in terms of three revolutions.
RatioOfTheDecision	The political revolution would end, he wrote, with independence.
Precedent	The social revolution meant 'to get (India) out of the medievalism based on birth, religion, custom, and community and reconstruct her social structure on modern foundations of law, individual merit, and secular education'.
Precedent	The third revolution was an economic one : The transition from primitive rural economy to scientific and planned agriculture and industry'.
Precedent	<NAME> (now President of India) believed India must have a 'socio- economic revolution' designed not only to bring about 'the real satisfaction of the fundamental needs of the common man', but to go much deeper and bring about 'a fundamental change in the structure of Indian so ciety'.
Precedent	On the achievement of this great social change depended India's survival.
Precedent	'If we cannot solve this problem soon, '<NAME> warned the <ORG>, 'all our paper Constitutions will become useless and purposeless.... * * * * * 'The choice for India, 'wrote <NAME>...is between rapid evolution and violent revolution...because the Indian masses cannot and will not wait for a long time to obtain the satisfaction of their minimum needs.'
Precedent	* * * * * What was of greatest importance to most <ORG> members, however, was not that socialism be embodied in the Constitution, but that a democratic Constitution and with a socialist bias be framed so as to allow the nation in the future to become as socialist as its citizens desired or its needs demanded.
Precedent	Being, in general, imbued with the goals, the humanitarian bases, and some of the techniques of social democratic thought, such was the type of Constitution that Constituent <ORG> members created.
Precedent	1535.
Precedent	Dealing with the Directive Principles, <NAME> writ es: In the Directive Principles, however, one finds an even clearer statement of the social revolution.
Precedent	They aim at making the Indian masses free in the positive sense, free from the passivity engendered by centuries of coercion by society and by nature, free from the abject physical conditions that had prevented them from fulfilling their best selves.
Precedent	* * * * * By establishing these positive obligations of the state, the members of <ORG> made it the responsibility of future Indian governments to find a middle way between individual liberty and the public good, between preserving the property and the privilege of the few and bestowing benefits on the many in order to liberate 'the powers of all men equally for contributions to the common good'.
Precedent	* * * * * The Directive Principles were a declaration of economic independence, a declaration that the privilege of the colonial era had ended, that the Indian people (through the democratic institutions of the Constitution) had assumed economic as  well as political control of the country, and that Indian capitalists should not inherit the empire of British colonialists.
Precedent	1536.
Precedent	Pt.
Precedent	<NAME>, in the course of his speech in support of the <ORG> (First Amendment) Bill, said:  And as I said on the last occasion the real difficulty we have to face is a conflict between the dynamic ideas contained in the Directive Principles of Policy and the static position of certain things that are called 'fundamental' whether they relate to property or whether they relate to something else.
Precedent	Both are important undoubtedly.
Precedent	How are you to get over them ?
Precedent	A <ORG> which is unchanging and static, it does not matter how good it is, how perfect it is, is a <ORG> that has past its use.
Precedent	1537.
Precedent	Again in the course of his speech in support of the <ORG> (Fourth Amendment) Bill, Pt.
Precedent	<NAME> said:  But, I say, that if that is correct, there is an inherent contradiction in the <ORG> between the fundamental rights and the Directive Principles of State Policy.
Precedent	Therefore, again, it is up to this <ORG> to remove that contradiction and make the fundamental rights subserve the Directive Principles of State Policy.
Precedent	1538.
Precedent	It cannot, therefore, be said that the stress in the impugned amendments to the Constitution upon changing the economic structure by narrowing the gap between the rich and the poor is a recent phenomenon.
Precedent	On the contrary, the above material shows that this has been the objective of the national leaders since before the dawn of independence, and was one of the underlying reasons for the First and Fourth Amendments of the Constitution.
Precedent	The material further indicates that the approach adopted was that there should be no reluctance to abridge or regulate the fundamental right to property if it was felt necessary to do so for changing the economic structure and to attain the objectives contained in the Directive Principles.
Precedent	1539.
Precedent	So far as the question is concerned as to whether the right to property can be said to pertain to basic structure or frame work of the Constitution, the answer, in my opinion, should plainly be in the negative.
Precedent	Basic structure or framework indicates the broad outlines of the Constitution, while the right to property is a matter of detail.
Precedent	It is apparent from what has been discussed above that the approach of the framers of the Constitution was to subordinate the individual right to property to the social good.
Precedent	Property right has also been changing from time to time.
Precedent	As observed by <NAME> in Grammar of Politics, the historical argument is fallacious if it regards the regime of private property as a simple and unchanging thing.
Precedent	The history of private property is, above all, the record of the most varied limitations upon the use of the powers it implies.
Precedent	Property in slaves was valid in Greece and Rome; it is no longer valid today.
Precedent	<NAME> in this context has quoted the following words of <NAME>:  The idea of property is not some one thing identical throughout history and incapable of alteration...at any given time it i s a brief expression denoting the rights over things conferred by the law or custom of some given society at that time; but neither on this point, nor on any other, has the law and custom of a given time and place, a claim to be stereotyped for ever.
Precedent	A proposed reform in laws or customs is not necessarily objectionable because its adoption would imply, not the adaptation of all human affairs to the existing idea of property, to the growth and improvement of human affairs.
Precedent	1540.
Precedent	The argument that <ORG> cannot by amendment enlarge its own powers is untenable.
Precedent	Amendment of the Constitution, in the very nature of things, can result in the conferment of powers on or the enlargement of powers of one of the organs of the state.
Precedent	Likewise, it can result in the taking away or abridgement of the powers which were previously vested in an organ of the state.
Precedent	Indeed nearly every expansion of powers and functions granted to <ORG> would involve consequential contraction of powers and functions in the <ORG> ernment of the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	The same is true of the converse position.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is nothing in the Constitution which prohibits or in any other way prevents the enlargement of powers of <ORG> as a result of Constitutional amendment and, in my opinion, such an amendment cannot be held to be impermissible or beyond the purview of Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	Indeed, a precedent is afforded by the Irish case of <NAME> (supra) wherein amendment made by the <NAME> as a result of which it enlarged its powers inasmuch as its power of amending the Constitution without a referendum was increased from eight years to 16 years was held to be valid.
RatioOfTheDecision	Even <NAME> who gave a dissenting judgment did not question the validity of the amendment on the ground that <NAME> had thereby increased its power.
RatioOfTheDecision	He struck it down on the ground that there was no power to amend the amending clause.
RatioOfTheDecision	No such difficulty arises under our Constitution because of the existence of an express provision.
RatioOfTheDecision	I am also unable to accede to the contention t hat an amendment of the Constitution as a result of which the President is bound to give his assent to an amendment of the Constitution passed in accordance with the provisions of Article 368 is not valid.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 itself gives, inter alia, the power to amend Article 368 and an amendment of Article 368 which has been brought about in the manner prescribed by that article would not suffer from any Constitutional or legal infirmity.
RatioOfTheDecision	I may mention in this context that an amendment of the US Constitution in accordance with Article 5 of the US Constitution does not require the assent of the President.
RatioOfTheDecision	The change made by the Twentyfourth Amendment in the Constitution of India, to which our attention has been invited, has not done away with the assent of the President but has made it obligatory for him to give his assent to the Constitution Amendment Bill after it has been passed in accordance with Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	As it is not now open to the President to withhold his assent to a Bill in regard to a Constitutional a mendment after it has been duly passed, the element of personal discretion of the President disappears altogether.
Precedent	Even apart from that, under our Constitution the position of the President is that of a Constitutional head and the scope for his acting in exercise of his personal discretion is rather small and limited.
Precedent	1541.
Precedent	Reference was made during the course of arguments to the provisions of Section 6 of the Indian Independence Act, 1947.
Precedent	According to Sub-section (1) of that section, the <ORG> of each of the new Dominions shall have full power to make laws for that Dominion, including laws having extra-territorial opeartion.
Precedent	Sub-section (6) of the section provided that the power referred to in Sub-section (1) of this section extends to the making of laws limiting for the future the powers of the <ORG> of the Dominion.
Precedent	No help, in my opinion, can be derived from the above provisions because <ORG> framed and adopted the Constitution not on the basis of any power derived from Sec tion 6 of the Indian Independence Act.
Precedent	On the contrary, the members of <ORG> framed and adopted the Constitution as the representatives of the people and on behalf of the people of India.
Precedent	This is clear from the opening and concluding words of the Preamble to the Constitution.
Precedent	There is, indeed, no reference to the Indian Independence Act in the Constitution except about its repeal in Article 395 of the Constitution.
Precedent	1542.
Precedent	Apart from the above, I find that all that Sub-section (6) of Section 6 of the Indian Independence Act provided for was that the power referred to in Sub-section (1) would extend to the making of laws limiting for the future the powers of <ORG>.
Precedent	<ORG> acting as <ORG> actually framed the Constitution which placed limitations on the ordinary legislative power of the future <ORG> by providing that the legislative laws would not contravene the provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	At the same time, the Constitue <ORG> inserted Article 368 in the Constitution which gave power to the two Houses of future Parliaments to amend the Constitution in compliance with the procedure laid down in that article.
Precedent	There is nothing in Section 6 of the Indian Independence Act which stood in the way of the Constitue<ORG> against the insertion of an article in the Constitution conferring wide power of amendment, and I find it difficult to restrict the scope of Article 368 because of anything said in Section 6 of the Indian Independence Act.
Precedent	1543.
Precedent	Argument on behalf of the petitioners that our Constitution represents a compact on the basis of which people joined <ORG> and accepted the Constitution is wholly misconceived.
Precedent	The part of India other than that comprised in erstwhile Indian States was already one territory on August 15, 1947 when India became free.
Precedent	So far as the erstwhile Indian States were concerned, they acceded to <ORG> long before the Constitution came into force on January 26, 1950 or was  adopted on November 26, 1949.
Precedent	There thus arose no question of any part of India comprising the territory of India joining <ORG> on the faith of any assurance furnished by the provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	Some assurances were given to the minorities and in view of that they gave up certain demands.
Precedent	The rights of minorities are now protected in Articles 25 to 30.
Precedent	Apart from the articles relating to protection to the minorities, the various articles contained in Part III of the Constitution are applicable to all citizens.
Precedent	There is nothing to show that the people belonging to different regions would have or indeed could have declined to either join <ORG> or to remain in <ORG> but for the incorporation of articles relating to fundamental rights in the Constitution.
Precedent	The Constitution containing fundamental rights was framed by the people of India as a whole speaking through their representative and if the people of India as a whole acting again through their representatives decide  to abridge or take away some fundamental right like one relating to property, no question of breach of faith or violation of any alleged compact can, in my opinion, arise.
Precedent	1544.
RatioOfTheDecision	This apart, compact means a bargain or agreement mutually entered into, which necessarily connotes a choice and volition for the party to the compact.
RatioOfTheDecision	Whatever may be the relevance or significance of the concept of compact in the context of the US Constitution where different States joined together to bring into existence the United States of America and where further each one of the States ratified the Constitution after it had been prepared by <ORG>, the above concept has plainly no relevance in the context of the Indian Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The whole of India was, as already mentioned, one country long before the Constitution was adopted.
RatioOfTheDecision	There was also no occasion here for the ratification of the Constitution by each State after it had been adopted by <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	1545.
RatioOfTheDecision	Reference has been made on beh alf of the petitioners to the case of <CITATION_1672729>  2 S.C.R.
Precedent	109 which related to the Punjab Reorganization Act, 1966.
Precedent	This Court while upholding the validity of the Act dealt with Article 4 , according to which any law referred to in Article 2 or Article 3 shall contain such provisions for the amendment of the First Schedule and the Fourth Schedule as may be necessary to give effect to the provisions of the law and may also contain such supplemental, incidental and consequential provisions (including provisions as to representation in <ORG> and in the <ORG> or <ORG>s of the State or States affected by such law) as <ORG> may deem necessary, and observed: Power with which the <ORG> is invested by Articles 2 and 3, is power to admit, establish, or form new States which conform to the democratic pattern envisaged by the Constitution; and the power which the <ORG> may exercise by law is supplemental, incidental or consequential to the admission, establishment or formation of  a <ORG> as contemplated by the Constitution, and is not power to override the Constitutional scheme.
RatioOfTheDecision	No <ORG> can therefore be formed, admitted or set up by few under Article 4 by the <ORG> which has not effective legislative, executive and judicial organs.
RatioOfTheDecision	1546.
RatioOfTheDecision	The above passage, in my opinion, does not warrant an inference of an implied limitation on the power of amendment as contended on behalf of the petitioners.
RatioOfTheDecision	This Court dealt in the above passage with the import of the words "supplemental, incidental and consequential provisions" and held that these provisions did not enable the <ORG> to override the Constitutional scheme.
RatioOfTheDecision	The words "Constitutional scheme" had plainly reference to the provisions of the Constitution which dealt with a <ORG>, its legislature, judiciary and other matters in Part VI.
RatioOfTheDecision	Once the <ORG> of Haryana came into being, it was to have the attributes of a <ORG> contemplated by the different articles of Part VI in the same way as did the other <ORG>s.
RatioOfTheDecision	No question arose in  that case about limitation on the power of amendment under Article 368 and as such, that case cannot be of any avail to the petitioners.
RatioOfTheDecision	1547. learned Counsel for the petitioner has invited our attention to the Constitutional position specially in the context of civil liberties in Canada.
Statute	In this respect we find that the opening words of the Preamble to the British North America Act, 1867 read as under: Whereas the provisions of Canada, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick have expressed their desire to be federally united into one dominion under the Crown of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland, with a Constitution similar in principle to that of the United Kingdom; Section 91 of the above mentioned Act deals with the legislative authority of <ORG> of Canada.
Statute	The opening words of Section 91 are as under:  It shall be lawful for the Queen, by and with the advice and consent of the <ORG> and <ORG>, to make laws for the peace, order, and good government of Canada, in relation to all matte rs not coming within the classes of subjects by this Act assigned exclusively to the Legislatures of the provinces; and for greater certainty, but not so as to restrict the generality of the foregoing terms of the section, it is hereby declared that (notwithstanding anything in this Act) the exclusive legislative authority of <ORG> extends to all matters coming within the classes of subjects next hereinafter enumerated; that is to say, ___.
Statute	There follows a list of different subjects.
Statute	The first amongst the subjects, which was inserted by British North America Act 1949, is : "The amendment from time to time of the Constitution of Canada, except as regards matters coming within the classes of subjects by this Act assigned exclusively to the Legislatures of the provinces, or...." It is not necessary to give the details of other limitations on the power of amendment.
Statute	Section 92 of the British North America Act enumerates the subjects of exclusive provincial legislation.
Statute	According to this section , in each province the <ORG> may exclusively make laws in relation to matters coming within the classes of subjects next hereinafter enumerated.
Statute	There then follows a list of subjects, the first amongst which is "The amendment from time to time, notwithstanding anything in this Act, of the Constitution of the province, except as regards the office of the Lieutenant Governor".
RatioOfTheDecision	In view of the fact that amendment of the Constitution is among the subjects of legislation, the only distinction in Canada, it has been said, between ordinary legislation by <ORG> and Constitutional law is that the former concerns all matters not specially stated as within the ambit of provincial legislation while the latter concerns any fundamental change in the division of rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	Further, although because of the federal character of the <ORG>, the Canadian Constitution cannot be called flexible, it is probably the least rigid of any in the modern federal states (see Modern Political Constitutions by C.F.
RatioOfTheDecision	Strong).
RatioOfTheDecision	1548.
RatioOfTheDecision	It a ppears that at least six different views have been propounded in Canada about the Constitutional position of basic liberties.
RatioOfTheDecision	To date, <ORG> has not given Judicial approval to any of these views.
RatioOfTheDecision	Different members of the <ORG> have voiced various opinions on the matter, but all of these fall far short of settling the issue.
RatioOfTheDecision	It should also be noted that the fundamental problem is not whether <ORG> or the legislature may give to the people basic freedom, but rather which one may interfere with them or take them away (see Civil Liberties in Canada by <NAME> P. 13).
RatioOfTheDecision	1549.
Precedent	An important case which had bearing on the question of civil liberties was the <ORG> case [1938] S.C.R.
Precedent	100 (Canada).
Precedent	That case related to the validity of an Act which had placed limitations on the freedom of the <ORG> and <ORG> held that the Act was ultra vires, since it was ancillary to and dependent upon the Alberta Social Credit Act, which itself was ultra vires.
Precedent	Three or judge s, including <NAME>, went further than this, and dealt with the freedom of speech and freedom of Press.
Precedent	It was observed that curtailment of the exercise of the right of the public discussion would interfere with the working of parliamentary institutions of Canada.
Precedent	Opinion of <NAME> was based not on the criminal law power but on the necessity for maintaining democratic society as contemplated by the Constitution.
Precedent	A later decision dealing with free speech was <NAME> v. <NAME> and Attorney-General of Quebec [1957] S.C.R.
Precedent	285 (Canada).
Precedent	In that case <ORG> declared invalid the Quebec Communistic Propaganda Act.
Precedent	All the judges but one were agreed that the statute did hot fall within provincial competence under property and Civil rights or matters of a merely local or private nature in the province.
Precedent	<NAME> held that the <ORG> itself could not abrogate the right of discussion and, debate.
Precedent	1550.
Precedent	An article by <NAME> in Volume 12-1966-67 in <ORG> shows that though the prop osition enunciated by <NAME> has commanded the allegiance of an impressive number of judges and has not been decisively rejected, it has never been accepted by a majority of the members of <ORG> or of any other court.
RatioOfTheDecision	Some judges have assumed that basic freedoms may properly be the subject matter of legislation separate and apart from any other-subject matter.
RatioOfTheDecision	Others have taken the view that unlimited jurisdiction falls within <ORG> control under its general power to make laws "for the peace, order and good government of Canada".
RatioOfTheDecision	A third view which has been taken is mat the creation of a <ORG> and reference in the Preamble to "a Constitution similar in principle to that of the United Kingdom" postulates that legislative body would be elected and function in an atmosphere of free speech.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is not necessary to give the other views or dialate upon different views.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> while dealing with the dictum of <NAME> has observed in Canadian Constitutional Law: Apart from the d ictum by <NAME> in the <NAME> case, supra, there is no high authority which places civil liberties beyond the legislative reach of both <ORG> and the provincial <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	There are no explicit guarantees of civil liberties in the B.N.A.
RatioOfTheDecision	Act nothing comparable to the Bill of Rights (the 1st ten amendments) in the Constitution of the United States, which, within limits and on conditions prescribed by <ORG> as ultimate expounder of the meaning and range of the Constitution, prohibits both federal and state action infringing, inter alia, freedom of religion, of speech, of the press and of assembly.
Precedent	(see p. 970).
Precedent	1551.
Precedent	It would appear from the above that the different views which have been expressed in Canada are in the context of the preamble and section of the British North America Act, the provisions of which are materially different from our Constitution.
Precedent	Even in the context of the British.
Precedent	North America Act, the observations of <NAME> relied upon on behalf of the petitioners have  not been accepted by the majority of the judges of <ORG>, and in my opinion, they afford a fragile basis for building a theory of implied limitations.
Precedent	1552.
Precedent	It may be mentioned that in August 1960 <ORG> passed the Canadian Bill of Rights.
Precedent	Section 1 of the Bill declared certain human rights and fundamental freedoms and reads as under:  1.
Precedent	It is hereby recognized and declared that in Canada there have existed and shall continue to exist without discrimination by reason of race, national origin, colour, religion or sex, the following human rights and fundamental freedoms, namely,  (a) the right of the individual to life, liberty, security of the person and enjoyment of property, and the right not to be deprived thereof except by due process of law;  (b) the right of the individual to equality before the law and the protection of the law;  (c) freedom of religion;  (d) freedom of speech;  (e) freedom of assembly and association; and  (f) freedom of the press.
Precedent	According to  Section 2 of the Bill, every law of Canada shall, unless it is expressly declared by an Act of the Parliament of Canada that it shall operate notwithstanding the Canadian Bill of Rights, be so construed and applied as not to abrogate, abridge or infringe or to authorise the abrogation, abridgement or infringement of any of the rights or freedoms therein recognized and declared.
Precedent	The relevant part of Section 2 reads as under:  Every law of Canada shall, unless it is expressly declared by an Act of the Parliament of Canada that it shall operate notwithstanding the Canadian Bill of Rights, be so construed and applied as not to abrogate, abridge or infringe or to authorize the abrogation, abridgement or infringement of any of the rights or freedoms herein recognized and declared, and in particular, no law of Canada shall be construed or applied so as to... (underlining supplied).
Precedent	Plain reading of Section 2 reproduced above makes it manifest that the human rights and fundamental freedoms mentioned in Section 1 of  the Bill are not absolute but are subject to abrogation or abridgement if an express declaration to that effect be made in a law of Canada.
Precedent	Section 2 of the Bill shows that if an express declaration to that effect be made an Act of the <ORG> can override the provisions of the Bill of Rights.
Precedent	Section 2 is thus inconsistent with the theory of implied limitations based on human rights on the power of <ORG>.
Precedent	1553.
Precedent	Another case from Canada which has been referred to on behalf of the petitioners and which in my opinion is equally of no avail to them is The Attorney General of Nova Scotia and The Attorney General of Canada [1950] S.C.R.
Precedent	31 (Canada) decided by <ORG>.
Precedent	It was held in that case that an Act respecting the delegation of jurisdiction from <ORG> to <ORG> and vice versa, if enacted, would not be Constitutionally valid since it contemplated delegation by <ORG> of powers, exclusively vested in it by Section 91 of th e British North America Act, to <ORG>; and delegation by that <ORG> of powers, exclusively vested in Provincial <ORG> under Section 92 of the Act, to <ORG>.
Precedent	The <ORG> of Canada and each Provincial <ORG>, according to <ORG>, was sovereign body within its sphere, possessed of exclusive jurisdiction to legislate with regard to the subject matters assigned to it under Section 91 or Section 92 , as the case may be.
Precedent	Neither was capable therefore of delegating to the other the powers with which it had been vested nor of receiving from the other the powers with which the other had been vested.
Precedent	It is plain that that case related to the delegation of powers which under the British North America Act had been assigned exclusively to <ORG> or to the Provincial <ORG>s.
Precedent	Such a delegation was held to be not permissible.
Precedent	No such question arises in the present case.
Precedent	1554.
Precedent	We may now deal with some of the other cases which have been referred t o on behalf of the petitioner.
Precedent	Two of those cases are from Ceylon.
Precedent	The Constitutional position there was that Section 29 of the Ceylon (Constitution) Order in Council, 1946 gave the power to make laws as well as the power to amend the Constitution though the procedure prescribed for the two was different.
Statute	Section 29 reads as under: 29 (1) Subject to the provisions of this Order, <ORG> shall have power to make laws for the peace, order and good government of the Island.
Statute	(2) No such law shall-  (a) prohibit or restrict the free exercise of any religion; or  (b) make persons of any community or religion liable to disabilities or restrictions to which persons of other communities or religions are not made liable; or  (c) confer on persons of any community or religion any privilege or advantage which is not conferred on persons of other communities or religions; or  (d) alter the Constitution of any religious body except with the consent of the governing authority of that body, so, however, that in any case w here a religious body is incorporated by law, no such alteration shall be made except at the request of the governing authority of that body:  1555.
Statute	Provided, however, that the preceding provisions of this, subsection shall not apply to any law making provision for, relating to, or connected with, the election of Members of <ORG>, to represent persons registered as citizens of Ceylon under the Indian and Pakistani Residents (Citizenship) Act.
RulingByPresentCourt	1556.
RulingByPresentCourt	This proviso shall cease to have effect on a date to be fixed by the Governor- General by Proclamation published in the <ORG>.
RulingByPresentCourt	(3) Any law made in contravention of Sub-section (2) of this section shall, to the extent of such contravention, be void.
RulingByPresentCourt	(4) In the exercise of its powers under this section, <ORG> may amend or repeal any of the provisions of this Order or of any other Order of <NAME> in its application to the Island:  1557.
RulingByPresentCourt	Provided that no Bill for the amendment or repeal of any of the provisions of this O rder shall be presented for the Royal Assent unless it has endorsed on it a certificate under the hand of the Speaker that the number of votes cast in favour thereof in <ORG> amounted to not less than two-thirds of the whole number of Members of the <ORG> (including those not present).
Precedent	1558.
Precedent	Every certificate of the Speaker under this sub-section shall be conclusive for all purposes and shall not be questioned in any court of law.
Precedent	1559.
Precedent	<CITATION_128948>  All E.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	650 the appellants had been charged with offences arising out of an abortive coup d'etat on January 27, 1962.
RatioOfTheDecision	The story of the coup d' etat was set out in a White Paper issued by <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	On March 16, 1962 the Criminal Law (Special Provisions) Act was passed and it was given restrospective effect from January 1, 1962.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Act was limited in operation to those who were accused of offences against the <ORG> in or about January 27, 1962.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Act legalised the imprisonment of the appellants while they were awaiting  trial, and modified a section of the Penal Code so as to enact ex post facto a new offence to meet the circumstances of the abortive coup.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Act empowered <NAME> to nominate the three judges to try the appellants without a jury.
Precedent	The validity of the Act was challenged as well as the nomination which had been made by <NAME> of the three judges.
Precedent	<ORG> upheld the objection about the vires of some of the provisions of the Act as well as the nomination of the judges.
Precedent	Subsequently the Act was amended and the power of nomination of the judges was conferred on the Chief Justice.
Precedent	The appellants having been convicted at the trial before a court of three judges nominated under the amended Act, went up in appeal before <ORG>.
Precedent	The conviction of the appellants was challenged on three grounds but <ORG> dealt with only two grounds.
Precedent	The first ground was that <ORG> was limited by an inability to pass legislation which was c ontrary to fundamental principles of justice.
Precedent	The two Acts of 1962, it was stated, were contrary to such principles in that they were not only directed against individuals but also ex post facto created crimes and for which those individuals would otherwise be protected.
Precedent	The second contention was that the Acts of 1962 offended against the Constitution in that they amounted to a direction to convict the appellants or to a legislative plan to secure the conviction and severe punishment of the appellants and thus constituted an unjustifiable assumption of judicial power by the legislature, or an interference with judicial power, which was outside the legislature's competence and was inconsistent with the severance of power between legislature, executive, and judiciary which the Constitution ordained.
Precedent	Dealing with the first contention, <ORG> referred to the provisions of the Ceylon (Constitution) Order in Council, 1946 and the Ceylon Independence Act, 1947 and observed that the joint effect of th e said Order and Act was intended to and resulted in giving <ORG> the full legislative powers of an independent sovereign state.
Precedent	The legislative power of <ORG>, it was held, was not limited by inability to pass laws which offended fundamental principles of justice.
Precedent	On the second ground, <ORG> held the Acts of 1962 to be invalid as they involved a usurpation and infringement by the legislature of judicial powers inconsistent with the written Constitution of Ceylon, which, while not in terms vesting judicial functions in the judiciary, manifested an intention to secure in the judiciary a freedom from a political, legislative and executive control.
Precedent	1560.
RatioOfTheDecision	It would thus appear that the decision is based upon the ground of severance of powers between legislature, judiciary and executive under the Ceylon Constitution and furnishes no support for the theory of implied limitations on the power of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the contrary, <ORG> while dealing with th e first contention rejected the theory of limitations on the power of <ORG> to make a law in violation of the fundamental principles of justice.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG>, it is also noteworthy, expressly pointed out that there had been no amendment of the Constitution in accordance with Section 29(4) of the Constitution by two-thirds majority and as such they had not to deal with that situation.
Precedent	1561.
Precedent	Another case to which reference was made on behalf of the petitioners was The Bribery Commissioner v. <NAME> [1965] A.C. 172.
Precedent	In that case it was found that the members of <ORG> had been appointed by the Governor-General on the advice of the Minister of Justice in accordance with Bribery Amendment Act but in contravention of Section 55 of the Ceylon Constitution.
Precedent	[Ceylon (Constitution) Order in Council, 1946] according to which the appointment of judicial officers was vested in <ORG>.
Precedent	It was held that a legislature has no power to ignore the conditions of l aw-making that are imposed by the instrument which itself regulates its power to make law.
Precedent	This restriction exists independently of the question whether the legislature is sovereign, as is that of Ceylon.
Precedent	1562.
RatioOfTheDecision	It would appear from the above that the point of controversy which arose for determination in that case was different from that which arises in the present case because we are not in this case concerned with any law made by a legislature in contravention of the Constitutional provisions Reference has been made on behalf of the petitioners to a passage in the judgment wherein while dealing with Sub-section (2) of Section 29 of the Ceylon Constitution, the provisions of which have been reproduced earlier, <ORG> observed that the various clauses of Sub-section (2) set out entrenched religious and racial matters which shall not be the subject of legislation.
RatioOfTheDecision	It was further observed that those provisions represented the solemn balance of rights between the citizens of Ceylon, the fundament al conditions on which inter se they accepted the Constitution and these are therefore unalterable under the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is contended that those observations show that the rights mentioned in Section 29(2) of the Ceylon Constitution which were similar to the fundamental rights in Part III of the Indian Constitution, were held by <ORG> to be unalterable under the Constitution.
Precedent	There was, it is further submitted, similarity between the provisions of Section 29(3) of the Ceylon Constitution and Article 13(2) of the Indian Constitution because it was provided in Section 29(3) that any law made in contravention of Section 29(2) shall to the extent of such contravention be void.
Precedent	1563.
Precedent	I find it difficult to accede to the contention that <ORG> laid down in the above case that Sections 29(2) and 29(3) placed a restriction on the power of amendment of the Constitution under Section 29(4) of the Constitution.
Precedent	The question with which <ORG> was concerned was regard ing the validity of the appointment of the members of <ORG>.
Precedent	Such appointment though made in compliance with the provisions of the Bribery Amendment Act, was in contravention of the requirements of Section 55 of the Ceylon Constitution.
Precedent	No question arose in that case relating to the validity of a Constitutional amendment brought about in compliance with Section 29(4) of the Constitution.
Precedent	Reference to the argument of the counsel for the respondent on the top of page 187 of that case shows that it was conceded on his behalf that "there is no limitation at the moment on the right of amendment or repeal except the requirement of the requisite majority".
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> nowhere stated that they did not agree with the above stand of the counsel for the respondent.
RatioOfTheDecision	Perusal of the judgment shows that <ORG> dealt with Sections 18 and 29 together and pointed out the difference between a legislative law, which was required to be passed by a bare majority of votes under Section 18 o f the Constitution, and a law relating to a Constitutional amendment which was required to be passed by a two-thirds majority under Section 29(4) .
RatioOfTheDecision	Dealing with the question of sovereignty, <ORG> observed:  A <ORG> does not cease to be sovereign whenever its component members fail to produce among themselves a requisite majority, e.g., when in the case of ordinary legislation the voting is evenly divided or when in the case of legislation to amend the Constitution there is only a bare majority if the Constitution requires something more.
RatioOfTheDecision	The minority are entitled under the Constitution of Ceylon to have no amendment of it which is not passed by a two-thirds majority.
RatioOfTheDecision	The limitation thus imposed on some lesser majority of members does not limit the sovereign power of <ORG> itself which can always, whenever it chooses, pass the amendment with the requisite majority.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has been submitted on behalf of the respondents that the above passage indicates that <ORG> took th e view that the amendment of all the provisions of the Ceylon Constitution including those contained in Sub-sections (2) and (3) of Section 29 could be passed by a two-thirds majority.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is also stated that the restrictions imposed by Sub-section (2) of Section 29 of the Ceylon Constitution are on the power of ordinary legislation by simple majority and not on the power of making Constitutional amendment by two-thirds majority in compliance with Section 29(4) of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It was in that sense that <ORG>, according to the submission, used the word "entrenched".
RatioOfTheDecision	Our attention has also been invited to the observations on pages 83 and 84 of the Constitutional structure by <ORG> that "these safeguards (contained in Section 29 ) of the rights of communities and religions could be repealed or amended by <ORG> provided it followed the prescribed procedure for amendment of the Constitution".
RatioOfTheDecision	These submissions may not be bereft of force, but it is, in my o pinion, not necessary to dilate further upon this matter and discuss the provisions of the Ceylon Constitution at greater length.
RatioOfTheDecision	The point of controversy before us would have to be decided in the light essentially of the provisions of our own Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Suffice it to say that <NAME>'s case does not furnish any material assistance to the stand taken on behalf of the petitioners.
Precedent	1564.
Precedent	We may now advert to the case of <NAME> v. The King [1920] A.C. 691 The said case related to the Constitution of <NAME>sland in Australia.
Fact	<NAME>sland was granted a Constitution in 1859 by an Order in <ORG> made on June 6.
Fact	The Order in <ORG> set up a <ORG> in the territory consisting of the <NAME>, <ORG> and <ORG> and the law making power was vested in the <NAME> acting with the advice and consent of the <ORG> and <ORG>.
Fact	Any law could be made for the "peace, welfare and good government of the colony", the phrase generally employed to denote the plenitude of sovereign legislat ive power even though that power be confined to certain subjects or within certain reservations.
Fact	The <ORG> passed a Constitution Act in 1867.
Fact	By Section 2 of that Act the legislative body was declared to have power to make laws for the peace, welfare and good government of the colony in all cases whatsoever.
Fact	The only express restriction on this comprehensive power was in Section 9 which required a two-thirds majority of the <ORG> and of the <ORG> as a condition precedent to the validity of legislation altering the Constitution of the <ORG>.
Fact	In 1916 the Industrial Arbitration Act was passed.
RulingByLowerCourt	The said Act authorised the Governor in <ORG> to appoint the President or a judge of <ORG> to be a judge of <ORG>.
RulingByLowerCourt	It was also provided that the judge so appointed shall have the jurisdiction of both offices, and shall hold office as a judge of <ORG> during good behaviour.
RulingByLowerCourt	The Governor in <ORG>, by a commission, appointed the appellant who  was the President of <ORG> to be a judge of <ORG> during good behaviour.
Precedent	<ORG> held that the appellant was not entitled to have the oath of office administered to him or to take his seat as a member of <ORG>.
Precedent	Subsequently, <ORG> of Queensland gave a judgment in ouster against the appellant.
Precedent	The provisions of Section 6 of the Industrial Arbitration Act of 1916 under which the appellant had been appointed a judge of <ORG> were held to be inconsistent with the provisions of the Constitution Act and as such void.
Precedent	On appeal four out of the seven judges of <ORG> agreed with <ORG> of Queensland, while the three other judges took the opposite view and expressed the opinion that the appeal should be allowed.
Precedent	The matter was then taken up in appeal to <ORG>.
Precedent	Lord <NAME> giving the opinion of <ORG> held (1) that <ORG> had power,  both under the Colonial Laws Validity Act, 1865, and apart therefrom, to authorise the appointment of a judge of <ORG> for a limited period; and (2) that Section 6 of the Industrial Arbitration Act authorised an appointment as a judge of <ORG> only for the period during which the person appointed was a judge of <ORG>.
Precedent	The appellant was further held to have been validly appointed.
Precedent	The above case though containing observations that a legislature has no power to ignore the conditions of law-making that are imposed by the instrument which itself regulates its power to make law, laid down the proposition that in the absence of a restriction, it is not possible to impose a restriction upon the legislative power.
Precedent	It was observed:  <ORG> is the master of its own household, except in so far as its powers have in special cases been restricted.
Precedent	No such restriction has been established, and none in fact exists, in such a case as is raised in t he issues now under appeal.
Precedent	1565.
Precedent	It was also observed:  Still less is the Board prepared to assent to the argument, at one time pressed upon it, that distinctions may be drawn between different matters dealt with by the Act, so that it becomes legitimate to say of one section : 'This section is fundamental or organic; it can only be altered in such and such manner'; and of another : 'This section is not of such a kind; it may consequently be altered with as little ceremony as any other statutory provision.'
RatioOfTheDecision	The decision in the above cited case can hardly afford any assistance to the petitioners.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the contrary, there are passages in the judgment which go against the stand taken on behalf of the petitioners.
RatioOfTheDecision	1566.
RatioOfTheDecision	Section 5 of the Colonial Laws Validity Act, 1865 to which there was a reference in the <NAME>'s case reads as under:  Every colonial legislature shall have, and be deemed at all times to have had, full power within its jurisdiction to establish courts of judicature, and to abolish and reconsti tute the same, and to alter the Constitution thereof, and to make provision for the administration of justice therein; and every representative legislature shall, in respect to the colony under its jurisdiction have, and be deemed at all times to have had, full power to make laws respecting the Constitution, powers, and procedure of such legislature; provided that such laws shall have been passed in such manner and form as may from time to time be required by any Act of Parliament, letters patent, Order in Council or colonial law for the time being in force in the said colony.
Precedent	Reference has been made during arguments to the decision of <ORG> in the case of Attorney-General for New South Wales v. <NAME> [1932] A.C. 526.
Precedent	The said case related to a Bill passed by the New South Wales Parliament for repeal of a section providing for referendum as well as to another Bill for abolition of <ORG>.
Precedent	<ORG> affirmed the decision of <ORG> which had held by m ajority that the Bills had not been passed in the "manner and form" within the meaning of Section 5 of the Colonial Laws Validity Act, and as such could not be presented for Royal assent.
Precedent	<ORG> based its decision upon the language of the above section and the meaning of the word "passed" in that section.
RatioOfTheDecision	We are not concerned in the present case with the aforesaid provisions.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is also nothing in the conclusions at which I have arrived which runs counter to the principles laid down in the <NAME>'s case.
RatioOfTheDecision	1567.
Precedent	Another Australian case to which reference has been made during the course of arguments is <ORG> v. The <ORG>.
Precedent	45 Australian Law Journal Reports 251 It has been laid down by <ORG> in that case that the <ORG> Parliament in exercise of its powers under Section 51(ii) of the Constitution may include the Crown in right of a State in the operation of a law imposing a tax or providing for the assessment of a tax.
Precedent	The inclusion of the Crown  in right of a <ORG>, according to the court, in the definition of "employer" in the Pay-roll Tax Assessment Act, thus making the Crown in right of a <ORG> liable to pay the tax in respect of wages paid to employees, including employees of departments engaged in strictly governmental functions, is a valid exercise of the power of the <ORG> under the above provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	There was discussion in the course of the judgment on the subject of implied limitation on the <ORG> legislative power under the Constitution arising from the federal nature of the Constitution and different views were expressed.
Precedent	Three of the Judges, including <NAME> took the view that there was no such limitation.
Precedent	As against that, four Judges were of the opinion that there was an implied limitation on <ORG> legislative power under the Constitution but the impugned Act did not offend such limitation.
Precedent	Opinion was expressed that the <ORG> Parliament while acting under the legislative entry of tax ation could hot so use the power of taxation as to destroy the States in a federal structure.
Precedent	The question as to what is the scope of the power of amendment was not considered in that case.
Precedent	The above case as such cannot be of much assistance for determining as to whether there are any implied limitations on the power to make Constitutional amendment.
Precedent	1568.
RatioOfTheDecision	I am, therefore, of the opinion that the majority view in <NAME> case that <ORG> did not have the power to amend any of the provisions of Part III of the Constitution so as to take away or abridge the fundamental rights cannot be accepted to be correct.
RatioOfTheDecision	Fundamental rights contained in Part III of our Constitution can, in my opinion, be abridged or taken away in compliance with the procedure prescribed by Article 368 , as long the basic structure of the Constitution remains unaffected.
RatioOfTheDecision	1569.
RatioOfTheDecision	We may now deal with the Twentyfourth Amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has sought to make clear matters regarding which doubt had arisen and conflicting views had been expr essed by this Court.
RatioOfTheDecision	We may in this context set forth the Statement of Objects and Reasons of the Constitution (Twentyfourth Amendment) Bill.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Statement of Objects and Reasons reads as under:  STATEMENT OF OBJECTS AND REASONS <ORG> in the well-known <NAME> case 1967 (2 SCR  762) reversed, by a narrow majority, its own earlier decisions upholding the power of <ORG> to amend all parts of the Constitution including Part III relating to fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	The result of the judgment is that <ORG> is considered to have no power to take away or curtail any of the fundamental rights guaranteed by Part III of the Constitution even if it becomes necessary to Jo so for giving effect to the Directive Principles of State Policy and for the attainment of the objectives set out in the Preamble to the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is, therefore, considered necessary to provide expressly that <ORG> has power to amend any provision of the Constitution so as to include the provisions of Part III within t he scope of the amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	1570.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Bill seeks to amend Article 368 suitably for the purpose and makes it clear that Article 368 provides for amendment of the Constitution as well as procedure therefor.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Bill further provides that when a Constitution Amendment Bill passed by both <ORG> is presented to the President for his assent, he should give has assent thereto.
Precedent	The Bill also seeks to amend Article 13 of the Constitution to make it inapplicable to any amendment of the Constitution under Article 368.
Precedent	1571.
Precedent	Section 2 of the Bill which was ultimately passed as the Constitution (Twentyfourth Amendment) Act has added a clause in Article 13 that nothing in that article would apply to any amendment of the Constitution made under Article 368.
Statute	As a result of Section 3 of the Amendment Act, Article 368 has been re-numbered as Clause (2) thereof and the marginal heading now reads "Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and procedure therefor".
Statute	Non-obstante Clause (1) has been inserted  in the article to emphasise the fact that the power exercised under that article is constituent power, not subject to the other provisions of the Constitution, and embraces within itself addition, variation and repeal of any provision of the Constitution.
Statute	Amendment has also been made so as to make it obligatory for the President to give his assent to the Amendment Bill after it has been passed in accordance with the article.
Statute	Clause (3) has further been added in Article 368 to the effect that nothing in Article 13 would apply to an amendment made under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	Although considerable arguments have been addressed before us on the point as to whether the power of amendment under Article 368 includes the power to amend Part III so as to take away or abridge fundamental rights, it has not been disputed before us that the Constitution (Twentyfourth Amendment) Act was passed in accordance With the procedure laid down in Article 368 of the Constitution as it existed before the passing of the said Act.
RatioOfTheDecision	In view of  what has been discussed above at length.
RatioOfTheDecision	I find no infirmity in the Constitution (Twentyfourth Amendment) Act.
RatioOfTheDecision	1, therefore, uphold the validity of the said Act.
RatioOfTheDecision	1572.
RatioOfTheDecision	We may now deal with the Constitution (Twentyfifth Amendment) Act, 1971.
Statute	The Twentyfifth Amendment has made three material changes:  (i) It has amended Article 31(2) in two respects.
Statute	(a) It substitutes the word "amount" for the word "compensation" for property acquired or requisitioned.
Statute	(b) It has provided that the law for the purpose of acquisition or requisition shall not be called in question on the ground that the whole or any part of the "amount" is to be given otherwise than in cash.
Precedent	(ii) It has provided that the fundamental right to acquire, hold and dispose of property under Article 19(1)(f) cannot be invoked in respect of any such law as is referred to in Article 31(2).
Precedent	(iii) It has inserted Article 31C as an overriding article which makes the fundamental rights conferred by Articles 14, 19 and 31 inapplicable to certain categorie s of laws passed by the <ORG> or by any State <ORG>.
Precedent	So far as the substitution of the word "amount" for the word "compensation" for property acquired or requisitioned in Article 31(2) is concerned, we find that this Court held in Mrs. <NAME>  S.C.R.
Precedent	558 case that by the guarantee of the right to compensation for compulsory acquisition under Article 31(2 ), before it was amended by the Constitution (Fourth Amendment) Act, the owner was entitled to receive a "just equivalent" or "full indemnification".
Precedent	In <NAME>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	614 case this Court held that notwithstanding the amendment of Article 31(2) by the Constitution (Fourth Amendment) Act and even after the addition of the words "and no such law shall be called in question in any Court on the ground that the compensation provided by that law is not adequate", the expression "compensation continued to have the same meaning as it had in Article 31(2) before it was amended, viz., just equivalent or full indemnification.
Precedent	Somewhat d ifferent view was taken by this <ORG> thereafter, in the case of <NAME>  3 S.C.R.
Precedent	341.
Precedent	In the case of <NAME> (supra) it was observed that the Constitutional guarantee was satisfied only if a just equivalent of the property was given to the owner.
Precedent	In the case of <NAME> (supra) it was held that "compensation" being itself incapable of any precise determination, no definite connotation could be attached thereto by calling it "just equivalent" or "full indemnification", and under Acts enacted after the amendment of Article 31(2) it is not open to the <ORG> to call in question the law providing for compensation on the ground that it is inadequate, whether the amount of compensation is fixed by the law or is to be determined according to principles specified therein (see observations of <NAME> on page 596 in the case of <CITATION_513801>  3 S.C.R.
Precedent	530.
Precedent	After further discussion of the views expressed in those two cases, <NAME> speaking for the majority, observed:  Both the  lines of thought which converge in the ultimate result, support the view that the principle specified by the law for determination of compensation is beyond the pale of challenge if it is relevant to the determination of compensation and is a recognized principle applicable in the determination of compensation for property compulsorily acquired and the principle is appropriate in determining the value of the class of property sought to be acquired.
Precedent	On the application of the view expressed in <NAME> case (supra) or in <NAME> case (supra) the Act, in our judgment, is liable to be struck down as it fails to provide to the expropriated banks compensation determined according to relevant principles.
Precedent	1573.
Precedent	The amendment in Article 31(2) made by the Twentyfifth Amendment by substituting the word "amount" for the word "compensation" is necessarily intended to get over the difficulty caused by the use of the word "compensation".
Precedent	As the said word was held by this <ORG> to have a partic ular connotation and was construed to mean just equivalent or full indemnification the amendment has replaced that word by the word "amount".
Precedent	In substituting the word "amount" for "compensation" the Amendment has sought to ensure that the amount determined for acquisition or requisition of property need not be just equivalent or full indemnification and may be, if the legislature so chooses, plainly inadequate.
Precedent	It is not necessary to further dilate upon this aspect because whatever may be the connotation of the word "amount", it would not affect the validity of the amendment made in Article 31(2).
Precedent	1574.
Precedent	Another change made in Article 31(2) is that the law for the purpose of acquisition or requisition shall not be called in question on the ground that the whole or any part of the "amount" fixed or determined for the acquisition or requisition of the property is to be given otherwise than in cash.
Precedent	I have not been able to find any infirmity in the above changes made in Article 31(2).
Precedent	1575.
Precedent	According to <NAME> e (2B) which has been added as a result of the Twentyfifth Amendment in Article 31 , nothing in Sub-clause (f) of Clause (1) of Article 19 shall affect any such law as is referred to in Clause (2).
Precedent	In this connection we find that this <ORG> held in some cases that Articles 19(1)(f) and 31(2) were exclusive.
Precedent	<CITATION_1857950>  S.C.R.
Precedent	88 a person detained pursuant to an order made in exercise of the power conferred by the Preventive Detention Act applied to this <ORG> for a writ of habeas corpus claiming that the Act contravened the guarantee under Articles 19, 21 and 22 of the Constitution.
Precedent	The majority of this <ORG> (<NAME>, and <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME>.)
Precedent	held that Article 22 being a complete code relating to preventive detention, the validity of an order of detention must be determined strictly according to the terms and "within the four corners of that Article".
Precedent	They held that a person detained may not claim that the freedom guaranteed under Article 19(1)(c) was infringed by his  detention, and that validity of the law providing for making orders of detention will not be tested in the light of the reasonableness of the restrictions imposed thereby on the freedom of movement, nor on the ground that his right to personal liberty is infringed otherwise than according to the procedure established by law.
Precedent	<NAME> expressed a contrary view.
Precedent	This case formed the nucleus of the theory that the protection of the guarantee of a fundamental freedom must be adjudged in the light of the object of <ORG> action in relation to the individual's right and not upon its effect upon the guarantee of the fundamental freedom, and as a corollary thereto, that the freedoms under Articles 19, 21, 22 and 31 are exclusive-each article enacting a code relating to protection of distinct rights (see p. 571 in the case of <NAME>, (supra).
Precedent	The view expressed in <NAME>'s case (supra) was reaffirmed in <CITATION_40472258>  S.C.R.
Precedent	451.
Precedent	The principle underlying the judgment of the majority was extended to the  protection of the right to property and it was held that Article 19(1)(f) and Article 31(2) were mutually exclusive in their operation.
Precedent	In the case of <CITATION_41574> .
Precedent	1 S.C.R.
Precedent	777 this <ORG> held that Article 19(1)(f) read with Clause (5) postulates the existence of property which can be enjoyed and over which rights can be exercised because otherwise the reasonable restrictions contemplated by Clause (5) could not be brought into play.
Precedent	If there is no property which can be acquired, held or disposed of, no restriction can be placed on the exercise of the right to acquire, hold or dispose it of.
Precedent	In <NAME>  3 S.C.R.
Precedent	887 case, <NAME> delivering the judgment of the majority of the <ORG>, observed that Clause (2) of Article 31 alone deals with compulsory acquisition of property by the <ORG> for a public purpose, and not Article 31(1) and he proceeded to hold that the expression "authority of law" means authority of a valid law, and on that account validity of the law seeking to  deprive a person of his property is open to challenge on the ground that it infringes other fundamental rights, e.g., under Article 19(1)(f).
Precedent	It was also observed that after the Constitution (Fourth Amendment) Act, 1955 <NAME> case (supra) "no longer holds the field".
Precedent	After the decision in <NAME> case (supra) there arose two divergent lines of authority.
Precedent	According to one view, "authority of law" in Article 31(1) was liable to be tested on the ground that it violated other fundamental rights and freedoms, including the right to hold property guaranteed by Article 19(1)(f).
Precedent	The other view was that "authority of a law" within the meaning of Article 31(2) was not liable to be tested on the ground that it impaired the guarantee of Article 19(1)(f) in so far as it imposed substantive restrictions-though it may be tested on the ground of impairment of other guarantees.
Precedent	In the case of <NAME> (supra), <NAME> speaking for the majority held that in determining the impact of <ORG> action upon Const itutional guarantees which are fundamental, the extent of protection against impairment of a fundamental right is determined not by the object of the <ORG> nor by the form of the action, but by its direct operation upon the individual's rights.
Precedent	It was further observed:  We are therefore unable to hold that the challenge to the validity of the provision for acquisition is liable to be tested only on the ground of non- compliance with Article 31(2).
Precedent	Article 31(2) requires that property must be acquired for a public purpose and that it must be acquired under a law with characteristics set out in that Article.
Precedent	Formal compliance with the conditions under Article 31(2) is not sufficient to negative the protection of the guarantee of the right to property.
Precedent	Acquisition must be under the authority of a law and the expression "law" means a law which is within the competence of the <ORG>, and does not impair the guarantee of the rights in Part III.
Precedent	We are unable, therefore, to agree that Article 19(1)(f) an d 31(2) are mutually exclusive.
Precedent	1576.
Precedent	The Twentyfifth Amendment seeks to overcome the effect of the above decision in <NAME> case.
RulingByPresentCourt	It has sought to resolve the earlier conflict of views noticeable in this respect in the judgments of this <ORG>.
Statute	Provision has accordingly been made that the fundamental right to acquire, hold or dispose of property under Article 19(1)(f) cannot be invoked in respect of any such law as is referred to in Article 31(2).
Statute	In view of what has been discussed earlier while dealing with the Twentyfourth Amendment, the change made by addition of Clause (2B) in Article 31(2) is permissible under Article 368 and cannot be held to be invalid.
Statute	1577.
Statute	We may now deal with Article 31C , introduced as a result of the Twentyfifth Amendment.
Statute	Perusal of this article which has been reproduced in the earlier part of this judgment shows that the article consists of two parts.
Statute	The first part states that notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 , no law giving effect to the policy of the  State towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Article 14 , Article 19 or Article 31.
Statute	According to the second part of this article, no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy.
Statute	There then follows the proviso, according to which where such law is made by the <ORG> of a State, the provisions of the article shall not apply thereto unless such law, having been reserved for the consideration of the President, has received his assent.
Statute	1578.
Statute	The first part of Article 31C is similar to Article 31A except in respect of the subject matter.
Statute	Article 31A was inserted by the Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951.
Statute	Clause (1) of Article 31A as then inserted was in the following words: (1) Notwithstanding anything in th e foregoing provisions of this Part, no law providing for the acquisition by the <ORG> of any estate or of any rights therein or for the extinguishment or modification of any such rights shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with, or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by, any provisions of this Part:  Provided that where such law is a law made by the Legislature of a <ORG>, the provisions of this article shall not apply thereto unless such law, having been reserved for the consideration of the President, has received his assent.
Statute	Subsequently, Clause (1) of Article 31A was amended by the Constitution (Fourth Amendment) Act, 1955.
