Statute	New Clause (1) was in the following words: (1) Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 , no law providing for-  (a) the acquisition by the <ORG> of any estate or of any rights therein or the extinguishment or modification of any such rights, or  (b) the taking over of the management of any property by the <ORG> for a limited period  either in the public interest or in order to secure the proper management of the property, or  (c) the amalgamation of two or more corporations either in the public interest or in order to secure the proper management of any of the corporations, or  (d) the extinguishment or modification of any rights of managing agents, secretaries and treasurers, managing directors, directors or managers of corporations, or of any voting rights of shareholders thereof, or  (e) the extinguishment or modification of any rights accruing by virtue of any agreement, lease or licence for the purpose of searching for, or winning, any mineral or mineral oil, or the premature termination or cancellation of any such agreement, lease or licence, shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with, or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Article 14 , Article 19 or Article 31: Provided that where such law is a law made by <ORG> State, the provisions of this article shall not apply theret o unless such law, having been reserved for the consideration of the President, has received his assent.
Statute	Clause (b) and (c) of Article 39 referred to in Article 31C read as under:  39.
Statute	The <ORG> shall, in particular, direct its policy towards securing- ...  (b) that the ownership and control of the material resources of the community are so distributed as best to subserve the common good;  (c) that the operation of the economic system does not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to the common detriment;  ...  1579.
RatioOfTheDecision	It would appear from the above that while Article 31A dealt with a law providing for the acquisition by the <ORG> of any estate or of any rights therein or the extinguishment or modification of such rights or other matters mentioned in Clauses (b) to (e) of that article, Article 31C relates to the securing of the objective that the ownership and control of the material resources of the community are so distributed as best to subserve the common good and that operation of  the economic system does not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to the common detriment.
RatioOfTheDecision	But for the difference in subjects, the language of the first clause of Article 31A and that of the first part of Article 31C is identical.
RatioOfTheDecision	Both Articles 31A and 31C deal with right to property.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31A deals with certain kinds of property and its effect is, broadly speaking, to take those kinds of property from the persons who have rights in the said property.
RatioOfTheDecision	The objective of Article 31C is to prevent concentration of wealth and means of production and to ensure the distribution of ownership and control of the material resources of the community for the common good.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31C is thus essentially an extension of the principle which was accepted in Article 31A.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fact that the provisions of Article 31C are more comprehensive and have greater width compared to those of Article 31A would not make any material difference.
RatioOfTheDecision	Likewise, the fact that Article 31A deals with law providing for cert ain subjects, while Article 31C deals with law giving effect to the policy towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 , would not detract from the conclusion that Article 31C is an extension of the principle which was accepted in Article 31A.
RatioOfTheDecision	Indeed, the legislature in making a law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) or Clause  (c) of Article 39 acts upon the mandate contained in Article 37 , according to which the Directive Principles are fundamental in the governance of the country and it shall be the duty of the <ORG> to apply those principles in making laws.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the amendment of the Constitution by which Article 31A was inserted was valid, I can see no ground as to how the Twentyfifth Amendment relating to the insertion of the first part of Article 31C can be held to be invalid.
RatioOfTheDecision	The validity of the First Amendment which introduced Article 31A was upheld by this <ORG> as long ago as 1952 in the case of <CIT ATION_1706770> (supra).
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31A having been held to be valid during all these years, its validity cannot now be questioned on account of the doctrine of stare decisis.
RatioOfTheDecision	Though the period for which <NAME> case stood unchallenged was not very long, the effects which have followed in the passing of the <ORG> laws on the faith of that decision, as observed by <NAME> in <NAME> case, are so overwhelming that we should not disturb the decision in that case upholding the validity of the First Amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	It cannot be disputed that millions of acres of land have changed hands and millions of new titles in agricultural lands which have been created and the <ORG> laws dealing with agricultural land which have been passed in the course of the years after the decision in <NAME> case have brought about an agrarian revolution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Agricultural population constitutes a vast mapority of the population in this country.
RatioOfTheDecision	In these circumstances, it would in my opinion be wrong to hold now that the deci sion upholding the First Amendment was not correct, and thus disturb all that has been done during these years and create chaos into the lives of millions of our countrymen who have benefited by these laws relating to agrarian reforms.
RatioOfTheDecision	I would, therefore, hold that this is one of the fittest cases in which the principle of stare decisis should be applied.
RatioOfTheDecision	The ground which sustained the validity of Clause (1) of Article 31A , would equally sustain the validity of the first part of Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	I may in this context refer to the observations of <NAME> in <NAME> v. <NAME> (258) U.S. 130 while upholding the validity of the 19th Amendment, according to which the right of citizens of the United States to vote shall not be denied or abridged by the United States or by States on account of sex.
RatioOfTheDecision	This case negatived the contention that a vast addition to the electorate destroyed the social compact and the residuary rights of the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	Justice <NAME> observed:  This amendment is in character and phraseology pre cisely similar to the 15th.
RatioOfTheDecision	For each the same method of adoption was pursued.
RatioOfTheDecision	One cannot be valid and the other invalid.
RatioOfTheDecision	That the 15th is valid...has been recognized and acted upon for half a century....
RatioOfTheDecision	The suggestion that the 15th was incorporated in the Constitution not in accordance with law, but practically as a war measure which has been validated by acquiesence cannot be entertained.
RatioOfTheDecision	1580.
RatioOfTheDecision	We may now deal with the second part of Article 31C , according to which no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to the policy of <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	The effect of the second part is that once the declaration contemplated by that article is made, the validity of such a law cannot be called in question in any court on the ground that it is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Articles 14, 19  or 31 of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The declaration thus gives a complete protection to the provisions of law containing the declaration from being assailed on the ground of being violative of Articles 14, 19 or 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	However tenuous the connection of a law with the objective mentioned in Clause (b) and Clause (c) of Article 39 may be and however violative it may be of the provisions of Articles 14, 19 and 31 of the Constitution, it cannot be assailed in a court of law on the said ground because of the insertion of the declaration in question in the law.
RatioOfTheDecision	The result is that if an Act contains 100 sections and 95 of them relate to matters not connected with the objectives mentioned in Clauses (b) and  (c) of Article 39 but the remaining five sections have some nexus with those objectives and a declaration is granted by the <ORG> in respect of the entire Act, the 95 sections which have nothing to do with the objectives of Clauses (b) and (c) of Article 39 , would also get protection.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is well-known that State Legis latures are quite often swayed by local and regional considerations.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is not difficult to conceive of laws being made by a <ORG> <ORG> which are directed against citizens of India who hail from other <ORG>s on the ground that the residents of the <ORG> in question are economically backward.
RatioOfTheDecision	For example, a law might be made that as the old residents in the <ORG> are economically backward and those who have not resided in the <ORG> for more than three generations have an affluent business in the <ORG> or have acquired property in the <ORG>, they shall be deprived of their business and property with a view to vest the same in the old residents of the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such a law if it contains the requisite declaration, would be protected and it would not be permissible to assail it on the ground of being violative of Articles 14, 19 and 31 of the Constitution even though such a law strikes at the integrity and unity of the country.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such a law might also provoke the <ORG>s of other <ORG>s to make laws which m ay discriminate in the economic sphere against the persons hailing from the <ORG> which was the first to enact such discriminate law.
RatioOfTheDecision	There would thus be a chain reaction of laws which discriminate between the people belonging to different <ORG>s and which in the very nature of things would have a divisive tendency from a national point of view.
RatioOfTheDecision	The second part of Article 31C would thus provide the cover for the making of laws with a regional or local bias even though such laws imperil the oneness of the nation and contain the dangerous seeds of national disintegration.
RatioOfTheDecision	The classic words of Justice <NAME> have a direct application to a situation like this.
RatioOfTheDecision	Said the great Judge: I do not think the United <ORG>s would come to an end if we lost our power to declare an Act of <ORG> void.
RatioOfTheDecision	I do think the Union would be imperiled if we could not make that declaration as to the laws of the several <ORG>s."
RatioOfTheDecision	(<NAME>, Collected Legal Papers (1920) 295-96).
RatioOfTheDecision	The fact that the assent of the President would have to be ob tained for such a law might not provide an effective safeguard because occasions can well be visualized when the <ORG> concerned might pressurise the <ORG> and thus secure the assent of the President.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such occasions would be much more frequent when the party in power at the <ORG> has to depend upon the political support of a regional party which is responsible for the law in question passed by the <ORG> Legislature.
RatioOfTheDecision	1581.
RatioOfTheDecision	It seems that while incorporating the part relating to declaration in Article 31C , the sinister implications of this part were not taken into account and its repercussions on the unity of the country were not realised.
RatioOfTheDecision	In deciding the question relating to the validity of this part of Article 31C , we should not, in my opinion, take too legalistic a view.
RatioOfTheDecision	A legalistic judgment would indeed be a poor consolation if it affects the unity of the country.
RatioOfTheDecision	It would be apposite in this context to reproduce a passage from <NAME>'s Commentaries on the Constitution of the United <ORG>s wherein he ad opted the admonition of <NAME> with a slight variation as under:  The remark of Mr. <NAME> may, with a very slight change of phrase be addressed as an admonition to all those, who are called upon to frame, or to interpret a Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Government is a practical thing made for the happiness of mankind, and not to furnish out a spectacle of uniformity to gratify the schemes of visionary politicians.
RatioOfTheDecision	The business of those, who are called to administer it, is to rule, and not to wrangle.
RatioOfTheDecision	It would "be a poor compensation, that one had triumphed in a dispute, whilst we had lost an empire; that we had frittered down a power, and at the same time had destroyed the republic (para 456).
RatioOfTheDecision	1582.
RatioOfTheDecision	The evil consequences which would flow from the second part of Article 31C would not, however, be determinative of the matter.
RatioOfTheDecision	I would therefore examine the matter from a legal angle.
RatioOfTheDecision	In this respect I find that there can be three types of Constitutional amendments which may be conceived to give protection to legislative measures  and make them immune from judicial scrutiny or attack in court of law.
RatioOfTheDecision	1583.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to the first type, after a statute has already been enacted by the <ORG> a Constitutional amendment is made in accordance with Article 368 and the said statute is inserted in the Ninth Schedule under Article 31B.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such a statute or any of the provisions thereof cannot be struck down in a court of law and cannot be deemed to be void or ever to have become void on the ground that the statute or any provisions thereof is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by any provision of Part III.
RatioOfTheDecision	In such a case, the provisions of the entire statute are placed before <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is open to not less than one-half of the members of each <ORG> and not less than two-thirds of the members of each <ORG> voting and present after applying their mind to either place the statute in the Ninth Schedule in its entirety or a part thereof or not to do so.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is only if not less than one-half of  the total members of each <ORG> and not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting in each <ORG> decide that the provisions of a particular statute should be protected under Article 31B either in their entirety or partly that the said provisions are inserted in the Ninth Schedule.
RatioOfTheDecision	A Constitutional amendment of this type relates to an existing statute of which the provisions can be examined by the two <ORG>s of Parliament and gives protection to the statute from being struck down on the ground of being violative of any provision of Part III of the Constitution.
Precedent	Such an amendment was introduced by the Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951 and its validity was upheld in <NAME> case (supra).
Precedent	1584.
RatioOfTheDecision	The second type of Constitutional amendment in that where the Constitutional amendment specifies the subject in respect of which a law may be made by the <ORG> and the amendment also provides that no law made in respect of that subject shall be deemed to be void on the grou nd that it is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Part III of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	In such a case the law is protected even though it violates the provisions of Part III of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is, however, open in such a case to the court, on being moved by an aggrieved party, to see whether the law has been made for the purpose for which there is Constitutional protection.
RatioOfTheDecision	The law is thus subject to judicial review and can be struck down if it is not for the purpose for which protection has been afforded by the Constitutional amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	To this category belong the laws made under Article 31A of the Constitution which has specified the subjects for which laws might be made, and gives protection to those taws.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is always open to a party to assail the validity of such a law on the ground that it does not relate to any of the subjects mentioned in Article 31A.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is only if the court finds that the impugned law relates to a subject mentioned in Article 31A that rite protect ion contemplated by that article would be afforded to the impugned law and not otherwise.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31A was introduced by the Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951 and as mentioned earlier, the validity of the First Amendment was upheld in <NAME> case (supra).
RatioOfTheDecision	1585.
RatioOfTheDecision	The third type of Constitutional amendment is one, according to which a law made for a specified object is protected from attack even though it violates Articles 14, 19 and  31.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitutional amendment further provides that the question as to whether the law is made for the specified object is not justiciable and a declaration for the purpose made by the legislature is sufficient and would preclude the court from going into the question as to whether the law is made for the object prescribed by the Constitutional amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	To such category belongs that part of Twentyfifth Amendment which inserted Article 31C when taken along with its second part.
RatioOfTheDecision	The law made under Article 31C is not examined and approved for the purpose of prote ction by not less than one-half of the members of each <ORG> of Parliament and not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting in each <ORG>, as is necessary in the case of laws inserted in the Ninth Schedule of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Nor can the law made under Article 31C be subject to judicial review with a view to find out whether the law has, in fact, been made for an object mentioned in Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31C thus departs from the scheme of Article 31A , because while a judicial review is permissible under Article 31A to find out as to whether a law has been made for any of the objects mentioned in Article 31A , such a judicial review has been expressly prohibited under Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	The result is that even if a law made under Article 31C can be shown in court of law to have been enacted not for the purpose mentioned in Article 31C but for another purpose, the law would still be protected and cannot be assailed on the ground of being violative of Articles 14, 19 and 31 of the Constitution because  of the declaration made by the legislature as contemplated by second part of Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may also be mentioned in this context that such a law can be passed by a bare majority in a legislature even though only the minimum number of members required by the quorum, which is generally one-tenth of the total membership of the legislature, are present at the time the law is passed.
RatioOfTheDecision	1586.
RatioOfTheDecision	The effect of the above amendment is that even though a law is in substance not in furtherance of the objects mentioned in Articles 39(b) and (c) and has only a slender connection with those objects, the declaration made by the <ORG> would stand in the way of a party challenging it on the ground that it is not for the furtherance of those objects.
RatioOfTheDecision	A power is thus being conferred upon the <ORG> and State <ORG>s as a result of this provision to make a declaration in respect of any law made by them in violation of the provisions of Articles 14, 19 and 31 and thus give it protection from being assailed on that groun d in a court of law.
RatioOfTheDecision	The result is that even though for the purpose of making an amendment of the Constitution an elaborate procedure is provided in Article 368 , power is now given to a simple majority in a State or Central <ORG>, in which only the minimum number of members are present to satisfy the requirement of quorum, to make any law in contravention of the provisions of Articles 14, 19 and 31 and make it immune from attack by inserting a declaration in that law.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is natural for those who pass a law to entertain a desire that it may not be struck down.
RatioOfTheDecision	There would, therefore, be an inclination to make an Act immune from attack by inserting such a declaration even though only one or two provisions of the Act have a connection with the objects mentioned in Article 39(b) and (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	Articles 14, 19 and 31 can thus be reduced to a dead letter, an ineffective purposeless showpiece in the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	1587.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power of making an amendment is one of the most important powers which can be conferred under  the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	As mentioned earlier, according to <NAME>, the amending clause is so fundamental to a Constitution that it may be called the Constitution itself while according to <NAME>, the amending clause is the most important part of a Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	This circumstance accounts for the fact that an elaborate procedure is prescribed for the amending of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power of amendment being of such vital importance can neither be delegated nor can those vested with the authority to amend abdicate that power in favour of another body.
RatioOfTheDecision	Further, once such a power is granted, either directly or in effect, by a Constitutional amendment to <ORG>s, it would be difficult to take away that power, because it can be done only by means of a Constitutional amendment and the States would be most reluctant, having got such a power, to part with it.
RatioOfTheDecision	In empowering <ORG> to make laws violative of Articles 14, 19 and 31 of the Constitution and in further empowering <ORG>  to make laws immune from attack on the ground of being violative of Articles 14, 19 and 31 by inserting the requisite declaration, the authority vested with the power to make amendment under Article 368 (viz ., the prescribed majority in each <ORG> of Parliament) has, in effect, delegated or granted the power of making amendment in important respects to <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Although the objects for which such laws may be made have been specified, the effect of the latter part of Article 31C relating to the declaration is that the law in question may relate even to objects which have not been specified.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31C taken along with the second part relating to the declaration departs from the scheme of Article 31A because while the protection afforded by Article 31A is to laws made for specified subjects, the immunity granted under Article 31C can be availed of even by laws which have not been made for the specified objects.
RatioOfTheDecision	The law thus made by the State Legislatures would have the effect of pro-tanto amendme nt of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such a power, as pointed out earlier, can be exercised by <ORG> by a simple majority in a <ORG> wherein the minimum number of members required by the rule of quorum are present.
RatioOfTheDecision	1588.
RatioOfTheDecision	In Re Initiative and Referendum Act [1919] A.C. 935 <ORG> after referring to a previous decision wherein <ORG> was held entitled to entrust to <ORG> authority to enact regulations relating to <ORG> observed on page 945:  But it does not follow that it can create and endow with its own capacity a new legislative power not created by the Act to which it owes its own existence.
RatioOfTheDecision	Their Lordships do no more than draw attention to the gravity of the Constitutional questions which thus arise.
RatioOfTheDecision	If it is impermissible for a legislature to create and endow with its own capacity a legislative power not created by the Act to which it owes its own existence, it should, in my opinion, be equally impermissible in the face of Article 368 in its pr esent form under our Constitution, for the amending authority to vest its amending power in another authority like <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has to be emphasised in this context that according to Article 368 , an amendment of this Constitution may be initiated only by the introduction of a Bill for the purpose in either <ORG> of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	The word "only" has a significance and shows that as long as Article 368 exists in its present form, the other methods of amendment are ruled out.
RatioOfTheDecision	1589.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may be mentioned that apart from the question of legislative competence, the articles for the violation of which statutes have been quashed in overwhelming majority of cases are Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	The question as to whether the impugned statute is beyond legislative competence can be agitated despite the protection of Article 31C in the same way as that question can be agitated despite the protection of Article 31A , but in other respects, as would appear from what has been stated above, Article 31C goes much beyond  the scope of Articles 31A and 31B.
RatioOfTheDecision	1590.
RatioOfTheDecision	In a federal system where the spheres of legislative powers are distributed between <ORG> and <ORG>, there has to be provided a machinery to decide in case of a dispute as to whether the law made by <ORG> encroaches upon the field earmarked for <ORG> as also a dispute whether a law made by <ORG> deals with a subject which can be exclusively dealt with by <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	This is true not only of a federal system but also in a Constitutional set up like ours wherein the Constitution-makers, though not strictly adopting the federal system, have imbibed the features of a federal system by distributing and setting apart the spheres of legislation between <ORG> and <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	The machinery for the resolving of disputes as to whether <ORG> has trespassed upon the legislative field of <ORG> or whether the State Legi slatures have encroached upon the legislative domain of <ORG> is furnished by the courts and they are vested with the powers of judicial review to determine the validity of the Acts passed by the legislatures.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power of judicial review is, however, confined not merely to deciding whether in making the impugned laws the <ORG> or State <ORG> have acted within the four comers of the legislative lists earmarked for them; the courts also deal with the question as to whether the laws are made in conformity with and not - in violation of the other provisions of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution-makers have provided for fundamental rights in Part III and made them justiciable.
RatioOfTheDecision	As long as some fundamental rights exist and are a part of the Constitution, the power of judicial review has also to be exercised with a view to see that the guarantees afforded by those rights are not contravened.
RatioOfTheDecision	Dealing with draft Article 25 (corresponding to present Article 32 of the Constitution) by which a  right is given to move <ORG> for enforcement of the fundamental rights, Dr. <NAME> speaking in <ORG> on December 9, 1948 observed: If I was asked to name any particular article in this Constitution as the most important-an article without which this Constitution would be a nullity-I could not refer to any other article except this one.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is the very soul of the Constitution and the very heart of it and I am glad that the <ORG> has realised its importance.
Precedent	(CAD debates, Vol.
Precedent	VII, p. 953).
RatioOfTheDecision	Judicial review has thus become an integral part of our Constitutional system and a power has been vested in <ORG> and <ORG> to decide about the Constitutional validity of provisions of statutes.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the provisions of the statute are found to be violative of any article of the Constitution, which is the touchstone for the validity of all laws, <ORG> and <ORG> are empowered to strike down the said provisions.
RatioOfTheDecision	The one sphere where there is no judicial  review for finding out whether there has been infraction of the provisions of Part III and there is no power of striking down an Act, regulation or provision even though it may be inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Part III of the Constitution is that incorporated in Article 31B taken along with the Ninth Schedule.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31B was inserted, as mentioned earlier, by the Constitution (First Amendment) Act.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to Article 31B , none of the Acts and regulations specified in the Ninth Schedule nor any of the provisions thereof shall be deemed to be void or ever to have become void on the ground that such Act, regulation or provision is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by any provision of Part III of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The one thing significant to be noted in this connection, however, is that the power under Article 31B of exclusion of judicial review, which might be undertaken for the purpose of finding whether there has been contr avention of any provision of Part III, is exercised not by the legislature enacting the impugned law but by the authority which makes the Constitutional amendment under Article 368 , viz., the prescribed majority in each <ORG> of Parliament.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such a power is exercised in respect of an existing statute of which the provisions can be scrutinized before it is placed in the Ninth Schedule.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is for the prescribed majority in each <ORG> to decide whether the particular statute should be placed in the Ninth Schedule, and if so, whether it should be placed there in its entirety or partly.
RatioOfTheDecision	As against that, the position under Article 31C is that though judicial review has been excluded by the authority making the Constitutional amendment, the law in respect of which the judicial review has been excluded is one yet to be passed by the legislatures.
RatioOfTheDecision	Although the object for which such a law can be enacted has been specified in Article 31C , the power to decide as to whether the law enacted is for the attainment of that o bject has been vested not in the courts but in the very legislature which passes the law.
RatioOfTheDecision	The vice of Article 31C is that even if the law enacted is not for the object mentioned in Article 31C , the declaration made by the legislature precludes a party from showing that the law is not for that object and prevents a court from going into the question as to whether the law enacted is really for that object.
RatioOfTheDecision	The kind of limited judicial review which is permissible under Article 31A for the purpose of finding as to whether the law enacted is for the purpose mentioned in Article 31A has also been done away with under Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	The effect of the declaration mentioned in Article 31C is to grant protection to the law enacted by a legislature from being challenged on grounds of contravention of Articles 14, 19 and 31 even though such a law can be shown in the court to have not been enacted for the objects mentioned in Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution postulates Rule of Law in the sense of supremacy of the Constitution  and the laws as opposed to arbitrariness.
RatioOfTheDecision	The vesting of power of exclusion of judicial review in a legislature, including <ORG> legislature, contemplated by Article 31C , in my opinion strikes at the basis structure of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The second part of Article 31C thus goes beyond the permissible limit of what constitutes amendment under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	1591.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has been argued on behalf of the respondents that the declaration referred to in Article 31C would not preclude the court from finding whether a law is for giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clauses (b) and (c) of Article 39 and that if an enactment is found by the court to be not for securing the aforesaid objectives, the protection of Article 31C would not be available for such legislation.
RatioOfTheDecision	1592.
RatioOfTheDecision	I find it difficult to accede to this contention in view of the language of Article 31C pertaining to the declaration.
RatioOfTheDecision	The above contention would have certainly carried weight if the second part of the a rticle relating to the declaration were not there.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the absence of the declaration in question, it would be open to, and indeed necessary, for the court to find whether the impugned law is for giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clauses (b) or (c) of Article 39 before it can uphold the validity of the impugned law under Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	Once, however, a law contains such a declaration, the declaration would stand as bar and it would not be permissible for the court to find whether the impugned law is for giving effect to the policy mentioned in Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31C protects the law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clauses (b) or (c) of Article 39 and at the same time provides that no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is, therefore, manifest that once a law contains  the requisite declaration, the court would be precluded from going into the question that the law does not give effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clauses (b) or (c) of Article 39.
RatioOfTheDecision	In view of the conclusive nature of the declaration, it would, in my opinion, be straining the language of Article 31C to hold that a court can despite the requisite declaration go into the question that it does not give effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clauses (b) or (c) of Article 39.
RatioOfTheDecision	The result is that if a law contains the declaration contemplated by Article 31C , it would have complete protection from being challenged on the ground of being violative of Articles 14, 19 and 31 of the Constitution, irrespective of the fact whether the law is or is not for giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clauses (b) or (c) of Article 39.
RatioOfTheDecision	To put it in other words, even those laws which do not give effect t o the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clauses  (b) or (c) of Article 39 would also have the protection if they contain the declaration mentioned in Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	1593.
RatioOfTheDecision	I am also of the view that the validity of the latter part of Article 31C relating to declaration cannot be decided on the basis of any concession made during the course of arguments on behalf of the respondents.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such a concession if not warranted by the language of the impugned provision, cannot be of much avail.
RatioOfTheDecision	Matters relating to construction of an article of the Constitution or the Constitutional validity of an impugned provision have to be decided in the light of the relevant provisions and a concession made by the <ORG> counsel or the opposite counsel would not absolve the court from determining the matter independently of the concession.
RatioOfTheDecision	A counsel may sometimes make a concession in order to secure favourable verdict on an other important point, such a concession would, however, not be binding upon anoth er counsel.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is well-settled that admission or concession made on a point of law by the counsel is not binding upon the party represented by the counsel, far less would such admission or concession preclude other parties from showing that the concession was erroneous and not justified in law.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may, therefore, be laid down as a broad proposition that Constitutional matters cannot be disposed of in terms of agreement or compromise between the parties, nor can the decision in such disputes in order to be binding upon others be based upon a concession even though the concession emanates from the <ORG> counsel.
RatioOfTheDecision	The concession has to be made good and justified in the light of the relevant provisions.
RatioOfTheDecision	1594.
RatioOfTheDecision	The position as it emerges is that it is open to the authority amending the Constitution to exclude judicial review regarding the validity of an existing statute.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is likewise open to the said authority to exclude judicial review regarding the validity of a staute which might be enacted by the legislature i n future in respect of a specified subject.
RatioOfTheDecision	In such an event, judicial review is not excluded for finding whether the statute has been enacted in respect of the spcified subject Both the above types of Constitutional amendments are permissible under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	What is not permissible, however, is a third type of Constitutional amendment, according to which the amending authority not merely excludes judicial review regarding the validity of a statute which might be enacted by the legislature in future in respect of a specified subject but also excludes judicial review for finding whether the statute enacted by the legislature is in respect of the subject for which judicial review has been excluded.
RatioOfTheDecision	1595.
RatioOfTheDecision	In exercising the power of judicial review, it may be mentioned that the courts do not and cannot go into the question of wisdom behind a legislative measure.
RatioOfTheDecision	The policy decisions have essential to be those of the legislatures.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is for the legislatures to decide as to what laws they should enact and bring  on the statute book.
RatioOfTheDecision	The task of the courts is to interpret the laws and to adjudicate about their validity, they neither approve nor disapprove legislative policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	The office of the courts is to ascertain and declare whether the impugned legislation is in consonance with or in violation of the provisions of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Once the courts have done that, their duty ends.
RatioOfTheDecision	The courts do not act as super legislature to suppress what they deem to be unwise legislation for if they were to do so the courts will divert criticism from the legislative door where it belongs and will thus dilute the responsibility of the elected representatives of the people.
RatioOfTheDecision	As was observed by <NAME> in speech in <ORG> on September 12, 1949 "The <ORG> may act wisely or unwisely.
RatioOfTheDecision	The principles formulated by the <ORG> may commend themselves to a <ORG> or they may not.
RatioOfTheDecision	The province of the <ORG> is normally to administer the law as enacted by the <ORG> within the limits of it s power".
RatioOfTheDecision	1596.
RatioOfTheDecision	In exercising the power of judicial review, the courts cannot be oblivious of the practical needs of the government.
RatioOfTheDecision	The door has to be left open for trial and error.
RatioOfTheDecision	Constitutional law like other mortal contrivances has to take some chances.
RatioOfTheDecision	Opportunity must be allowed for vindicating reasonable belief by experience.
RatioOfTheDecision	Judicial review is not intended to create what is sometimes called Judicial Oligarchy, the the Aristrocracy of the Robe, Covert Legislation, or Judge-made law.
RatioOfTheDecision	The proper forum to fight for the wise use of the legislative authority is that of public opinion and legislative assemblies.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such contest cannot be transferred to the judicial arena.
RatioOfTheDecision	That all Constitutional interpretations have political consequences should not obliterate the fact that the decision has to be arrived at in the calm and dispassionate atmosphere of the court room, that judges in order to give legitimacy to their decision have to keep aloof from the din and controversy of politics and that the fluctuating fo rtunes of rival political parties can have for them only academic interest.
RatioOfTheDecision	Their primary duty is to uphold the Constitution and the laws without fear or favour and in doing so, they cannot allow any political ideology or economic theory, which may have caught their fancy, to colour the decision.
RatioOfTheDecision	The sobering reflection has always to be there that the Constitution is meant not merely for people of their way of thinking but for people of fundamentally differing views.
RatioOfTheDecision	As observed by Justice <NAME> while dealing with the Fourteenth Amendment to the US Constitution:  The Fourteenth Amendment does not enact Mr. <NAME> Social Statics....
RatioOfTheDecision	Some of these laws embody convictions or prejudices which judges are likely to share.
RatioOfTheDecision	Some may not But a Constitution is not intended to embody a particular economic theory, whether of paternalism and the organic relation of the citizen to the <ORG> or of laissez faire.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is made for people of fundamentally differing views, and the accident of our finding certain opini ons natural and familiar or novel and even shocking ought not to conclude our judgment upon the question whether statutes embodying them conflict with the Constitution of the United States.
RatioOfTheDecision	(see Mr. Justice <NAME>, p. 82-83 (1931 Edition).
RatioOfTheDecision	It would also be pertinent in this context to reproduce the words of <NAME> in the case of <CITATION_554839>  S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	597 while dealing with reasonable restrictions:  In evaluating such elusive factors and forming their own conception of what is reasonable, in all the circumstances of a given case, it is inevitable that the social philosophy and the scale of values of the judges participating in the decision should play an important part, and the limit to their interference with legislative judgment in such cases can only be dictated by their sense of responsibility and self-restraint and the sobering reflection that the Constitution is meant not only for people of their way of thinking but for all, and that the majority of the elected representatives of the  people have, in authorising the imposition of the restrictions, considered them to be reasonable.
RatioOfTheDecision	1597.
RatioOfTheDecision	In my opinion, the second part of Article 31C is liable to be quashed on the following grounds:  (1) It gives a carte blanche to the <ORG> to make any law violative of Articles 14, 19 and 31 and make it immune from attack by inserting the requisite declaration.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31C taken along with its second part gives in effect the power to the <ORG>, including a State <ORG>, to amend the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	(2) The legislature has been made the final authority to decide as to whether the law made by it is for the objects mentioned in Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	The vice of second part of Article 31C lies in the fact that even if the law enacted is not for the object mentioned in Article 31C , the declaration made by the <ORG> precludes a party from showing that the law is not for that object and prevents a court from going into the question as to whether the law enacted is really for that object.
RatioOfTheDecision	The exclusi on by the <ORG>, including a State <ORG>, of even that limited judicial review strikes at the basic structure of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The second part of Article 31C goes beyond the permissible limit of what constitutes amendment under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The second part of Article 31C can be severed from the remaining part of Article 31C and its invalidity would not affect the validity of the remaining part.
RatioOfTheDecision	I would, therefore, strike down the following words in Article 31C:   and no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	1598.
RatioOfTheDecision	We may now deal with the Constitution (Twentyninth Amendment) Act.
RatioOfTheDecision	This Act , as mentioned earlier, inserted the Kerala Act 35 of 1969 and the Kerala Act 25 of 1971 as entries No.
RatioOfTheDecision	65 and 66 in the Ninth Schedule to the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	I have been able to find no infirmity in the Constitution (Twentyninth Amendment) Act.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may be mentioned that an arg ument was advanced before us that Articles 31B and 31A are linked together and that only those enactments can be placed in the Ninth Schedule as fall within the ambit of Article 31A.
Precedent	Such a contention was advanced in the case of <CITATION_1681028>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	636.
Precedent	Repelling the contention <NAME> (as he then was) speaking for the Constitution Bench of this Court observed: The learned Attorney-General contended that Articles 31-A and Article 31-B should be read together and that if so read Article 31-B would only illustrate cases that would otherwise fall under Article 31-A and, therefore, the same construction as put upon Article 31-B should also apply to Article 31-A of the Constitution.
Precedent	This construction was sought to be based upon the opening words of Article 31-B , namely, 'without prejudice to the generality of the provisions contained in Article 31-A.
RatioOfTheDecision	We find it difficult to accept this argument.
RatioOfTheDecision	The words 'without prejudice to the generality of the provisions', indicate that the Acts and regulations  specified in the Ninth Schedule would have the immunity even if they did not attract Article 31-A of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	If every Act in the Ninth Schedule would be covered by Article 31-A , this article would become redundant.
RatioOfTheDecision	Indeed, some of the Acts mentioned therein, namely, items 14 to 20 and many other Acts added to the Ninth Schedule, do not appear to relate to estates as defined in Article 31-A(2) of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	We, therefore, hold that Article 31-B is not governed by Article 31-A and that Article 31-B is a Constitutional device to place the specified statutes beyond any attack on the ground that they infringe Part III of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	I see no cogent ground to take a different view.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the result I uphold the validity of the Constitution (Twentyninth Amendment) Act.
Precedent	1599.
Precedent	I may now sum up my conclusions relating to power of amendment under Article 368 of the Constitution as it existed before the amendment made by the Constitution (Twentyfourth Amendment) Act as well as about the validity o f the Constitution (Twentyfourth Amendment) Act, the Constitution (Twentyfifth Amendment) Act and the Constitution (Twentyninth Amendment) Act:  (i) Article 368 contains not only the procedure for the amendment of the Constitution but also confers the power of amending the Constitution.
Precedent	(ii) Entry 97 in List I of the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution does not cover the subject of amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	(iii) The word "law" in Article 13(2) does not include amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	It has reference to ordinary piece of legislation.
Precedent	It would also in view of the definition contained in Clause (a) of Article 13(3) include an ordinance, order, bye-law, rule, regulation, notification, custom or usage having in the territory of India the force of law.
Precedent	(iv) Provision for amendment of the Constitution is made with a view to overcome the difficulties which may be encountered in future in the working of the Constitution.
Precedent	No generation has a monopoly of wisdom nor has it a right to place fetters on fu ture generations to mould the machinery of governments.
Precedent	If no provision were made for amendment of the Constitution, the people would have recourse to extra-Constitutional method like revolution to change the Constitution.
Precedent	(v) Argument that <ORG> can enact legislation under entry 97 List I of Seventh Schedule for convening <ORG> or holding a referendum for the purpose of amendment of Part III of the Constitution so as to take away or abridge fundamental rights is untenable.
Precedent	There is no warrant for the proposition that as the amendments under Article 368 are not brought about through referendum or passed in a <ORG> the power of amendment under Article 368 is on that account subject to limitations.
Precedent	(vi) The possibility that power of amendment may be abused furnishes no ground for denial of its existence.
Precedent	The best safeguard against abuse of power is public opinion and the good sense of the majority of the members of <ORG>, It is also not correct to assume that if <ORG> is h eld entitled to amend Part III of the Constitution, it would automatically and necessarily result in abrogation of all fundamental rights.
Precedent	(vii) The power of amendment under Article 368 does not include power to abrogate the Constitution nor does it include the power to alter the basic structure or framework of the Constitution.
Precedent	Subject to the retention of the basic structure or framework of the Constitution, the power of amendment is plenary and includes within itself the power to amend the various articles of the Constitution, including those relating to fundamental rights as well as those which may be said to relate to essential features.
Precedent	No part of a fundamental right can claim immuniy from amendatory process by being described as the essence or core of that right.
Precedent	The power of amendment would also include within itself the power to add, alter or repeal the various articles.
Precedent	(viii) Right to property does not pertain to basic structure or framework of the Constitution.
Precedent	(ix) There are no implied or inher ent limitations on the power of amendment apart from those which inhere and are implicit in the word "amendment".
Precedent	The said power can also be not restricted by reference to natural or human rights.
Precedent	Such rights in order to be enforceable in a court of law must become a part of the statute or the Constitution.
Precedent	(x) Apart from the part of the Preamble which relates to the basic structure or framework of the Constitution, the Preamble does not restrict the power of amendment.
Precedent	(xi) The Constitution (Twentyfourth Amendment) Act does not suffer from any infirmity and as such is valid.
Precedent	(xii) The amendment made in Article 31 by the Constitution (Twentyfifth Amendment) Act is valid.
Precedent	(xiii) The first part of Article 31C introduced by the Constitution (Twentyfifth Amendment) Act is valid.
Precedent	The said part is as under.
Precedent	31C.
Precedent	Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 , no law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 shall be deemed to  be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with, or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Article 14 , Article 19 or Article 31:   Provided that where such law is made by the <ORG> of a State, the provisions of the article shall not apply thereto unless such law, having been reserved for the consideration of the President, has received his assent.
Precedent	(xiv) The second part of Article 31C contains the seed of national disintegration and is invalid on the following two grounds:  (1) It gives a carte blanche to the <ORG> to make any law violative of Articles 14, 19 and 31 and make it immune from attack by inserting the requisite declaration.
Precedent	Article 31C taken along with its second part gives in effect the power to the <ORG>, including a State <ORG>, to amend the Constitution in important respects.
Precedent	(2) The legislature has been made the final authority to decide as to whether the law made by it is for objects mentioned in Article 31C.
Precedent	The vice of second part of Article 31C  lies in the fact that even if the law enacted is not for the object mentioned in Article 31C , the declaration made by the <ORG> precludes a party from showing that the law is not for that object and prevents a court from going into the question as to whether the law enacted is really for that object.
Precedent	The exclusion by <ORG>, including a State <ORG>, of even that limited judicial review strikes at the basic structure of the Constitution.
Precedent	The second part of Article 31C goes beyond the permissible limit of what constitutes amendment under Article 368.
Precedent	The second part of Article 31C can be severed from the remaining part of Article 31C and its invalidity would not affect the validity of remaining part 1 would, therefore, strike down the following words in Article 31C:   and no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy.
Precedent	(xv) The Constitution (Twentyninth Amendment)  Act does not suffer from any infirmity and as such is valid.
Precedent	1600.
Precedent	The petition shall now be posted for hearing before <ORG> for disposal in the light of our findings.
Precedent	<NAME>, J.
Precedent	1601.
Precedent	In the cases before us, the Constitution of our country, in its most vital parts has to be considered and an opinion expressed which may essentially influence the destiny of the country.
Precedent	It is difficult to approach the question without a deep sense of its importance and of the awesome responsibility involved in its resolution.
Precedent	1602.
Precedent	I entertain little doubt that in important cases it is desirable for the future development of the law that there should be plurality of opinions even if the conclusion reached is the same.
Precedent	There are dangers in there being only one opinion.
Precedent	"Then the statements in it have tended to be treated as definitions and it is not the function of a <ORG> to frame definitions.
Precedent	Some latitude should be left for future developments.
Precedent	The true ratio of a decision generally appears more clea rly from a comparison of two or more statements in different words which are intended to supplement each other" see Lord <NAME> in <NAME> v. <NAME>, [1970] 3 W.L.R.
Precedent	1078.
Precedent	<CITATION_1419344> .
Precedent	1 All E.R.
Precedent	801, 821, Lord Chancellor Lord <NAME> said that Lord <NAME>'s statement of the law in <NAME> v. <NAME>  1 All E.R.
Precedent	367 has been misunderstood particulary by his critics and that the view of <ORG> has suffered to some extent from the fact that its reasons were given in a single speech and that whatever might be the advantages of a judgment delivered by one voice, the result may be an unduly fundamentalist approach to the actual language employed.
Precedent	In <NAME> v. New York 306 U.S. 466.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. in his concurring judgment, characterised the expression of individual opinions by the justices as a healthy practice rendered impossible only by the increasing volume of the business of the <ORG>.
Precedent	1603.
Precedent	As the arguments were addressed mainly in Writ Petition No.
Precedent	135/1970, I will deal with it now.
Precedent	In this  writ petition the petitioner challenged the validity of the Kerala Land Reforms Amendment Act, 1969, and the Kerala Land Reforms Amendment Act, 1971, for the reason that some of the provisions thereof violated Article 14 , 19(1)(f ), 25 , 26 and 31 of the Constitution.
Precedent	1604.
Precedent	During the pendency of the Writ Petition, <ORG> under the Constitution passed three Constitutional amendments, namely, the Constitution 24th, 25th and 29th Amendment Act s.  1605.
Precedent	The 24th Amendment made certain changes in Article 368 to make it clear that the <ORG>, in the exercise of its constituent power, has competence to amend by way of addition, variation or repeal, any of the provisions of the Constitution in accordance with the procedure laid down in the article and that Article 13(2) would not be a bar to any such amendment.
Precedent	By the 25th Amendment, the word 'amount' was substituted for the word 'compensation' in Clause (2) of Article 31.
Precedent	That was done in order to make it clear that the law for acquisition or req uisition of the property need only fix an amount or lay down the principles for determining the amount and not the just equivalent in money of the market value of the property acquired or requisitioned.
Precedent	The Amendment also makes it clear that no such law shall be called in question in any Court on the ground that the whole or any part of such amount is to be given otherwise than in cash.
Precedent	The 29th Amendment put the two Acts in question, viz., the Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act, 1969, and the Kerala Land Reforms (Amendment) Act, 1971, in the Ninth Schedule with a view to make the provisions thereof immune from attack on the ground that the Acts or the provisions thereof violate any of the Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	1606.
Fact	The petitioner challenges the validity of these Amendments.
Fact	1607.
Fact	As the validity of the 25th and the 29th Amendments essentially depends upon the validity of the 24th Amendment, it is necessary to consider and decide that question first.
Fact	I, therefore, torn to the circumstances which necessitated  the Constitutional 24th Amendment Act .
Fact	1608.
Fact	The Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951, was passed by <ORG> on June 18, 1951.
Fact	Sections 2 , 3 and 4 of the Act made amendments in some of the articles in Part III of the Constitution.
Fact	The validity of the Amendment was challenged before this <ORG> in <CITATION_1706770>  S.C.R.
Fact	89, and one of the questions which fell for decision was whether, in view of Clause 2 of Article 13 , <ORG> had power to amend the Fundamental Rights in such a way as to take away or abridge them.
Fact	And the argument was that the word "State" in Clause 2 of Article 13 includes <ORG> and the word 'law' would take in an amendment of the Constitution and, therefore, <ORG> had no power to pass a law amending the Constitution in such a way as to take away or abridge the Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. who delivered the judgment of the <ORG> said that although the word 'law' would ordinarily include Constitutional law, there is a distinction between ordinary law made  in the exercise of legislative power and Constitutional law made in the exercise of constituent power and that in the context of Clause 2 of Article 13 , the word 'law' would not include an amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	1609.
Precedent	This decision was followed in <CITATION_1308308>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	933.
Precedent	There, <NAME>, C.J., speaking for himself and two of his colleagues, substantially agreed with the reasoning of <NAME> J. in <CITATION_1706770>  S.C.R.
Precedent	89.
Precedent	<NAME> and <NAME>, JJ.
Precedent	expressed certain doubts as to whether Fundamental Rights could be abridged or taken away by amendment of the Constitution under Article 368.
Precedent	1610.
Precedent	The question again came up before this <ORG> in <CITATION_120358>  2 S.C.R.
Precedent	762, hereinafter called 'Golaknath Case' where the validity of the 17th Amendment was challenged on much the same grounds.
Precedent	The majority constituting the <ORG> decided that <ORG> has no power to amend the Fundamental Rights in such a way as to take away or abridge them, but that the 1st, 4th  and 17th Amendments were valid for all time on the basis of the doctrine of prospective overruling and that the Acts impugned in the case were protected by the Amendments.
Precedent	1611.
Precedent	The reasoning of the leading majority (<NAME>, <NAME>, and the colleagues who concurred in the judgment pronounced by him) was that Article 368 , as it stood then, did not confer the substantive power to amend the provisions of the Constitution but only prescribed the procedure for the same that the substantive power to amend is in Articles 245, 246 and 248 read with entry 97 of List I of the Seventh Schedule, that there is no distinction between a law amending the Constitution and an ordinary law passed in the exercise of the legislative power of <ORG> and that the word law' in Clause 2 of Article 13 would include an amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	1612.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME>, J. who wrote a separate judgment concurring with the conclusion of the leading majority, however, took the view that Article 368 conferred the substantive power  to amend the Constitution but that Fundamental Rights cannot be amended under the article so as to take away or abridge them.
RatioOfTheDecision	He said that there is no distinction between Constitutional law and ordinary law, that both are laws that the Constitution limited the powers of the Government but not the sovereignty of the <ORG>, that the <ORG> can, in the exercise of its supremacy, put a limit on its supremacy, echoing in effect the view that there could be 'auto-limitation' by a sovereign of his own supreme power and that, by Clause 2 of Article 13 , the <ORG> and all its agencies, including <ORG>, were prohibited from making any law, including a law amending the Constitution, in such a way as to take away or abridge the Fundamental Rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	1613.
RatioOfTheDecision	Let me first take up the question whether Article 368 as it stood before the 24th Amendment gave power to <ORG> to amend the rights conferred by Part III in such a way as to take away or abridge them.
RatioOfTheDecision	1614.
Precedent	In Golaknath Case [1967] 2 S.C.R.
Precedent	762, Hidayatu <NAME>, J. said that it is difficult to take a narrow view of the word 'amendment' as including only minor changes within the general framework, that by an amendment, new matter may be added, old matter removed or altered, and that except two dozen articles in Part III, all the provisions of the Constitution could be amended.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. speaking for the leading minority in that case was of the view that the word 'amendment' in its setting in the article was of the widest amplitude and that any provision of the Constitution could be amended.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME>, J. was also inclined to give the widest meaning to the word.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME>, J. did not specifically advert to the point, but it seems clear from the tenor of his judgment that he was also of the same view.
RatioOfTheDecision	1615.
RatioOfTheDecision	Mr. <NAME> for the petitioner contended that the word 'amendment' in the article could only mean a change with a view to make improvement; that in the context, the term connoted only power to make such changes as were consistent with the nature and purpose  of the Constitution, that the basic structure and essential features of the Constitution cannot be changed by amendment, and that the assumption made by these judges that the word 'amendment' in the article was wide enough to make any change by way of alteration, addition or repeal of any of the provisions of the Constitution was unwarranted.
RatioOfTheDecision	He said that the article was silent as regards the subject matter in respect of which amendments could be made or the extent and the width thereof, that it was set in a low key as it did not contain the words "amend by way of addition, variation or repeal", that these circumstances should make one pause before ascribing to the word 'amendment' its widest meaning and that, in the context, the word has only a limited meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	1616.
RatioOfTheDecision	I do not think that there is any substance in this contention.
RatioOfTheDecision	1617.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the Oxford English Dictionary, the meanings of the word 'amend' are given as: to make professed improvements (in a measure before <ORG>); formally to alter in detail,  though practically it may be to alter its principle so as to thwart it.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to "Standard Dictionary", <NAME> and <NAME> (1894), the meanings of 'amendment' are:  The act of changing a fundamental law, as of political Constitution, or any change made in it according to a prescribed mode of procedure; as, to alter the law by amendment; an amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	1618.
RatioOfTheDecision	The proviso to Article 368 used the expression 'change' and that could indicate that the term 'amend' really means 'change'.
RatioOfTheDecision	The main part of Article 368 thus gave power to amend or to make changes in the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Normally, a change is made with the object of making an improvement; at any rate, that is the professed object with which an amendment is sought to be made.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fact that the object may not be achieved is beside the point.
Precedent	Amendment contains in it an element of euphemism of conceit in the proposer, an assumption that the proposal is an improvement.
Precedent	Beyond this euphemistic things, amendment as applied to alteration of  laws according to dictionaries means 'alter' or 'change' see <NAME>, "Is the Eighteenth Amendment Void Because of its Contents?"
Precedent	<ORG>, Vol.
Precedent	20.
Precedent	1619.
Precedent	In the National Prohibition Cases Rhode Island v. <NAME> 253 U.S. 350, it was argued before <ORG> that an amendment under Article V of the United States Constitution must be confined in its scope to an alteration or improvement of that which is already contained in the Constitution and cannot change its basic features but this argument was overruled.
Precedent	1620.
Precedent	In <NAME>'s Case The State (At the Prosecution of <NAME> and <NAME>.
Precedent	v. Captain <NAME> and <NAME>.
Precedent	(1935) Irish Reports 173 <ORG> held by a majority that the word 'amendment' occurring in Article 50 of the Irish Constitution was of the widest amplitude.
Precedent	<NAME> observed after reading the various meanings of the word 'amendment' that the word as it occurred in a Constitution Act must be given its widest meaning.
Precedent	<NAME>, J. ob served that although complete abolition of the Constitution without any substituted provisions might not properly be called in law an 'amendment', the word is wide enough to allow of the repeal of any number of articles of the Constitution, however important they might be.
Precedent	<NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	did not specifically deal with the meaning of the word.
Precedent	1621.
Precedent	In this context it is relevant to keep in mind the general rules of construction for interpreting a word like 'amendment' occurring in a constituent Act like the Constitution of India.
Precedent	1622.
Precedent	In In Re the Central Provinces and Berar Sales of Motor Spirit and Lubricants Taxation Act, 1938, etc (1939) F.C.R.
Precedent	18.
Precedent	Sir <NAME> said that a broad and liberal spirit should inspire those whose duty it is to interpret a Constitution, that a <ORG> should avoid a narrow and pedantic approach and that when a power is granted without any restriction, it can be qualified only by some express provision or by scheme of the instrument.
Precedent	1623.
Precedent	The basic principles of constructi on were definitively enunciated by <ORG> in The Queen v. <NAME> (1878) 3 A.C. 889, 904-905 and those principles were accepted and applied by <NAME> in Attorney General for Ontario v. Attorney General for Canada (1912) A.C. 572 at 583 Lord <NAME> said in the former case that the question whether the prescribed limits of a power have been exceeded has to be decided by looking to the terms of the instrument by which, affirmatively, the power was created, and by which, negatively, it is restricted and that if what has been done is within the general scope of the affirmative words which give the power, and if it violates no express condition of restriction by which that power is limited, it is not for any court of justice to inquire further, or to enlarge constructively those conditions and restrictions.
Precedent	In other words, in interpreting a Constitution, as Lord <NAME> said in the latter case, if the text is explicit, the text is conclusive alike in what it directs and what it prohibits.
Precedent	1624.
Precedent	I  should think that in such matters everything turns upon the spirit in which a judge approaches the question before him.
Precedent	The words must construe are, generally speaking, mere vessels in which he can pour nearly anything he will.
Precedent	"Men do not gather figs of thistles, nor supply institutions from judges whose outlook is limited by parish or class.
Precedent	They must be aware that there are before them more than Verbal problems; more than final solutions cast in generalisations in every society which make it an organism; which demand new schemata of adaptation; which will disrupt it, if rigidly confined" See the passage of <NAME> quoted in "Cases and Materials on the Legal Process" by <NAME> and <NAME>., 2nd ed., p. 498.
Precedent	An this is why President <NAME> said that the judges of <ORG> must be not only great justices, but they must be great constructive :statesmen See the passage quoted by <NAME> in <ORG>, p. 338.
Precedent	1625.
Precedent	Therefore, although the word 'amendment' has a variety  of meanings, we have to ascribe to it in the article a meaning which is appropriate to the function to be played by it in an instrument apparently intended to endure for ages to come and to meet the various crises to which the body politic will be subject.
Precedent	The nature of that instrument demands awareness of certain presupposition.
Precedent	The Constitution has no doubt its roots in the past but was designed primarily for the unknown future.
Precedent	The reach of this consideration was indicated by Justice <NAME> in language that remains fresh no matter how often repeated : Missouri v. <NAME> 252 U.S. 416, 433 ...when we are dealing with words that also are a constituent act, like the Constitution of the United States, we must realize that they have called into life a being the development of which could not have been foreseen completely by the most gifted of its begetters....  1626.
Precedent	Every well drawn Constitution will therefore provide for its own amendment in such a way as to forestall as is humanly possible, all revolutionary  upheavals See <NAME>, "Constitutional Government and Democracy", p. 135.
Precedent	That the Constitution is a framework of great governmental power to be exercised for great public ends in the future, is not a pale intellectual concept but a dynamic idea which must dominate in any consideration of the width of the amending power.
Precedent	No existing Constitution has reached its final form and shape and become, as it were a fixed thing incapable of further growth.
Precedent	Human societies keep changing; needs emerge, first vaguely felt and unexpressed, imperceptibly gathering strength, steadily becoming more and more exigent, generating a force which, if left unheeded and denied response so as to satisfy the impulse behind it, may burst forth with an intensity that exacts more than reasonable satisfaction See <NAME>, "Of Law and Men", p. 35.
Precedent	As <NAME> said, a living Constitution must be Darwinian in structure and practice See Constitutional Government in the United States, p. 25.
Precedent	The Constitution of a nation is th e outward and visible manifestation of the life of the people and it must respond to the deep pulsation for change within.
Precedent	"A Constitution is an experiment as all life is an experiment."
Precedent	See Justice <NAME> in <NAME> v. United States 250 U.S. 616.
Precedent	If the experiment fails, there must be provision for making another.
Precedent	<NAME> said that there is nothing sanctimonious about a Constitution and that nobody should regard it as the ark of the covenant, too sacred to be touched.
Precedent	Nor need we ascribe to men of preceding age, a wisdom more than human and suppose that what they did should be beyond amendment.
Precedent	A Constitution is not end in itself, rather a means for ordering the life of a nation.
Precedent	The generation of yesterday might not know the needs of today, and, 'if yesterday is not to paralyse today', it seems best to permit each generation to take care of itself.
Precedent	The sentiment expressed by <NAME> in this behalf was echoed by Dr. Ambedkar Constitution Assembly Debates, Vol.
Precedent	X, pp.
Precedent	296-297.
Precedent	If there is one sure conclusi on which I can draw from this speech of Dr. <NAME>, it is this : He could not have conceived of any limitation upon the amending power.
Precedent	How could he have said that what <NAME> said is "not merely true, but absolutely true", unless he subscribed to the view of <NAME> that "each generation as a distinct nation with a right, by the will of the majority to bind themselves but none to bind the succeeding generations more than the inhabitants of another country", and its corrollary which follows as 'the night the day' that each generation should have the power to determine the structure of the Constitution under which they live.
Precedent	And how could this be done unless the power of amendment is plenary, for it would be absurd to think that Dr. <NAME> contemplated a revolution in every generation for changing the Constitution to suit its needs and aspirations.
Precedent	I should have thought that if there is any implied limitation upon any power, that limitation is that the amending body should not limit power of amendment  of the future generation by exercising its power to amend the amending power.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> said that if the power of amendment of the amending power is plenary, one generation can, by exercising that power, take away the power of amendment of the Constitution from the future generations and foreclose them from ever exercising it.
Precedent	I think the argument is too speculative to be countenanced.
Precedent	It is just like the argument that if men and women are given the freedom to choose their vocations in life, they would all jump into a monastery or a nunnery, as the case may be, and prevent the birth of a new generation; or the argument of some political thinkers that if freedom of speech is allowed to those who do not believe in it, they would themselves deny it to others when they get power and, therefore, they should be denied that freedom today, in order that they might not deny it to others tomorrow.
Precedent	1627.
Precedent	Seeing, therefore, that it is a "Constitution that we are expounding" and that the Constitution-makers had bef ore them several Constitutions where the word 'amendment' or 'alteration' is used to denote plenary power to change the fundamentals of the Constitution, I cannot approach the construction of the word 'amendment' in Article 368 in niggardly or petty fogging spirit and give it a narrow meaning; but "being a familiar expression, it was used in its familiar legal sense" See Justice <NAME> in <NAME> v. United States 251 U.S. 293, 295.
Precedent	1628.
Precedent	However, Mr. <NAME> contended that there are provisions in the Constitution which would militate against giving the word 'amendment' a wide meaning in the article and he referred to the wording in Schedule V, para 7(1) and Schedule VI, para 21(1).
Argument	These paragraphs use along with the word 'amend', the expression "by way of addition, variation or repeal".
Argument	Counsel said that these words were chosen to indicate the plenitude of the power of amendment and that this is in sharp contrast with the wording of Article 368 where only the word 'amendment' was used.
Precedent	But Schedule V, para  7(2) and Schedule VI, para 21(2) themselves indicate that, but for these provisions, an amendment of the schedule by way of addition, variation or repeal would be an amendment of the Constitution under Article 368.
Precedent	In other words, the sub-paragraphs show clearly that the expression "amend by way of addition, variation or repeal" in para 7(1) of Schedule V and para 21(1) of Schedule VI has the same content as the word 'amendment' in Article  368.
Precedent	1629.
Precedent	Reliance was also placed by counsel on Section 291 of the Government of India Act, 1935, as amended by the Third Amendment Act 1949, which provided that "such amendments as he considers necessary whether by way of addition, modification or repeal in the Act".
Precedent	No inference can be drawn from the use of these words as to the meaning to be assigned to the word 'amendment' in Article 368 or its width as it is well known that draftsmen use different words to indicate the same idea for the purpose of elegance or what is called "the graces of style" or their wish to av oid the same word, or sometimes by the circumstance that the Act has been compiled from different sources and sometimes by alteration and addition from various hands which the Acts undergo in their progress in <ORG> See Maxwell on the Interpretation of Statutes, 12th ed., p. 286.
Precedent	1630.
Precedent	It was submitted that if the word 'amendment' is given an unlimited amplitude, the entire Constitution could be abrogated or repealed and that certainly could not have been the intention of the makers of the Constitution.
Precedent	The question whether the power of amendment contained in Article 368 as it stood before the amendment went to the extent of completely abrogating the Constitution and substituting it by an entirely new one in its place is not beyond doubt I think that the power to amend under that article included the power to add any provision to the Constitution, to alter any provision, substitute any other provision in its place and to delete any provision.
Precedent	But when the article said that, on the bill for the amendment  of the Constitution receiving the President's assent, "the Constitution shall stand amended", it seems to be fairly clear that a simple repeal or abrogation of the Constitution without substituting anything in the place of the repealed Constitution would be beyond the scope of the amending power, for, if a Constitution were simply repealed, it would not stand amended.
Precedent	An amendment which brings about a radical change in the Constitution like introducing presidential system of government for cabinet system, or, a monachy for a republic, would not be an abrogation or repeal of the Constitution.
Precedent	However radical the change might be, after the amendment, there must exist a system by which the <ORG> is constituted or organised.
Precedent	As already stated, a simple repeal or abrogation without more, would be contrary to the terms of Article 368 because it would violate the Constitutional provision that "the Constitution shall stand amended".
Precedent	1631.
