Precedent	<ORG> fixes either the compensation itself or the principles governing that compensation and they should not be challenged except for one reason, where it is thought that there has been a gross abuse of the law, where in fact there has been a fraud on the Constitution.
Precedent	1800.
Precedent	<NAME> (who spoke in <ORG> on the draft Article 24 on September 12, 1949, observed : Co nstituent Assembly Debates, Vol.
Precedent	IX, p. 1299.
Precedent	We find on the English Statute Book several Acts, the Land Acquisition Act , the Land Clauses Act, the Housing Act , in all of which a varying basis of compensation has been adopted to suit not only to the nature of the property but also the purpose for which it is to be acquired.
Precedent	<ORG> therefore is the judge and master of deciding what principles to apply in each case.
Precedent	1801.
Precedent	<CITATION_1890860> (1954) S.C.R.
Precedent	pp.
Precedent	558, 563-4, the expectation entertained by <ORG> that the <ORG> will not interfere with the fixation of compensation by <ORG> was belied.
Precedent	The <ORG> said in that case that the owner of the property expropriated must be paid the just equivalent of what he has been deprived of and that within the limits of this basic requirement of full indemnification of the expropriated owner, the Constitution allows free play to the legislative judgment as to what principles should guide the determination of the amount payable.
Precedent	1802.
Precedent	In order  to bring Article 31(2) in conformity with the clear inten tion of the framers of the Constitution, the Fourth Amendment to the Constitution was passed and it came into effect on April 27, 1955.
Precedent	At the end of Article 31(2) the following words were introduced by the Amendment : "...and no such law shall be called in question in any <ORG> on the ground that the compensation provided by the law is not adequate."
Precedent	The effect of the amendment was considered by this <ORG> in <CITATION_1634289> (1965) 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	pp.
Precedent	614, 626.
Argument	<NAME> (as he then was) said that the fact that <ORG> used the same expressions namely, 'compensation' and 'principles' as were found in Article 31 before the amendment is a clear indication that it accepted the meaning given by this <ORG> to those expressions in Mrs. <NAME>'s Case and that it follows that a Legislautre in making a law of acquisition or requisition shall provide for a just equivalent of what the owner has been deprived of or specify the principles for the purpos e of ascertaining the 'just equivalent' of what the owner has been deprived of.
Argument	1803.
Argument	<CITATION_602096> (1967) 1 S.C.R.
Argument	p. 255, it was laid down that to provide written down value of a machinery (as it was understood under the Income Tax Act ) was not in compliance with Article 31(2) because it did not represent the just equivalent of the machinery, meaning thereby, the price at or about the time of its acquisition.
Argument	<NAME> said that the law to justify itself has to provide for the payment of a 'just equivalent' to the land acquired or lay down principles which will lead to that result.
RatioOfTheDecision	1804.
RatioOfTheDecision	Two years later, in <CITATION_673450> (1969) 3 S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	341, this <ORG> overruled the decision in the Metal Corporation Case and <NAME>, <NAME> observed that if the quantum of compensation fixed by the <ORG> is not Habile to be canvassed before the <ORG> on the ground that it is not a just equivalent, the principles specified for determination of compensation will also not be open to challenge on the plea that the c ompensation determined by the application of those principles is not a just equivalent.
RatioOfTheDecision	1805.
Precedent	In the Bank Nationalisation Case R.C.
Precedent	Cooper v. <ORG>  3 S.C.R.
Precedent	530, the majority decision virtually overruled the decision in <CITATION_673450> .
Precedent	The majority was of the view that even after the Fourth Amendment 'compensation' meant "the equivalent in terms of money of the property compulsorily acquired" according to 'relevant principles' which principles must be appropriate to the determination of compensation for the particular class of property sought to be acquired.
Precedent	1806.
Precedent	It was in these circumstances that the word 'amount' was substituted for 'compensation' in the sub-article by the 25th Amendment.
Precedent	1807.
Argument	It was submitted on behalf of the petitioner that the word 'amount' implies a norm for fixing it and that at any rate, when principles for fixing the amount are referred to, the principles must have some relevancy to the amount to be fixed.
RatioOfTheDecision	1808.
RatioOfTheDecision	The whole purpose of the amendment was to exclude judi cial review of the question whether the 'amount' fixed or the principle laid down by law is adequate or relevant.
RatioOfTheDecision	1809.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME>, <NAME> said in <CITATION_1318432> (1955) 2 S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	225, 237, that the <ORG>, enjoying as it does, a majority in the legislature concentrates in itself the virtual control of both legislative and executive functions; and as the Ministers constituting the <ORG> are presumably agreed on fundamentals and act on the principle of collective responsibility, the most important questions of policy are all formulated by them.
Argument	1810.
Argument	Much the same sentiment was expressed by <NAME>, J. see <CITATION_705604> .
Argument	(1972), 29 Sales Tax Cases, 206:  In a <ORG> form of Government, the executive is expected to reflect the views of the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	In fact in most matters it gives the lead to the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	However much one might deplore the "New Despotism" of the executive, the very complexity of the modern society and the demand it makes on its government have set in motion forces which have mad e it absolutely necessary for the legislatures to entrust more and more powers to the executive.
RatioOfTheDecision	Text book doctrines evoked in the 19th century have become out of date....  1811.
RatioOfTheDecision	When the <ORG> formulates a proposal for acquisition of property, it will have the relevant materials to fix the amount to be paid to the owner or the principles for its fixation.
RatioOfTheDecision	Several factors will have to be taken into account for fixing the amount or laying down the principles; the nature of the property sought to be acquired, the purpose for which the acquisition is being made, the real investment of the owner excluding the fortuitous circumstances like unearned increment and also marginal utility of the property acquired to the owner.
RatioOfTheDecision	Principles of social justice alone will furnish the yardstick for fixing the amount or for laying down the principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	The proposal becomes embodied in law, if the <ORG> agrees to the Bill embedying the proposal.
RatioOfTheDecision	The whole point is that the fixation of the amount or the laying down of the  principle for fixing it is left to the absolute discretion of the <ORG> or <ORG> on the basis of consideration of social justice.
RatioOfTheDecision	That the fixation is in the absolute discretion of <ORG> or <ORG> is further made clear when it is laid down that "no such law shall be called in question in any <ORG> on the ground that the amount so fixed or determined is not adequate."
RatioOfTheDecision	If the <ORG> or <ORG> legislature can fix any amount, on consideration of principles of social justice, it can also formulate the principle for fixing the amount on the very same consideration.
RatioOfTheDecision	And the principle of social justice will not furnish judicially manageable standards either for testing the adequacy of the amount or the relevancy of the principle.
RatioOfTheDecision	1812.
RatioOfTheDecision	The article as amended provides no norm for the <ORG> to test the adequacy of the amount or the relevancy of the principle.
RatioOfTheDecision	Whereas the word 'compensation', even after the Fourth Amendment, was thought to give such a norm, namely, the just'  equivalent in money of the property acquired or full indemnification of the owners the word 'amount' conveys no idea of any norm.
RatioOfTheDecision	If supplies no yard-stick.
RatioOfTheDecision	It furnishes no measuring rod.
RatioOfTheDecision	The neutral word 'amount' was deliberately chosen for the purpose.
RatioOfTheDecision	I am unable to understand the purpose in substituting the word 'amount' for the word 'compensation' in the sub article unless it be to deprive the <ORG> of any yardstick or norm for determining the adequacy of the amount and the relevancy of the principles fixed by law.
RatioOfTheDecision	I should have thought that this coupled with the express provision precluding the <ORG> from going into the adequacy of the amount fixed or determined should put it beyond any doubt that fixation of the amount or determination of the principle for fixing it is a matter for the <ORG> alone and that the <ORG> has no say in the matter.
Precedent	This <ORG> said in Shantilal's Case [1969] 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	pp.
Precedent	341, 366: ...it does not however mean that something fixed or determined by the application of specif ied principles which is illusory or can in no sense be regarded as compensation must be upheld by the courts, for, to do so, would be to grant a charter of arbitrariness.
Argument	1813.
Argument	These observations were made with reference to the sub-article as it stood before the 25th Amendment, namely, before the substitution of the word 'amount' for the word 'compensation' in it Even if the decision of this <ORG> in <NAME>'s Case is assumed to be correct, what is its relevancy after the substitution of the word 'amount' in Article 31(2) as regards the jurisdiction of the <ORG> to test the adequacy of the amount on the ground of arbitrariness.
Argument	1814.
Argument	I do not propose to decide nor is it necessary for the purpose of adjudging the validity of the 25th Amendment whether a law fixing an amount which is illusory or which is a fraud on the Constitution, can be struck down by <ORG>.
Argument	It is said that the instances in which the <ORG> can interefere to test the adequacy of compensation or the relevancy of the principles for determin ation of compensation had been laid down in the Bank Nationalisation Case and when the 25th Amendment did not make any change in the clause, namely, "no such law shall be called in question in any court on the ground that the amount so fixed or determined is not adequate" but retained it in its original form, the only inference is that the <ORG> approved the interpretation placed upon the clause by this <ORG> and, therefore, the <ORG> has power to examine the question Whether the amount fixed by law is adequate or illusory or that the principles for fixation of the amount are relevant.
RatioOfTheDecision	I am not quite sure about the nature of the presumption when the word "compensation" has, been deleted from the sub-article and the word "amount" substituted.
RatioOfTheDecision	1815.
Precedent	In The Royal <ORG> Derby Procelain Co. Ltd. v. <NAME> [1949] 2 K.B.
Precedent	417 at 429 Denning, <NAME> said:  I do not believe that whenever <ORG> re-enacts a provision of a statute if thereby gives statutory authority to every erroneous interpretation whic h has been put upon it.
Precedent	The true view is that the <ORG> will be slow to overrule a previous decision on the interpretation of a statute when it has long been acted on, and it will be more than usually slow to do so when <ORG> has, since the decision, re-enacted the statute in the same terms.
Precedent	1816.
Precedent	See also the speech of Lord <NAME> in <NAME> v. <NAME> [1956] A.C. 299.
RatioOfTheDecision	The presumption, if there is any, is always subject to an intention to the contrary.
RatioOfTheDecision	1817.
Argument	Counsel for the petitioner argued that as Article 19(i)(f) is still retained it would be paradoxical if a law could provide for acquisition or requisition of property on payment of an inadequate or illusory amount.
Argument	He said, even if the amount given is not the just equivalent in money of the value of the property acquired, it must at least be an amount having reasonable relation to its value as <ORG> cannot be deemed to have intended by the Amendment to enable a law being passed fixing an unreasonably low amount as the right to acquire and  hold property is still a Fundamental Right under Article 19.
RatioOfTheDecision	If we are to import into the concept of 'amount' the implication of reasonableness with reference to the market value of the property, it would immediately open the door to the justiciability of the question of the adequacy of the amount fixed or determined which the sub-article expressly says it is not open to the Court to go into.
RatioOfTheDecision	1818.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Fundamental Right to property is attenuated to a certain extent.
RatioOfTheDecision	But it is not wholly taken away.
RatioOfTheDecision	The right that the property could be acquired only under a law fixing an amount or the principles for determining it and for a public purpose would still remain.
Precedent	This Court can strike down an amendment of the Constitution only on the ground that the amendment was not made in the manner and form required by Article 368 , or that the amendment was made in violation of some express or implied limitation upon the power of amendment.
Precedent	1819.
Precedent	A Constitutional amendment which provides for the law fixing the 'amount' or th e principles for determining the amount instead of compensation or the principles for its determination and which deprives the <ORG> of the power of judicial review of the question whether the amount or the principles fixed by law is adequate or are relevant, cannot be adjudged bad on the ground of some invisible radiation from the concept that the right to acquire, hold or dispose of property is a Fundamental Right.
Precedent	1820.
Precedent	If full moon compensation has to be paid, concentratiion of wealth in the form of immovable or movable property will be transformed into concentration of wealth in the form of money and how is the objective underlying Article 39(b) and (c) achieved by the transformation ?
Precedent	And with there be enough money in the coffers of the <ORG> to pay full compensation?
Precedent	1821.
Precedent	As the 24th Amendment which empowers <ORG> to take away or abridge Fundamental Right has been held by me to be valid, I do not think there is any conceivable basis on which I can strike down the amendment to Article 31(2).
Precedent	Nor  can I read any implication in to the word 'amount' and say that it must be reasonable as that would imply a standard.
Precedent	Having regard to the neutral and colourless character of the word 'amount' and the express provision excluding judicial review of the question of the adequacy of the amount, the question of reasonableness of the amount or the relevancy of the principle is entirely outside the judicial ken.
Precedent	1822.
Precedent	Now I turn to the question of the validity of Article 31C.
Precedent	1823.
Argument	Counsel for the petitioner submitted that there is a fundamental distinction between amending Fundamental, Rights in such a way as to abridge or take them away and making an amendment in the Constitution which enables <ORG> in its legislative capacity and the legislatures of the States to pass a law violating Fundamental Rights and making it valid.
Argument	According to counsel what has been done by Article 31C is to enable <ORG> and State <ORG> to make Constitution-breaking laws and put them beyond challenge in any Court with  the result that laws which would be void as contravening the Fundamental Rights are deemed, by a fiction of law, to be not void and that is a repudiation of the supremacy of the Constitution which is an essential feature of the Constitution.
Argument	Counsel further said the Directive Principles which were intended by the Constitution-makers to run as subsidiary to Fundamental Rights have been made paramount to them and laws to implement the Directive Principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c) are made immune from attack, even if they violate Fundamental Rights under Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Argument	He further said that a declaration by <ORG> of the <ORG> legislature that a law is to give effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Article 39(b) or (c) has been made final which, in effect, means that <ORG> and <ORG> legislatures can pass any laws in the exercise of their legislative power, whether they give effect to the policy of <ORG> towards securing the Directive Principles c ontained in Article 39(b) and (c) or not, and get immunity for those laws from attack under Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	1824.
Precedent	I should have thought that Article 31C is a proviso to Article 13(2) in that it enables <ORG> or <ORG> <ORG> to pass laws of a particular type which would not be deemed to be void even if they violate the provision of Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	1825.
Precedent	I have no doubt that 'law' in Article 31C can only mean a law passed by <ORG> or the <ORG> legislatures.
Precedent	The word must take its colour from the context.
Precedent	1826.
Precedent	The makers of the Constitution imposed a ban by Article 13(2) upon the '<ORG>' passing a law in contravention of the rights conferred by Part III.
Precedent	If 24th Amendment which enablies <ORG> to make an amendment of the Fundamental Rights in such a way as to take away or abridge them is valid, what is there to prevent <ORG> or state legislatures to pass law for implementing the Directive Principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c) which would be immune from attack on  the ground that those laws violate Articles 14, 19 and 31?
Precedent	Is it not open to <ORG> to enact an amendment saying in effect that although all laws passed by <ORG> and <ORG> legislatures, which violate fundamental rights are void, laws passed by <ORG> and <ORG> legislatures for giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing directive principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c) would not be void, even if they contravene some of the fundamental rights, namely, those under Articles 14, 19 and 31?
Precedent	Article 31C merely carves out a legislative field with reference to a particular type of law, and exempts that law from the ambit of Article 13(2) in some respects.
Precedent	<ORG> or <ORG> legislatures pass a law for giving effect to the Directive Principles specified in Article 39(b) or (c), not by virtue of Article 31C , but by virtue of their power under the appropriate legislative entires.
Precedent	What Article 31C does is to confer immunity on those laws from attack on the ground that they viola te the provision of Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	1827.
Precedent	The material portion of Article 31A is in pari materia with the first part of Article 31G.
Precedent	Article 31A has been held to be valid by this Court in <NAME> Case  S.C.R.
Precedent	89.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fact that the argument now urged did not occur to counsel who appeared in the case or the great judges who decided it is a weighty consideration in assessing its validity.
RatioOfTheDecision	To make a distinction between Article 31A on the ground that Article 31A provides for laws dealing with certain specified subjects only whereas Article 31C makes provisions for laws to give effect to the <ORG> policy for securing the directive principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c) is, to my mind, to make a distinction between Tweedledum and Tweedledee.
RatioOfTheDecision	One can very well say that the subject matter of the law referred to in Article 31C is that dealt with by Article 39(b) and (c) or that 31A provides for immunity of the laws for securing the objects specified therein from attack on the ground that they  violate Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	Does the artificial characterisation of a law as one with reference to the object or subject make any difference in this context ?
RatioOfTheDecision	think not.
RatioOfTheDecision	1828.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is a bit difficult to understand how Article 31C has delegated or, if I may say so more accurately, invested the <ORG> in its legislative capacity or the <ORG> legislatures, with any power to amend the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Merely because a law passed by them to give effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the Directive Principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c) in pursuance to valid legislative, entries in the appropriate Lists in the Seventh Schedule might violate the Fundamental Rights under Articles 14, 19 and 31 and such law is deemed not void by virtue of Article 31C , it would not follow that Article 31C has invested the <ORG> in its legislative capacity or the <ORG> legislatures with power to amend the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is by virtue of the 25th Amendment that the law, although it might violate the Fundamen tal Rights under Articles 14, 19 and 31 is not deemed viod.
RatioOfTheDecision	Whenever <ORG> or State legislatures pass such a law, the law so passed gets immunity from attack on the ground that it violates the Fundamental Rights under Articles 14, 19 and 31 by Virtue of Article 31C which in effect has made a pro-tanto amendment of Article 13(2) in respect of that category of laws.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is a mistake to suppose that every time when <ORG> in its legislative capacity or a State legislature passes such a law and if the law violates the Fundamental Rights under Articles 14, 19 and 31, it is that law which amends the Constitution and makes it valid.
RatioOfTheDecision	The amendment of the relevant provision of the Constitution, namely Article 13(2 ), has already been made by the 25th Amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	And as I said it is that amendment which confers upon the law immunity from attack on the ground that it violates the Fundamental Rights under the above said articles.
RatioOfTheDecision	1829.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> in its legislative capacity or the State legislatures cannot confer  any immunity upon the laws passed by them from the attack and they do not do so.
RatioOfTheDecision	They rely upon the 25th Amendment as conferring the immunity upon the law which gives effect to the <ORG> Policy towards securing the above mentioned purpose.
RatioOfTheDecision	I confess my inability to understand the distinction between a law passed in pursuance of an amendment of the Constitution which lifts the ban of Article 13(2) and a law passed in pursuance of an amendment which says that the law shall not be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges the rights conferred by the articles in Part III.
RatioOfTheDecision	The distinction, to my mind, is invisible.
Precedent	Take one illustration : Article 15(4) says: Nothing in this article or in Clause (2) of Article 29 shall prevent the <ORG> from making any special provisions for the advancement of any socially and educationally backward classes of citizens or for the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes.
Precedent	Suppose the sub-article had said:  Notwithstanding anything containe d in this article, or Clause 2 of Article 29 the <ORG> shall be competent to make special provision for the advancement of any socially and educationally backward classes of citizens or for the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes and such a law shall not be deemed to be void under Article 13(2).
Precedent	In both the cases, the amendment has brought about the same effect, namely, the law shall not be deemed to be void for contravention of the right conferred by Article 15 or Article 29(2 ), notwithstanding the difference in the wording by which the effect was brought about.
Precedent	And, in both cases it is the amendment of the Constitution which gives the law the immunity from attack on the ground that it is in contravention of the rights conferred by Part III.
Precedent	1830.
Precedent	If Article 31C is assumed to invest <ORG> in its legislative capacity or <ORG> legislatures with power to pass a law of the description in question amending Fundamental Rights under Articles 14, 19 and 31 in such a way as to take away or abridge them is  the grant of such a power valid.
Precedent	The answer seems to me to be simple.
Precedent	If the effect of Article 31C is as assumed, then it is a pro-tanto amendment of Article 368.
Precedent	It is not necessary that Article 31C should in such a case purport to amend Article 368.
Precedent	See <NAME> v. <NAME> and <NAME>.
Precedent	(1968) A.C. 717, 739 to 744.
RatioOfTheDecision	Nor is it necessary that Article 31C should commence with the words "Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 368".
RatioOfTheDecision	Just as the Dog Act under an uncontrolled <ORG>, pro-tanto amends the so called <ORG> if it is inconsistent with it, so also under a controlled <ORG> an amendment of the <ORG>, if inconsistent with any provision of the <ORG> would pro-tanto amend it.
RatioOfTheDecision	The 25th Amendment was passed in the manner and form required for amendment of Article 368.
Precedent	I cannot read any limitation upon the power to amend the amending power which would preclude Article 368 from being amended in such a way as to invest part of the amending power in Parl iament in its ordinary legislative capacity or in State legislature, to be exercised by them in a form and manner different from that prescribed by Article 368.
Precedent	1831.
Precedent	The supposed bad odour about the article should not upset our judgment in adjudging its Constitutionality.
Precedent	We have no power under the Constitution to adjudge a Constitutional amendment as unConstitutional on the ground that the amendment would in effect vest large powers in <ORG> and State legislatures to pass laws which might violate Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	1832.
Precedent	Counsel for the petitioner asked the question why the right to pass laws violating the freedom of speech guaranteed under Article 19(1)(a) is given to <ORG> in its legislative capacity and to the State legislatures by Article 31C when it is seen that Clauses (b) and (c) of Article 39 are concerned with matters which have no connection with that freedom.
Precedent	1833.
Precedent	In my dissenting judgment in <CITATION_125596> .
Precedent	etc.
Precedent	2 S.C.C.
Fact	788, I had occasion to deal with certain aspects of  the modern press.
Argument	Mr. <NAME> has rightly emphasized its commercial character and how that aspect, though connected with freedom of speech might require control.
Argument	Though the press stands as the purveyor of truth and the disinterested counsellor of the people, it is now primarily a business concern; an undertaking conducted for profit like any other, that the proprietor is a man of business and though he may desire power as well as money, profit comes before political opinions.
Argument	According to Lord <NAME> the power of the newspaper has two peculiar features.
Argument	It has no element of Compulsion and no element of Responsibility.
Argument	Whoever exposes himself to its influence does so of his own free will.
Argument	He need not buy the paper, nor read it nor believe it.
Argument	If he takes it for his guide, that is his own doing.
Argument	The newspaper, as it has no legal duty, is subject to no responsibility, beyond that which the law affixes to indefensible attacks on private character or incitements to illegal conduct.
Argument	The temptations to use the influe nce of a newspaper for the promotion of pecuniary interests, whether of its proprietors or of others, have also increased.
Precedent	Newspapers have become one of the most available instruments by which the Money power can make itself felt in politics, and its power is practically irresponsible, for the only thing it need fear is the reduction of circulation, and the great majority of its readers, interested only in business and sport, know little of and care little for the political errors it may commit.
Precedent	See <NAME>, "Modern Democracies", Vol.
Precedent	I, the Chapter on "The Press in a Democracy", pp.
Precedent	104-124.
Precedent	1834.
Precedent	The news content of the press enters at once into the thought process of the public.
Precedent	The fulness and unbent integrity of the news thus becomes a profund social concern.
Precedent	That which is a necessary condition of performing a duty is a right; we may therefore speak of the moral right of a people to be well served by its press.
Precedent	Since the citizen's political duty is at stake, the right to have an adequate service of n ews becomes a public responsibility as well.
Precedent	So freedom of the press must now cover two sets of rights and not one only.
Precedent	With the rights of editors and publishers to express themselves there must be associated a right of the public to be served with a substantial and honest basis of fact for its judgments of public affairs.
Precedent	Of these two, it is the latter which today tends to take precedence in importance.
Precedent	The freedom of the press has changed its point of focus from the editor to the citizen.
Precedent	This aspect of the question was considered by <ORG> in United States v. <ORG> 326 U.S. 20.
Precedent	Mr. Justice <NAME> who wrote the majority opinion sees the welfare of the public as the central issue.
Precedent	The fundamental acknowledgement that press functions are now, in the eyes of the law as well as common sense "clothed with a public interest" suggest an affirmative obligation on the part of the Government.
Precedent	1835.
Precedent	Nobody demurs when a law preventing adulteration of food is passed.
Precedent	Is the adultera tion of news, the everyday mental pabulum of the citizen, a less serious matter?
Precedent	The need of the consumer to have adequate and uncontaminated mental food is such that he is under a duty to get it.
Precedent	Because of this duty his interest acquires the status of a right since the consumer is no longer free not to consume and can get what he requires only through the existing press organs, the protection of the freedom of the issuer is no longer sufficient to protect automatically the consumer or the community.
Precedent	The general policy of laissez faire in this matter must be reconsidered.
Precedent	The press is a public utility in private hands and cannot be left free from all kinds of regulation.
Precedent	The ante-thesis between complete laissez faire and complete governmental operation or control of the press is for our society unreal Therefore, the question is whether, without intruding on the press activity, the <ORG> may regulate the conditions under which those activities take place so that the public interest is better served.
Precedent	See <NAME> ng, "The Freedom of the Press", pp.
Precedent	167-9.
Precedent	As I said in my judgment, concentration of power substitutes one controlling policy for many independent policies, it lessens the number of competitOrs.
Precedent	The influential part of the nation's press is large scale enterprise closely inter-locked with the system of finance and industry.
Precedent	It will not escape the natural bias of what it is.
Precedent	Yet, if freedom is to be secure, the bias must be known and overcome.
Precedent	It may also be necessary for the <ORG> to extend the scope of present legal remedies, if a given type of abuse amounts to poisoning the wells of the public opinion.
Precedent	It might be necessary in passing a Jaw for giving effect to the <ORG> policy towards securing the Directive Principles contained in Article 39(b) and (c) to deal with the commercial aspect of the press, and that aspect being connected with the freedom of speech, it might become inevitable for the law to abridge that freedom.
Precedent	1836.
Precedent	Whatever one's personal views might be about the wisdom of Article 31C , what ever distrust one might have in the attempt at improving society by what one may think as futile if not mischievous economic tinkering, it is not for us to prescribe for the society or deny the right of experimentation to it within very wide limits.
Precedent	1837.
Precedent	It was said that, as Article 31C bars judicial scrutiny of the question that a law containing the declaration gives effect to the policy of the <ORG>, <ORG> and <ORG> legislatures can pass laws having no nexus with the Directive Principles specified in Article 39(b) or (c) and violate with impunity the Fundamental Rights under Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Precedent	1838.
Precedent	The purpose of Article 31C is only to give immunity to a law for giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the Directive Principles under Article 39(b) and  (c) from attack on the ground that its provisions violate Articles 14, 19 and 31.
Argument	A law which will never give effect the <ORG> policy towards securing these principles will enjoy no immunity, if any of its provisions violates thes e articles.
Argument	It is only a law for giving effect to the <ORG> policy towards securing the principles specified in Article 39 (b) and  (c) that can contain a declaration that it is for giving effect to such a policy and it is only such a declaration that will bar the scrutiny by the <ORG> of the question that the law does not give effect to the policy.
Argument	The expression 'no law' in the latter part of Article 31C can only mean the type of law referred to in the first part.
Argument	To be more specific the expression 'no law' occurring in the latter part of the article can only mean 'no such law' as is referred to in the first part.
Precedent	It would be very strange were it otherwise.
Precedent	If any other construction were to be adopted, a declaration could shield any law, even if it has no connection with the principles specified in Article 39(b) or (c) from attack on the ground of violation of these articles.
Precedent	Any law under the Sun can be brought under the protective umbrella of the declaration.
Precedent	Therefore, as I said, it is only a law for giv ing effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clauses (b) and (c) of Article 39 , that can contain a declaration.
Precedent	If a declaration is contained in any law which does not give effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in these clauses, the <ORG> can go into the question whether the law gives effect to the said policy.
Precedent	Whenever a question is raised that the <ORG> or <ORG> legislatures have abused their power and inserted a declaration in a law not for giving effect to the <ORG> policy towards securing the Directive Principles specified in Article 39(b) or (c), the <ORG> must necessarily go into that question and decide it.
Precedent	To put it in other words, the legislative jurisdiction to incorporate a declaration that the law gives effect to the policy of the <ORG> is conditioned upon the circumstances that the law gives effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the Directive Principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c).
Precedent	If this is so , the declaration that the law is to give effect to the policy of the <ORG> cannot bar the jurisdiction of the <ORG> to go into the question whether the law gives effect to the policy.
Precedent	The declaration can never oust the jurisdiction of the <ORG> to see whether the law is one for giving effect to such a policy, as the jurisdiction of the legislature to incorporate the declaration is founded on the law being one to give effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing these principles.
Precedent	1839.
Precedent	In order to decide whether a law gives effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the Directive Principles specified in Article 39(b) or (c), a <ORG> will have to examine the pith and substance, the true nature and character of the law as also its design and the subject matter dealt with by it together with its object and scope.
Precedent	If the <ORG> comes to the conclusion that the declaration was merely a pretence and that the real purpose of the law is the accomplishment of some object other than to give effect to the  policy of the <ORG> towards securing the Directive Principles in Article 39(b) and (c ), the declaration would not be a bar to the <ORG> from striking down any provision therein which violates Articles 14, 19 or 31.
Precedent	In other words, if a law passed ostensibly to give effect to the policy of the <ORG> is, in truth and substance, one for accomplishing an unauthorized object, the <ORG> would be entitled to tear the veil created by the declaration and decide according to the real nature of the law.
Precedent	1840.
Precedent	Apart from the safeguard furnished by judicial scrutiny, there is sufficient guarantee in Article 31C that a <ORG> legislature will not abuse the power as the law passed by it will be valid only when it has been reserved for the assent of the President and has obtained his assent.
Precedent	In the light of what I have said, the apprehension expressed in some quarters that if judicial scrutiny of the question whether the law gives effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing these Directive Principles is barred, it wi ll lead to the disintegration of the country has no real foundation.
Precedent	Nor has the dictum of Justice <NAME> : <NAME>, "Collected Legal Papers", pp.
Precedent	295-296.
Precedent	"I do not think that the United States would come to an end if <ORG> lost our power to declare an Act of the <ORG> void.
Precedent	But I do think that the Union would be imperilled if we could not make that declaration as to the laws of the several States", any relevance in the context.
Precedent	1841.
Precedent	It was said that the Constitution-makers never intended that Fundamental Rights should be subservient to Directive Principles and that they visualized a society where the rights in Part III and the aspirations in Part IV would co-exist in harmony.
Precedent	(The doctrine of harmonious construction has been a panacea for many of our ills.
Precedent	But I am not sure of its efficiency.)
Precedent	A succeeding generation might view the relative importance of the Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles in a different light or from a different perspective.
Precedent	The value judgment of the succeeding g enerations as regards the relative weight and importance of these rights and aspirations might be entirely different from that of the makers of the Constitution.
Precedent	And it is no answer to say that the relative priority value of the Directive Principles over Fundamental Rights was not apprehended or even if apprehended was not given effect to when the Constitution was framed or to insist that what the Directive Principles meant to the vision of that day, it must mean to the vision of our time.
Precedent	1842.

Precedent	1843.

Precedent	I would, therefore, sum up my findings.
Precedent	1844.
Precedent	I hold that the decision in Golaknath Case that the <ORG> had no power to amend Fundamental Rights in such a way as to take away or abridge them was wrong, that the power to amend under Article 368 as it stood before the 24th Amendment was plenary in character and extended to all the provisions of the Constitution, that the 24th Amendment did not add anything to the content of Article 368 as it stood before the amendment, that it is declaratory in character except as regards the compulsory nature of the assent of the President to a bill for amendment and that the article as amended makes it clear that all the provisions of the Constitution can be amended by way of addition, variation or repeal.
Precedent	The only limitation is that the Constitution cannot be repealed or abrogated in the exercise of the power of amendment without substituting a mechanism by which the <ORG> is constituted  and organized.
Precedent	That limitation flows from the language of the article itself.
Precedent	1845.
Precedent	I do not think there were or are any implied or inherent limitations upon the power of amendment under the article.
Precedent	1846.
Precedent	The 24th Amendment is valid.
Precedent	1847.
Precedent	The 25th Amendment, including Article 31C , is valid.
Precedent	The word 'amount' in Article 31(2 ), as amended, does not convey the idea of any norm.
Precedent	The fixation of the amount or the principle for determining the amount is a matter within the absolute discretion of the <ORG> or the <ORG> <ORG>.
Precedent	The <ORG> cannot go into the question whether the amount fixed by law or the principle laid down for determining the amount is adequate or relevant.
Precedent	1848.
Precedent	The declaration visualized in Article 31C that the law gives effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c) of the Constitution would not oust the jurisdiction of the <ORG> to go into the question whether the law gives effect to the policy.
Precedent	The jurisdiction of Parliamen t or the <ORG> legislatures to incorporate the declaration in a law is conditioned upon the circumstance that the law is one for giving effect to the <ORG> policy towards securing the aforesaid principles.

Precedent	2 S.C.R.
