Precedent	1852.
Precedent	The cases before us have become so much loaded with learning and marked by brilliance of exposition of all the points involved, either directly or indirectly, both by my learned brethren and the members of <ORG>, in view of  the crucial importance, for the future Constitutional history of this country, of the issues placed before us, that it would be presumptuous on my part to attempt to deal with every point which has been raised.
Precedent	Indeed, it is not necessary for me to repeat such views as I accept as correct expressed by my learned brethren with whose conclusion I agree.
Precedent	The reasons for my very respectful disagreement with those conclusions of some of my other learned brethren with which I do not concur will become evident in the course of the few observations with which I shall content myself before recording my conclusions.
Precedent	I venture to make these observations because, as my learned Brother <NAME> has pointed out, in cases of the nature before us, the healthier practice is to follow the example of <ORG> even though a multiplicity of opinions may produce a "thicket", which, according to Judge <NAME>, it is the function of judicial learning and wisdom to remove.
Precedent	I do hope that my observations will not add to the t hickness of this thicket without some useful purpose served by making them.
Precedent	1853.
Precedent	I think that we do stand in danger, in the circumstances stated above, of losing sight of the wood for the trees, and, if we get entangled in some of the branches of the trees we may miss reaching the destination; the correct conclusion or decision.
Precedent	I think I can speak for all my learned brethren as well as myself when I say that we are all conscious of the enormous burden which rests upon our shoulders in placing before the country the solution or solutions which may not only be correct but beneficial for it without doing violence to the law embodied in our Constitution to which we take oaths of allegiance.
Precedent	1854.
Precedent	I am reminded here of what, Prof. <NAME> wrote in "Law in a Changing Society'.
Precedent	He said at page 61:  The task of the modern judge is increasingly complex.
Precedent	Hardly any major decision can be made without a careful evaluation of the conflicting values and interests of which some examples have been given in the precedin g pages.
Precedent	Totalitarian government eliminates much of the conflict by dictating what should be done".
Precedent	The lot of the democratic judge is heavier and nobler.
Precedent	He cannot escape the burden of individual responsibility, and the great, as distinct from the competent, judges have, I submit, been those who have shouldered that burden and made their decisions as articulate a reflection of the conflicts before them as possible.
Precedent	They do not dismiss the techniques of law, but they are aware that by themselves, they provide ho solution to the social conflicts of which the law is an inevitable reflection".
Precedent	He also wrote there (at page 62):  The law must aspire at certainly at justice, at progressiveness, but these objectives are constantly in conflict one with the other.
Precedent	What the great judges and jurists have taught is not infallible knowledge, or a certain answer to all legal problems, but an awareness of the problems of contemporary society and an acceptence of the burden of decision which no amount of technical legal kn owledge can take from us.
Precedent	1855.
Precedent	The 'Core', a term and concept which Mr. <NAME> has tried to impress upon us repeatedly with his extra-ordinary forensic ability and eloquence, or crux of the problem before us is thus stated in writing, in part 10 of Book 3, containing the concluding written submission of Mr. <NAME>.
Precedent	It is submitted that it would be impossible to dispose of these petitions without dealing with the most crucial question the true ambit of the amending power.
Precedent	This question can be decided either on the ground of the meaning of the word "amendment" in the unamended Article 368 or on the ground of inherent and implied limitations or on both the grounds, since they converge on the same point.
Precedent	It is submitted with great respect that it would be impossible to deal with the questions relating to the 24th and 25th Amendments without deciding the true ambit of the amending power".
Precedent	The questions of the correct interpretation of the 24th Amendment and its validity cannot be decided unless this Hon '<ORG> first comes to a conclusion as to whether the original power was limited or unlimited.
Precedent	If it was originally limited the question would arise whether the 24th Amendment should be "read down" or whether it should be held to be unConstitutional.
Precedent	Even the question of the correct construction of the 24th Amendment cannot be decided unless the starting point is first established, namely, the true, scope of the original amending power".
Precedent	Again, it would be impossible to decide the question whether Article 31(2) which has been altered by the 25th Amendment should be "read down" in such a way as to preserve the right to property or should be declared unConstitutional as abrogating the right to property,-unless and until it is first decided whether <ORG> has the right to abrogate the right to property.
Precedent	This directly involves the question whether the amending power is limited or unlimited.
Precedent	When one comes to Article 31C the necessity of deciding the limits of the amending power becomes unmistakable.
Precedent	The  Article violates 7 essential features of the Constitution and makes the Constitution suffer a loss of identity.
Precedent	There can be no question of 'reading down' Article 31C.
Precedent	It can only be held to be unConstitutional on the ground that <ORG>'s amending power was limited".
Precedent	To decide the question of the validity of Article 31C only on the ground that it virtually provider for amendment of the Constitution in a "manner and form" different from that prescribed by Article 368 would be a most unsatisfactory ground of decision.
Precedent	The question of prune importance is the limit on the amending power.
Precedent	The question of manner and form pales into total insignificance compared to the question of substantive limitation on the amending power".
Precedent	It is submitted with the greatest respect that the 69 days hearing would be virtually wasted if the judgment were to rest merely on the point of manner and form, avoiding the real issue of momentous significance, namely, the scope of the amending power.
Precedent	It is this vital issue which has r eally taken up the time of the <ORG> for almost five months".
Precedent	1856.
Precedent	Before tackling the core or crux of the case which, as Mr. <NAME> has rightly pointed out, is the question of the limits of the amending power found in Article 368 of the Constitution, I must make some preliminary observations on the very concepts of a Constitution and of legal sovereignty embodied in it, and the nature of the amending power as I conceive it.
Precedent	This and other parts of my judgment may also disclose what I think a judge should not hesitate to explore and expose leaving it merely to be inferred from the judgment as his "undisclosed major premises".
Precedent	It is part of judicial function, in my estimation, to disclose and to justify to the citizens of this country what these premises are.
Precedent	1857.
Precedent	I think that it is clear from the Preamble as well as the provisions of Parts III and IV of our Constitution that it seeks to express the principle : "Solus Populi Seprema Lex".
Precedent	In other words, the good of the mass of citizens of our country i s the supreme law embodied in our Constitution prefaced as it is by the preamble or the 'key' which puts "justice, social, economic and political" as the first of the four objectives of the Constitution by means of which "the people" of India constituted "a sovereign democratic Republic".
Precedent	1858.
Precedent	A modern democratic Constitution is to my mind, an expression of the sovereign will of the people, although, as we all know, our Constitution was drawn up by <ORG> which was not chosen by adult franchise.
Precedent	Upon this <ORG> was conferred the legal power and authority, by Section 8 of the Indian Independence Act, passed by <ORG>, to frame our Constitution.
Precedent	Whether we like it or not, Section 6 and 8 of an Act of <ORG> transferred, in the eye of law, the legal sovereignty, which was previously vested in <ORG>, to <ORG> which was given the powers of <ORG> for framing our Constitution.
Precedent	1859.
Precedent	The result may be d escribed as the transfer of political as well at legal sovereignty from one nation to another, by means of their legally authorised channels.
Precedent	This transfer became irrevocable both as a matter of law and even more so of fact.
Precedent	Whatever theory some of the die-hard exponents of the legal omnipotence of <ORG> may have expounded, the modern view, even in Britain, is that what was so transferred from one nation to another could not be legally revoked.
Precedent	The vesting of the power of making the Constitution was however, legally in <ORG> thus constituted and recognised and not in "the people of India", in whose name <ORG> no doubt spoke in the Preamble to the Constitution.
Precedent	<ORG> thus spoke for the whole of the people of India without any specific or direct legal authority conferred by the people themselves to perform this function.
Precedent	1860.
Precedent	The voice of the people speaking through <ORG> constituted a new "Republic" which was both "S overeign and Democratic".
Precedent	It no doubt sought to secure the noble objectives laid down in the Preamble primarily through both the Fundamental rights found in Part III and the Directive Principles of State Policy found in Part IV of the Constitution.
Precedent	It would, however, not be correct, in my opinion, to characterise, as Mr. <NAME> did, the Fundamental rights contained in Part III, as merely the means whereas the Directive Principles, contained in Part IV as the ends of the endeavours of the people to attain the objectives of their Constitution.
Precedent	On the other hand, it appears to me that it would be more correct to describe the Directive Principles as laying down the path which was to be pursued by our <ORG> and State Legislatures in moving towards the objectives contained in the Preamble.
Precedent	Indeed, from the point of view of the Preamble, both the fundamental rights and the Directive Principles are means of attaining the objectives which were meant to be served both by the fundamental rights and Directive Pri nciples.
Precedent	1861.
Precedent	If any distinction between the fundamental rights and the Directive Principles on the basis of a difference between ends or means were really to be attempted, it would be more proper, in my opinion to view fundamental rights as the ends of the endeavours of the Indian people for which the Directive principles provided the guidelines.
Precedent	It would be still better to view both fundamental rights and the "fundamental" Directive Principles as guide lines.
Precedent	1862.
Precedent	Perhaps, the best way of describing the relationship between the fundamental rights of individual citizens, which imposed corresponding obligations upon the <ORG> and the Directive Principles, would be to look upon the Directive principles as laying down the path of the country's progress towards the allied objectives and aims stated in the Preamble, with fundamental rights as the limits of that path, like the banks of a flowing river, which could, be mended or amended by displacements replacements or curtailments or enlargements of any part  according to the needs of those who had to use the path.
Precedent	In other words, the requirements of the path itself were more important.
Precedent	A careful reading of the debates in <ORG> also lead me to this premise or assumption.
Precedent	If the path needed widening or narrowing or changing, the limits could be changed.
Precedent	It seems to be impossible to say that the path laid down by the Directive Principles is less important than the limits of that path.
Precedent	Even though the Directive Principles are "non-justiciable," in the sense that they could not be enforced through a Court, they were declared, in Article 37 , as "the principles...fundamental in the governance of the country".
Precedent	The mandate of Article 37 was : "it shall be the duty of the <ORG> to apply these principles in making laws".
Precedent	Primarily the mandate was addressed to the <ORG> and the <ORG> Legislatures, but, in so far as Courts of justice can indulge in some judicial law making, within the interstices of the Constitution or any Statute before them for cons truction, the <ORG> too are bound by this mandate.
Precedent	1863.
Precedent	Another distinction, which seems to me to be valid and very significant it that, whereas, the fundamental rights were "conferred" upon citizens, with corresponding obligations of the <ORG>, the Directive Principles lay down specific duties of the <ORG> organs.
Precedent	In conferring fundamental rights, freedom of individual citizens, viewed as individuals, were sought to be protected, but, in giving specific directives to <ORG> organs, the needs of social welfare, to which individual freedoms may have to yield, were put in the forefront.
Precedent	A reconciliation between the two was, no doubt, to be always attempted whenever this was reasonably possible.
Precedent	But, there could be no doubt, in cases of possible conflict, which of the two had to be subordinated when found embodied in laws properly made.
Precedent	1864.
Precedent	Article 38 shows that the first of the specific mandates to <ORG> organs says:  38.
Precedent	The <ORG> shall strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting  as effectively as it may a social order in which justice, social, economic and political, shall inform all the institutions of the national life.
Precedent	In other words, promotion of a social order in which "justice, social, economic, and political" was the first duty of all the organs of the <ORG>.
Precedent	1865.
Precedent	The second specific mandate to <ORG>, organs, found in Article 39 , contains the principles of what is known as the socialistic "welfare <ORG>".
Precedent	It attempts to promote social justice by means of nationalisation and <ORG> action for a better distribution of material resources of the country among its citizens and to prevent the exploitation of She weak and the helpless.
Precedent	It runs as follows:  39.
Precedent	The <ORG> shall, in particular, direct its policy towards securing:  (a) that the citizens, men and women equally, have the light to an adequate means of livelihood.
Precedent	(b) that the ownership and control of the material resources or the community are so distributed as best to subserve the common good;  (c) that the operation  of the economic system does not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to the common deteriment;  (d) that there is equal pay for equal work for both men and women;  (e) that the health and strength of workers, men and women, and the tender age of children are not abused and that citizens are not forced by economic necessity to enter avocations unsuited to their age or strength;  (f) that childhood and youth are protected against exploitation and against moral and material abandonment.
Precedent	1866.
Precedent	On the views stated above, it would be difficult to hold that, the necessarily changeable limits of the path, which is contained in the Directive Principles, are more important than the path itself.
Precedent	I may mention here that it was observed in one of the early <ORG> decisions of <ORG> in Motilal and Ors.
Precedent	v. <ORG> and Ors.
Precedent	A.I.R.
Fact	1951 All.
Fact	257 @ 296 by <NAME> I shall also say a few words about the directives of <ORG> policy which, thoug h not justiciable, may be taken info account in considering the Constitution as a whole.
Fact	These directives lay down the principles which it will be the duty of the <ORG> to apply in the making of laws and their execution.
Fact	Article 38 states that the <ORG> shall strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting as effectively as it may a social order in which justice, social, economic and political shall inform all the institutions of the national life".
Fact	Article 39 lays down the principles which must inspire <ORG> policy.
Fact	Articles 40 to 51 concern themselves with such questions inter alia, as, for example, the right to work, to education and to public assistance, the promotion of education and economic interest of scheduled castes and the duty of the <ORG> to raise the level of of nutrition and to improve public health".
Fact	My object in drawing attention to the nature of these objectives is to show that what the framers of the Constitution were after was to establish, what is generally known,  now as the 'welfare' or the 'social service state', in this country.
Fact	They had taken a comprehensive view of <ORG> activities and it is quite clear that they were not dominated by the laissez faire thought of the last century.
Fact	So much about Directives.
Precedent	Now we come to fundamental rights".
Precedent	The object of these fundamental rights, as far as I can gather from a reading of the Constitution itself, was not merely to provide security to and equality of citizenship of the people living in this land and thereby helping the process of nation-building, but also and not less importantly to provide certain standards of conduct, citizenship, justice and fair play.
Precedent	In the background of the Indian Constitution, they were intended to make all citizens and persons appreciate that the paramount law of the land has swept away privilege and has laid down that there is to be perfect equality between one section of the community and another in the matter of all those rights which are essential for the material and moral perfection  of man".
Precedent	1867.
Precedent	Indeed, in <CITATION_682447> A.I.R.
Precedent	1968 All.
Precedent	14, <NAME> went so as far to hold that "the duty of the <ORG>" under Article 37 to apply these principles in "making laws" was to be carried out even by the judiciary of the <ORG> whenever it had a choice between two possible constructions that is to say, when it could indulge in judicial "law making".
Precedent	1868.
Precedent	The next topic on which I will venture to make some observations is the significance and meaning of the word "sovereign".
Precedent	What was constituted by <ORG>, speaking for the people of India, was a "Sovereign Democratic Republic".
Precedent	1869.
Precedent	Here, I may, mention the well-known distinction between "political sovereignty" and "legal sovereignty".
Precedent	<NAME> in his <ORG> (tenth edition), discussing the nature of Parliamentary Sovereignty said (at page 73):  The matter indeed may be carried a little further, and we may assert that the arrangements of the Constitution are now such as to ensure that the will of the electors  shall by regular and Constitutional means always in the end assert itself as the predominant influence in the country.
Precedent	But this is a political, not a legal fact.
Precedent	The electors can in the long run, always enforce their will.
Precedent	But the courts will take no, notice of the will of the electOrs.
Precedent	The judges know nothing about any will of the people except in so far as that will be expressed by an Act of Parliament, and would never suffer the validity of a statute to be questioned on the ground of its having been passed or being kept alive in opposition to the wishes of the electOrs.
Precedent	The political sense of the word 'sovereignty' is, it is true, fully as important as the legal sense or more so.
Precedent	But the two significations, though intimately connected together, are essentially different, and in some part of his work <NAME> has apparently confused the one sense with the other".
Precedent	1870.
Precedent	Legally, <ORG> transferred the whole of its legal sovereignty over the people and territories of this country in British In dia to <ORG> which spoke in the name of the people of India.
Precedent	The Princely States came in through "Instruments of accession".
Precedent	This means that the legal sovereignty was vested in <ORG> whereas the people of India may be said to be only politically "sovereign".
Precedent	Their views were carefully ascertained and expressed, from various angles, by the Members of <ORG>, political sovereign thus operated outside the ambit of law yet made its impact and effect felt upon the legal sovereign, that is to say, <ORG>.
Precedent	In recognition of this fact and to bring out that it was really speaking on behalf of the people of India, <ORG> began the Preamble with the words : "We, the people of India".
Precedent	This meant, in my estimation, nothing more than that <ORG> spoke for the people of India even though it was vested with the legal authority to shape the destiny of this country through the Constitution framed by it.
Precedent	There is not to  be found, anywhere in our Constitution, any transfer of legal sovereignty to the people of India.
Precedent	1871.
Precedent	The people of India speak through their representatives in the two <ORG>.
Precedent	They approach the courts for the assertion of their rights.
Precedent	The courts adjudicate upon the rights claimed by them and speak for the Constitution and not directly for the people.
Precedent	Judges and other dignitaries of <ORG> as well as Members of <ORG> take oadhs of allegiance to the Constitution and not to the people of India.
Precedent	In other words, the Constitution is the "Legal sovereign" recognised by <ORG>, although the ultimate 'political' sovereignty may and does reside in "the people".
Precedent	1872.
Precedent	We need not, I think", embark on any academic discourse upon the various meanings of the term "sovereignty" which has given much trouble to political thinkers and jurists such as <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and others.
Precedent	I will be content with quoting the views of Prof. <NAME> expressed in his "Principles of Social &  Political Theory" an the nature and meaning of the term "sovereignty", as the lawyers generally understand it.
Precedent	He says (at page 59):  There must exist in the <ORG>, as a legal association, a power of of final legal adjustment of all legal issues which arise in its ambit.
Precedent	The legal association will not be a single unit, and law will not be a unity, unless there is somewhere one authority to which crucial differences ultimately come, and which gives, as the authority of last resort, the ultimate and final decision.
Precedent	Different social groups may press different views of what is, or ought to be, law; it is even possible that different departments of the <ORG> may hold, and seek to enforce, different notions of what is legally right; there must be a final adjustment centre.
Precedent	That final adjustment-centre is the sovereign, the topmost rung of the ladder, the superanus or soverano, the 'authority of the last word'.
Precedent	Sovereignty is not the same as general <ORG>-authority, or puissance publique : it is the particular sort  of <ORG> authority which is the power and the right of ultimate decision".
Precedent	In one sense sovereignty is 'unlimited-unlimited and illimitable.
Precedent	There is no question arising in the legal association, and belonging to the sphere of its operation, which may not come up to the sovereign, and which will not be finally decided by the sovereign if it so comes up to the topmost rung.
Precedent	The adjustment-centre must be competent to adjust every issue, without exception, which may stand in need of adjustment.
Precedent	But there are other considerations also to be noticed; and these will show us that sovereignty, if it is not limited to particular questions and definite objects (limited, that is to say, in regard to the things which it handles), is none the less limited and defined by its own nature and its own mode of action".
Precedent	In the first place, and as regards its nature, sovereignty is the authority of the last word.
Precedent	Only questions of the last resort will therefore be brought to the sovereign.
Precedent	Much will be settled in the lower range s and in the ordinary course of the action of general State-authority.
Precedent	In the second place, and as regards its mode of action, the sovereign is a part : and an organ of the legal association.
Precedent	Nothing will therefore come to the soverign which does not belong to the nature and operation of the legal association, as such.
Precedent	Sovereignty moves within the circle of the legal association, and only within that circle; it decides upon questions of a legal order, and only upon those questions.
Precedent	Moving within that circle, and deciding upon those questions, sovereignty will only make legal pronouncements, and it will make them according to regular rules of legal procedure.
Precedent	It is hot a capricious power of doing anything in any way : it is legal power of settling finally legal questions in a legal way".
Precedent	1873.
Precedent	Prof. <NAME> went on to say  (a) Ultimately, and in the very last resort, the sovereign is the Constitution itself-the Constitution which is the efficient and formal cause of the association; which brings it into  being; which forms and defines the organs and methods of its operations, and may also form and define (if the Constitution either contains or is accompanied by a 'declaration of right') the purposes of its operation.
Precedent	It may be objected to this view that the sovereign is a body of living persons, and not an impersonal scheme; and that ultimate sovereignty must accordingly be ascribed, not to the Constitution, but to the Constitution-making body behind it which can alter and amend its provisions.
Precedent	But there is an answer to that objection.
Precedent	The impersonal scheme of the Constitution is permanently present, day by day, and year by year; it acts continuously, and without interruption, as the permanent control of the whole operation of the <ORG>.
Precedent	The body of persons which can alter and amend the Constitution (and which, by the way, can act only under the Constitution, and in virtue of the Constitution) is a body which acts only at moments of interruption,, and therefore at rare intervals.
Precedent	The continuous control may m ore properly be termed sovereign than the occasional interruption; and we may accordingly say that the Constitution itself, in virtue of being such a control, is the ultimate sovereign".
Precedent	(b) Secondarily, however, and subject to the ultimate sovereignty of the Constitution we may say that the body which makes ordinary law, in the sense of issuing the day-to-day and the year-by-year rules of legal conduct, is the immediate sovereign.
Precedent	That body may be differently composed in different political systems.
Precedent	In the United States, for example, it is composed of <ORG> and President acting independently (though with mutual checks and reciprocal powers of overriding one another's authority) on a system of co-ordination.
Precedent	In the United Kingdom it is composed of <ORG> and His Majesty's Ministers acting interdependently, and with a mutual give and take (though here too there are mutual checks, the <ORG> can dismiss the Ministers by an adverse vote as vice versa they can dismiss <ORG> by advising His Majest y to use his power of dissolution), on a system which is one of connextion rather than co-ordination.
Precedent	However composed, the body which makes the ordinary law of the land is the immediate sovereign, which issues final legal pronouncements on ordinary currrent questions to the extent and by the methods authorized under the Constitution.
Precedent	The immediate sovereign which makes the ordinary law in the United Kingdom is authorized by the Constitution to a greater extent of action, and to action by easier and speedier methods, than the the immediate sovereign which makes the ordinary law in the United States; but in either case the immediate sovereign is a body authorized by the Constitution, acting and able to act because it is so authorized".
Precedent	On the argument which is here advanced the Constitution is the ultimate sovereign, in virtue of being the permanent scheme, or standing expression, of what may be called the primary law of the political association; and the law and rule-making body is the immediate sovereign, i n virtue of being the constant source and perennially active fountain of what may be called the secondary law of the land.
Precedent	Two difficulties confront the argument, one of them largely formal, but the other more substantial The first and largely formal difficulty is that it would appear to be inconsistent to begin by ascribing ultimate sovereignty to the Constitution rather than to the Constitution-making body, and then to proceed to ascribe immediate sovereignty to the law and rule making body rather than to the law.
Precedent	Does not consistency demand either that both sovereigns should be impersonal systems, or that both should be personal bodies; either that the ultimate sovereign should be 'the rule of the Constitution' and the immediate sovereign 'the rule of law', or that the ultimate sovereign should be the Constitution-making body and the immediate the law and rule-making body ?
Precedent	We may answer that inconsistency is inherent in the nature of the case.
Precedent	The position of the primary law of the <ORG> is different from  that of the secondary law".
Precedent	1874.
Precedent	I have quoted rather extensively from the views of Prof. <NAME> as they appeared to me to have a special significance for explaining the relevant provisions of our Constitution.
Precedent	Indeed, Prof. <NAME> begins his exposition by citing the Preamble to the Constitution of India; and, he gives this explanation in his preface for such a beginning:  I ought to explain, as I end, why the preamble to the Constitution of India is printed after the table of contents.
Precedent	It seemed to me, when I read it, to state in a brief and pithy form the argument of much of the book; and it may accordingly serve as a key-note.
Precedent	I am the more moved to quote it because I am proud that the people of India should begin their independent life by subscribing to the principles of a political tradition which we in the West call Western, but which is now something more than Western.
Precedent	1875.
Precedent	The "sovereignty of the Constitution", as I see it, is "a feature", as <NAME> put it in his Theory of the Stal e, "inherent in a genuine whole".
Precedent	This means that it is not vested in all its aspects in any one of the three organs of the <ORG> but may be divided between them A mark of such sovereignty is certainly the possession of "Constituent Power", although the totality of sovereign power may be divided.
Precedent	<NAME> wrote, in his "Grammar of Politics" (pages 296-297):  It may yet be fairly argued that, in every <ORG>, some distinction between the three powers is essential to the maintenance of freedom.
Precedent	Since the work of <NAME> and <NAME>, we have come generally to admit the truth of <NAME>'s remark that the accumulation of all powers...in the same hands...may justly be pronounced the very definition of tyranny.
Precedent	1876.
Precedent	In order to avoid concentration of such excessive power in few hands that it may corrupt or be misused by chose who wield it, our Constitution also divides or distributes legal sovereignty into three branches or organs of the <ORG> the <ORG>, the <ORG>, and the <ORG>.
Precedent	The sphere of the sove reignty of each is sought to be so demarcated by our.
Precedent	Constitution that the "genuine whole" appears in the form of three intersecting circles.
Precedent	In those portions of these circles where the judicial power intersects the legislative and the executive powers, the judicature acts as the supervisor or guardian of the Constitution and can check legislative or executive action.
Precedent	But, in the remaining parts of the two interhecting circles of the Legislative and the Executive spheres, the two other branches are supreme legally, just as the judicature is in its own, so that their decisions there cannot be questioned by the judicial branch of the <ORG>.
Precedent	1877.
Precedent	Here we are concerned only with the relationship between judicial and the legislative organs.
Precedent	Our Constitution makes the judicature the ultimate testing authority, as the guardian of the Constitution, in so far as the ordinary law making is concerned.
Precedent	In the sphere of the primary fundamental law of the Constitution lies also the amending power contained in Article 36 8 of the Constitution over which the control of the judicature is limited to seeing that the form and the manner of the amendment is properly observed.
Precedent	Beyond that, the authority of the judicial organ over the Constituent power vested in the Constitutional bodies or organs mentioned in Article 368 of the Constitution ceases.
Precedent	No doubt the judicial organ has to decide the question of the limits of a sovereign authority as well as that of other authorities in cases of dispute.
Precedent	But, when these authorities act within these limits, it cannot interfere.
Precedent	1878.
Precedent	After having made a few observations about the nature of the sovereignty of the Constitution and the judicial function connected with it.
Precedent	I will say something about the urge for dynamic changes amply disclosed by the speeches in <ORG>, which is found embodied in the Preamble as well as the Directive Principles of our Constitution.
Precedent	<NAME> observed in the "Indian Constitution : Cornerstone of a Nation" (at page 43):  What was of grea test importance to most <ORG> members, however, was not that socialism be embodied in the Constitution, but that a democratic Constitution, with a socialist bias be framed so as to allow the nation in the future to become as socialist as its citizens desired or as its needs demanded.
Precedent	Being, in general, imbued with the goals, the humanitarian bases, and some of the techniques of social democratic thought, such was the type of Constitution that Constituent <ORG> members created.
Precedent	1879.
Precedent	Thus, the direction towards which the nation was to proceed was indicated but the precise methods by which the goals were to be attained, through socialism or state action, were left to be determined by the <ORG> organs of the future.
Precedent	In laying down the principles, by means of which the poverty-stricken, exploited, down-trodden, ignorant, religion and superstition ridden masses of India, composed of diverse elements, were to be transferred into a strong united, prosperous, modern nation, it was assumed and said repeatedly t hat India's economy must change its feudal character.
Precedent	Its social patterns, modes of thought and feeling, were to be changed and guided by scientific thinking and endavour so as to lead its people on towards higher and higher ranges of achievement in every direction.
Precedent	1880.
Precedent	Our Constitution-makers, who included some of the most eminent jurists in the country, could not have been ignorant of the teachings of our own ancient jurists, <NAME> and <NAME>, who had pointed out that the laws of each age are different.
Precedent	In support of this view, the late Dr. <NAME>, in his treatise on Hindu Law, has cited the original passages from <NAME> and <NAME> which run as follows:  (1) <NAME> tretaayam duaaparey parey anye kali yugey nreenaam yoga roopaanusaaratah-<NAME>.
Precedent	(2) <NAME> <NAME> parey anye kali yugey nreenaam yuga roopaanusaratah-<NAME>.
Precedent	1881.
Precedent	An English translation of the sense of the above passages runs as follows: 1882.
Precedent	"The fundamental laws (imposing fu ndamental duties or conferring fundamental rights) differ from age to age; they are different in the age known as krita from those in the dvaapara age; the fundamental, laws of the kali age are different from all previous ages; the laws of each age conform to the distinctive character of the age (yuga roopa nusaara tah)".
Precedent	In other words, even our ancient jurists recognised the principle that one generation has no right to down future generations to its own views or laws even on fundamentals.
Precedent	The fundamentals may be different not merely as between one society and another but also as between one generation and another of the same society or nation.
Precedent	1883.
Precedent	At any rate, I am convinced that we cannot infer from anything in the language of the unamended Article 368 any distinction, beyond that found in the more difficult procedure prescribed for amendment of certain Articles, between more and less basic parts of the Constitution.
Precedent	None are sacrosanct and transcendental, in the sense that they are immune from and outs ide the process of amendment found in Article 368 and while others only are subject to and within its ambit even before its amendment.
Precedent	1884.
Precedent	My learned Brother <NAME>, J., has, very, aptly, compared the mode of progress visualized by the Constitution as the movement of the chakra.
Precedent	Such a movement naturally involves that a part of the nation which may have been at the top at one time may move towards the bottom and then come back to the top again.
Precedent	The Constitution, however, visualizes the progress of the whole nation towards greater equality as well as prosperity.
Precedent	The function of the amending provision, in such a Constitution, must necessarily be that of an instrument for dynamic and basic changes in the future visualized by our Constitution makers.
Precedent	The whole Constitution is based on the assumption that it is a means of progress of all the people of India towards certain goals.
Precedent	The course of progress may involve, as choices of lesser of two evils, occasional abrogations or sacrifices of some fundamental right s, to achieve economic emancipation of the masses without which they are unable to enjoy any fundamental rights in any real sense.
Precedent	The movement towards the goals may be so slow as to resemble the movement of a bullock-cart.
Precedent	But, in this age of the automobile and the aeroplane, the movement could be much faster.
Precedent	1885.
Precedent	The Constitutional function with which the judiciary is entrusted, in such a Constitution, is to see that the chosen vehicle does not leave the charted course or path or transgress the limits prescribed by the Constitution at a particular time.
Precedent	The fundamental rights, as I have said eariler, may be viewed as such limits.
Precedent	The power of amendment, in a Constitution such as ours, must include the power to change these limitations to suit the needs of each age and generation.
Precedent	As the celebrated Justice <NAME> said in his "Common Law", the life of law has not been logic, but the "felt necessities" of the times.
Precedent	Every kind of law, whether fundamental or ordinary, has to be an attempted adaptation to the  needs of the people at a particular time.
Precedent	The power of adaptation in a progressive nation, with a Constitution which visualizes a movement towards socialism must, therefore, be construed in the context of the whole setting of urges enshrined in the Constitution and what their satisfaction demands.
Precedent	So construed, it may involve changes in the very features considered basic today.
Precedent	1886.
Precedent	I think it has been properly pointed out by Mr. <NAME>, the Attorney General, and Mr. <NAME>, the Advocate-General of Maharashtra, that the proper function of Article 368 , in a Constitution is to act as a safety valve against violent revolution.
Precedent	It can only so operate as a safety valve if we do not construe the powers of amendment contained in it so narrowly as to import, contrary to the clear meaning of its explicit language, any bar against the alteration or change of any features of our Constitution which may be characterised as basic.
Precedent	1887.
Precedent	We have been taken through a number of principles of interpretation and constru ction of documents, including a document such as our Constitution, containing the fundamental law of the land.
Precedent	It has been properly pointed out that the amending power, in so elaborate a Constitution, could not possibly omit from its ambit or scope the power of amendment of any part of it so that the 24th Amendment merely clarifies the original intention to lodge a wide amending power within the bosom of Article 368.
Precedent	It has been rightly pointed out that the careful manner in which the Constitution, and, particularly, the amending Article 368 was framed precludes the possibility of a delibrate casus omissus so as to exclude from its scope the making of any provision which may either take away or abridge or affect a fundamental right or any other basic feature.
Precedent	In any case, in such a Constitution as ours, we must strongly lean against a construction which may enable us to hold that any part of the Constitution is exempt from the scope of Article 368 as originally framed.
Precedent	Without express words in Article 368 its elf to that effect, I am not prepared to merely presume or infer the presence of any casus omissus here.
Precedent	1888.
Precedent	It was no doubt argued, on the strength of the Golak Nath case (supra), that direct or indirect abridgement or taking away of a fundamental right by an amendment under Article 368 was expressly barred by the language of Article 13(2) of the Constitution.
Precedent	I am in agreement with the views of my learned brethern who hold that Article 13(2) is meant to deal with ordinary laws or the functions of the <ORG> and of <ORG> <ORG> in their ordinary law-making capacities.
Precedent	It was not intended to extend its scope indirectly to Article 368 which deals with the amendment of the fundamental law itself of which Article 13(2) is a part.
Precedent	The language and the context as well as the subject matter of it, found stated in Article 13(2) of the Constitution itself, preclude me from holding that it could possibly operate as a restriction on the powers of amendment of any part of the Constitution contained in Articl e 368 of the Constitution even before it was amended by the 24th Amendment.
Precedent	1889.
Precedent	The majority of the learned Judges of this Court in <NAME> case (<NAME>) held that the power of amendment itself and not merely its procedure was contained in Article 368 of the Constitution.
Precedent	They also held this power of amendment to be wide.
Precedent	<NAME>, J., however, thought that the ambit of the term "law", as used in Article 13(2) of the Constitution, was wide enough to cover a change in the fundamental law on which Article 368 exclusively operates.
Precedent	The view of <NAME>, J., turned the scales by a narrow majority of one in favour of the opinion that Article 13(2) operates as an express restriction upon the powers contained in Article 368 even though it does not say so expressly.
Precedent	The limitation was inferred from the wide meaning given to the term "law".
Precedent	But the view of the majority of Judges of this Court who have had the occasion to consider this question, that is, if we include or add the number of those who gave de cisions in <CITATION_1308308>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	933 and <CITATION_1706770>  S.C.R.
Precedent	89, is still in favour of the view that the word "law", as used in Article 13(2) of the Constitution, does not extend to the fundamental law or the Constitution.
Precedent	If it was really the intention to so extend it, at least Article 13(2) would have clarified it.
Precedent	1890.
Precedent	I am not impressed by the contention that Article 13(2 ), as originally passed by <ORG>, contained a specific exemption of the powers of amendment exercised under Article 368 of the Constitution which was dropped afterwards.
Precedent	If the dropping of this clause was intended to bring about also drastic a change in the intention of the Constitution makers as the counsel for the petitioners contends for, there would have been some explanation given by <ORG> for such a change.
Precedent	Moreover, we have not been shown what authority the drafting committee had to adopt language implying so drastic a change of intention of <ORG> without even  bringing the matter to the notice of <ORG>.
Precedent	The safer presumption is that the drafting committee dropped the addition proposal by Mr. <NAME> and adopted by <ORG> merely because it considered the additional words to be otiose and unnecessary.
Precedent	1891.
Precedent	Our Constitution itself contains in various places a distinction between the Constitution and the law.
Precedent	It mentions both the "Constitution and the law" suggesting that there is a difference between them made by the Constitution itself.
Precedent	See : e.g.
Precedent	: (1) Form of oath of the President prescribed by Article 60 of the Constitution to "preserve protect, and defend "the Constitution and the law".
Precedent	(2) The form of oath or affirmation, prescribed by Article 159 of the Constitution for the Governor of a State to "protect and defend the Constitution and the law".
Precedent	(3) The form of oath prescribed by Article 75(4) for a Union Minister given in Schedule III-Form I to do "right to all manner of people in accordance with the Constitution and  the law", (4) The form of oath prescribed for a Judge of the Supreme Court, under Article 124(6) of the Constitution, given in Third Schedule-Form IV, to "uphold the Constitution and the laws".
Precedent	The form is the same for the Comptroller and Auditor-General of India under Article 148(2) of the Constitution.
Precedent	(5) The form of the oath prescribed by Article 164(4) of the Constitution for a Minister of a State Government given in Third Schedule Form V to "do right to all manner of people in accordance with Constitution and the law".
Precedent	(6) The form of oath prescribed by Article 219 of the Constitution for a <ORG> judge given in Form VIII-Third Schedule to "uphold the Constitution and the laws".
Precedent	1892.
Precedent	Clause 7 of the Fifth Schedule part D, of the Constitution only explains the meaning of word amend as covering an "addition, variation or repeal" and similar is the case with Clause 21 of the Sixth Schedule.
Precedent	I am not attracted by the distinction between amendments, which are "deemed" not to be amendments, falling wi thin Article 368 , mentioned in the Fifth and Sixth Schedules, and actual amendments covered by Article  368.
Precedent	The word "deemed" was used in these provisions and Articles 4 and 169 merely to indicate that the procedure required by Article 368 was not required here.
Precedent	These provisions certainly furnish an aid in construing and fixing the meaning of the word "amendment" wherever used in the Constitution.
Precedent	And, as I have already held, the scope of amendment must necessarily be wide in the context of the whole Constitution.
Precedent	1893.
Precedent	It may also be noticed that the term "law", which is not used in Article 368 at all, is sought to be defined in Article 13 , Sub- article (3) of the Constitution, after stating explicitly "unless the context otherwise requires".
Precedent	I have already dealt with the context of Article 368 containing the power of amendment which necessarily operates on every part of the Constitution so long as its operation on any part is not found expressly excluded.
Precedent	1894.
Precedent	However, even ignoring the context in which  Article 13(3) itself occurs and other foregoing reasons, if we were to assume, for the sake of argument, that, because law is not exhaustively defined by Article 13(3) of the Constitution, the term "law" used there could include the law of the Constitution, another principle of construction could also apply here.
Precedent	This is that even a prior general provision followed by an express provision dealing with a particular type of law could reasonably exclude the particular and special from the purview and scope of the general.
Precedent	It is immaterial if the general provision precedes the provision containing a special law.
Precedent	This could not really affect the basis of the principle applicable.
Precedent	1895.
Precedent	The principle indicated above has been usually applied between different pieces of legislation or to different <ORG>.
Precedent	There is no doubt that when the subsequent Act is general and the prior Act is special, the Special Act is not repealed by the provisions of the general Act by the application of the maxim : "Generalia specialibus n on derogant" i.e.
Precedent	provisions will not abrogate special provisions (See : Crates on Statute Law p. 376).
Precedent	Again, "if a special enactment, whether it be in a public or private Act, and a subsequent general Act or absolutely repugnant and inconsistent with one another", it has been said that "the <ORG> have no alternative but to declare the prior special enactment repealed by the subsequent general Act".
Precedent	See : Craies on Statute Law p. 380).
Precedent	On the same principle, it has been held that a subsequent particular Act may have the effect of partially repealing the earlier general Act.
Precedent	(See : <NAME> v. <NAME>  L.R.
Precedent	4 Q.B.
Precedent	330 Heston & Isleworth U.D.C.
Precedent	v. <NAME>  2 Ch.
Precedent	306 <CITATION_1355522> ).
Precedent	A.I.R.
Precedent	1954 S.C. 465.
Precedent	1896.
Precedent	The above mentioned principle has been applied generally where the question has arisen whether the particular law prevails over, and, therefore, repeals the general law.
Precedent	It has, however, also been held that the principle may operate to merely curtail the operation of the general law by exempting from  its scope the special cases dealt with by the particular law (See : Re <NAME>; [1887] 36 Ch.
Precedent	D. 573 @ p. 577 <NAME> v. <NAME>, Harishanker <NAME> v. <ORG> (Supra).).
Precedent	In other words, the principle may so operate as to curb or reduce the extent or ambit of applicability of the general law.
Precedent	An application of this principle would also show that Constitutional law, as Special Law, may be removed from the purview of "law", as found in Article 13 of the Constitution, even if, by stretching one's imagination, it was really possible to so stretch the scope of the term "law", as used in Article 13 of the Constitution, as would include, but for such a principle, amendments of the Constitution.
Precedent	Prima facie, however, amendments of the Constitution operate on every provision of the Constitution unless any part of it is expressly excluded from the scope of such operation.
Precedent	The use of such a principle to remove an assumed conflict does not appear necessary.
Precedent	1896.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME>, presumably faced with insurmountable di fficulties in relying entirely upon the very narrow majority decision in <NAME> case (Supra), in favour of the view that Article 13(2) operates as a restriction upon the power of amendment contained in Article 368 of the Constitution, relied primarily upon a theory of implied limitations.
Precedent	The only "implied" limitation which I can read into the word amendment, as "perhaps" necessarily implied, or, as part of the meaning of the word "amendment" is the one so characterised by <NAME> in <NAME> case (supra).
Precedent	In other words, it may not include the power of completely abrogating the Constitution at one stroke.
Precedent	It, however, seems wide enough to erode the Constitution completely step by step so as to replace it by another.
Precedent	1897.
Precedent	The Attorney General himself had, very properly, conceded that the scope of amendment could not be so wide as to create a vacuum by abrogating the rest of the Constitution leaving nothing behind to amend.
Precedent	The Attorney General's argument was that, short of creating such a vacu um, the power is wide enough to cover a replacement of the present Constitution by another.
Precedent	It seems to me that the necessary implication of the word "amendment" or the meaning of the term itself may exclude a possible complete abrogation of the present Constitution although that could be done, step by step, by the bodies empowered to amend if they so desired and followed the appropriate procedure.
Precedent	1898.
Precedent	For the reasons already given at length by my brethern <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME> with whom I concur, I find that there is nothing in cases cited which could enable us to put in implied limitations, in a Constitution such as ours, on Article 368 , containing expressly the sovereign law-making power of amendment of every part of it.
Precedent	The cases have really little bearing on the interpretation of such a provision containing the constituent power.
Precedent	As they were cited before us and examined by us, I will very briefly refer to the main cases cited.
Precedent	1899.
Precedent	The American cases really go against the submission that  relied limitations could be put on expressly stated Constitutional powers.
Precedent	They were : <NAME> v. <NAME> 258 U.S. p. 130 U.S.A. v. <NAME> & <NAME> 282 U.S. p. 716 State of Rhode Island v. <NAME>, Attorney General etc.
Precedent	253 U.S. p. 350.
Precedent	<NAME> v. U.S. 320 U.S. p. 118 @ p. 137-145.
Precedent	1900.
Precedent	The cases from Australia decided by <ORG> were : <NAME> v. The King 1920 A.C. p. 691., <NAME> v. Attorney General of Queensland 23 C.L.R.
Precedent	p. 457 where an interpretation of Section 5 of the Colonial Law Validity Act was given in the light of a presumption that the power transferred to a British Colonial Legislature must be read subject to the fundamental assumption underlying the Constitution of the British Empire that the position of the <ORG> has not been affected; <NAME> v. <NAME> [1907] A.C. p. 81 where the theory of implied restrictions on powers found in the <ORG> Parliament Act was rejected; <NAME> v. <ORG>, 45 Australian L.J.
Precedent	p. 251 where , without questioning the basic principle of grant of plenary powers of legislation, laid down by Lord <NAME> in Q. v. Burah (1878) 3 A.C. 889 a decision was given on the lack of powers in <ORG>, to tax a State, on a subject falling outside Section 51 of the Australian Constitution, which laid down the powers of taxation of <ORG>, in the course of which some observations were made on the implications of Federalism which assumes the continued existence of States.
Precedent	1901.
Precedent	The cases from Canada may lend some support to the implications of a grant of power contained by an enactment of the sovereign British <ORG>, but they do not appear to me to be helpful in the context of the theory of the sovereignty of our Constitution, of which Article 368 is a pivotal part, which we have adopted.
Precedent	The cases from Canada cited before us were : <ORG> cases 1938 (2) D.L.R.
Precedent	p. 81 <NAME> v. <NAME> & Attorney General of Quebec 1957 (7) D.L.R.
Precedent	p. 337 <NAME> v. <ORG> <ORG> 1953 (4) D.L.R.
Precedent	p. 461 A.G. for the Province of Ontario and <NAME>.
Precedent	v. A.G. for the Dominion of Canada and Anr.
Precedent	A.C. p. 571 where the assumption, underlying some of the decisions, that Canada did not possess fully blossomed legislative power, seems to have been repelled; In Re the Initiative and Referendum Act, where legislation offending Section 92 head 1 of the British North America Act, 1867; was held to be invalid.
Argument	1902.
Argument	So far as <NAME>'s case, [1935] <ORG> p. 170 is concerned, Mr. <NAME> could only rely on the minority judgment of <NAME>, <NAME> In <NAME> v. Attorney General for the Irish State [1935] A.C. p. 484 it was conceded on behalf of a petitioner who had challenged the validity of an Act of the Irish Parliament that the majority decision in <NAME>'s case was correct.
Precedent	I do not think that the Irish cases give much help to the petitioners' submissions on implied limitation.
Precedent	1903.
Precedent	Cases coming up from Ceylon also do not assist the petitioners.
Precedent	In <ORG> v.  <NAME> 1965 A.C. p. 172 a provision of the Bribery Amendment Act, 1958, was held to be bad because it conflicted with the provisions of Section 29 of the Ceylon (Constitution) Order in Council, 1946, by which the Constituion of Ceylon was governed.
Precedent	It is, therefore, a simple case of conflict of an enactment of subordinate law making authority with the instrument of Government which regulated subordinate law-making powers and was, therefore, supreme.
Precedent	In that case the requirements of manner and form as laid down in Attorney-General for <CITATION_991082> .
Precedent	1932 A.C. p. 526 were also held not to have been complied with.
Precedent	In <NAME> and Ors.
Precedent	v. The Queen 1967 (1) A.C. p. 259 it was held, with regard to the Acts the validity of which was impugned: ...the Acts could not be challenged on the ground that they were contrary to the fundamental principles of justice.
Precedent	The Colonial Laws Validity Act, 1865, which provided that "colonial laws should be void to the extent that they were r epugnant to an Act of the United Kingdom applicable to the colony but not otherwise and should not be void on the grounds of repugnancy to the law of England, did not leave in existence a fetter of repugnancy to some vague and unspecified law of natural justice : those liberalising provisions were incorporated in, and enlarged by, the Ceylon Independence Act, 1947, of <ORG>, the joint effect of which, with the Ceylon (Constitution) Order in Council, 1946, was to confer on <ORG> the full legislative powers of a sovereign independent state.
Precedent	1904.
Precedent	This case shows that repugnancy to some vague principle of "natural justice" could not invalidate the enactments of a fully competent legislative authority.
Precedent	1905.
Precedent	There can be no question of delegation of the power of amendment if, as I have already indicated, I hold that the Constitution is the principal and the source of all Constitutionally valid power and authority in the eye of law.
Precedent	The principle delegatus non potest delegare is o nly applicable against a delegate but not against the principal.
Precedent	When an amendment is made by an appropriate procedure, the amendment becomes a part of the principal's own will and intention and action.
Precedent	Of course, if the principal is and must necessarily be a human authority, the bodies of persons authorised to amend under Article 368 of the Constitution would share the legislative sovereignty and would constitute the "Principal" whose will is expressed in the amendment.
Precedent	1906.
Precedent	It may be possible to use the test of consequences in order to check an abuse of power by a legally non-sovereign law-making body as the <ORG> is when it does not exercise the Constituent power by the use of the two-thirds' majorities in both Houses of <ORG> as required by Article 368 of the Constitution.
Precedent	It may also be possible to use the theory of implied limitations by implying and annexing rules of natural justice to particular kinds of non-legislative functions laid down by statutory or even Constitutional law.
Precedent	But, this  is done only by presuming that the Constitution did not intend abrogation of the fundamental rules of natural justice.
Precedent	If these rules are sought to be dispensed with by any particular ordinary enactment it may be possible to assail the validity of that enactment when Articles 14 and 19 of the Constitution apply.
Precedent	The exclusion of Articles 14 and 19 by a Constitutionally valid amendment only carves out or creates a new legislative field by a provision which becomes a part of the Constitution by amendment, so that the Constitutional validity of its creation cannot be assailed in any court of law so long as the form and manner prescribed by Article 368 of the Constitution have been observed in making the necessary amendment.
Precedent	Enactments properly falling within this field would be immune from attack for any alleged violations of Articles 14 and 19 and  31.
Precedent	1907.
Precedent	Mr. <NAME> then made an impassioned appeal to the theories of natural law and natural rights sought to be embodied in present day international laws as  well as Constitutional laws.
Precedent	It is not necessary for me to deal at length with the political philosophy or the juristic implications of various and conflicting natural law theories, such as those of <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> or <NAME>, discussed by <NAME> in his "Principles of Political Obligation".
Precedent	I also do not find it necessary to embark on an academic discussion of ancient and medeival theories of natural law.
Precedent	I will, however, quote a passage from <NAME> on Legal Theory (5th Edition-p. 95-96), where the position, place, and uses of "natural law" theories are thus summarised: The history of natural law is a tale of the search of mankind for absolute justice and of its failure.
Precedent	Again and again, in the course of the last 2,500 years, the idea of natural law has appeared, in some form or other, as an expression of the search for an ideal higher than positive law after having been rejected and derided in the interval.
Precedent	With changing social and political conditions the notions about natural law have change d. The only thing that has remained constant is the appeal to something higher than positive law.
Precedent	The object of that appeal has been as often the justification of existing authority as a revolt against it.
Precedent	Natural law has fulfilled many functions.
Precedent	It has been the principal instrument in the transformation of the old civil law of the Romans into a broad and cosmopolitian system; it has been a weapon used by both sides in the fight between the medieval <ORG> and the German emperors; in its name the validity of international law has been asserted, and the appeal for freedom of the individual against absolutism launched.
Precedent	Again it was by appeal to principles of natural law that American judges, professing to interpret the Constitution, resisted the attempt of state legislation to modify and restrict the unfettered economic freedom of the individual.
Precedent	It would be simple to dismiss the whole idea of natural law as a hypocritical disguise for concrete political aspirations and no doubt it has sometimes exercised lit tle more than this function.
Precedent	But there is infinitely more in it.
Precedent	Natural law has been the chief though not the only way to formulate ideals and aspirations of various peoples and generations with reference to the principal moving forces of the time.
Precedent	When the social structure itself becomes rigid and absolute, as at the time of <ORG>, the ideal too will take a static and absolute content.
Precedent	At other times, as with most modern natural law theories, natural law ideals become relative or merely formal, expressing little more than the yearning of a generation which is dissatisfied with itself and the world, which seeks something higher, but is conscious of the relativity of values.
Precedent	It is as easy to deride natural law as it is to deride the futility of mankind's social and political life in general, in its unceasing but hitherto vain search for a way out of the injustice and imperfection for which Western civilisation has found no other solution but to move from one extreme to another".
Precedent	The appeal to some absolu te ideal finds a response in men, particularly at a time of disillusionment and doubt, and in times of simmering revolt.
Precedent	Therfore natural law theories, far from being theoretical speculations, have often heralded powerful political and legal developments".
Precedent	1908.
Precedent	I am not prepared to use any natural law theory for putting a construction on Article 368 of the Constitution which will defeat its plain meaning as well as the objects of the Constitution as stated in the Preamble and the Directive Principles of State Policy.
Precedent	I do not know of any case in which this has been done.
Precedent	Even in <NAME> case (supra) <NAME>, C.J.
Precedent	relied on a natural law theory to strengthen his views really based on an application of the supposed express bar contained in Article 13(2).
Precedent	1909.
Precedent	I have already stated my point of view, that we should approach the questions placed before us from the pragmatic angle of the changing needs of social and economic orders visualised by those who were or are the final Judges of these needs in  exercise of the Constituent power.
Precedent	Checks on possible abuses of such powers do not lie through actions in <ORG> of law.
Precedent	The pressure of public opinion, and the fear of revolt due to misuse of such powers of amendment are the only practically possible checks which can operate if and when such contingencies arise.
Precedent	These checks lie only in the political fields of operation.
Precedent	They are not subject to judicial review or control.
Precedent	In other words, what <NAME> calls the external and the internal limits may operate to control and check possible misuses of such power.
Precedent	<ORG> of justice have no means of control over a power expressly sanctioned by the Constitution which is the legal sovereign.
Precedent	They can only speak for the Constitution.
Precedent	Through their pronouncements must be heard the voice of the Constitution and of nothing beyond it.
Precedent	1910.
Precedent	Although the <ORG> must recognise the validity of the exercise of a legally sovereign constituent power, such power may itself be ineffective for actually bringing about the desired res ults.
Precedent	Whether the change is in the direction of what may be considered better may itself be a matter of dispute.
Precedent	The answers to such questions and disputes depend upon many conditions which are outside the control of law courts.
Precedent	The very existence or absence of such conditions cannot be appropriately investigated or determined in law Courts.
Precedent	Therefore, such investigations lie outside the judicial domain when once a change is brought in by the exercise of constituent or sovereign law making power in accordance with the prescribed procedure.
Precedent	1911.
Precedent	A socialistic state, must have the power and make the attempt to build a new social and economic order free from exploitation, misery and poverty, in the manner those in charge of framing policies and making appropriate laws think best for serving the public good.
Precedent	We do not today conceive of public good or progress in terms of a "movement from status to contract", but in terms of a movement for control of economic and other kinds of powers of exploitation by individu als so as to ensure that public good not merely appears to be served but is actually served by all individuals wherever or however placed.
Precedent	The emphasis today is upon due performance of their social obligations by individuals before claiming any right however fundamental or important it may be because rights and duties are correlative.
Precedent	1912.
Precedent	Another contention advanced was that a creature of the Constitution could not possibly possess the power to create or recreate the Constitution.
Precedent	Therefore, it was contended, resort could not be had to Article 368 to expand the power of amendment.
Precedent	I am unable to accept this contention in the face of the express provision in Clause (e) to the proviso to the Article 368(2) of the Constitution.
Precedent	There Article 368 expressly provided either for the expansion or diminution of the scope of the powers of amendment.
Precedent	It cannot, therefore, be reasonably contended that the power of recreation even of the whole Constitution by stages was not already contained in the unamended Article 36 8.
Precedent	This part of proviso also shows that the Constitution makers contemplated a wide amending power so as to meet the challenges of the times offered by rapidly changing social, political, economic, national and international conditions and situations.
Precedent	We cannot contract what the Constitution makers clearly intended to make elastic and expansible.
Precedent	1913.
Precedent	For the foregoing reasons, I hold that the 24th Amendment of the Constitution is valid.
Precedent	It would, therefore, follow that the 25th and 29th Amendments are also valid.
Precedent	The reasons for the validity of each of these amendments have been so fully dealt by my learned brethren <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, and <NAME>, with most of which I respectfully concur, that I need not discuss or repeat any of them here.
Precedent	Nor have I, for this very reason, attempted to discuss the enormous array of cases, both Indian and foreign, or the great many juristic writings, placed before and closely examined by us.
Precedent	I will, however, indicate before I conclude, my special reasons for holding Sect ion 3 of the Constitution (25th Amendment) Act 1971, adding Article 31C to the Constitution also as valid.
Precedent	1914.
Precedent	Article 31C has two parts.
Precedent	The first part is directed at removing laws passed for giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause  (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 of the Constitution from the vice of invalidity on the ground that any such law "is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Articles 14, 19 and 31 of the Constitution".
Precedent	If we, stop here, the question whether the law is really for the purpose of giving effect to the principles specified in Clauses (b) or (c) of Article 39 would still be justiciable whenever laws passed under this provision come up before Courts.
Precedent	In other words, the question of relevancy of the law passed to the specified principles could still be examined by courts although the effect of invalidity for alleged violations of Articles 14 or 19 or 31 would vanish so long as the law was really mean t to give effect to the principles of Article 39(b) and (c).
Precedent	A colourable piece of legislation with a different object altogether but merely dressed up as a law intended for giving effect to the specified principles would fail to pass the test laid down by the first part.
Precedent	The second part of Article 31C goes on to provide that, if such a law contains a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy, it will become immune from judicial review altogether.
Precedent	In cases of laws passed by State legislatures there is a further safeguard that such laws must have been reserved for consideration by the President and assented to by him.
Precedent	The purpose of the declaration is, therefore, to take the place of a judicial verdict on relevancy of the grounds to the principles found in Clauses  (b) and (c) of Article 39 as well as on effectiveness of these laws for the intended purposes.
Precedent	Nevertheless, the Attorney General and the Solicitor General, appearing for <ORG>, conceded, both in written submissions and in  the course of arguments, that the question of relevancy or nexus with the specified principles would be open to judicial scrutiny in such cases of declarations annexed to laws passed.
Precedent	1915.
Precedent	My learned brother <NAME> has been pleased, despite the concession mentioned above, to declare the second part of Article 31C to be void on the ground among others, that it involves a trespass on the judicial field.
Precedent	It was said that, under the guise of exercise of the power of amendment, one of the pillars of the Constitution or one of the essential features of its basic structure, that is to say, judicial review, had been removed.
Precedent	1916.
Precedent	I think that the concession made on behalf of the Union of India is quite justifiable on a ground which I now proceed to adopt.
Precedent	It is that a declaration by itself is not part of the law made, but it is something only attached to the law even though this annexation is by a purported law.
Precedent	In other words, the declaration, though provided for by law, takes the place of judicial consideration  by the <ORG> and involves consideration of the question whether it is reasonable and necessary to attach such a declaration to a particular law.
Precedent	1917.
Precedent	I do not think that it is necessary for me to decide what the exact nature of the function in giving the declaration is or whether it carries with it, by implication, the proposition that some rules of natural justice must be complied with.
Precedent	Such questions were not argued before us by any party.
Precedent	Nevertheless, I think that the concession could only be made on the strength of the view that the declaration by itself would not preclude a judicial examination of the nexus so that <ORG> can still determine whether the law passed is really one covered by the field carved out by Article 31C or merely pretends to be so protected by parading under cover of the declaration.
Precedent	I, therefore, adopt this reason as perfectly good one for making the concession.
Precedent	Hence, I hold that both parts of Article 31C are valid.
Precedent	1918.
Precedent	On questions relating to the Amendment of Article 31(2)  and the 29th Amendment of the Constitution, I adopt the reasons of my learned brethern <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME> with whose conclusions I concur on these and other questions.
Precedent	1920.
Precedent	My conclusions may now be stated as follows:  (1) The majority view in <NAME> case (supra), holding that Article 13 operated as a limitation upon the powers of Constitutional amendment found in Article 368 , was erroneous.
Precedent	The minority view there was correct on this question.
Precedent	(2) The 24th Amendment is valid.
Precedent	(3) The 25th Amendment, including addition of Article 31C , is valid.
Precedent	(4) The word 'amount' in Article 31(2 ), as amended, does not convey the idea of any prescribed norm.
Precedent	The fixation of the amount or the laying down of a principle for determining the amount are matters within the exclusive power of <ORG> or <ORG> concerned.
Precedent	In other words, the norms and their satisfaction on the question of adequacy of compensation or its reasonableness, are matters within the exclusive competence of the legislativ e authorities to determine.
Precedent	(5) The declaration contemplated by Article 31C is like a certificate given after considering the relevancy of the principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c) of the Constitution, and, therefore, the jurisdiction of the <ORG> is not ousted.
Precedent	The <ORG>s can still consider and decide whether the declaration is really good or a mere pretence attached to a colourable piece of legislation or to a law which has no bearing on or nexus with the principles found in Article 39(b) and (c) of the Constitution.
Precedent	Out of two equally acceptable views, even on the question of nexus, the one in conformity with the legislative verdict should prevail.
Precedent	(6) The 29th Amendment is valid.
Precedent	1921.
Precedent	I would also have the petitions disposed of in the light of decisions given above.
Precedent	I make no order as to costs incurred by parties for this stage of hearing <ORG>, J.

Precedent	9 page 1194 (emphasis added).
Precedent	At bottom the controversy in these cases is as to whether the meaning of the Constitution consists in its being or in its becoming.
Precedent	The <ORG> is called upon to decide whether it is a prison-house or a freeland, whether it speaks for the few or for the many.
Precedent	These issues can hardly be resolved with the aid of foreign legal know-how.
Precedent	Decisions of foreign courts and treatises and articles written on various Constitutions by foreign writers would not be safe guide in construing our Constitution.
Precedent	"(I)n the last analysis the decision must depend upon the words of the Constitution and since no two Constitutions are in identical terms, it is extremely unsafe to assume that a decision on one of them can be applied without qualification to another.
