Precedent	"A Constitution is the expression in national life of the genius of a people.
Precedent	It reflects the tendencies of the age and the articles have to be interpreted, without doing violence to the language, in the light of the pervailing phase of sentiments in the country in which the Constitution is intended to operate."
Precedent	( <CITATION_288909> .)
Precedent	A.I.R.
Precedent	1911 All.
Precedent	251 at page 297 per <NAME> Constitutions which grew up in the 17th, 18th and 19th centuries reflected the hopes and aspirations of men of those times; the Constitution of India reflects the hopes and aspirations of the people of India emerging from colonial economy in the second half of the 20th century.
Precedent	Constitutions framed in the past for organising political democracy cannot serve as a safe guide in construing the Constitution of India framed for ushering in social and economic democracy.
Precedent	1926.
Precedent	Constitutions which grew up in the preceding three centuries were understood to sanctify the Supremacy of Property.
Precedent	Said <NAME> : "The French Revolution has allowed one exclusive right to remain, the right of property, and the main problems of politics will deal with the alterations to be brought about in the right of property-holders."
Precedent	As quoted in French Political Thought in the 19th Century by <NAME>, p. 55.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution is conceived in a radically different tradition.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our forbears did not  believe in the acquisition of things of pleasure (Preya); they stood for the good and the whoelsome (Shrey).
RatioOfTheDecision	They addressed their king as <NAME> because it was his duty to secure the welfare of his people (See <NAME>, "The Acquisitive Society", Chapter II & IV) Their rule of law (Dharma) was intended to help the power-minus keep the power-plus in check.
RatioOfTheDecision	Their rule of law (rita) was a stream, not a puddle.
RatioOfTheDecision	It recognised the inevitability of change.
RatioOfTheDecision	They believed in the moral precept : distribute and enjoy the residue of wealth.
Precedent	(Mahabharata, Shanti Prava, 57 : 11.)
Precedent	1927.
Precedent	The Constitution bears the imprint of the philosophy of our <ORG>.
Precedent	That philosophy was shaped by two pre-eminent leaders of the <ORG>- <NAME> and <NAME>.
Precedent	<NAME> gave to the <ORG> the philosophy of Ahimsa.
Precedent	Two essential elements of his Ahimsa are : (1) equality; and (2) absence of the desire of self-acquisition (Aparigrah).
Precedent	He declared that "to live above the means befitting a p oor country is to live on stolen food."
Precedent	Dr. <NAME>, "The History of the Indian Congress, Vol.
Precedent	I, page 386.
Precedent	And he also said : "I consider it a sin and injustice to use machinery for the purpose of concentration of power and riches in the hands of the few.
Precedent	Today the machinery is used in this way."
Precedent	<NAME> : <ORG>, <ORG>, 1956, page 432.
Precedent	1928.
Argument	While <NAME> laid stress on the ethics of the <ORG>, <NAME> enriched its economic content.
Argument	In his presidential address to the Lahore Congress Session of 1929 he said : "The philosophy of socialism has gradually permeated the entire structure of the society the world over and almost the only point in dispute is the phase and methods of advance to its full realisation.
Argument	India will have to go that way too if she seeks to end her poverty and inequality though she may evove her own methods and may adopt the ideal to the genius of her race."
Precedent	<NAME>, Economic Aspect of a Welfare State in India, page 32.
Precedent	1929.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> sising the intimate and inseverable connection between national liberation and social liberation, he said : "(I)f an indigenous Government took place of the foreign government and kept all the vested interests in tact, this would not be even the shadow of freedom.
RatioOfTheDecision	India's immediate goal can only be considered in terms of the ending of the exploitation of her people.
RatioOfTheDecision	Politically it must mean independence and cession of the British connection; economically and socially it must mean the ending of all special class privileges and vested interests.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> Whither India, 1933.
RatioOfTheDecision	1930.
RatioOfTheDecision	The philosophy of <NAME> was rooted in our ancient tradition; the philosophy of <NAME> was influenced by modern progressive thinking.
RatioOfTheDecision	But the common denominator in their philosophies was humanism.
Precedent	The humanism of the Western Enlightenment comprehended mere poltical equality; the humanism of <NAME> and <NAME> was instinct with social and economic equality.
Precedent	The former made man a political cit izen; the latter aims to make him a 'perfect' citizen.
Precedent	This new humanist philosophy became the catalyst of <ORG>.
Precedent	1931.
Precedent	In 1929 <ORG> resolved that the great poverty and misery of the Indian people was due also "to the economic structure of the society."
Precedent	Indian National <ORG> Resolutions on Economic Policy, Programme and Allied Matters, 1924-1969, p. 3.
Precedent	The Karachi <ORG> resolution, on fundamental rights and economic programme revised in the All India <ORG> Session of Bombay in 1931 declare that in order to end the exploitation of the masses political freedom must include economic freedom of the starving millions.
Precedent	Resolutions, supra pp.
Precedent	6-9.
Precedent	It provided that "property was not to be sequestered or confiscated "save in accordance with law" Ibid (emphasis added).
Precedent	It also provided that the <ORG> shall own or control the key industries and services, mining resources, railways waterways, shipping and other means of public transport."
Precedent	Ibid.
Precedent	According to  the <ORG> Election Manifesto of 1945, "the most vital and urgent of India's problems is how to remove the curse of poverty and raise the standard of masses.
Precedent	Ibid p. 14.
Precedent	It declared that for that purpose it was "necessary...to prevent the concentration of wealth and power in the hands of individuals and groups, and to prevent vested interests inimical to society from growing."
Precedent	Ibid.
Precedent	p. 14.
Precedent	It proposed acquisition of the land of intermediaries on payment of equitable compensation.
Precedent	Ibid.
Precedent	pp.
Precedent	15-16.
Precedent	In November 1947 the All India <ORG> Committee Session at Delhi passed a resolution to the effect that the object of the <ORG> should be to secure "an economic structure which would yield maximum production without the creation of private monopolies and the concentration of wealth."
Precedent	Ibid.
Precedent	pp.
Precedent	18-19.
Precedent	It was thought that such "social structure can provide an alternative to the acquisition of economic and political equality."
Precedent	Ibid.
Precedent	pp.
Precedent	18-19.
Precedent	1932.
Precedent	In sum, <ORG> was committeed : (1) to work for  social, economic and political equality of the weaker sections of the people; (2) to disperse concentration of wealth in any form in a few hands; and (3) to acquire property in accordance with law.
Precedent	Payment of compensation would be determined by equitable considerations and not by market value.
Precedent	The men who took the leading part in framing the Constitution were animated by these noble ideals.
Fact	They embodied them in the Preamble to the Constitution; they proliferated them in the Directive Principles of the State Policy; they gave them ascendancy over the rights in Part III of the Constitution.
Fact	(See Articles 15(3), 16(4), 17, 19(2) to (6), 24, 25(a) and (b), 31(4), (5) and (6)).
Fact	They made them 'fundamental' in the governance of the country.
Precedent	Pandit <NAME> called them 'vital principles'.
Precedent	C.A.D.
Precedent	Vol 9 p. 1288.
Precedent	And indeed so they are, for when translated into life, they will multiply the number of owners of fundamental rights and transform liberty and equality from a privilege into a universal human right .
RatioOfTheDecision	1933.
RatioOfTheDecision	However, pleasing its name-plate or its trumpet, every form of focussed power was suspect in the eyes of the Constitution-makers.
RatioOfTheDecision	They apprehended that concentration of the ownership of the means of production and material resources and the resultant incarceration of wealth in a few profit-seeking hand may bring into being an economic power as all-assimilating and omnicompetent as the Hegelian State.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may manipulate a fall in the prices of raw-materials; it may inflate the prices of manufactures by low production and hoarding; it may increase unemployment and bring down wages; it may shrink investments and control the industrial progress of the nation.
Precedent	<NAME> : American Capitalism, pp.
Precedent	21, 40 and 64; Report of the <ORG> (1965) Vol.
Precedent	1 pp.
Precedent	125, 128, 132 and 134.
Precedent	It may seek to influence politics and public opinion.
Precedent	<NAME>, Ibid, p. 123; <NAME> : Power (<ORG>) p. 85; <ORG> Report p. 136.
Fact	It may try to threaten, restrain a nd change governments in self-interest.
Fact	<NAME>, Ibid.
Fact	pp.
Fact	86, 88 and 124; <ORG> pp.
Fact	1, 135 and 193.
Fact	It may endanger liberty, the rule of law and peace.
Fact	<NAME>, Ibid, pp.
Fact	67 and 70; <NAME>.
Fact	An Introduction to World Politics : <ORG> 1962, p. 4.
Fact	It may retard national unity, the growth of culture and education.
Fact	<ORG>, p. 136.
Fact	To prevent these manifold abuses of the economic power, the Constitution-makers enacted Articles 39(b) and (c).
Fact	It will be legitimate to bear in mind the preemptive significance of Part IV in understanding the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	1934.
Precedent	It is now necessary to consider whether the majority decision in Golaknath (1967) 2 S.C.R.
Precedent	762 is correct.
Precedent	Residence of Amending Power 1935.
Precedent	In Golaknath <NAME> and two other Judges who associated with him and <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME> JJ.
Fact	took the view that the power to amend the Constitution is located in Article 368.
Fact	<NAME> four ot her learned Judges who associated with him, on the contrary, held that Article 368 does not grant the power of amending the Constitution.
Fact	It merely provides for the procedure for amendment of the Constitution.
Fact	I respectfully agree with the view that the amending power resides in the original Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	1936.
RatioOfTheDecision	Despite the marginal note to Article 368 , which indicates that Article 368 is prescribing the procedure for amendment, several considerations clearly show that the amending power is located in Article 368.
Fact	Article 368 provides specifically for a procedure for amending the Constitution.
Fact	When the prescribed procedure is strictly followed, "the Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill."
Fact	<ORG> can bring about this result by strictly following the prescribed procedure.
Fact	Who can bring about a certain result may truly be said to have the power to produce that result.
Fact	Power to amend the Constitution is accordingly necessarily implied in Article 368.
Fact	1937.
Fact	Article 368 fin ds place in Part XX of the Constitution.
Fact	It is the solitary Article in that part.
Fact	If provision was being made in Article 368 merely for procedure for amending the Constitution by <ORG>, the Constitution-makers would have placed it logically under the heading "Legislative procedure" in Part V of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Including the solitary Article 368 in a separate part suggests that it was intended to confer the amending power as well as to provide for the amending procedure.
RatioOfTheDecision	The heading of Part XX is "amendment of the Constitution" and not "procedure for amendment of the Constitution".
RatioOfTheDecision	The heading will include both power as well as procedure.
RatioOfTheDecision	The proviso to Article 368 also shows that the amending power is lodged therein.
RatioOfTheDecision	1938.
RatioOfTheDecision	Power to amend the Constitution cannot reasonably be located in Entry 97 of List I of Schedule VII read with Article 248 of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The idea of a provision for amending the Constitution was indisputably present in the minds of the Constitution- makers.
RatioOfTheDecision	If they had considere d that the power to amend the Constitution was in its nature legislative, they would have surely included in express words this power in a specific entry in List I.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 248 and Entry 97 of List I confer residuary power on <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 246 and List I confer certain specific powers on <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Residuary power is intended to comprehend matters which could not be foreseen by the Constitution-makers at the time of the framing of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	As the topic of amending the Constitution was foreseen by them, it could not have been put in the residuary power.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 245(1) confers power on <ORG> "subject to the provisions of this Constitution."
Fact	Articles 246 and 248 are subject to Article 245.
Precedent	Accordingly, a law made under Article 348 and Entry 97 of List I cannot be inconsistent with any provision of the Constitution.
Precedent	But a law made under Entry 97 for amending any provision of the Constitution would be inconsistent with that provision.
Precedent	Accordingly it would be invalid.
Precedent	But on following the presc ribed procedure in Article 368 there ensues a valid amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	So Article 248 and Entry 97 cannot include the power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	The history of residuary power in our country also indicates that the power to amend the Constitution cannot be subsumed in the residuary power.
Precedent	Section 104 of the Government of India Act, 1935 provided for residuary power.
Precedent	The Governor-General could by public notification empower either <ORG> or <ORG> to enact a law with respect to any matter not enumerated in any of the Lists in Schedule VII.
Precedent	Acting under Section 104 , the Governor-General could not empower either <ORG> to make a law for, amending the Government of India Act.
Precedent	The power to amend the said Act vested exclusively in the British <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	While the Constitution was on the anvil, residuary power was proposed to be vested in the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	If that power had been vested in the States, it could not have been possible to argue that the Constitution  could be amended by resort to residuary power because the amending bill is to be initiated in <ORG> and not in the States.
RatioOfTheDecision	It was only at a later stage that the residuary power was included in List I.
RatioOfTheDecision	The foregoing considerations show that the amending power does not reside in Article 248 and Entry 97 of List I.
RatioOfTheDecision	As already stated, it is located in Article 368 of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 304(1) of the Draft Constitution was similar to Article 368.
Fact	Article 304(2) enabled States to amend the Constitution as regards the method of choosing a Governor or the number of <ORG>.
Fact	In Clause 18 of his letter dated February 21, 1948 to the President of <ORG>, Dr. <NAME>, while forwarding the Draft Constitution, said that a provision giving 'a limited constituent power' to <ORG> has been inserted in Article 304.
Fact	1939.
Fact	The procedure prescribed in Article 368 is the exclusive procedure for amendment of the Constitution.
Fact	The word 'only' in Article 368 r ules out all other procedures for amendment.
Fact	So no law can be made for a referendum or a constituent assembly.
Fact	A referendum or a constituent assembly will reduce Article 368 to redundance.
Fact	Referendum was not accepted by the framers of the Constitution.
Precedent	Dr. <NAME> said : "The Draft Constitution has eliminated the elaborate and difficult procedure such as a decision by a convention or a referendum.
Precedent	The powers of amendment are left with <ORG> Central and Provincial".
Precedent	C.A.D.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	7, page 43.
Precedent	Nature of Amending Power 1940.
Precedent	With respect I find it difficult to share the view of <NAME> the amending power in Article 368 is a legislative power.'
Precedent	(<ORG>, <ORG> at page 900).
Precedent	1941.
Precedent	During the British period neither the people of this country nor their elected representatives were endowed with the power to make or amend their Constitution Act .
Precedent	The Constitution Act by which they were governed until August 14, 1947 was enacted by <ORG>.
Precedent	The power to amend that Act was v ested in that <ORG>.
Precedent	The elected representatives of the people could until that date make only legislative laws under the Constitution Act .
Fact	The Constitution Act endowed them with a legislative power.
Fact	Under Sections 99 and 100 of the Government of India Act, 1935, the <ORG> and Provincial Legislatures made legislative laws.
Fact	Under Sections 42 , 43 and 44 and Section 72 of Schedule IX the Governor General made ordinances.
Fact	The Governor made ordinances and Acts under Sections 88 , 89 and 90 .
Fact	The headings of all those provisions describe the law-making power as 'legislative power'.
RatioOfTheDecision	The framers of the Constitution were familiar with the historical meaning of the expression 'legislative power' in this country.
RatioOfTheDecision	The were also aware of the meaning of 'constituent power'.
RatioOfTheDecision	Accordingly, it is reasonable to believe that they have made a distinction between legislative power' and 'constituent power'.
RatioOfTheDecision	Indeed they have described the power of making legislative laws as a 'legislative power'.
RatioOfTheDecision	The heading of Part XI is 'Di stribution of Legislative Powers'; the heading of Article 123 is 'legislative power of the President'; the heading of Article 213 is 'legislative power of the Governor'.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may be observed that the framers did not include Article 368 under the heading legislative power' or in Part XI or in the company of the provisions dealing with the legislative procedure in Part V of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	They placed it in a separate part.
RatioOfTheDecision	This omission is explained by the fact that they were making a distinction between 'legislative power' and 'constituent power'.
RatioOfTheDecision	1942.
RatioOfTheDecision	Broadly speaking, 'constituent power' determines the frame of primary organs of Government and establishes authoritative standards for their behaviour.
RatioOfTheDecision	In its ordinary sense, legislative power means power to make laws in accordance with those authoritative standards.
RatioOfTheDecision	Legislative power may determine the form of secondary organs of Government and establish subordinate standards for social behaviour.
RatioOfTheDecision	The subordinate standards are derived from the authoritative st andards established by the constituent power.
RatioOfTheDecision	Discussing the concept of 'legislative power', <NAME> said : "We have to try and discover from the Constitution itself what the concept of legislative power looked like in the eyes of <ORG> which conferred it.
RatioOfTheDecision	When that body created an Indian <ORG> for the first time and endowed it with life, what did they think they were doing ?
RatioOfTheDecision	What concept of legislative power had they in mind ?
RatioOfTheDecision	...First and foremost, they had the British model in view where <ORG> is supreme in the sense that it can do what it pleases and no <ORG> of law can sit in judgment over its Acts.
RatioOfTheDecision	That model it rejected by introducing a federation and dividing the ambit of legislative authority.
Precedent	It rejected by drawing a distinction between the exercise of constituent powers and ordinary legislative activity..." (In re.
Precedent	The Delhi Laws Act 1912 (<ORG>) at page 1112).
RatioOfTheDecision	1943.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG>'s additional power to amend certain provisions of the Constitution by ordinary law would not  obliterate the distinction between constituent power and legislative power.
RatioOfTheDecision	Constitutions may be uncontrolled like the British Constitution, or controlled like the Constitution of the United States of America.
RatioOfTheDecision	There may be a hybrid class of Constitutions, partly controlled and partly uncontrolled.
RatioOfTheDecision	In an uncontrolled Constitution the distinction between constituent power and legislative power disappears, because the legislature can amend by the law-making procedure any part of the Constitution as if it were a statute.
RatioOfTheDecision	In a controlled Constitution the procedure for making laws and for amending the Constitution are distinct and discrete.
RatioOfTheDecision	No part of the Constitution can be amended by the law-making procedure.
RatioOfTheDecision	This distination between constituent power and legislative power in a controlled Constitution proceeds from the distinction between the law-making procedure and the Constitution-amending procedure.
RatioOfTheDecision	Our Constitution is of a hybrid pattern.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is partly controlled and partly uncontrolled.
Precedent	It is uncontrolled wi th respect to those provisions of the Constitution which may be amended by an ordinary law through the legislative procedure; it is controlled with respect to the remaining provisions which may be amended only by following the procedure prescribed in Article 368.
Precedent	When any part of the Constitution is amended by following the legislative procedure, the amendment is the result of the exercise of the legislative power; when it is amended through the procedure prescribed by Article 368 , the amendment is the result of the exercise of the constituent power.
Precedent	The amending power conferred by Article 368 is a constituent power and not a legislative power.
Precedent	Dominion of Amending Power 1944.
Precedent	The phrase "amendment of this Constitution" is the nerve-centre of Article 368.
Precedent	It is determinative of the dominion as well as the magnitude of the amending power.
Precedent	The words "this Constitution" in the phrase embrace the entire Constitution, as according to Article 393 " this Constitution" is called "the Constitution of India".
Precedent	These w ords are also used in Articles 133(2) and 367(1), (2) and (3).
RatioOfTheDecision	In those provisions these words would envelop each and every provision of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	They should convey the same meaning in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	Accordingly each and every provision of the Constitution including Part III falls within the sway of the amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	1945.
RatioOfTheDecision	In re : Barubari Union and Exchange of Enclaves  3 S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	250 at page 282 it is said that "the preamble is not a part of the Constitution".
RatioOfTheDecision	This remark cannot assist the argument that a Preamble is not liable to amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	It seems to me that the Court really intended to say that the Preamble is not enacting part of the Constitution.
Fact	On October 17, 1949 <ORG> passed a resolution to the effect that "the Preamble stand part of the Constitution."
Precedent	C.A.D.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	X, p. 456.
Precedent	1946.
Precedent	According to Article 394 that article and Articles 5 to 9, Articles 60, 324, 366, 367, 379, 380, 388 and 391 to 393 came into force on November 26, 1949, while "the remaining provisions of this  Constitution" were to come into force on January 26, 1950.
Fact	It is clear from the phrase "the remaining provisions of this Constitution" that the Preamble also came into force on January 26, 1950.
Fact	Replying to <NAME> question in regard to the date of the coming into force of the Preamble, <NAME> said : "The Preamble will come into force in all its plentitude when the Constitution comes into force."
Fact	C.A.D.
Fact	Vol.
Fact	X, p. 418.
RatioOfTheDecision	1947.
RatioOfTheDecision	A statute has four parts-title, preamble, enacting clause and purview or body.
RulingByPresentCourt	<NAME> : Statutory Construction (1948 Edn.)
RulingByPresentCourt	p. 123 : <NAME> : Statutory Construction (1943 Edn.)
RulingByPresentCourt	Vol.
Fact	2, pp.
Fact	348-349; <ORG> Laws of England, Vol.
Fact	36, p. 370, Craies on Statute Law (1963 Edn.)
Fact	pp.
Fact	190 and 201.
Fact	The Preamble to the Constitution of the United States of America is regarded as a part of the Constitution.
Fact	<ORG>, Constitutional Law of the United States (1929 Edn.
Fact	), Vol.
Fact	I, p. 62.
Fact	The heading "the Constitution of India" above the Preamble shows that  the Preamble is a part of it.
RatioOfTheDecision	1948.
RatioOfTheDecision	As the Preamble is a part of the Constitution, it is liable to amendment under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	Those parts of the Preamble which operate on the past such as "this 26th day of November, 1949" may perhaps not be capable of miodification.
RatioOfTheDecision	'Even <NAME> hath not power on the past'.
RatioOfTheDecision	But there is little doubt that such parts can be deleted by the exertion of the amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	1949.
RatioOfTheDecision	In sum, no provision of the Constitution can claim immunity from the sway of the amending power.
Precedent	The amending power can amend each and every provision of the Constitution including the Preamble and Part III.
Precedent	Magnitude of Amending Power 1950.
Precedent	The magnitude of amending power is measurable by the broad-shouldered word "amendment" in Article 368.
Precedent	According to <NAME> the word "amendment" should be given its full meaning as used in law and that means that by amendment an existing Constitution...can be changed, and this change can take the form either of addition to the existing provisions or alteration o f existing provisions and their substitution by others or deletion of certain provisions altogether."
Precedent	(<ORG>, supra at page 834).
Precedent	<NAME> said : "I do not take a narrow view of the word "amendment" as including only minor changes within the general frame-work.
Precedent	By amendment new matter may be added, old matter removed or altered."
Precedent	(<ORG>, p. 862) <NAME> and <NAME> gave the same extensive meaning to the word "amendment".
Precedent	Thus according to six out of eleven judges in <ORG>, the word "amendment" means amending by addition, alteration or repeal.
Precedent	<NAME>g to <ORG> "amendment" means "removal of faults or errors; reformation esp.
Precedent	(law) in a writ or process 1607."
Precedent	<NAME>g to Webster's Third New International Dictionary, it means "act of amending esp.
Statute	for the better, correction of a fault or faults, the process of amending as a motion, bill, act or Constitution that will provide for its own amendment; an alteration proposed or effected by such process."
Statute	<NAME> g to <ORG> (Unabridged Edn.)
Statute	"amendment" means "to alter, modify, rephrase or add to, substract from (a motion, bill, Constitution etc.)
Statute	by formal procedure, to change for the better, improve, to remove or correct faults."
Statute	According to <NAME> (Statutory Construction (1940 Edn.)
Statute	page 170) there "are many different definitions of the term amendment, as it applies to legislation.
Statute	Generally, it may be defined as an alteration or change of something, proposed in a bill or established as law.
RatioOfTheDecision	We are not, however, here concerned with the amendment of the proposed bills, but with the amendment of existing laws.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thus limited, a definition as suitable as any, defines an amendment as a change in some of the existing provisions of a statute.
RatioOfTheDecision	Or stated in more detail, a law is amended when it is in whole or in part permitted to remain and something is added to or taken from it or it is in some way changed or altered in order to make it more complete or perfect or effective."
RatioOfTheDecision	According to these definitions the power to amend means the power to make an addition to or alteration in or subtraction from the text The purpose of addition, alteration or subtraction may vary; it may be to make the text or some part of it more complete or perfect or effective.
RatioOfTheDecision	It also appears that the whole text of a law cannot be repealed or abrogated in one step; some part of it must remain while the other is repealed.
RatioOfTheDecision	1951.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution does not define the word "amendment".
Statute	Article 367(1) applies the General Clauses Act to the interpretation of the Constitution.
Statute	The Act also does not define "amendment".
Statute	However, Section 6A provides that where any Central Act repeals any enactment by which, the text of any Central Act was "amended by express omission, insertion or substitution of any matter" the repeal unless different intention appears, shall not affect the continuance of "any suck amendment made by the enactment so repealed" and in operation at the time of such repeal.
RatioOfTheDecision	Section 6A shows that "amend ment" includes addition, substitution and omission.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no reason why this definition which was known to the Constitution-makers should not apply to "amendment" in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	1952.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to the petitioners, "amendment" in Article 368 is used in the narrow sense of making improvements.
RatioOfTheDecision	Now, an improvement may be made not only by an addition, but also by omission or repeal.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thus the curing of an error in the text undoubtedly improves it.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to <NAME> it "was an error to include (the right of property) in (Part III)".
RatioOfTheDecision	(<NAME>, supra at page 887).
RatioOfTheDecision	The removal of this error by an amendment under Article 368 will surely improve the text of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It will remove the roadblock in the way of implementing Part IV of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Further, every mover of an amendment considers his proposal as an improvement in the existing text and the <ORG> should not substitute its own evaluation for that of the mover of the amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	1953.
RatioOfTheDecision	The grants of legislative power are ordinarily a ccorded the widest amplitude.
RatioOfTheDecision	A fortiori, the constituent power in Article 368 should receive the same hospitable construction.
RatioOfTheDecision	The word "amendment" should be so construed as to fructify the purpose underlying Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The framers of the Constitution have enacted Article 368 for several reasons.
RatioOfTheDecision	First, the working of the Constitution may reveal errors and omissions which could not be foreseen by them.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 was designed to repair those errors and omissions.
RatioOfTheDecision	Second, the <ORG>'s construction of the Constitution may not correspond with the Constitution-makers' intention or may make the process of orderly government difficult.
RatioOfTheDecision	The first Amendment to the Constitution became necessary on account of the decision of this <ORG> in the <CITATION_149321>  S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	525 and the decision of the Patna High <ORG> in <CITATION_219225> A.I.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	1951, Patna p. 91.
RatioOfTheDecision	Third, <ORG> which framed the Constitution was not elected on adult franchise and was in fact not fully representative of the entire people.
RatioOfTheDecision	On January 22, 1947 <NAME> said : "We shall frame the Constitution, and I hope it will be a "good Constitution, but does anyone in this <ORG> imagine that when a free India emerges it will be bound down by anything that even this <ORG> might lay down for it ?
RatioOfTheDecision	A free India will see the bursting forth of the energy of a mighty nation.
RatioOfTheDecision	What it will do and what it will not, I do not know, but I do know that it will not consent to be bound down by anything....
RatioOfTheDecision	It may be that the Constitution, this <ORG> may frame may not satisfy an India, that free India.
RatioOfTheDecision	This <ORG> cannot bind down the next generation or people who will duly succeed us in this task."
Precedent	C.A.D.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	2, pages 322-323.
Precedent	On November 8, 1948 he reiterated : "While we who are assembled in this <ORG> undoubtedly represent the people of India, nevertheless, I think it can be said and truthfully that when a new <ORG>, by whatever name it goes, is elected in terms of this Constitution and every adult in India has the right to vote, the <ORG> that eme rges then will certainly be fully representative of every section of the Indian people.
Precedent	It is right that that <ORG> elected so...should have an easy opportunity to make such changes as it wants to...." C.A.D.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	V, pp.
Precedent	322-323.
Precedent	The Constitution-makers conferred very wide amending power on <ORG> because it was believed that <ORG> elected on adult franchise would be fully representative of the entire people and that such a <ORG> should receive a right to have a fresh look at the Constitution and to make such changes therein as the entire people whom it represents desire.
Precedent	Fourth, at the apex of all human rights is the right of self-preservation.
Precedent	People collectively have a similar right of self- preservation.
Precedent	Self-preservation implies mutation, that is adaptation to the changing environment.
Precedent	It is in the nature of man to adjust himself to the changing social, economic and political conditions in the country.
Precedent	Without such adaptation the people decays and there can be no progress.
Precedent	<NAME> said : "One  age cannot enter into an alliance on oath to put the next age in a position when it would be impossible for it to extend and correct its knowledge; or to make any progress whatsoever in enlightenment.
Precedent	This would be a crime against human nature whose original destiny lies precisely in such progress.
Precedent	Later generations are thus perfectly entitled to dismiss these agreements as unauthorised and criminal."
Precedent	<NAME>'s Political Writings, Edited by <NAME>, <ORG>, 1970, p. 57.
Precedent	1954.
Precedent	Speaking in the same vein, <NAME> said : "In any event we should not make a Constitution such as some other great countries have, which are so rigid that they do not and cannot be adapted to changing conditions.
Precedent	Today-especially, when the world is in turmoil and we are passing through a very swift period of transition, what, we may do today may not be wholly applicable tomorrow.
Precedent	Therefore, while we make a Constitution which is sound and as basic as we can, it should also be flexible.
Precedent	C.A.D.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	7, p. 322.
RatioOfTheDecision	1955.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 is shaped by the philosophy that every generation should be free to adapt the Constitution to the social, economic and political conditions of its time.
RatioOfTheDecision	Most of the Constitution-makers were freedom-fighters.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is difficult to believe that those who had fought for freedom to change the social and political organisation of their time would deny the identical freedom to their descendents to change the social, economic and political organisation of their times.
RatioOfTheDecision	The denial of power to make radical changes in the Constitution to the future generation would invite the danger of extra Constitutional changes of the Constitution.
Precedent	"The State without the means of some change is without means of its conservation.
Precedent	Without such means it might even risk the loss of that part of the Constitution which it wished the most religiously to preserve."
Precedent	<NAME> : Recollections on the Revolution in France and other writings <ORG>, 1958 Reprint, p.  23.
RatioOfTheDecision	1956.
RatioOfTheDecision	The context also reinforces the widest me aning of the word "amendment".
RatioOfTheDecision	The proviso to Article 368 states that if an amendment of the Constitution seeks to make any "change" in the provisions specified therein, such amendment shall also require the ratification by at least half of the State <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thus the proviso contemplates an amendment by way of a 'change' in certain provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	According to <ORG> (3rd Edition Vol.
Statute	1, page 291) "change" means "substitution, or succession of anything in place of another; alteration in the State or quality of anything; variation, mutation, that which is or may be substituted for another of the same kind."
Statute	The power to amend accordingly includes the power to substitute one provision for another.
Statute	For instance, it will be open to <ORG> to remove List II in the Seventh Schedule and substitute another List therefor by strictly following the procedure prescribed in Article 368 and its proviso.
Statute	The words "amendment" and "amend" have been used in Articles 107 (2), 108(1) and (4), 190(3), 110(1)(b), proviso to Article 111 , Articles 147, 196(2), 197(1)(c) and (2)(c), 198(3), 199(1)(b), 200, 201 and 395.
Statute	In all these provisions those words include the power of repeal or abrogation.
Statute	Article 110(1)(b) provides that a Bill shall be deemed to be a Money Bill if it contains a provision dealing with "the amendment of the law with respect to any financial obligations undertaken or to be undertaken by <ORG>."
Statute	Without doubt, the word "amendment" would also include repeal or abrogation of a law with respect to any financial obligation undertaken or to be undertaken by <ORG>.
Statute	The word "amendment" cannot be confined to mere minor changes.
Statute	To the same effect is Article 199(1)(b) in relation to the States.
Statute	Article 147 provides that in Chapter IV of Part V and in Chapter V of Part VI references to any substantial question of law as to the interpretation of the Constitution shall be construed as including reference to any substantial question of  law as to the interpretation) of the Government of India Act, 1935 (including any enactment "amending or supplementing that Act").
RatioOfTheDecision	Here also the word "amending" would take in any enactment which has repealed any provision of the Government of India Act, 1935.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 395 provides that the "Indian Independence Act, 1947 and the Government of India Act, 1935, together with all other enactments amending or supplementing the law...are hereby repealed."
RatioOfTheDecision	Here again, the word "amending" includes an enactment which has repealed any provision of the Government of India Act, 1935.
RatioOfTheDecision	It cannot be said that the framers of the Constitution intended to continue an enactment which has repealed an essential provision of the Government of India Act, 1935.
RatioOfTheDecision	1957.
Statute	Paragraph 7 of Schedule V to the Constitution reads : "(1) <ORG> may from time to time by law amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any of the provisions of this Schedule and, when the Schedule is so amended, any reference to this Schedule in this Constitut ion shall be construed as reference to such Schedule as so amended : (2) No such law as is mentioned in sub-paragraph (I) of this paragraph shall be deemed to be an amendment of this Constitution for the purpose of Article 368.
Statute	1958.
RatioOfTheDecision	In paragraph 7(1) the words, "addition, variation, or repeal" do not enlarge the meaning of 'amend'; they are expositive of it.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the word "amendment" in Article 368 did not include the power of repealing a provision of the Constitution, sub-paragraph (2) could not have been enacted.
RatioOfTheDecision	It has been held by this <ORG> that <ORG> may change the boundaries of a State by a law enacted under Article 3 or by an amendment of the Constitution under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	(Berubari Union, supra).
RatioOfTheDecision	It would follow from this decision that <ORG> may repeal any provision of Schedule V by an ordinary law enacted under paragraph 7 of Schedule V or by an amendment under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The amending power under Article 368 which provides for amendment of the Constitution by a more difficult procedure t han the one by which any provision of Schedule V may be repealed under paragraph 7 cannot surely be narrower than the power under paragraph 7 of Schedule V. The same consideration equally applies to paragraph 21 of Schedule VI to the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	1959.
RatioOfTheDecision	According "to Article 33 <ORG> may by law determine to what extent any of the rights conferred by Part III shall in their application to the members of the Armed forces or forces charged with the maintenance of public order be restricted or abrogated so as to ensure better discharge of their duties and the maintenance of discipline amongst them.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is open to <ORG> to make a law abrogating the fundamental rights of the citizens for the time being employed in the <ORG> and the forces charged with the maintenance of public order.
RatioOfTheDecision	For instance, it is open to it to make a law abrogating the freedom of speech of persons employed in the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	For the reasons already discussed in relation to paragraph 7 of Schedule V, it cannot be disputed that <ORG> m ay abrogate the fundamental rights of the citizens employed in the <ORG> or forces charged with the maintenance of public order in the exercise of the amending power under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	1960.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power of <ORG>, which is a representative body, to frame a Constitution is unlimited and unconfined.
RatioOfTheDecision	Its absolute power is explained by the fact that it is called upon to chart a process of government of a country.
RatioOfTheDecision	In carrying out its task it has to take decisions on matters of high policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	The high power is made to match the high purpose.
RatioOfTheDecision	The nature of the power conferred on <ORG> by Article 368 is similar to the power exercisable by <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore the amending power in Article 368 is as unlimited and unconfined as the power of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Indeed, it may truly be said that <ORG> acts as <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	1961.
RatioOfTheDecision	The history of Article 368 supports the broadest construction of the word "amendment".
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 is similar to Article 304 of the Draft  Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 305 of the Draft Constitution is material for our purpose.
Precedent	It relevantly read : "Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 304 , the provisions of this Constitution relating to the reservation of seats for the Muslims, the Scheduled Castes, the Scheduled Tribes or the Indian Christians either in <ORG> or in the legislature of any <ORG>...shall not be amended during a period of 10 years from the commencement of this Constitution.
Precedent	1962.
Precedent	Part XIV of the Draft Constitution made reservation of seats in <ORG> and <ORG> Legislatures for Muslims, Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes and Indian Christians.
RatioOfTheDecision	The word "amended" in Article 305 unmistakably include the repeal of the provisions prescribing the reservations.
RatioOfTheDecision	As Article 305 was an exception to Article 304 , the word "amendment" in Article 304 would include the power of abrogating the reservations.
RatioOfTheDecision	As in Article 304 , so in Article 368 " amendment" should include the sense of repeal and abrogation.
RatioOfTheDecision	1963.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to <NAME> <NAME>, whenever the Constitution-makers intended to confer the power of repeal on any authority, they have expressly said so as in Articles 35(b), 252(2), the proviso to Article 254(2) and Article 372(1) and (2).
RatioOfTheDecision	In all these provisions the Words "alter, repeal or amend" are used with reference to a law.
RatioOfTheDecision	As "amend" would not authorise repeal simpliciter of the entire law, the framers of the Constitution have expressly conceded the power of repealing the entire law.
RatioOfTheDecision	So these provisions do not help the argument of Sri Pal<NAME> that "amendment" in Article 368 should be given a narrow meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	1964.
RatioOfTheDecision	To sum up, the nature, object and history of the amending power and the context of Article 368 leave little room for doubt that the word "amendment" includes the power of repealing or abrogating each and every provision of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may be that <ORG> may not be able to annhilate the entire Constitution by one stroke of pen.
RatioOfTheDecision	But it can surely repeal or abrogate all provisions in Part III.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 3 68 permits <ORG> to apply not only the physician's needle but also the surgeon's saw.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may amputate any part of the Constitution if and when it becomes necessary so to do for the good health and survival of the other parts of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Meaning of 'Law' in Article 13(2) 1965.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is a distinction between 'Constitution' and 'law'.
RatioOfTheDecision	(Ordinarily) a 'Constitution' signifies a politico-legal document.
RatioOfTheDecision	President <NAME> once said that the U.S. Constitution has been, to a considerable extent, a political document and not a mere 'lawyers document'.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> : Role of <ORG> in American Government and Politics, 1944 Edn., p. 44.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the other hand, in its ordinary sense 'law' signifies a statute or a legislative enactment.
RatioOfTheDecision	Again, a 'Constitution' prescribes the paramount norm or norms; a law prescribes derivative norms.
RatioOfTheDecision	They are derived from the paramount norms.
RatioOfTheDecision	The reckoning of a Constitutional amendment in the eye of law is the same as that of a Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore ordinarily a Constit utional amendment is not law.
RatioOfTheDecision	Significantly, there is not a whisper of the word 'law' in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	1966.
RatioOfTheDecision	The context of the word 'law' in Article 13(2) does not show that it includes an amendment of the Constitution made under Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The word 'law' in Article 13(1) obviously does not include a Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	No Constitution existing at the time of the commencement of our Constitution and taking away or abridging the fundamental rights of the people conferred by Part III of the Constitution has been brought to our notice in spite of the assiduous research of Sri <NAME>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 13(3)(a) provides for an extensive definition of the word 'law' by including things which are not ordinarily regarded as included in it.
RatioOfTheDecision	It mentions an ordinance, order, bye-law, rule, regulation, notification, custom or usage having the force of law.
RatioOfTheDecision	But it does not include the Constitution which in the ordinary sense does not mean 'law'.
RatioOfTheDecision	1967.
RatioOfTheDecision	A distinction between 'Constitution' and 'law' is made in the Constitution it self.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to Article 60 the President of India has to take the oath that he will preserve, protect and defend "the Constitution and the law".
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 159 requires the Governor of a <ORG> to take the same oath.
RatioOfTheDecision	A Minister of the Union and a <ORG>, the Judges of the Supreme Court and <ORG> and the Comptroller and Auditor General also take the same kind of oath.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the framers of the Constitution had regarded the Constitution as 'law', they would not have separately mentioned the Constitution in various oaths.
RatioOfTheDecision	1968.
RatioOfTheDecision	Various provisions of the Constitution indicate that the product which comes into being by fallowing the legislative procedure prescribed in Articles 107 to 111 is called 'law'.
RatioOfTheDecision	The heading over Articles 107 and 196 reads as "Legislative Procedure".
RatioOfTheDecision	When the prescribed legislative procedure is followed, the end-product is law.
RatioOfTheDecision	But when the procedure prescribed in Article 368 is strictly followed, it results in the amendment of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution-makers did not call it 'law '.
RatioOfTheDecision	1969.
RatioOfTheDecision	Ordinarily fundamental rights avail against the <ORG> organs, that is, the <ORG>, the <ORG> and the <ORG> and other agencies of the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	While making an amendment under Article 368 , <ORG> acts as a constituent authority and not as a <ORG> organ.
RatioOfTheDecision	The body making a law in accordance with the procedure prescribed under Articles 107 to 111 and an amendment according to the procedure prescribed in Article 368 may be the same, but the two functions are fundamentally different in character.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is common knowledge that often there is a polarisation of various functions in one and the same body.
RatioOfTheDecision	For instance, <ORG> in Great Britain exercises legislative functions as well as judicial functions.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may pass a Bill by a bare majority of the Lords assembled in a particular session.
RatioOfTheDecision	But all the Lords minus the Lord Chancellor, the Law Lords and such other Lords as have held or are holding high judicial offices cannot decide a civil appeal.
RatioOfTheDecision	On the other hand, three Lords selected  from any one of the last three categories of Lords may decide a civil appeal.
RatioOfTheDecision	The functional difference accounts for this apparent paradox of numbers.
RatioOfTheDecision	The members of <ORG> could not, by their unanimous vote, make the Constitution of India.
RatioOfTheDecision	But the same members-acting as <ORG> could, by a bare majority, make the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The functional difference in making a legislative law and an amendment of the Constitution likewise explains the basic difference in the procedures prescribed in Articles 107 to 111 and Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	In case of difference on a Bill between <ORG> and <ORG>, the two <ORG> may meet unicamerally and pass a legislative measure.
RatioOfTheDecision	The President cannot refuse his assent to a Bill passed by both <ORG> bicamerally or unicamerally.
RatioOfTheDecision	But an amendment of the Constitution under Article 368 cannot be made by a vote in a joint sitting of the two <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	The two <ORG> must meet separately and pass tile amending bill by the req uisite majority.
RatioOfTheDecision	The President may withhold his assent to the Constitution amending bill.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is on account of the functional difference between law making and Constitution amending that a law passed by the unanimous vote of <ORG> according to the procedure in Articles 107 to 111 cannot override any fundamental right.
RatioOfTheDecision	A Bill passed by more than half of the members of each <ORG> assembled separately and by two third of the members present and voting will, however, result in the amending of the fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	1970.
RatioOfTheDecision	Legislative power in Article 245 is made 'subject to the provisions of this Constitution'.
RatioOfTheDecision	But Article 368 is not made 'subject to the provisions of this Constitution'.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 places only one express fetter on the amending power, that is, the procedural fetter.
RatioOfTheDecision	A substantive fetter on the amending power is accordingly not contemplated by Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The framers of the Constitution were aware of the fact that certain foreign Constitutions have expressly put the amending power in substa ntive fetters.
RatioOfTheDecision	Indeed Article 305 sought to place such a fetter on the Draft Article 304 (corresponding to Article 368 ).
Precedent	In the absence of clear textual evidence, I am unable to expand the meaning of 'law' in Article 13(2 ), for an expansive construction would permanently rule out the lawful making of structural reforms in the social, economic and political frame of the country.
Precedent	Speaking on the First amendment to the Constitution following the decision of this Court in State of Madras v. <NAME>, (1951) S.C.R.
Precedent	525 on May 29, 1951 <NAME> said : "We have to give them (the weaker sections of the society) opportunities-economic opportunities, educational opprtunities and the like.
Precedent	Now in doing that we have been told that we come up against some provisions in the Constitution which rather lay down some principles of equality or some principles of non-discrimination etc.
Precedent	So we arrive at a peculiar tangle.
Precedent	We cannot have equality because in trying to attain equality we come up agains t some principles of equality.
Precedent	That is a very peculiar position.
Precedent	We cannot have equality because we cannot have non- discrimination because if you think in terms of giving a lift to those who are down, you are somehow affecting the present status quo undoubtedly.
Precedent	Therefore, if this argument is correct, then we cannot make any major change in the status quo, whether economic or in any sphere of public or private activity."
Precedent	<ORG>-XIII, Part II- 1951, pages 9616-9617.
Precedent	1971.
Precedent	The word 'compensation' in the unamended Article 31(2) has been construed by this <ORG> to mean full market value of the acquired property.
RatioOfTheDecision	This construction creates a direct conflict between Article 31(2) and Article 39(c).
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 39(c) enjoins the <ORG> to direct its policy towards securing "that the operation of the economic system does not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to the common detriment."
RatioOfTheDecision	This object can never be achieved if full market value of the acquired properly is to  be paid to its owner.
RatioOfTheDecision	The payment of full market value to the owner will change the form of the concentration of wealth from property to cash.
RatioOfTheDecision	The concentration would remain.
RatioOfTheDecision	The history of <ORG> clearly shows that the Constitution-makers were committed to the accomplishment of the objects specified in Part IV of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	They have expressly declared that those objects are 'fundamental'.
RatioOfTheDecision	in the governance of the country.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is accordingly reasonable to think that they have provided for the means of resolving the conflict between Articles 31(2) and 39(c) or between Articles 29 and 46.
RatioOfTheDecision	They must have intended that when a conflict arises between the rights in Part III and the obligations of the <ORG> in the Part IV, that conflict may be resolved by an amendment of the Constitution under Article 368.
Precedent	"My concept of a fundamental right is something which <ORG> cannot touch save by an amendment of the Constitution" (emphasis added) (<NAME> versus <ORG> of Madras)  S.C.R.
Precedent	621 at p age 652 per Bose J.
RatioOfTheDecision	1972.
RatioOfTheDecision	The phrase 'notwithstanding anything in the Constitution' is used in a provision granting power for emancipating the grant from any restrictive provision in the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	As the word 'law' in the Article 13(2) is not intended to include an amendment of the Constitution, Article 368 does not open with the non-obstante clause.
RatioOfTheDecision	1973.
RatioOfTheDecision	No unmistaking conclusion can be drawn from the history of Article 13(2) as to the meaning of the word 'law'.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Draft Report of the Sub-Committee on Fundamental Rights, dated April 3, 1947, contained an annexure dealing with Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	<NAME>, Framing of India's Constitution, Vol.
Precedent	II, p. 137.
Fact	Clause 2 of the annexure relevantly provided that "any law which may hereafter be made by the <ORG> inconsistent with the provisions of this Chapter/Constitution shall be void to the extent of such inconsistency."
Fact	By a letter of April 16, 1947, the <NAME>rman of the Fundamental Rights sub-Committee forwarded an annexure on Fundamental Rights to the <NAME> <NAME>, <ORG>.
Fact	Clause 2 of the annexure materially read: "All existing laws or usages in force...inconsistent with the rights guaranteed under this Constitution shall stand abrogated to the extent of such inconsistency : nor shall the <ORG> or any unit make any law taking away or abridging any such right."
Fact	<ORG>, p. 171.
Fact	On April 23, 1947, the <ORG> presented an interim report to the President of <ORG>.
Fact	The Report contained an annexure providing for fundamental rights.
Fact	Clause (2) of the annexure materially read : "All existing laws, notifications, regulations, customs or usages in force...inconsistent with the rights guaranteed under this Part of the Constitution shall stand abrogated to the extent of such inconsistency, nor shall the <ORG> or any unit make any law taking away or abridging any such right."
Fact	<ORG>, p. 290.
Fact	<NAME> proposed an amendment substituting for the last words in Clause (2) the words "Nor shall  any such right be taken away or abridged except by an amendment of the Constitution."
Argument	In his speech he explained that "if the clause stands as it is even by an amendment of the Constitution we shall not be able to change any of these rights if found unsatisfactory or inconvenient....
Precedent	In order to avoid any such doubts I have moved this amendment."
Precedent	C.A.D.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	3, pp.
Precedent	415-416.
Fact	So according to him the amendment was by way of abundant caution.
Fact	Sardar <NAME> accepted the amendment.
Fact	It was put to vote and adopted.
Fact	Ibid, p. 415.
Fact	<ORG> thus accepted the position that fundamental rights could be abrogated by a Constitutional amendment.
Fact	1974.
Fact	In October, 1947, a Draft Constitution was prepared by the Constitutional Adviser.
Fact	<NAME>, supra, p. 7.
Fact	Section 9(2) of his Draft Constitution materially read : "Nothing in this Constitution shall be taken to empower the <ORG> to make any law which curtails, or takes away any of the rights conferred by Chapter II of this Constitution except by wa y of amendment of this Constitution under Section 232 and any law made in contravention of this section shall to the extent of such contravention be void."
Fact	Although <ORG> had expressly accepted the amendment of <NAME>, <ORG> omitted the words "except by way of amendment of this Constitution."
Fact	The relevant portion of Article 8(2) of the Draft Constitution read : "The State shall not make any law which takes away or abridges the rights conferred by this Part and any law made in contravention of this Part shall to the extent of the contravention be void."
Fact	No explanation for excluding the words "except by way of amendment of this Constitution", which were approved by <ORG>, is to be found in the records.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is, however, important to observe that when the words "except by way of amendment of the Constitution" are omitted from <NAME>'s amendment, the remaining words "nor shall any such rights be taken away or abridged" are quite wide to proh ibit the abrogation or abridgment of fundamental rights even by a Constitutional amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	The same effect seems to be produced by the words "nothing in this Constitution" in Section 9(2) of the Draft Constitution prepared by the Constitutional Adviser.
RatioOfTheDecision	But <ORG> substituted Section 9(2) by Article 8(2) of the Draft Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 8(2) of the Draft Constitution does not enmesh in plain words all the provisions of the Constitution including Article 304.
RatioOfTheDecision	This may perhaps explain the omission of the words "except by way of amendment of this Constitution."
RatioOfTheDecision	from Article 8(2) of the Draft Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	In any case, this history of Article 13(2) does not prove that <ORG> intended Up give supremacy to fundamental rights over the Constitution amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	In this connection it is important to refer to a note from the Constitutional Adviser's office that 'law' in Section 9(2) did not include an amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	<NAME>, Vol.
Precedent	IV, p. 26.
Argument	1975.
Argument	A careful readin g of Dr. <NAME>'s speeches would show that the Constitution amending power can be used to abrogate or abridge the fundamental rights.
Fact	On November 4, 1948 he said:  The provisions of the Constitution relating to the amendment of the Constitution divide the Articles of the Constitution into two groups.
Fact	In the one group are placed Articles relating to : (a) the distribution of legislative powers between the <ORG> and the <ORG>, (b) the representation of the <ORG>s in <ORG>; and (c) the powers of the <ORG>, All other Articles are placed in another group.
Fact	Articles placed in the second group cover a very large part of the Constitution and can be amended by <ORG> by a double majority, namely, a majority of not less than two third of the members of each <ORG> present and voting and by a majority of the total membership of each <ORG>.
Fact	The amendments of these articles did not require ratification by the <ORG>s."
Fact	C.A.D.
Fact	Vol.
Fact	VII, p. 36.
Fact	(emphasis added).
Fact	He reiterated:  It is only for amendments of s pecific matters-and they are only few-that the ratifications of the State legislatures is required.
Fact	All other articles of the Constitution are left to be amended by <ORG>."
Precedent	C.A.D.
Precedent	Vol.
Fact	VII, p. 43.
Fact	(emphasis added).
Fact	On another occasion he repeated:  Now, what is it we do ?
Fact	We divide the articles of the Constitution under three categories.
Fact	The first category is one which consists of articles which can be amended by <ORG> by a bare majority.
Fact	The second set of articles are articles which require two-thirds majority.
Fact	If the future <ORG> wishes to amend any particular article which is not mentioned in Part III or Article 304 , all that is necessary is to have two-thirds majority.
Fact	Then, they can amend it.
Precedent	Mr. President : Of members present The Honourable Dr. <NAME> : Yes, Now, we have no doubt put certain articles in a third category where for the purpose of amendment the mechanism is somewhat different or double.
Precedent	It requires two-thirds majority plus ratification by the States."
Precedent	C.A.D.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	I X, pp.
Precedent	660-663.
Precedent	1976.
Precedent	It would appear from these speeches that for the purpose of amendment Dr. <NAME> has classified all the Articles of the Constitution in three categories.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Articles must fit in one or the other of the three categories, for according to him there is no fourth category.
RatioOfTheDecision	Articles in Part III of the Constitution should accordingly fit into one of these categories.
RatioOfTheDecision	It seems to me that having regard to his threefold classification of the Articles it is not fair to interpret his speeches as showing that the Articles in Part III are not at all amendable.
RatioOfTheDecision	The word "not" in the sentence "if the future <ORG> wishes to amend any particular article which is not mentioned in Part III or Article 304" is presumably either a slip of tongue or a printer's devil.
RatioOfTheDecision	When <NAME> said that the fundamental rights were intended to be "permanent in the Constitution", he did not really mean that they are not amendable.
RatioOfTheDecision	His speeches, already quoted by me, would clearly show that he regarded the en tire Constitution to be subject to amendment by any future <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	1977.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> had moved an amendment to Article 304 which expressly provided for amendment in the provisions of Part III, but that amendment was rejected by <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	No inference of unamendability of those provisions can be drawn from the rejection of his motion, for the members of <ORG> might have thought that the language of Article 304 of the Draft Constitution was sufficiently spacious to include an amendment of the provisions of Part III and that accordingly <NAME>'s motion was unnecessary.
RatioOfTheDecision	1978.
RatioOfTheDecision	The phrase "Constitution as by law established" in the President's oath would not establish that the Constitution is a law in the ordinary sense of the term.
RatioOfTheDecision	The word 'law' in the phrase, in my view, means lawful.
RatioOfTheDecision	The phrase would mean "Constitution established in a lawful manner, that is, by the people through their representatives.
RatioOfTheDecision	1979.
RatioOfTheDecision	The oath of the President to defend "the Constitution and t he law" does not bind him to the Constitution as it stood on the day he took the oath.
RatioOfTheDecision	The word 'law' undoubtedly means the law for the time being in force.
RatioOfTheDecision	A variation or repeal of a part of a law would not compromise the oath.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the context of law, the 'Constitution' would mean the Constitution as varied or repealed from time to time.
RatioOfTheDecision	1980.

Precedent	The constituent power in Article 368 is a legislative power.
Precedent	1989.
Precedent	The constituent power is subject to inherent and implied limitations.
Precedent	1990.

Precedent	According to Sri <NAME>, an implied limitation is one which is implicit in the scheme of various provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	The scheme "of various provisions is to create primary organs of <ORG> and to define, demarcate and limit their powers and functions.
Precedent	The scheme of Article 368 , on the other hand, is to re-create the primary organs of <ORG> and to re-define, re-demarcate and re-limit their powers and functions if and when it becomes imperative to do so for the good of the people.
Precedent	Accordingly it must plainly have been the intention of the Constitution-makers that Article 368 should control and condition rather than be controlled and conditioned by other provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	Article 368 is the master, not the slave of the othe r provisions.

RatioOfTheDecision	The Constitution-makers who were familiar with the English Constitutional history could not conceivably have left undetermined the test of distinguishing the essential features from the non-essential features or their core.
RatioOfTheDecision	The test is writ large in Article 368 itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	Every provision of the Constitution which may be amended only by the procedure prescribed in Article 368 is an essential feature of the Constitution, for it is more set than legislative laws.
RatioOfTheDecision	The test is the rigid proced ure.
RatioOfTheDecision	The more rigid the procedure, the more essential the provision amendable thereby.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thus the provisions specified in the proviso to Article 368 are more essential than the rights in Part III.

Precedent	Repelling the abuse of power argument, <NAME> observed:  (W)hat, I ask, is our protection against the legislature in the matter of deprivation of property by the exercise of the power of taxation?
