Precedent	Twentyfourth Amendment 2020.
Precedent	It consists of two relevant sections, Sections 2 and 3 , These sections have been drawn in the light of various judgments in Golaknath (supra).
Precedent	Section 2 adds Clause (4) to Article 13.
Precedent	As the majority decision in Golaknath had taken the view that Article 13(2) is a limitation on the amending power to take away or abridge the fundamental rights, Clause (4) removes that limitation.
Statute	Section 3 consists of four clauses.
Statute	Clause (a) substitutes the marginal note to the unamended Article 368.
Statute	The substituted marginal note reads as "Power of <ORG> to amend the Co nstitution and procedure therefor".
Statute	Clause (b) renumbers the unamended Article 368 as Clause (2) and adds Clause (1) to it.
Statute	The new Clause (1) calls the amending power as 'constituent power'.
RatioOfTheDecision	It empowers <ORG> to amend 'by way of addition, variation or repeal' any provision of the Constitution in accordance with the prescribed procedure.
RatioOfTheDecision	It opens with the wellknown phrase "Notwithstanding anything in this Constitution".
RatioOfTheDecision	In the renumbered Clause (2) also, that is, the unamended Article 368 , there is an amendment It says that the President shall give his assent to the Bill.
RatioOfTheDecision	Clause (d) adds Clause (3) no Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	It provides that nothing in Article 13 shall apply to any amendment made under Article 368: 2021.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may be observed that except as regards the assent of the President to the Bill, everything else in the 24th Amendment was already there in the unamended Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	I have already held to that effect earlier in this judgment.
RatioOfTheDecision	Accordingly, the amendment is really declaratory in nature.
Precedent	It remov es doubts cast on the amending power by the majority judgment in Golaknath (supra) I am of opinion that the 24th Amendment is valid.
Precedent	2022.
Precedent	The unamended Article 368 imposed a procedural limit to the amending power.
Precedent	The amending Bill could not become a part of the Constitution until it had received the assent of the President.
Precedent	I have held earlier that the President could withhold his assent.
Precedent	After the amendment the President cannot withhold assent.
Precedent	The procedural restrictions are a part of Article 368.
Precedent	The unamended Article 368 provided for its own amendment.
Precedent	It was accordingly open to <ORG> to amend the procedure.
Precedent	So I find no difficulty in upholding the amendment that the President "shall give his assent to the Bill 2023.
Precedent	One thing more.
RatioOfTheDecision	Let us assume for the sake of argument that the amending power in the unamended Article 368 was subject to certain inherent and implied limitiations.
RatioOfTheDecision	Let us also assume that it was restricted by the provisions of Article 13(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	The unamended Article 368 would impliedly  read as "subject to Article 13(2) and any inherent and implied limitations."
RatioOfTheDecision	So the restrictions imposed by Article 13(2) and inherent and implied limitations were a part of the body of Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	As Article 368 is itself liable to amendment, these restrictions are now removed by <ORG> for they will fall within the ambit of the word "amendment".
RatioOfTheDecision	The phrase "notwithstanding anything in this Constitution" in the newly added Clause (1) of Article 368 is apt to sweep away all those restrictions.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the result, the amending power is now free of the incubus of Article 13(2) and inherent and implied limitations, if any.
RatioOfTheDecision	In my opinion, the whole of the 24th amendment is perfectly valid.
Statute	Section 2 of the 25th Amendment 2024.
Statute	Section 2 amends Article 31(2).
Statute	The unamended Article 31(2) obligated the <ORG> to pay 'compensation' for any property acquired or requisitioned by it.
Statute	Section 2 substitutes the word 'compensation" by the words "an amount".
Statute	It also provides that the amount fixed by law or determined in ac cordance with the principles prescribed by law may be "given in such a manner as may be specified in such law.
Statute	2025.
Statute	The last part of the main part of the amended Article 31(2) also states that "No such law shall be called in question in any Court on the ground that the amount so fixed or determined is not adequate or that the whole or any part of such amount is to be given otherwise than in cash.
Statute	2026.
Statute	A proviso has also been added to Article 31(2).
Statute	According to the proviso, while making any law-providing for the compulsory acquisition of any property of educational institution, established and administered by a minority referred to in Clause (1) of Article 30 , the <ORG> shall ensure that the amount fixed by or determined under the law is such as would not restrict or abrogate the rights guaranteed under that clause.
Statute	2027.
Statute	Section 2 adds Clause (2B) to Article 31.
Statute	Clause (2B) states that the provisions of Article 19(1)(f) shall not affect any law referred to in the amended Article 31(2).
Precedent	2028.
Precedent	The birth o f Section 2 is dictated by the history of Article 31(2).
Precedent	Article 24 of the Draft Constitution became Article 31(2).
Precedent	Article 24 was moved by <NAME> in <ORG> on September, 10, 1949.
Precedent	Then he said that compensation could not be questioned "except where it is thought that there has been a gross abuse of law, where in fact there has been a fraud on the Constitution C.A.D.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	IX, p. 1193".
Precedent	His construction of Article 24 received support from Sri <NAME> and Sri <NAME>.
Precedent	Sri <NAME> narrated his personal experience.
Precedent	In 1938 <ORG> acquired the Bardoli lands.
Precedent	In one case the property acquired was worth over rupees five lacs.
Precedent	It was sold during <ORG> to an old <ORG> of a native State for something like Rs.
Precedent	6000.
Argument	The income from the property was about Rs.
Argument	80,000.00 a year.
Argument	The <ORG> had received that income for about 10 years.
Argument	<ORG> acquired the property by paying compensation equal to the amount invested by the  Diwan in the property plus 6%.
Precedent	In direct opposition to the manifest intention of the Constitution makers, this <ORG> held that the word "compensation" in Article 31(2) means "full cash equivalent" ( <CITATION_1890860> ) (1954) S.C.R.
Precedent	558.
Precedent	2029.
Precedent	To give effect to the intention of the Constitution-makers, Article 31(2) was amended by the 24th Amendment to the Constitution in 1955.
Precedent	The 4th Amendment added to Article 31(2) these words: "and no such law shall be called in question in any court on the ground that the compensation provided by law is not adequate."
Precedent	The effect of the 4th amendment was considered by this <ORG> in <CITATION_1634289>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	614.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> said:  The fact that <ORG> Used the same expressions, namely, 'compensation' and 'principles' as were found in Article 31 before the amendment is a clear indication that it accepted the meaning given by this <ORG> to those expressions in Mrs. <NAME>'s case.
RatioOfTheDecision	It follows that a <ORG> in making a law of acquisition or requisition s hall provide for a just equivalent of what the owner has been deprived of or specify the principles for the purpose of ascertaining the 'just equivalent' of what the owner has been deprived of.
RatioOfTheDecision	If <ORG> intended to enable a <ORG> to make such a law without providing for compensation so defined, it would have used other expressions like 'price', 'consideration' etc.
Statute	Ibid.
Statute	at page 626.
Statute	2030.
RatioOfTheDecision	Regarding the amendment he said:  (A) more reasonable interpretation is that neither the principles prescribing the 'just equivalent' nor the 'just equivalent' can be questioned by the <ORG> on the ground of the inadequacy of the compensation fixed or arrived at by the working of the principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	To illustrate, a law is made to acquire a house; its value at the time of the acquisition has to be fixed; there are many modes of valuation, namely, estimate by an engineer, value reflected by comparable sales, capitalisation of rent and similar others.
RatioOfTheDecision	The application of different principles may lead to "different resu lts.
RatioOfTheDecision	The adoption of one principle may give a higher value and the adoption of another principle may give a lesser value.
RatioOfTheDecision	But none the less they are principles on which and the manner in which compensation is determined.
RatioOfTheDecision	The <ORG> cannot obviously say that the law should have adopted one principle and not the other, for it relates only to the question of adequacy.
Precedent	On the other hand, if a law lays down principles which are not relevant to the property acquired or to the value of the property at or about the time it is acquired it may be said that they are not principles contemplated by Article 31(2) [1965] 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	Supra, at page 627.
Precedent	2031.
Precedent	<CITATION_602096>  2 S.C.R.
Precedent	255 at page 264-265 <NAME> spoke again on the implications of the Fourth Amendment.
Precedent	He said: The law to justify itself has to provide for the payment of a 'just equivalent' to the land acquired or lay down principles which will lead to that result.
Precedent	If the principles laid down are relevant to the fixation of compensation and are not arbitra ry, the adequacy of the resultant product cannot be questioned in a court of law.
Precedent	The validity of the principles judged by the above tests falls within judicial scrutiny, and if they stand the tests, the adequacy of the product falls outside its jurisdiction.
Precedent	2032.
Precedent	These two decisions neutralised the object of the 4th Amendment.
Precedent	<CITATION_673450>  3 S.C.R.
Precedent	341 this <ORG> overruled <ORG>.
Precedent	<NAME> at page 363 of the Report:  Right to compensation in the view of this <ORG> was intended by the Constitution to be a right to a just equivalent of the property of which a person was deprived.
RatioOfTheDecision	But the just equivalent was not capable of precise determination by the application of any recognised principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	The decisions of this <ORG> in the two cases-Mrs. <NAME> case and <NAME> case were therefore likely to give rise to formidable problems, when the principles specified by the <ORG> as well as the amounts determined by the application of those principles were declared jus ticiable.
RatioOfTheDecision	By qualifying 'equivalent' by the adjective 'just' the enquiry was made more controversial; and apart from the practical difficulties the law declared by this <ORG> also placed serious obstacles in giving effect to the directive principles of <ORG> policy incorporated in Article 39.
RatioOfTheDecision	(emphasis added).
RatioOfTheDecision	2033.
RatioOfTheDecision	He added:  If the quantum of compensation fixed by the <ORG> is not liable to be canvassed before the <ORG> on the ground that it is not a just equivalent, the principles specified for determination of compensation will also not be open to challenge on the plea that the compensation determined by the application of those principles is not a just equivalent...(I)t does not mean however that something fixed or determined by the application of specified principles which is illusory or can in no sense be regarded as compensation must be held by the <ORG>s, for, to do so would be to grant a charter of arbitrariness, and permit a "device to defeat the Constitutional guarantiee.
RatioOfTheDecision	A challenge to a s tatute that the principles specified by it do not award a just equivalent will be in clear violation of the Constitutional declaration that adequacy of compensation provided is not justiciable.
Precedent	[1969] 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	pages 365-366.
Precedent	2034.
Precedent	<NAME> transfused blood in the 4th Amendment made anaemic by <ORG> and <ORG>.
Precedent	But soon thereafter came the majority decision in <ORG> v. <ORG> [1970] 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	530.
Fact	<ORG> in substance overruled <NAME> and restored the old position.
Fact	More, it also added the test of Article 19(1)(f) to valid acquisition of property.

Statute	2035.
Statute	Having regard to this history, it will not be proper to import the concept of compensation in Article 31(2 ), Section 2 has substituted the word 'compensation' by the word 'amount' at every relevant place in Article 31(2).
Statute	The <ORG> should not minimize or neutralize its operation by introducing notions taken from or i nspired by the old Article 31(2) which the words of Section 2 are intended to abrogate and do abrogate.
Statute	2036.
Statute	According to Webster's Dictionary on Synonyms (1st Edn.
Statute	page 47) the word 'amount' means 'sum, total, quantity, number, aggregate, whole'.
Statute	According to the Shorter Oxford English Dictionary, the word 'principle' means 'that from which something takes its rise originates or derives'.
RatioOfTheDecision	The word 'adequate', according to the same Dictionary, means 'equal in magnitude or extent, commensurate in fitness, sufficient, suitable'.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to the Words and Phrases (Permanent Ed.
Precedent	Vol.
Precedent	II, p. 363) the word "adequate" some time means that which is equal to the value; but in its primary and more properly significance nothing can be said to be adequate which is not equal to what is required suitable to the case or occasion, wholly sufficient, proportionate and satisfactory.
Precedent	2037.
Precedent	Unlike 'compensation' the word 'amount' is not a term of art.
Precedent	It bears no specific legal meaning.
Precedent	The amount fixed by law or determined in  accordance with the principles specified by law may be paid partly in cash and partly in kind.
Precedent	In such a case it may often be difficult to quantify the aggregate value of the cash and the thing given.
Statute	Again, the amount may be paid in such a manner as may be specified in the law.
Statute	Thus the law may provide for payment of the amount over a long period of years.
Statute	Article 19(1)(f) shall now have no impact on Article 31(2).
Statute	Having regard to all these circumstances, it is, I think, not permissible to import the notion of reasonableness in Article 31(2) as amended by Section 2 .
Fact	The phrase 'principle on which and the manner in which the compensation is to be determined and given' in the old Article 31(2) is now substituted by the phrase 'amount which may be determined in accordance with such principles and given in such a manner as may be specified in such law?
Fact	As the word 'compensation' found place in the former phrase, the <ORG> has held that the principles should be relevant to 'compensation', that is, to the 'just  equivalent' of the property acquired.
Fact	That phrase is no more there now in Article 31(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	The notion of 'the relevancy of principles to compensation' is jettisoned by Section 2 .
RatioOfTheDecision	Obviously, where the law fixes the amount, it cannot be questioned in any court on the ground that it is not adequate, that is, not equal to the value of the property acquired or requisitioned.
RatioOfTheDecision	The legislative choice is conclusive.
RatioOfTheDecision	It would accordingly follow that the amount determined by the principles specified in the law is equally unquestionable in courts.
RatioOfTheDecision	2038.
RatioOfTheDecision	The newly added proviso to Article 31(2) appears to me to fortify this construction.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to the proviso, the law providing for compulsory acquisition of any property of an educational institution which would receive the protection of Clause (1) of Article 30 , should ensure that the amount fixed by or determined under it for the acquired property would not restrict or 'abrogate' the right guaranteed under that clause.
RatioOfTheDecision	Now, the object of a proviso is to take out som ething which is included in the main part of a provision.
RatioOfTheDecision	So the amount payable under the main part of the amended Article 31(2) may be such as would 'abrogate' the right of property of all and sundry.
RatioOfTheDecision	Accordingly it is not permissible to import in the amended Article 31(2) the notions of 'arbitrary amount' or 'illusory amount' or 'fraudulent amount'.
RatioOfTheDecision	As some amount must be paid, the law may be virtually confiscatory, but not literally confiscatory.
RatioOfTheDecision	The position now is akin to the legal position in Section 25 of the Contract Act.
RatioOfTheDecision	Under that provision the adequacy of consideration negotiated by the contracting parties cannot be questioned in court.
RatioOfTheDecision	Most trifling benefit or detriment is sufficient.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is however this difference between Section 25 and Article 31(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	While the consideration is settled by the contracting parties, the amount payable for the acquisition or requisitioning of property is settled by the legislature.
RatioOfTheDecision	Like the former, the latter is also not to be questioned in courts.
RatioOfTheDecision	2039.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31 (2) is distinguishable from Articles 31A, 31B and 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	While some amount is payable under a law protected by Article 31(2 ), no amount whatsoever may be paid under a law protected by Articles 31A, 31B and 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	The former may be virtually confiscatory, the latter may be wholly confiscatory.
RatioOfTheDecision	The amount fixed by law or determined in accordance with the principles in such a law is now not justiciable even though it may seem to be an 'arbitrary amount' or 'illusory amount' or 'fraudulent amount' by the measure of compensation.
RatioOfTheDecision	The ouster of judicial oversight does not imply that the legislature would act whimsically.
RatioOfTheDecision	The value of the property acquired or requisitioned, the nature of the property acquired or requisitioned, the circumstances in which the property is being acquired or requisitioned and the object of acquisition or requisition will be the guiding principles for legislative determination of amount.
RatioOfTheDecision	The second principle may involve, inter alia, consideration of the income already received by the owner o f the property and the social contribution to the value of the property by way of public loans at lower rates of interest, cheap state supply of energy and raw materials subsidies and various kinds of protection, tax holidays, etc.
RatioOfTheDecision	It should be remembered that the value of a property is the resultant of the owner's industry and social contribution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The owner ought not to receive any amount for the value contributed by society.
RatioOfTheDecision	He is entitled to payment for his own contribution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The third principle will include the element of social justice.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is thus wrong to say that on my interpretation of Article 31(2) the legislatures will act arbitrarily in determining the amount.
Fact	The amended Article 31(2) does not remove the bar of Article 14.
Fact	If the amount paid to the owner of property is in violation of the principles of Article 14 , the law may even now be struck down.
Fact	Although the amended Article 31(2 ), according to my construction of it, will abrogate the right of property, it is Constitutional as it falls within  the scope of the 24th Amendment which I have held to be Constitutional.
Statute	Section 3 of the 75th Amendment 2040.
Statute	Section 3 adds Article 31C to Part III of the Constitution.
Statute	It reads : "Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 , no law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) and (c) of Article 39 , shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Article 14 , Article 19 or Article 31; and no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy.
Statute	2041.
Statute	Provided that where such law is made by the Legislatures of a <ORG>, the provisions of this article shall not apply thereto unless such law, having been reserved for the consideration of the President, has received his assent.
Statute	2042.
Statute	Section 3 , like Section 2 , is made under Article 368 as amended by the 24 th Amendment.
Statute	The provisions of Article 31C fall within the scope of the amended Article 368 , and its validity, too, cannot be assailed.
Statute	2043.
Statute	It is pointed out by <NAME> that Article 31C authorises <ORG> and <ORG> as a legislative body to make laws contravening the rights conferred by Articles 14, 19 and 31 and that it, in effect, delegates the power of making amendments in those articles.
Statute	Pointedly, the argument is that the <ORG> as the constituent power has delegated the constituent power to the <ORG> as a legislative body and the <ORG>.
Statute	2044.
Statute	It is also stressed that the second part of Section 3 arms the legislatures with the absolute power of sheltering laws which violate Articles 14, 19 and 31 and have no relation to the principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c).
Statute	2045.
Statute	The second part prohibits any court from inquirying whether the law protected by Article 31C has relevancy to Article 39(b) and (c) if it contains a declaration that it gives effect  to the policy specified in that provision.
Statute	Howsoever shocking it may seem, it is not an innovation.
Statute	You will find several articles having a close resemblance to it.
Statute	Article 77(2) provides that the validity of an order or instrument which is authenticated as provided therein 'shall not be called in question on the ground that it is not an order or instrument made or executed by the President'.
Statute	A similar provision is made in Article 166(2) in relation to the Governor.
Statute	Article 103(1) provides that if any question arises as to whether a member of either House of Parliament has become subject to any of the qualifications mentioned in Article 102(1 ), the question shall be decided by the President and 'his decision shall be final.'
Statute	A similar provision is to be found in Article 192(1) as regards the members of <ORG> with respect rto the decision of the Governor, Article 311(2) gives a right of licaring to an employee sought to be dismissed or removed or reduced in rank.
Statute	Clause (b) of the proviso to t he article enacts that where the appointing authority'is satisfied that for some reason it is not reasonably practicable to hold such inquiry, the pre-requisite of hearing may be dispensed with.
Statute	Clause (3) of Article 311 then enacts that if a question arises whether it is reasonably practicable to hold an inquiry, 'the decision thereon of the authority...shall be final'.
Statute	Article 329(a) enacts that notwithstanding anything in the Constitution the validity of any law relating to the delimitation of constituencies or allotment of seats to such constituencies made or purporting to be made under Article 327 or Article 328 shall not be called in question in any court.
Statute	Like these articles, the second part of Section 3 excludes judicial review to a limited extent.
Statute	2046.
Statute	The main part of Article 31C consists of two parts; The first part provides that no law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c) shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is in consistent with or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fast part may be split up into two : (a) giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing  (b) the principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	Under the first part the <ORG> has to see two things before a particular law can receive protection of Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	Firstly, the law must have relevancy to the principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c); secondly, the law should give effect to those principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 39(b) provides that the <ORG> shall strive to secure that the ownership and control of the material resources of the community are so distributed as best to subserve the common good.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 39(c) urges the <ORG> to strive to secure that the operation of the economic system does not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to the common detriment, It may be observed that "subserve the common good" in Clause (b) and 'common detriment' in Clause (c) raise questions of fac t Now, it is possible to imagine a state of affairs where a law having relevancy to the principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c) may not appear to the <ORG> to subserve the common good or to prevent common detriment.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such a law will not prevail over Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thus the first part retains the <ORG>'s power to decide the legal question of the law's relevancy to the principles specified in Article 39(b) and  (c) as well as the factual question of the law's efficacy to subserve the common good or to prevent common detriment.
RatioOfTheDecision	It can test the ends as well as the means of the law.
RatioOfTheDecision	2047.
RatioOfTheDecision	Coming to the second part, it excludes judicial review 'on the ground that (the law) does not give effect to such policy'.
RatioOfTheDecision	So the law cannot be challenged on the ground that the means adopted by the law are not sufficient to subserve the common good and prevent common detriment.
RatioOfTheDecision	In other words, the sufficiency of the law's efficacy alone is made non-justiciable.
RatioOfTheDecision	The <ORG> still retains power to determine whether the  law has relevancy to the distribution of the ownership and control of the material resources of the community and to the operation of the economic system and concentration of wealth and means of production.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the <ORG> finds that the law has no such relevancy, it will declare the law void if it offends the provisions of Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	2048.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fate of a provision included in a law containing the requisite declaration but having no relevancy as discussed will be no better.
RatioOfTheDecision	It will also be void if it offends against Articles 14, 19 and 31 unless it is subordinate, ancillary or consequential to any provision having such relevancy or forms an integral part of the scheme of such provision.
RatioOfTheDecision	Delegation of Amending Power 2049.
RatioOfTheDecision	As Article 368(2) as now amended provides that 'only' <ORG> may amend the Constitution by the prescribed procedure, it is said that <ORG> may not delegate the constituent power to any extraneous authority.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is not necessary to decide this question.
RatioOfTheDecision	Assuming that <ORG> ent may not delegate the constituent power, the question still remains whether Article 31C authorise <ORG> and <ORG> as a legislative body to amend any part of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	2050.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power of the <ORG> and <ORG> to make a law with respect to the principles specified in Articles 39(b) and (c) is derived from Article 246 read with Lists I, II and III of the Seventh Schedule.
RatioOfTheDecision	Their legislative power is however not absolute.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is restricted by various fundamental rights including those in Articles 14, 19 and 31, for Article 13(2) expressly prohibits the legislatures from making a law which will be violative of those rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	2051.
RatioOfTheDecision	What does Article 31C seek to do?
RatioOfTheDecision	One, the non-obstante clause in Article 31C removes the bar of Article 13(2) against law making with respect to the principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	The bar, however, is not removed in respect of all the fundamental rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is removed in respect of the rights in Articles 14, 19 and 31 only.
RatioOfTheDecision	Second, Articles 14, 19 and 31 remain operative as a bar against law-making with respect to all matters other than the principles specified in Articles 39(b) and (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	They are in partial eclipse as regards laws having relevancy to the principles specified in Article 39(b) and (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	This is the true nature and character of Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	We should be guided by what it really does and not by how it seems, by its effect and not by its semantic garb.
RatioOfTheDecision	Looked at in this manner, Article 31C is in the nature of a saving clause to Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	Instead of being placed at the end of each of these articles, it is placed at one place for the sake of drafting elegance and economy.
RatioOfTheDecision	As a saving clause, Article 31C saves certain kinds of laws from destruction at the hands of Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	2052.
RatioOfTheDecision	This effect is brought about directly and immediately by the choice of the constituent power expressed in Article 31C itself and not by the laws which claim its protection.
RatioOfTheDecision	Those laws do not expressly or impliedly ta ke away or abridge the rights in Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	The constituent power itself has brought about that effect through Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is therefore no delegation of the constituent power.
RatioOfTheDecision	<CITATION_1355522>  1 S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	380 this <ORG> has considered the question of delegation of legislative power.
RatioOfTheDecision	Section 3 of the Essential Supplies.
Statute	(Temporary Powers) Act, 1946 enabled <ORG> to make orders for maintaining or increasing supplies of any essential commodity or for securing for their equitable distribution and availability at fair prices and for regulating or prohibiting the production, supply and distribution thereof and trade and commerce therein Section 6 provided that any order made under Section 5 would have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent therewith contained in any enactment other than the Act or any instrument having effect by virtue of any enactment other than the Act.
Precedent	It was argued before the High <ORG> that Section 6 delegated legislative power to <ORG> nt because an order made under Section 3 had the effect of repealing an existing law.
RatioOfTheDecision	<ORG> accepted the argument.
RatioOfTheDecision	But on appeal this <ORG> reversed the judgment of the High <ORG> and held that Section 6 did not delegate legislative power.
RatioOfTheDecision	The <ORG> said:  The effect of Section 6 certainly is not to repeal any one of these laws or abrogate them.
RatioOfTheDecision	Its object is simply to by-pass them where they are inconsistent with the provisions of the Essential Supplies (Temporary Powers) Act, 1946 or the orders made thereunder.
RatioOfTheDecision	In other words, the orders made under Section 3 would be operative in regard to the essential commodity covered by the Textile Control Order wherever there is repugnancy in this Order with the existing laws and to that extent the existing laws with regard to those commodities will not operate.
RatioOfTheDecision	By passing a certain law does hot necessarily amount to repeal or abrogation of that law.
RatioOfTheDecision	That law remains unrepealed but during the continuance of the order made under Section 3 it does not operate in  that field for the time being.
RatioOfTheDecision	The ambit of its operation is just limited without there being any repeal of any one of its provisions.
RatioOfTheDecision	Conceding, however, for the sake of argument that to the extent of a repugnancy between an order made under Section 3 , and the provisions of an existing law the existing law stands repealed by implication, it seems to us that the repeal is not by any Act of the <ORG> itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	By enacting Section 6 <ORG> itself has declared that an order made under Section 3 shall have effect notwithstanding any inconsistency in this order with any enactment other than that Act.
RatioOfTheDecision	This is not a declaration made by the delegate but the <ORG> itself has declared its will that way in Section 6 .
RatioOfTheDecision	The abrogation or the implied repeal is by force of the order made by the delegate under Section 3 .
RatioOfTheDecision	The power of the delegate is only to make an order under Section 3 .
RatioOfTheDecision	Once the delegate has made that order its power is exhausted.
RatioOfTheDecision	Section 6 then steps in wherein the <ORG> has declared  that as soon as such an order comes into being that will have effect notwithstanding any inconsistency therewith contained in any enactment other than this Act....
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no delegation involved in the provisions of Section 6 at all... 1 S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	380 at page 391-392.
RatioOfTheDecision	2053.
RatioOfTheDecision	These observations squarely apply to the provisions of Article 31C accordingly hold that there is no delegation of the constituent power.
RatioOfTheDecision	2054.
RatioOfTheDecision	Since the laws claiming protection of Article 31C themselves do not work an amendment in Articles 14, 19 and 31, it is not necessary that they should pass through the procedure prescribed in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The meaning of 'distributed' in Article 39(b) 2055.

RatioOfTheDecision	But the question will be sufficient at this stage to refer to certain aspects briefly.
RatioOfTheDecision	The <ORG> is the representative and trustee of the people.
RatioOfTheDecision	A nationalised property is vested in the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Through the <ORG>, the  entire people collectively may be said to own property.
RatioOfTheDecision	It may be said that in this way the ownership of the nationalised property is distributed amongst the people represented by the <ORG>.

RatioOfTheDecision	2067.
Precedent	<NAME>, an extreme exponent of the sovereignty of amending power under Article V of the American Constitution, has described that power as 'sui generis'.
Precedent	I will borrow that expression to say that the whole matter before us is truly sui generis.
Precedent	The largest <ORG> sat for the longest time to decide issues described as being of grave moment not merely to the future of this country but to the future of democracy itself.
Precedent	For a proper understanding of the meaning and sc ope of the amending provisions contained in Article 368 of our Constitution.
Precedent	We were invited to consider parallel clauses in the Constitutions of 71 countries of the world spread far and wide, with conflicting social and political philosophies.
Precedent	We travelled thus to new lands like Bolivia, Costa Rica, El Salvador, Gautemala, Honduros, Liberia, Nicarague, Paraguay, Uruguay and Venezuela.
Precedent	Constitutional sojourns to Australia, Canada, Ceylon, France, Germany, Ireland, Switzerland, U.S.S.R. and U.S.A. were of course of frequent occurrence.
Precedent	These excursions were interesting but not proportioned to their utility, for I believe there is no international yardstick with which" to measure the width of an amending power.
Precedent	2068.

Precedent	2069.
Argument	At one end is the view propounded by writers like <NAME> Garner ('Political Science and Government') and <NAME> ('The Government of the United States') that an unamendable Constitution is the worst tyranny of time or rather the very tyranny of time and that such a Constitution constitutes government by the gra veyard.
Argument	At the other end is the view expressed with equal faith and vigour by writers like Dr. <NAME> ('Limitation of Amendment Procedures and the Constituent Power'), <NAME> ('The Limitations upon the Amending Power'-Harvard Law Review, Vol.
Argument	XXXIII) and <NAME> 'Intrinsic Limitations on the Power of Constitutional Amendment'-Michigan Law Review, Vol.
Argument	18 that any amending body organised within the statutory scheme, however verbally unlimited its power, cannot by its very structure change the fundamental pillers supporting its Constitutional authority; that the constituent assembly cannot create a second perpetual pouvoir constitueant above the nation; that it may be safely premised that the power to amend the Constitution cannot include the power to destroy it; that the greatest delusion of the modern political world is the delusion of popular sovereignty-a fiction under which all the dictators have sprung up and thrived; and that men should be afraid that any Judge complaint enough to read in to a Constitution a beneficial power patently not there, might at another time be complient enough to read within it any or all of the guarantees of their liberty for, a Judge willing to take orders from a benevolent despot might be equally subservient to a malevolent one.
RatioOfTheDecision	Someone has said in a lighter vein that Law comes from the west and Light from the east, but brushing aside such considerations, the conflicting views of these writers, distinguished though they be, cannot conclude the controversy before us, which must be decided on the terms of our Constitution and the genius of our Nation.
RatioOfTheDecision	The learning of these scholars has lighted my path and their views must be given due weight and consideration.
RatioOfTheDecision	But the danger of relying implicitly on everyone of the standpoints of everyone of these authors is apparent from what <NAME> said in answer to a question put to him at the end of his lecture on 'Government under Law : A Civilian View'.

Precedent	The answer to this contention would consist in the inquiry, firstly as regards the validity of the core and hence the consequences of natural law thinking; and secondly, on whether our organic document supports the inference that natural rights were either recognised by it-explicitly or implicitly-and if so, whether any of such rights were permitted to be reserved by the people without any qualification, so that an individual would be entitled to protect and nurse a minimal core of suc h rights, uninfluenced by social considerations.

Precedent	2075.
Precedent	During the course of arguments, a catena of decisions of several courts were cited before us.
Precedent	I thought when the arguments began-yes, I remember it because the commencement of the case is not that lost in antiquity-that the judgments of this <ORG> will form the focus of discussion, foreign decisions making a brief appearance.
Precedent	But in retrospect, I think I was wrong.
Precedent	Learning, like language, is no one's monopoly and counsel were ent itled to invite us to consider how heroically courts all over the world had waged battles in defence of fundamental freedoms and on the other hand how, on occasions, the letter of law was permitted to prevail in disregard of evil consequences.
Precedent	Between such extremes, the choice is always difficult and delicate but it has to be made for, in a matter involving the cherished freedoms of the subject and the powers of the <ORG>, I do not want to project my freedom to say, as Justice <NAME> of <ORG> did in the National Prohibition Cases involving the validity of the Eighteenth Amendment to the American Constitution, that I am unable to come to any conclusion.
Precedent	But I am quite clear that I have no use for the advice of <NAME> ('Freedom, Virtue & The First Amendment' 1957), that since there can be no freedom to end freedom even if the people desire to enslave themselvs, "<ORG> must act undemocratically in order to preserve democracy".
Precedent	Nor indeed shall I walk down the garden -path laid by <NAME> ('Constitution Amendment and the implied Bill of Rights', <ORG>, Volume 12), that "where an issue as vital as the protection of civil liberties is concerned, and where the legislators have demonstrated their inability to provide adequate safeguards, the courts are entirely justified (perhaps even morally obliged) in employing all the ingenuity and imagination at their command to preserve individual rights".
RatioOfTheDecision	Such exhortations have a spartan air which lends colourfulness to arid texts but they overlook the fundamental premise that judges, unlike <NAME>, are not law- givers.
RatioOfTheDecision	Besides, it cannot ever be too strongly stressed that the power of substantive 'due process of law' available under the Fourteenth Amendment to the American Constitution was considered and rejected by our <ORG> which contained a galaxy of legal talent.
Precedent	In America, under the due process clause, there was a time when <ORG> used to invalidate laws because they were thought to be u nwise or incompatible with some particular economic or social philosophy.
Precedent	Thus, in <NAME> v. New York, 49 L. ed.

Precedent	It was decades later that the <ORG> recognised the value and the validity of the dissenting opinion recorded by Justice <NAME>:  This case is decided upon an economic theory which a large part of the country does not entertain.
Precedent	If it were a question whether agreed with that theory, I should desire to study it further and long before making up my mind.
Precedent	But I do not conceive that to be my duty, because I strongly believe that my agreement or disagreement has nothing to do with the right of a majority to embody their opinions in law.
Precedent	It is settled by various decisions of this <ORG> that State Constitutions and State laws may regulate life in many ways which we as legis lators might think as injudicious or if you like as tyranical as this, and which equally with this interfere with the liberty to contract.
Precedent	* * * The Fourteenth Amendment does not enact Mr. <NAME>'s Social Statics.
Precedent	* * * But a Constitution...is made for people of fundamentally differing views and the accident of our finding certain opinions natural and familiar or novel and even shocking ought not to conclude our judgment upon the question whether statutes embodying them conflict with the Constitution.
Precedent	In course of time such shining dissents became the majority view and the due process clause came to be construed as permitting enactment of laws limiting the hours of labour in mines, prohibiting employment of children in hazardous occupations, regulating payment of wages, preserving minimum wages for women and children, the 'Blue Sky laws' and the 'Man's Best Friend (Dog) laws'.
Precedent	Even laws like the Kentuky Statutes requiring Banks to turn over to the protective custody of that State deposits that were i nactive for 10 or 25 years were upheld, as not involving taking over the property of the banks <ORG> v. <NAME> 321 U.S. 233.

Precedent	2096.
Precedent	The validity of the Amendment Act , 1951 was challenged in this Court in Sri Shankar Prasad Singh Deo v. <ORG> and State of Bihar  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	89.
Precedent	It was urged in that case that the Amendment.
Precedent	Act in so far as it purported to take away or abridge the rights conferred by Part III felt within the prohibition of Article 13(2) and was therefore unConstitutional.
Precedent	<NAME> who spoke for the unanimous court rejected this argument by holding that although 'law' would ordinarily include Constitutional law, there was a clear demarcation between ordinary law made in the exercise of legislative power and Constitutional law made in exercise of constituent power; and therefore, in the absence of a clear indication to the contrary, Fundamental Rights were not immune from Constitutional amendment.
Precedent	The challenge to the Amendment Act , 1951 was on these grounds rejected.

Argument	Out of the the several arguments which were urged in that case the only one which is relevant for the present purpose is that the Amendment Act was void in view of the provisions of Article 13(2 ), in so far as the Act purported to abridge the Fundamental Rights guaranteed by Part III.
Argument	Delivering the majority judgment, <NAME> took the view on behalf of himself, <NAME> and <NAME> that the expression 'amendment of the Constitution' plainly and unambiguously means amendment of all the provisions of the Constituion and therefore the amending power conferred by Article 368 extended to all the provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	The majority judgment rejected the contention that the word 'law' in Article 13(2) would take in C onstitution Amendment Act s passed under Article 368 , as there was a clear distinction between the constituent power conferred by Article 368 and the ordinary legislative power and Article 13(2) would take in laws made in the exercise of the latter power only.
Precedent	<NAME> and <NAME> concurred in the final conclusion but by separate judgments they doubted the majority view and observed that it was possible that Article 368 merely laid down the procedure for amending the Constitution but did not confer the power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	Both the learned Judges however stated expressely that they should not be taken to have expressed a final opinion on that question.
