Precedent	762 were sown by the doubt thus expressed by <NAME> and <NAME>  2100.
Precedent	The decision in the <NAME> case was rendered by a Bench of 11 Judges of this Court on February 27, 1967.
Precedent	The petitioners therein had challenged the validity of Punjab Act 10  of 1953 and the Mysore Act 10 of 1962 as amended by Act 14 of 1965, on the ground that these Acts violated their Fundamental Rights, alleging that though the impugned acts were included in the Ninth Schedule, they did not receive the protection of the 1st, 4th and 17th Amendment Act s. It was common case that if the 17th Amendment which included the impugned Acts in the Ninth Schedule was valid, the Acts would not be open to challenge on any ground.
Precedent	2101.
Precedent	Chief Justice <NAME> delivered the leading majority judgment for himself and for Justices <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME>.
Precedent	<NAME> concurred with their conclusion but delivered a separate judgment.
Precedent	<NAME> delivered the leading minority judgment on behalf of himself and Justices <NAME> and <NAME>.
Precedent	Justice <NAME> and Justice <NAME> concurred by their separate judgments with the view expressed in the leading minority judgment.
Precedent	2102.
Precedent	The leading majority judgment recorded the following conclusions:  1.
Precedent	That Fundamental Rights are the p rimordial rights necessary for the development of human personality and as such they are rights of the people preserved by the Constitution.
Precedent	2103.
Precedent	The Constitution has given by its scheme a place of permanence to the fundamental freedoms.
Precedent	In giving to themselves the Constitution the people have reserved the fundamental freedoms to themselves.
Precedent	The incapacity of the <ORG>, therefore, in exercise of its amending power to modify, restrict or impair fundamental freedoms in Part III arises from the scheme of the Constitution and the nature of the freedoms.
Precedent	2104.
Precedent	Article 368 assumes the power to amend found elsewhere.
Precedent	In other words, Article 368 does not confer power on <ORG> to amend the Constitution but merely prescribes the procedure for the exercise of such power to amend.
Precedent	2105.
Precedent	The power to amend is to be found in Articles 245 and 248 read with Entry 97 in List I of the Seventh Schedule to the Constitution.
Precedent	2106.
Precedent	In the exercise of the power of amendment, <ORG> could not destroy the structure  of the Constitution but it could only modify the provisions thereof within the framework of original instrument for its better effectuation.
Precedent	In other words, the provisions of the Constitution could undoubtedly be amended but not so as to take away or abridge the Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	2107.
Precedent	There is no distinction between the power to amend the Constitution and the ordinary power to make laws.
Precedent	2108.
Precedent	Article 13(2) which contains an inclusive definition, prima facie takes in Constitutional law.
Precedent	2109.
Precedent	The residuary power of <ORG> could be relied upon to call for <ORG> for making the new Constitution or radically changing it.
Precedent	(This opinion however was tentative and not final).
Precedent	2110.
Precedent	The Seventeenth Amendment Act impugned before the court as also the First, Fourth and Sixteenth Amendments were Constitutionally invalid.
Precedent	Declaring these amendments invalid was, however, likely to lead to confusion and chaos and therefore these amendments would be deemed to be valid except for future purposes, b y application of the principle of 'prospective invalidation'.
Precedent	2111.
Precedent	In future, <ORG> will have no power to amend Part III of the Constitution so as to take away or abridge the Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	2112.
Precedent	<NAME> agreed with the final decision expressed in the leading majority judgment and his views can be summarised as follows:  1.
Precedent	The power of amendment must be possessed by the <ORG>.
Precedent	One could not take a narrow view of the word 'amendment' as including only minor changes within the general framework.
Precedent	By an amendment, new matter may be added, old matter removed or altered.
Precedent	2113.
Precedent	Article 368 outlines a process which if followed strictly results in the amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	The article gives power to no particular person or persons.
Precedent	2114.
Precedent	The procedure of amendment, if it can be called a power at all is a legislative power but it is sui generis and outside the three Lists of Schedule Seven of the Constitution.
Precedent	2115.
Precedent	There is no distinction in our Constitution between laws made ordinarily  and laws made occasionally for the amendment of the Constitution.
Precedent	Therefore, Constitutional amendments must fall within the scope of Article 13(2).
Precedent	2116.
Precedent	The whole Constitution is open to amendment, only two dozen articles being outside the reach of Article 368; that too, because the Constitution has made them fundamental.
Precedent	2117.
Precedent	Fundamental Rights cannot be abridged or taken away by the ordinary amending process.
Precedent	<ORG> must amend Article 368 to convoke <ORG>, pass a law under Item 7 of List I to call <ORG> and then that <ORG> may be able to abridge or take away the Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	The <ORG> was constituted with powers of legislation which included amendments of the Constitution but only so far as Article 13(2) allowed.
Precedent	2118.
Precedent	<ORG> had no power to amend Article 368 so as to confer on itself constituent powers over the Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	This would be wrong and against Article 13(2).
Precedent	2119.
Precedent	The conclusion recorded by the leading majority judgmen t was correct, not on the ground of prospective invalidation of laws but on the ground of acquiescence.
Precedent	The First, Fourth and Seventh Amendments were part of the Constitution by acquiescence for a long time and could not therefore be challenged.
Precedent	They also contained authority for the Seventeenth Amendment.
Precedent	2120.
Precedent	<NAME> who delivered the leading minority judgment came to the following conclusions:  1.
Precedent	Both the procedure and the power to amend the Constitution are to be found in Article 368 and not in Entry 97 of List I.
Precedent	2121.
Precedent	The word 'amendment' must be given its full meaning, that is, that the power was not restricted to improvement of details but extended to the addition to or substitution or deletion of existing provisions.
Precedent	2122.
Precedent	In exercise of the power conferred by Article 368 it was competent to the <ORG> by observing the procedure prescribed therein to amend any provision of the Constitution.
Precedent	2123.
Precedent	The word 'law' in Article 13(2) could only take in laws made by <ORG> and State Legislat ures in the exercise of their ordinary legislative power but not amendments made under Article 368.
Precedent	2124.
Precedent	The power to amend being a constituent power cannot be held to be subject to any implied limitations on the supposed ground that the basic features of the Constitution could not be amended.
Precedent	2125.
Precedent	<NAME> agreed with <NAME> and stated:  1.
Precedent	No limitation on the amending power could be gathered from the language of Article 368.
Precedent	Each and every part of the Constitution could therefore be amended under that Article.
Precedent	2126.
Precedent	The distinction between the Constitution and the laws is so fundamental that the Constitution cannot be regarded as a law or a legislative act.
Precedent	2127.
Precedent	Article 368 indicates that the term 'amend' means 'change'.
Precedent	A change is not necessarily an improvement.
Precedent	2128.
Precedent	It was unnecessary to decide the contention whether the basic features of the Constitution, as for example, the republic form of government or the federal structure thereof could be amended, as the question did not arise for  decision.
Precedent	2129.
Precedent	<NAME> adopted a similar line of reasoning and held: 2130.
Precedent	That the definition of 'law' in Article 13(3) did not include in terms 'Constitutional amendment'.
Precedent	Had it been intended by the Constitution-makers that the Fundamental Rights guaranteed by Part III should be completely outside the scope of Article 368 it is reasonable to assume that they would have made an express provision to that effect.
Precedent	2131.
Precedent	The Preamble to the Constitution which declared India as a sovereign democratic republic was not beyond the scope of the amending power; similarly certain other basic features of the Constitution like those relating to distribution of legislative power, the parliamentary power of Government and the establishment of <ORG> and <ORG> were also not beyond the power of amendment.
Precedent	2132.
Precedent	Every one of the articles of the Constitution is amendable under Article 368 and there was no room for any implication in the construction of that article.
Precedent	2133.
Precedent	It is thus clear that the  majority of Judges in the <NAME> case consisting of Justices <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME>, <NAME> and <NAME> rejected the argument that Article 368 merely prescribes the procedure to be followed in amending the Constitution.
Precedent	They held that Article 368 also conferred the power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	They rejected the argument that the power to amend could be found in Entry 97 of List I.
Precedent	The majority of Judges consisting of <NAME>, <NAME> and his 4 colleagues as well as <NAME> J. held that there was no distinction between constituent power and legislative power and that the word 'law' used in Article 13(2) includes a law passed by <ORG> to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	<NAME> <NAME> and his 4 colleagues suggested that if a Constitution had to be radically altered the residuary power could be relied upon to call for <ORG>.
Precedent	<NAME> J. took a different view and held that for making radical alterations so as to abridge Fundamental Rights Article 368 should be su itably amended and <ORG> should be called after passing a law under Entry 97 in the light of the amended provisions of Article 368.
Precedent	It is important to mention that all the eleven Judges who constituted the <ORG> were agreed that even Fundamental Rights could be taken away but they suggested different methods for achieving that purpose.
Precedent	<NAME> and his 4 colleagues suggested calling of <ORG>; <NAME> suggested an amendment of Article 368 for calling <ORG> after passing a law under Entry 97; the remaining 5 Judges held that the <ORG> itself had the power to amend the Constitution so as to abridge or take away the Fundamental Rights.
Precedent	2134.
Precedent	The leading majority judgment did not decide whether Article 368 itself could be amended so as to confer a power to amend every provision of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The reason for this was that the <NAME> case was decided on the basis of the unamended Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	The question whether Fundamental Rights co uld be taken away by amending Article 368 was not before the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	The question also whether in future <ORG> could by amending Article 368 assume the power to amend every part and provision of the Constitution was not in issue before the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such a question could arise directly, as it arises now, only after an amendment was in fact made in Article 368 , and the terms of that amendment were known.
RatioOfTheDecision	The observation in the leading majority judgment putting restraints on the future power of the <ORG> to take away Fundamental Rights cannot therefore constitute the ratio of the majority judgment.
Precedent	The learned Judges did not evidently consider that in future the chapter on Fundamental Rights could be made subject to an amendment by first amending Article 368 as is now done under the Twenty- Fourth Amendment.
Precedent	2135.
Precedent	It shall have been seen that the petitioners in the Golak Nath case won but a Pyrrhic victory.
Precedent	They came to the <ORG>, not for the decision of an academic issue, but to obtain a declaration th at laws which affected their fundamental rights were unConstitutional.
Precedent	Those laws were upheld by the court but I suppose that the petitioners left the court with the consolation that posterity will enjoy the fruits of the walnut tree planted by them.
Precedent	But it looks as if a storm is brewing threatening the very existence of the tree.
Precedent	2136.
Precedent	As stated above, 6 out of the 11 learned Judges held in the <NAME> case that Article 368 prescribed not merely the procedure for amendment but conferred the power to amend the Constitution and that the amending power cannot be traced to the Residuary Entry 97 of List I, Schedule VII read with Articles 245, 246 and 248 of the Constitution.
Precedent	I respectfully adopt this view taken by the majority of Judges.
Precedent	2137.
Precedent	Part XX of the Constitution is entitled "Amendment of the Constitution", not "Procedure for Amendment of the Constitution".
Precedent	Article 368 , which is the only article in Part XX must therefore be held to deal both with the procedure and the product of that procedure.
RatioOfTheDecision	The  marginal note to Article 368: " Procedure for Amendment of the Constitution" was only a catchword and was in fact partially correct.
RatioOfTheDecision	It did not describe the consequence of the adoption of the procedure because the title of the part described it clearly.
RatioOfTheDecision	The justification of the somewhat inadequate marginal note to Article 368 can be sought in the fact that the article does not confer power on any named authority but prescribes a self-executing procedure which if strictly followed results in this : "the Constitution shall stand amended".
RatioOfTheDecision	The history of the residuary power since the days of the Government of India Act, 1935, and the scheme of distribution of legislative power show that if a subject of legislative power was prominently present to the minds of the framers of the Constitution, it would not have been relegated to a Residuary Entry, but would have been included expressly in the legislative list-more probably in List I.
RatioOfTheDecision	That the question of Constitutional amendment was prominently present to the mind s of the Constitution-makers is clear from the allocation of a separate Part-Part XX-to "Amendment of the Constitution".
RatioOfTheDecision	Then, the legislative power under Entry 97, List I, belongs exclusively to the <ORG>.
Precedent	The power to amend the Constitution cannot be located in that Entry because in regard to matters falling within the proviso to Article 368 , <ORG> does not possess exclusive power to amend the Constitution.
Precedent	The Draft Constitution of India also points in the direction that the power of amendment cannot be located in the Residuary Entry.
Precedent	Draft Article 304 , which corresponds to Article 368 , conferred by Sub- article (2) a limited power of amendment on the State <ORG> also and those <ORG> neither possessed the residuary power of legislation nor did the State List, List II, include 'Amendment of the Constitution' as a subject of legislative power.
Precedent	Finally, the power to legislate under Article 245 is "subject to the provisions of this Constitution", so that under the residuary power, no  amendment could be made to any part of the Constitution, as any amendment is bound, to some extent, to be inconsistent with the article to be amended.
Precedent	2138.
Precedent	Having located the amending power in Article 368 and having excluded the argument that it can be traced to Entry 97 of List I, it becomes necessary to determine the width and scope of that power.
Precedent	Is the power unfettered and absolute or are there any limitations-express, implied or inherent on its exercise?
Precedent	2139.
Argument	Counsel for the petitioner urges : (1) That the word 'amendment' is not a term of art and has no precise and definite, or primary and fundamental, meaning; (2) That Article 368 carries vital implications by its very terms and there is inherent evidence in that Article to show chat in the context thereof the word 'amendment' cannot cover alterations in, damage to, or destruction of any of the essential features of the Constitution; (3) That Article 13(2) by taking in Constitutional amendments constitutes an express limitations on the power of am endment; (4) That there are implied and inherent limitations on the amending power which disentitle <ORG> to damage or destroy any of the essential features, basic elements or fundamental principles of the Constitution; and (5) That in construing the ambit of the amending power, the consequences on the power being held to be absolute and unfettered must be taken into account.
Argument	Counsel says that Article 368 should not be read as expressing the death-wish of the Constitution or as being a provision for its legal suicide.
Argument	<ORG>, he says, cannot arrogate to itself, under Article 368 , the role of an Official Liquidator of the Constitution.
Precedent	Each of these propositions is disputed by the Respondents as stoutly as they were asserted.
Precedent	2140.
Precedent	'Amendment' is undoubtedly not a term of art and the various dictionaries, texts and law lexicons cited before us show that the word has several shades of meaning.
Precedent	(Sec for example the meanings given in The Shorter Oxford English Dictionary on historical Principles, 3rd E d.; Webster's Third New International Dictionary of the English Language; The Random House Dictionary of the English Language; The Dictionary of English Law, <NAME>; Judicial and Statutory Definitions of Words and Phrases, Second Series; Words and Phrases legally defined, <NAME>; Wharton's Law Lexicon, 14 Ed.
Precedent	; Words and Phrases Permanent Edition; and The Construction of Statutes by Earl T.
Precedent	Crawford.)
Precedent	2141.
Precedent	Some of <ORG> have taken the view that the term 'amendment' implies such an addition or change within the lines of the original instrument as will effect an improvement, or better carry out the purpose for which it was framed.
Precedent	(See <NAME> v. <NAME> (1894) 102 Cal.
Precedent	113; <NAME> v. <NAME> 32, Cal.
Precedent	2d.
Argument	330; <NAME> v. <NAME> 144, <ORG>).
Argument	Another line of decisions, again of <ORG>, has accepted a wider meaning of the word 'amendment' so as to include within it even a 'revision' of a Constitutional document.
Argument	(See <NAME> v. <NAME>, Sout hwestern Reporter, Vol.
Argument	33, p. 1120; Ex Parte Dillon, Federal Reporter No.
Precedent	262, p. 563; <ORG> v. <NAME>, American Law Reports Annotated, Vol.
Precedent	158, p. 495).
Precedent	2142.
Precedent	In brief, it would be correct to say that at least three different meanings have been generally given to the word 'amendment':  (a) to improve or better; to remove an error;  (b) to make changes which may not improve the instrument but which do not alter, damage or destroy the basic features, essential elements or fundamental principles of the instrument sought to be amended; and  (c) to make any changes whatsoever.
Precedent	2143.
Precedent	These texts and authorities are useful in that they bring a sense of awareness of the constructional difficulties involved in the interpretation of a seemingly simple word like 'amendment'.
Precedent	But enriched by such awareness, we must in the last analysis go to our own organic document for determining whether the word 'amendment' in Article 368 is of an ambiguous and uncertain import.
Precedent	2144.
Precedent	The various shades of meaning of the word ' amendment' may apply differently in different contexts, but it seems to me that in the context in which that word occurs in Article 368 , it is neither ambiguous nor amorphous, but has a definite import.
Precedent	2145.
RatioOfTheDecision	The proviso to Article 368 furnishes intrinsic evidence to show that the word 'amendment' is used in that article not in a narrow and insular sense but is intended to have the widest amplitude.
Statute	Article 368 provides that "An amendment of this Constitution may be initiated only by the introduction of a Bill for the purpose in either <ORG>", and after the Bill is passed by the prescribed majority, "the Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill".
Statute	The proviso says that the amendment shall also require to be ratified by the State legislatures of not less than one- half of the States if "such amendment seeks to make any change in" the matters mentioned in Clauses (a) to (e) of the proviso.
RatioOfTheDecision	"Such amendment" obviously means 'amendment' referred to in the main body of  Article 368 and thus the article itself envisages that the amendment may take the form of 'change'.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is in this case a dictionary at every corner for every word and we were referred to various meanings of 'change' also.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is enough to cite the meaning of the word from <ORG> (Vol.
Argument	I, p. 291): "Change: substitution...of one thing for another.
Argument	Alteration in the state or quality of any thing".
Precedent	<NAME>'s 3rd New International Dictionary Vol.
Precedent	III pp.
Precedent	373-4, gives the same meaning.
Precedent	It is clear beyond doubt that 'change' does not mean only 'such an addition...within the lines of the original instrument as will effect an improvement or better carry out the purpose for which it was framed'.
Precedent	2146.
Precedent	Paragraph 7 of Part D of the Fifth Schedule and paragraph 21 of the Sixth Schedule also furnish similar proof of the meaning of the word 'amendment'.
Precedent	These two paragraphs provide for amendment of the respective Schedules in identical terms: Amendment of the Schedule.-(1) <ORG> may from time  to time by law amend by way of addition, variation or repeal any of the provisions of this Schedule and, when the Schedule is so amended, any reference to this Schedule in this Constitution shall be construed as a reference to such Schedule as so amended.
Precedent	(2) No such law as is mentioned in sub-paragraph (1) of this paragraph shall be deemed to be an amendment of this Constitution for the purposes of Article 368.
Precedent	2147.
Precedent	Two things emerge from these provisions of Paragraphs 7 and 21 of the Fifth and Sixth Schedules.
Precedent	Firstly, that the concept of "amendment" as shown by Clause (1) takes in "addition, variation or repeal" and secondly, that an amendment even by way of "addition, variation or repeal" would fall within the terms of Article 368.
Precedent	It is expressly excepted from the scope of that article so that it may not fall within it, which it otherwise would.
Precedent	2148.
Precedent	The expression 'amendment' was used in a large number of articles of the Constitution as originally enacted: Articles 4(1)(2), 108(4), 109(3)(4), 111,  114(2), 169(2), 196(2), 198(3) and (4), 200, 201, 204(2), 207(1)(2)(3), 240(2), 274(1), 304(b) and  349.
Precedent	A reference to the content and the subject matter of these articles would show that in almost every one of the cases covered by these articles, 'amendment' would be by way of addition, variation or repeal.
Precedent	2149.
Precedent	In several provisions of the original Constitution, different expressions were used to indicate conferment of the amending power.
Precedent	Article 35(b) called it "altered, repealed, amended"; Article 243(1) described it as "repeal or amend".
Precedent	The proviso to Article 254(2) described it as "adding to, amending, varying Or repealing"; and Article 392(1) used the expression "such adaptations, whether by way of modification, addition or omission".
Precedent	The English language has a rich vocabulary and there are such nice and subtle differences in the shades of meaning of different words that it is said that there are, in that language, no synonyms.
Precedent	But I find it impossible to believe that the various expressions enume rated above have behind them any calculated purpose or design.
Precedent	Their use may easily, though with a little generosity, be attributed to a common failing to attain elegance of language.
Precedent	Reading more than meets the eye tends to visit the writing with the fate reserved for the poems of Sir <NAME>.
Precedent	When he wrote them, two persons knew what they meant  - he and the <NAME>.
Precedent	After hearing the critics, <NAME> alone knew what the poet intended: 2150.
Precedent	Constitutions of several countries of the world show the words 'amendment', 'alteration', 'revision' and 'change' are used promiscuously.
Precedent	The Constitutions of Liberia, Trinidad and Tobago show that there is no difference in meaning between 'amendment' and 'alteration'.
Precedent	Those of Somalia, Jordan, Kuwait, Lebanon, and the Vietnam Democratic Republic show that there is no difference between 'amendment' and 'revision'.
Precedent	The Constitution of Belgium shows that the words 'revision' and 'alteration' are used in the same sense.
Precedent	The Constitution of Barundi shows that 'amendment' de notes 'change'.
Precedent	The Constitutions of Monaco, Costa Rica, Cuba and Nicaragua show that 'amendment' can be total or partial.
Precedent	2151.
Precedent	Dr. <NAME> says of Article 368 , in "Limitation of Amendment Procedures and the Constituent Power" that "it is hardly possible to restrict the legal meaning of amendment to 'improvement', nor can it be denied that by amendment complete articles may be removed or replaced".
Precedent	The author is justified in this view.
Precedent	The Indian Constitution is neither the first written Constitution of the world nor of course the last.
Precedent	Since the time that the first written Constitution, namely the American Constitution was framed in 1787 until today, the expression 'amendment' is known to occur at least in 57 Constitutions out of 71.
Precedent	It is inconceivable that the power of changing a written instrument of fundamental importance would be so expressed for so long and in the Constitutions of so many countries, if the word 'amendment' was of doubtful import.
Precedent	2152.
Precedent	On August 21, 1946, <ORG>  passed the Government of India (Third Amendment) Act, 1949, which substituted a new Section 291 in the Government of India Act, 1935 giving to the Governor General the power to make such amendments as he considered necessary, whether by way of "addition, modification or repeal" in certain provisions.
Precedent	Shortly thereafter, that is, on September 17, 1949, <ORG> debated Article 304 corresponding to present Article 368 , using the word 'amendment' simpliciter.
Precedent	In the debate on Article 304 amendment No.
Precedent	3239 moved by <NAME> which sought to introduce in that article the words "whether by way of variation, addition or repeal" was rejected.
Precedent	2153.
RatioOfTheDecision	I am unable to read in this legislative history an inference that the word 'amendment' was used in Article 304 in order to curtail the scope of the amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is significant that the Government of India (Third Amendment) Act, 1939 was described in its title as an "Act to further amend the G.I.
RatioOfTheDecision	Act 1935" and the Preamble stated that it was  expedient to amend the Government of India Act, 1935.
RatioOfTheDecision	By Section 4 the old Section 291 was "repealed" totally and the new Section 291 was "substituted".
RatioOfTheDecision	By Section 3 a new sub-section was "inserted".
RatioOfTheDecision	By Section 5 a new item was "substituted" and totally new itmes Nos.
RatioOfTheDecision	31B and 31C were "inserted".
RatioOfTheDecision	The Act of 1949 therefore leaves no room for doubt that the word 'amend' included the power of addition, alteration and repeal.
RatioOfTheDecision	Apart from this it is well recognized that the use of different words does not necessarily produce a change in the meaning.
Precedent	(See <NAME> 'Interpretation of Statutes' 12th Ed., pp.
Precedent	286 to 289; <CITATION_1587029> A.I.R.
Precedent	1952 Bom.
Precedent	16, 20 per <NAME> and <NAME>).
Precedent	2154.
RatioOfTheDecision	Finally, it is important that 5 out of the 11 Judges in the <NAME> case took the view that the word 'amendment' must be given a wide meaning.
RatioOfTheDecision	The leading majority judgment did not consider that question on the ground that so far as Fundamental Rights were concerned, the question could be answered on a narrower  basis.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> also did not consider the meaning of the word 'amendment'.
Precedent	However, <NAME> who delivered the leading minority judgment, <NAME> and <NAME> took the view that the word must be given a wide meaning.
Precedent	According to <NAME>, "By an amendment new matter may be added, old matter removed or altered".
Precedent	2156.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thus the word 'amendment' in Article 368 has a clear and definite import and it connotes a power of the widest amplitude to make additions, alterations or variations.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power contained in Article 368 to amend the Constitution is indeed so wide that it expressly confers a power by Clause (e) of the proviso to amend the amending power itself.
RatioOfTheDecision	No express restraint having been imposed on the power to amend the amending power, it is unnecessary to seek better evidence of the width of the power of amendment under our Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	2157.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 , manifestly, does not impose any express limitations.
RatioOfTheDecision	The reason for this is obvious.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power of amendment is in substance an d reality a power to clarify the original intention obscured, for example, by limitations of language and experience, so as to adjust the intention as originally expressed to meet new challenges.
RatioOfTheDecision	As a nation works out its destiny, new horizons unfold themselves, new challenges arise and therefore new answers have to be found.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is impossible to meet the new and unforeseen demands on the enervated strength of a document evolved in a context which may have largely lost its relevance.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power of amendment is a safety valve and having regard to its true nature and purpose, it must be construed as being equal to the need for amendment.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power must rise to the occasion.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to <ORG>, 4th Ed.
RatioOfTheDecision	p. 139, "The constituent power bears an intimate relation to revolution.
RatioOfTheDecision	When the amending provisions fail to work in adjusting the Constitutional document to altered needs, revolution may result."
RatioOfTheDecision	That is why, the rule of strict construction which applies to a penal or taxi ng statute is out of place in a Constitutional Act and a 'construction most beneficial to the widest possible amplitude" of its powers must be adopted <ORG> v. <ORG> 1935 (A.C.) 500, 518.
RatioOfTheDecision	2158.
RatioOfTheDecision	If, on the terms of Article 368 the power of amendment is wide and unfettered, does Article 13(2) impose any restraint on that power?
RatioOfTheDecision	Hereby hangs a tale.
RatioOfTheDecision	A majority of Judges held in the <NAME> case that the power of amendment was to be traced to Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	But a majority, differently composed, held that amendment of the Constitution was 'law' within the meaning of Article 13(2) and, therefore, the <ORG> had no power to take away or abridge the rights conferred by Part III of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	This finding contained in the judgment of the leading majority and of <NAME> is the nerve of the decision in the <NAME> case.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is therefore necessary to consider that question closely.
RatioOfTheDecision	2159.
RatioOfTheDecision	I will set out in juxtaposition Articles 13(2), 245 and 368 in order to highlight their inter-r elation:  Article 13(2)  Article 245  Article 368 The State shall Subject to the Amendment of this not make any Provisions of Constitution may be law which takes this Constitution initiated only by the away or abridges <ORG> may make introduction of a Bill the rights conferred laws for the whole for the purpose in by this part.
RatioOfTheDecision	or any part of the either <ORG> of parliament, territory of India.
RatioOfTheDecision	and when the Bill, is passed each <ORG> by a majority of not less than two thirds of that <ORG> present and voting, it shall be presented to the Emphasis supplied) president for his assent and upon such assent being given to the Bill, the Constitution shall stand amended in accordance with the terms of the Bill.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 13(2) clearly echoes the language of Article 245.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 245 gives the power to 'make laws', while Article 13(2) imposes a limitation on the exercise of the power to 'make laws'.
RatioOfTheDecision	As between the two articles, Article 13(2) is the paramount law for, Article 245 is expressly subject to all the provi sions of the Constitution including Article 13(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	2160.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 368 avoids with scrupulous care the use of the word 'law', because there is a fundamental distinction between Constitutional law and ordinary law.
RatioOfTheDecision	The term 'Constitutional law' is never used in the sense of including the laws made under the Constitution.
Precedent	(See <NAME>-The Law and the Constitution, 5th Ed., pp.
Precedent	62-65).
Precedent	Constitutional law is the fundamental, superior or paramount law.
Precedent	Its authority and sanction are higher than those of ordinary laws.
Precedent	(<ORG>, Vol.
Precedent	VI, Constitution and Constitutional Law, p. 314).
Precedent	As stated by <NAME> in his 'Introduction to the study of the Law of the Constitution' (10th Ed.,) pp.
Precedent	149-151), the legislature in a federal Constitution is a subordinate law-making body whose laws are in the nature of bye-laws within the authority conferred by the Constitution.
Precedent	2161.
Precedent	Articles 3, 4, 169, Paragraph 7 of the Fifth Schedule and Paragraph 21 of the Sixth Schedule emphasises an important aspect of the distinct ion between Constitutional law and ordinary law.
Precedent	What is authorised to be done by these provisions would normally fall within the scope of Article 368.
Precedent	In order however to take out such matters from the scope of that article and to place those matters Within the ordinary legislative sphere, special provisions are made in these articles that any laws passed thereunder shall not be deemed to be an amendment of the Constitution for the purposes of Article 368.
Precedent	2162.
Precedent	Article 13(1) provides:  Laws inconsistent with or in derogation of the fundamental rights.-(1) All laws in force in the territory of India immediately before the commencement of this Constitution, in so far as they are in consistent with the provisions of this Part shall, to the extent of such inconsistency, be void.
Precedent	This article deals with the effect of inconsistency between the provisions of Part III and the pre-Constitution laws and provides that to the extent of such inconsistency the pre- Constitution laws shall be void Article 13(2) pursues t he same strain of thought by making void postConstitution laws to the extent of their inconsistency with the provisions of Part III.
Precedent	The pre-Constitution and the post-Constitution laws dealt with by the two clauses of Article 13 are in nature and character identical.
Precedent	They are ordinary laws as distinguished from Constitutional laws.
Precedent	2163.
Argument	Counsel for the petitioner urged that Article 395 of the Constitution repealed only the Indian Independence Act, 1947 and the Government of India Act of 1935 and under Article 372 , notwithstanding the repeal of these two enactments, all the laws in force in the territory of India immediately before the commencement of the Constitution continued in force until altered, repealed or amended.
Argument	It is urged that several Constitutional laws of the then Indian States were in force on the 26th January, 1950 and the object of Article 13(1) was partly to save those laws also.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is no substance in this contention.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is in the first place a proposition of doubtful authority that the  Indian States had a Constitution properly so-called.
Precedent	But even assuming that such Constitutions were at one time in force, they would cease to be in operation as Constitutional Laws on the integration of the States with <ORG>.
Precedent	Article 13(1) therefore does not include any Constitutional laws.
Precedent	2164.
Precedent	Article 13(3)(a) contains an inclusive definition of 'law' as including any Ordinance, order bye-law, rule regulation, notification, custom or usage having in the territory of India the force of law.
Precedent	It is surprising that the necessity to include amendments of the Constitution within the inclusive definition of 'law' should have been overlooked if indeed Article 13(2) was intended to take in Constitutional amendments.
Precedent	There is high and consistent authority for the view that Constitution is the fundamental or basic law, and that it is a law of superior obligation to which the ordinary law must conform.
Precedent	(Corpus Juris Secundum, Vol.
Precedent	16, pp.
Precedent	22-25; Weaver-Constitutional Law and its Administration (1946) p. 3 ; Burgess-Political Science and Constitutional Law, Vol.
Precedent	1, pp.
Precedent	145-146).
Precedent	Unless, therefore, Constitutional law was expressly included in Article 13(3)(a ), it would fall outside the purview of Article 13(2).
Precedent	2165.
RatioOfTheDecision	In America, there is a large volume of authority that the legislatures of the various <ORG>, in initiating Constitutional amendments do not exercise ordinary legislative power.
RatioOfTheDecision	This distinction is brought out clearly by saying that in relation to the federal Constitution of America, a State Constitutional provision or amendment is 'law' within the meaning of the federal Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Again, when under Article V of the Constitution the <ORG> makes a proposal for amendment and the <ORG> ratify it, neither the <ORG> nor the <ORG> are legislating.
Precedent	(Corpus Juris Secundum, Vol.
Precedent	16, pp.
Precedent	48, 49; <NAME>-The Law of the American Constitution, pp.
Precedent	40-42).
Precedent	2166.
Precedent	The fundamental distinction between Constitutional law and ordinary law lies in the criterion of validity.
Precedent	In the case of Consti tutional law, its validity is inherent whereas in the case of an ordinary law its validity has to be decided on the touchstone of the <ORG>, With great respect, the majority view in <NAME> case, did not on the construction of Article 13(2 ), accord due importance to this essential distinction between legislative power and the constituent power.
Precedent	In a controlled <ORG> like ours, ordinary powers of legislatures do not include the power to amend the <ORG> because the Body which enacts and amends the <ORG> functions in its capacity as <ORG>.
Precedent	The <ORG> performing its functions under Article 368 discharges those functions not as a <ORG> but in a constituent capacity.
Precedent	2167.
Precedent	There is a fundamental distinction between the procedure for passing ordinary laws and the procedure prescribed by Article 368 for affecting amendments to the <ORG>.
Precedent	Under Article 368 , a bill has to be initiated for the express purpose of amending the <ORG>, it has to be p assed by each <ORG> by not less than two-thirds members present and voting and in cases falling under the proviso, the amendment has to be ratified by the legislatures of not less than half the States.
Precedent	A bill initiating an ordinary law can be passed by a simple majority of the members present and voting at the sitting of each <ORG> or at a joint sitting of the two <ORG>s.
Precedent	Article 368 does not provide for a joint sitting of the two <ORG>s.
Precedent	The process of ratification by the States under the Proviso cannot possibly be called an ordinary legislative process for, the ratification is required to be made by "resolutions" to that effect.
Precedent	Ordinary bills are not passed by resolutions.
Precedent	2168.
Precedent	The distinction between constituent power and ordinary legislative power can best be appreciated in the context of the nature of the Constitution which the court has to interpret in regard to the amending power.
Precedent	In <NAME> v. The King  A.C. 691, Lord <NAME> used the words 'controlled' and 'uncontrolled' for bringing about the  same distinction which was made between 'rigid' and 'flexible' Constitution first by <NAME> and then by <NAME>.
Precedent	In a 'controlled' or 'rigid' Constitution, a different procedure is prescribed for amendming the Constitution than the procedure prescribed for making ordinary laws.
Precedent	2169.
Precedent	In an 'uncontrolled' or 'flexible' Constitution the procedure for amending the Constitution is same as that for making ordinary laws.
Precedent	In such a Constitution, the distinction between Constitutional laws and ordinary laws tends to become blurred because any law repugnant to the Constitution repeals the Constitution pro tanto [<NAME> v. The King [1920] A.C. 691].
Precedent	2170.
Precedent	Thus, the true ground of division, by virtue of the nature of the Constitution, is whether it is flexible or rigid.
Precedent	That depends upon whether the process of Constitutional law-making is or is not identi cal with the process of ordinary law-making.
Precedent	A typical instance of a flexible Constitution is that of the United Kingdom.
Precedent	The Constitution of the former Kingdom of It aly was also flexible, so flexible indeed, that <NAME> was able profundly to violate the spirit of the Constitution without having to denounce it.
Precedent	The Constitution of the United States is rigid, as it cannot be amended without the special machinery being set in motion for that purpose.
Precedent	"In short, then, we may say that the Constitution which cannot be bent without being broken is a rigid Constitution."
Precedent	(See Modern Political Constitutions : an Introduction to the Comparative Study of Their History and Existing Form by <NAME>, 1970 Reprint).
Precedent	The Indian Constitution, considered as a whole is a 'controlled' or 'rigid' Constitution, because, broadly, none of the articles of that Constitution can be amended otherwise than by the special procedure prescribed by Article 368.
Precedent	Certain provisions thereof like Article 4 read with Articles 2 and 3, Article 169 , para 7 of the Fifth Schedule and para 21 of the Sixth Schedule confer power to amend the provisions of the Constitution by the ordinary law-making process  but these amendments are expressly excepted by the respective provisions from the purview of Article 368.
Precedent	Schedules V and VI of the Constitution are in fact a Constitution within a Constitution.
Precedent	2171.
Precedent	The distinction between 'flexible' and 'rigid' Constitutions brings into sharp focus the true distinction between legislative and constituent power.
Precedent	This is the distinction which, with respect, was not given its due importance by the majority in the <NAME> case.
Precedent	In a rigid Constitution, the power to make laws is the genus, of which the legislative and constituent powers are species, the differentia being the procedure for amendment.
Precedent	If the procedure is ordinary, the power is legislative; if it is special, the power is constituent.
Precedent	2172.
Precedent	This discussion will show that in a rigid' or 'uncontrolled' Constitution-like ours-a law amending the Constitution is made in exercise of a constituent power and partakes fully of the character of Constitutional law.
Precedent	Laws passed under the Constitution, of which the validity  is to be tested on the anvil of the Constitution are the only laws which fall within the terms of Article 13(2).
Precedent	2173.
Precedent	The importance of this discussion consists in the injunction contained in Article 13(2) that the <ORG> shall not make any 'law' which takes away or abridges the rights conferred by Part III.
Precedent	An Amendment of Constitution within the terms of Article 368 not being law within the meaning of Article 13(2 ), it cannot become void on the ground that it takes away or abridges the rights conferred by Part III.
Precedent	2174.
Precedent	Fundamental Rights undoubtedly occupy a unique place in civilized societies, whether you call them "transcendental", "inalienable", or as <NAME> called them, "Primordial".
Precedent	There is no magic in these words for, the strength and importance of these rights is implicit in their very description in the Constitution as "fundamental".
Precedent	But the special place of importance which they occupy in the scheme of the Constitution, cannot by itself justify the conclusion that they are beyond the reach  of the amending power.
Precedent	Article 13(2) clearly does not take in the amending power and Article 368 does not except the Fundamental Rights from its scope.
Precedent	2175.
Precedent	But they cannot be tinkered with and the Constitution has taken care to ensure that they do not become a mere 'plaything' of a special majority.
Precedent	Members of <ORG> are elected on adult universal suffrage by people of the States.
Precedent	Whereas, ordinary laws can be passed by a bare majority of those present, Constitutional amendments are required to be passed under Article 368 by a majority of the total membership of each <ORG> and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members of each <ORG> separately present and voting.
Precedent	In matters falling within the proviso, amendments are also required to be ratified by the Legislatures of not less than half of the States.
Precedent	<ORG>, unlike <ORG>, is a perpetual body, which changes one-third of its membership every two years.
Precedent	Members of the <ORG> are elected by <ORG> tates, that is, by those who are directly elected by the people themselves.
Precedent	The mode of election to <ORG> constitutes to some extent an insurance against gusts and waves of public opinion.
Precedent	2176.
Precedent	I will now proceed to consider an important branch of the petitioner's argument which, frankly, seemed to me at first sight plausible.
Precedent	On closer scrutiny, however, I am inclined to reject the argument.
Precedent	It is urged by the learned Counsel that it is immaterial whether the amending power can be found in Article 368 or in Entry 97 of List I, because wherever that power lies, its exercise is subject to inherent and implied limitations.
Precedent	2177.
Precedent	The argument takes this form : Constitutions must of necessity be general rather than detailed and prolix, and implication must therefore play an important part in Constitutional construction.
Precedent	Implied limitations are those which are implicit in the scheme of the Constitution while inherent limitations are those which inhere in an authority from its very nature, character and com position.
Precedent	Implied limitations arise from the circumstances and historical events which led to the enactment of our Constitution, which represents the solemn balance of rights between citizens from various States of India and between various sections of the people.
Precedent	Most of the essential features of the Constitution are basic Human Rights, sometimes described as "Natural Rights", which correspond to the rights enumerated in the "Universal Declaration of Human Rights", to which India is a signatory.
Precedent	The ultimate sovereignty resides in the people and the power to alter or destory the essential features of a Constitution is an attribute of that sovereignty.
Precedent	In Article 368 , the people are not associated at all with the amending process.
Precedent	The Constitution gives the power of amendment to the <ORG> which is only a creature of the Constitution.
Precedent	If the <ORG> has the power to destroy the essential features it would cease to be a creature of the Constitution, the Constitution would cease to be supreme and the Pa <ORG> would become supreme over the Constitution.
Precedent	The power given by the Constitution cannot be construed as authorising the destruction of other powers conferred by the same instrument.
Precedent	If there are no inherent limitations on the amending power of the Pa<ORG>, that power could be used to destroy the judicial power, the executive power and even the ordinary legislative power of the Pa<ORG> and the State legislatures.
Precedent	The Preamble to our Constitution which is most meaningful and evocative, is beyond the reach of the amending power and therefore no amendments can be introduced into the Constitution which are inconsistent with the Preamble.
Precedent	The Preamble walks before the Constitution and is its identity card.
Precedent	2178.
Precedent	Counsel has made an alternative submission that assuming for purposes of argument that the power of amendment is wide enough to reach the Fundamental Rights, it cannot be exercised so as to damage the core of those rights or so as to damage or destory the essential features and the fundamental  principles of the Constitution.
Precedent	Counsel finally urges that the history of implied and inherent limitations has been accepted by the highest courts of countries like U.S.A., Canada, Australia and Ireland.
Precedent	The theory is also said to have been recognised by this <ORG>, the Federal <ORG> and <ORG>.
Precedent	2179.
Precedent	In answer to these contentions, it was urged on behalf of the respondents that there is no scope for reading implied or inherent limitations on the amending power, that great uncertainty would arise in regard to the validity of Constitutional amendment if such limitations were read on the amending power, that the Preamble is a part of the Constitution and can be amended by <ORG>, that there is in our Constitution no recognition of basic human or natural rights and that the consensus of world opinion is against the recognition of inherent limitations on the amending power.
RatioOfTheDecision	2180.
RatioOfTheDecision	Before dealing with these rival contentions, I may indicate how the argument of inherent limitations was dealt with in  the <NAME> case.
Precedent	<NAME> who delivered the leading majority judgment said that there was considerable force in the argument but it was unnecessary to decide it (p. 805).
Precedent	According to <NAME> "the whole Constitution is open to amendment.
Precedent	Only two dozen articles are outside the reach of Article 368.
Precedent	That too because the Constitution has made them fundamental."
Precedent	(p. 878).
Precedent	<NAME> who delivered the leading minority judgment rejected the argument by observing : "The power to amend being a constituent power cannot in our opinion...be held subject to any implied limitations thereon on the ground that certain basic features of the Constitution cannot be amended."
Precedent	(p. 836).
Precedent	<NAME> observed that it was unnecessary to decide the question, as it was sufficient for the disposal of the case to say that Fundamental Rights were within the reach of the amending power (p. 906).
Precedent	<NAME> considered and rejected the argument by observing that there was no room for an implication in the construction  of Article 368 and it was unlikely that if certain basic features were intended to be unamendable, the Constitution makers would not have expressly said so in Article 368 .
Precedent	2181.
Precedent	It is difficult to accept the argument that inherent limitations should be read into the amending power on the ground that Fundamental Rights are natural rights which inhere in every man.
Precedent	There is instrinsic evidence in Part III of the Constitution to show that the theory of natural rights was not recognised by our Constitution-makers.
Precedent	Article 13(2) speaks of rights "conferred" by Part III and enjoins the States not to make laws inconsistent therewith.
Precedent	Article 32 of the Constitution says that the right to move the Supreme Court for the enforcement of rights 'conferred' by Part III is guaranteed.
Precedent	Before the Fundamental Rights were thus conferred by the Constitution, there is no tangible evidence that these rights belonged to the Indian people.
Precedent	Article 19 of the Constitution restricts the grant of the seven freedoms to the citizens o f India.
Precedent	Non-citizens were denied those rights because the conferment of some of the rights on the Indian citizens was not in recognition of the pre-existing natural rights.
Precedent	Article 33 confers upon the <ORG> the power to determine to what extent the rights conferred by Part III should be restricted or abrogated in their application to the members of <ORG>.
Precedent	Article 359(1) empowers the President to suspend the rights "conferred" by Part III during the proclamation of an emergency.
Precedent	Articles 25 and 26 by their opening words show that the right to freedom of religion is not a natural right but is subject to the paramount interest of society and that there is no part of that right, however important, which cannot and in many cases has not been regulated in civilised societies.
Precedent	Denial to a section of the community, the right of entry to a place of worship, may be a part of religion but such denials, it is well-known, have been abrogated by the Constitution.
Precedent	(1958 S.C.R.
Precedent	895 at 919, per <NAME> <NAME>; <NAME> and Ors.
Precedent	v. The <ORG> of Mysore and Ors.
Precedent	see also <NAME> v. <NAME> 1919 A.C. 815 at 861 per Lord <NAME>-head L.C.).
Precedent	Thus, in India, citizens and non-citizens possess and are entitled to exercise certain rights of high significance for the sole reason that they are conferred upon them by the Constitution.
Precedent	2182 The 'natural right' theory stands, by and large repudiated today.
Precedent	The notion that societies and governments find their sanction on a supposed contract between independent individuals and that such a contract is the sole source of political obligation is now regarded as untenable.
Precedent	<NAME> and his followers have discarded this doctrine, while theorists like <NAME> have modified it extensively.
Precedent	The belief is now widely held that natural rights have no other than political value.
Precedent	According to <NAME>, "there never was, and there never can be any liberty upon this earth among human beings, outside of <ORG> organisation."
Precedent	According to <NAME>, natural rights do not even have  a moral value in the supposed "state of nature"; they would really be equivalent to force and hence have no political significance.
Precedent	Thus, Natural Right thinkers had once "discovered the lost title- deeds of the human race" but it would appear that the deeds are lost once over again, perhaps never to be resurrected.
Precedent	2183.
Precedent	The argument in regard to the Preamble is that it may be a part of the Constitution but is not a provision of the Constitution and therefore, you cannot amend the Constitution so as to destroy the Preamble.
Precedent	The Preamble records like a sun-beam certain glowing thoughts and concepts of history and the argument is that in its very nature it is unamendable because no present or future, however mighty, can assume the power to amend the true facts of past history.
Precedent	Counsel relies for a part of this submission on the decision in Beru Ban case [1960] 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	250, 282.
Precedent	Our attention was also drawn to certain passages from the chapter on "preamble" in "commentaries on the Constitution of the United St ates" by <NAME>.
Precedent	2184.
Precedent	I find it impossible to accept the contention that the Preamble is not a provision of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The record of <ORG> leaves no scope for this contention.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is transparent from the proceedings that the Preamble was put to vote and was actually voted upon to form a part of the Constitution.
Precedent	(Constituent Assembly Debates, Vol.
Precedent	X, pp.
Precedent	429, 456).
Precedent	As a part and provision of the Constitution, the Preamble came into force on January 26, 1950.
Precedent	The view is widely accepted that the Preamble is a part of the enactment (Craies on Statute Law, 7th Ed., p. 201; <ORG>, Vol.
Precedent	36, 3rd.
Precedent	Ed., p. 370).
RatioOfTheDecision	2185.
RatioOfTheDecision	In considering the petitioner's argument on inherent limitations, it is well to bear in mind some of the basic principles of interpretation.
RatioOfTheDecision	Absence of an express prohibition still leaves scope for the argument that there are implied or inherent limitations on a power, but absence of an express prohibition is highly relevant for inferring that there is no implied pro hibition.
RatioOfTheDecision	This is clear from the decision of <ORG> in The Queen v. <NAME> 5 I.A.
Precedent	178, 195.
Precedent	This decision was followed by this Court in State of Bombay v. <NAME> 1951 (2) S.C.R.
Precedent	51, 81 and in <CITATION_1519712> 1957 S.C.R.
Precedent	605, 616-17.
Precedent	In saying this, I am not unmindful of the fact that <NAME>'s case and the two cases which followed it, bear primarily on conditional legislation.
Precedent	2186.
Precedent	Another principle of interpretation is that it is not open to the courts to declare an Act void on the ground that it is opposed to a 'spirit' supposed to pervade the Constitution but not manifested in words.
Precedent	As observed by <NAME> in <NAME>'s case 1950 S.C.R.
Precedent	88, 121, a wide assumption of power to construction is apt to place in the hands of judiciary too great and to indefinite a power, either for its own security or the protection of private rights.
Precedent	The argument of 'spirit' is always attractive and quite some eloquence can be infused into it.
Precedent	But one should remember what <NAME> said in <NAME> <NAME> case 1951 S.C.R.
Precedent	228, 231 that one must gather the spirit from the words or the language used in the Constitution.
Precedent	I have held that the language of Article 368 is clear and explicit.
Precedent	In that view, it must be given its full effect even if mischievous consequences are likely to ensue; for, judges are not concerned with the policy of law- making and "you cannot pass a covert censure against the legislature."
Precedent	(<ORG> v. <ORG>) 1913 (A.C.) 107 at 112, 117, 121.
RatioOfTheDecision	The importance of the circumstance that the language of Article 368 admits of no doubt or ambiguity is that such a language leaves no scope for implications, unless in the context of the entire instrument in which it occurs, such implications become compulsive.
RatioOfTheDecision	I am tempted to say that 'context' does not merely mean the position of a word to be construed, in the collocation of words in which it appears, but it also means the context of the times in which a fundamental instrument falls to be construed .
RatioOfTheDecision	2187.
RatioOfTheDecision	An important rule of interpretation which, I think, has a direct bearing on the submissions of the petitioner on inherent limitations is that if the text is explicit, it is conclusive alike in what it directs and what it forbids.
RatioOfTheDecision	The consequences of a particular construction, if the text be explicit, can have no impact on the construction of a Constitutional provision (Attorney-General, Ontario v. Attorney-General, Canada) [1892] A.C. 571.
RatioOfTheDecision	As observed by Chief Justice <NAME> in <ORG> v. <NAME> L. ed.
RatioOfTheDecision	939, 957 a power may be capable of being abused but the Constitution is not intended to furnish a corrective for every abuse of power which may be committed by the government I see no warrant for the assumption that the <ORG> will be disposed to out a perverse construction on the powers plainly conferred on it by the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	And talking of abuse of powers, is there not the widest scope for doing so under several provisions of the Constitution ?
RatioOfTheDecision	The powers of war and peace , the powers of finance and the powers of preventive detention, are capable of the widest abuse and yet the Founding Fathers did confer those powers on the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	When I look at a provision like the one contained in Article 22 of the Constitution, I feel a revolt rising within myself, but then personal predilections are out of place in the construction of a Constitutional provision.
RatioOfTheDecision	Clause (7) of Article 22 permits the <ORG> to enact a law under which a person may be detained for a period longer than three months without obtaining the opinion of <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	While enacting certain laws of Preventive Detention, the Government has shown some grace in specifying the outer limits, however, uncertain, of the period of detention though, so it seems, it is under no obligation to do so.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thus, even when the original Constitution was passed, powers capable of the gravest abuse were conferred on the <ORG>, which as the petitioner's counsel says, is but a creature of the Constitution.
Precedent	In assessing th e argument that the gravity of consequences is relevant on the interpretation of a Constitutional provision, I am reminded of the powerful dissent of Justice <NAME> in <NAME> v. New York 49 L. ed.
Precedent	937 regarding a labour statute.
Precedent	The test according to the learned Judge was not whether he considered the law to be reasonable but whether other reasonable persons considered it unreasonable.
Precedent	In <ORG> v. <NAME>  A.C. 575, 586 Lord <NAME> observed: "People who are trusted with the great power of making laws for property and civil rights may well be trusted to levy taxes."
Precedent	Trust in the elected representatives is the corner stone of a democracy.
Precedent	When that trust fails, everything fails.
Precedent	As observed by Justice <NAME> in "the spirit of liberty" : "I often wonder, whether we do not rest our hopes too much upon Constitution, upon laws and upon courts.
Precedent	These are false hopes, believe me, these are false hopes.
Precedent	Liberty lies in the hearts of men and women; when it dies there, no Constitution, no law, no court c an save it; no Constitution, no law, no court can even do much to help it.
Precedent	While it lies there it needs no Constitution, no law, no court to save.
Precedent	2188.
Precedent	Established text books on Interpretation also take the view that "where the language of an Act is clear and explicit, we must give effect to it, whatever may be the consequences, for in that case the words of the statute speak the intention of the legislature <NAME> on "Statute Law", 6th Ed., p. 66.
Precedent	2189.
Precedent	It is thus clear that part from Constitutional limitations, no law can be struck down on the ground that it is unreasonable or unjust.
Precedent	That is the view which was taken by this <ORG> in the <CITATION_49043> 1952 S.C.R.
Precedent	889, 936, 937.
Precedent	<NAME> Described the Bihar Land Reforms Act, which was under consideration in that case, as repugnant to the sense of justice of the court.
Precedent	In fact, the learned Judge says in his judgment that it was not seriously disputed by the Attorney-General, that the law was highly unjust and inequitous and the compensation provided t herein in some cases was purely illusory.
Precedent	The <ORG>, however, found itself powerless to rectify an "unjustice" perpetrated by the Constitution itself.
Precedent	No provision incorporated in a Constitution at the time of its original enactment can ever be struck down as unConstitutional.
Precedent	The same test must apply to what becomes a part of that Constitution by a subsequent amendment, provided that the conditions on which alone such amendments can be made are strictly complied with.
Precedent	Amendments, in this sense, pulpate with the vitality of the Constitution itself.
Precedent	2190.
Precedent	The true justification of this principle is, as stated by <NAME> in the <CITATION_1560414> .,  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	896, 899 that a construction which will introduce uncertainty into the law must be avoided.
Precedent	It is conceded by the petitioner that the power to amend the Constitution is a necessary attribute of every Constitution.
Precedent	In fact, amendments which were made by the Constitution (First Amendment) Act, 1951 to Articles 15 and 19 were never assailed and have bee n conceded before us to have been properly made.
Precedent	It was urged by the learned Counsel that the substitution of new Clause (2) in Article 19 did not abrogate the Fundamental Rights, but on the other hand enabled the citizens at large to enjoy their fundamental freedoms more fully.
Precedent	This, I think, is the crux of the matter.
Precedent	What counsel concedes in regard to Article 19(2) as substituted by the First Amendment Act can be said to be equally true in regard to the amendments now under challenge.
Precedent	Their true object and purpose is to confer upon the community at large the blessings of liberty.
Precedent	The argument is that the <ORG> may amend the provisions of Part III, but not so as to damage or destroy the core of those rights or the core of the essential principles of the Constitution.
Precedent	I see formidable difficulties in evolving an objective standard to determine what would constitute the core and what the peripheral layer of the essential principles of the Constitution.
Precedent	I consider the two to be inseparable.
Precedent	2191.
Precedent	Counsel  painted a lurid picture of the consequences which will ensue if a wide and untrammelled power is conceded to the <ORG> to amend the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	These consequences do not scare me.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is true that our confidence in the men of our choice cannot completely silence our fears for the safety of our rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	But in a democratic policy, people have the right to decide what they want and they can only express their will through their elected representatives in the hope and belief that the trust will not be abused.
RatioOfTheDecision	Trustees are not unknown to have committed breaches of trust but no one for that reason has abolished the institution of Trusts.
RatioOfTheDecision	Can we adopt a presidential system of government in place of the parliamentary system?
RatioOfTheDecision	Can we become a monarchial or theocratic State ?
RatioOfTheDecision	Shall we permit the <ORG> to first destroy the essential features of the Constitution and then amend the amending power itself so to as provide that in future no amendment shall be made except by a 99 per cent majority?
RatioOfTheDecision	Can the Parliame nt extend its term from 5 to 50 years and create a legislative monopoly in its favour ?
RatioOfTheDecision	These are the questions which counsel has asked.
RatioOfTheDecision	My answer is simple.
RatioOfTheDecision	History records that in times of stress, such extreme steps have been taken both by the people and by the <ORG>.
Precedent	In 1640, when England was invaded by Scots, <NAME> the I was obliged to recall <ORG> to raise money for the war.
Precedent	The 'Short' <ORG> insisted on airing its grievances before voting the money and was dismissed.
Precedent	<NAME> had to summon a new <ORG> immediately, and this 'Long' <ORG> lasting until 1660, set out to make personal government by a monarch impossible.
Precedent	The true sanction against such political crimes lies in the hearts and minds of men.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is there that the liberty is insured.
RatioOfTheDecision	I therefore say to myself not in a mood of desperation, not in a mood of helplessness, not cynically but in the true spirit of a democrat: If the people acting through the <ORG> want to put the Crown of a King on a head they like, or if y ou please, on a head they dislike, (for uneasy lies the head that wears a Crown), let them have that liberty.
RatioOfTheDecision	If and when they realise the disaster brought by them upon themselves, they will snatch the Crown and scatter its jewels to the winds.
RatioOfTheDecision	As I say this, I am reminded of a famous saying of Justice <NAME>: "About seventy-five years ago, I learnt that I was not God.
RatioOfTheDecision	And so, when the people...want to do something I can't find anything in the Constitution expressly forbidding them to do, I say, whether I like it or not : 'God-dammit, let 'em do it 2192.
RatioOfTheDecision	No name is mentioned with greater honour in the history of American democracy than that of <NAME>.
RatioOfTheDecision	He was the central figure in the early development of American democracy, and on his death he was politically canonized.
Precedent	<NAME> said in regard to the necessity of a wide amending power that "The earth belongs in usufruct to the living; the dead have neither powers nor rights over it."
Precedent	"If one generation could hind another, the dead and not the livin g would rule.
Precedent	Since conditions change and men change, there must be opportunity for corresponding change in political institutions, and also for a renewal of the principle of government by consent of the governed."
Precedent	According to President <NAME>, "a Constitution must of necessity be a vehicle of life; that its substance is the thought and habit of the nation and as such it must grow and develop as the life of the nation changes.
Precedent	2193.
Precedent	In support of his argument on implied limitations, learned Counsel for the petitioner drew our attention to certain decisions on the theory of immunity of instrumentalities : The means and instrumentalities of the State Governments should be left free and unimpaired.
Precedent	Our <ORG> rejected this theory in <CITATION_603736>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	394, 407.
Precedent	<NAME> observed that the argument presented before the <ORG> was : "a resucitation of the new exploded doctrine of the immunity of instrumentalities which originating from the observations of <NAME> in <NAME> v. Maryland has been  decisively rejected by <ORG>...and has been practically given up even in the United States."
Precedent	The doctrine originally arose out of supposed existence of an implied prohibition that the Federal and State Governments being sovereign and independent must each be free from the control of the other.
Precedent	Dr. <NAME> observes in his book : "Legislative, Executive and Judicial Powers in Australia (4th Edition)" that the doctrine has undergone considerable change in the United States and its progressive retreat is traced by <NAME> in the <ORG> case  74 C.L.R.
