Precedent	As it is often said, a Constitution is a living organism and there can be no doubt that a Constitution is evolved to suit the history and genius of t he nation.
Precedent	Therefore, I will only make a brief reference to a few important decisions.
Precedent	2205.
Precedent	<NAME>'s [1935] Irish Reports 170 case created a near sensation and was thought to cover the important points arising before us.
Precedent	<ORG> upheld the amendment made by the <ORG>, by deleting Article 47 of the Constitution which contained the provision for referendum, and which also incorporated an amendment in Article 50.
Precedent	This latter article conferred power on the <ORG> to make amendments to the Constitution within the terms of the Scheduled Treaty.
Precedent	An amendment made after the expiration of a period of 8 years from the promulgation of the Constitution was required to be submitted to a referendum of the people.
Precedent	The period of 8 years was enlarged by the amendment into 16 years.
Precedent	<ORG> upheld the amendment and so did <ORG>, by a majority of 2 to 1.
Precedent	<NAME> delivered a dissenting judgment striking down the amendment on the ground that there were implied limitati ons on the power of amendment An important point of distinction between our Constitution and the Irish Constitution is that whereas Article 50 did not contain any power to amend that article itself, Article 368 of our Constitution confers an express power by Clause (e) of the Proviso to amend that article.
Precedent	The reasoning of the learned Chief Justice therefore loses relevance in the present case.
Precedent	I might mention that in <NAME> v. Attorney General for the Irish State  A.C. 484 in which a Constitutional amendment made in 1933 was challenged, it was conceded before <ORG> that the amendment which was under fire in <NAME>'s case was validly made.
Precedent	<ORG> added to the concession the weight of its own opinion by saying that the concession was made 'rightly'.
Precedent	2206.
Precedent	Several Australian decisions were relied upon by the petitioner but I will refer to the one which was cited by the petitioner's counsel during the course of his reply; <NAME> v. Attorney General of Queens-land 23 C.L.R.
Precedent	457.
Precedent	The observatio ns of Isaacs J. on which the learned Counsel relies seem to me to have been made in the context of the provisions of the Colonial Laws Validity Act.
Precedent	The real meaning of those observations is that when power is granted to a colonial legislature to alter the Constitution, it must be assumed that the power did no comprehend the right to eliminate the <ORG> as a part of the colonial legislature.
Precedent	It may be mentioned that well-known Constitutional writers <NAME> K.C.
Precedent	: "The Safety Valve of the Commonwealth Constitution", <ORG>, Vol.
Precedent	12, (1938-39), p. 108 at 109; <NAME> : "Legislative, Executive and Judicial Powers in Australia", 4th Edn., Chapter XVII, p. 507 have expressed the view that all the provisions of the Australian Constitution, including Article 128 itself which confers power to amend the Constitution, are within the power of amendment.
Precedent	This view has been taken even though Article 128 does not confer express power to amend that article itself.
Precedent	2207.
Precedent	While winding up this  discussion of authorities, it is necessary to refer to the decision of <ORG> in <NAME> v. the Queen (1967) 1 A.C. 259 in which it was held that the powers of the Ceylon legislature could not be cut down by reference to vague and uncertain expressions like 'fundamental principles of British law'.
Precedent	2208.
Precedent	It must follow from what precedes that The Constitution (Twenty-fourth Amendment) Act, 1971 is valid.
Precedent	I have taken the view that Constitutional amendments made under Article 368 fell outside the purview of Article 13(2).
Precedent	Section 2 of the 24th Amendment Act reiterates this position by adding a new Clause (4) in Article 13 : "(4) Nothing in this article shall apply to any amendment of this Constitution made under Article 368."
Precedent	I have also taken the view that the old Article 368 not only prescribed the procedure for amendment of the Constitution but conferred the power of amendment.
Precedent	That position is made clear by Section 3 of the 24th Amendment which substitutes by Clause (a) a fully expressive margin al heading to Article 368.
Precedent	I have held that the power of amendment conferred by Article 368 was wide and untrammelled.
Precedent	Further, that Constitutional amendments are made in the exercise of constituent power and not in the exercise of ordinary law-making power.
Precedent	That position is reiterated by Clause (b) of Section 3 .
Precedent	Clause (c) of Section 3 makes it obligatory for the President to give his assent to the bill for a Constitutional amendment.
Precedent	Rightly no arguments have been addressed on this innovation.
Precedent	Finally, Clause (d) of Section 3 of the 24th Amendment excludes the application of Article 13 to an amendment made under Article 368.
Precedent	As indicated in this judgment that was the correct interpretation of Articles 13 and 368.
Precedent	2209.
Precedent	The Constitution (Twenty-fourth Amendment) Act, 1971, thus, merely clarifies what was the true law and must therefore be held valid.
Precedent	The Twenty-Fifth Amendment 2210.
Precedent	The Constitution (Twenty-Fifth Amendment) Act, 1971, which came into force on April 20, 1972 consists of two effective sectio ns : Sections 2 and 3 .
Precedent	Section 2(a) substitutes a new Clause (2) for the original Clause (2) of Article 31 of the Constitution.
Precedent	Under the original Article 31(2 ), no property could be acquired for a public purpose under any law unless it provided for compensation for the property taken possession of or acquired and either fixed the amount of the compensation, or specified the principles on which, and the manner in which, the compensation was to be determined and given.
Precedent	<CITATION_1890860>  S.C.R.
Precedent	558, a unanimous <ORG> presided over by <NAME> held that the principles of compensation must ensure the payment of a just equivalent of what the owner was deprived of.
Precedent	The Constitution (Fourth Amendment) Act was passed on April 27, 1955 in order to meet that decision.
Precedent	By the Fourth Amendment, an addition was made to Article 31(2) providing that "...no such law shall be called in question in any court on the ground that the compensation provided by the law is not adequate."
Precedent	The effect of the amendment w as considered by this <ORG> in <CITATION_1634289>  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	614.
Precedent	<ORG> had passed an Act providing for the acquisition of lands for housing schemes and had laid down principles for fixing compensation different from those prescribed in the Land Acquisition Act , 1894.
RulingByLowerCourt	Delivering the judgment of the <ORG>, <NAME> held that the fact that <ORG> used the same expressions, 'compensation' and 'principles' as were found in Article 31 before its Amendment, was a clear indication that it accepted the meaning given by this <ORG> to those expressions in <NAME> case.
RulingByLowerCourt	The <ORG>, therefore, had to provide for a just equivalent of what the owner was deprived of or specify the principles for the purpose of ascertaining the just equivalent.
RulingByLowerCourt	The new clause added by the Fourth Amendment, excluding the jurisdiction of the <ORG> to consider the adequacy of compensation, was interpreted to mean that neither the principles prescribing the 'just equivalent' nor the 'just equivalent' could  be questioned by the court on the ground of the inadequacy of the compensation fixed or arrived at by the working of the principles.
RulingByLowerCourt	By applying this test, the Court upheld the principles of compensation fixed under the Madras Act as not contravening Article 31(2).
RulingByLowerCourt	The Act , however, was struck down under Article 14 on the ground that full compensation had still to be paid under a parallel Law : The Land Acquisition Act .
Precedent	2211.
Precedent	<CITATION_602096> ,  1 S.C.R.
Precedent	255 a Bench of two Judges consisting of <NAME> and <NAME> held that the law of acquisition in order to justify itself had to provide for the payment of a 'just equivalent' or lay down principles which will lead to that result.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is only if the principles laid down are relevant to the fixation of compensation and are not arbitrary that the adequacy of the resultant product could not be questioned in a court of law.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is evident that this decision marked a departure from the judgment in <NAME>'s case.
RatioOfTheDecision	2212.
RatioOfTheDecision	<CITATION_673450>  3 S.C.R.
RulingByLowerCourt	341 S <NAME> speaking for himself and three other learned Judges expressed his disagreement with the observations of <NAME> in <ORG> case and expressly over-ruled that decision.
RulingByLowerCourt	It was held that if the quantum of compensation was not liable to be challenged on the ground that it was not a just equivalent, the principles specified for determination of compensation could also not be challenged on the plea that the compensation determined by the application of those principles was not a just equivalent.
RulingByLowerCourt	The learned Judge observed that this did not, however, mean that something fixed or determined by the application of specified principles which is illusory or can in no sense be regarded as compensation must be upheld by the <ORG>, for, to do so, would be to grant a charter of arbitraries, and permit a device to defeat the Constitutional guarantee.
RulingByLowerCourt	Principles could, therefore, be challenged on the ground that they were irrelevant to the determination of compensation, but not on the ground tha t what was awarded as a result of the application of those principles was not just or fair compensation.
RulingByLowerCourt	2213.
RulingByLowerCourt	<CITATION_513801>  3 S.C.R.
RulingByLowerCourt	530, (the Bank Nationalisation case), the judgment in <NAME> case, was in substance overruled by <ORG> Judges by a majority of 10 to 1.
RulingByLowerCourt	The majority referred to the meaning of compensation as an equivalent of the property expropriated.
RulingByLowerCourt	It was held that if the statute in providing for compensation devised a scheme for payment of compensation in the form of bonds and the present value of what was determined to be given was thereby substantially reduced, the statute impired the guarantee of compensation.
RulingByLowerCourt	2214.
RulingByLowerCourt	This chain of decisions on the construction of Articles 31(2) introduced uncertainty in law and defeated to a large extent the clearly expressed intention of the amended Article 31(2) that a law providing for compensation shall not be called in question in any court on the ground that the compensation provided by it was not adequate.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> in S <NAME>  S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	341 at 362, 363 case had observed with reference to the decision in <NAME> case and <NAME>  S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	587 case that those decisions had raised more problems than they solved and that they placed serious obstacles in giving effect to the Directive Principles of State Policy incorporated in Article 39.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME> had also observed in <NAME>'s  1 S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	614, 626 case that if the intention of the <ORG> was to enable the legislature to make a law without providing for compensation it would have used other expressions like, 'price', 'consideration', etc.
RatioOfTheDecision	This is what the <ORG> has now done partially by substituting the word 'amount' for the word 'compensation' in the new Article 31(2).
RatioOfTheDecision	2215.
Fact	The provision in the newly added Clause 2B of Article 31 that nothing in Article 19(1)(f) shall affect any law referred to in Article 31(2) has been obviously incorporated because the Bank Nationalisation case overruled a long line of authorities which had consistently tak en the view that Article 19(1)(f) and Article 31(2) were mutually exclusive so far as acquisition and requisition were concerned [See for example <NAME>'s case, 1950 S.C.R.

Fact	949; <NAME> case, 1969 S.C.R.
Fact	341; and <NAME> case, 1969(2) S.C.R.
Precedent	392].
Precedent	2216.
Argument	Learned Counsel appearing for the petitioner mounted a severe attack on the Twenty-Fifth Amendment, particularly on the provisions of Article 31C.
Argument	He contends that Article 31C subverts seven essential features of the Constitution, and destroys ten Fundamental Rights, which are vital for the survival of democracy, the rule of law and integrity and unity of the Republic.
Argument	Seven of these Fundamental Rights, according to the counsel are unconnected with property rights.
Argument	The argument continues that Article 31C destroys the supremacy of the Constitution by giving a blank charter to <ORG> and to all the State Legislatures to defy and ignore the Con stitution; it subordinates the Fundamenal Rights to Directive Principles of State Policy, destroying thereby one of the foundations of the Constitution; it virtually abrogate the "manner and form" of amendment laid down in Article 368 by empowering the State <ORG> and the <ORG> to take away important Fundamental Rights by an ordinary law passed by a simple majority; that it destroys by conclusiveness of the declaration the salient safeguard of judicial review and the right of enforcement of Fundamental Rights; and that, it enables the <ORG>, under the guise of giving effect to the Directive Principles, to take steps calculated to affect the position of religious, regional, linguistic, cultural and other minorities.
Argument	Counsel complaints that the article abrogates not only the most cherished rights to personal liberty and freedom of speech but it also abrogates the right to equality before the law, which is the basic principle of Republicanism.
Argument	By enacting Article 31C , the <ORG> has resor ted to the strange procedure of maintaining the Fundamental Rights unamended, but authorising the enactment of laws which are void as offending those rights, by validating them by a legal fiction that they shall not be deemed to be void.
Argument	Today, Article 31 permits the enactment of laws in abrogation of Articles 14, 19 and 31, but what guarantee is there that tomorrow all the precious freedom will not be excepted from the range of laws passed under that article?
Argument	Learned Counsel wound up his massive criticism against Article 31C by saying that the article is a monstrous outrage on the Constitution and its whole object and purpose is to legalise despotism.
RatioOfTheDecision	2217.
RatioOfTheDecision	Having given a most anxious consideration to these arguments, I have come to the conclusion that though Article 31C is pregnant with possible mischief, it cannot, by the application of any of the well-recognised judicial tests be declared unConstitutional.
RatioOfTheDecision	2218.
RatioOfTheDecision	For a proper understanding of the provisions of Article 31C , one must in the first place appr eciate the full meaning and significance of Article 39(b) and (c) of the Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 39 appears in Part IV of the Constitution, which lays down the Directive Principles of State Policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	The idea of Directive Principles was taken from <ORG>, which in turn had borrowed it from the Constitution of Republican Spain.
RatioOfTheDecision	These preceding examples, as said by Sir <NAME> Some Characteristics of the Indian Constitution, 1953, 30-32, are significant because they came from countries whose peoples are predominantly Roman Catholic, "and the Roman Catholics are provided by their <ORG> not only with a faith but also with a philosophy".
RatioOfTheDecision	On matters of faith and philosophy-social or political-there always is a wide divergence of views and in fact Republican Spain witnessed a war on the heels of the enactment of its Constitution and in <ORG>, <NAME> was openly accused of smuggling into the Constitution the pet policies of his own party.
RatioOfTheDecision	Articles 38 and 39 of our Constitution are principally based on Article 45 of  the Constitution of Eire, which derives its authority from the Papal Bulls.
Precedent	Article 39 provides by Clause (b) that the <ORG> shall, in particular, direct its policy towards securing-"that the ownership and control of the material resources of the community are so distributed as best to subserve the common good".
Precedent	Clause (c) of the article enjoins the <ORG> to direct its policy towards securing-"that the operation of the economic system does not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to common detriment."
Precedent	Article 31C has been introduced by the 25th Amendment in order to achieve the purpose set out in Article 39(b) and (c).
Precedent	2219.
RatioOfTheDecision	I have stated in the earlier part of my judgment] that the Constitution accords a place of pride to Fundamental Rights and a place of permanence to the Directive Principles.
RatioOfTheDecision	I stand by what I have said.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Preamble of our Constitution recites that the aim of the Constitution is to constitute India into a Sovereign Democratic Republic and to secure to "all its  citizens", Justice-social, economic and political-liberty and equality.
RatioOfTheDecision	Fundamental Rights which are conferred and guaranteed by Part III of the Constitution undoubtedly constitute the ark of the Constitution and without them a man's reach will not exceed his grasp.
RatioOfTheDecision	But it cannot be overstressed that, the Directive Principles of State Policy are fundamental in the governance of the country.
RatioOfTheDecision	What is fundamental in the governance of the country cannot surely be less significant than what is fundamental in the life of an individual.
RatioOfTheDecision	That one is justiciable and the other not may show the intrinsic difficulties in making the latter enforceable through legal processes but that distinction does not bear on their relative importance.
RatioOfTheDecision	An equal right of men and women to an adequate means of livelihood; the right to obtain humane conditions of work ensuring a decent standard of life and full enjoyment of leisure; and raising the level of health and nutrition are not matters for compliance with the Writ of a Court.
RatioOfTheDecision	As I  look at the provisions of Parts III and IV, I feel no doubt that the basic object of conferring freedoms on individuals is the ultimate achievement of the ideals sec out in Part IV.
RatioOfTheDecision	A circumspect use of the freedoms guaranteed by Part III is bound to subserve the common good but voluntary submission to restraints is a philosopher's dream.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, Article 37 enjoins the <ORG> to apply the Directive Principles in making laws.
RatioOfTheDecision	The freedom of a few have then to be abridged in order to ensure the freedom of all.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is in this sense that Parts III and IV, as said by <NAME> The Indian Constitution-Cornerstone of a <ORG>, Edn.
Argument	1966, together constitute "the conscience of the Constitution".
Argument	The <ORG> stands to- day at the cross-roads of history and exchanging the time-honoured place of the phrase, may I say that the Directive Principles of <ORG> Policy should not be permitted to become "a mere rope of sand".
Argument	If the <ORG> fails to create conditions in which the Fundamental freedoms could be enjoyed by al l, the freedom of the few will be at the mercy of the many and then all freedoms will vanish.
RatioOfTheDecision	In order, therefore, to preserve their freedom, the privileged few must part with a portion of it.
RatioOfTheDecision	2220.
RatioOfTheDecision	Turning first to the new Article 31(2 ), the substitution of the neutral expression "amount" for "compensation" still binds the <ORG> to give to the owner a sum of money in cash or otherwise.
RatioOfTheDecision	The <ORG> may either lay down principles for the determination of the amount or may itself fix the amount.
RatioOfTheDecision	There is, however, intrinsic evidence in Article 31(2) that it does not empower the <ORG> to confiscate or expropriate property.
Precedent	Not only does Article 31(2) not authorise the legislature to fix "such amount as it deems fit", "in accordance with such principles as it considers relevant", but it enjoins the legislature by express words either to fix an "amount" for being paid to the owner or to lay down "principles" for determining the amount to be paid to him.
Precedent	If it was desired to authorise the legislature to  pass expropriatory laws under Article 31(2 ), nothing would have been easier for <ORG> than to provide that the <ORG> shall have the right to acquire property for a public purpose without payment of any kind or description.
RatioOfTheDecision	The obligation to pay an "amount" does not connote the power not to pay any amount at all.
RatioOfTheDecision	The alternative obligation to evolve principles for determining the amount also shows that there is no choice not to pay.
RatioOfTheDecision	The choice open to the <ORG> is that the amount may directly be fixed by and under the law itself or alternatively, the law may fix principles in accordance with which the amount will be determined.
RatioOfTheDecision	The amount may, of course, be paid in cash or otherwise.
RatioOfTheDecision	2221.
RatioOfTheDecision	The specific obligation to pay an "amount" and in the alternative the use of the word "principles" for determination of that amount must mean that the amount fixed or determined to be paid cannot be illusory.
RatioOfTheDecision	If the right to property still finds a place in the Constitution, you cannot mock at the man  and ridicule his right.
Precedent	You cannot tell him; "I will take your fortune for a farthing".
Precedent	2222.
RatioOfTheDecision	But this is subject to an important, a very important, qualification.
RatioOfTheDecision	The amount fixed for being paid to the owner is wholly beyond the pale of a challenge that it is inadequate.
RatioOfTheDecision	The concept of adequacy is directly co-related to the market value of the property and therefore such value cannot constitute an element of that challenge.
RatioOfTheDecision	By the same test and for similar reasons, the principles evolved for determining the amount cannot be questioned on the ground that by application of those principles the amount determined to be paid is inadequate, in the sense that it bears no reasonable relationship with the market value of the property.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thus the question whether the amount or the principles are within the permissible Constitutional limits must be determined without regard to the consideration whether they bear, a reasonable relationship with the market value of the property.
RatioOfTheDecision	They may not bear a reasonable relationship  and yet they may be valid.
RatioOfTheDecision	But to say that an amount does not bear reasonable relationship with the market value is a different thing from saying that it bears no such relationship at all, none whatsoever.
RatioOfTheDecision	In the latter case the payment becomes illusory and may come within the ambit of permissible challenge.
Precedent	2223.
Precedent	It is unnecessary to pursue this matter further because we are really concerned with the Constitutionality of the Amendment and not with the validity of a law passed under Article 31(2).
Precedent	If and when such a law comes before this Court it may become necessary to consider the matter closely.
Precedent	As at present advised, I am inclined to the view which as I have said is unnecessary to discuss fully, that though it is not open to the court to question a law under Article 31(2) on the ground that the amount fixed or determined is not adequate, courts would have the power to question such a law if the amount fixed thereunder is illusory; if the principles, if any are stated, for determining the amount are whol ly irrelevant for fixation of the amount; if the power of compulsory acquisition or requisition is exercised for a collateral purpose; if the law offends Constitutional safeguards other than the one contained in Article 19(1)(f); or, if the law is in the nature of a fraud on the Constitution.
Precedent	I would only like to add, by way of explanation, that if the fixation of an amount is shown to depend upon principles bearing on social good it may not be possible to say that the principles are irrelevant.
Precedent	2224.
Precedent	As regards the new Article 31(2B) I see no substance in the submission of the petitioner that the exclusion of challenge under Article 19(1)(f) to a law passed under Article 31(2) is bad as being in violation of the principles of natural justice.
Precedent	I have stated earlier that Constitutional amendments partake of the vitality of the Constitution itself, provided they are within the limits imposed by the Constitution.
Precedent	The exclusion of a challenge under Article 19(1)(f) in regard to a law passed under Article 31(2) c annot therefore be deemed unConstitutional.
Precedent	Besides, there is no reason to suppose that the legislature will act so arbitrarily as to authorise the acquisition or requisitioning of property without so much as complying with the rules of natural justice.
Precedent	Social good does not require that a man be condemned unheard.
Precedent	2225.
Precedent	Article 31C presents a gordian knot.
Precedent	King <NAME> of Phrygia had tied a knot which an oracle said would be undone only by the future master of Asia.
Precedent	<NAME>, failing to untie the knot, cut it with his sword.
RatioOfTheDecision	Such a quick and summary solutions of knotty problems is, alas, not open to a Judge.
RatioOfTheDecision	The article reads thus: 31C.
Precedent	Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 , no law giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Clause (b) or Clause (c) of Article 39 shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent with, or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Article 14 , Article 19 or Article 31; and no law contai ning a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy:  Provided that where such law is made by the <ORG> of a State, the provisions of this article shall not apply thereto unless such law, having been reserved for the consideration of the President, has received his assent.
Precedent	2226.
Precedent	A misconception regarding the ambit of this article may first be removed.
Precedent	The article protects only "law" and not an executive action.
Precedent	The term 'law' is used in Article 13(3) in a wider sense, so as to include an Ordinance, order, bye-law, etc., but that definition is limited to the purposes of Article 13.
Precedent	Article 31C cannot therefore be said to violate the provisions of Article 31(1) under which no person can be deprived of his property save by authority of law.
Precedent	It is, however, not to be denied that the word 'law' in Article 31C may include all incidents and aspects of law-making.
Precedent	2227.
Precedent	In order properly to understand  the scope of Article 31C , it would be necessary to refer to the history of the allied provisions of the Constitution.
Precedent	Prior to the 4th Constitutional Amendment which came into force on April 27, 1915, Articles 31A and 31B which were introduced by the First Amendment Act, 1951 excluded wholly the provisions of Part III in regard to laws providing for the acquisition of any estate or of any rights therein.
Precedent	The reason of the rule was that the rights of society are paramount and must be placed above those of the individual.
Precedent	2228.
RatioOfTheDecision	The language of Article 31C makes it clear that only such laws will receive its protection as are for giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Article 39(b) or (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	Under Clause (b) the <ORG> has to direct its policy towards securing that the ownership and control of the material resources of the community are so distributed as best to subserve the common good.
RatioOfTheDecision	Under Clause (e) the <ORG> has to take steps towards securing that the operation  of the economic systems does not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production to the common detriment.
RatioOfTheDecision	Apart from the declaration contained in the latter part of Article 31B it seems to me transparent that the nexus between a law passed under Article 31C and the objective set out in Article 39(b) and (c) is a condition precedent to the applicability of Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	The declaration cannot be utilised as a cloak to protect laws bearing no relationship with the objective mentioned in the two clauses of Article 39.
RatioOfTheDecision	2229.
RatioOfTheDecision	The objectives set out in Part IV of the Constitution were not limited in their application to agrarian reform.
RatioOfTheDecision	The 4th and 17th Amendments extended the basic principle underlying the First Amendment by introducing changes in Articles 31 and 31A and the Twenty-Fifth Amendment has taken one step further by extending the principle to a vaster field.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31C will operate substantially in the same way as Article 31A has operated in the agrarian sphere.
RatioOfTheDecision	In fact Article 31C is a l ogical extention of the principles underlying Article 31(4) and (6) and Article 31A.
RatioOfTheDecision	2230.
RatioOfTheDecision	I find it difficult to accept the argument, so strongly pressed upon us, that Article 31C delegates the amending power to State Legislatures and empowers them to make amendments to the Constitution without complying with the form and manner prescribed by Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	I am also unable to appreciate that the article empowers the <ORG> likewise.
RatioOfTheDecision	The true nature and character of Article 31C is that it identifies a class of legislation and exempts it from the operation of Articles 14, 19 and 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	Articles 31(4) and (6) identified laws in reference to the period of their enactment.
RatioOfTheDecision	Articles 31(2) and 31A identified the legislative field with reference to the subject-matter of the law.
RatioOfTheDecision	Articles 15(4) and 33 identified laws with reference to the objective of the legislation.
RatioOfTheDecision	In this process no delegation of amending power is involved.
RatioOfTheDecision	Thus, these various provisions, like Article 31C , create a field exempt from the operat ion of some of the Fundamental Rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	The field of legislation is not created by Article 31C.
RatioOfTheDecision	The power to legislate exists apart from and indepedently of it.
RatioOfTheDecision	What the article achieves is to create an immunity against the operation of the specified Fundamental Rights in a pre-existing field of legislation.
RatioOfTheDecision	In principle, I see no distinction between Article 31C on the one hand and Articles 15(4), 31(4), 31(5)(b)(ii), and 31(6) on the other.
RatioOfTheDecision	I may also call attention to Article 31A introduced by the First Amendment Act, 1951 under which "Notwithstanding anything contained in Article 13 ", no law providing for matters mentioned in Clauses (a) to (e) "shall be deemed to be void on the ground that it is inconsistent or takes away or abridges any of the rights conferred by Articles 14, 19 or  31.
RatioOfTheDecision	The fact that the five clauses of Article 31A referred to the subject-matter of the legislation whereas Article 31C refers to laws in relation to their object does not, in my opinion, make any difference in principle.
RatioOfTheDecision	22 31.
RatioOfTheDecision	The argument that Article 31C permits a blatant violation of the form and manner prescribed by Article 368 overlooks that the article took birth after a full and complete compliance with the form and manner spoken of in Article 368.
RatioOfTheDecision	Besides, implicit in the right to amend Article 368 is the power, by complying with the form and manner of Article 368 , to authorise any other body to make the desired amendments to Constitutional provisions.
RatioOfTheDecision	The leading majority judgment in <NAME> case and <NAME> thought of a somewhat similar expedient in suggesting that <ORG> could be convoked for abridging the Fundamental Rights.
RatioOfTheDecision	I do not see any distinction in principle between creating an authority like <ORG> with powers to amend the Constitution and authorising some other named authority or authorities to exercise the same power.
RatioOfTheDecision	This aspect of the matter does not, however, arise for further consideration, because Article 31C does not delegate the power to amend.
RatioOfTheDecision	2232.
RatioOfTheDecision	Th e latter part of Article 31C presents to me no difficulty: "no law containing a declaration that it is for giving effect to such policy shall be called in question in any court on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy."
RatioOfTheDecision	Clearly, this does not exclude the jurisdiction of the court to determine whether the law is for giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Article 39(b) or (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	Laws passed under Article 31C , can, in my opinion, be upheld only, and only if, there is a direct and reasonable nexus between the law and the Directive Policy of the <ORG> expressed in Article 39(b) or (c).
RatioOfTheDecision	The law cannot be called in question on the ground that it does not give effect to such policy but I suppose no court can ever take upon itself the task of finding out whether a <ORG> in fact gives effect to its true policy.
RatioOfTheDecision	If such a latitude were open to the Judges, laws of Prohibition and Gambling should have lost their place on the statute booklong since.
RatioOfTheDecision	2233.
RatioOfTheDecision	In my  opinion, therefore, Section 3 of the Twenty-Fifth Amendment, which introduces Article 31C , is valid.
Precedent	THE CONSTITUTION (TWENTY-NINTH AMENDMENT) ACT, 1972.
Precedent	2234.
Precedent	In regard to the inclusion of the two Kerala Acts, (Act 33 of 1969 and Act 25 of 1971) in the Ninth Schedule by the Twenty-Ninth Amendment, it is urged by the petitioner's counsel that if the provisions of the two Acts do not fall within the terms of 31A(1)(a), the Acts will not get the protection of Article 31B.
Precedent	2235.
RatioOfTheDecision	The validity of Article 31B has been accepted in a series of decisions of this <ORG> and I suppose it is too late in the day to re-open that question; nor indeed did the learned Counsel for the petitioner challenge the validity of that article.
RatioOfTheDecision	<CITATION_41868>  S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	889, a similar contention was considered and rejected by <NAME>, who spoke for the <ORG>.
RatioOfTheDecision	The same view was reiterated in <CITATION_45759>  S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	1020 by <NAME> The argument fell to be considered once again in <CITATION_1681028>  1 S.C.R.
RatioOfTheDecision	636, but <NAME> <NAME> confirmed the view taken in the earlier cases.
RatioOfTheDecision	These cases have consistently held that the opening words of Article 31B: " without prejudice to the generality of the provisions contained in Article 31A" only indicate that the Acts and Regulations specified in the Ninth Schedule would obtain immunity even if they did not attract Article 31A.
RatioOfTheDecision	If every Act in the Ninth Schedule has to be covered by Article 31A , Article 31B would become redundant.
RatioOfTheDecision	Article 31B was, therefore, held not to be governed by Article 31A.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Twenty-Ninth Amendment must, accordingly be held to be valid.
RatioOfTheDecision	2236.
RatioOfTheDecision	Debates of <ORG> and of <ORG> were extensively read out to us during the course of arguments.
RatioOfTheDecision	I read the speeches with interest, but in my opinion, the debates are not admissible as aids to construction of Constitutional provisions.
Precedent	In <NAME>'s case [1950] 1 S.C.R.
Precedent	88, 110, Kania C.J., following the decisions in <ORG> v. The <ORG> [1904] 1 C om.
Precedent	L.R.
Precedent	208 and United States v. <NAME> [169] U.S. 649, 699, observed that while it is not proper to take into consideration the individual opinions of Members of <ORG> to construe the meaning of a particular clause, a reference to the debates may be permitted when a question is raised whether a certain phrase or expression was up for consideration at all or not.
RatioOfTheDecision	According to <NAME> (p. 274), the debates of <ORG> are of doubtful value as an aid to discover the meaning of the words in a Constitution.
RatioOfTheDecision	The learned Judge said that a resort can be had to the debates with great caution and only when latent ambiguities are to be resolved.
Precedent	A similar view was expressed by this Court in <CITATION_1334665>  S.C.R.
Precedent	113.
Precedent	In the <NAME> case, <NAME> clarified that he had not referred to the speeches made in <ORG> for the purpose of interpreting the provisions of Article 368.
Precedent	<NAME> also took the same view.
Precedent	2237.
Precedent	It was urged by the learned Advoc ate-General of Maharashtra that there is a noticeable change in the attitude of this <ORG> to parliamentary debates since the decision in <NAME>'s case and that the most pronounced trend manifested itself first in <NAME> case and then decisively in the Privy Purse case [1971] 3 S.C.R.
Precedent	9, 83.
Precedent	The practice followed in the Privy Purse case is said to have been adopted both by the majority and the minority in <CITATION_1235907>  2 S.C.R., 779, 784, 829-30.
Precedent	2238.
RatioOfTheDecision	I am unable to agree that any reliance was placed in the Privy Purse case or in <NAME>'s case on parliamentary speeches, for the purpose of interpreting the legal provisions.
RatioOfTheDecision	<NAME>, in the Privy Purse case, referred to the speech of Sardar <NAME> in order to show the circumstances in which certain guarantees were given to the former <NAME>.
RatioOfTheDecision	The Advocate-General is right that Mitter J. made use of a speech for construing Article 363 , but that was done without discussing the question as regards the admissibility of the speech.
RatioOfTheDecision	In <NAME> n's case, it is clear from the judgment of the learned Chief Justice, that no use was made of the speeches in <ORG> for construing any legal provision.
RatioOfTheDecision	In fact, the learned Chief Justice observed that he was glad to find from the debates that the interpretation which he and two his colleagues had put on the legal provision accorded with what was intended.
RatioOfTheDecision	2239.
RatioOfTheDecision	It is hazardous to rely upon parliamentary debates as aids to statutory construction.
RatioOfTheDecision	Different speakers have different motives and the system of 'Party Whip' leaves no warrant for assuming that those who voted but did not speak were of identical persuasion.
RatioOfTheDecision	That assumption may be difficult to make even in regard to those who speak.
RatioOfTheDecision	The safest course is to gather the intention of the legislature from language it uses.
RatioOfTheDecision	Therefore, parliamentary proceedings can be used only for a limited purpose as explained in <NAME>'s case.
RatioOfTheDecision	2240.
RatioOfTheDecision	Before summarising my conclusions, let me say that it is with the greatest deference and not without he sitation that I have decided to differ from the eminent Judges who constituted the majority in the <NAME> case.
RatioOfTheDecision	Two of them still adorn this <ORG> and to them as to the other learned Brothers of this <ORG> with whom it has not been possible to agree, I say that it has been no pleasure to differ from them, after being with some of them for a part of the time, on a part of the case.
RatioOfTheDecision	Their concern for common weal, I guess, is no less than mine and so let me express the hope that this long debate and these long opinions will serve to secure at least one blessing-the welfare of the common man.
RatioOfTheDecision	We are all conscious that this vast country has vast problems and it is not easy to realise the dream of the Father of the Nation to wipe every tear from every eye.
Precedent	But, if despite the large powers now conceded to the <ORG>, the social objectives are going to be a dustbin of sentiments, then woe betide those in whom the country has placed such massive faith.
Precedent	2241.
Precedent	My conclusions are briefly these:  2242.
Precedent	The decisi on of the leading majority in the Golak Nath case that the then Article 368 of the Constitution merely prescribed the procedure for amendment of the Constitution and that the power of amendment had to be traced to Entry 97 of List I, Schedule VII read with Articles 245, 246 and 248 is not correct.
Precedent	2243.
Precedent	The decision of the leading majority and of <NAME> there is no distinction between an ordinary law and a law amending the Constitution is incorrect.
Precedent	Article 13(2) took in only ordinary laws, not amendments to the Constitution effected under Article 368.
Precedent	2244.
Precedent	The decision of the leading majority and of <NAME> <ORG> had no power to amend the Constitution so as to abrogate or take away Fundamental Rights is incorrect.
Precedent	2245.
Precedent	The power of amendment of the Constitution conferred by the then Article 368 was wide and unfettered.
Precedent	It reached every part and provision of the Constitution.
Precedent	2246.
Precedent	Preamble is a part of the Constitution and is not outside the reach of the amending power un der Article 368.
Precedent	2247.
Precedent	There are no inherent limitations on the amending power in the sense that <ORG> lacks the power to make amendments so as to damage or destroy the essential features or the fundamental principles of the Constitution.
Precedent	2248.
Precedent	The 24th Amendment only declares the true legal position as it obtained before that Amendment and is valid.
Precedent	2249.
Precedent	Section 2(a) and Section 2(b) of the 25th Amendment are valid.
Precedent	Though courts have no power to question a law described in Article 31(2) substituted by Section 2(a) of the Amendment Act, OP the ground that the amount fixed or determined for compulsory acquisition or requisition is not adequate or that the whole or any part of such amount is to be given otherwise than in cash, courts have the power to question such a law if (i) the amount fixed is illusory; or (ii) if the principles, if any are stated, for determining the amount are wholly irrelevant for fixation of the amount; or (iii) if the power of compulsory acquisition or requisition is e xercised for a collateral purpose; or (iv) if the law of compulsory acquisition or requisition offends the principles of Constitution other than the one which is expressly excepted under Article 31(2B) introduced by Section 2(b) of the 25th Amendment Act - namely Article 19(1)(f); or (v) if the law is in the nature of a fraud on the Constitution.
Precedent	2250.
Precedent	Section 3 of the 25th Amendment which introduced Article 31C into the Constitution is valid.
Precedent	In spite, however, of the purported conclusiveness of the declaration therein mentioned, the <ORG> has the power and the jurisdiction to ascertain whether the law is for giving effect to the policy of the <ORG> towards securing the principles specified in Article 39(b) or (c).
Precedent	If there is no direct and reasonable nexus between such a law and the provisions of Article 39(b) or (c), the law will not, as stated in Article 31C , receive immunity from a challenge under Articles 14, 19 or 31.
Precedent	2251.
Precedent	The 29th Amendment Act is valid.
Precedent	The two Kerala Acts mentioned therein, havi ng been included in the Ninth Schedule, are entitled to the protection of Article 31B of the Constitution.
Precedent	2252.
Precedent	I would direct each party to bear its own costs.
Precedent	2253.
Precedent	As I am coming to the close of my judgment, drafts of judgments of several of my esteemed colleagues are trickling in.
Precedent	As I look at them, I hear a faint whiser of Lord <NAME>.
Precedent	And then I thought : I began this judgment by saying that I wanted to avoid writing a separate judgment of my own.
Precedent	Are first thoughts best?
Precedent	ORDER 2263.
Precedent	<ORG> will determine the validity of the Constitution (Twenty- sixth Amendment) Act , 1971 in accordance with law.
Precedent	2264.
RulingByPresentCourt	The cases are remitted to <ORG> for disposal in accordance with law.
